### [1] [GeoISF: Instance Semantic Forest Inspired Large-Scale Cross-View Geo-Localization via Ground LiDAR-to-Satellite Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28371)
*Di Hu,Xia Yuan,Chunxia Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoISF is a large-scale LiDAR-to-image cross-view geo-localization pipeline that uses an instance semantic forest built on WordNet to bridge the modality gap between point clouds and satellite images, achieving 13.22× improvement in R@10 on KITTI over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization methods struggle in large-scale scenarios due to limited semantic alignment and the modality gap between point clouds and satellite images. A robust solution addressing both computational and accuracy challenges is needed.

Method: GeoISF introduces an instance semantic forest constructed using WordNet, which enhances temporal semantic representation and discriminative power by integrating semantic trees from multiple frames. Environmental semantic representation is leveraged as a shared medium to bridge the modality gap and improve semantic matching accuracy.

Result: GeoISF achieves 13.22 times better performance than the parallel LiDAR-to-image method in the R@10 metric on the KITTI dataset, demonstrating superior large-scale cross-view localization performance.

Conclusion: GeoISF addresses the existing gap in large-scale LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization, offering a robust solution to computational and accuracy challenges. Code will be released as open-source.

Abstract: The problem of localization on a large-scale satellite image given a frame of query ground view point clouds remains challenging. Existing LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization methods struggle in large-scale scenarios due to limited semantic alignment and the modality gap between point clouds and satellite images. This paper introduces the large-scale LiDAR-to-image geo-localization pipeline called GeoISF. GeoISF introduces an instance semantic forest constructed using WordNet, which enhances temporal semantic representation and discriminative power by integrating semantic trees from multiple frames. By leveraging environmental semantic representation as a shared medium, GeoISF effectively bridges the modality gap and improves semantic matching accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of GeoISF in large-scale cross-view localization, 13.22 times better than the parallel LiDAR-to-image method in the R@10 metric on the KITTI dataset. The proposed method addresses the existing gap in large-scale LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization, offering a robust solution to the computational and accuracy challenges inherent in such scenarios. We will release the code as an open-source resource available online for the broader research community.

</details>


### [2] [Memory-Augmented LSTM Autoencoder for Unsupervised Activity Recognition with IMU Sensor Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28377)
*Saeid Arabzadeh,Farshad Almasganj,Mohammad Mahdi Ahmadi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a fully unsupervised spatiotemporal feature fusion framework using a memory-augmented autoencoder for HAR with IMU sensors, achieving 96.6% and 98.4% accuracy on DaLiAc and PAMAP2 datasets, surpassing supervised and unsupervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses key challenges in HAR: reliance on labeled data, multi-sensor fusion complexity, and limited ability of unsupervised methods to capture spatiotemporal dependencies in real-world scenarios with noisy data, overlapping activities, and missing labels.

Method: A fully unsupervised framework using a memory-augmented autoencoder with: (1) Stacked Autoencoder for hierarchical static feature extraction and fusion within/across sensors; (2) Sequence-to-sequence LSTM Autoencoder for temporal refinement using historical motion patterns; (3) Short temporal windows of multi-sensor IMU data for real-time applicability.

Result: 96.6% accuracy on DaLiAc and 98.4% on PAMAP2; up to 9% improvement in feature separability over baselines; realistic inter-class segmentation reduces accuracy by ~7% but better reflects real-world transitions.

Conclusion: The proposed unsupervised framework effectively captures spatiotemporal dependencies without labels, outperforms supervised methods, and demonstrates practical relevance for real-world HAR applications with short temporal windows.

Abstract: HAR using Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) sensors is vital for healthcare monitoring and rehabilitation. Despite deep learning advancements, major challenges remain: reliance on labeled data, multi-sensor fusion complexity, and the limited ability of unsupervised methods to capture spatiotemporal dependencies. These issues are pronounced in real-world scenarios with noisy data, overlapping activities, and missing labels. We propose a fully unsupervised spatiotemporal feature fusion framework using a memory-augmented autoencoder. It enhances activity representations via short temporal windows of multi-sensor IMU data, enabling real-time applications. Our framework extracts hierarchical static features via a Stacked Autoencoder, fusing them within and across sensors. A sequence-to-sequence LSTM Autoencoder then temporally refines these features, incorporating historical motion patterns without labels. We analyze key hyperparameters to identify configurations that maximize feature separability under short-window constraints. Evaluated on DaLiAc and PAMAP2 using realistic inter-class window segmentation, our method achieves 96.6% and 98.4% accuracy, respectively, surpassing supervised baselines and unsupervised approaches. Our method improves feature separability by up to 9% despite shorter temporal windows. While our realistic inter-class segmentation reduces accuracy by ~7%, it was intentionally adopted to better reflect real-world activity transitions and practical relevance.

</details>


### [3] [Zero-Label Driving Scenario Complexity Detection via Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28383)
*Santosh Jaiswal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) trained on unlabelled driving data uses temporal prediction error as a zero-shot complexity score, successfully identifying complex scenarios (unprotected turns, crosswalk interactions, pedestrian proximity) without any labels, validated through ablations and anomaly detection (AP 0.512 vs 0.436 baseline).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifying complex/safety-critical driving scenarios in large unlabelled datasets is expensive and typically requires human annotators, supervised classifiers, or engineered rule sets, all needing substantial prior knowledge. The authors seek a label-free method for automatic complexity discovery.

Method: Train a minimal Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) on structured agent state data from nuPlan mini dataset; use temporal prediction error as a zero-shot complexity score, requiring no labels during training or evaluation.

Result: Model assigns significantly higher scores to unprotected turns, crosswalk interactions, and pedestrian proximity scenarios, and lower scores to lane-following and stationary-traffic scenarios. Validated through four ablations and downstream anomaly detection achieving AP 0.512 vs 0.436 chance baseline.

Conclusion: Temporal prediction error in a self-supervised latent world model serves as a practical proxy for driving scenario complexity without requiring any labels.

Abstract: Identifying complex and safety-critical driving scenarios in large unlabelled datasets is an important but expensive problem. Existing approaches rely on human annotators, supervised classifiers, or carefully engineered rule sets, all of which require substantial prior knowledge about what constitutes a difficult scenario. We ask whether a model can discover scenario complexity on its own, with no labels at any stage. We train a minimal Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) on structured agent state data from the nuPlan mini dataset and use the temporal prediction error as a zero-shot complexity score. Without access to any ground-truth labels during training or evaluation setup, the model assigns significantly higher scores to scenarios involving unprotected turns, crosswalk interactions, and pedestrian proximity, and significantly lower scores to lane-following and stationary-traffic scenarios. We validate this finding through four ablation experiments that isolate the source of the signal, and through a downstream anomaly detection evaluation that achieves Average Precision of 0.512 against a 0.436 chance baseline. The results show that temporal prediction error in a self-supervised latent world model is a practical proxy for driving scenario complexity.

</details>


### [4] [Semantic-Aware, Physics-Informed, Geometry-Grounded Weather Video Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29020)
*Chenghao Qian,Nedko Savov,Lingdong Kong,Yeying Jin,Rui Song,Wenjing Li,Zhun Zhong,Jiaqi Ma,Gustav Markkula,Luc Van Gool*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A Semantic-Aware, Physics-Informed, and Geometry-Grounded framework (W-Crafter) that uses three conditional signals (semantics, dynamics, geometry) to steer off-the-shelf video editors for synthesizing diverse and controllable weather effects, improving autonomous driving robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing weather synthesis methods lack diversity in weather appearance and effective control over weather dynamics (temporal evolution, particle motion); text prompts are underspecified and video editors suppress heavy weather phenomena.

Method: (1) Semantic-aware appearance anchoring for target appearance from semantics and user input; (2) Physics-informed dynamic simulation (Gaussian particle field under gravity, wind, turbulence); (3) Geometry-grounded video synthesis aligning particles with scene geometry.

Result: Produces diverse, physically and visually realistic weather effects; synthesized data significantly improves robustness of autonomous driving semantic segmentation under adverse weather.

Conclusion: Factorizing weather synthesis into semantics, dynamics, and geometry enables controllable, realistic weather effects that benefit downstream tasks like autonomous driving perception.

Abstract: Weather synthesis aims to add weather effects to input videos while preserving scene identity, structure, and motion. The key limitation of existing methods is the lack of diversity in weather appearance and effective control over weather dynamics (e.g., temporal evolution and particle motion). Most approaches rely on text prompts, which are inherently underspecified and often fail to produce detailed weather characteristics. Additionally, general-purpose video editors optimized for clean and aesthetic outputs tend to suppress heavy weather phenomena, making dense particle effects difficult to generate. To address these, we propose a Semantic-Aware, Physics-Informed, and Geometry-Grounded framework that steers an off-the-shelf video editor to synthesize diverse global appearances and detailed particle dynamics. We factorize the synthesis into three conditional signals, so that each provides a distinct and stable source of guidance: semantics specifies what the weather should look like, dynamics governs how it evolves over time, and geometry determines where it should appear in the scene. Specifically, we introduce (1) semantic-aware appearance anchoring to establish the target appearance from scene semantics and user input; (2) physics-informed dynamic simulation to generate particle effects by simulating a Gaussian-represented particle field under gravity, wind, and turbulence; and (3) geometry-grounded video synthesis to align the simulated particles with target scene geometry and synthesize the final video. Experiments demonstrate that our method produces diverse, physically and visually realistic weather effects. Furthermore, we show that our synthesized data significantly improves the robustness of autonomous driving semantic segmentation under adverse weather conditions. Project page: https://jumponthemoon.github.io/w-crafter/.

</details>


### [5] [Data Provenance for Image Auto-Regressive Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28386)
*Bihe Zhao,Louis Kerner,Michel Meintz,Tameem Bakr,Franziska Boenisch,Adam Dziedzic*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a post-hoc framework for detecting characteristic patterns in images generated by autoregressive models (IARs) to enable robust provenance tracing without requiring modifications to the generative process or outputs, making it applicable to already-published content and models without built-in watermarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As image autoregressive models (IARs) become widely accessible for photorealistic content generation, there is a critical need for robust data provenance to trace generated images back to their source model, in order to prevent misinformation, detect fraud, and attribute harmful content.

Method: A post-hoc (post-generation) framework that detects characteristic patterns inherently introduced by the autoregressive generation process in IAR outputs, without requiring any modifications to the generative process or the outputs themselves.

Result: The approach demonstrates effectiveness across a wide range of IARs, proving its high potential for robust data provenance tracing in autoregressive image generation.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables provenance tracing for IAR-generated content even in scenarios where prior watermarking methods cannot be used, such as for already-published content without additional marks or models that do not integrate watermarking.

Abstract: Image autoregressive models (IARs) have recently demonstrated remarkable capabilities in visual content generation, achieving photorealistic quality and rapid synthesis through the next-token prediction paradigm adapted from large language models. As these models become widely accessible, robust data provenance is required to reliably trace IAR-generated images to the source model that synthesized them. This is critical to prevent the spread of misinformation, detect fraud, and attribute harmful content. We find that although IAR-generated images often appear visually identical to real images, their generation process introduces characteristic patterns in their outputs, which serves as a reliable provenance signal for the generated images. Leveraging this, we present a post-hoc framework that enables the robust detection of such patterns for provenance tracing. Notably, our framework does not require modifications of the generative process or outputs. Thereby, it is applicable in contexts where prior watermarking methods cannot be used, such as for generated content that is already published without additional marks and for models that do not integrate watermarking. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across a wide range of IARs, highlighting its high potential for robust data provenance tracing in autoregressive image generation.

</details>


### [6] [KM-Speaker: Keypoint-Based Style Control for High-Quality Speech-Driven 3D Facial Animation and Dialogue Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28568)
*Arthur Josi,Emeline Got,Abdallah Dib,Luiz Gustavo Hafemann,Rafael M. O. Cruz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KM-Speaker is a keypoint-conditioned flow-based generative framework for speech-driven 3D facial animation that separates audio-driven lip motion from keypoint-driven upper-face dynamics, achieving high-fidelity motion and flexible temporal control while outperforming SOTA in lip-sync accuracy, style adherence, and expressive control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing controllable speech-driven 3D facial animation models struggle to simultaneously achieve high-fidelity motion and precise artistic control. They rely on large-scale low-quality in-the-wild datasets compromising realism, and lack fine-grained temporal precision for tasks like dialogue localization where matching specific facial expressions is as critical as lip synchronization.

Method: KM-Speaker proposes a keypoint-conditioned flow-based generative framework with: (1) a disentanglement strategy separating audio-driven lip motion from keypoint-driven upper-face dynamics, (2) a global style context preservation mechanism for coherent full-face expressiveness, enabling both global style guidance and frame-level temporal control from reference performances.

Result: KM-Speaker achieves high-fidelity motion and flexible controllability in a data-constrained setting, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods in lip-sync accuracy, style adherence, and expressive temporal control.

Conclusion: The proposed approach advances example-based 3D facial animation by enabling production-quality animation with both global style control and fine-grained temporal precision, addressing key limitations of existing controllable models.

Abstract: Speech-driven 3D facial animation methods face significant challenges in simultaneously achieving high-fidelity motion and precise artistic control at production quality. Existing controllable models typically learn global style control by relying on large-scale, low-quality \emph{in-the-wild} datasets that compromise overall animation realism. Furthermore, these frameworks often lack the fine-grained temporal precision required for demanding tasks such as dialogue localization (e.g., dubbing), where matching specific facial expressions is as critical as lip synchronization. We present KM-Speaker (Keypoint-Matching Speaker), a novel keypoint-conditioned flow-based generative framework that provides both global style guidance and frame-level temporal control from reference performances. We propose a disentanglement strategy that separates audio-driven lip motion from keypoint-driven upper-face dynamics, together with a global style context preservation mechanism to ensure coherent full-face expressiveness. KM-Speaker advances example-based 3D facial animation by achieving high-fidelity motion and flexible controllability in a data-constrained setting, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods in lip-sync accuracy, style adherence, and expressive temporal control.

</details>


### [7] [SoccerNet 2026 Player-Centric Ball Action Spotting: Per-Player Attention with Agreement-Based Ensembling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28389)
*Faisal Altawijri,Ismail Mathkour*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage pipeline (TAAD + DST) with ensemble fusion improves SoccerNet 2026 ball action spotting from 48.6 to 58.94 Macro-F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve player-centric ball action spotting in broadcast soccer video by combining per-player action detection with structured event sequence transduction and ensemble fusion.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) Track-Aware Action Detector (TAAD) with temporal transformer and training fixes for per-player action logits; (2) Denoising Sequence Transduction (DST) transformer with two-stage per-player attention (spatial-first ordering). Four model variants trained and combined via Weighted Event Fusion with agreement filtering and a tackle-class exception.

Result: Validation Macro-F1 improved by 1.87% with spatial-first attention. Final system achieves 58.94 Macro-F1, up from 48.6 baseline.

Conclusion: The two-stage pipeline with spatial-first attention and weighted ensemble fusion significantly outperforms the baseline, with agreement filtering and tackle exception effectively balancing precision and recall.

Abstract: We present our submission to the SoccerNet 2026 Player-Centric Ball Action Spotting challenge, which uses a two-stage pipeline: a Track-Aware Action Detector (TAAD) produces per-player action logits from broadcast video, and a Denoising Sequence Transduction (DST) transformer converts game-state features and TAAD logits into structured event sequences. We improve the TAAD with a temporal transformer that adds cross-frame context, alongside several training fixes. For the DST stage, we introduce a two-stage per-player attention mechanism operating on game-state features, and show that a spatial-first attention ordering (cross-player attention before temporal attention) improves validation Macro-F1 by 1.87%. To exploit architectural diversity, we train four model variants and combine them with a Weighted Event Fusion ensemble that applies agreement filtering to suppress single-model false positives while preserving recall, plus a dedicated exception for the rare tackle class. Our final system improves the challenge Macro-F1 from a baseline of 48.6 to 58.94.

</details>


### [8] [Meshtryoshka: Differentiable Rendering of Real-World Scenes via Mesh Rasterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28622)
*David Charatan,Daniel Xu,Richard Szeliski,George Kopanas,Vincent Sitzmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Meshtryoshka introduces a mesh-based differentiable rendering framework using nested mesh shells extracted from a signed distance function and composited via alpha blending, achieving competitive novel view synthesis on both object-centric and unbounded real-world scenes using only off-the-shelf triangle rasterizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing differentiable rendering methods rely on custom 3D representations and specialized renderers, while mesh-based approaches are limited to object-centric scenes and fail on large-scale, unbounded environments. The goal is to enable differentiable rendering with mesh representations that scales to unbounded real-world scenes using standard graphics tools.

Method: The method uses a 3D signed distance function (SDF) from which nested mesh shells (like a matryoshka doll) are extracted via iso-surface extraction each forward pass. Vertex opacities are computed as a function of signed distance. Each shell is rasterized independently with an off-the-shelf triangle rasterizer, and results are alpha-composited. Gradients flow through opacity values into the SDF, so vertex positions are updated indirectly, making the method compatible with non-differentiable mesh renderers.

Result: On object-centric scenes, the method performs competitively with surface-based differentiable rendering techniques. Crucially, it scales to unbounded, real-world 3D scenes, producing high-quality novel view synthesis results approaching those of state-of-the-art non-mesh methods.

Conclusion: The work suggests that differentiable rendering can be achieved without specialized renderers, using only conventional computer graphics tools (off-the-shelf rasterizers), opening the door for mesh-based differentiable rendering on large-scale scenes.

Abstract: Differentiable rendering has emerged as a powerful approach for 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis. State-of-the-art differentiable rendering methods combine a variety of custom representations of 3D geometry and appearance with specialized renderers. However, most downstream tasks in computer graphics rely on 3D meshes. While prior work has attempted differentiable rendering with mesh representations, these approaches are limited to object-centric scenes and fail to reconstruct large-scale, unbounded scenes. In this work, we introduce Meshtryoshka, a novel mesh differentiable rendering framework that combines an off-the-shelf triangle rasterizer with a 3D representation that consists of nested mesh shells which resemble a matryoshka doll. In every forward pass, the mesh shells are extracted anew from a 3D signed distance function via iso-surface extraction, and the opacities for each vertex are computed as a function of signed distance. Each mesh shell is then rasterized independently, and the final image is created via alpha compositing. Crucially, mesh vertex positions are updated only indirectly via gradients that flow through the opacity values into the signed distance function, and hence, our method is compatible with off-the-shelf mesh renderers that need not be differentiable with respect to vertex positions. On object-centric scenes, our method performs competitively with surface-based differentiable rendering techniques. Our differentiable mesh rendering method scales to unbounded, real-world 3D scenes, where it yields high-quality novel view synthesis results approaching those of state-of-the-art, non-mesh methods. Our method suggests that it may be possible to solve the differentiable rendering problem without relying on specialized renderers, only using conventional tools from the computer graphics toolbox.

</details>


### [9] [Automated Quality Assessment of Geospatial Vector Data: A GeoAI Approach using Spatial Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28390)
*Hao Li,Chen Chu,Filip Biljecki,Cyrus Shahabi,Wenwen Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Topo4Vec is an automated GeoAI framework that uses Spatial Representation Learning (SRL) and topological error simulation to assess geospatial vector data quality, achieving up to 0.99 accuracy for building footprint overlap detection and 0.60 for street network connectivity errors across three global cities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classic rule-based quality assessment algorithms struggle with diverse urban morphologies and massive data volumes, and GeoAI's application to native vector data quality assessment remains largely underexplored.

Method: Topo4Vec simulates topological errors (overlapping polygons, overshoots/undershoots) to bypass manual annotation, then uses SRL to encode vector geometries into a latent space that separates topological errors from valid geometries.

Result: Peak accuracy of 0.99 for detecting overlapping building footprints and 0.60 for overshoots and undershoots in street networks across Los Angeles, Munich, and Singapore.

Conclusion: Topo4Vec demonstrates a scalable and autonomous GeoAI approach for large-scale vector data quality monitoring in fast-growing geospatial data ecosystems.

Abstract: Geospatial vector data quality is a foundational research topic in GIS, yet classic rule-based quality assessment algorithms often struggle with diverse urban morphologies and massive data volumes. Recently, Geospatial Artificial Intelligence (GeoAI) shows promising potential for automating geospatial analysis, while its application to native vector data remains largely underexplored. To fill this research gap, we proposed Topo4Vec, an automated GeoAI framework, designed for scalable vector data quality assessment via advanced Spatial Representation Learning (SRL). Specifically, Topo4Vec relax the labor-intensive manual annotation process via topological error simulation, such as overlapping polygons and street network connectivity errors e.g., overshoots and undershoots. Then, it leverages state-of-the-art SRL approaches to encode complex, native vector geometries (e.g., polylines and polygons) into a latent space where topological errors are isolated from valid ones. A systematic performance evaluation across three study areas (Los Angeles, Munich, and Singapore) demonstrates the effectiveness and robustness of Topo4Vec, achieving a peak accuracy of 0.99 for detecting overlapping building footprints and 0.60 for overshoots and undershoots in street networks. Moreover, lessons learned from Topo4Vec shed a promising light into a scalable and autonomous GeoAI approach for large-scale vector data consistency and quality monitoring within the fast-growing geospatial data ecosystems. The code and data used in the paper are made openly available in https://figshare.com/s/612148eeb4bccadbd715.

</details>


### [10] [ScAle: Attention Head Scaling as a Minimal Adapter for Spatial Reasoning in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29579)
*Rahul Chowdhury,Timothy A Rupprecht,Xuan Shen,Pu Zhao,Yanzhi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScAle is an ultra-lightweight adaptation method that learns scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in frozen VLMs, achieving up to 134.1% relative accuracy gains on spatial reasoning tasks using only 1K trainable parameters, outperforming standard PEFT methods like LoRA in parameter efficiency while preserving non-spatial VQA accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial reasoning remains challenging for VLMs, and existing improvement methods require fine-tuning with substantial additional parameters. The authors observed that rescaling activations in selected transformer layers can significantly influence downstream performance without modifying pretrained weights, motivating an extremely parameter-efficient approach.

Method: ScAle learns a small set of scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in a fully frozen backbone, using only 1K trainable parameters, making it an ultra-lightweight adaptation method.

Result: Achieves up to 134.1% relative accuracy gains on spatial reasoning benchmarks (SpatialEval) and strong performance on real-world VQA datasets (COCOQA, VGQA) across multiple model families, using only 1K trainable parameters compared to millions required by LoRA and other standard PEFT methods.

Conclusion: Bounded activation reweighting provides a simple, architecture-agnostic, and highly parameter-efficient alternative for adapting pretrained VLMs, recovering substantial PEFT performance with extreme compactness.

Abstract: Spatial reasoning remains a persistent challenge for many vision language models (VLMs), and improving it typically requires fine-tuning with substantial additional parameters. Our preliminary analysis reveals that rescaling activations in selected transformer layers-without modifying pretrained weights-can significantly influence downstream performance. Motivated by this observation, we propose ScAle, an ultra-lightweight adaptation method that learns a small set of scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in a fully frozen backbone. We evaluate our method on the synthetic spatial reasoning benchmark SpatialEval and on real-world VQA datasets (COCOQA and VGQA) across multiple model families. Our method, ScAle, achieves up to 134.1% relative accuracy gains using only 1K trainable parameters without requiring millions of trainable parameters as in standard PEFT methods such as LoRA. Despite its extreme compactness, our approach recovers a substantial fraction of standard PEFT performance while preserving strong non-spatial VQA accuracy. These results demonstrate that bounded activation reweighting provides a simple, architecture-agnostic, and highly parameter-efficient alternative for adapting pretrained VLMs.

</details>


### [11] [AEGIR: Modeling Area Emitters for Indoor Inverse Rendering using Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28635)
*Mohamed Shawky Sabae,Philipp Langsteiner,Jan-Niklas Dihlmann,Hendrik Lensch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AEGIR introduces explicit local area emitters into relightable Gaussian Splatting, enabling accurate illumination-material decomposition and realistic light transport through differentiable deferred rendering with multiple importance sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing relightable Gaussian Splatting methods approximate lighting with point lights, global environment maps, or implicit representations, which ignore the physical spatial extent of real-world emitters, leading to incorrect light attenuation and unrealistic shadows.

Method: AEGIR models explicit local area emitters within a relightable Gaussian Splatting framework, using a differentiable deferred rendering pipeline with multiple importance sampling and targeted regularization to jointly optimize emitters, materials, and geometry.

Result: Explicit area emitters improve illumination reconstruction and enhance novel view synthesis, controlled relighting, and virtual object insertion, especially in scenes with complex local lighting.

Conclusion: Modeling physical spatial extent of emitters leads to more consistent illumination-material decomposition and realistic light transport compared to discrete or implicit lighting approximations.

Abstract: Inverse rendering requires separating illumination from surface materials, which is highly ambiguous due to their tight coupling in observed images. While Gaussian Splatting is efficient for novel view synthesis, existing relightable methods approximate scene lighting using discrete point lights, global environment maps, or implicit representations. By ignoring the physical spatial extent of real-world emitters, these approaches produce incorrect light attenuation and unrealistic shadows. We present AEGIR (Area Emitters for Gaussian Inverse Rendering), a framework that explicitly models local area emitters within a relightable Gaussian Splatting representation. Joint optimization of emitters, materials, and geometry is challenging due to flexible emitter parameterization, which increases both the number of parameters and the ambiguity between illumination and materials. We address this by introducing a differentiable deferred rendering pipeline that integrates multiple importance sampling with targeted regularization. As a result, AEGIR accurately simulates local light transport and achieves more consistent decomposition. Experiments show that explicit area emitters improve illumination reconstruction and enhance downstream tasks, including novel view synthesis, controlled relighting, and virtual object insertion, particularly in scenes with complex local lighting.

</details>


### [12] [Few-class Fidelity: Evaluating Explanations of Real-conditions CNN classifiers with Optimized Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28391)
*Wistan Marchadour,Pedro Soto Vega,Franck Vermet,Mathieu Hatt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a variation of Fidelity-based XAI metrics designed for real-world, low-class-count applications, using in-distribution uncertainty-provoking perturbations to measure faithfulness of XAI methods. The framework is validated against human-centric metrics on medical and natural imaging datasets, revealing complex interactions between domain, data curation, and XAI method selection for CNN model validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing XAI evaluation methods lack consensus and diverge in comparisons. There is a need for reliable metrics that work under real-world conditions (low number of classes) to faithfully measure XAI method performance and support CNN model validation.

Method: The authors propose a Fidelity-based metric variation that uses in-distribution, uncertainty-provoking perturbations to evaluate XAI faithfulness. The framework is compared with human-centric object localization and segmentation metrics, and tested on both medical and natural imaging datasets.

Result: The proposed evaluation framework reveals intricate correlations between domain (medical vs. natural imaging), data curation strategies, and the choice of XAI method when validating CNN model training.

Conclusion: The proposed Fidelity-based metric variation is useful for evaluating XAI methods in real-world settings with few classes, and highlights the importance of considering domain and data characteristics when selecting XAI techniques for CNN validation.

Abstract: The wide use of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) in numerous domains and real-world classification applications is justified by their high precision and automation speed, helping users concentrate on higher-expertise tasks. To better understand the models and avoid bias during deployment, eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques can be used after training. But as the list of XAI solutions expand, comparisons between them diverge, and consensus over their evaluation cannot be reached. This paper proposes a variation of Fidelity-based XAI metrics, with a focus on real-conditions applications, where the number of classes is often low. The approach generates in-distribution, uncertainty-provoking perturbations, to ensure proper measurement of the XAI methods faithfulness. As demonstration of the evaluation framework usefulness, it is compared with human-centric object localization and segmentation metrics. Once applied to both medical and natural imaging applications, it highlights the intricate correlation between domain, data curation, and XAI solution choices in order to validate training of a new CNN model.

</details>


### [13] [LEIQ-Assessor: Multi-dimensional Quality Assessment of Low-light Enhanced Images via Multi-task Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29752)
*Wei Sun,Yanwei Jiang,Dandan Zhu,Jinqiu Sang,Jikai Xu,Weixia Zhang,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LEIQ-Assessor is a multi-task learning model for low-light image enhancement quality assessment, using a SigLIP2 ViT backbone to predict overall MOS and six perceptual sub-attributes, achieving second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-light image enhancement often introduces artifacts (noise, color shift, structural damage, over-exposure), necessitating a reliable IQA metric to evaluate enhancement quality for algorithm development and practical applications.

Method: Uses a pre-trained SigLIP2 Vision Transformer backbone with multi-task learning to jointly predict overall MOS and six sub-attributes (lightness, color fidelity, noise level, exposure quality, naturalness, content recovery), optimized via PLCC loss.

Result: Significantly outperforms existing no-reference IQA models and hand-crafted descriptors on the MLE benchmark; achieved second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge.

Conclusion: Multi-task learning with shared representations captures richer quality-aware features than single-task approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly optimizing correlated perceptual objectives for low-light image enhancement quality assessment.

Abstract: Low-light image enhancement algorithms (LIEAs) aim to improve the visibility of images captured under poor illumination. However, the enhancement process often introduces artifacts such as noise amplification, color shift, structural damage, and over-exposure, which degrade the perceptual quality of the enhanced images. Therefore, a reliable image quality assessment (IQA) metric for evaluating enhancement effects is of great importance for both the development of LIEAs and their practical applications. In this paper, we present \textbf{LEIQ-Assessor}, a multi-dimensional quality assessment model for low-light image enhancement based on multi-task learning, developed for the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge on Low-light Enhanced Image Quality Assessment. Specifically, our method leverages a pre-trained SigLIP2 Vision Transformer as the backbone and simultaneously predicts the overall Mean Opinion Score (MOS) together with six perceptual sub-attributes: lightness, color fidelity, noise level, exposure quality, naturalness, and content recovery. By jointly optimizing these correlated objectives via the PLCC loss, the shared representation captures richer quality-aware features than its single-task counterpart. Experiments on the MLE benchmark demonstrate that LEIQ-Assessor significantly outperforms existing no-reference IQA models and hand-crafted quality descriptors. Our method achieved second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge on Low-light Enhanced Image Quality Assessment. The code is available at https://github.com/sunwei925/LEIQ-Assessor.

</details>


### [14] [HorizonRelight: Relighting Long-horizon Videos Consistently via Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29095)
*Jing Yang,Mayoore Jaiswal,Zian Wang,Steven Zeng,Rochelle Pereira,Yajie Zhao,Jianyuan Min*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper addresses temporal discontinuities in diffusion-based video relighting for long-horizon videos by reframing it as temporally conditioned latent domain translation with masked target-domain self-conditioning and warm-start prompting, achieving improved temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based video relighting methods, when applied to long videos via chunked sliding-window inference, suffer from temporal discontinuities at chunk boundaries due to the backbone being trained on short clips.

Method: (1) Reframes long-horizon relighting as temporally conditioned latent domain translation. (2) Enforces cross-chunk continuity by propagating target-domain latents across boundaries. (3) Uses masked target-domain self-conditioning to make this propagation learnable. (4) Introduces warm-start prompting with a relit prompt anchor from a controllable generative model to establish the initial target-domain state.

Result: Experiments on in-the-wild long-horizon videos show markedly improved temporal consistency, with chunk-boundary artifacts largely reduced and unwanted appearance changes across chunks greatly suppressed.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles long-horizon video relighting by maintaining cross-chunk temporal consistency, reducing boundary artifacts, and providing a general interface for prompt-based relighting.

Abstract: Diffusion-based video relighting enables controllable relighting from a single input video, but modern video diffusion backbones are trained on short clips and applied to long-horizon videos through chunked sliding-window inference, often causing temporal discontinuities at chunk boundaries. We address this by reframing long-horizon relighting as \emph{temporally conditioned latent domain translation}. Our framework enforces cross-chunk continuity by propagating target-domain latents across boundaries and makes this behavior learnable using \emph{masked target-domain self-conditioning}, training the model to continue from temporally masked propagated context. We further introduce \emph{warm-start prompting} with a relit prompt anchor from a controllable generative model, which establishes the initial target-domain state and creates a general interface for prompt-based relighting. Experiments on in-the-wild long-horizon videos show markedly improved temporal consistency, with chunk-boundary artifacts largely reduced and unwanted appearance changes across chunks greatly suppressed.

</details>


### [15] [RADIANT-PET: Reasoning-Augmented PET/CT Lesion Segmentation with Large Language Models and Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28392)
*Jiasheng Wang,Tanun Jitwatcharakomol,Piyawadee Jongpradubgiat,Simeng Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RADIANT-PET is a reasoning-augmented framework for PET/CT lesion segmentation that combines a permissive voxel-level segmentation model with an LLM-based adjudication layer. It converts candidate uptake regions into structured textual descriptions, then uses an LLM (further optimized via GRPO reinforcement learning) to classify each candidate as true lesion vs. false positive, optionally leveraging radiology reports. The approach consistently outperforms image-only baselines on AutoPET and OSU cohorts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lesion segmentation in PET/CT is challenging because physiologic tracer uptake and artifacts can mimic malignant signal, leading to false positives. Existing image-only segmentation models lack the clinical reasoning capability to distinguish true lesions from physiologic uptake.

Method: (1) A high-sensitivity (permissive) voxel-level segmentation stage generates candidate uptake regions. (2) These regions are converted into structured textual descriptions summarizing uptake intensity, morphology, and regional/global anatomical context. (3) An LLM classifies each candidate as true lesion vs. false positive, optionally using the radiology report. (4) A local LLM is further optimized via reinforcement learning (GRPO) with rewards for correct lesion classification and anatomically concordant site assignment.

Result: RADIANT-PET consistently outperforms strong image-only baselines on both AutoPET and OSU test cohorts, with the largest improvements observed when radiology reports are provided as additional clinical context.

Conclusion: LLM-based lesion-level reasoning adds a novel reasoning layer beyond conventional segmentation, effectively suppressing physiologic false positives and aligning voxel-level predictions with clinical interpretation.

Abstract: Accurate lesion segmentation in PET/CT is critical for oncology, yet remains challenging because physiologic tracer uptake and artifacts can mimic malignant signal. We present RADIANT-PET, a reasoning-augmented framework that couples a high-sensitivity voxel-level segmentation model with lesion-level large language model (LLM) adjudication. Candidate uptake regions are generated with a deliberately permissive segmentation stage, then converted into structured textual descriptions that summarize uptake intensity, morphology, and regional and global anatomical context. An LLM classifies each candidate as true lesion vs. false positive, optionally leveraging the radiology report as additional clinical context. To strengthen lesion-level reasoning, we further optimize a local LLM via reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization, rewarding correct lesion classification and anatomically concordant site assignment. Across AutoPET and an OSU test cohort, RADIANT-PET consistently outperforms strong image-only baselines, with the largest improvements observed when radiology reports are provided. Overall, these results demonstrate that LLM-based lesion-level reasoning adds a novel reasoning layer beyond conventional segmentation, suppressing physiologic false positives and aligning voxel-level predictions with clinical interpretation. The project repository is available at: https://github.com/jwang-580/RADIANT-PET.

</details>


### [16] [GPC: Large-Scale Generative Pretraining for Transferable Motor Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29148)
*Yi Shi,Yifeng Jiang,Chen Tessler,Xue Bin Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GPC uses tokenization and next-token prediction with FSQ and RL to create reusable generative controllers for physics-based character animation, achieving 99.98% motion reproduction success with emergent robust behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable practical physics-based character animation, there is a need for controllers that can handle a wide range of tasks naturally and robustly, but existing approaches face challenges in scalability, reusability, and training complexity.

Method: End-to-end RL jointly optimizes a motion vocabulary via Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) and a control policy mapping discrete codes to physics-based controls. A GPT-style autoregressive transformer models the codebook structure for next-token prediction, with a suite of adaptation techniques for downstream finetuning.

Result: Achieves 99.98% success rate in reproducing a large corpus of motion clips, exhibits emergent behaviors like perturbation response and fall recovery, and simplifies training compared to prior tokenized methods.

Conclusion: GPC produces highly robust, general-purpose controllers for physics-based character animation, suitable for a variety of downstream applications with natural and life-like motion capabilities.

Abstract: Developing controllers capable of completing a wide range of tasks in a natural and life-like manner is a key challenge in enabling practical applications of physics-based character animation. In this work, we introduce Generative Pretrained Controllers (GPC), which leverage tokenization and next-token modeling to create general-purpose, reusable generative controllers from large-scale motion datasets. Our framework utilizes end-to-end reinforcement learning to jointly optimize a "motion vocabulary", modeled via Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ), along with a corresponding control policy that can map the discrete codes to physics-based controls. After the "codebook" has been learned, the underlying structure of this large vocabulary is modeled by training a GPT-style autoregressive transformer, leading to a powerful generative controller that generates controls for a physically simulated character by performing next-token prediction. Once the generative controller has been trained, we propose a suite of adaptation techniques for finetuning the controller for new downstream tasks. Our proposed framework greatly simplifies the training process compared to previous tokenized methods, and achieves a 99.98% success rate in reproducing a vast corpus of motion clips. The generative controller exhibits a variety of natural emergent behaviors, such as responsive behaviors to perturbations and recovery behaviors after falling. This results in highly robust general purpose controllers for a variety of downstream applications.

</details>


### [17] [Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28393)
*Halil Ibrahim Gulluk,Max Van Puyvelde,Wim Van Criekinge,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First training-free best-of-N sampling for chest X-ray report generation that is explicitly aware of longitudinal (prior-to-current) transitions, using directional set distances to score candidate reports by how well they reflect change from prior exams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In longitudinal clinical practice, radiologists interpret chest X-rays by comparing them to the patient's prior exam, emphasizing changes over time. Existing report generators are not explicitly aware of this longitudinal transition, which limits their clinical utility.

Method: Proposes transition-aware best-of-N sampling: splits reports into sentences embedded as unordered sets in Rd; computes a directional vector from (prior, current) pairs via set-to-set distances; scores candidates by cosine similarity of their transition vector to cached ground-truth training transition vectors (min or kNN). Instantiates with four directional set distances: mean-shift, novelty residual, directed-Hausdorff anchor, and cost-weighted optimal transport.

Result: Evaluated on a multi-visit AP-PA cohort with three prompts and three vision-language generators. Transition-aware best-of-N outperforms random selection across all settings, with largest relative gains on the Impression section.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling longitudinal change via directional set distances and best-of-N sampling improves chest X-ray report generation, particularly for clinically critical Impression sections, without requiring additional training.

Abstract: In longitudinal clinical practice, every chest X-ray is read in the context of the patients prior exam, and much of what the radiologist communicates is the change from one visit to the next. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first training-free best-of-N sampling scheme for pre-trained chest X-ray report generators that is explicitly aware of this longitudinal prior to current transition. We call it transition-aware best-of-N sampling, each report is split into sentences and embedded into an unordered set in Rd; each (prior, current) pair is reduced to a fixed-dim directional vector via a set-to-set distance designed to encode the change between the two sets; and candidates are scored by cosine distance from their candidate transition vector to a cached bank of ground-truth training transition vectors, aggregated as min or kNN. We instantiate the framework with four directional set distances (mean-shift, novelty residual, directed-Hausdorff anchor, and cost-weighted optimal transport) and evaluate on a multi-visit AP-PA cohort, running inference under three prompts on three vision-language generators. Transition-aware best-of-N outperforms random selection across the board, with the largest relative gains on the Impression section.

</details>


### [18] [L2D2-GS: Learning to Densify for Feedforward Dynamic Gaussian Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29374)
*Zetian Song,Chenming Wu,Junnan Liu,Chitian Sun,Liangliang He,Hangjun Ye,Jiaqi Zhang,Siwei Ma,Wen Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: L2D2-GS is a unified framework that reformulates dynamic urban scene reconstruction from feedforward 3DGS models as an iterative optimization-densification process, using self-supervised densification and geometric regularization to achieve state-of-the-art fidelity with fewer primitives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS methods face a trade-off: per-scene optimization is expensive and not scalable, while feedforward methods struggle with memory constraints at high resolutions and inconsistent multi-view fusion. This work aims to bridge the gap between speed and fidelity for generalizable dynamic urban reconstruction.

Method: 1) Reformulates reconstruction as iterative optimization and densification rather than one-shot regression. 2) Self-supervised densification policy that uses global reconstruction gains as reward signals to guide local primitive generation. 3) Geometric regularization via reparameterization to constrain the optimization manifold and avoid poor local minima.

Result: State-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity and strong zero-shot generalization on PandaSet and Waymo datasets, while using fewer primitives than competing baselines.

Conclusion: L2D2-GS effectively resolves the ambiguity in primitive supervision and irreversible early-stage artifacts, offering a scalable and high-fidelity solution for dynamic urban environment reconstruction.

Abstract: High-fidelity reconstruction of dynamic urban environments is a cornerstone of autonomous driving simulation and large-scale world modeling. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has established a new standard for real-time rendering, its reliance on expensive per-scene optimization limits scalability. Conversely, recent feedforward methods that infer Gaussian parameters offer faster speed but face fundamental bottlenecks: they are memory-prohibitive at high resolutions and struggle to fuse dense multi-view observations consistently. This paper presents L2D2-GS, a unified framework that reformulates generalizable reconstruction not as a one-shot regression, but as a robust iterative process of optimization and densification. To resolve the ambiguity of supervision in primitive generation, we propose a self-supervised densification policy that derives explicit reward signals from global reconstruction gains to guide local densification. Furthermore, we mitigate irreversible early-stage artifacts through a geometric regularization mechanism, utilizing reparameterization to constrain the optimization manifold and prevent convergence to poor local optima. Extensive experiments on the PandaSet and Waymo datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity and strong zero-shot generalization, while using fewer primitives than competing baselines.

</details>


### [19] [GPU-Accelerated Inverse Structural Anastylosis from Block Collapse Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28394)
*L. A. Muñoz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JIP-2 is a GPU-accelerated deep learning framework that treats structural anastylosis (reassembling collapsed stone monuments) as an inverse prediction task, using physics simulation and a dual-stream ResNet-18 to predict block removal sequences from images of collapsed assemblies, tested on Jenga-like configurations with implications for the UNESCO Maya site of Uxmal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical anastylosis is labor-intensive and subjective, relying on expert judgment and manual block correspondence. Inspired by the combinatorial complexity seen in Jenga, the authors aim to automate and accelerate the reconstruction process using deep learning and physics simulation.

Method: (1) Rigid-body physics engine with OBB/SAT collision detection and PGS contact solver (GPU-accelerated via Numba JIT and CuPy CUDA); (2) analytical force thresholds (Ziglar, CMU 2006) over three friction levels, 450 simulated episodes; (3) dual-stream ResNet-18 injecting friction one-hot vector, jointly predicting block removal count, per-position probabilities, COM imbalance, and Ziglar torque risk; (4) smooth 3-D video generation of reverse reconstruction.

Result: The framework successfully predicts block removal sequences from images of collapsed Jenga-like configurations across three friction levels, generating plausible reverse reconstructions validated via simulation.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates potential for computer-assisted anastylosis at the UNESCO Maya site of Uxmal, with implications for automating reconstruction of collapsed architectural monuments.

Abstract: The physical anastylosis of collapsed architectural monuments -- the meticulous reassembly of fallen stone elements into their original structural configuration -- represents one of the most intellectually demanding challenges in conservation science. Traditional approaches depend heavily on expert archaeologist judgement and manual block-by-block correspondence, a process that is both labour-intensive and inherently subjective. Inspired by the combinatorial complexity of this problem as manifested in the game of Jenga, we present Jenga Inverse Predictor , a GPU-accelerated deep learning framework that addresses structural anastylosis as an inverse prediction task. Given an image of a collapsed block assembly, JIP-2 reconstructs the most probable prior tower configuration by: (1) implementing a complete rigid-body physics engine with OBB/SAT collision detection and a Projected Gauss-Seidel (PGS) contact solver accelerated with Numba JIT and CuPy CUDA; (2) applying the analytical force thresholds of Ziglar (CMU, 2006) -- F_app = 3*mu_s*m*g (Y-axis, torque-free) and F_app = 4*mu_s*m*g (X-axis, torque risk) -- over three friction levels (mu_s in {0.25, 0.40, 0.60}) across 450 simulated episodes; (3) training a dual-stream ResNet-18 that injects a friction one-hot vector and jointly predicts block removal count, per-position removal probabilities, centre-of-mass imbalance, and Ziglar torque risk; and (4) generating a smooth 3-D video of the block-by-block reverse reconstruction. We discuss implications for computer-assisted anastylosis at the UNESCO Maya site of Uxmal, Yucatan, and provide a detailed technical description of the full pipeline, architecture, and loss formulation.

</details>


### [20] [DR-GS: Physically-Based Deformable and Relightable 2D Gaussians](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29379)
*Jiaxin Li,Tong Wu,Yi Wei,Tailin Wu,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DR-GS is a unified Gaussian framework for deformable and relightable objects that disentangles geometry, illumination, and material representations, enabling physically consistent rendering under dynamic deformation and lighting changes while supporting post-reconstruction material editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GS-based methods for deformable objects suffer from (i) illumination baked into textures causing physically inconsistent appearance under dynamic deformations/lighting changes, and (ii) snapshot-based reconstruction preventing post-reconstruction material editing.

Method: DR-GS integrates physically-based inverse rendering, relighting, and deformation-aware manipulation within a unified Gaussian framework by explicitly disentangling geometry, illumination, and material representations.

Result: Achieves leading visual quality across static reconstruction, dynamic deformation, and relighting, reliably preserving reflections and specular highlights on glossy surfaces.

Conclusion: Establishes a fully decoupled geometry-illumination-material pipeline enabling high-quality 3D asset creation and comprehensive post-editing.

Abstract: Gaussian splatting (GS) has garnered significant attention in VR/AR and digital content creation due to its explicit parameterization and efficient rendering capabilities. However, existing GS-based methods for deformable objects face two key limitations: (i) illumination is erroneously baked into textures, causing physically inconsistent responses under dynamic deformations and lighting changes; (ii) snapshot-based reconstruction restricts post-reconstruction material editing. To address these challenges, we propose Deformable and Relightable GS (DR-GS), a unified Gaussian framework that integrates physically-based inverse rendering, relighting, and deformation-aware manipulation. Through explicitly disentangling geometry, illumination, and material representations, DR-GS overcomes the limitations of static snapshots, resolving unrealistic appearance under varying conditions while enabling post-reconstruction parameter editing. Extensive experiments show that DR-GS achieves leading visual quality across static reconstruction, dynamic deformation, and relighting, reliably preserving reflections and specular highlights on glossy surfaces. It further establishes a fully decoupled geometry-illumination-material pipeline, enabling high-quality 3D asset creation and comprehensive post-editing.

</details>


### [21] [JASPR: Joint Spatial Representation learning of histology and spatial genomics for improved virtual genomic screening and clinical prognostication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28395)
*Marija Pizurica,Eric Zimmermann,Neil Tenenholtz,James Hall,Olivier Gevaert,Ava P. Amini,Lorin Crawford,Kristen A. Severson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JASPR is a self-supervised deep learning framework that integrates HE images and spatial transcriptomics data through cross-modal reconstruction with spatial context, improving gene expression prediction and breast cancer prognosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial properties of tumors are critical for understanding disease biology and predicting outcomes, but existing methods fail to effectively learn joint representations that incorporate spatial context across spatial transcriptomics (ST) and histology (HE) modalities.

Method: JASPR is a self-supervised deep learning framework that integrates HE images and ST data via cross-modal reconstruction with spatial context. It uses shared modules for universal spatial properties across modalities and modality-specific experts for features unique to morphological and genomic data.

Result: On breast cancer datasets, JASPR's joint representation substantially improves HE-based prediction of 9,248 genes and provides prognostic value for breast cancer outcomes.

Conclusion: JASPR effectively learns joint spatial representations across HE and ST modalities, enabling better gene prediction from histology alone and offering prognostic insights for breast cancer.

Abstract: Recent studies have shown that spatial properties of tumors are critical for understanding disease biology and predicting patient outcomes. These spatial properties are increasingly uncovered through complementary modalities: spatial transcriptomics (ST) captures spatially-resolved molecular states, while hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images (HE) reveal tissue morphology. While approaches are emerging to fuse these modalities, effective methods that learn not only joint representations but also incorporate spatial context across modalities are lacking. Here, we present JASPR (Joint Spatial Representation learning), a self-supervised deep learning framework that integrates HE images and ST data through a cross-modal reconstruction objective that incorporates spatial context within HE images and ST profiles. It employs shared modules to capture universal spatial properties across modalities, while modality-specific experts encode features unique to morphological and genomic data. We train and validate JASPR on breast cancer datasets, demonstrating that its learned joint representation substantially improves HE-based prediction of 9,248 genes and provides prognostic value for breast cancer outcomes.

</details>


### [22] [Learning to Adaptively Allocate Gaussians for Arbitrary-Scale Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29400)
*Giulio Federico,Giuseppe Amato,Claudio Gennaro,Fabio Carrara,Marco Di Benedetto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QuADA-GS is a feed-forward Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution model that uses Neural Routing Architecture for adaptive densification and Hierarchical Pointer Convolution for efficient non-grid neighbor lookup, achieving SOTA ASR performance with low latency and memory footprint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ASR methods either rely on sub-optimal interpolation for continuous resolutions or process pixels uniformly, creating computational overhead since fine details are sparse. Gaussian Splatting offers advantages via adaptive densification but transitioning it into a feed-forward model for ASR is challenging because standard GS optimization needs high-resolution gradients for primitive growth that are unavailable during inference.

Method: QuADA-GS encodes inputs into latent space, then uses a Neural Routing Architecture to evaluate local complexity and distribute a global budget, assigning specific upsampling factors to features. Features are dynamically densified based on these factors into an irregular topology, decoded into 2D Gaussian primitives. Hierarchical Pointer Convolution (O(1) neighbor lookup) coordinates features before decoding, bypassing dense bottlenecks.

Result: QuADA-GS achieves state-of-the-art Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution performance while maintaining low latency and a lean memory footprint.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive densification strategy combined with efficient non-grid convolution enables high-quality continuous super-resolution without the overhead of uniform pixel processing or reliance on high-resolution gradient signals during inference.

Abstract: In computer graphics, visual content is continuously warped, zoomed and resampled. This occurs when engines upscale frames, users zoom into 3D scenes, or foveated VR applies varying scaling. Handling these transformations requires Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution (ASR). Traditional models, designed for fixed scales, typically predict at a lower integer scale (e.g., x4) and rely on sub-optimal interpolation for continuous resolutions, compromising quality. Furthermore, most methods process pixels uniformly. Since fine details are sparse, this creates overhead; efficiency dictates concentrating resources only where structural complexity demands it. While implicit models and Gaussian Splatting (GS) enable continuous representation, GS is advantageous due to adaptive densification. However, transitioning GS into a feed-forward model for ASR is non-trivial. Standard GS optimization needs high-resolution gradients to drive primitive growth, which are unavailable during inference. Thus, the network must autonomously predict GS densification from low-resolution inputs. To solve this, we propose QuADA-GS. After encoding inputs into a latent space, a Neural Routing Architecture evaluates local complexity to distribute a global budget, assigning specific upsampling factors to features to avoid redundant processing. Features are dynamically densified based on these factors, forming an irregular topology decoded into 2D Gaussian primitives. To coordinate features before decoding, we introduce Hierarchical Pointer Convolution. This non-grid operator achieves O(1) neighbor lookup complexity, facilitating efficient spatial communication and bypassing dense bottlenecks. Experiments show QuADA-GS achieves state-of-the-art ASR performance, maintaining low latency and a lean memory footprint.

</details>


### [23] [RadarTwin: Scene-Specific mmWave Radar Simulation and Learning for Mobile Indoor Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28396)
*Emily Bejerano,Federico Tondolo,Devang Gupta,Aaron Mano Cherian,Taeyoo Kim,Ayaan Qayyum,Xiaofan Yu,Xiaofan Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RadarTwin is a framework that uses a 3D reconstruction of a target space, a vision-language model for material inference, and a physics-based ray tracer to synthesize realistic FMCW radar training data before any real data collection, achieving 95.3% accuracy on 12-way object recognition with few real labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Millimeter-wave radar perception suffers from data scarcity—models trained on existing radar datasets fail to generalize to new objects, environments, and sensing trajectories.

Method: RadarTwin takes a 3D reconstruction of a target space (from phone LiDAR, robot sensing, or RGB-to-3D), uses a vision-language model to infer radar-relevant surface materials, and applies physics-based ray tracing to synthesize raw FMCW radar measurements with multi-bounce propagation.

Result: Simulated and real radar share the same object-discriminative shape and material features; multipath modeling is essential. A simulation-only representation recognizes real objects at 2.5× chance, and adding few labeled examples achieves 95.3% on a 12-way recognition task.

Conclusion: RadarTwin enables training radar perception for a new space before any real radar data is collected there, addressing the generalization bottleneck in mmWave radar.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar perception is limited by data scarcity: models trained on existing radar datasets fail to generalize to new objects, environments, and sensing trajectories. We present RadarTwin, a framework for generating deployment-specific radar training data before real data collection. Given a 3D reconstruction of a target space (phone LiDAR, robot-mounted sensing, or RGB-to-3D), RadarTwin uses a vision-language model to infer radar-relevant surface materials and a physics-based ray tracer to synthesize raw frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar measurements with multi-bounce propagation. To study what transfers from simulation to reality, we collect a paired real-simulated dataset spanning household objects, material classes, distances, rotations, translations, and mobile sensing trajectories. We show that simulated and real radar share the same object-discriminative shape and material features, and that modeling the environment's multipath is essential to matching real measurements. A representation trained on simulation alone recognizes real objects at 2.5 times chance with no real radar labels, and a few labeled examples raise this to 95.3% on a 12-way recognition task. RadarTwin enables training radar perception for a new space before any real radar data is collected there.

</details>


### [24] [Resonant Brane Splatting for Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29453)
*Giulio Federico,Giuseppe Amato,Claudio Gennaro,Fabio Carrara,Marco Di Benedetto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RBS replaces 2D Gaussian Splatting with "Brane" primitives (Gaussian-Hermite modes) for arbitrary-scale super-resolution, enabling higher-quality reconstruction with fewer overlapping primitives and faster rasterization via quantum-inspired culling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard 2D Gaussian Splatting (GS) for ASR uses smooth low-pass primitives, requiring many overlapping splats to model edges and textures, which creates bottlenecks during rasterization.

Method: RBS introduces Branes: primitives that augment the Gaussian envelope with internal Gaussian-Hermite modes. Each mode has a distinct color coefficient; zero-order recovers standard GS, higher-order modes capture high frequencies. Parameters are predicted from low-resolution features. An efficient rasterizer uses a quantum turning point-based culling strategy to skip negligible regions.

Result: RBS improves reconstruction quality over implicit and GS baselines on standard ASR benchmarks, achieving a superior speed-quality trade-off compared to prior GS methods.

Conclusion: Branes offer a mathematically richer formulation than Gaussians, requiring fewer overlapping primitives per pixel, which combined with the culling strategy drastically reduces rendering overhead.

Abstract: Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution (ASR) reconstructs images at continuous magnification factors. Recent methods accelerate inference by replacing computationally heavy implicit neural decoders with explicit 2D Gaussian Splatting (GS). However, since standard Gaussians are smooth low-pass primitives, modeling edges and fine textures requires multiple overlapping, well-aligned splats, which creates severe bottlenecks during rasterization. To address this, we introduce Resonant Brane Splatting (RBS), a feed-forward ASR framework. RBS replaces flat Gaussians with Branes: expressive primitives that emit spatially varying colors to natively model local contrast and complex textures within a single footprint. We achieve this by augmenting the standard Gaussian envelope with internal Gaussian-Hermite modes, assigning a distinct color coefficient to each. The zero-order mode recovers standard GS, while higher-order modes capture high frequencies. We predict Brane parameters directly from low-resolution features. Because Branes provide a mathematically richer formulation than simple Gaussians, far fewer primitives need to overlap to reconstruct a given target pixel. To exploit this, we introduce an efficient fully differentiable rasterizer with a precise culling strategy based on the classical quantum turning point. This allows us to safely skip negligible regions, drastically reducing the rendering overhead. Experiments on standard ASR benchmarks show that RBS improves reconstruction quality over implicit and GS baselines, while achieving superior speed-quality trade-off than prior GS methods.

</details>


### [25] [CLOSER-VLN: Closed-Loop Self-Verified Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning for Aerial Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28397)
*Shaoxuan Li,Xiangyu Dong,Xiaoguang Ma,Junfeng Chen,Haoran Zhao,Yaoming Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLOSER is a training-policy-free method for aerial VLN that performs closed-loop reasoning—action generation, reliability verification, targeted retrieval, and correction—before executing actions, achieving 32.01% SR and 21.28% SPL on CityNav benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLN methods using large models adopt open-loop decision-execution, where candidate actions are rarely verified or corrected before execution. This causes critical issues in aerial VLN, where minor errors in intermediate actions accumulate into large trajectory deviations and target loss.

Method: CLOSER performs closed-loop self-verified retrieval-augmented reasoning with three components: (1) hierarchical reasoner for generating candidate actions, (2) multidimensional action verifier for assessing action reliability, and (3) verification-triggered multimodal retriever that retrieves targeted exemplars from a memory bank only when verification fails.

Result: On CityNav benchmark, CLOSER-VLN achieves 32.01% SR and 21.28% SPL on the test-unseen split.

Conclusion: The closed-loop reasoning approach effectively addresses error accumulation in aerial VLN, confirming that verification and correction before execution improves navigation performance without requiring task-specific policy training.

Abstract: Vision-language navigation (VLN) has recently advanced with large language and multimodal models, enabling agents to follow natural-language instructions in unseen environments without training a task-specific navigation policy. However, most existing VLN methods relying on large models still adopt an open-loop decision-execution approach, where candidate actions are generated from instructions and observations but are rarely verified or corrected before execution. This causes critical issues in aerial VLN, where minor errors in intermediate actions may quickly accumulate into large trajectory deviations and lead to target loss. To address this issue, we propose Closed-loop Self-verified Retrieval-augmented Reasoning (CLOSER), a training-policy-free method that sequentially performs action reasoning, reliability verification, targeted retrieval, and action correction in a closed-loop manner before executing concrete actions. We instantiate the CLOSER in aerial VLN tasks and develop a CLOSER-VLN framework, which is composed of three components: a hierarchical reasoner for generating candidate actions based on available information, a multidimensional action verifier for assessing the reliability of actions generated by the reasoner, and a verification-triggered multimodal retriever for retrieving targeted exemplars from a memory bank only when verification fails. We conduct experimental evaluations on the CityNav benchmark, where CLOSER-VLN achieves 32.01% SR and 21.28% SPL on the test-unseen split, confirming the effectiveness of closed-loop reasoning.

</details>


### [26] [Scenes as Objects, Not Primitives: Instance-Structured 3D Tokenization from Unposed Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29513)
*Mijin Yoo,In Cho,Subin Jeon,Jiwoo Lee,Eunbyung Park,Seon Joo Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A feed-forward framework decomposes 3D scenes into instance-structured token groups (instance token + anchor tokens) from unposed multi-view images, enabling reconstruction, segmentation, and manipulation natively without needing 3D annotations or post-hoc grouping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feed-forward reconstruction methods output unstructured points/Gaussians, requiring post-processing to recover object-level structure. The goal is to make object instances a native interface of the representation.

Method: Proposes a two-level factorization: instance tokens (entity-level identity) paired with anchor tokens (local geometry/appearance), decoded into 3D Gaussians. Learned via differentiable rendering with joint reconstruction and segmentation supervision, no 3D annotations needed.

Result: Surpasses per-scene optimization baselines in class-agnostic instance segmentation, competitive in novel view synthesis. Enables direct instance-level editing (remove/translate/insert) and efficient open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval (scaling with instance count, not primitive count).

Conclusion: Instance-structured token groups provide a native object-level representation for 3D scenes, unifying reconstruction, segmentation, and manipulation in a feed-forward manner.

Abstract: A 3D scene is understood through its objects, not the primitives that compose them. Yet feed-forward reconstruction methods output dense, unstructured sets of points or Gaussians, leaving object-level structure to be recovered after the fact. We propose a feed-forward framework that decomposes a scene into instance-structured 3D token groups directly from unposed multi-view images -- compact object-centric units from which reconstruction, segmentation, and manipulation all follow. Each token group pairs an instance token capturing entity-level identity with anchor tokens that encode local geometry and appearance, which are decoded into a set of 3D Gaussians. This two-level factorization decouples object identity from local appearance, making object instances a native interface of the representation rather than a derived product.
