The token groups are learned through differentiable rendering with joint reconstruction and segmentation supervision, requiring no 3D annotations. Our feed-forward model surpasses per-scene optimization baselines in class-agnostic instance segmentation while remaining competitive in novel view synthesis. Beyond these metrics, the same token groups directly unlock instance-level scene editing -- removing, translating, or inserting objects by operating on their groups -- as well as efficient open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval, where retrieval complexity scales with the number of instances rather than primitives.

</details>


### [27] [Semantic-Aware Generative Image Transmission for Resource-Constrained Visual IoT Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28398)
*Chenyang Zhang,Changwang Liu,Jinqi Zhu,Jiayi Chang,Yuxuan Wang,Shuqing He,Jia Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a semantic-aware generative image transmission framework for resource-constrained visual IoT systems, where a VQ encoder discretizes images into tokens, and a spatial dispersal sampler selects semantically important tokens for transmission based on prediction entropy, local complexity, and instance segmentation. The receiver uses MaskGIT with Halton scheduling to recover masked tokens. At 0.074 bpp, it achieves 29.9 dB PSNR using only 44.6% of the bits of a 0.167-bpp DeepJSCC/WITT baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image communication methods for visual IoT compress or transmit complete global representations, leaving limited room to exploit receiver-side generative restoration. The goal is to enable efficient transmission of task-relevant visual evidence over low-rate wireless links.

Method: A VQ encoder encodes the image into discrete tokens. Token recoverability is estimated from prediction entropy and local structure complexity, fused with semantic importance from instance segmentation and category-aware scoring. A spatial dispersal sampler selects tokens under a bitrate budget. Only quantization indices of kept tokens and a binary mask are sent. The receiver recovers masked tokens via MaskGIT with Halton sequence scheduling.

Result: At 0.074 bpp, the method uses 44.6% of the transmitted bits of the 0.167-bpp DeepJSCC/WITT reference while achieving 29.9 dB PSNR. Semantic-aware masking preserves task-relevant objects better than random masking at both 30% and 50% mask ratios in a pseudo-GT downstream detection study on Kodak.

Conclusion: The proposed semantic-aware generative framework provides a flexible bitrate-quality tradeoff for narrowband visual IoT links, outperforming baselines in both reconstruction quality and downstream task preservation.

Abstract: Resource-constrained visual Internet of Things (IoT) systems, such as edge cameras, unmanned sensing platforms, industrial inspection nodes, and remote monitoring sensors, often need to transmit task-relevant visual evidence over low-rate wireless links to an edge/cloud service. Existing image communication methods usually compress or transmit complete global representations, leaving limited room to exploit receiver-side generative restoration. This paper proposes a semantic-aware generative image transmission framework for edge-assisted visual IoT. The image captured by an IoT visual sensor is encoded into a discrete token grid by a VQ encoder. At the IoT transmitter or nearby gateway, token recoverability, estimated from prediction entropy and local structure complexity, is fused with semantic importance obtained from instance segmentation and category-aware scoring. A spatial dispersal sampler then selects the tokens to be transmitted under a bitrate budget. The transmitter sends only the quantization indices of kept tokens and a binary mask map, while the edge/cloud receiver recovers masked tokens through MaskGIT with Halton sequence scheduling. Experiments on Kodak and VisDrone scenes under AWGN and Rayleigh channels show that the proposed method provides a flexible bitrate-quality tradeoff for narrowband visual IoT links. At 0.074 bpp, it uses 44.6% of the transmitted bits of the 0.167-bpp DeepJSCC/WITT reference while achieving 29.9 dB PSNR. A pseudo-GT downstream detection study on Kodak further shows that semantic-aware masking preserves task-relevant objects better than random masking at both 30% and 50% mask ratios.

</details>


### [28] [SICAGE: Speaker-Independent Culture-Aware Gesture Generation using TED4C-L Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30001)
*Ariel Gjaci,Antonio Sgorbissa,Vittorio Murino*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SICAGE is a modular framework for culture-aware co-speech gesture generation that uses speaker-independent cultural representations learned via domain generalization approaches (adversarial learning and Fishr regularization), combined with a real-time diffusion-based generator (ALaDiT), to produce culturally appropriate and realistic gestures, validated on a 106-hour dataset of 764 TED speakers from four cultural groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing co-speech gesture generation methods ignore cultural differences and are often evaluated without speaker-disjoint splits, confounding cultural behavior with speaker-specific styles.

Method: SICAGE learns speaker-independent cultural representations from audio and text by treating each speaker as a separate domain and imposing invariance via adversarial learning or Fishr regularization. These embeddings condition a multimodal generator (ALaDiT), a real-time diffusion-based gesture generator.

Result: SICAGE improves motion realism, diversity, beat synchronization, semantic relevance, and cultural consistency on the TED4C-L dataset (106 hours, 764 speakers, 4 cultural groups).

Conclusion: Speaker-independent cultural representations effectively decouple culture from speaker identity, enabling more transferable and culturally appropriate gesture generation in human-agent interaction.

Abstract: Recent co-speech gesture generation methods often overlook cultural differences, limiting their effectiveness in human-agent interaction. Moreover, culture-conditioned models are rarely evaluated under speaker-disjoint splits, so apparent "cultural" behavior may be confounded with speaker-specific gesturing style. We introduce SICAGE, a modular framework for culture-aware co-speech gesture generation that conditions motion synthesis models on speaker-independent cultural representations. SICAGE learns these representations from audio and text by treating each speaker as a separate domain while imposing invariance across speakers. This encourages representations to remain culture-discriminative while reducing dependence on speaker identity. The resulting cultural embeddings condition a multimodal generator to produce culturally appropriate gestures. We instantiate this idea with two domain generalization approaches: adversarial learning and Fishr regularization. We further introduce ALaDiT, a real-time diffusion-based gesture generator designed to efficiently incorporate the learned cultural embeddings. To validate our method, we built TED4C-L, a 106-hour multimodal dataset of 764 TED speakers from four cultural groups. Experiments show that SICAGE improves motion realism, diversity, beat synchronization, semantic relevance, and cultural consistency.

</details>


### [29] [Meta-learning as a principle for human-like visual representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28399)
*Can Demircan,Marcel Binz,Alireza Modirshanechi,Eric Schulz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Meta-learning (learning to learn) produces visual representations that better predict human similarity judgments, semantic rule learning, and high-level visual cortex compared to standard pretrained networks, suggesting human visual flexibility arises from pressure to learn new tasks from few examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretrained neural networks model human visual representations well but still have a large discrepancy. The authors propose this is because these networks optimize a single fixed objective, whereas human representations must support open-ended tasks.

Method: A sequence model was trained without human data supervision across thousands of semantically rich tasks mapping images to high-level concepts using meta-learning (learning to learn).

Result: Meta-learned representations better predict human similarity judgments, semantic rule learning, and high-level visual cortex compared to pretrained base encoders. Behavioral gains depend on disentangled, high-level task distributions; brain alignment is driven primarily by the learning-to-learn pressure.

Conclusion: The flexibility of human visual representations reflects the functional demand to learn new semantic relationships on the fly.

Abstract: The structure of human visual representations underpins our capacity for adaptive behaviour. While pretrained neural networks model human visual representations with unprecedented success, a large discrepancy remains. We propose one reason: these networks optimise a single fixed objective, whereas human representations must support open-ended tasks. We hypothesise this flexibility arises from meta-learning (learning to learn), a pressure shaping representations to acquire new tasks from few observations. To test this, we train a sequence model, without any supervision from human data, across thousands of semantically rich tasks mapping images to high-level concepts. Compared to their pretrained base encoders, meta-learned representations better predict human similarity judgements, semantic rule learning, and high-level visual cortex. Behavioural gains depend on disentangled, high-level task distributions, while brain alignment is driven primarily by the learning-to-learn pressure. Our results suggest the flexibility of human visual representations reflects the functional demand to learn new semantic relationships on the fly.

</details>


### [30] [Vision-driven Preference Synthesis for Mitigating Hallucinations in VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28401)
*Yunhun Nam,Jongheon Jeong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViPSy is a two-stage framework for constructing visually grounded and policy-aligned preference pairs to reduce hallucinations in VLMs, achieving state-of-the-art results on hallucination benchmarks (AMBER -35.7%, Object HalBench -24.5%) while improving general visual grounding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing preference alignment methods for mitigating VLM hallucinations suffer from two limitations: (a) intervention-based methods introduce significant deviation from the policy distribution, and (b) sampling-based methods underuse visual information during preference pair construction.

Method: ViPSy operates in two stages: (1) deriving a visual cue from recurring object-level content across semantically aligned image variants, so preference construction relies on visual information rather than language priors; (2) conditioning the policy's own rollouts on this cue, allowing candidates to be guided by visually grounded content while staying close to the policy's response distribution.

Result: The VLM aligned with ViPSy achieves new SOTA in hallucination mitigation: 35.7% reduction on AMBER, 24.5% reduction on Object HalBench. It also improves on MMStar, MMVP, CV-Bench, and yields gains in semantic segmentation and ImageNet linear probing.

Conclusion: ViPSy effectively enhances visual capabilities and reduces hallucinations by constructing preference pairs that are both policy-aligned and visually grounded.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong performance in visual understanding, yet they still suffer from hallucinations, generating content that is not grounded in the image. Preference alignment is a promising approach to improve visual faithfulness, but its success depends heavily on how preference pairs are constructed. Existing methods exhibit two key limitations; (a) intervention-based methods often introduce significant deviation from the policy distribution, and (b) sampling-based methods often underuse visual information during the construction. In this paper, we propose ViPSy (Vision-driven Preference Synthesis), a framework for constructing preference data that are both policy-aligned and visually grounded. Our framework consists of two stages; in the first stage, ViPSy derives a visual cue from recurring object-level content across semantically aligned image variants, so preference construction can rely on visual information rather than language priors. In the second stage, ViPSy conditions the policy's own rollouts on this cue, allowing candidates to be guided by visually grounded content while staying close to the policy's response distribution. The resulting candidates remain close to the policy's response distribution while better leveraging visual information from the image. Experiments show that the resulting VLM, preference-aligned with ViPSy-constructed preference pairs, achieves a new state-of-the-art in hallucination mitigation. Compared with the previous state-of-the-art method, it reduces hallucination rates on AMBER and Object HalBench by 35.7% and 24.5%, respectively. The resulting model further improves on general visual grounding benchmarks, e.g., MMStar, MMVP, and CV-Bench, while also yielding gains in semantic segmentation and ImageNet linear probing, underscoring the effectiveness of our framework in enhancing the model's visual capabilities.

</details>


### [31] [DCSNet: Multiscale Feature Aggregation for Small Medical Object Segmentation with Detection-guided Hierarchical Cropping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28402)
*Shanfeng Zhang,Bo Gou,Yue Cao,Lei Zhang,Zhang Yi,Tao He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DCSNet is an end-to-end framework for small object segmentation in medical imaging that transforms global dense prediction into localized refinement using Detection-guided Hierarchical Cropping (DGHC) and Multiscale Feature Aggregation (MSFA), achieving state-of-the-art performance across three medical datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small object segmentation in medical imaging suffers from class imbalance and boundary complexity, causing conventional global networks to fail on sparse targets or produce severe edge degradation.

Method: DCSNet integrates two components: (1) Detection-guided Hierarchical Cropping (DGHC) uses region proposals to dynamically extract object-centric features, filtering background interference to mitigate class imbalance; (2) Multiscale Feature Aggregation (MSFA) operates within purified regions, combining a Transformer encoder with pixel-adaptive fusion to aggregate multiscale features for semantic context and fine-grained boundary details.

Result: Extensive experiments across three diverse medical datasets show DCSNet significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, with substantial improvements in boundary precision and robust performance for clinical micro-lesion segmentation.

Conclusion: DCSNet provides a highly robust solution for clinical micro-lesion segmentation by effectively addressing class imbalance and boundary complexity through a localized refinement paradigm.

Abstract: Small object segmentation in medical imaging is primarily hindered by class imbalance and inherent boundary complexity. Consequently, conventional global networks frequently fail to detect sparse targets or suffer from severe edge degradation. To overcome these limitations, we propose the Detection-guided Cropping Segmentation Network (DCSNet), an end-to-end framework that transforms global dense prediction into a localized refinement process. This framework integrates two core components, namely Detection-guided Hierarchical Cropping (DGHC) and Multiscale Feature Aggregation (MSFA). The DGHC module leverages region proposals to dynamically extract object-centric features, effdataectively filtering out massive background interference to mitigate class imbalance. Subsequently, the MSFA module operates strictly within these purified regions, synergizing a Transformer encoder with a pixel-adaptive fusion strategy. This mechanism dynamically aggregates multiscale features to capture both semantic context and fine-grained details for sharp boundary delineation. Extensive experiments across three diverse medical datasets demonstrate that DCSNet significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, yielding substantial improvements in boundary precision and offering a highly robust solution for clinical micro-lesion segmentation.

</details>


### [32] [Enhancing Layer Interaction Using Key-Correlated Layer Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28405)
*Jianlong Xiong,ChuanBo Xie,Le Yu,Quansong He,Tao He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KCLA achieves linear computational complexity in layer attention by leveraging the high cosine similarity of Key representations, maintaining dynamic updates and long-range cross-layer connections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Layer attention suffers from quadratic complexity in network depth, and prior solutions like RLA and linear attention have static updates and limited long-range dependency modeling.

Method: Key-Correlated Layer Attention (KCLA) exploits the observation that Key representations in layer attention have high cosine similarity to derive linear complexity while preserving dynamic information updates directly from the foundational definition of layer attention.

Result: Good performance across image recognition, object detection, and medical image segmentation tasks with linear computational complexity and fixed spatial complexity independent of depth.

Conclusion: KCLA efficiently addresses the quadratic complexity bottleneck of layer attention while maintaining dynamic updates and long-range cross-layer connections, validated across diverse vision tasks.

Abstract: Recent advances in network architecture design have introduced layer attention to enhance inter-layer interactions. In such frameworks, each layer queries all preceding layers to establish cross-layer connections. However, layer attention results in quadratic computational complexity with respect to network depth. To mitigate this issue, prior works have proposed Recurrent Layer Attention (RLA) and linear attention mechanisms, which suffer from static information updates and limited long-range cross-layer dependency modeling. To overcome these limitations, we propose Key-Correlated Layer Attention (KCLA), inspired by our observation that Key representations in layer attention exhibit high cosine similarity. KCLA achieves linear computational complexity while preserving dynamic information updates, directly derived from the foundational definition of layer attention. Furthermore, KCLA maintains long-range cross-layer connections and features a fixed spatial complexity, independent of network depth. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that KCLA delivers good performance across diverse tasks, including image recognition, object detection, and medical image segmentation. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/bgx666/KCLA.

</details>


### [33] [RSGPNet: Geometric Prompting for Remote Sensing Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28410)
*Shanwen Wang,Xin Sun,Sirui Wang,Xiao Xiang Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RSGPNet is a training-free geometric prompting framework for open-vocabulary semantic segmentation in remote sensing that uses Text-guided Coarse Mask, Geometric Re-prompting, and Consistency Verification modules to refine segmentation, outperforming SOTA methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OVSS methods modify CLIP attention but suffer from unstable local segmentation in the remote sensing domain, motivating the need for a domain-specific solution that improves boundary accuracy without training.

Method: RSGPNet has three modules: (1) TCM generates coarse masks from text prompts and input images; (2) GRP converts coarse masks to geometric box prompts for re-segmentation; (3) CVM applies consistency verification to prevent error reinforcement during prompting.

Result: RSGPNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on remote sensing datasets across both quantitative and qualitative metrics, with excellent interpretability.

Conclusion: The training-free geometric prompting approach effectively addresses OVSS challenges in remote sensing by leveraging geometric priors and consistency constraints, enabling more accurate segmentation at category boundaries.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) enables text-guided segmentation of unseen objects, breaking fixed-class limitations to achieve open-world understanding. However, existing OVSS methods primarily focus on modifying the CLIP attention mechanism, which still suffers from unstable local segmentation for remote sensing (RS) domain. To address these limitations, we propose RSGPNet, a training-free geometric prompting framework for RS OVSS that refines segmentation by leveraging object geometric areas and consistency constraints. Specifically, RSGPNet comprises three core modules: a Text-guided Coarse Mask module (TCM), a Geometric Re-prompting Module (GRP), and a Coarse-to-fine Consistency Verification Mechanism (CVM). TCM utilizes text prompts and the input image to construct initial coarse segmentation masks. GRP then converts these coarse masks into geometric box prompts, feeding them back into the segmentation model to generate refined masks. Finally, CVM employs consistency computation to prevent prompting from reinforcing erroneous regions. They allow the model to improve segmentation accuracy in complex areas, such as category boundaries. Extensive experiments on RS datasets demonstrate that RSGPNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods across both quantitative and qualitative metrics while exhibiting excellent interpretability. The code is released at \href{https://github.com/wangshanwen001/RSGPNet}{https://github.com/wangshanwen001/RSGPNet}.

</details>


### [34] [AEGIS: A Semantic GAN and Evidential Learning Frameworkfor Robust Adversarial Detection in Vision Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28416)
*Maher Boughdiri,Mounira Msahli,Albert Bifet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AEGIS is a semantic-aware and uncertainty-guided adversarial detection framework for vision sensors that uses a SemantiGAN discriminator, stochastic augmentation with instability metrics, and Evidential Deep Learning to detect six types of adversarial attacks, achieving 92.1% AUROC, 90.2% AUPRC, and 90.7% accuracy on Tiny ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks in vision sensor networks are vulnerable to adversarial manipulations and imperceptible perturbations that cause erroneous predictions, motivating the need for robust detection mechanisms.

Method: AEGIS consists of three stages: (1) SemantiGAN module acts as a multi-class semantic discriminator to filter visually inconsistent adversarial inputs; (2) stochastic augmentation generates test-time variations from which handcrafted instability metrics (FlipScore, Prediction Inconsistency, Layerwise Cosine Similarity for early/mid layers, Entropy) are computed and aggregated into a 5D vector; (3) an Evidential Deep Learning classifier models output evidence via Dirichlet distribution for predictions and calibrated uncertainty estimates.

Result: Evaluated on Tiny ImageNet across six attack types (clean, FGSM, PGD, patch-based, functional, geometric), AEGIS achieves 92.1% AUROC, 90.2% AUPRC, and 90.7% accuracy, outperforming conventional softmax-based detectors.

Conclusion: AEGIS demonstrates superior detection performance, robustness, interpretability, and uncertainty calibration compared to traditional softmax-based adversarial detectors.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have shown outstanding performance in visual recognition tasks within vision sensor networks; however, they are still vulnerable to adversarial manipulations and imperceptible perturbations that can lead to erroneous predictions. To address that, this paper presents AEGIS, a semantic aware and uncertainty guided adversarial detection framework designed for robust image classification in vision sensors pipelines. At its core, a SemantiGAN module functions as a multi class semantic discriminator, identifying and filtering visually inconsistent adversarial inputs before they propagate further in the pipeline. For inputs that pass this stage, a stochastic augmentation process generates test time variations, from which handcrafted instability metrics FlipScore, Prediction Inconsistency, Layerwise Cosine Similarity (early and mid layers), and Entropy are computed. These features are aggregated into a compact five dimensional vector and processed by an Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) classifier, which models output evidence using a Dirichlet distribution to yield both class predictions and calibrated uncertainty estimates. Evaluations on the Tiny ImageNet dataset across six categories clean, FGSM, PGD, patch based, functional, and geometric attacks demonstrate the effectiveness of AEGIS. The proposed framework achieves an AUROC of 92.1\%, an AUPRC of 90.2\%, and an accuracy of 90.7\%, outperforming conventional softmax-based detectors in terms of detection performance, robustness, interpretability, and uncertainty calibration.

</details>


### [35] [DiffRGD: An Inference-Time Diffusion Guidance Through Riemannian Gradient Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28417)
*Jia-Wei Liao,Li-Xuan Peng,Mei-Heng Yueh,Min Sun,Cheng-Fu Chou,Jun-Cheng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffRGD is a plug-and-play guidance framework for diffusion models that preserves the latent Gaussian distribution during inference-time steering by formulating each sampling step as a constrained optimization on a spherical manifold and solving it via Riemannian Gradient Descent (RGD), achieving superior performance on image restoration and conditional generation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time guidance methods for diffusion models introduce distributional drift that degrades sample quality because they cannot effectively preserve the original Gaussian distribution of the latent variables during steering.

Method: DiffRGD formulates each sampling step as a constrained optimization problem on a spherical manifold induced by the latent Gaussian distribution, solved efficiently via Riemannian Gradient Descent (RGD), explicitly preserving the Gaussian structure throughout inference-time guidance.

Result: Extensive experiments show DiffRGD outperforms previous methods in most image restoration and conditional generation tasks, while being plug-and-play compatible with any pre-trained diffusion model.

Conclusion: Distribution-aware guidance via Riemannian optimization effectively preserves latent Gaussian structure and improves sample quality over prior inference-time steering methods.

Abstract: Recently, diffusion models have been widely adopted in generative modeling and have served as foundational models for many image generation tasks. To control the generation without costly re-training or fine-tuning, many works seek inference-time guidance methods to steer the latent via a differentiable objective at inference time. However, these methods cannot effectively preserve the original Gaussian distribution because they introduce distributional drift, thereby degrading the sample quality. To address this gap, we propose DiffRGD, a distribution-aware guidance framework that explicitly preserves the latent Gaussian structure. DiffRGD formulates each sampling step as a constrained optimization problem on a spherical manifold induced by the latent Gaussian distribution, and solves it efficiently via Riemannian Gradient Descent (RGD). DiffRGD is a plug-and-play method that can be seamlessly integrated into any pre-trained diffusion model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiffRGD outperforms previous methods in most image restoration and conditional generation tasks. Our codebase is available at https://github.com/jwliao1209/DiffRGD.

</details>


### [36] [MedDiffuseMix: Preserving Diagnostic Evidence with Saliency-Aware Diffusion Medical Image Data Augmentatio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28419)
*Teerath Kumar,Raja Vavekanand,Muhammad Turab*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedDiffuseMix is a saliency-guided diffusion mixing framework for medical image augmentation that selectively applies diffusion-based mixing to low-saliency background regions while preserving diagnostically important areas, improving classification performance across multiple medical imaging benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited data, class imbalance, and domain variability hinder medical image classification. Conventional augmentation may distort diagnostic structures, while generative augmentation can introduce label-inconsistent content.

Method: Uses classifier-derived saliency maps to separate high-saliency diagnostic regions from low-saliency background, then applies diffusion-guided mixing mainly to low-saliency regions with adaptive mixing, Gaussian boundary blending, and a saliency-preservation constraint to reduce semantic distortion.

Result: Outperforms standard augmentation, Mixup, GenMix, SaliencyMix, and diffusion-based baselines on pneumonia chest radiography, musculoskeletal radiographs, PatchCamelyon, and breast cancer histopathology datasets; improves accuracy, F1-score, and AUC for both CNN and transformer classifiers.

Conclusion: Saliency-guided diffusion mixing is an effective augmentation strategy for limited-data medical image classification, as it better preserves diagnostically salient regions while improving model generalization.

Abstract: Limited data availability, class imbalance, and domain variability remain major barriers to reliable medical image classification. Conventional augmentation can improve training diversity but may distort diagnostically informative structures, whereas unconstrained generative augmentation may introduce label-inconsistent content. This paper proposes MedDiffuseMix, a saliency-guided diffusion mixing framework for controlled medical image augmentation. The method uses classifier-derived saliency maps to separate high-saliency diagnostic regions from low-saliency background areas and applies diffusion-guided mixing mainly to regions with lower diagnostic importance. Adaptive mixing, Gaussian boundary blending, and a saliency-preservation constraint reduce semantic distortion and reject or attenuate samples that shift model attention away from clinically relevant evidence. The framework is evaluated on four public benchmarks: the Radiological Society of North America pneumonia chest radiography dataset, Musculoskeletal Radiographs, PatchCamelyon, and the Breast Cancer Histopathological Image Classification dataset. Experiments with convolutional and transformer-based classifiers show that MedDiffuseMix improves accuracy, F1-score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve compared with standard augmentation, Mixup, GenMix, SaliencyMix, and diffusion-based augmentation baselines. Ablation studies confirm the importance of saliency guidance, adaptive region mixing, and smooth boundary blending. Visual attribution analysis further indicates that MedDiffuseMix better preserves diagnostically salient regions. These results suggest that saliency-guided diffusion mixing is an effective augmentation strategy for limited-data medical image classification.

</details>


### [37] [JuZhou 1.0 Technical Report: The First Edge-Native Text-to-Image Foundation Model Trained Entirely on China-Developed AI Accelerators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28421)
*Ce Chen,Congrui Wang,Yonglin Li,Zhenchen Wan,Mingyang Geng,Junhao Xiao,Zhengpeng Xing,Yaqing Hu,Yao Wu,Zhaoyang Qu,Long Lan,Xinwang Liu,Yingqi Peng,Shijia Li,Zufeng Zhang,Chen Ma,Jingjing Zhou,Xingyu Wang,Qilin Lu,Bin Jiang,Qilin Sun,Shanzhi Gu,Yaoguang Jin,Tongliang Liu,Kede Ma,Yifan Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JuZhou 1.0 is an ultra-lightweight T2I diffusion model (0.387B params) designed for fully offline on-device execution, using Rectified Flow + DMD2 distillation (4 steps), Chinese semantic alignment, and training on domestic Sugon K100 accelerators, achieving GenEval 0.69 (outperforming SDXL, SD3-Medium, IF-XL) and ~1.6s U-Net denoising on Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of deploying text-to-image diffusion models on edge devices—high latency, cloud cost, and user privacy concerns—by designing a model that runs fully offline on smartphones without cloud or NVIDIA GPU dependency.

Method: Four key designs: (1) compact backbone with 0.385B-param U-Net + 1.90M-param distilled decoder (total ~0.387B); (2) Rectified Flow training + DMD2 distillation reducing inference to 4 steps; (3) Chinese semantic alignment trained on 9M curated image-text pairs for direct Chinese prompting; (4) training/distillation pipeline on domestic Sugon K100 AI accelerators (no NVIDIA GPUs).

Result: 28-step base model achieves GenEval 0.69, outperforming SDXL (0.55), SD3-Medium (0.62), IF-XL (0.61). On Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5: 4-step U-Net denoising in ~1.6s; full Android poetry-to-image pipeline in 4.5s on Xiaomi 17 Pro Max.

Conclusion: JuZhou 1.0 demonstrates a practical, efficient approach to mobile T2I generation, providing a reference for Chinese-native generation, domestic-compute training, and fully offline on-device deployment after one-time installation.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models typically require substantial computational resources and cloud infrastructure, posing significant challenges for edge deployment in terms of latency, cost, and user privacy. We present JuZhou 1.0, an ultra-lightweight T2I foundation model designed for fully offline, on-device execution. JuZhou 1.0 achieves its efficiency through four key designs: (1) a compact image-generation backbone consisting of a 0.385B-parameter denoising U-Net and a 1.90M-parameter distilled decoder, totaling approximately 0.387B parameters; (2) Rectified Flow training combined with DMD2 distillation, reducing inference to 4 sampling steps; (3) Chinese semantic alignment trained on 9M curated image-text pairs, enabling direct Chinese prompting without external translation at inference time; and (4) a training and distillation pipeline completed on domestically developed Sugon K100 AI accelerators without relying on NVIDIA GPUs for training or distillation. Despite its compact scale, the 28-step base model of JuZhou 1.0 achieves an overall GenEval score of 0.69, outperforming published baselines including SDXL (2.6B, 0.55), SD3-Medium (2B, 0.62), and IF-XL (4.3B, 0.61). We further validate the full poetry-to-image pipeline on Android and the core CLIP-U-Net-VAE generation branch on iOS. On a smartphone powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform, the 4-step U-Net denoising branch runs in approximately 1.6 seconds, while the full Android poetry-to-image pipeline takes 4.5 seconds with on-device prompt refinement on Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. These results position JuZhou 1.0 as a practical approach to mobile text-to-image generation and provide a concrete reference for Chinese-native generation, domestic-compute training, and fully offline on-device deployment after one-time installation.

</details>


### [38] [CLEAR-MoE: Shared-Basis Expert Extraction from Frozen Vision Transformers via Calibration-Driven Layer Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28516)
*Md Irtiza Hossain,Humaira Ayesha,Junaid Ahmed Sifat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLEAR-MoE converts frozen pretrained ViTs into sparse MoE models via a four-phase post-training pipeline (scoring, SVD+clustering decomposition, supervised router training, CUDA dispatch), retaining ~99.9% accuracy (86.70% vs 86.73% on Imagenette with DeiT-Small) while being 1.3-1.7x slower due to routing/scatter-gather overhead. Surprisingly, the shared SVD basis is the key accuracy driver—routing method and architecture have negligible impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To convert frozen pretrained Vision Transformers into sparse Mixture-of-Experts models without updating backbone weights, enabling conditional computation while preserving learned representations.

Method: Four-phase post-training pipeline: (i) score FFN layers by sparsity, clusterability, and output sensitivity; (ii) decompose selected layers into shared low-rank SVD basis + per-cluster residual experts via k-means; (iii) train lightweight routers with supervised cluster labels; (iv) dispatch tokens through pluggable CUDA backends.

Result: CLEAR-MoE retains 99.9% dense accuracy (86.70% vs 86.73% on Imagenette with DeiT-Small). Shared SVD basis is the primary accuracy driver; routing method/architecture has negligible effect (≤0.06 pp variation). Generalizes across five ViT backbones (5.7M-86.6M params, ≤0.10 pp accuracy difference). FFN is 1.3-1.7x slower on GTX 960; routing is an order of magnitude more memory-bound than expert computation.

Conclusion: CLEAR-MoE effectively converts frozen ViTs to sparse MoEs with negligible accuracy loss, where the shared SVD basis—not the routing mechanism—is responsible for preservation. Fused dispatch kernels are identified as a promising optimization direction to address the memory-bound routing overhead.

Abstract: We present CLEAR-MoE, a four-phase post-training pipeline that converts a frozen pretrained Vision Transformer (ViT) into a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model without updating backbone weights. The pipeline (i) scores feed-forward network (FFN) layers by sparsity, clusterability, and output sensitivity; (ii) decomposes selected layers into a shared low-rank SVD basis and per-cluster residual experts using k-means clustering; (iii) trains lightweight routers supervised by cluster labels; and (iv) dispatches tokens through pluggable CUDA backends. On Imagenette with DeiT-Small, CLEAR-MoE retains 99.9% of the dense model's accuracy (86.70 +/- 0.02% versus 86.73%). Extensive ablation studies reveal a consistent empirical finding: the shared SVD basis is the primary factor responsible for preserving accuracy. Random routing, learned routing, and three different router architectures produce nearly identical performance, with accuracy varying by at most 0.06 percentage points (86.62%-86.68%). Accuracy also remains stable across different SVD ranks, expert counts (2-8), calibration set sizes (50-500), and random seeds. This behavior generalizes across five ViT backbones (DeiT-Tiny, DeiT-Small, DeiT-Base, ViT-Small, and ViT-Base), covering models from 5.7M to 86.6M parameters, with accuracy differences <= 0.10 percentage points from their dense counterparts. On a GTX 960 GPU, routing and scatter-gather overhead make the CLEAR-MoE FFN 1.3-1.7x slower than the dense implementation. A dispatch microbenchmark further shows that routing is an order of magnitude more memory-bound than expert matrix multiplications, identifying fused dispatch kernels as a promising direction for future optimization.

</details>


### [39] [Detecting Clinical Hallucinations in LVLMs via Counterfactual Visual Grounding Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28520)
*Xiao Song,Haonan Qin,Zhaoxu Zhang,Jiong Zhang,Yuqi Fang,Caifeng Shan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A vision-traceable hallucination detection framework for LVLMs that uses counterfactual entity perturbation and visual grounding to detect hallucinations in clinical image understanding without requiring model internal access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs used in clinical image understanding are prone to hallucinations (generating text not supported by images), and existing detection methods lack robust, interpretable, and model-agnostic solutions.

Method: Extracts visually verifiable entities from LVLM responses, uses a medical-domain-adapted Qwen-VL grounding verifier to localize entities, applies counterfactual entity perturbation to estimate visual evidence uncertainty, and computes entity-level uncertainty scores from positive/counterfactual confidence and grounding overlap for binary hallucination detection.

Result: The method consistently outperforms recent baselines in hallucination detection across multiple medical imaging modalities and LVLM backbones, with interpretable localization evidence and strong cross-model transferability.

Conclusion: Counterfactual-based visual grounding provides an effective, model-agnostic approach to detect and localize hallucinations in clinical LVLM responses, improving reliability for medical image understanding.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) are increasingly used for clinical image understanding, yet they remain vulnerable to \emph{hallucinations}--producing textual findings or attributes not supported by the image. We present a vision-traceable hallucination detection framework that audits arbitrary LVLM responses via visual evidence grounding, requiring neither modification nor internal access to the hidden states of LVLMs. Given an LVLM response, we extract visually verifiable entities and use a medical-domain-adapted Qwen-VL grounding verifier to localize each entity on the input image. To enhance the robustness of our detection method, we introduce a counterfactual entity perturbation method and estimate visual evidence uncertainty by contrasting factual and counterfactual grounding results. Specifically, we compute an entity-level uncertainty score from the positive confidence, counterfactual confidence, and their grounding overlap for binary hallucination decision-making. Experiments on multiple medical imaging modalities and LVLM backbones demonstrate that our method consistently improves hallucination detection performance over recent baselines, while providing interpretable localization evidence and strong cross-model transferability. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Agentic-CliniAI/CounterVHD.

</details>


### [40] [MammoFlow: Multiview Mammogram Synthesis with Anatomically Consistent Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28537)
*Yuexi Du,Leya Barrientos,Laura Sheiman,John Lewin,Hemant D. Tagare,Nicha C. Dvornek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MammoFlow synthesizes multiview mammogram pairs (CC and MLO) with geometric consistency using a flow matching model, an alignment module for anatomical correspondence, and an Earth Mover's Distance-based self-consistency loss, improving downstream classification AUC by 5%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Acquiring high-quality, balanced multiview mammography datasets is challenging for deep learning; existing methods lack explicit enforcement of 3D geometric consistency between paired CC and MLO views.

Method: An alignment module searches a 2D affine transformation subspace for optimal anatomical correspondence between views; a pixel-space self-consistency loss using Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) enforces consistent 1D AP-axis tissue distributions; integrated into a pretrained flow matching model (MammoFlow).

Result: Superior image quality, passes expert radiologist evaluation, and generates physically consistent pairs that improve downstream classification AUC by 5%.

Conclusion: First work to guide multiview mammogram generation using implicit geometric tissue correspondence; MammoFlow produces physically plausible, clinically useful synthetic mammogram pairs.

Abstract: Multiview mammography relies on paired craniocaudal (CC) and mediolateral oblique (MLO) views to provide complementary projections of a 3D breast volume, enabling precise anomaly localization. However, acquiring high-quality, balanced datasets remains challenging for deep learning applications. We propose a novel method to synthesize multiview mammograms by leveraging the inherent geometric relationship between CC and MLO views. To enforce an implicit 3D consistency prior during generation, we develop an alignment module that searches a 2D affine transformation subspace to establish optimal anatomical correspondence. Leveraging this alignment, we introduce a pixel-space self-consistency loss based on the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between the 1D anteroposterior (AP) axis tissue distributions of the generated images. Integrated into a pretrained flow matching model, MammoFlow forces synthesized pairs to share physically plausible tissue distributions from the chest wall to the nipple. To our knowledge, this is the first work to guide multiview mammogram generation using implicit geometric tissue correspondence. Our method demonstrates superior image quality, passes expert radiologist evaluation, and generates physically consistent pairs that improve downstream classification AUC by 5%. Code is available at https://github.com/XYPB/MammoFlow

</details>


### [41] [DataComp-VLM: Improved Open Datasets for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28551)
*Matteo Farina,Vishaal Udandarao,Thao Nguyen,Selim Kuzucu,Maximilian Böther,Andreas Hochlehnert,Adhiraj Ghosh,Marianna Nezhurina,Karsten Roth,Joschka Struber,Yuhui Zhang,Sebastian Dziadzio,Elaine Sui,Soumya Jahagirdar,Dhruba Ghosh,Hasan Hammoud,Thomas De Min,Simone Caldarella,Jehanzeb Mirza,Sedrick Keh,Mehdi Cherti,Hilde Kuehne,Bernt Schiele,Serena Yeung-Levy,Muhammad Ferjad Naeem,Federico Tombari,Ana Klimovic,Elisa Ricci,Matthias Bethge,Sewoong Oh,Ameya Prabhu,Alessio Tonioni,Jenia Jitsev,Massimiliano Mancini,Ludwig Schmidt,Nikhil Parthasarathy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DCVLM is a benchmark for data-centric experiments in Vision-Language Model training, featuring 160 datasets (6T tokens), enabling evaluation of curation strategies across model scales (1B-8B) and token budgets (6.25B-200B). Key finding: data mixing (especially instruction-heavy) outperforms filtering; DCVLM-Baseline achieves 63.6% accuracy on 33 tasks, +5.4pp over FineVision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The community lacks systematic benchmarks for evaluating data curation strategies (filtering, mixing, formatting, sampling) for Vision-Language Model training.

Method: Created a benchmark with 160 datasets (image-caption pairs, multimodal interleaved documents, text-only, instruction-tuning data) totaling 6T tokens; tested curation strategies across 1B-8B models and 6.25B-200B token budgets; evaluated on up to 52 downstream benchmarks across 9 domains.

Result: Data mixing (particularly instruction-heavy mixtures) is more important than filtering; DCVLM-Baseline achieves 63.6% accuracy on 33-task core suite with 200B tokens, outperforming FineVision by +5.4pp.

Conclusion: Instruction-heavy data mixtures scale better than caption-heavy ones for VLM training, and data mixing is the key curation strategy. DCVLM and artifacts will be publicly released.

Abstract: Building performant Vision-Language Models (VLMs) requires carefully curating large-scale training datasets, yet the community lacks systematic benchmarks for evaluating such curation strategies. We introduce DataComp for VLMs (DCVLM), a benchmark for controlled data-centric experiments to improve VLM training. As part of DCVLM, we collect 160 datasets spanning four data types -- image-caption pairs, multimodal interleaved documents, text-only, and instruction-tuning data -- into a corpus of 6T multimodal tokens. DCVLM allows participants to test curation strategies (filtering, mixing, formatting, sampling) across 1B-8B models and 6.25B-200B token budgets. Models are then evaluated on a carefully selected suite of up to 52 downstream benchmarks across 9 domains. We conduct extensive experiments on DCVLM and find that data mixing, not filtering, is key to a high-quality training dataset: instruction-heavy mixtures scale better than caption-heavy ones, with gains widening at larger scales. The resulting dataset, DCVLM-Baseline, enables training an 8B VLM to 63.6% accuracy on our 33-task core suite with 200B training tokens. Compared to FineVision, the state-of-the-art open VLM training dataset, this represents an improvement of +5.4pp. DCVLM and all accompanying artifacts will be made publicly available at https://www.datacomp.ai/dcvlm/.

</details>


### [42] [Digitizing Coaching Intelligence: An Agentic Framework for Holistic Athlete Profiling using VLM and RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28570)
*Deep Ghosal,Ishani Sen,Wazib Ansar,Amlan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an LLM-based hybrid agentic framework for automated athlete assessment that combines computer vision (MediaPipe) for kinematic tracking with Vision-Language Models (Llama-4-scout) for semantic reasoning, orchestrated via LangGraph. To address latency and token constraints, it introduces a 3×3 "Smart Grid" temporal chunking strategy (88% computational reduction), an "LLM-as-a-Judge" self-correction loop to mitigate hallucination, and a dual-persistence RAG pipeline with ChromaDB for natural language querying of athlete profiles. The system aligns with SAI protocols and bridges biometric tracking with actionable coaching insights for scalable talent identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional athlete assessment methods—manual observation and basic CV systems—are subjective, unscalable, and fail to evaluate qualitative physiological markers like form degradation, spinal articulation, and fatigue, lacking the "coaching intelligence" needed for holistic profiling and mass talent identification.

Method: The framework uses a dual-pipeline architecture: (1) MediaPipe for geometric-precision kinematic tracking, and (2) Llama-4-scout for semantic reasoning via Vision-Language Models. Orchestration is handled by LangGraph. A 3×3 "Smart Grid" temporal chunking strategy reduces computational overhead by 88%. An "LLM-as-a-Judge" autonomous self-correction loop cross-references quantitative and qualitative metrics to ensure data integrity. A dual-persistence RAG pipeline with ChromaDB enables natural language semantic queries.

Result: The proposed multi-agent approach significantly bridges the gap between raw biometric tracking and actionable coaching insights, offering a scalable, objective solution for national talent identification. The 3×3 Smart Grid chunking achieves over 88% reduction in computational overhead while preserving temporal continuity.

Conclusion: The LLM-based hybrid agentic framework provides a scalable, objective solution for holistic athlete profiling aligned with SAI protocols, overcoming limitations of manual observation and basic CV systems through integration of geometric tracking, semantic reasoning, temporal chunking, self-correction, and natural language querying.

Abstract: Athlete assessment is a critical process for tracking physical progress and identifying elite talent. However, during mass recruitment drives, traditional methods rely on manual observation, which is inherently subjective and unscalable, or basic computer vision (CV) systems limited to quantitative repetition counting. These standard approaches lack the "coaching intelligence" required to evaluate qualitative physiological markers such as form degradation, spinal articulation, and fatigue. This paper presents a novel, LLM-based hybrid agentic framework for automated, holistic athlete profiling that strictly aligns with the Sports Authority of India (SAI) assessment protocols. Orchestrated via LangGraph, our dual-pipeline architecture synthesizes the geometric precision of CV (MediaPipe) for kinematic tracking with the semantic reasoning of Vision-Language Models (Llama-4-scout). To overcome the latency and token constraints associated with multimodal video processing, we introduce a 3 X 3 "Smart Grid" temporal chunking strategy, reducing computational overhead by over 88% while preserving critical temporal continuity. To ensure data integrity and mitigate hallucination, the framework pioneers an autonomous "LLM-as-a-Judge" self-correction loop that cross-references quantitative and qualitative metrics before persistence. Finally, we implement a dual-persistence Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline utilizing a vector search engine (ChromaDB). This enables coaches to bypass rigid SQL databases and perform complex semantic queries (e.g., "Identify athletes with high endurance but poor core rigidity") using natural language. Experimental results demonstrate that this multi-agent approach significantly bridges the gap between raw biometric tracking and actionable coaching insights, offering a scalable, objective solution for national talent identification.

</details>


### [43] [SatSplat: Geometrically-Accurate Gaussian Splatting for Satellite Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28581)
*Shuang Song,Jiyong Kim,Rongjun Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SatSplat adapts 2D Gaussian Splatting to satellite photogrammetry with online camera adjustment, handling diverse illumination and shadows, achieving strong geometric accuracy with 11.93% lower MAE and 31% less memory than prior 3DGS methods on DFC2019 benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution satellite imagery requires fast and geometrically accurate 3D reconstruction, but existing 3DGS adaptations degrade under diverse illumination across multi-date, high-altitude acquisitions with small intersection angles, limiting applicability to remote sensing.

Method: SatSplat adapts 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) to satellite photogrammetry with an affine camera model, minimal delta parameterization for online in-splat camera refinement, geometric shadow mapping, and per-camera color correction to handle time-varying illumination.

Result: On DFC2019 and IARPA2016 benchmarks, SatSplat outperforms prior 3DGS baselines in geometric accuracy, reducing MAE by 11.93% and peak video memory by 31% on DFC2019 compared to the previous state of the art.

Conclusion: SatSplat enables large-scale digital surface modeling with practical computational efficiency, establishing the first 2DGS adaptation for satellite photogrammetry with robust illumination handling.

Abstract: High-resolution satellite imagery demands 3D reconstruction methods that deliver both speed and geometric accuracy. Recent adaptations of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to satellite imagery demonstrate strong efficiency, but reconstruction quality often degrades under diverse illumination across multi-date, high-altitude acquisitions (with small intersection angles), limiting applicability to remote sensing and vision tasks. We present SatSplat, the first framework to adapt 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) to satellite photogrammetry, with online camera adjustment. We approximate satellite cameras with an affine model and learn a minimal delta parameterization for in-splat camera refinement from dense observations. The formulation is implemented with a 2DGS scene representation. To handle time-varying shadows and illumination changes, we integrate geometric shadow mapping and per-camera color correction during training. Across the evaluated DFC2019 and IARPA2016 benchmark sites, SatSplat achieves strong geometric accuracy while significantly outperforming prior 3DGS-based baselines. On our processed DFC2019 benchmark, SatSplat reduces mean absolute error by 11.93% and peak video memory by 31% relative to the previous state of the art. Our approach enables large-scale digital surface modeling with practical computational efficiency. The project page is available at https://gdaosu.github.io/satsplat/.

</details>


### [44] [Animation2Code: Evaluating Temporal Visual Reasoning in Video-to-Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28593)
*Anya Ji,Abhijith Varma Mudunuri,David M. Chan,Alane Suhr*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Animation2Code is a benchmark with 1,069 web animation videos and paired HTML/CSS/JS code, evaluating whether VLMs can reconstruct executable animations from videos. Results show VLMs achieve decent appearance similarity but struggle with temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs excel at static visual-to-code tasks (webpages, charts, SVGs), but it is unknown whether they can recover temporal dynamics from videos containing motion. Animation2Code addresses this gap.

Method: The benchmark includes 1,069 web animation videos paired with HTML/CSS/JS implementations. Two human-aligned metrics—appearance similarity and temporal similarity—are proposed to disentangle visual fidelity from temporal alignment. State-of-the-art VLMs are benchmarked under finetuning and iterative refinement settings.

Result: Current VLMs struggle to maintain temporal consistency in reconstruction even when achieving high appearance similarity, and this gap persists under finetuning and iterative refinement.

Conclusion: Temporal visual reasoning remains a significant challenge for current VLMs, highlighting the need for models that can better capture motion dynamics from video inputs.

Abstract: While recent vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved significant improvements on static visual-to-code tasks such as generating code for webpages, charts, or SVGs, it remains unclear whether they can recover temporal dynamics when motion is present. To this end, we introduce Animation2Code, a benchmark for evaluating temporal visual reasoning via reconstructing executable web animation code from videos. Animation2Code consists of 1,069 web animation videos with diverse visual appearances and motion patterns, paired with corresponding HTML/CSS/JavaScript implementations. We propose two human-aligned metrics, appearance similarity and temporal similarity, which allow us to disentangle visual fidelity from temporal alignment when comparing rendered animations against ground-truth samples. Benchmarking state-of-the-art VLMs on this dataset shows that current VLMs struggle to maintain temporal consistency in reconstruction, even when achieving high appearance similarity, including under finetuning and iterative refinement settings. Code and data are available at https://anya-ji.github.io/animation2code-website .

</details>


### [45] [IMU-HOI: A Symbiotic Framework for Coherent Human-Object Interaction and Motion Capture via Contact-Conscious Inertial Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28604)
*Lizhou Lin,Songpengcheng Xia,Zengyuan Lai,Lan Sun,Jiarui Yang,Ling Pei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IMU-HOI jointly recovers full-body human pose and 6-DoF object trajectory from sparse IMUs by modeling human-object interaction through probabilistic hand-object contact inference and a three-stage fusion pipeline, outperforming prior inertial mocap methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-based methods for capturing human motion with object interactions suffer from occlusions and limited capture volumes. While IMUs avoid line-of-sight issues, existing IMU-based motion capture assumes isolated humans and ignores object contacts and dynamics, leaving a gap in handling human-object interaction.

Method: The framework infers probabilistic hand-object contacts directly from IMU streams, uses these as high-level signals to route between kinematic and inertial reasoning, and employs a three-stage fusion pipeline that refines human pose/root translation and fuses hand-based forward kinematics with object-IMU integration for coherent, drift-resilient trajectories.

Result: Substantial accuracy gains over prior inertial motion capture methods on challenging human-object interaction scenarios. The framework can be plugged into existing sparse-IMU mocap backbones with minimal changes.

Conclusion: IMU-HOI extends purely inertial motion capture from isolated humans to full human-object interaction and joint motion estimation, offering a practical solution for AR/VR and robotics applications.

Abstract: Capturing full-body human motion with object interactions is crucial for AR/VR and robotics applications, yet it remains challenging for conventional vision-based methods due to occlusions and constrained capture volumes. Inertial measurement units (IMUs) offer a compelling alternative without line-of-sight requirements, but existing IMU-based motion capture assumes an isolated human and ignores object contacts and dynamics. To bridge this gap, we present IMU-HOI, a novel framework that jointly recovers full-body human pose and 6-DoF object trajectory from sparse IMUs on the body and object, explicitly modeling human-object interaction. Our approach first infers probabilistic hand-object contacts directly from IMU streams and uses them as a high-level signal to route between kinematic and inertial reasoning. These contact cues drive a three-stage fusion pipeline that refines human pose and root translation, and fuses hand-based forward kinematics with object-IMU integration for object motion, yielding coherent, drift-resilient trajectories for both human and object. Experiments on challenging human-object interaction scenarios demonstrate substantial accuracy gains over prior inertial motion capture methods. Moreover, IMU-HOI can be plugged into existing sparse-IMU mocap backbones with minimal changes, effectively extending the scope of purely inertial motion capture from isolated humans to full human-object interaction and joint motion estimation.

</details>


### [46] [SIGNET: Motion-Level Knowledge Transfer for Cross-Language Sign Language Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28626)
*Sobhan Asasi,Ozge Mercanoglu Sincan,Richard Bowden*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SIGNET is a framework for cross-language sign language translation that enables motion-level knowledge transfer across multiple datasets and languages using pretrained backbones and attention-based hand-prior aggregation, achieving SOTA on four SLT benchmarks and WLASL recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SLT approaches are dataset/language-specific, struggle to scale, and overlook cross-lingual relationships between sign languages that could improve transfer learning. The field lacks a unified framework that leverages motion-level visual patterns across languages.

Method: SIGNET integrates multiple pretrained sign language backbones via an attention-based, hand-prior aggregation mechanism that guides a gated fusion network to dynamically select the most relevant experts for motion-level knowledge transfer across languages and datasets.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art translation performance on four benchmarks (How2Sign, Phoenix14T, CSL-Daily, MeineDGS) and surpasses prior methods on WLASL for sign language recognition.

Conclusion: Pretrained models capture reusable motion-level visual patterns across sign languages despite grammar/lexicon differences, enabling effective cross-lingual transfer without gloss annotations.

Abstract: Sign language translation (SLT) remains challenging due to its high spatio-temporal complexity, long sequences, and the need to model multiple articulators without relying on gloss annotations. Existing approaches are typically tailored to individual datasets or languages and struggle to scale, while overlooking the relationships between sign languages that could inform more effective cross-lingual transfer. We present \textbf{SIGNET}, a framework that enables motion-level knowledge transfer for cross-language sign language translation. Our key insight is that, although sign languages differ in grammar and lexicon, pretrained models capture motion-level visual patterns that can be reused across datasets and languages. \textbf{SIGNET} integrates multiple pretrained sign language backbones through an attention-based, hand-prior aggregation mechanism that guides a gated fusion network in dynamically selecting the most relevant experts. Comprehensive experiments on four benchmarks (How2Sign, Phoenix14T, CSL-Daily, and MeineDGS) demonstrate state-of-the-art translation performance, and \textbf{SIGNET} also surpasses prior methods on WLASL for sign language recognition.

</details>


### [47] [Physics-Grounded Disentangled Flow Modeling for Brain Disease Progression Trajectory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28630)
*Jun Wang,Peirong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PDF is a physics-grounded disentangled flow matching framework that separately models morphology evolution (structural deformation) and intensity evolution (signal changes) for longitudinal brain lesion forecasting, using PDE-regularized losses based on diffusion-reaction-advection dynamics, achieving SOTA on multiple brain disease datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing longitudinal brain lesion forecasting methods directly map baseline images to future observations without explicit modeling of the underlying physical mechanisms, leading to entangled modeling of structural deformation and intensity variation that limits physical plausibility, generalization, and interpretability.

Method: Proposes a Physics-grounded Disentangled Flow matching (PDF) framework that decomposes lesion progression into two separate flow matching networks: (1) morphology evolution for lesion growth and structural deformation, and (2) intensity evolution for signal changes due to lesion concentration variations. A PDE-regularized loss based on diffusion-reaction-advection formulation enforces physics-grounded constraints on morphological evolution.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on three public longitudinal datasets covering diverse brain diseases, validating the effectiveness of the disentangled modeling and physics-grounded learning design.

Conclusion: The proposed disentangled, physics-constrained approach improves physical plausibility, generalization, and interpretability for longitudinal brain lesion forecasting, with publicly available code.

Abstract: Forecasting longitudinal brain lesion evolution is critical for disease monitoring and treatment planning. Existing approaches typically learn a direct mapping from a baseline image to a future observation, without explicitly modeling the physical mechanisms underlying the lesion progression. Such an entangled modeling of structural deformation and image intensity variation limits physical plausibility, model generalization, and interpretability. To address this, we propose PDF, a Physics-grounded Disentangled Flow matching framework for longitudinal brain disease forecasting. We explicitly decompose the longitudinal modeling of lesion growth into two processes, each learned by a dedicated flow matching network: morphology evolution, which captures lesion growth and structural deformation; and intensity evolution, which models signal changes driven by variations in lesion concentration. To enforce physics-grounded constraints, we introduce a PDE-regularized loss based on lesion growth dynamics, that enforces a diffusion-reaction-advection formulation for morphological evolution. Experiments on three public longitudinal datasets spanning diverse brain diseases demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of the disentangled modeling framework and physics-grounded learning design. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/jhuldr/PDF.

</details>


### [48] [Obliviate: Erasing Concepts from Autoregressive Image Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28643)
*Hossein Shakibania,Jonas Henry Grebe,Tobias Braun,Ege Aktemur,Saleh Aslani,Mehmet Görkem Yiğit,Marcus Rohrbach*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Obliviate introduces a guidance-based concept erasure method for autoregressive image generation, using KL supervision, trajectory-level updates, and aligned visual prefixes to remove harmful content (nudity, violence, brand imagery) from models like Liquid, Emu3-Gen, and Janus-Pro, reducing RAB nudity from 91.58 to 3.15.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept erasure for autoregressive image generation remains largely unexplored despite the growing relevance of these models in unified multimodal architectures, and existing methods do not address the specific challenges of autoregressive generation.

Method: Obliviate is a guidance-based concept erasure method with three key design choices: (1) KL-based supervision over visual token distributions, (2) trajectory-level updates over full autoregressive rollouts, and (3) aligned visual prefixes for stable target construction.

Result: Obliviate consistently outperforms current alternatives across three SOTA autoregressive text-to-image models (Liquid, Emu3-Gen, Janus-Pro), reducing nudity on the RAB benchmark from 91.58 to 3.15 while preserving overall model utility.

Conclusion: Obliviate effectively fills the gap in concept erasure for autoregressive image generation, demonstrating strong performance in removing explicit content, graphic violence, and branded imagery from generative models.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of generative AI models has intensified concerns about misuse, including the creation of unsafe or disturbing imagery. To mitigate such issues, several concept erasure approaches have been proposed to remove harmful content from multimodal generative models. Yet concept erasure for autoregressive image generation remains largely unexplored, despite the growing relevance of these models in recent trends toward unified multimodal architectures. In this work, we fill this gap by introducing Obliviate, a guidance-based concept erasure method for autoregressive image generation. Our method builds on three key design choices: KL-based supervision over visual token distributions, trajectory-level updates over full autoregressive rollouts, and aligned visual prefixes for stable target construction. We evaluate Obliviate on three state-of-the-art autoregressive text-to-image models, Liquid, Emu3-Gen, and Janus-Pro, covering the erasure of explicit content, graphic violence, and branded imagery. Obliviate consistently outperforms current alternatives, reducing nudity on the defensive RAB benchmark from 91.58 to 3.15 while preserving overall model utility.

</details>


### [49] [FedLAS: Feature-Modulated Bidirectional Label Smoothing for Neural Network Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28654)
*Thiru Thillai Nadarasar Bahavan,Sachith Seneviratne,Saman Halgamuge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FedLAS is a plug-and-play algorithm that improves DNN calibration via feature-norm-based confidence detection and bidirectional smoothing to handle both over- and under-confidence, outperforming existing label smoothing methods on vision benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing label smoothing methods rely on predefined uniform smoothing rules and only address overconfidence, failing to adapt to diverse sample characteristics (e.g., difficulty/ambiguity) and evolving model states during training.

Method: FedLAS introduces two key components: a Feature Norm-based Confidence Indicator (NCI) to control smoothing levels, and a Bidirectional Calibration Gating (BCG) module to detect both over- and under-confidence, enabling sample-adaptive smoothing adjustments per training step.

Result: Experiments on standard and fine-grained high-resolution vision benchmarks show FedLAS consistently reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Adaptive ECE while maintaining Top-1 accuracy, outperforming modern baselines.

Conclusion: FedLAS enhances model calibration by adaptively addressing both over- and under-confidence per sample, serving as a plug-and-play improvement for LS and MbLS based losses in standard DNNs.

Abstract: Deep Neural Network (DNN) classifiers suffer from poor calibration when their softmax outputs (predictive confidence) deviate from the empirical likelihoods. This manifests itself as either overconfident incorrect predictions or under-confident correct predictions. Label smoothing (LS) enhances model calibration by introducing entropy regularization during training through redistributing probability mass from the ground-truth label to the remaining classes. LS, including Margin-based LS (MbLS), have restrictive assumptions: they rely on predefined, uniform smoothing rules and only tackle overconfidence. In reality, samples exhibit diverse characteristics, such as difficulty/ambiguity, that interact with the evolving nature of the model being trained. In training, samples may have various degrees of under- or overconfidence. To overcome this, a mechanism that identifies the specific confidence state of each sample and determines the appropriate degree of smoothing in each training step is needed, tailoring the adjustment to the individual sample. We propose FedLAS: Feature-Modulated Bidirectional Label Smoothing, a plug-and-play algorithm for label smoothing-based losses. In FedLAS, we introduce a Feature Norm-based Confidence Indicator (NCI) to control smoothing and a Bidirectional Calibration Gating (BCG) module to detect both over and under-confidence. Our algorithm can be integrated with LS and MbLS based losses when applied to standard DNNs, enhancing performance. Extensive experiments on standard and fine-grained high-resolution vision benchmarks show that FedLAS consistently improves calibration compared to modern baselines, reducing Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Adaptive ECE while maintaining Top-1 accuracy. Code: github.com/nadarasarbahavan/FEDLAS

</details>


### [50] [SemDynReg: Semantics-Guided Deformation Regularization for Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28656)
*Ruitao Chen,Mozhang Guo,Jinge Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a semantics-guided regularization framework for Deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting that enforces object-level deformation consistency using SAM-derived segmentation masks and feature matching, improving rendering quality and deformation coherence across dynamic scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deformation field-based approaches for dynamic 3DGS lack explicit object-level modeling, causing inconsistent Gaussian deformations within objects and unwanted coupling between different objects.

Method: Extracts segmentation masks via SAM, derives semantic features, constructs object-ID maps via feature relevance matching, identifies pixel-wise top-k contributing Gaussians per object, and imposes consistency regularization on deformation parameters (position, scale, rotation).

Result: Outperforms baseline methods in rendering quality with higher PSNR, SSIM, and lower LPIPS; achieves improved object-level deformation consistency.

Conclusion: Incorporating semantic cues as object-level regularization improves deformation consistency and rendering quality in dynamic 3DGS, addressing limitations of prior deformation field methods.

Abstract: Deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as an efficient approach for rendering dynamic scenes in a wide range of 3D applications. However, existing deformation field-based approaches largely lack explicit object-level modeling, often resulting in inconsistent Gaussian deformations within individual objects and unwanted coupling between different objects. To address this limitation, we introduce a semantics-guided framework that enforces dynamic regularization at the object level, aiming to achieve spatially consistent object-wise deformation. Specifically, we first extract segmentation masks using the Segment Anything Model (SAM) and derive semantic features from input images. An object-ID map is then constructed via feature relevance matching with a predefined object dictionary. Guided by this object-ID map, we identify the pixel-wise top-k contributing Gaussians for each object and impose consistency regularization on their deformation parameters, including position, scale, and rotation. Unlike prior methods that learn deformation fields without explicit object-level constraints, our approach incorporates semantic cues to guide deformation behavior at the object level. Experimental results demonstrate that our semantics-aware regularization improves object-level deformation consistency and outperforms baseline methods in rendering quality, achieving higher PSNR and SSIM and lower LPIPS in dynamic 3DGS rendering. Our project page is available at https://dyn-reg-3dgs.github.io/.

</details>


### [51] [BackTranslation2.0 -- A Linguistically Motivated Metric to Assess Sign Language Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28673)
*Oliver Cory,Maksym Ivashechkin,Karahan Sahin,Oline Ranum,Jianhe Low,Edward Fish,Anton Pelykh,Ozge Mercanoglu Sincan,Richard Bowden*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BackTranslation2.0 is a linguistically grounded evaluation metric for text-to-sign translation that uses an agentic framework with specialized tools and LLM-based cross-referential modules to assess grammatical correctness, phonological accuracy, motion fluency, and generation fidelity, demonstrating strong correlation with human judgments on a British Sign Language dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for generated Sign Language are simplistic and poorly aligned with human judgments, lacking linguistic grounding and comprehensive assessment capabilities.

Method: An agentic framework with a deterministic pipeline orchestrating specialized tools to assess four scoring dimensions (grammatical correctness, phonological accuracy, motion fluency, generation fidelity), augmented by LLM-based cross-referential comparison modules for structured reasoning and deterministic weighted formulas for final scoring.

Result: Strong correlation with human judgments across all four quality dimensions on a British Sign Language (BSL) dataset, outperforming six baseline metrics.

Conclusion: BackTranslation2.0 provides a more comprehensive, interpretable, and linguistically principled evaluation framework for sign language production systems.

Abstract: Sign Languages (SLs) are the primary means of communication for millions of deaf individuals, yet existing evaluation metrics for generated SL remain simplistic and poorly aligned with human judgements. We introduce BackTranslation2.0, a linguistically grounded evaluation metric for text-to-sign translation that moves beyond naïve backtranslation. Our approach adopts an agentic framework in which a deterministic pipeline orchestrates a suite of specialised tools to assess four scoring dimensions - grammatical correctness, phonological accuracy, motion fluency, and generation fidelity - aligned with human rater assessments. Tool outputs are not treated independently: a set of large language model (LLM)-based cross-referential comparison modules evaluates consistency across tools and checks outputs against linguistic expectations, enabling structured reasoning over grammatical, phonological, and motion-level evidence. Final dimension scores are computed through deterministic weighted formulas over validated tool outputs. To validate BackTranslation2.0, we introduce and evaluate on a British Sign Language (BSL) dataset rated in a human rater study across the same quality dimensions, following a protocol developed in collaboration between linguists and deaf experts, benchmarking against six baseline metrics. Our method demonstrates strong correlation with human judgements across all dimensions, providing a more comprehensive, interpretable, and linguistically principled evaluation framework for sign language production systems.

</details>
