Result: Produces complete, simulation-ready 3D objects without manual masks; enables realistic dynamic interactions on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Formulating multi-object decoupling as a sparse-view reconstruction problem effectively addresses occlusion and limited viewpoints, yielding physically plausible reconstructions suitable for MPM simulation.

Abstract: We propose a mask-free method for lossless multi-object 3D reconstruction from sparse and occluded real-world views, enabling physically plausible interaction via Material Point Method (MPM) simulation. Our key insight is that object coupling stems from occlusion and limited viewpoints, which we address by formulating multi-object decoupling as a sparse-view reconstruction problem. Using 3D Gaussian Splatting as base representation, we first obtain coarse instance partitions with a SAM2-trained segmentation field. Rather than relying on masks, we reconstruct fragmented geometries by leveraging a joint Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) process, which integrates reference-view supervision with novel-view synthesis guided by 2D and 3D diffusion priors to enforce both texture fidelity and 3D consistency. Furthermore, we incorporate geometry-aware priors such as intra-object and inter-object similarity to regularize geometric reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that our method produces complete, simulation-ready 3D objects without requiring manual masks, enabling realistic dynamic interactions on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [104] [MirrorPPR: Exemplar-Based Portrait Photo Retouching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29308)
*Zhihong Liu,Zheng Li,Jiachun Jin,Siqi Kou,Yitao Jian,Fengpei Yu,Zhijie Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MirrorPPR is a novel framework for exemplar-based structural portrait retouching that captures and transfers subtle facial and body modifications using a Retouching Operation Extractor, DiT integration with LoRA, a self-augmentation data paradigm, and a large-scale MirrorPPR47M dataset, significantly outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-guided image editing struggles with structural portrait retouching (fine-grained facial features and body proportion changes) because textual descriptions cannot convey precise localized modifications. Existing exemplar-based methods handle pronounced visual transformations but fail at the delicate, localized edits required for structural portrait retouching.

Method: MirrorPPR uses a Retouching Operation Extractor to capture subtle differences from an exemplar pair, injects these representations into a pre-trained Diffusion Transformer (DiT) via a connector and LoRA modules, and employs a data self-augmentation paradigm to ensure strictly aligned retouching operations. The MirrorPPR47M dataset (47M+ pairs) with simulated and professional subsets enables progressive curriculum learning.

Result: Extensive experiments show MirrorPPR significantly outperforms existing baselines in both retouching quality and identity preservation.

Conclusion: MirrorPPR effectively addresses the challenge of exemplar-based structural portrait retouching, enabling precise transfer of delicate facial and body modifications while preserving identity, supported by a large-scale dataset and curriculum learning strategy.

Abstract: While text-guided image editing has made remarkable progress, it remains limited in structural portrait retouching. Textual descriptions struggle to convey fine-grained changes to facial features and body proportions. To address this gap, we introduce Exemplar-Based Portrait Photo Retouching, where the model is given an exemplar pair and tasked with inferring and applying the same retouching operations to a new query image. Existing exemplar-based editing methods primarily focus on tasks with pronounced visual transformations. In contrast, structural portrait retouching involves extremely delicate and localized modifications, making accurate extraction and transfer of these edits challenging. To tackle this, we propose MirrorPPR, a novel framework designed to capture and transfer subtle structural retouching operations. Our method uses a Retouching Operation Extractor to capture the subtle differences from the exemplar pair. The extracted representations are then injected into a pre-trained Diffusion Transformer (DiT) through a connector and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules. Furthermore, constructing perfectly aligned cross-identity training pairs is severely hindered by operation misalignment. To overcome this, we propose an advanced data self-augmentation paradigm that ensures strictly aligned retouching operations. To alleviate data scarcity and support this novel task, we introduce MirrorPPR47M, a large-scale dataset with over 47 million retouched pairs. By structuring the dataset into simulated and professional subsets, we enable progressive curriculum learning to smoothly optimize the network. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MirrorPPR significantly outperforms existing baselines in both retouching quality and identity preservation. The project page is available at https://sjtu-deng-lab.github.io/MirrorPPR.

</details>


### [105] [D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR: Degradation-Disentangled Representation for Real-World Omnidirectional Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29314)
*Hongyu An,Xinfeng Zhang,Xu Fan,Shijie Zhao,Li Zhang,Ruiqin Xiong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: D²R²OSR is a degradation-disentangled representation framework for real-world omnidirectional image super-resolution that explicitly models distortions from both fisheye capture and Equirectangular Projection, using a Perspective Projection Representation branch and a Degradation-Specific Module to achieve state-of-the-art performance in high-fidelity 360° image enhancement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Real-SR methods fail for omnidirectional images because their degradation models do not account for the complex fisheye capture and ERP projection pipeline, which introduces severe aliasing and projection-specific distortions, limiting fine-grained 360° detail rendering under real-world degradations.

Method: D²R²OSR uses a dual-branch architecture: (1) a Perspective Projection Representation (PPR) branch captures viewpoint-aware features, and (2) an ERP branch models geometric distortions. A Degradation-Specific Module (DSM) jointly models ERP-induced geometric distortions and PPR-specific real-world degradations, guided by projection priors and viewpoint-centric perception.

Result: D²R²OSR achieves state-of-the-art performance on real-world omnidirectional super-resolution, producing visually compelling high-fidelity results while maintaining favorable computational efficiency suitable for low-resource deployment.

Conclusion: Explicitly disentangling fisheye imaging degradations from ERP projection distortions, combined with viewpoint-aware feature modeling, is effective for real-world omnidirectional image super-resolution, enabling practical deployment on resource-constrained platforms.

Abstract: With the growing demand for immersive visual experiences, high-quality omnidirectional images (ODIs) have become increasingly important. However, limitations in imaging devices and transmission bandwidth often lead to low-resolution ODIs, hindering the rendering of fine-grained 360° details, especially in the presence of real-world degradations and geometric distortions. Existing real-world super-resolution (Real-SR) methods are inadequate for ODIs, as their degradation models fail to account for the complex imaging pipeline involving fisheye capture and Equirectangular Projection (ERP), introducing severe aliasing and projection-specific distortions. To address these challenges, we propose D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR, a Degradation-Disentangled Representation framework for Real-world Omnidirectional image Super-Resolution. D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR explicitly models degradations arising from both fisheye imaging and ERP projection, guided by two key insights: (1) projection priors play a critical role in shaping real-world degradations, and (2) human perception in immersive environments is inherently viewpoint-centric. Accordingly, we introduce a Perspective Projection Representation (PPR) operating alongside the ERP branch to capture viewpoint-aware features, together with a Degradation-Specific Module (DSM) that jointly models ERP-induced geometric distortions and PPR-specific real-world degradations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR achieves state-of-the-art performance and produces visually compelling, high-fidelity omnidirectional Real-SR results while maintaining favorable computational efficiency for low-resource deployment.

</details>


### [106] [FDM-MFVT: Few-step Sampling Diffusion Model for Mask-Free Virtual Try-On](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29319)
*Jiaxin Liu,Xiaoye Liang,Lai Jiang,Mai Xu,Jun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FDM-MFVT is a few-step diffusion model for mask-free virtual try-on that uses an Outfit-aware Noise Optimization Module (6 steps vs 30) and an Instruction-driven Try-on Module, along with a new 30K-pair MFVT dataset, achieving superior results with fewer inference steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based IVTON methods require many sampling steps and depend on masks with costly auxiliary networks, and the lack of large-scale mask-free paired datasets limits mask-free IVTON development.

Method: Proposes FDM-MFVT with two modules: (1) Outfit-aware Noise Optimization Module (OANO) that initializes alignment space with noise using the input image and generates results in 6 steps; (2) Instruction-driven Try-on Module (IDT) using virtual try-on prompts and efficient adaptation. Also introduces MFVT dataset with 30,000 mask-free pairs.

Result: FDM-MFVT achieves superior quantitative and qualitative results with fewer inference steps (6 vs 30) compared to both mask-based and mask-free baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed few-step diffusion model effectively addresses efficiency and data limitations in mask-free virtual try-on, outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: Image-based Virtual Try-On (IVTON) has greatly advanced through diffusion models, yet existing methods require many sampling steps and depend on masks with costly auxiliary networks. In addition, the absence of large-scale mask-free paired datasets further limits the development of mask-free IVTON. We propose FDM-MFVT, a few-step diffusion model for mask-free IVTON, integrating an Outfit-aware Noise Optimization Module (OANO) and an Instruction-driven Try-on Module (IDT) to enhance efficiency and flexibility.The OANO module initializes the alignment space with noise using the input image and only needs 6 steps to generate a higher-fidelity try-on image compared to 30 steps.The IDT module uses virtual try-on prompts and efficient adaptation to generate high-quality results from garment and person images alone. We further introduce MFVT, a 30,000-pair mask-free IVTON dataset. Experiments show that FDM-MFVT achieves superior quantitative and qualitative results with fewer inference steps than mask-based and mask-free baseline methods.

</details>


### [107] [RAGA: Real Time Ray Traced Gaussian Shadow Casting for 3DGS Avatar-Scene Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29329)
*Aymen Mir,Riza Alp Guler,Jian Wang,Peter Wonka,Bing Zhou,Gerard Pons-Moll*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAGA introduces a ray-traced shadow casting method for 3D Gaussian Splatting avatars using exact ray-Gaussian line integrals, enabling physically plausible, temporally stable shadows without mesh reconstruction, running at ~50 FPS via custom CUDA/OptiX kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for shadow casting in 3DGS rely on binary hit tests and mesh-based shadow casters, which lack physical plausibility and require mesh reconstruction. The goal is to enable realistic, temporally stable shadows for animated 3DGS avatars in single/multi-avatar and object-interaction scenarios.

Method: RAGA computes shadows entirely in Gaussian space using exact ray-Gaussian line integrals: for each occluding Gaussian, the opacity profile is integrated along the shadow ray and normalized by the theoretical maximum integral, yielding a continuous weight reflecting ray traversal. An avatar proxy representation reduces temporal variance from clothing deformations. Implemented with custom CUDA kernels integrated into NVIDIA OptiX.

Result: Achieves ~50 FPS shadow tracing. Evaluated on single-avatar, multi-avatar, and avatar-object interaction scenarios, demonstrating substantially improved shadow realism, temporal stability, and scene coherence over prior methods.

Conclusion: RAGA provides a mesh-free, physically plausible shadow casting approach for 3DGS avatars, enhancing visual quality and temporal stability in dynamic multi-avatar and object-interaction settings.

Abstract: We study the problem of physically plausible shadow casting when animating 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) avatars, either individually or in multi-avatar and object-interaction scenarios, within existing 3DGS scenes. In contrast to prior methods that rely on binary hit tests and mesh-based shadow casters, our method performs shadow computation entirely in Gaussian space, without requiring any mesh reconstruction. We introduce RAGA, a Ray-Traced Gaussian Shadow Casting formulation based on exact ray-Gaussian line integrals. For each occluding Gaussian, we integrate the opacity profile along the shadow ray and normalize by the theoretical maximum integral, producing a weight that captures how the ray traverses the occluder rather than merely whether an intersection occurred. To reduce temporal variance from clothing deformations in animated avatars, we further introduce an avatar proxy representation that stabilizes shadow casting while preserving visual fidelity. We implement RAGA using custom CUDA kernels integrated with the NVIDIA OptiX framework; as such, our shadow tracer runs at rates of about 50 FPS. We evaluate on single-avatar, multi-avatar, and avatar-object interaction scenarios across multiple datasets, demonstrating substantially improved shadow realism, temporal stability, and scene coherence. Our project page is available at https://miraymen.github.io/raga/.

</details>


### [108] [HiReFF: High-Resolution Feedforward Human Reconstruction from Uncalibrated Sparse-View Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29333)
*Yiming Jiang,Hanzhang Tu,Wenfeng Song,Siyou Lin,Liang An,Shuai Li,Aimin Hao,Yebin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiReFF is a feed-forward method for 2K-resolution 360° human video reconstruction from uncalibrated sparse-view videos (four views at 90° intervals). It uses Scale-synchronized Camera Calibration, Gaussian-wise Foreground Masking, and High-resolution Side-tuning to achieve efficient, temporally consistent volumetric video streaming without per-scene optimization or calibrated cameras.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncalibrated volumetric video streaming for human reconstruction remains challenging due to the need for temporal consistency and computational efficiency from sparse-view inputs. Existing methods rely on per-scene optimization or calibrated cameras, and recent feed-forward models are limited to low-resolution (0.5K) single-frame synthesis.

Method: (1) Scale-synchronized Camera Calibration to resolve scale ambiguity for multi-view supervision; (2) Gaussian-wise Foreground Masking to reconstruct clean foregrounds by modulating Gaussian parameters; (3) High-resolution Side-tuning that achieves 2K rendering by augmenting the Gaussian head with supplementary features while keeping the backbone at 0.5K to reduce computational overhead.

Result: HiReFF significantly outperforms existing methods in high-resolution streaming volumetric video reconstruction, achieving 2K resolution from uncalibrated sparse-view inputs.

Conclusion: HiReFF demonstrates that feed-forward models can achieve high-resolution (2K), temporally consistent 360° human video reconstruction from uncalibrated sparse views, advancing holographic communication and AR/VR applications.

Abstract: Uncalibrated volumetric video streaming for human reconstruction is essential for holographic communication and AR/VR, yet remains challenging due to the need for temporal consistency and computational efficiency from sparse-view inputs. Existing methods rely on per-scene optimization or calibrated cameras, while recent feed-forward models are limited to low-resolution (0.5K) single-frame synthesis. We present HiReFF, a feed-forward method for 2K-resolution 360° human video reconstruction from uncalibrated sparse-view videos. Our framework decomposes the problem into two key tasks: foreground 3D Gaussian reconstruction from sparse-view videos (four views separated by 90°) and computationally efficient high-resolution synthesis. To enable the former, we propose Scale-synchronized Camera Calibration to resolve scale ambiguity for multi-view supervision, and Gaussian-wise Foreground Masking to reconstruct clean foregrounds by modulating Gaussian parameters. For efficient high-resolution synthesis, our High-resolution Side-tuning achieves 2K rendering by augmenting the Gaussian head with supplementary features while keeping the backbone at 0.5K, drastically reducing computational overhead. Experiments demonstrate that HiReFF significantly outperforms existing methods in high-resolution streaming volumetric video reconstruction. https://iridescentjiang.github.io/HiReFF

</details>


### [109] [Multi-scale Object-Aware Gaze Estimation via Geometric Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29334)
*Jiajie Mi,Xinyu Liu,Mengke Song,Chenglizhao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a two-stage gaze target estimation framework that reformulates the task as hierarchical reasoning using object semantics, achieving state-of-the-art results (AUC 0.961-0.987) with only 7.1M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing gaze target estimation methods treat the task as pixel-level regression from global features, failing to explicitly represent gazed objects as distinct semantic entities, leading to unstable and semantically inconsistent predictions in complex scenes.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) object-level representations are incorporated during feature encoding to align image features with discrete semantic entities; (2) multi-scale feature fusion and geometric constraints from head pose/gaze direction are used for fine-grained localization and object-level discrimination.

Result: Achieves AUC of 0.961, 0.948, 0.987, and 0.977 on GazeFollow, VideoAttentionTarget, ChildPlay, and GOO-Real benchmarks respectively, with only 7.1M parameters.

Conclusion: Object-semantics-guided hierarchical reasoning improves gaze target estimation stability and semantic consistency, outperforming pixel-level regression approaches with compact model size.

Abstract: Gaze target estimation aims to predict the semantic object an observer fixates upon within an image, a task deeply rooted in the object-oriented nature of human gaze. Observers tend to select a specific semantic entity as the attentional target, rather than responding randomly across arbitrary regions of the image. However, existing methods typically model this task as a direct mapping from global features to gaze heatmaps, essentially treating it as a pixel-level regression problem. This approach fails to explicitly represent the gazed object as a distinct entity, making it difficult to produce stable and semantically consistent predictions in complex scenes. To address this, we propose a two-stage gaze estimation framework guided by object semantics, reformulating gaze target estimation as a hierarchical reasoning process. Our method incorporates object-level representations during feature encoding to align image features with discrete semantic entities, then introduces multi-scale feature fusion and geometric constraints from head pose and gaze direction for fine-grained localization and object-level discrimination. Extensive experiments on GazeFollow, VideoAttentionTarget, ChildPlay, and GOO-Real demonstrate that our method achieves AUC of 0.961, 0.948, 0.987, and 0.977 respectively, delivering strong performance across all benchmarks while maintaining a compact parameter size of 7.1M.

</details>


### [110] [W4A4 Quantization for Inference on Wan2.2-I2V-A14B](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29337)
*Yidong Chen,Chengyu Shi,Jiahao Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A W4A4 quantization pipeline for Wan2.2-I2V-A14B combining MixQ-style mixed precision, SmoothQuant-style smoothing, and block-wise HiF4 packing, achieving under 3.5% FP16 degradation on VBench I2V metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable 4-bit weight and 4-bit activation inference on the large Wan2.2 I2V-A14B model while minimizing accuracy loss compared to FP16, addressing the ICME 2026 Low-Bit-width Large-Model Quantization Challenge.

Method: Combines MixQ-style mixed precision for sparse activation outliers, SmoothQuant-style per-channel smoothing, block-wise HiF4 packing for feed-forward linear layers, calibration on OpenS2V-5M batches to identify heavy-tailed activation channels, and a dual-branch GEMM that preserves outlier columns in higher precision.

Result: Stays within 2-3.5% of FP16 on most VBench I2V quality axes, improves motion smoothness, and outperforms the native HiFloat4 baseline which degrades roughly 5% relative to FP16.

Conclusion: The proposed mixed-precision + smoothing pipeline effectively reduces quantization degradation for W4A4 inference on large video generation models, outperforming a straightforward HiFloat4 baseline.

Abstract: We summarize our submission to Sub-Challenge 1: W4A4 Quantization for Inference (HiF4 / MXFP4) of the ICME 2026 Low-Bit-width Large-Model Quantization Challenge. The sub-challenge targets 4-bit weight and 4-bit activation inference on Wan-AI/Wan2.2-I2V-A14B under HiF4 or MXFP4 numerical formats. We adapt two complementary ideas from LLM quantization, MixQ-style mixed precision for sparse activation outliers and SmoothQuant-style per-channel smoothing, together with block-wise HiF4 packing for Wan2.2 feed-forward linear layers. Calibration on representative OpenS2V-5M batches identifies heavy-tailed activation channels; smoothing rebalances dynamic range before W4A4 rounding; and a dual-branch GEMM preserves outlier columns in higher precision while the bulk of channels use strict W4A4. On official VBench I2V metrics, our pipeline stays within 2-3.5 percent of FP16 on most quality axes and improves motion smoothness, outperforming a native HiFloat4 baseline that degrades roughly 5 percent relative to FP16 across all reported scores.

</details>


### [111] [Fast Enough to Act: Spatio-Temporal Visual Token Merging for Low-Latency Robotic VLMs and VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29350)
*Junzhou Chen,Jindong Wang,Gang Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ST-Merge is a plug-and-play, training-free framework that fuses redundant visual tokens during encoding using 3D spatiotemporal coordinates and multi-queue matching, achieving up to 8.3× speedup on VLA policies with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video and high-resolution image inputs in vision-language and vision-language action models produce massive visual tokens, causing high inference latency that hinders real-time robot control.

Method: ST-Merge explicitly constructs 3D spatiotemporal coordinates, uses multi-queue parallel matching and weighted aggregation to fuse redundant tokens across frames during visual encoding, and introduces a post-merge positional correction mechanism that dynamically re-evaluates RoPE for the weighted centroid to eliminate spatial deviation.

Result: On Qwen2.5-VL (VideoQA), ST-Merge achieves 2× inference speedup with only 1% precision loss. On π_{0.5} VLA policy, it achieves 8.3× speedup at 1024×1024 resolution matching baseline success rate; lower resolutions see a small accuracy drop.

Conclusion: ST-Merge effectively breaks the computational bottleneck of visual token overload in real-time robotic control by providing efficient, geometrically consistent token fusion without training, while preserving spatial precision for dexterous operation.

Abstract: Vision-language models and vision-language action models endow the robot with unprecedented capabilities. However, the input of video and high-resolution images yields a massive number of visual tokens, leading to extremely high inference latency and severely hindering the robot's real-time control. To break through this computational bottleneck, we propose ST-Merge, a plug-and-play, training-free framework that efficiently fuses redundant tokens directly during the visual encoding phase. By explicitly constructing 3D spatiotemporal coordinates, it employs a multi-queue parallel matching and weighted aggregation mechanism to achieve efficient and geometrically consistent fusion of redundant tokens across frames. In addition, we introduce a post-merge positional correction mechanism that effectively eliminates spatial deviation caused by merging by dynamically re-evaluating the rotational position code of the weighted centroid of the vision token, thereby ensuring the high-precision spatial awareness required for dexterous operation. In the Video Question Answering task on the mainstream VLM, Qwen2.5-VL, ST-Merge achieves a 2$\times$ inference speedup with only a tiny 1\% loss in precision. When deployed on the $π_{0.5}$ VLA policy, ST-Merge achieves an 8.3$\times$ speedup at 1024 $\times$ 1024 resolution and matches the baseline success rate at this high-resolution setting. At lower resolutions, it introduces a small drop in accuracy.

</details>


### [112] [Dynamic Parsing and Updating Natural Language Specification using VLMs for Robust Vision-Language Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29357)
*Xiao Wang,Liye Jin,Dan Xu,Yuehang Li,Lan Chen,Yaowei Wang,Yonghong Tian,Jin Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a language dependency parsing mechanism that uses Qwen-VL to perform component-aware adaptive textual description updates, improving vision-language tracking by distilling core tracking components (target objects, semantic concepts, background context) and mitigating issues like erroneous updating, background distraction, and hallucination artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-language tracking methods that adaptively optimize textual descriptions suffer from inherent defects: erroneous target updating, excessive background distraction, and hallucination artifacts caused by directly generating text via sequence models or LLMs.

Method: A language dependency parsing mechanism that distills core tracking principal components (target objects, semantic concepts, background contextual information), then performs component-aware adaptive textual description updates using the pre-trained vision-language model Qwen-VL.

Result: Consistent and superior tracking performance on multiple large-scale benchmarks: TNL2K, LaSOT, TNLLT, and OTB-LANG.

Conclusion: The proposed modules integrated into a baseline framework achieve superior performance, and the approach mitigates defects like erroneous updating, background distraction, and hallucination artifacts in vision-language tracking.

Abstract: Vision-language tracking guided by natural language specifications leverages high-level semantic cues of target objects to substantially boost tracking accuracy and robustness. Existing studies have verified that adaptively optimizing textual descriptions throughout the tracking process can effectively mitigate the semantic-visual mismatch induced by dynamic variations in target appearance, position, and other inherent attributes. Nevertheless, mainstream methods that directly generate textual information via sequence models or large language models inevitably suffer from inherent defects, including erroneous target updating, excessive background distraction, and pervasive hallucination artifacts. To address the aforementioned limitations, this paper proposes a novel language dependency parsing mechanism to precisely distill core tracking principal components, encompassing target objects, semantic concepts, and background contextual information. On this basis, we perform component-aware adaptive textual description updates by exploiting the powerful cross-modal understanding capability of the pre-trained vision-language model Qwen-VL. By integrating the proposed elaborately designed modules into the baseline framework, our method achieves consistent and superior tracking performance on multiple large-scale vision-language tracking benchmarks, including TNL2K, LaSOT, TNLLT, and OTB-LANG. The source code and pre-trained models will be released at https://github.com/Event-AHU/Open_VLTrack.

</details>


### [113] [SAFE-DiT: Semantics-Aware Fast-path Execution for High-Resolution Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29360)
*Xuanhua Yin,Yuxuan Jia,Chuanzhi Xu,Weidong Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAFE-DiT is a training-free framework that eliminates redundant attention masks in Diffusion Transformer inference, achieving up to 5.09× speedup and 3.4× memory reduction without quality degradation by separating mask elision from spatial scheduling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution Diffusion Transformer (DiT) inference suffers from spatial redundancy, but existing spatially adaptive implementations encode regional computation as attention masks, which pushes SDPA away from FlashAttention fast paths, creating a systems bottleneck called Mask-Induced Dispatch Tax (MIDT) that grows with sequence length.

Method: SAFE-DiT separates exact mask elision (removing only provenance-certified image self-attention masks that induce row-wise constant shifts) from approximation-based spatial scheduling. Spatial adaptation is realized via prompt-conditioned token partitioning, selective state updates with global context, and periodic context refresh. An acceleration-only configuration (SAFE-Core) and a variant with sensitivity-weighted classifier-free guidance (SAFE-DiT+SW) are defined.

Result: Redundant masks cause 4.1×–5.8× slowdown versus mask-free path. On Lumina-Next, SAFE-DiT achieves 2.69× speedup at 1024², 5.09× at 2560², reduces peak memory from 94.1→27.9 GB at 2560², and enables 3072² generation where dense inference OOMs. Visual non-inferiority confirmed via paired metrics, ablations, and blinded human study.

Conclusion: SAFE-DiT effectively eliminates the MIDT bottleneck in high-resolution DiT inference, providing substantial acceleration and memory savings while preserving visual quality, with SAFE-Core matching dense baselines and SAFE-DiT+SW offering an alternative prompt-alignment operating point.

Abstract: High-resolution Diffusion Transformer (DiT) inference contains substantial spatial redundancy, but many spatially adaptive implementations encode regional computation as attention masks, which can inadvertently move scaled dot-product attention (SDPA) away from FlashAttention fast paths. We identify this avoidable systems bottleneck as Mask-Induced Dispatch Tax (MIDT) and show that it grows with latent sequence length. We introduce SAFE-DiT, a training-free Semantics-Aware Fast-path Execution framework that separates exact mask elision from approximation-based spatial scheduling. SAFE-DiT removes only provenance-certified image self-attention masks that induce a row-wise constant shift in attention logits, preserves semantics-bearing masks such as text-padding masks, and realizes spatial adaptation through prompt-conditioned token partitioning, selective state updates with global context, and periodic context refresh. We call this acceleration-only configuration SAFE-Core and report sensitivity-weighted classifier-free guidance separately as SAFE-DiT+SW. On the evaluated PyTorch SDPA stack, redundant masks make long-sequence attention $4.1\times$ to $5.8\times$ slower than the mask-free path. On Lumina-Next, SAFE-DiT achieves $2.69\times$ end-to-end acceleration at $1024^2$ resolution and $5.09\times$ at $2560^2$, reduces peak memory at $2560^2$ from 94.1 to 27.9 GB, and enables $3072^2$ generation when dense inference runs out of memory. Paired metrics, component ablations, and a blinded human study support visual non-inferiority of SAFE-Core to the dense fast-path baseline, while SAFE-DiT+SW provides a separate prompt-alignment operating point without reintroducing spatial self-attention masks. Code is available at https://github.com/xuanhuayin/SAFE-DiT.

</details>


### [114] [SAD-GS: Learning Reliable 3D Semantic Gaussian Fields via Dynamic Geo-Semantic Anchoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29376)
*Yufei Zhang,Chenlu Zhan,Gaoang Wang,Hongwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAD-GS learns reliable 3D semantic Gaussian fields by using dynamic geo-semantic anchoring, which includes Semantic Anchor Distillation (SAD) for viewpoint-invariant semantics and a Geo-Semantic Feedback Loop (GSFL) for filtering tracker anomalies, achieving state-of-the-art results on LERF-OVS, 3D-OVS, and Mip-NeRF360.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-view 2D supervision for 3D semantic Gaussian fields suffers from unreliable semantic targets and spatial assignments due to view-dependent feature drift, boundary leakage, and identity switches from tracker masks, leading to error accumulation in the 3D representation.

Method: Semantic Anchor Distillation (SAD) distills per-view visual embeddings into consensus text anchors for viewpoint-invariant semantics; Geo-Semantic Feedback Loop (GSFL) leverages the evolving 3D field to filter tracker anomalies and refine spatial mask assignments via a conservative three-gate update rule.

Result: SAD-GS achieves the best overall performance in open-vocabulary localization and semantic segmentation on LERF-OVS, 3D-OVS, and Mip-NeRF360 benchmarks.

Conclusion: Dynamic geo-semantic anchoring is effective and robust for reliable 3D semantic Gaussian field learning, consistently outperforming prior methods.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D semantic Gaussian field learning relies on multi-view 2D supervision, whose semantic targets and spatial assignments are often unreliable. Across varying viewpoints, view-dependent features cause semantic identity drift, while propagated tracker masks introduce boundary leakage and identity switches. Directly optimizing against these unreliable 2D targets forces the 3D representation to absorb multi-view contradictions, leading to severe error accumulation. To resolve this limitation, we propose SAD-GS, a framework for learning reliable 3D semantic Gaussian fields via dynamic geo-semantic anchoring. Specifically, Semantic Anchor Distillation (SAD) distills per-view visual embeddings into consensus text anchors to establish a viewpoint-invariant semantic identity. Concurrently, the Geo-Semantic Feedback Loop (GSFL) leverages the evolving 3D field to actively filter tracker anomalies and refine spatial mask assignments via a conservative three-gate update rule. Extensive evaluations on LERF-OVS, 3D-OVS, and Mip-NeRF360 show that SAD-GS consistently achieves the best overall performance in both open-vocabulary localization and semantic segmentation. These comprehensive improvements validate the effectiveness and robustness of dynamic geo-semantic anchoring for reliable 3D semantic Gaussian field learning.

</details>


### [115] [Event-VLA: Action-Conditioned Event Fusion for Robust Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29384)
*Jiaxin Liu,Xun Xu,Zhenhao Zhang,Hanqing Wang,Ruiqi Chen,Shi Chang,Weiyu Guo,Laurent Kneip*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Event-VLA proposes an event-enhanced VLA framework that uses event streams (illumination-robust, motion-sensitive) via an action-query routing pathway with gated cross-attention to improve robotic manipulation robustness under degraded lighting conditions, without disrupting pretrained RGB-language priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models assume well-lit, stable indoor settings, but real-world robotics face degraded RGB observations from illumination shifts, which critically challenges robustness. There is a gap in addressing VLA-based manipulation under varying visibility conditions.

Method: Event-VLA injects event streams through an action-query routing pathway: learnable action queries extract task-relevant semantics from VLA reasoning, and event tokens are selectively aggregated via gated cross-attention to construct event-aware action representations, avoiding direct fusion into the global semantic token space.

Result: In simulation and real-world deployment, Event-VLA maintains strong performance under normal lighting and improves success rates under low-light degradation and near-dark conditions.

Conclusion: Event-VLA demonstrates that incorporating event streams via a targeted action-query routing mechanism effectively enhances VLA robustness to illumination variations while preserving pretrained RGB-language priors, addressing a key limitation in real-world embodied manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have become an important paradigm of embodied AI. However, existing VLA models typically assume well-lit and stable indoor settings, while real-world embodied manipulation may involve degraded RGB observations caused by illumination shifts, posing critical challenges for robust robotic manipulation. To address this gap, we propose \textbf{Event-VLA}, an event-enhanced VLA framework for generalizable manipulation across varying illumination conditions. We formulate VLA-based manipulation under degraded visibility as a practical robustness problem for RGB-centric policies, and introduce event streams as an illumination-robust, motion-sensitive complementary observation to improve robustness across visibility levels. Specifically, unlike conventional multimodal fusion that directly merges event features into the global semantic token space, Event-VLA injects event information through an action-query routing pathway. It uses learnable action queries to extract task-relevant semantics from the VLA reasoning process, and selectively aggregates event tokens via gated cross-attention to construct event-aware action representations. This design preserves the pretrained RGB-language semantic priors while effectively leveraging event information for robust action prediction. Experiments in simulation and real-world deployment show that Event-VLA maintains strong manipulation performance under normal lighting and improves success rates under low-light degradation and near-dark real-world settings.

</details>


### [116] [NaLA: A 3D Native LLM Layout Agent for High-quality 3D Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29395)
*Cheng Wan,Yongsen Mao,Wenzheng Wu,Yuxuan Xie,Chucheng Xiang,Runze Wang,Xiang Zhang,Zhongyuan Liu,Rushi Dai,Yuan Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NaLA is a native 3D LLM layout agent that directly encodes 3D scene boundaries and assets into the LLM (avoiding text conversion modality loss), uses coarse-to-fine pose prediction (autoregressive discrete + continuous regression), and outperforms prior methods in quality and efficiency for 3D scene generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based layout agents convert 3D assets and layouts into text descriptions, causing severe information loss due to the modality gap between text and 3D, leading to implausible layout generation.

Method: NaLA encodes 3D scene boundaries and 3D assets directly into the LLM to preserve fine-grained geometry and enable explicit reasoning over spatial relationships (collisions, surface supporting, containment). It uses a coarse-to-fine prediction mechanism: first autoregressively predicts discrete poses, then refines them with continuous regression.

Result: NaLA outperforms prior layout agents in both generation quality and inference efficiency. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each component.

Conclusion: Direct 3D encoding into LLMs combined with coarse-to-fine pose prediction significantly improves layout plausibility and efficiency for 3D scene generation.

Abstract: Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as promising layout agents for 3D scene generation. Existing layout agents still suffer from implausible layout generation because most of them convert 3D assets and 3D layouts into textual descriptions as inputs and outputs, which involves severe information loss due to the modality gap between texts and 3D assets and 3D layouts. We propose NaLA, a native 3D LLM layout Agent for high-quality 3D scene generation by placing 3D assets in the scene. For the inputs, NaLA encodes 3D scene boundaries and 3D assets directly into the LLM, preserving fine-grained geometry and enabling explicit reasoning over relationships like collisions, surface supporting, and containment. To accurately output the positions and orientations of assets, NaLA adopts a coarse-to-fine prediction mechanism that first predicts discrete poses in an autoregressive manner and then refines the discrete poses with a continuous regression. Trained on diverse layout datasets, NaLA attains strong geometric perception and layout coherence. Experiments demonstrate that NaLA outperforms prior layout agents in both generation quality and inference efficiency, with comprehensive ablation studies to verify each component's effectiveness.

</details>


### [117] [FiRe: Frequency Reparameterization as a Preconditioner for Periodic Implicit Neural Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29414)
*Harinandan Shukla,Rajarshi Verma,Jitin Singla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FiRe (Frequency Reparameterization) improves periodic implicit neural representations by assigning each neuron an input-dependent frequency via a low-rank gating path, which acts as an implicit preconditioner that improves NTK conditioning at initialization, leading to faster convergence and higher PSNR (up to +1 dB) on 2D image fitting, especially under short training budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Periodic INRs (e.g., SIREN, FINER) assign every neuron the same global frequency, wasting representational capacity when local signal content varies. This paper aims to improve optimization efficiency and reconstruction quality by reparameterizing per-neuron frequencies.

Method: FiRe reparameterizes per-neuron frequency of periodic INRs via a separate low-rank gating path that produces bounded, input-dependent frequencies. The gate acts as an implicit preconditioner improving NTK conditioning at initialization. Applicable to any periodic activation function.

Result: On 2D image fitting, FiRe achieves up to +1 dB PSNR gain over parameter-matched baselines under short training budgets. Gains vary with resolution and diminish at full convergence. Performance trends are characterized w.r.t. resolution, rank, and budget.

Conclusion: FiRe enables faster convergence and better tracking of high-frequency content under fixed computational budget by improving initialization conditioning via NTK, with predictable performance trends.

Abstract: Periodic Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) such as SIREN and FINER assign every neuron, the same global frequency, spending the representational budget inefficiently when local signal content varies. We introduce FiRe (Frequency Reparameterization), that accelerates optimization by reparameterizing per-neuron frequency of periodic INRs without changing their underlying activation function. FiRe gives each neuron a bounded, input-dependent frequency via a separate low-rank gating path and is applicable to any periodic activation function. The gate acts as an implicit preconditioner that improves optimization conditioning at initialization via the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). This better-conditioned initialization makes optimization converge faster, and the high-frequency content of the reconstruction tracks the target more closely at a fixed computational budget. On 2D image fitting, FiRe increases PSNR over a parameter-matched baseline (up to +1 dB at short training budgets), with gains that vary with resolution and diminish at full convergence. We characterize how performance depends on resolution, rank, and training budget, and give an NTK account that predicts these trends.

</details>


### [118] [Tool-Augmented Spatiotemporal Reasoning for Streamlining Video Question Answering Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.10359)
*Sunqi Fan,Jiashuo Cui,Meng-Hao Guo,Shuojin Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Video Toolkit and a Spatiotemporal Reasoning Framework (STAR) to enhance Multimodal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) ability to perform spatiotemporal reasoning in Video Question Answering, achieving significant gains on VideoMME and LongVideoBench benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with simultaneously modeling spatial relationships within video frames and understanding temporal causal dynamics on complex, reasoning-intensive VideoQA tasks.

Method: The authors equip MLLM with a comprehensive and extensible Video Toolkit, and propose a Spatiotemporal Reasoning Framework (STAR) that strategically schedules temporal and spatial tools to progressively localize key areas in the video while avoiding toolchain shortcut issues.

Result: The STAR framework enhances GPT-4o using lightweight tools, achieving an 8.2% gain on VideoMME and 4.6% on LongVideoBench.

Conclusion: The proposed Video Toolkit and STAR framework represent an important step towards building autonomous and intelligent video analysis assistants.

Abstract: Video Question Answering (VideoQA) task serves as a critical playground for evaluating whether foundation models can effectively perceive, understand, and reason about dynamic real-world scenarios. However, existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with simultaneously modeling spatial relationships within video frames and understanding the causal dynamics of temporal evolution on complex and reasoning-intensive VideoQA task. In this work, we equip MLLM with a comprehensive and extensible Video Toolkit, to enhance MLLM's spatiotemporal reasoning capabilities and ensure the harmony between the quantity and diversity of tools. To better control the tool invocation sequence and avoid toolchain shortcut issues, we propose a Spatiotemporal Reasoning Framework (STAR) that strategically schedules temporal and spatial tools, thereby progressively localizing the key area in the video. Our STAR framework enhances GPT-4o using lightweight tools, achieving an 8.2% gain on VideoMME and 4.6% on LongVideoBench. We believe that our proposed Video Toolkit and STAR framework make an important step towards building autonomous and intelligent video analysis assistants. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/fansunqi/VideoTool.

</details>


### [119] [Can Machines Really See Objects in Images? A Study Based on Syntactic Distance and Visual Self-Referential Instances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29416)
*Xingyu Peng,Junran Wu,Yue Hou,Zhongliang Qiao,Jiaheng Liu,Shangzhe Li,Jichang Zhao,Wenjun Wu,Xianglong Liu,Yongxin Tong,Li Dong,Ke Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Current vision models fail on tasks requiring true global semantic understanding, collapsing to random guessing when local statistical shortcuts are removed, regardless of model size or training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether vision models truly understand objects (global semantics) or merely exploit surface-level local statistical cues for classification, following Wittgenstein's view that recognition is bounded by learned descriptive systems.

Method: Introduces syntactic distance to quantify class separability via symmetry of operations mapping one class to another. Constructs a visual self-referential task using maximum-variance binary noise where positive samples contain a closed square and negative samples have one flipped boundary pixel, yielding zero syntactic distance. Tests ResNets and Vision Transformers at varying image scales.

Result: Accuracy collapses to random guessing once image scale crosses a critical point, with no recovery in tested range. Larger training sets and models only delay collapse; globally attentive ViTs reach it earlier. A consistent phase-transition phenomenon is observed across architectures.

Conclusion: Current architectures have a structural capability boundary on global-concept tasks, suggesting general intelligence may require creating new descriptive language rather than reusing existing statistical patterns.

Abstract: Can a vision model truly see an object, or does it only fit surface-level visual cues? Following Wittgenstein's view that the limits of language are the limits of the world, we view a model's recognition ability as bounded by the descriptive system it has learned. In current vision models, this system is often realized through learned feature representations that exploit local statistical cues. We therefore ask whether a model can still classify correctly when such local cues provide no stable basis for distinction. We formalize this question with syntactic distance, which measures class separability through the symmetry of the operations mapping one class to the other: positive distance exposes exploitable local features, whereas zero distance requires global semantics rather than local rules. We construct a visual self-referential task in maximum-variance binary noise: positive samples contain a closed square, while negative samples contain an otherwise identical square with one flipped boundary pixel. The two classes differ in global semantics but have zero syntactic distance, making local statistical shortcuts unreliable. Experiments on ResNets and Vision Transformers reveal a consistent phase-transition phenomenon, with accuracy collapsing to random guessing once the image scale crosses a critical point and does not recover within the tested range. Larger training sets and models only delay this collapse, while globally attentive ViTs reach it earlier. These results reveal a structural capability boundary of current architectures on global-concept tasks, suggesting that general intelligence may require creating new language, not reusing an existing one.

</details>


### [120] [Bit-ViP: Leveraging Bit-planes to Preserve Visual Privacy in Images through Obfuscation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29417)
*Vishesh Kumar Tanwar,Ashish Gupta,Sanjay Madria,Sajal K. Das*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Bit-ViP is a bit-plane-based image obfuscation scheme that uses Lorenz's chaotic system and differential privacy to generate secure, non-invertible, and trainable obfuscated images for privacy-preserving activity recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image obfuscation methods are either vulnerable to model inversion attacks reconstructing original images or produce non-trainable obfuscated images that fail to achieve reasonable recognition accuracy.

Method: The proposed Bit-ViP scheme incorporates an end-to-end obfuscation function using bit-plane manipulation, Lorenz's chaotic system for non-invertible noise generation, and differential privacy mechanisms to produce secure yet usable obfuscated images.

Result: Experimental validation on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets demonstrates effectiveness, with rigorous security analysis showing improvements over existing schemes against reconstruction attacks, pixel frequency attacks, information entropy attacks, and pixel inter-correlation attacks.

Conclusion: Bit-ViP achieves both security (resistance to adversarial reconstruction) and usability (trainability for recognition tasks), addressing the key limitations of prior obfuscation approaches.

Abstract: The unprecedented growth of computer vision applications, such as surveillance systems and social media, raises security and visual privacy concerns, especially when data is stored on cloud servers. Image obfuscation offers a way to preserve visual privacy while maintaining an adequate level of usability; thus, it has been a topic of great interest in recent years. However, prior obfuscation schemes are either vulnerable to malicious attacks, such as model inversion to reconstruct original images from obfuscated images, or generate non-trainable obfuscated images, making them unusable for achieving reasonable accuracy. This paper proposes a novel bit-plane-based image obfuscation scheme, {\em Bit-ViP}, to preserve visual privacy for image-based recognition tasks. The Bit-ViP scheme produces secure, usable images by incorporating an innovative end-to-end obfuscation function. While doing so, the obfuscated image would contain non-invertible noise (generated by Lorenz's chaotic system and differential privacy), making it hard for an adversary to reconstruct the original image. We conduct extensive experiments on two popular activity recognition datasets, namely UCF101 and HMDB51, to validate the effectiveness of Bit-ViP. In the face of attacks on reconstruction, pixel frequency, information entropy, and pixel inter-correlation, we present a rigorous security analysis demonstrating tangible improvements over existing schemes.

</details>


### [121] [Robust Zero-shot Anomaly Detection under Limited Auxiliary Anomaly Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29428)
*Guanyu Lu,Fang Zhou,Cheqing Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DIVE introduces a shallow-and-deep text embedding injection strategy and a disentanglement mechanism to handle limited auxiliary anomaly priors in zero-shot anomaly detection, significantly improving performance over SOTA baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot anomaly detection models assume rich diversity in auxiliary anomaly data, which fails to handle the unpredictable variations in real-world target domains. This work addresses the previously unexplored scenario of limited auxiliary anomaly priors.

Method: (1) A shallow-and-deep text embedding injection strategy during visual encoding to learn generic anomaly concepts shared across domains. (2) A disentanglement mechanism to resolve suboptimal alignment between object-semantics-entangled visual embeddings and object-agnostic textual prompts.

Result: Under limited anomaly patterns, DIVE outperforms SOTA by up to 16.2% and 28.5% on classification metrics, and 23.4%, 24.1%, 47.0% on segmentation metrics (average over 12 datasets). It also maintains competitive performance when anomaly diversity is sufficient.

Conclusion: DIVE is the first approach to tackle zero-shot anomaly detection with limited anomaly priors, achieving robust generalization across diverse target domains while remaining competitive under sufficient anomaly diversity.

Abstract: Zero-shot anomaly detection aims to identify defects in arbitrary novel domains; however, existing models assume that the auxiliary data contains a rich diversity of anomalies, neglecting the far more complex and unpredictable variations in real-world target domains. This study introduces DIVE, the first approach to investigate the scenario of limited auxiliary anomaly priors and resolve the resulting substantial performance degradation. Through a shallow-and-deep text embedding injection strategy during visual encoding, DIVE learns to abstract generic anomaly concepts shared across the auxiliary training domain and diverse target domains. Moreover, we propose a disentanglement mechanism to tackle the suboptimal alignment between visual embeddings entangled with object semantics and object-agnostic textual prompts. Experiments demonstrate that, under the setting of limited anomaly patterns in auxiliary data, DIVE outperforms SOTA baselines by up to 16.2% and 28.5% on two classification metrics, and 23.4%, 24.1%, and 47.0% on three segmentation metrics, in terms of average performance across twelve datasets. Furthermore, it maintains highly competitive performance when auxiliary data exhibits sufficient anomaly diversity.

</details>


### [122] [EvLIR: Learning Illumination Residuals from Ordered Events for Low-Light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29430)
*Haoxian Zhou,Chuanzhi Xu,Langyi Chen,Pengfei Ye,Haodong Chen,Qiang Qu,Ali Anaissi,Weidong Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvLIR is a temporal-residual low-light image enhancement framework that uses event cameras and a Temporal Event Residual Module (TERM) with ConvGRU to model ordered event dynamics, achieving state-of-the-art results on 11 of 12 benchmark metric pairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-light image enhancement is ill-posed due to missing structure, saturated noise, and weak contrast. Event cameras offer high temporal resolution, but prior works fail to explicitly model the temporal evolution of event voxel channels within a window, weakening the temporal evidence.

Method: EvLIR preserves ordered temporal bins of event streams, introduces a Temporal Event Residual Module (TERM) with lightweight ConvGRU to encode short-window event dynamics, converts the temporal state into bounded illumination correction, and uses it for spatially adaptive photometric guidance in Retinex-style illumination estimation and reliability-aware image-event restoration.

Result: Achieves best results on 11 of 12 dataset-metric pairs across SDE and SDSD indoor/outdoor benchmarks, with average scores of 25.63 dB PSNR, 28.30 dB PSNR*, and 0.827 SSIM.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling temporal evolution of ordered events significantly improves low-light image enhancement, outperforming prior methods that treat event voxels as unordered or static feature stacks.

Abstract: Low-light image enhancement is severely ill-posed when the input frame contains missing structure, saturated noise, and weak local contrast. Event cameras provide asynchronous brightness-change observations with high temporal resolution, but prior works often treat voxel channels as an unordered or static feature stack before fusion, rather than explicitly modeling their within-window temporal evolution, weakening the temporal evidence that makes events useful. We propose EvLIR, a temporal-residual enhancement framework that learns illumination residuals from ordered events for low-light image enhancement. Given a low-light frame and its aligned event voxel, EvLIR preserves the ordered temporal bins of the event stream and introduces a Temporal Event Residual Module (TERM) to encode short-window event dynamics with a lightweight ConvGRU. The resulting temporal state is converted into a bounded illumination correction, which provides spatially adaptive photometric guidance for Retinex-style illumination estimation and subsequent reliability-aware image-event restoration. On SDE and SDSD indoor/outdoor benchmarks, EvLIR achieves the best result on eleven of twelve dataset-metric pairs, with average scores of 25.63~dB PSNR, 28.30~dB PSNR*, and 0.827 SSIM across the four benchmarks.

</details>


### [123] [Bridging VideoQA and Video-Guided Agentic Tasks via Generalized Keyframe Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29445)
*Sunqi Fan,Qingle Liu,Runqi Yin,Meng-Hao Guo,Shuojin Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces VG-GUIBench, a benchmark to evaluate whether MLLM-based GUI agents can follow video tutorials to complete interactive tasks, and proposes TASKER, a task-driven and scene-aware keyframe extraction algorithm that improves performance on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic tasks by 2.0% on EgoSchema and 1.8% on NExT-QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VideoQA benchmarks primarily evaluate shallow visual perception but rarely assess whether MLLMs can learn deeper knowledge or procedural skills from video tutorials and generalize them to downstream long-horizon agentic tasks. This gap motivated the creation of a benchmark to evaluate video-guided GUI agentic capabilities.

Method: The paper proposes VG-GUIBench for evaluating MLLM-based GUI agents on video-guided interactive tasks, and TASKER, a keyframe extraction algorithm that jointly considers task relevance and scene dynamics to identify informative frames.

Result: TASKER outperforms the best baseline by 2.0% on the EgoSchema fullset and 1.8% on the NExT-QA dataset, demonstrating significant performance improvements on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic task benchmarks.

Conclusion: Effective keyframe extraction is critical for both VideoQA and video-guided agentic tasks, and TASKER's results highlight the potential of generalized keyframe extraction methods for video understanding tasks.

Abstract: Video understanding is a fundamental capability for multimodal intelligence, and recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance on Video Question Answering (VideoQA) benchmarks. However, existing benchmarks primarily evaluate whether models can perceive shallow visual cues, while rarely examining whether MLLMs can learn deeper knowledge or procedural skills from video tutorials and generalize them to downstream long-horizon agentic tasks. To address this gap, we introduce VG-GUIBench (Video-Guided GUI Benchmark), a new benchmark designed to evaluate whether MLLM-based GUI agents can follow video tutorials to complete corresponding GUI interactive tasks. Furthermore, we observe that the performance of models on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic tasks critically depends on effective keyframe extraction. Based on this observation, we propose TASKER (Task-driven And Scene-aware Keyframe searchER), a keyframe extraction algorithm that jointly considers task relevance and scene dynamics to identify informative frames. Experimental results demonstrate that TASKER achieves significant performance improvements on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic task benchmarks, outperforming the best baseline by 2.0% on the EgoSchema fullset and 1.8% on the NExT-QA dataset, respectively. These results further highlight the potential of generalized keyframe extraction methods for video understanding tasks. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/VG-GUI-TASKER/VG-GUI-TASKER.

</details>


### [124] [Miti360: A Comprehensive Dataset for Improved Reforestation Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29447)
*Cedric Kiplimo,Samuel Mbatia,Ciira wa Maina,Arthur Sichangi,Dennis Gitundu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Miti360 is a comprehensive reforestation monitoring dataset from Kenya's Kieni Forest (2023-2025), featuring aerial/terrestrial imagery, ground truth data, and weather data. Fine-tuning DeepForest on Miti360 improved box precision by 12% and recall by 69%, addressing the critical geographic gap in African forestry ML training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ML training datasets for forest monitoring lack geographic diversity, particularly missing African forestry data. This gap limits the applicability of ML systems for reforestation monitoring in Sub-Saharan Africa.

Method: Data was collected from a 770-ha reforested section of Kieni Forest, Kenya (March 2023–February 2025). Aerial surveys used DJI Mavic 2 Pro with Agisoft Metashape stitching and ArcGIS Pro tiling. Terrestrial captures used smartphones and custom stereo cameras. The dataset includes orthophotos with tree bounding box annotations, terrestrial images, tree biophysical parameters, species, GPS coordinates, and historical weather data.

Result: Fine-tuning the DeepForest model on Miti360 improved box precision by 12% and box recall by 69% for tree crown tracking across three years.

Conclusion: Miti360 enables ML training for tree censuses, species-geography matching, growth-weather modeling, and digital twin frameworks, advancing reforestation monitoring in underrepresented Sub-Saharan African regions.

Abstract: Over the past decade, interest in applying machine learning (ML) to automate forest monitoring has grown significantly. However, existing training datasets are predominantly drawn from North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, leaving a critical gap in African forestry data. To address this limited geographic diversity, we present Miti360, a comprehensive dataset for reforestation monitoring that comprises high-resolution imagery, ground truth data, and longitudinal weather data. Data collection occurred within a 770-ha reforested section of the Kieni Forest in Kenya between March 2023 and February 2025. Miti360 comprises aerial photos (orthophotos and tiles) with tree bounding box annotations, terrestrial images (single and stereo), and detailed data records including tree biophysical parameters, species, and GPS coordinates, alongside historical weather data. Aerial surveys utilized a DJI Mavic 2 Pro, with imagery stitched via Agisoft Metashape and tiled using ArcGIS Pro, while terrestrial captures used smartphones and custom stereo cameras. Miti360 enables the training of ML systems for tasks such as accelerating tree censuses, matching species to geographical areas, modelling growth based on weather conditions, and developing digital twin frameworks. Models can be trained on Miti360 to address challenges specific to Sub-Saharan Africa, ultimately advancing reforestation monitoring and fostering sustainable forestry practices in underrepresented regions. We demonstrate the utility of this dataset by successfully tracking tree crowns across three years and improving the DeepForest model's box precision and box recall by 12% and 69% respectively through fine-tuning on Miti360.

</details>


### [125] [The Platonic Defense: Backdoor Defense for Self-Supervised Encoders in the Era of Large Scale Pre-training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29451)
*Tuo Chen,Minjing Dong,Benlei Cui,Jian Liu,Jie Gui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Platonic Representation Defense, a black-box test-time defense against backdoor attacks on SSL models, using an energy function trained via noise-contrastive estimation and denoising score matching to detect and purify poisoned representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised learning models are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, and existing defenses fail in fully black-box settings as they require labels, attack patterns, or training data.

Method: A conditional energy function is defined over source representations and reference representations, trained via noise-contrastive estimation for detection and denoising score matching for purification, inspired by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis.

Result: Effective on multiple SSL encoders and over 10 attacks, achieving substantial performance gains in both representation detection and purification.

Conclusion: The proposed attack-agnostic, model-agnostic, and modality-agnostic defense paradigm successfully defends against backdoor attacks in black-box settings, with theoretical guarantees linking energy gap to mutual information.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) pretrained models have become a dominant paradigm for visual representation learning, but they are vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing defenses struggle to defend against such attacks in a fully black-box setting because they often require access to labels, attack patterns, or training data. To tackle this issue, we propose a new attack-agnostic, model-agnostic, and modality-agnostic black-box test-time defense paradigm, called \emph{Platonic Representation Defense}. It is inspired by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, which suggests that large-scale independently trained encoders converge toward compatible projections of the same underlying reality. We formalize this idea as a conditional energy function defined over source representations and a set of reference representations. The energy function is trained for detection through noise-contrastive estimation and for representation purification through denoising score matching. Theoretically, the energy gap between matched and mismatched samples is lower bounded by the mutual information between source and reference representations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on multiple self-supervised encoders and more than 10 attacks. The method can perform both representation detection and purification, and achieves substantial performance gains across multiple attacks. Code is available \href{https://github.com/jsrdcht/Platonic-Representation-Defense}{here}.

</details>


### [126] [From Phase to Phenomenon: Self-Supervised Learning of Subsurface Scattering with Minimal Phase-shift Inputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29461)
*Arjun Majumdar,Raphael Braun,Andreas Engelhardt,Hendrik PA. Lensch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised pretraining framework for learning sub-surface scattering light transport using only 8 phase-shift profilometry images per view, with a tailored augmentation strategy that outperforms standard SSL augmentations and enables high-fidelity reconstruction of complex SSS effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To learn generalizable sub-surface scattering representations from minimal input (only 8 images per view), drastically reducing the image acquisition cost compared to prior SSS acquisition methods that require orders of magnitude more images.

Method: A self-supervised pretraining framework using a stereo projector-camera setup capturing 8 high-frequency PSP images per view. It employs a tailored augmentation strategy for PSP-based SSS data and a dedicated cost function for anisotropic scattering footprint reconstruction, combined with an encoder-decoder architecture for representation learning and dense response reconstruction.

Result: The pretrained encoder learns generalizable SSS representations that transfer well to spatially varying relighting and kNN classification tasks. The model achieves high-fidelity reconstructions on unseen objects with complex geometry and materials, using orders of magnitude fewer images than prior methods.

Conclusion: Self-supervised pretraining with PSP-specific augmentations and a dedicated cost function enables efficient SSS light transport learning from minimal input, significantly outperforming standard SSL augmentations and prior acquisition-heavy approaches.

Abstract: We propose a self-supervised pretraining framework for learning sub-surface scattering (SSS) light transport representations from minimal input. Our method leverages a stereo projector-camera setup that captures only eight high-frequency phase-shift profilometry (PSP) images per view to pretrain an encoder in a multi-view, multi-object setting. We introduce a tailored augmentation strategy for PSP-based SSS data, and show that it significantly outperforms standard ImageNet-style augmentations for SSL pretraining. The pretrained encoder learns generalizable SSS representations that transfer effectively to downstream tasks, including spatially varying relighting and representation evaluation using a kNN classifier. Combined with a decoder, the model reconstructs dense scattering footprint responses, trained using a dedicated cost function that improves accuracy, particularly for anisotropic footprints. Despite using only eight input images per view, our approach generalizes to unseen objects with complex geometry and material properties, achieving high-fidelity reconstructions while requiring orders of magnitude fewer images than prior methods.

</details>


### [127] [MIRROR: Aligning Semantic Relations from Language to Image via Gromov--Wasserstein](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29462)
*Hong-Han Wang,Yuntao Wang,Hu Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MIRROR introduces a geometric regularization framework (Semi-Inverse Gromov-Wasserstein) that transfers relational priors from language to visual representations in MLLMs, addressing the failure of projection-based alignment to preserve inter-concept relationships across modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs inherit rich relational priors from language but fail to apply them in visual contexts because projection-based alignment trains visual tokens to carry correct semantics without preserving relationships between concepts when crossing from language to vision.

Method: Proposes MIRROR using a Semi-Inverse Gromov-Wasserstein (SI-GW) problem as a surrogate loss to align visual representations with language-derived relational priors. It has a unique closed-form solution prescribing ideal visual relational structure, with layer/head/token-level strategies for decoder-only Transformers without extra parameters or inference cost.

Result: MIRROR improves relational consistency while preserving performance on general vision-language tasks.

Conclusion: The SI-GW formulation effectively transfers language relational priors to visual representations, addressing a structural blind spot in MLLM alignment, with efficient computation suitable for long token sequences.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) inherit rich relational priors from their language backbones, yet often fail when asked to apply these relationships in visual contexts. We trace this failure to a structural blind spot: projection-based alignment trains each visual token to carry the right semantics, but never asks whether the relationships between concepts survive the crossing from language to vision. To address this, we propose MIRROR (Mapping Inter-concept Relations from language to visual Representation via Optimal-transport-based Regularization), a geometric regularization framework that transfers relational priors from language to vision by exploiting the rich relational structure encoded in language representations. Specifically, we derive a surrogate loss from the proposed Semi-Inverse Gromov-Wasserstein (SI-GW) problem, an inverse geometric problem that aligns visual representations with language-derived relational priors. We show that this formulation admits a unique closed-form solution that prescribes the ideal visual relational structure implied by language geometry and cross-modal coupling. The structure of the formulation also enables efficient computation, making it applicable to long token sequences. Applying SI-GW inside decoder-only Transformers requires careful design. We introduce targeted strategies at the layer, head, and token levels to ensure stable extraction without additional parameters or inference cost. MIRROR improves relational consistency while preserving performance on general vision-language tasks.

</details>


### [128] [CellDETR: A Detection-Guided Framework for Scalable Cell Representation Learning from Histopathology Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29463)
*Shikang Zhang,Guojun Li,Yicong Mao,Chulin Sha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CellDETR is a detection-guided framework based on Deformable DETR for scalable cell-level representation learning from whole-slide images. It introduces location feature decoupling and box-constrained attention, outperforms SOTA on PanNuke cell classification, supports contrastive pretraining from unlabeled WSIs, and demonstrates cross-dataset transferability using Xenium spatial transcriptomics annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cell-level representation learning from WSIs remains underexplored despite progress in patch and slide-level methods, limiting cell-resolved interpretability, biological discovery, and clinical translation.

Method: CellDETR builds on Deformable DETR with location feature decoupling and box-constrained attention. It incorporates contrastive learning for pretraining on unlabeled WSIs and can leverage Xenium spatial transcriptomics-derived cell annotations for cross-dataset transfer.

Result: Outperforms existing SOTA in supervised cell classification on PanNuke; improves downstream classification via contrastive pretraining; achieves accurate cross-dataset classification after pretraining with spatial transcriptomics annotations.

Conclusion: CellDETR provides a scalable route toward a general cell-level representation learning framework for interpretable computational pathology.

Abstract: Recent advances in pathology foundation models have substantially improved patch and slide level representation learning from whole-slide images (WSIs).However, cell-level representations learning remain underexplored, limiting cell resolved interpretability, biological discovery, and clinical translation. We propose CellDETR, a detection-guided framework built on Deformable DETR for scalable cell representation learning from WSIs. By introducing location feature decoupling and box-constrained attention mechanism, CellDETR enables automated extraction of cell-level embeddings, and outperform existing state-of-the-art methods in supervised cell classification on PanNuke data. In addition, by incorporating contrastive learning design, we build a CellDETR-based pretraining model for scalable cell representation learning from unlabeled WSIs, which improves downstream cell classification performance. Furthermore, we show that after pretraining with Xenium spatial transcriptomics-derived cell annotations, CellDETR achieves accurate cross-dataset cell classification, demonstrating the transferability and biological relevance of the learned cell embeddings. Together, CellDETR provides a scalable route toward general cell-level representation learning framework for interpretable computational patholog

</details>


### [129] [Rank-Aware Hyperbolic Alignment for Vision-Language Dataset Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29464)
*Jongoh Jeong,Sun-Kyung Lee,Kuk-Jin Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAHA is a rank-aware hyperbolic alignment method for vision-language dataset distillation that uses hyperbolic geometry and asymmetric objectives to align shared semantics while preserving modality-private diversity, achieving competitive cross-modal retrieval and improved transfer robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLDD methods enforce full-dimensional alignment in Euclidean space, which is overly restrictive due to rank-deficient image-text correlation where shared semantics lie in a low-dimensional subspace and residual variation is weakly correlated. LoRS uses low-rank factorization but lacks explicit control over dominant alignment capacity and structure.

Method: RAHA lifts multimodal representations to hyperbolic space and optimizes distilled pairs with asymmetric objectives: enforcing geodesic alignment in the shared range while regularizing the residual subspace to preserve modality-private diversity and improve transfer robustness.

Result: RAHA demonstrates competitive cross-modal retrieval and improved transfer indicators under fixed data and compute budgets on benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: Rank-aware hyperbolic alignment with explicit capacity control and hierarchical geometry is more effective for VLDD than Euclidean full-dimensional or low-rank alignment methods.

Abstract: Vision-language dataset distillation (VLDD) compresses a large image-text paired dataset into a small set of synthetic pairs that can efficiently train contrastive vision-language models under strict data and compute budgets.
