Most existing methods match expert trajectories or cross-modal statistics, yet still enforce full-dimensional alignment in a Euclidean embedding space. This is often overly restrictive due to rank-deficient image--text correlation, with shared semantics concentrated in a low-dimensional range and remaining variation spread across a weakly correlated residual subspace. LoRS relaxes alignment at the similarity level by low-rank factorization, but does not explicitly control dominant alignment capacity and structure in the representation space. We thus propose a rank-aware hyperbolic alignment (RAHA) that combines hierarchical geometry with explicit alignment-capacity control. RAHA lifts multimodal representations to hyperbolic space and optimizes distilled pairs with asymmetric objectives that enforce geodesic alignment in the shared range while regularizing the residual subspace to preserve modality-private diversity and improve transfer robustness. Experiments on benchmarks show that RAHA demonstrates competitive cross-modal retrieval and improved transfer indicators under fixed budgets.

</details>


### [130] [MAVIN: Multi-Shot Audio-Visual Generation with Narrative Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29473)
*Kaiqi Liu,Yunyao Mao,Ziqi Cai,Zheng Geng,Jing Wang,Qiulin Wang,Xintao Wang,Pengfei Wan,Kun Gai,Shuchen Weng,Boxin Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAVIN is the first framework for multi-shot audio-visual generation with customized narrative control, addressing temporal misalignment, limited controllability, and incomplete scripting via boundary-aware attention, ID-aware propagation, and a multi-agent scripting pipeline, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models produce high-fidelity videos but struggle with coherent multi-shot audio-visual generation requiring complex narrative control, suffering from temporal misalignment, limited controllability, and incomplete scripting.

Method: Proposes three key components: (1) boundary-aware attention with hierarchical captions and boundary-aware token routing to render audio-visual elements within temporal boundaries; (2) ID-aware propagation using identity embeddings and an identity-aware mask to bind identities to consistent visual appearances and vocal timbres; (3) a multi-agent scripting pipeline to transform free-form user inputs into hierarchical captions. Also constructs MAVINSet, a multi-shot audio-visual dataset.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multi-shot audio-visual generation tasks, enabling integration of generative models into professional filmmaking workflows.

Conclusion: MAVIN opens a new avenue for integrating generative models into professional filmmaking by enabling coherent multi-shot audio-visual generation with narrative control.

Abstract: While recent generative models produce high-fidelity videos, they struggle with the complex narrative control required for coherent multi-shot audio-visual generation. Existing methods suffer from temporal misalignment, limited controllability, and incomplete scripting. In this paper, we propose MAVIN, the first framework for multi-shot audio-visual generation with customized narrative control. To resolve temporal misalignment, we propose boundary-aware attention, which leverages hierarchical captions and boundary-aware token routing to render audio-visual elements within their respective temporal boundaries. To improve the controllability for multi-subject scenarios, we propose ID-aware propagation, utilizing identity embeddings and an identity-aware mask to bind specific identities to consistent visual appearances and vocal timbres. To provide comprehensive audio-visual narratives, we present a multi-agent scripting pipeline to transform free-form user inputs into hierarchical captions. Furthermore, we construct MAVINSet, a multi-shot audio-visual dataset for robust training and evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MAVIN achieves state-of-the-art performance, opening up a new avenue for integrating generative models into professional filmmaking workflows.

</details>


### [131] [VCS-SLAM: Geometry-Validated Semantic Evidence Fusion for 3D Gaussian SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29494)
*Raman Jha,Shuaihang Yuan,Yi Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VCS-SLAM improves semantic 3D Gaussian SLAM by evaluating geometric reliability of semantic priors (visibility consistency, boundary evidence, ray conflict) to reduce artifacts, achieving better semantic consistency and tracking on Replica and ScanNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual SLAM performance degrades in complex environments because semantic priors used in 3D Gaussian SLAM are not equally reliable—occlusions, unsupported boundaries, and ambiguous ray geometry introduce persistent artifacts when uniform optimization weights are applied.

Method: VCS-SLAM proposes a geometry-validated semantic evidence fusion framework that assesses geometric reliability via three criteria: visibility consistency, surface-supported boundary evidence, and ray-level conflict uncertainty. A reliability-aware objective suppresses occluded updates, reduces semantic bleeding, and delays label assignment in ambiguous regions.

Result: On Replica, VCS-SLAM shows improved semantic consistency, boundary preservation, and reconstruction quality. On ScanNet, it maintains competitive tracking performance under real RGB-D inputs.

Conclusion: Explicitly validating geometric reliability of semantic priors instead of treating them uniformly is an effective strategy to reduce artifacts and improve both semantic mapping and tracking in 3D Gaussian SLAM.

Abstract: Visual SLAM performance often deteriorates in complex real-world applications. Semantic 3D Gaussian SLAM commonly fuses 2D semantic priors into a persistent 3D map using uniform optimization weights. However, such priors are not equally reliable in online mapping: occlusions, unsupported semantic boundaries, and ambiguous ray geometry can introduce persistent semantic artifacts into the global Gaussian map. We propose VCS-SLAM, a geometry-validated semantic evidence fusion framework for RGB-D 3D Gaussian SLAM. Instead of treating all semantic observations as uniformly valid supervision, VCS-SLAM evaluates their geometric reliability through visibility consistency, surface-supported boundary evidence, and ray-level conflict uncertainty. The resulting reliability-aware objective suppresses occluded semantic updates, reduces unsupported semantic bleeding, and delays premature label assignment in ambiguous regions. Experiments on Replica demonstrate improved semantic consistency, boundary preservation, and reconstruction quality. Results on ScanNet further show that VCS-SLAM maintains competitive tracking performance under real RGB-D inputs

</details>


### [132] [Rectifying Mask via Entropy for Distractor-Free 3DGS in Ambiguous Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29496)
*Wongi Park,Jiyeon Lim,Minjae Lee,Myeongseok Nam,Seongjun Choi,Jungwoo Kim,Soomok Lee,William J. Beksi,SangHyun Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RefineSplat proposes an entropy-aware adaptive masking and density control framework for distractor-free novel view synthesis, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets including a newly introduced Ambiguous wild dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle to distinguish transient elements (distractors) from static scenes due to color or semantic ambiguity, limiting the quality of novel view synthesis in real-world scenes with diverse ambiguous distractors.

Method: (1) Entropy-aware adaptive masking that captures ambiguous distractors by combining entropy with instance masks; (2) Entropy-aware density control that aligns Gaussians in ambiguous scenarios using entropy-aware positional gradients; (3) Introduction of the Ambiguous wild dataset (18 scenes) with color/semantic ambiguity between distractors and static scenes.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on various datasets for distractor-free novel view synthesis, with rigorous validation on the newly created Ambiguous wild dataset.

Conclusion: RefineSplat effectively removes diverse ambiguous distractors for high-quality novel view synthesis, outperforming existing approaches that cannot handle color or semantic ambiguity.

Abstract: We present RefineSplat, a systematic framework that effectively constructs transient masks to identify diverse ambiguous distractors. To do this, we qualitatively and quantitatively analyze issues and propose a novel entropy-aware adaptive masking method. Unlike existing approaches that struggle to distinguish transient elements from static scenes due to color or semantic ambiguity, RefineSplat captures ambiguous distractors leveraging entropy and instance masks. Furthermore, we propose a simple yet effective entropy-aware density control to align Gaussians in ambiguous scenarios considering Entropy-aware positional gradients. Additionally, to rigorously validate our method, we first create and release the Ambiguous wild dataset, including 18 scenes where distractors and static scenes are hard to distinguish due to color or semantic resemblances. Experimental results on various datasets demonstrate that RefineSplat shows state-of-the-art performance, showing distractor-free novel view synthesis.

</details>


### [133] [Learning Where and When: Patch-Based Spatiotemporal Localization in Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29498)
*Hamza Karim,Nghia Nguyen,Lokman Bekit,Yasin Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a patch-based spatiotemporal framework for weakly supervised video anomaly detection that jointly localizes anomalies in both space (where) and time (when), using a Proximity-Aware Top-k selection strategy to generate fine-grained spatial anomaly maps without bounding-box supervision, achieving state-of-the-art results and releasing new bounding-box annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WSVAD methods focus primarily on temporal anomaly localization (when anomalies occur) while neglecting spatial localization (where anomalies occur in frames), limiting interpretability and practical deployment in real-world settings.

Method: A patch-based spatiotemporal framework operating on grid-level patch features under a multiple instance learning paradigm, with a Proximity-Aware Top-k spatiotemporal selection strategy that generates fine-grained spatial anomaly maps without requiring bounding-box supervision during training.

Result: Surpasses existing state-of-the-art approaches across multiple benchmarks with substantial gains in spatiotemporal localization accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively enables joint spatial and temporal anomaly localization in a weakly supervised setting, and the release of frame-level bounding-box annotations and code will facilitate future research in spatially grounded WSVAD.

Abstract: Weakly supervised video anomaly detection (WSVAD) has predominantly focused on temporal localization, identifying when anomalies occur while largely neglecting their spatial extent within frames. Yet, spatial localization is essential for interpretability and practical deployment in real-world settings. We introduce a patch-based spatiotemporal framework for weakly supervised anomaly localization that jointly models where and when anomalies occur. Our approach operates on grid-level patch features and learns region-level anomaly scores under a multiple instance learning paradigm. We further propose a Proximity-Aware Top-k spatiotemporal selection strategy that enables the model to generate fine-grained spatial anomaly maps without requiring bounding-box supervision during training. Our method surpasses existing state-of-the-art approaches across multiple benchmarks, yielding substantial gains in spatiotemporal localization accuracy. In addition, we release frame-level bounding-box annotations for the test sets of two widely used datasets, along with our code and pretrained models, providing new resources to facilitate future research in spatially grounded WSVAD.

</details>


### [134] [Empirical Evaluation of Multi-Modal Touch Detection in Over-the-Shoulder Video Surveillance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29504)
*Mohammadreza Rashidi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates a multi-modal Behavioral Intelligence framework for reconstructing keystroke events from over-the-shoulder video footage of mobile keypad interactions. The system combines MediaPipe hand landmarks, HSV skin filtering, frame differencing, and Canny edge detection. Testing on staged passcode entry video yields low F1-scores (16.7-18.5%) and near-zero sequence similarity (3.0%). Generalization to real-world footage fails entirely, with skin filtering producing massive false positives (up to 205 detections per frame). The system does not achieve reliable keystroke reconstruction outside calibrated staged settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore Video Intelligence Surveillance (VIDINT) as a non-invasive method for monitoring human-computer interaction patterns and reconstructing keystroke events from physical finger interactions captured in over-the-shoulder footage, without requiring direct screen recording access.

Method: A multi-modal Behavioral Intelligence (BEHINT) framework integrating four parallel detection modalities: (1) MediaPipe anatomical hand landmarks, (2) HSV skin color filtering, (3) temporal frame differencing for motion detection, and (4) shape-guided Canny edge analysis. Relative touch coordinates are mapped to a reference screen layout for sequence reconstruction. Evaluated via ablation, resolution decay, noise sensitivity, and proximity threshold tuning.

Result: On a 120-frame staged passcode entry video: MediaPipe and Skin Detection fail autonomously; Motion-Only F1 = 18.5%, Edge-Only F1 = 18.2%; combined multi-modal F1 = 16.7% with 3.0% sequence similarity. On real third-person phone videos: median 57 false positive touch points per frame (peak 205), one to three orders of magnitude above actual tap rates. System fails to generalize beyond calibrated staged settings.

Conclusion: The proposed keystroke reconstruction framework shows marginal performance in controlled staged settings and fails entirely when applied to uncontrolled real-world footage. The core limitation is that skin filtering detects the entire hand rather than fingertip contact points, making reliable keystroke reconstruction from over-the-shoulder video infeasible with current approaches.

Abstract: Video Intelligence Surveillance (VIDINT) on over-the-shoulder footage is a proposed vector for monitoring human-computer interaction patterns without direct screen recording access. In this paper, we evaluate a Behavioral Intelligence (BEHINT) touch-detection framework designed to reconstruct keystroke events on mobile keypad interfaces from physical finger interactions. Our system integrates four parallel detection modalities: (1) anatomical hand landmarks via MediaPipe, (2) HSV skin color filtering, (3) temporal frame differencing for motion detection, and (4) shape-guided Canny edge analysis. We map relative touch coordinates to a reference screen layout to reconstruct typing sequences. Evaluation on a 120-frame first-person staged video of passcode entry reveals that while MediaPipe and Skin Detection fail to run autonomously due to partial hand occlusion and ambient noise, Motion-Only and Edge-Only configurations achieve F1-scores of 18.5% and 18.2%, respectively. The combined multi-modal configuration achieves an F1-score of 16.7% and a sequence similarity of 3.0% when mapped to the iOS passcode layout. We conduct ablation, resolution decay, noise sensitivity, and proximity threshold tuning to characterize the system's operational envelope. We then audit generalization on 5 real, publicly licensed third-person phone videos and find that the detector emits a median of 57 touch points per frame (peaking at 205), one to three orders of magnitude more than the rate of real taps, because the skin filter responds to the whole hand rather than to fingertip contact. The staged keystroke result does not survive contact with uncontrolled footage; the system does not achieve reliable keystroke reconstruction outside the calibrated staged setting.

</details>


### [135] [Benchmark AUC Is Not Deployable Reliability: A Cross-Dataset Audit of Off-the-Shelf Features for Surveillance Video Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29506)
*Mohammadreza Rashidi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AI surveillance anomaly detection achieves high same-dataset AUC (~0.704) but collapses to chance (0.499) in cross-dataset settings; DINOv2 shows the largest drop; false-alarm rates reach ~31,931/hour; benchmark numbers overstate real-world reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To audit whether the high ROC-AUC scores reported for video anomaly detection generalize across different cameras and scenes, or are artifacts of training and testing on the same dataset.

Method: Built an unsupervised normality model using all-normal training frames from one dataset, frozen embeddings (CLIP, DINOv2, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0), and a nearest-neighbour distance; evaluated same-dataset and cross-dataset on UCSD Ped1/2, CUHK Avenue, ShanghaiTech; also tested PaDiM-style Mahalanobis detector as a control.

Result: Same-dataset AUC averages 0.704; cross-dataset AUC averages 0.499 (chance). DINOv2 has best same-dataset (0.901 on Ped2) but largest cross-dataset drop. Mahalanobis detector reproduces pattern (gap 0.202 vs 0.208). False-alarm rate ~31,931/hour at favorable operating point.

Conclusion: Benchmark numbers for surveillance anomaly detection describe a calibrated laboratory setting and substantially overstate deployable reliability; cross-scene generalization is at chance level.

Abstract: Automated "suspicious behavior" flagging is a headline promise of AI surveillance, and the field reports high frame-level ROC-AUC on standard video anomaly detection benchmarks. Those numbers are measured by training and testing on the same camera and scene. We audit what happens when that assumption is dropped. We build an unsupervised normality model from the all-normal training frames of one dataset, using frozen off-the-shelf embeddings (CLIP, DINOv2, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0) and a nearest-neighbour distance, and score the test frames of the same and of other datasets. Across 4 real datasets (UCSD Ped1, UCSD Ped2, CUHK Avenue, ShanghaiTech) and 4 backbones, same-dataset AUC averages 0.704 but cross-dataset AUC averages 0.499, which is chance: a detector calibrated on one scene is no better than a coin flip on another, and in several pairs it is below chance. The strongest backbone makes this worse, not better: DINOv2 has the best same-dataset AUC (up to 0.901 on Ped2) and the largest cross-dataset drop. The collapse is not an artefact of the scoring rule: replacing the nearest-neighbour detector with a PaDiM-style Mahalanobis detector reproduces it almost exactly (cross-dataset gap 0.202 versus 0.208). Even at a favourable operating point the false-alarm rate is on the order of 31,931 per hour. We conclude that the benchmark numbers quoted for surveillance anomaly detection describe a calibrated laboratory setting and overstate deployable reliability by a wide margin, and we release the code that reproduces every number.

</details>


### [136] [MotionAtlas: Detailed Region Captioning for Motion-Centric Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29531)
*Weisong Liu,Haochen Wang,Kuan Gao,Yuhao Wang,Yikang Zhou,Zhongwei Ren,Jacky Mai,Anna Wang,Yanwei Li,Jason Li,Zhaoxiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotionAtlas is a system for region-aware motion captioning in videos, comprising a human-annotated benchmark (MotionAtlas-Bench with 2,073 MCQs), a scalable pipeline producing 159k high-quality training samples via self-bootstrap refinement, and a family of Video-MLLMs that achieve consistent gains (e.g., 5.2% improvement over Qwen3-VL-4B) by focusing on spatiotemporally masked regions to avoid visual clutter and motion entanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion captioning datasets and models focus on global video-level descriptions, which suffer from visual clutter and motion entanglement across multiple objects. The authors aim to enable region-aware, fine-grained motion understanding by requiring models to describe motion only within a specified spatiotemporal mask, allowing reliable and quantifiable evaluation.

Method: (1) MotionAtlas-Bench: a benchmark of 2,073 multiple-choice questions on curated motion-centric videos, with meticulous human annotations for fine-grained motion evaluation. (2) A scalable data pipeline using self-bootstrap refinement to suppress hallucinations, producing 159k high-quality motion captioning samples. (3) A tailored training data composition strategy applied to multiple Video-MLLM baselines (Molmo2, Qwen3-VL).

Result: The proposed training strategy yields consistent and substantial gains across diverse Video-MLLMs. MotionAtlas-4B outperforms Qwen3-VL-4B by an average of 5.2 percentage points across general motion benchmarks.

Conclusion: Region-aware motion captioning effectively alleviates visual clutter and motion entanglement, leading to better fine-grained motion understanding. The benchmark, dataset, and code are publicly released to facilitate future research.

Abstract: We propose MotionAtlas, a system for detailed captioning of motion-centric videos, comprising (1) a dedicated human-annotated benchmark, (2) a scalable, high-quality pipeline to construct training samples, and (3) a family of powerful Video-MLLMs. Unlike conventional global motion captioning datasets, we focus on region-aware motion captioning: given a video and a spatiotemporal mask, the model generates precise descriptions of motion within the target region, thereby alleviating visual clutter and motion entanglement and enabling reliable, quantifiable evaluation. Concretely, we first build MotionAtlas-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 2,073 multiple-choice questions, meticulously annotated for a curated set of high-quality, motion-centric videos, to evaluate fine-grained motion understanding of the objects in question. Second, we design a rigorous and scalable data pipeline that leverages self-bootstrap refinement to suppress fine-grained hallucinations, yielding 159k high-quality motion captioning data. Third, we design a tailored training data composition strategy, which achieves consistent and substantial performance gains across diverse baseline Video-MLLMs, including Molmo2 and Qwen3-VL. For instance, MotionAtlas-4B surpasses Qwen3-VL-4B by an average of 5.2 percentage points across general motion benchmarks. The benchmark, dataset, and code have been released.

</details>


### [137] [GarmentZoom: Generating Zoomable Images from Garment Listings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29535)
*Renjie Zhao,Jingwei Ma,Huy Huynh Cao,Brian Curless,Steven M. Seitz,Ira Kemelmacher-Shlizerman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GarmentZoom is a system that enhances full-view garment photos to match close-up detail fidelity using a single model trained for continuous 3-20× scale factors without requiring spatial alignment or per-instance fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online garment listings show separate overview and close-up photos, forcing users to switch between views and disrupting browsing continuity. The goal is to enable seamless zoom-and-pan exploration from the full view alone.

Method: A single model trained to support a continuous range of scale factors (3-20×) across diverse garments, synthesizing details without requiring spatial alignment between the full-view and close-up reference images, avoiding per-instance fine-tuning.

Result: The approach matches the quality of per-instance methods while requiring only a fraction of the training cost.

Conclusion: GarmentZoom enables seamless zoom-and-pan exploration of garment photos by enhancing full-view images to close-up fidelity without the need for alignment or costly per-instance optimization, offering an efficient alternative to standard reference-based super-resolution approaches.

Abstract: Online product listings for garments often include an overview photo and a close-up to show garment details. However, each photo focuses on either field of view or garment detail, forcing users to alternate between views and breaking browsing continuity. We present GarmentZoom, a system that enhances the full-view photo to match the fidelity of its accompanying close-up, enabling seamless zoom-and-pan exploration. Unlike standard reference-based super-resolution, our setting involves close-up references that are spatially unaligned with the full view, and scale factors that vary substantially across garments 3-20$\times$. Prior work typically relies on alignment to transfer details or requires per-instance fine-tuning to memorize them. Instead, we train a single model that supports a continuous range of scales across diverse garments. Our approach synthesizes details without requiring spatial alignment and matches the quality of per-instance methods with a fraction of the training cost.

</details>


### [138] [Reliability-Prioritized Fine-Grained Generation in Multimodal Large](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29573)
*Xiaomeng Fan,Wu Wei,Yuwei Wu,Zhi Gao,Shiyu Luo,Mingyang Gao,Haoyu Zhao,Zhenxin Diao,Yuxuan Ba,Lijia Feng,Yunde Jia,Mehrtash Harandi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies and addresses a reliability challenge in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) where fine-grained visual descriptions are more error-prone than coarse-grained ones. The authors introduce GranFact, a granularity-aware benchmark with expert-verified multi-object images and coarse-to-fine annotations, along with a hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm. They also propose a reliability-prioritized preference optimization method based on DPO that penalizes unreliable fine-grained claims and rewards reliable specificity, showing improved fine-grained generation while maintaining reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-grained generation in MLLMs is more error-prone than coarse-grained generation, posing a reliability challenge. Models should generate the finest description that remains reliable rather than simply produce more specific outputs.

Method: (1) GranFact: a granularity-aware benchmark with expert-verified multi-object images and coarse-to-fine category annotations. (2) A hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm that assesses visual correctness and specificity of predictions. (3) A reliability-prioritized preference optimization method based on Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that penalizes unreliable fine-grained claims while rewarding reliable specificity.

Result: The proposed method improves fine-grained generation while preserving reliability on the GranFact benchmark.

Conclusion: Fine-grained generation in MLLMs requires a reliability-first approach; the proposed benchmark, evaluation, and optimization method effectively balance specificity and correctness.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to generate fine-grained descriptions of visual content. However, we observe and theoretically show that generating fine-grained responses poses a reliability challenge, \textit{i.e.}, fine-grained generation is more error-prone than coarse-grained generation. This phenomenon suggests that models should generate the finest description that remains reliable rather than simply produce more specific outputs. To investigate this problem, we develop \textsc{GranFact}, a granularity-aware benchmark consisting of expert-verified multi-object images with coarse-to-fine category annotations. Then, we design a hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm, which assesses both whether model predictions are visually correct and how specific the correct predictions are. We also propose a reliability-prioritized preference optimization method based on Direct Preference Optimization, which penalizes unreliable fine-grained claims while rewarding reliable specificity. Experiments on \textsc{GranFact} show that our method improves fine-grained generation while preserving reliability. Code and data are available \href{https://github.com/WeiWu2025/GranFact}{here}.

</details>


### [139] [ReMAP-PET: Beyond Visual Understanding -- Learning Region-Guided Metabolic Alignment Semantics from Brain PET](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29577)
*Dasen Dai,Yanteng Zhang,Shuoqi Li,Yuxiang Wei,Hongjie Yu,Qingxin Zhang,Qizhen Lan,Jagath C. Rajapakse,Vince D. Calhoun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReMAP-PET proposes a framework that supervises a 3D ResNet-50 with brain regional SUVR profiles using joint regression and contrastive objectives, enabling PET encoders to learn metabolic semantics rather than treating PET as generic volumetric data. It achieves 0.070 SUVR MAE and 77.8% Recall@1, and enables downstream tasks like PET-to-report generation and clinical classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D brain foundation models treat PET as generic volumetric data, missing the structured regional metabolic information that distinguishes it from structural neuroimaging, limiting its utility for neurodegenerative disease assessment.

Method: ReMAP-PET uses a partially-tuned MedicalNet 3D ResNet-50 supervised with brain regional SUVR profiles through joint regression and contrastive objectives, and further aligns metabolic embeddings to clinical language via contrastive alignment with frozen BioClinicalBERT.

Result: On 1015 paired PET-SUVR samples, ReMAP-PET achieves 0.070 SUVR MAE and 77.8% PET SUVR Recall@1, outperforming five frozen pretrained baselines, and demonstrates effective PET-to-report generation and clinically relevant linear probing results.

Conclusion: Grounding PET encoders in regional metabolic semantics produces structured, interpretable, and language-compatible representations, pointing to a new direction for metabolic-aware PET understanding.

Abstract: Positron Emission Tomography (PET) reveals brain metabolism and is clinically central to neurodegenerative disease assessment, yet existing 3D brain foundation models treat PET as generic volumetric data, missing the structured regional metabolic information that distinguishes it from structural neuroimaging. To address these limitations, we propose ReMAP-PET, a framework that moves beyond visual encoding by supervising a partially-tuned MedicalNet 3D ResNet-50 with brain regional standardized uptake value ratio (SUVR) profiles through joint regression and contrastive objectives, enabling the encoder to learn the metabolic semantics underlying PET modality. On 1015 paired PET--SUVR samples, ReMAP-PET achieves 0.070 SUVR MAE and 77.8% PET SUVR Recall@1, substantially outperforming five frozen pretrained baselines. We further connect the metabolic embedding to clinical language via contrastive alignment with frozen BioClinicalBERT and demonstrate end-to-end PET-to-report generation through SUVR-constrained verbalization. Linear probing on diagnostic classification and cognitive regression tasks confirms that the embeddings retain clinically relevant information without task-specific fine-tuning. Our results show that grounding PET encoders in regional metabolic semantics -- rather than treating PET as generic volumetric data -- yields representations that are structured, interpretable, and language-compatible, pointing to a new direction for metabolic-aware PET understanding.

</details>


### [140] [SonoCLIP: Mask-Guided Region-Aware Vision-Language Pretraining for Fetal Ultrasound Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29586)
*Hang Su,Chao Sun,Zhaofan Li,Wei Hu,Juhua Liu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SonoCLIP is the first million-scale region-controllable fetal ultrasound vision-language foundation model that integrates segmentation masks as visual prompts for joint global-local contrastive learning, achieving superior zero-shot transfer performance on a 1.44M-image dataset spanning 24 standard planes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CLIP-based models rely on global image-text alignment, limiting sensitivity to clinically decisive local structures in fetal ultrasound, which suffers from speckle noise, acquisition variability, and subtle anatomical boundaries.

Method: SonoCLIP integrates segmentation masks as mask-channel visual prompts within the vision encoder for joint global-local contrastive representation learning, using a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss for stable large-scale supervision, pretrained on a curated 1.44M-image multimodal fetal ultrasound dataset spanning 24 standard planes.

Result: Superior zero-shot transfer performance under both global and mask-guided inference across cross-center evaluations.

Conclusion: SonoCLIP establishes a controllable and clinically oriented foundation model for fetal ultrasound analysis.

Abstract: Vision-language foundation models have shown strong potential in medical image analysis. Although foundation models for ultrasound imaging have recently emerged, the domain remains particularly challenging due to severe speckle noise, acquisition variability, and subtle anatomical boundaries, leading to high inter-observer variability. Existing CLIP-based models rely primarily on global image-text alignment, limiting their sensitivity to clinically decisive local structures. We propose SonoCLIP, the first million-scale region-controllable fetal ultrasound vision-language foundation model that integrates segmentation masks as mask-channel visual prompts within the vision encoder, enabling joint global-local contrastive representation learning. To support scalable region-text alignment, we introduce a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss that improves stability under large-scale supervision. We further curate a 1.44M-image multimodal fetal ultrasound dataset spanning 24 standard planes for large-scale pretraining. Extensive cross-center evaluations demonstrate that SonoCLIP achieves superior zero-shot transfer performance under both global and mask-guided inference, establishing a controllable and clinically oriented foundation model for fetal ultrasound analysis. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/Harrison-one/SonoCLIP.

</details>


### [141] [One Scene, Two Depths: Probing Geometric Ambiguity in Monocular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29600)
*Xiaohao Xu,Feng Xue,Xiang Li,Haowei Li,Shusheng Yang,Tianyi Zhang,Matthew Johnson-Roberson,Xiaonan Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper challenges monocular depth estimation's assumption of single-depth-per-pixel, introduces the MD-3k benchmark for measuring depth-layer preferences in transparent scenes, and shows that Laplacian Visual Prompting can shift layer selection in frozen models, revealing complementary geometric hypotheses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular depth estimation reduces layered 3D geometry to a single scalar per pixel, which is ambiguous in transparent scenes where multiple surfaces exist along a ray. The paper seeks to expose and measure this ambiguity as depth-layer preference rather than treating it as scene-intrinsic truth.

Method: The authors introduce MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k), a sparse two-layer ordinal benchmark, and propose Multi-Layer Spatial Relationship Accuracy (ML-SRA) for evaluation. They also introduce Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP), a training-free spectral input transformation, to probe how frozen depth models change their layer preference.

Result: Leading depth foundation models exhibit diverse layer preferences under standard RGB input on MD-3k. LVP can substantially change the reported layer for certain frozen models. The best RGB/LVP pair (DAv2-L) achieves 75.5% ML-SRA.

Conclusion: Depth foundation models express complementary geometric hypotheses that standard RGB inference leaves unexpressed. The community should adopt an ambiguity-aware lens where multiple valid 3D interpretations are measured, preserved, and expressed.

Abstract: A faithful 3D world representation should account for layered geometry, where a single camera ray may contain multiple visible and geometrically valid surfaces. Monocular depth estimation, however, reduces this structure to one scalar depth per pixel. Transparent scenes make this ambiguity measurable: the same ray can pass through foreground glass and observe the background, turning the supervised target into a convention of annotation, data, and training rather than a scene-intrinsic truth. A learned predictor exposes this convention as its depth-layer preference. We introduce MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k), a sparse two-layer ordinal benchmark for measuring depth-layer preference and multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA). On MD-3k, leading depth foundation models exhibit diverse layer preferences under standard RGB input, showing that the same layered geometry can be resolved differently across models. We further find that Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP), a training-free spectral input transformation, can substantially change the reported layer for certain frozen models. The strongest RGB/LVP pair, DAv2-L, reaches 75.5% ML-SRA. These results suggest that depth foundation models may express complementary geometric hypotheses that standard RGB inference leaves unexpressed. We invite the community to rethink depth supervision and evaluation through an ambiguity-aware lens, where multiple valid 3D interpretations are treated as geometric structure to be measured, preserved, and expressed.

</details>


### [142] [Benchmarking Geospatial Foundation Models for Agriculture Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29664)
*Zhuocheng Shang,Sanmay Das,Ahmed Eldawy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geospatial foundation models (Prithvi, SpectralGPT, SatMAE) sharply degrade in agricultural tasks when tested on unseen regions, predicting only common crops and missing rare ones; the paper calls for region-aware evaluation standards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geographic transferability of geospatial foundation models has not been systematically tested, especially in agriculture, despite their promise of broad generalization across remote sensing tasks and regions.

Method: A controlled benchmark evaluating three models (Prithvi, SpectralGPT, SatMAE) on multi-temporal crop segmentation and change detection across four U.S. states (Iowa, NC, California, Minnesota), with train/validation/test splits assigned to separate regions to measure unseen-land transfer performance.

Result: All three models degrade sharply under regional distribution shift, predicting only the most common crops while missing rare ones. Fitting models to a shared input format affects each model differently, complicating direct architectural comparison.

Conclusion: Current geospatial foundation models have key limitations for agriculture; region-aware evaluation should be a necessary standard for assessing geographic transferability.

Abstract: Geospatial foundation models pretrained on satellite imagery promise broad generalization across remote sensing tasks and regions, but their geographic transferability has not been systematically tested, especially in agriculture applications. This paper presents a controlled benchmark that evaluates three models, Prithvi, SpectralGPT, and SatMAE, on multi-temporal crop segmentation and change detection across four U.S. states, Iowa, North Carolina, California, and Minnesota. By assigning each train, validation, and test split to a separate region, we measure how well each model transfers to land it has not seen. All three degrade sharply under regional distribution shift, predicting only the most common crops while missing rare ones. We further find that fitting these models to a shared input format affects each one differently, which complicates direct architectural comparison. These results expose key limitations of current geospatial foundation models for agriculture and point to region aware evaluation as a necessary standard.

</details>


### [143] [Unlocking the Visual Record of Materials Science: A Large-Scale Multimodal Dataset from Scientific Literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29667)
*Subham Ghosh,Shubham Tiwari,Mohammad Ibrahim,Abhishek Tewari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MatMMExtract is an open-source pipeline that decomposes compound scientific figures into sub-panels and generates structured annotations, producing MatSciFig — a dataset of 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs from 180,571 figures in 14,810 materials science articles, with a domain-specific detector (MaterialScope) achieving 0.9227 mAP_50 and a dual-encoder retrieval baseline showing 4.4× improvement in R@1 over zero-shot CLIP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Materials science figures contain decades of experimental knowledge but remain inaccessible to AI at scale because most scientific figures are compound (multiple sub-panels under a single caption), making direct image-text pairing unreliable.

Method: An end-to-end pipeline that (1) decomposes compound figures into individual sub-panels using MaterialScope (a domain-specific detection dataset of 2,811 manually annotated figures with fine-tuned YOLO12-m detector), and (2) generates structured, grounded annotations using a large language model guided by a curated materials science taxonomy.

Result: MatSciFig dataset with 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs; MaterialScope detector achieves mAP_50 of 0.9227; Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite achieves best cost-quality trade-off (82% good outputs, 4.8% hallucination rate); dual-encoder retrieval baseline achieves 4.4× improvement in R@1 over zero-shot CLIP.

Conclusion: All resources are released openly to the community, demonstrating immediate utility for vision-language learning in materials science.

Abstract: The materials science literature encodes decades of experimental knowledge in figures, yet this visual record remains locked away and inaccessible to AI at scale. The core difficulty is structural: most scientific figures are compound, with a single caption describing multiple sub-panels simultaneously, making direct image-text pairing unreliable. We present MatMMExtract, an end-to-end open-source pipeline that resolves this by decomposing compound figures into individual sub-panels and generating structured, grounded annotations using a large language model guided by a curated materials science taxonomy. Applied to 14,810 open-access articles, MatMMExtract produces MatSciFig; 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs from 180,571 figures, each annotated with a sub-caption, a two-level visualisation category spanning 19 classes and over 100 subtypes, and a scientific summary. To enable accurate panel localisation, we introduce MaterialScope, a domain-specific detection dataset of 2,811 manually annotated materials science figures, on which a fine-tuned YOLO12-m detector achieves mAP_50 of 0.9227. Among six benchmarked language models, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite delivers the best cost-quality trade-off for annotation generation, with 82% of outputs rated good and a hallucination rate of 4.8%. A dual-encoder retrieval baseline on MatSciFig achieves a 4.4 times improvement in R@1 over zero-shot CLIP, demonstrating the dataset's immediate utility for vision-language learning. All resources are released openly to the community.

</details>


### [144] [PoseShield: Neural Collision Fields for Human Self-Collision Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29686)
*Zhengyuan Li,Zeyun Deng,Yifan Shen,Liangyan Gui,Miaolan Xie,Joseph Campbell,Xifeng Gao,Kui Wu,Zherong Pan,Aniket Bera*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PoseShield is a neural collision constraint operating directly in SMPL pose space that corrects self-collisions in human pose estimation and motion generation via Eikonal-regularized constrained optimization, achieving 95.8% success rate on a new benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-collision is a persistent challenge in SMPL-based human pose estimation and motion generation. Under extreme articulations or stochastic motion synthesis, generated meshes frequently exhibit self-penetrations, leading to physically implausible results.

Method: Proposes PoseShield, a neural collision constraint defined directly in SMPL pose space, formulating collision correction as a constrained optimization problem and connecting the learned constraint with the Eikonal equation. Enforcing Eikonal regularization ensures non-vanishing gradients near the collision boundary, improving numerical stability. The approach operates in low-dimensional pose space and serves as a generator-agnostic post-hoc corrector.

Result: Achieves 95.8% success rate on a newly constructed SMPL pose benchmark, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: PoseShield provides a theoretically grounded, generator-agnostic post-hoc collision corrector in low-dimensional pose space that extends to human motion sequences without retraining the underlying motion model.

Abstract: Self-collision remains a persistent challenge in SMPL-based human pose estimation and motion generation. Under extreme articulations or stochastic motion synthesis, generated meshes frequently exhibit self-penetrations, leading to physically implausible results. We propose PoseShield, a neural collision constraint defined directly in SMPL pose space. We formulate collision correction as a constrained optimization problem and connect the learned constraint with the Eikonal equation. Enforcing Eikonal regularization ensures non-vanishing gradients near the collision boundary, improving numerical stability and robustness of the optimization process. Unlike prior methods that operate in the mesh space or rely on heuristic penalties, our approach operates directly in the low-dimensional space of human poses and is theoretically grounded. The same learned constraint extends to human motion sequences, providing a generator-agnostic post-hoc collision corrector without retraining the underlying motion model. Experiments on a newly constructed SMPL pose benchmark show that our method achieves a 95.8% success rate and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [145] [Progressive Self-Supervised Learning with Individualized Community Assignment for Brain Network Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29695)
*Hairui Chen,Yanwu Yang,Jianfeng Cao,Hanyang Peng,Chenfei Ye,Ting Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BrainPICM is a self-supervised framework for brain network analysis that uses progressive individualized community-aware masking via unbalanced optimal transport to learn subject-specific modular structures, outperforming existing methods on three fMRI datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SSL methods for brain network analysis use generic masking strategies that overlook the heterogeneous, subject-specific modular community structure across individuals and neurological conditions.

Method: BrainPICM employs: (1) progressive unbalanced optimal transport for ROI-to-community soft mapping with confidence scores; (2) curriculum-style masking that progressively incorporates low-confidence regions; (3) deviation-aware aggregation measuring mass redistribution relative to a population template for interpretability.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL methods on ABIDE-I, ADHD-200, and ADNI fMRI datasets in diagnostic accuracy.

Conclusion: Explicitly injecting modular community structure into masked modeling yields more functionally consistent and generalizable brain network representations.

Abstract: Brain networks exhibit a modular community structure that varies across individuals and neurological conditions. However, existing self-supervised learning (SSL) methods often overlook this heterogeneity, relying on generic masking strategies that fail to capture subject-specific functional organization. We propose BrainPICM, a self-supervised framework for brain network analysis via progressive individualized community aware masking. BrainPICM formulates ROI-to-community mapping as a progressive unbalanced optimal transport process, yielding soft assignments and per-ROI confidence scores. Guided by these confidence estimates, a curriculum-style masking strategy gradually incorporates low-confidence, potentially pathological regions into training, enabling the model to learn both stable modular structures and individual variations. Additionally, a deviation-aware aggregation module quantifies functional reorganization by measuring mass redistribution relative to a population template, enhancing interpretability and downstream prediction. Experiments on three fMRI datasets (ABIDE-I, ADHD-200, ADNI) show that BrainPICM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL methods in diagnostic accuracy, indicating that explicitly injecting modular community structure into masked modeling yields more functionally consistent and generalizable representations. The source code for this approach will be released at https://github.com/Hrychen7/BrainPICM.

</details>


### [146] [MF-UAVPose6D: A Model-Free Monocular 6-DoF Pose Estimation Framework for Fixed-Wing UAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29697)
*Juanqin Liu,Leonardo Plotegher,Eloy Roura,Shaoming He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MF-UAVPose6D is a model-free monocular 6-DoF pose estimation framework for fixed-wing UAVs that uses only a single RGB image and camera intrinsics, employing heatmap-guided center localization, a Perspective-Aware Module, Dynamic Topological Sampling, and a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism, achieving accurate pose estimation without CAD models as demonstrated on the new FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating 6-DoF poses of non-cooperative UAV targets is essential for airspace situational awareness, target tracking, and counter-UAV operations, but existing model-based or keypoint-matching methods are unreliable because non-cooperative targets typically lack CAD models and keypoint priors.

Method: The framework takes a single RGB image and camera intrinsics as input; performs heatmap-guided center localization to obtain a stable target anchor; uses a Perspective-Aware Module (PAM) to model observation-ray priors; applies Dynamic Topological Sampling (DTS) to complement weak structural cues from wings, fuselage, and tail; and adopts a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism for 6-DoF estimation.

Result: MF-UAVPose6D achieves accurate and efficient monocular 6-DoF pose estimation without requiring CAD models, and demonstrates strong robustness in long-range rotation estimation, depth recovery, and joint pose evaluation on the constructed FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed model-free approach successfully addresses the challenge of 6-DoF pose estimation for non-cooperative fixed-wing UAVs, eliminating reliance on CAD models and keypoint priors while maintaining accuracy and robustness across diverse distances, viewpoints, and poses.

Abstract: For uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), estimating six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) poses is essential for airspace situational awareness, target tracking, and counter-UAV operations. However, non-cooperative targets usually lack computer-aided design (CAD) models and keypoint priors, making existing model-based or keypoint-matching methods difficult to apply reliably. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MF-UAVPose6D, a model-free monocular 6-DoF pose estimation framework for fixed-wing UAVs. During inference, the method takes only a single red-green-blue (RGB) image and camera intrinsics as input. It first obtains a stable target anchor through heatmap-guided center localization, introduces a Perspective-Aware Module (PAM) to model observation-ray priors, exploits Dynamic Topological Sampling (DTS) to complement weak structural cues from the wings, fuselage, and tail, and adopts a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism to estimate the 6-DoF pose. In addition, we construct the FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset, which covers fixed-wing UAV observations across diverse distances, viewpoints, and poses. Experimental results show that MF-UAVPose6D achieves accurate and efficient monocular 6-DoF pose estimation without requiring CAD models, and demonstrates strong robustness in long-range rotation estimation, depth recovery, and joint pose evaluation.

</details>


### [147] [Early Warning Signals for OpenVLA Failure under Visual Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29699)
*Dipesh Tharu Mahato,Rachel Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OpenVLA's internal activations at layer 16 linearly decode near-term task failure under occlusion (AUROC 0.972) better than baselines, but the monitor is not a causal mechanism or generalizable recovery system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Language Action models are hard to diagnose when visual conditions shift; the authors aim to test whether internal feedforward activations of OpenVLA contain linearly decodable information about imminent task failure under controlled perceptual stress.

Method: Log OpenVLA internal activations during LIBERO rollouts with a fixed policy; fit lightweight logistic probes (and baselines) after collection; test under occlusion, color shift, and camera jitter; sweep layers 8, 10, 16 for decodability.

Result: Occlusion drops success from 57% to 17%; layer 16 logistic probe achieves AUROC 0.972, AUPRC 0.352 for 15-step failure prediction, outperforming baselines; layer 16 strongest, layer 8 informative, layer 10 weak; occlusion-trained monitor still above random under camera jitter; color shift causes no failures (benign control).

Conclusion: OpenVLA internal states encode failure-relevant structure under controlled perceptual shift, but results are limited: no causal mechanism, no held-out task generalization, and no deployable recovery system.

Abstract: Vision Language Action models combine perception, language grounding, and control in a single policy, but their failures are hard to diagnose once visual conditions shift. We test whether OpenVLA feedforward activations contain linearly decodable information about near term task failure in LIBERO manipulation rollouts. The policy is fixed throughout. We log internal activations during execution and fit lightweight monitors after the rollouts are collected. Occlusion is the main controlled stress test. It reduces OpenVLA success from $57\%$ to $17\%$ over $100$ episodes per condition. Under this shift, a logistic probe at layer 16 reaches AUROC $0.972$ and AUPRC $0.352$ for predicting failure within a $15$ step horizon. It outperforms both a mean difference direction and an action disagreement baseline. A sparse layer sweep finds uneven decodability across depth: layer 16 is strongest among the tested layers, layer 8 remains informative, and layer 10 is weaker. To check whether the monitor is just an occlusion detector, we also evaluate color shift and camera jitter without refitting. Color shift produces no failures in this setting, so it is a benign control rather than a failure benchmark. Camera jitter does induce failures, and the occlusion trained monitor remains above random. The result is deliberately limited: OpenVLA internal states contain failure relevant structure under controlled perceptual shift, but these experiments do not establish a causal mechanism, task held out generalization, or a deployable recovery system.

</details>


### [148] [UniVAD v2: Unified Visual Anomaly Detection via Support-Conditioned Boundary Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29714)
*Zhaopeng Gu,Bingke Zhu,Zhaowen Li,Guibo Zhu,Yingying Chen,Ming Tang,Peng Su,Jinqiao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniVAD v2 proposes a two-sided (normal and abnormal) support-conditioned boundary construction framework for unified visual anomaly detection, introducing optimal transport-based relational modeling, adaptive fusion mechanisms, and few-shot abnormal references to improve cross-domain generalization across industrial, logical, and medical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing few-shot unified anomaly detection methods rely primarily on normal-side evidence for boundary estimation, lacking abnormal-side tolerance for deployment-specific customization, and struggle to jointly capture local correspondences and global support-query relations, resulting in unreliable boundaries for unseen anomalies.

Method: UniVAD v2 extends the component-patch divide-and-conquer framework of UniVAD with three new modules: (1) OTRM - Optimal Transport-based Relational Modeling for support-query matching via transport-style allocation; (2) ACRRM - Adaptive Coordination mechanism for Retrieval and Relational Modeling to fuse evidence sources with episode-conditioned reliabilities; (3) FAR - Few-Shot Abnormal Reference module to convert optional abnormal references into rejection-side evidence for boundary adjustment.

Result: Under 1N-shot protocol, UniVAD v2 improves mean image-level AUC from 83.0% to 84.5%, and reaches 85.7% in 1N+1A-shot setting. On MVTec-AD-SS, achieves 96.2% image-level AUC and 96.9% pixel-level AUC, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization across industrial, logical, and medical datasets.

Conclusion: Incorporating both normal-side relational reasoning and optional abnormal-side references enables more reliable and customizable boundary estimation for few-shot unified visual anomaly detection, without requiring retraining for deployment-specific tolerance.

Abstract: Unified visual anomaly detection seeks to train a single detector that can be deployed across categories, domains, and application scenarios. In the few-shot transfer regime, the key challenge is to estimate an episode-specific boundary for an unseen target category from a small support set. Existing approaches mainly infer this boundary from normal-side evidence and provide limited abnormal-side evidence for deployment-specific tolerance. Within the normal side, they often struggle to jointly capture local correspondences and global support-query relations, making their boundaries less reliable for unseen anomalies. To address these issues, we propose UniVAD v2, a two-sided support-conditioned boundary construction framework for unified visual anomaly detection. Built on the component-patch divide-and-conquer framework of UniVAD, UniVAD v2 strengthens the normal side with an Optimal Transport-based Relational Modeling module (OTRM), which complements retrieval with support-query matching through transport-style allocation, and an Adaptive Coordination mechanism for Retrieval and Relational Modeling (ACRRM), which estimates episode-conditioned reliabilities to fuse the two sources of evidence. On the abnormal side, a Few-Shot Abnormal Reference module (FAR) converts optional abnormal references into rejection-side evidence for boundary adjustment. Experiments on six datasets spanning industrial, logical, and medical anomaly detection demonstrate strong cross-domain generalization. Under the 1N-shot protocol, UniVAD v2 improves the mean image-level AUC over UniVAD from 83.0\% to 84.5\%, and further reaches 85.7\% in the 1N+1A-shot setting. On the MVTec-AD Severity Split (MVTec-AD-SS), UniVAD v2 achieves 96.2\% image-level AUC and 96.9\% pixel-level AUC, showing that abnormal references enable controllable boundary customization without retraining.

</details>


### [149] [Accurate Recognition of Pneumonia and COVID-19 by Geometric Shape Normalization of Lung Region using Automatic Landmark Detection and Piecewise Affine Warping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29715)
*Salvador E. Ayala-Raggi,Rafael Alejandro Cruz-Ovando,Lauro Reyes-Cocoletzi,Aldrin Barreto-Flores*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A system for pulmonary disease recognition in chest X-rays using geometric normalization (landmark detection, Procrustes alignment, affine warping) and a ResNet-18 classifier, achieving 98.60% accuracy on COVID-19 Radiography Database and showing robustness against acquisition artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve pulmonary disease recognition in chest X-rays by reducing the influence of acquisition artifacts and anatomical variability through geometric normalization of lung regions.

Method: Three-module pipeline: (1) ResNet-18 landmark detector with coordinate attention ensemble (4 models + TTA) for 15 lung-contour landmarks; (2) GPA-based geometric normalizer using Delaunay triangulation and piecewise affine warping; (3) ResNet-18 classifier with transfer learning and SAHS contrast enhancement.

Result: 98.60+/-0.26% accuracy and 98.00% F1-Macro on COVID-19 Radiography Database; normalized images outperformed artifact-masked/cropped unaligned images (98.60% vs 96.24%), and on a mixed adult-pediatric dataset (94.67% vs 94.17%).

Conclusion: Anatomical alignment via geometric normalization provides a more controlled, artifact-resistant representation for pulmonary disease recognition, though raw images may show slightly higher accuracy due to acquisition artifacts.

Abstract: This paper presents an automatic system for recognizing pulmonary diseases in chest X-rays using geometric normalization of the lung region. The method combines three modules: (1) a ResNet-18 landmark detector with coordinate attention that predicts 15 lung-contour landmarks, achieving a mean localization error of 3.61 pixels through an ensemble of four models with test-time augmentation; (2) a geometric normalizer based on Generalized Procrustes Analysis, Delaunay triangulation, and piecewise affine warping to map each lung region to a standardized shape; and (3) a ResNet-18 classifier with transfer learning and SAHS contrast enhancement to classify images as COVID-19, Viral Pneumonia, or Normal. On the COVID-19 Radiography Database, the normalized-image classifier achieved 98.60+/-0.26% accuracy and 98.00% F1-Macro using five-fold cross-validation. Although original images produced slightly higher raw accuracy, Grad-CAM and cropping experiments suggest that this advantage is partly influenced by acquisition artifacts. In contrast, geometrically normalized images outperformed artifact-masked/cropped unaligned images on both the COVID-19 Radiography Database (98.60% vs. 96.24%) and a balanced adult-pediatric mixed dataset including pediatric cases from the Kermany dataset (94.67% vs. 94.17%). These results suggest that anatomical alignment can provide a more controlled and artifact-resistant representation for pulmonary disease recognition.

</details>


### [150] [AerialMetric: Benchmarking and Adapting UAV Monocular Metric Depth Estimation in the Real World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29716)
*Zhongqiang Song,Guanying Chen,Yuqi Zhang,Yin Zou,Chuanyu Fu,Zhiyuan Yuan,Chuan Huang,Shuguang Cui,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AerialMetric is a new benchmark dataset (52K real + 16K synthetic image-depth pairs) for monocular metric depth estimation in aerial UAV imagery, enabling domain adaptation from ground-level to aerial viewpoints and establishing state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing monocular metric depth estimation models trained on street-view and indoor datasets suffer from significant domain gaps when applied to aerial UAV viewpoints. There is a lack of a dedicated benchmark for evaluating and adapting these models to aerial settings.

Method: The authors introduce AerialMetric, a dataset with four complementary subsets: real-world photogrammetry data, controlled aerial acquisition, photorealistic synthetic scenes, and in-the-wild Internet imagery. They systematically evaluate existing SOTA models under aerial settings, studying the impact of viewpoint, altitude, and camera parameters, and fine-tune a representative metric depth model on the dataset.

Result: AerialMetric provides 52K real-world and 16K synthetic image-depth pairs with reliable metric ground truth. Fine-tuning on this dataset achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse aerial imagery.

Conclusion: AerialMetric effectively addresses the domain gap for monocular metric depth estimation in aerial UAV imagery and establishes a comprehensive benchmark for future research. Dataset, code, and model weights are publicly available.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of monocular metric depth estimation in aerial UAV imagery. Although recent data-driven methods have achieved remarkable progress in ground-level scenarios, models trained primarily on street-view and indoor datasets exhibit significant domain gaps when applied to aerial viewpoints. To tackle these challenges, we introduce AerialMetric, a benchmark dataset designed to evaluate and facilitate the adaptation of monocular metric depth estimation under UAV aerial viewpoints. The dataset consists of four complementary subsets collected from different sources, jointly covering real-world photogrammetry data, controlled aerial acquisition settings, photorealistic synthetic scenes, and in-the-wild Internet imagery. Totally, AerialMetric provides 52K real-world and 16K synthetic image-depth pairs with reliable metric ground truth. Based on this dataset, we conduct systematic evaluations of existing state-of-the-art models under aerial settings and investigate the impact of viewpoint, altitude, and camera parameters on metric depth prediction. In addition, by fine-tuning representative metric depth model on our dataset, we establish a comprehensive aerial benchmark and achieve state-of-the-art performance across diverse aerial imagery. Our dataset, code, and model weight are publicly available at https://kuieless.github.io/AerialMetric-ECCV2026-page/.

</details>


### [151] [HTC-SGA Former: A Hybrid Transformer-CNN Network with Self-Guided Attention and a New Boundary-Weighted Adaptive Loss for Coronary DSA Vessel Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29744)
*Rayan Merghani Ahmed,Marwa Omer Mohammed Omer,Mohamed Elmanna,Shijie Li,Bin Li,Shoujun Zhoua*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HTC-SGA Former, a lightweight hybrid Transformer-CNN framework (0.81M parameters), outperforms 14 SOTA methods on coronary DSA vessel segmentation by using MS-GLWA for global-local attention, SGFA for weak-vessel enhancement, and BWACL for boundary-weighted adaptive loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing coronary DSA vessel segmentation methods struggle with thin low-contrast vessels, background interference, severe vessel-background class imbalance, and the inability to balance global contextual reasoning with preservation of weak vessels, vessel continuity, and fine boundaries.

Method: A lightweight hybrid Transformer-CNN framework with: (1) CNN encoder for local vessel morphology extraction, (2) Transformer decoder for contextual feature modeling, (3) Multi-Scale Global-Local Window Attention (MS-GLWA) block, (4) Self-Guided Feature Attention (SGFA) module for weak-vessel enhancement, and (5) Boundary-Weighted Adaptive Compound Loss (BWACL) emphasizing thin-vessel boundaries and adaptive balance.

Result: Outperforms 14 state-of-the-art segmentation methods on private right and left coronary artery DSA subsets with only 0.81M parameters. BWACL improves performance over BCE and Dice losses across four different encoder-decoder architectures.

Conclusion: HTC-SGA Former improves thin-vessel recovery, vessel continuity, and boundary localization via complementary global-local contextual modeling, vessel-focused refinement, and adaptive optimization, providing a reliable and computationally efficient solution for coronary vessel analysis in computer-assisted cardiovascular interventions.

Abstract: Accurate coronary Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) vessel segmentation is essential for computer-aided diagnosis and treatment planning of coronary artery disease (CAD). However, thin low-contrast vessels, background interference, and severe vessel-background class imbalance make reliable segmentation of weak distal branches and vessel boundaries challenging. Existing methods struggle to balance global contextual reasoning with preservation of weak vessels, vessel continuity, and fine boundaries. To address these limitations, we propose HTC-SGA Former, a lightweight hybrid Transformer-CNN framework for coronary DSA vessel segmentation. It employs a CNN encoder for local vessel morphology extraction and a Transformer decoder for contextual feature modeling. A Multi-Scale Global-Local Window Attention (MS-GLWA) block performs efficient global-local contextual modeling, while a Self-Guided Feature Attention (SGFA) module enhances weak-vessel responses. In addition, a Boundary-Weighted Adaptive Compound Loss (BWACL) emphasizes thin-vessel boundaries and adaptively balances vessel recovery and boundary refinement. Experiments on private right and left coronary artery DSA subsets show that HTC-SGA Former outperforms 14 state-of-the-art segmentation methods while maintaining a compact architecture with only 0.81M parameters. BWACL also improves performance over binary cross-entropy and Dice losses across four encoder-decoder architectures, demonstrating strong cross-backbone applicability. HTC-SGA Former improves thin-vessel recovery, vessel continuity, and boundary localization through complementary global-local contextual modeling, vessel-focused refinement, and adaptive optimization, supporting reliable and computationally efficient coronary vessel analysis for future computer-assisted cardiovascular interventions.

</details>


### [152] [MR-IQA: A Unified Margin View of Regression and Ranking for Blind Image Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29760)
*Yuan Li,Youyuan Lin,Zitang Sun,Yung-Hao Yang,Kiyofumi Miyoshi,Chenhui Chu,Shin'ya Nishida*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies "quality margin" (pairwise relational distance) as the common bridge between regression and ranking paradigms in BIQA, and proposes MR-IQA, a direct quality-margin optimization framework using reinforcement learning that outperforms existing regression- or ranking-based RL methods on six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although joint regression-ranking supervision often improves BIQA, the relation between regression and ranking paradigms remains largely empirical and underexplored. The authors aim to theoretically unify these two paradigms.

Method: The authors derive that both regression and ranking fit quality margins at the objective-optimization level — regression fits margins induced by score endpoints, ranking fits transformed/sign-level margins via preference probabilities. They propose MR-IQA, a direct quality-margin optimization framework for RL-based BIQA that samples quality scores and optimizes pairwise margin errors as policy rewards.

Result: MR-IQA achieves competitive general performance on six BIQA benchmarks and achieves the strongest average PLCC/SRCC over regression- or ranking-based RL methods in controlled comparisons.

Conclusion: The findings provide a new insight into unifying regression and ranking, offering a theoretical basis for understanding quality-structure modeling in BIQA and beyond.

Abstract: Blind image quality assessment (BIQA) is commonly built on two basic learning paradigms: regression and ranking. Regression calibrates absolute scores, whereas ranking recovers quality structure from ordinal relations. Although joint regression-ranking supervision often improves BIQA, the relation between the two paradigms remains largely empirical and underexplored. In this work, we revisit what underlies regression and ranking and identify pairwise relational distance, termed quality margin, as their common bridge. Our derivation shows that, at the objective-optimization level, both paradigms fit quality margins: regression fits margins induced by score endpoints, while ranking fits transformed or sign-level margins through preference probabilities. Motivated by this insight, we propose MR-IQA, a direct quality-margin optimization framework for reinforcement learning (RL)-based BIQA. MR-IQA samples quality scores and optimizes pairwise margin errors as policy rewards, thereby modeling quality structure more explicitly. Experiments on six BIQA benchmarks show competitive general performance, and controlled comparisons demonstrate that MR-IQA achieves the strongest average PLCC/SRCC over regression- or ranking-based RL methods. Our findings provide a new insight into unifying regression and ranking, offering a theoretical basis for understanding quality-structure modeling in BIQA and beyond.

</details>


### [153] [TopoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Automated Topology Learning in Medical Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29763)
*Guangyu Meng,Pengfei Gu,Xueyang Li,Yiyu Shi,Erin Wolf Chambers,Danny Z. Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TopoAgent is the first LLM-based agentic framework that automatically selects optimal topological descriptors for medical image analysis by reasoning about image characteristics and generating suitable topological feature vectors without task-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TDA methods default to a single fixed topological descriptor (e.g., persistence images), ignoring the diversity of topological representations. No LLM-based agentic framework exists to automatically determine the most suitable topological descriptor for a given image dataset.

Method: TopoAgent operates via a Perception-Reasoning-Action-Reflection loop, supported by 21 domain-specific tools and dual memory for experience accumulation. Its skill set is distilled from systematic evaluation of 15 topological descriptors across 26 datasets with 6 classifiers.

Result: TopoAgent analyzes input images and their topological characteristics, reasons about suitable topological descriptors, and determines the optimal descriptor and its configuration without task-specific training.

Conclusion: TopoAgent successfully automates topology learning for medical image analysis by leveraging LLM-based reasoning to select and configure the most appropriate topological descriptors for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Topological data analysis (TDA), particularly persistent homology (PH), captures geometric structural properties in medical images (e.g., connected components, loops, shape characteristics), which conventional pixel-level deep learning approaches often neglect. While many topological descriptors are known for converting persistence diagrams (PDs) or raw images into topological feature vectors, existing methods mostly default to a single fixed descriptor (e.g., persistence images), leaving the diversity of topological representations largely unexplored. To the best of our knowledge, there is no known large language model (LLM)-based agentic framework that can automatically determine the most suitable topological descriptors for a given image dataset and produce the corresponding topological feature vectors for downstream tasks. To fill this gap, we propose \textbf{TopoAgent}, an LLM-based agentic framework that automates topology learning for medical image analysis.TopoAgent operates through a Perception--Reasoning--Action--Reflection loop supported by 21 domain-specific tools and dual memory that accumulates experience across runs. Its skill set is distilled from systematic evaluation of 15 topological descriptors across 26 datasets with six classifiers. TopoAgent analyzes input images and their topological characteristics, reasons about which topological descriptors best suit the input, and determines the optimal descriptor and its configuration, all without task-specific training.

</details>


### [154] [UrbanCDNet: Appearance-Robust and Boundary-Aware Bitemporal Change Detection for Korean Urban Building Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29781)
*Abdirashid Omar,Jonghyuk Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UrbanCDNet, a task-specific Siamese CNN, achieves state-of-the-art building change detection on a Korean benchmark (0.7511 F1, 0.6014 IoU), significantly outperforming baselines by addressing sparse changes, appearance gaps, and boundary precision through multi-cue comparison, alignment-aware differencing, and boundary supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban building change detection in Korean scenes is challenging due to sparse changed regions, strong appearance variations between acquisition dates, and the need for precise building-footprint outputs rather than coarse blobs, which generic models struggle to handle.

Method: UrbanCDNet is a Siamese CNN combining (1) appearance-robust multi-cue comparison, (2) alignment-aware middle-scale differencing, (3) lightweight context refinement, (4) scene calibration, and (5) auxiliary boundary supervision.

Result: On a corrected AIHub Korean benchmark (3,998/503/499 train/val/test splits), UrbanCDNet achieves 0.7335 precision, 0.7696 recall, 0.7511 F1, 0.6014 IoU, surpassing Siamese U-Net (0.7108 F1, 0.5514 IoU) and ChangeFormer-MIT-B0 (0.7107 F1, 0.5512 IoU). On sparse-change subsets (<5% changed area), F1 improves from 0.4765 to 0.6175; on high photometric-gap subsets, from 0.6349 to 0.7285. Boundary F1 (3-pixel tolerance) rises from 0.3445 to 0.4447; object F1 (IoU 0.3) from 0.0690 to 0.2258.

Conclusion: Task-shaped temporal comparison and boundary-aware supervision are more important than generic model scale for Korean urban building change detection, as evidenced by UrbanCDNet's targeted gains on challenging subsets.

Abstract: Urban building change detection from bi-temporal aerial imagery is important for redevelopment monitoring, infrastructure management, and unauthorized-construction screening, but Korean urban scenes remain difficult because changed regions are often sparse, appearance varies strongly between acquisition dates, and useful outputs must follow building footprints rather than coarse blobs. This paper presents UrbanCDNet, a task specific Siamese CNN that combines appearance-robust multi-cue comparison, alignment-aware middle-scale differencing, lightweight context refinement, scene calibration, and auxiliary boundary supervision. Experiments use a corrected AIHub-based Korean benchmark with 3,998 training, 503 validation, and 499 test pairs, and report changed-class precision, recall, F1, and IoU. On the locked test split, UrbanCDNet achieves 0.7335 precision, 0.7696 recall, 0.7511 F1, and 0.6014 IoU, outperforming a strong Siamese U-Net baseline (0.7108 F1, 0.5514 IoU) and the strongest external competitor, ChangeFormer-MIT-B0 (0.7107 F1, 0.5512 IoU). Additional diagnostic slicing shows that the gain is concentrated in the operating regimes that motivated the design: on the sparse-change subset with less than 5% changed area, F1 improves from 0.4765 to 0.6175, and on the high photometric-gap subset it improves from 0.6349 to 0.7285. Boundary F1 at 3-pixel tolerance rises from 0.3445 to 0.4447, while object F1 at IoU 0.3 rises from 0.0690 to 0.2258.
