These results indicate that, on this Korean benchmark, task-shaped temporal comparison and boundary-aware supervision matter more than generic model scale alone

</details>


### [155] [Graph-GSReg: Leveraging 3D Scene Graphs for Gaussian Splatting Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29782)
*Jaewon Lee,Mangyu Kong,Euntai Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a method for merging multiple 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes by reformulating 3DGS registration as a graph registration problem using a 3D scene graph, followed by self-supervised test-time optimization to reduce occlusion artifacts, achieving competitive registration accuracy and rendering quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Merging multiple 3DGS scenes is essential for large-scale 3D mapping and long-term map management, but existing solutions have limitations: learning-based methods require training on large datasets, and image-based optimization methods depend on coarse initialization from generic foundation models and incur expensive refinement.

Method: The method constructs a 3D scene graph from a 3DGS and its rendered images, reformulating 3DGS registration as a graph registration problem. This graph captures semantic and structural context for globally consistent alignment. A Self-Supervised Test-Time Optimization then refines the merged Gaussians to reduce hollows and floaters by enforcing visual consistency between original and merged scenes.

Result: Evaluated on real and synthetic benchmarks, the method demonstrates competitive registration accuracy and merged scene rendering quality compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed graph-based reformulation with self-supervised test-time optimization effectively addresses limitations of prior 3DGS merging methods, enabling seamless large-scale scene merging without requiring large training datasets or expensive coarse initialization.

Abstract: Merging multiple 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes into a single unified Gaussian representation is essential for large-scale 3D mapping and long-term map management. Despite its importance, this area remains underexplored, and existing solutions exhibit several limitations. Learning-based methods attempt direct correspondence between Gaussian primitives and require training on large 3DGS datasets. Image-based optimization methods depend heavily on coarse initialization from generic foundation models and often incur expensive refinement. We present \ourmodel. Our method constructs a 3D scene graph from a 3DGS and its rendered images, \textit{reformulating 3DGS registration as a graph registration problem}. The proposed 3D scene graph represents each 3DGS at a higher-level representation, enabling a globally consistent understanding of semantic information and structural context for accurate registration. To further construct a seamless unified scene, we introduce a Self-Supervised Test-Time Optimization. Naively merging two 3D Gaussian scenes often suffers from occlusion artifacts such as hollows and floaters. To alleviate this issue, we refine the merged Gaussians to preserve visual consistency between the original scenes and the merged scene. We evaluate our method on real and synthetic benchmarks, demonstrating competitive registration accuracy and merged scene rendering quality.

</details>


### [156] [OP3DSG: Open-Vocabulary Part-Aware 3D Scene Graph Generation for Real-World Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29786)
*Yirum Kim,Ue-Hwan Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OP3DSG is an open-vocabulary part-aware 3D scene graph generation framework that jointly models objects, parts, spatial relations, functional relations, and affordances, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the new UniGraph3D benchmark and demonstrating effectiveness as a perception backbone for real-world robotics tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DSG approaches are object-centric and encode limited relational information, restricting applicability in real-world scenarios requiring fine-grained understanding. The paper aims to enable open-vocabulary part-aware 3DSG generation with rich relational modeling.

Method: OP3DSG integrates (1) object-part knowledge-guided detection with part-aware 3D fusion to preserve small interaction-relevant components, and (2) a geometry-initialized prior graph with LLM-based refinement to reduce spurious relational predictions and enable efficient graph construction.

Result: OP3DSG achieves state-of-the-art performance on UniGraph3D, a newly introduced benchmark for part-aware perception and multi-level relational reasoning, and demonstrates effectiveness as a perception backbone in diverse real-world robotics tasks.

Conclusion: Open-vocabulary part-aware 3DSG generation with rich relational encoding outperforms existing object-centric approaches and has practical utility in robotics applications requiring fine-grained scene understanding.

Abstract: 3D scene graphs (3DSGs) provide a compact and structured abstraction of 3D environments. Although advances in foundation models have enabled open-vocabulary 3DSG generation, existing approaches remain object-centric and encode limited relational information -- restricting their applicability in real-world scenarios that require fine-grained understanding. We propose OP3DSG, an open-vocabulary part-aware 3DSG generation framework that constructs unified graphs that jointly model objects, interactive parts, spatial relations, functional relations, and affordances. OP3DSG integrates object-part knowledge-guided detection with part-aware 3D fusion to preserve small and interaction-relevant components, and employs a geometry-initialized prior graph with LLM-based refinement to reduce spurious relational predictions while enabling efficient graph construction. To systematically evaluate unified 3D scene graph construction, we introduce UniGraph3D, a benchmark designed for part-aware perception and multi-level relational reasoning. Experimental results show that OP3DSG achieves state-of-the-art performance and demonstrates its effectiveness as a perception backbone in diverse real-world robotics tasks.

</details>


### [157] [UniTriSplat: A Unified 3D Gaussian Splatting Framework with Uniform Spherical Rasterization for Universal Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29794)
*Yipeng Zhu,Huajian Huang,Tristan Braud,Sai-Kit Yeung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniTriSplat unifies 3D Gaussian Splatting for universal cameras by performing spherical Gaussian splatting via HEALPix discretization, enabling consistent rendering and optimization from narrow-FoV to 360-degree images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS frameworks are camera-specific and suffer from inconsistent solid-angle sampling, leading to degraded performance across heterogeneous camera models (perspective, fisheye, omnidirectional).

Method: UniTriSplat reformulates Gaussian splatting on the unit sphere using HEALPix discretization. It constructs a spherical sampling grid aligned with input angular resolution, derives forward rendering and gradient propagation in the spherical radian domain, and introduces a HEALPix-aware SSIM loss for perceptual quality.

Result: UniTriSplat consistently improves cross-camera generalization while preserving geometric fidelity and rendering quality across diverse camera models.

Conclusion: UniTriSplat improves cross-camera generalization and rendering quality across diverse camera models (perspective, fisheye, omnidirectional) by reformulating 3D Gaussian Splatting on the unit sphere.

Abstract: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) frameworks rely on camera-specific rasterization, suffering from inconsistent solid-angle sampling and degraded performance across heterogeneous camera models (e.g., perspective, fisheye, omnidirectional). To address this limitation, we propose UniTriSplat, a unified 3DGS framework for universal cameras that reformulates Gaussian splatting on the unit sphere via HEALPix discretization. Leveraging the equal-area property of HEALPix, we construct a spherical sampling grid aligned with the angular resolution of input images. We derive the forward rendering and gradient propagation of Gaussians directly in the spherical radian domain, yielding uniform optimization behavior from narrow-FoV images to full 360-degree panoramas. To enhance perceptual reconstruction quality, we additionally introduce a HEALPix-aware SSIM loss that respects spherical neighborhood structure. Extensive experiments across diverse camera models demonstrate that UniTriSplat consistently improves cross-camera generalization while preserving geometric fidelity and rendering quality.

</details>


### [158] [Concept Removal Guidance: Evidence-Calibrated Negative Guidance for Safe Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29801)
*Yoonseok Choi,Chaeyoung Oh,Hyunjun Choi,Seokin Seo,Kee-Eung Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CRG adaptively calibrates negative guidance at each diffusion step via closed-form constrained updates to remove unwanted concepts while minimally perturbing conditional trajectories, improving attack robustness and fidelity without training or external classifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time safety controls (fixed-weight negative guidance, dynamic reweighting, similarity-based methods) suffer from safety-fidelity trade-offs, brittleness under open-vocabulary compositional prompts, or neglect of evolving image evidence during denoising.

Method: CRG estimates unwanted-concept presence from the model's noise predictions at each step, then adaptively calibrates negative guidance via a closed-form constrained update that enforces a target presence threshold while minimally perturbing the conditional trajectory.

Result: Across red-teaming benchmarks, CRG reduces attack success rates while preserving benign fidelity, and extends to suppression targets like artist style and violence without fine-tuning or external classifiers.

Conclusion: Training-free adaptive negative guidance via step-wise constrained optimization effectively resolves the safety-fidelity trade-off and generalizes to diverse suppression targets.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts that elicit disallowed content, motivating reliable inference-time controls. A popular approach is negative guidance, which subtracts a negative prompt direction with a fixed weight. However, it often forces a safety-fidelity trade-off, causing artifacts or prompt drift when over-applied and failing under attacks when under-applied. Dynamic variants reweight guidance using posterior-odds signals, which can be brittle for open-vocabulary compositional prompts, while lightweight similarity-based methods ignore the evolving image evidence along the denoising trajectory. We introduce Concept Removal Guidance (CRG), a training-free method that estimates unwanted-concept presence at each diffusion step from the model's noise predictions, and adaptively calibrates negative guidance via a closed-form constrained update enforcing a target presence threshold while minimally perturbing the conditional trajectory. Across red-teaming benchmarks, CRG reduces attack success rates while preserving benign fidelity, and extends to additional suppression targets such as artist style and violence without fine-tuning or external classifiers.

</details>


### [159] [Clearer Sight, Fewer Lies: Oriented Pickup Preference Optimization for Multimodal Hallucination Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29805)
*Xin Zou,Haolin Deng,Yibo Yan,Shuliang Liu,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Xuming Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OPPO improves MLLM faithfulness by learning preferences over the strength of visual evidence, contrasting responses under stronger, anchored, and weaker evidence views, achieving state-of-the-art results on hallucination benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs hallucinate because their generation preferences are insufficiently calibrated to visual evidence, causing reliance on linguistic priors rather than faithful grounding; empirical observation shows strengthening query-relevant visual evidence via the model's own attention improves accuracy.

Method: Proposes Oriented Pickup Preference Optimization (OPPO), an evidence-aware alignment objective that contrasts the same faithful response under stronger, anchored, and weaker visual evidence views, combined with span-level and token-level regularization for training stability.

Result: OPPO consistently outperforms baseline methods across hallucination and general-purpose benchmarks; theoretical analysis shows ordered evidence margins induce a positive lower bound on local visual sensitivity.

Conclusion: Many MLLM failures stem from insufficient tendency to trust already-attended visual evidence rather than missing perception; OPPO effectively addresses this through ordered visual-evidence alignment.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are prone to hallucination as their generation preferences are insufficiently calibrated to visual evidence, causing them to fall back on linguistic priors, rather than faithful grounding. In this work, we start from an empirical observation: when query-relevant visual evidence is explicitly strengthened using the model's own attention, generation becomes more accurate, suggesting that many failures do not arise solely from missing perception, but from an insufficient tendency to trust the evidence the model has already attended to. Motivated by this finding, we propose Oriented Pickup Preference Optimization (\texttt{OPPO}), an evidence-aware alignment objective that learns preferences over the strength of visual evidence, rather than only response quality. Concretely, \texttt{OPPO} contrasts the same faithful response under stronger, anchored, weaker-evidence views, turning naive visual preference into ordered visual-evidence alignment. We further combine this objective with fine-grained span-level and token-level regularization to stabilize the training. Besides, we provide a theoretical analysis showing that ordered evidence margins induce a positive lower bound on local visual sensitivity. Extensive evaluations across hallucination and general-purpose benchmarks demonstrate that \texttt{OPPO} consistently outperforms baseline methods.

</details>


### [160] [Consistency as Inductive Bias: Learning Cross-View Invariance for Robust Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29812)
*Xin Zou,Haolin Deng,Yibo Yan,Shuliang Liu,Kening Zheng,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Xuming Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConsistRoll injects cross-view consistency into RLVR training for MLLMs by reusing GRPO's group-sampling mechanism, pairing original and semantically invariant transformed views in the same generation group and assigning joint rewards, improving multimodal reasoning without extra overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifies missing cross-view consistency bias in MLLMs; standard RLVR assigns pointwise rewards independently to each view, and even with data augmentation, transformed views lack a consistency constraint, leading to reward saturation and no generalization signal.

Method: ConsistRoll reuses GRPO's group-sampling mechanism: original and semantically invariant transformed views are placed in the same generation group; a joint reward is assigned only when paired completions are both correct and consistent, turning consistency into an online credit-assignment signal.

Result: Theoretically shows cross-view consistency is a valid inductive bias with a cross-view correction term that penalizes view dependence and alleviates advantage collapse; empirically achieves robust improvements across math, general-purpose, and hallucination benchmarks.

Conclusion: ConsistRoll is a simple, effective method that injects cross-view consistency into RLVR without extra generation overhead or annotations, yielding consistent gains in multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Inductive biases steer learning toward generalizable solutions by encoding task structure. In this work, we identify a crucial missing bias in MLLMs: cross-view consistency, \textit{i.e.}, semantically invariant views of the same instance should lead to the same answer. Standard reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) objectives do not impose this constraint, but instead assign pointwise rewards to each visual input. Even with data augmentation (DA), transformed views are typically rewarded independently, providing little signal once within-view rewards saturate. We propose \textbf{ConsistRoll}, a simple but effective method that injects cross-view consistency into RLVR training by reusing the group-sampling mechanism of GRPO. Specifically, ConsistRoll places original and semantically invariant transformed views in the same generation group, and assigns a joint reward only when paired completions are both correct and consistent. In this way, ConsistRoll turns consistency into an online credit-assignment signal, \textbf{without extra generation overhead and annotations}. Theoretically, we show that cross-view consistency is a valid inductive bias, and ConsistRoll introduces a cross-view correction term absent from DA, penalizing view dependence and alleviating advantage collapse. Comprehensive benchmarks across math, general-purpose, hallucination domains confirm that ConsistRoll achieves robust improvements in multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [161] [Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion-Image: Advancing Masked Discrete Diffusion for High-Resolution Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29814)
*Shufan Li,Greg Heinrich,Hanrong Ye,Yonggan Fu,Aditya Grover,Jan Kautz,Pavlo Molchanov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion-Image is a state-of-the-art masked discrete diffusion model for text-to-image synthesis that introduces a token-editing mechanism for dynamic revision of unmasked tokens and a Grouped Cross-Entropy (GCE) objective to address signal sparsity in large-vocabulary discrete tokenizers, achieving strong results on GenEval (0.90), DPG (86.9), and HPSv3 (10.76).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard masked discrete diffusion models (MDMs) lack self-correcting capability since discrete tokens cannot be modified once unmasked, and increasing vocabulary size for better reconstruction fidelity introduces optimization difficulties due to sparse per-token training signals.

Method: (1) A token-editing mechanism that allows dynamic revision of already-unmasked tokens during inference; (2) A Grouped Cross-Entropy (GCE) objective that assigns positive learning signals to neighboring tokens in embedding space to alleviate sparsity; (3) A custom fused operator for GCE to reduce VRAM usage in large-vocabulary settings.

Result: Achieves 0.90 on GenEval, 86.9 on DPG, and 10.76 on HPSv3, demonstrating substantial improvements in both training efficiency and image fidelity over prior masked discrete image generators.

Conclusion: The proposed innovations effectively address key limitations of masked discrete diffusion models, enabling high-fidelity text-to-image synthesis without sacrificing the advantages of discrete token representations.

Abstract: We propose Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion-Image, a state-of-the-art masked discrete diffusion model (MDM) for high-resolution text-to-image synthesis. Compared with prior work on masked image generation, Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion-Image addresses two key challenges. First, unlike continuous diffusion models which progressively refine latent representations across the entire image, standard MDMs lack self-correcting capability because discrete tokens cannot be modified once they are unmasked. Second, although increasing the vocabulary size of discrete image tokenizers improves reconstruction fidelity, it introduces optimization difficulties for generative modeling as the per-token training signal becomes increasingly sparse. To address the first challenge, Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion-Image incorporates a token-editing mechanism that enables the model to dynamically revise already-unmasked tokens during inference, similar to how a sculptor iteratively refines their work. To tackle the second challenge, we propose a Grouped Cross-Entropy (GCE) objective that assigns positive learning signals to tokens neighboring the ground truth in embedding space, thereby alleviating signal sparsity. To further improve training efficiency, we implement a custom fused operator for GCE that significantly reduces VRAM usage in large-vocabulary settings. Experimental results demonstrate that these innovations substantially improve both training efficiency and image fidelity of masked discrete image generators, achieving a score of 0.90 on GenEval, 86.9 on DPG and 10.76 of HPSv3.

</details>


### [162] [Learning Cross-view Correspondences for Geo-localization on Planetary Surfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29821)
*Hong Minh Nguyen,Marcus Märtens,Tat-Jun Chin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new cross-view geo-localization benchmark for planetary surfaces (specifically the Moon), featuring physically rendered surface panoramas and overhead tiles from a high-resolution lunar terrain model. The dataset contains 10,438 ground view panoramas with matching overhead images, plus off-center localization tiles. A transformer-based method achieves promising retrieval accuracy, showing that learning-based cross-view localization can serve as a GNSS alternative for planetary exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global position awareness is critical for planetary surface exploration, but satellite-based positioning systems are unavailable and onboard odometry drifts over time. Orbital mapping products (overhead imagery, terrain maps) provide global context, yet aligning them with surface observations is difficult due to large viewpoint differences, low texture, repetitive terrain, and appearance changes caused by varying illumination and topography.

Method: The authors construct a cross-view geo-localization benchmark using physically rendered 360° surface panoramas and overhead tiles derived from a high-resolution lunar terrain model. The dataset includes 10,438 ground panoramas with precisely co-located overhead images, plus overlapping tiles for off-center localization. A state-of-the-art transformer-based geo-localization method is trained from scratch on this data.

Result: The transformer-based method achieves successful retrieval accuracy on the lunar cross-view localization task, demonstrating that learning-based approaches can be effectively applied to planetary surfaces.

Conclusion: Learning-based cross-view localization methods can serve as a viable vision-based alternative to global navigation satellite systems for planetary surface exploration.

Abstract: Maintaining global position awareness is a fundamental challenge for planetary surface exploration, since satellite-based positioning systems are unavailable and onboard odometry drifts over time. Although orbital mapping products, such as overhead imagery and terrain-derived maps, provide global context, aligning them with surface observations is challenging due to large viewpoint differences, low texture, repetitive terrain, and drastic changes in appearance caused by varying illumination and topography. We introduce a new cross-view geo-localization benchmark built from physically rendered surface panoramas and overhead tiles derived from a high-resolution lunar terrain model. Our dataset contains 10438 ground views rendered as 360$^\circ$ surface panoramas with matching overhead images precisely centered at the same location. Additionally, a set of overlapping tiles is provided to study off-center localization with multiple plausible candidates per panorama. We study the performance of a state-of-the-art transformer-based geo-localization method on our data, by training it from scratch and reporting retrieval accuracy. Our results demonstrate that learning-based cross-view localization methods can be successfully applied to the domain of planetary surfaces, providing a vision-based alternative to global navigation satellite systems.

</details>


### [163] [HomeDiffusion: Zero-Shot Object Customization with Multi-View Representation Learning for Indoor Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29828)
*Guoqiu Li,Jin Song,Yiyun Fei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HomeDiffusion is a zero-shot object customization method that uses multi-viewpoint reference images and diffusion models to generate objects that harmonize with background scenes while preserving high-fidelity details, outperforming existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot object customization methods suffer from detail divergence (patterns, curves) from the reference object and cannot generate poses that harmonize with background scenes, especially for asymmetrical objects lacking multi-viewpoint information.

Method: HomeDiffusion constructs a novel multi-angle furniture and indoor scene dataset, uses multi-viewpoint images of the reference object as input, extracts high-fidelity details during diffusion, and performs cross-attention between those details and noise latents in latent space.

Result: Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate superior performance over existing zero-shot and few-shot object customization approaches.

Conclusion: Leveraging multi-viewpoint information enables more accurate and harmonious object customization generation while preserving fine-grained details.

Abstract: Recently, zero-shot object customization generation methods have rapidly developed and shown tremendous potential for applications. For instance, in the e-commerce domain, consumers can observe the visual effect of furniture placed within their personal living spaces or clothes worn on their own bodies. Many existing approaches perform object customization generation based on diffusion models and extracted reference object features. However, the generated object significantly diverges from the original reference object in details such as patterns and curves. Particularly for asymmetrical reference objects, the absence of comprehensive multi-viewpoint information prevents the generation of object poses that harmonize with the background scene. To address these shortcomings, we have constructed a novel dataset comprising multi-angle images of furniture and indoor scenes. Based on diffusion models, we introduce HomeDiffusion, which can leverage multi-viewpoint images of the same reference object to accurately generate visually harmonious object poses within specified areas of the background scene. During the diffusion process, we further extract high-fidelity details of the reference object and perform cross-attention with the noise latents in the latent space, thereby ensuring the preservation of details in the customized object generation. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance over other existing zero-shot as well as few-shot object customization approaches.

</details>


### [164] [Robust Trajectory Distillation: Hybrid Reweighting Meets Teacher-Inspired Targets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29837)
*Kaifeng Chen,Lechao Cheng,Jiyang Li,Shengeng Tang,Fan Zhang,Yantao Pan,Yaxiong Wang,Tuanrui Hui,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a trajectory-based dataset distillation framework (SGR + TIAT) that suppresses label noise and preserves transferable knowledge without requiring clean subsets or relabeling, outperforming state-of-the-art DD methods under various noise types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset distillation under noisy supervision risks condensing corrupted associations alongside useful signals, degrading robustness. Existing noisy-label remedies rely on clean anchors and tight coupling between noise estimation and optimization, which are misaligned with DD's goal of compact, plug-and-play supervision.

Method: The framework has two components: (1) Selective Guidance Reweighting (SGR) — fuses global forgetting patterns (second-split forgetting) with local neighborhood consistency to progressively prioritize clean supervision along the teacher trajectory; (2) Teacher-Inspired Auxiliary Targets (TIAT) — injects auxiliary residual guidance distilled from intermediate teacher dynamics to reinforce informative signals while maintaining internal consistency.

Result: Consistent gains over state-of-the-art DD baselines across symmetric, asymmetric, and real-world noise conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed framework is robust, label-preserving, computationally lightweight, and broadly applicable, yielding distilled datasets with cleaner and richer representations under noisy supervision.

Abstract: Dataset distillation (DD) condenses large corpora into compact, information-rich subsets for efficient training and reuse. However, under noisy supervision, DD risks condensing corrupted associations together with useful signals, degrading robustness. Conventional noisy-label remedies (sample selection, loss weighting, label correction) tightly couple noise estimation with model optimization, often require clean anchors, and can amplify confirmation bias-assumptions that are misaligned with DD's goal of compact, plug-and-play supervision. We therefore propose a trajectory-based DD framework that jointly suppresses noise and preserves transferable knowledge without relabeling or clean subsets. It comprises two complementary components: Selective Guidance Reweighting (SGR), which fuses global forgetting patterns (second-split forgetting) with local neighborhood consistency into a progressive reweighting scheme that prioritizes clean supervision along the teacher trajectory; and Teacher-Inspired Auxiliary Targets (TIAT), which inject auxiliary residual guidance distilled from intermediate teacher dynamics to reinforce informative signals while remaining internally consistent. Together, SGR and TIAT produce distilled datasets with cleaner and richer representations under noisy supervision. The framework is robust, label-preserving, computationally lightweight, and broadly applicable, yielding consistent gains over state-of-the-art DD baselines across symmetric, asymmetric, and real-world noise.

</details>


### [165] [Bricker to BRACE: A Bracket Exposure RAW Dataset and Restoration Model for Flicker-Banding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29845)
*Zihan Zhou,Libo Zhu,Jue Gong,Zhiyi Zhou,Jiezhang Cao,Yong Guo,Yulun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BRACE is a multi-frame bracketed RAW restoration model for removing flicker-banding artifacts from screen-captured images, using a synthetic-real dataset (Bricker) built via ray-tracing simulation, frequency-aware banding prior, and a multi-scale spatial cross-attention modulator (MSCAM). It achieves state-of-the-art performance and introduces the Stripe Frequency Consistency (SFC) metric for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-frame methods with simplified parametric stripe models cannot reliably distinguish flicker-banding artifacts from genuine texture, and the complex FB morphologies vary significantly across exposure settings.

Method: (1) Bricker dataset: synthetic-real bracketed RAW dataset via ray-tracing-based physical simulation and automated multi-exposure capture; (2) BRACE model: multi-frame restoration using frequency-aware banding prior and multi-scale spatial cross-attention modulator (MSCAM) for cross-exposure spatial fusion; (3) Stripe Frequency Consistency (SFC) metric for evaluation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on both synthetic and real benchmarks.

Conclusion: Multi-frame bracketed RAW restoration effectively addresses flicker-banding where single-frame methods fail, and the proposed dataset and model advance the state of the art.

Abstract: Flicker-banding (FB), arises from temporal aliasing between a camera's rolling shutter and a display's brightness modulation, degrading screen-captured image readability with color shifts and jagged patterns. Existing single-frame methods with simplified parametric stripe models cannot reliably distinguish these artifacts from genuine texture. To address this, we conduct a systematic analysis of complex FB morphologies and reveal their significant variation across exposure settings, motivating a multi-frame bracketed RAW restoration paradigm. We construct Bricker, a synthetic-real bracketed RAW dataset built via ray-tracing-based physical simulation and automated multi-exposure capture tool. We further propose BRACE: Bracketed RAW Flicker-Banding Removal, a multi-frame restoration model that utilizes frequency-aware banding prior and a multi-scale spatial cross-attention modulator (MSCAM) for cross-exposure spatial fusion. We also introduce the Stripe Frequency Consistency (SFC) metric to evaluate banding removal. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both synthetic and real benchmarks. Our dataset and code are available at: https://github.com/ZZH-qwq/BRACE.

</details>


### [166] [See Only When Needed: Context-Aware Attention Intervention for Mitigating Hallucinations in LVLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29847)
*Yuqing Lei,Wenbo Lyu,Yingjun Du,Xiantong Zhen,Cees G. M. Snoek,Ling Shao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAI is a training-free, inference-time mechanism that selectively strengthens visual grounding only for uncertain tokens and relevant regions, reducing object hallucinations in LVLMs while preserving fluency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior training-free hallucination mitigation methods uniformly strengthen visual signals, which can amplify irrelevant regions and introduce spurious evidence, harming fluency.

Method: CAI enforces a "see only when needed" principle via two-axis selectivity: (1) where to look — deriving token-specific visual relevance from early-layer representations to localize semantically aligned regions; (2) when to intervene — applying conservative, entropy- and depth-gated attention tilt only for uncertainty-spiking tokens in deeper layers where visual grounding degrades, leaving confident tokens and irrelevant regions unchanged.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation across multiple LVLM backbones and benchmarks; yields consistent improvements even without contrastive decoding.

Conclusion: CAI is characterized as a KL-minimal attention reweighting with bounded interference under inactive gates or small tilts, effectively reducing hallucinations while preserving linguistic fluency.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) excel at multimodal tasks but remain prone to object hallucinations. Prior training-free remedies often uniformly strengthen visual signals, which may also amplify irrelevant regions and introduce spurious evidence, harming fluency. We propose Context-aware Attention Intervention (CAI), a training-free inference-time mechanism that enforces a see only when needed principle via two-axis selectivity: where to look and when to intervene. At each decoding step, CAI derives token-specific visual relevance from early-layer representations to localize semantically aligned regions, and applies a conservative, entropy- and depth-gated attention tilt only for uncertainty-spiking tokens in deeper layers where visual grounding degrades, leaving confident tokens and irrelevant regions largely unchanged. This targeted intervention strengthens visual grounding while preserving linguistic fluency, and it yields consistent improvements even without contrastive decoding, which remains optional as an auxiliary bias-suppression module. Extensive experiments across multiple LVLM backbones and benchmarks show that CAI achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation, and our analysis characterizes CAI as a KL-minimal attention reweighting with bounded interference under inactive gates or small tilts. Code is available at https://github.com/Iris1946/CAI.

</details>


### [167] [Efficient Visual Pointing for Embodied AI:Agent-Driven Data Synthesis, Cross-Block Attention, and Iterative Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29850)
*Zijian Hong,Qi Lv,Yuxiang Xie,Jianming Xing,Xiang Deng,Weili Guan,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PointArena 2026 solution achieves 77.2% accuracy (2nd place) by combining agent-driven synthesis (55K+ outputs), a deterministic steerable-data pipeline (10K verified samples), and two model-side modules (AttnRes for steerability, ABC correction for coordinate grounding) with category-aware routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual pointing maps language instructions to pixel coordinates, a core embodied AI skill. The authors target three specific failure modes in existing approaches.

Method: Three-pronged approach: (1) Agent-driven synthesis builds large semantic and anchor-relative candidate pools (55,372 processed outputs, 53,772 de-duplicated IDs, 37,574 trainable rows). (2) A deterministic steerable-data pipeline creates a verified 10,000-sample main set plus reserves using masks, templates, and path verification. (3) Two model-side modules: AttnRes adds gated cross-block attention for steerability; ABC correction encodes perturbed coordinates with visual features for coordinate grounding. Category-aware routing combines complementary specialists.

Result: 77.2% overall accuracy, 2nd place on PointArena 2026 benchmark. Local validation: 93.9% Affordance, 82.6% Spatial Relation, 78.2% Reasoning, 70.4% Counting, 63.0% Steerability.

Conclusion: The system effectively addresses three failure modes via data-level synthesis, pipeline verification, and model-side architectural improvements, demonstrating strong category-specific performance across diverse visual pointing tasks.

Abstract: Visual pointing maps a language instruction to pixel co ordinates, a core skill for embodied AI. We describe our PointArena 2026 solution, which achieves 77.2% overall accuracy and ranks second on the benchmark. The ap proach targets three failure modes. First, agent-driven syn thesis builds large semantic and anchor-relative candidate pools; the server inventory contains 55,372 processed out puts, 53,772 de-duplicated sample IDs, and 37,574 train able completed or accepted rows. Second, a determinis tic steerable-data pipeline creates a verified 10,000-sample main set, plus reserve samples, using masks, templates, and path verification. Third, two model-side modules address complementary errors: AttnRes adds gated cross-block at tention for steerability, while ABC correction encodes per turbed coordinates with visual features for general coordi nate grounding. Category-aware routing combines comple mentary specialists; local validation used to select experts records 93.9% Affordance, 82.6% Spatial Relation, 78.2% Reasoning, 70.4% Counting, and 63.0% Steerability.

</details>


### [168] [RainODE: Continuous-Time Precipitation Forecasting with Latent Neural ODEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29855)
*Yeeun Seong,Doyi Kim,Minseok Seo,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RainODE reformulates precipitation forecasting as a continuous-time dynamical system using Neural ODEs with stochastic Brownian Bridge refinement, enabling arbitrary-time inference with sharp predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precipitation forecasting faces a trade-off between temporal resolution and computational cost/observational constraints. Dense discrete modeling is expensive and limited by data availability, motivating a continuous-time formulation that can infer at arbitrary temporal resolutions without dense observations.

Method: RainODE models precipitation evolution in latent space using a Neural ODE for continuous-time deterministic dynamics (capturing advective motion), combined with a stochastic Brownian Bridge module to refine residual non-advective intensity variations (growth, decay, sub-grid variability).

Result: Evaluated on SEVIR and a new RAPID dataset, RainODE achieves consistent improvements across multiple temporal intervals and precipitation regimes, maintaining sharp predictions at arbitrary time resolutions.

Conclusion: The hybrid deterministic-stochastic continuous-time framework effectively overcomes temporal resolution limitations in precipitation forecasting, enabling flexible inference while preserving both advective consistency and fine-grained structure.

Abstract: In precipitation forecasting, not only accuracy but also temporal resolution is critical. However, increasing temporal resolution is constrained by observational limitations and the computational cost of dense discrete modeling. To overcome this limitation, we reformulate precipitation forecasting as a continuous-time dynamical system and propose RainODE, a framework that models precipitation evolution in latent space using a Neural ODE. This formulation enables derivative-consistent temporal dynamics and captures the dominant large-scale advective motion of precipitation systems. Nevertheless, a purely deterministic ODE struggles to represent non-advective intensity changes such as localized growth, decay, and sub-grid variability, often leading to over-smoothed predictions. To address this issue, we introduce a stochastic source modeling module based on a Brownian Bridge formulation, which refines residual intensity variations and restores fine-grained structures while preserving advective consistency. By combining deterministic continuous dynamics with stochastic refinement, RainODE enables arbitrary-time inference while maintaining sharp predictions. Experiments on SEVIR and the newly introduced Radar-based Precipitation Integrated Dataset (RAPID) demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple temporal intervals and precipitation regimes. The code is available at https://github.com/SeongYE/RainODE.

</details>


### [169] [SUMO: Segment and Track Any Motion with Nonlinear State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29861)
*Kexin Tian,Sixu Li,Keshu Wu,Yang Zhou,Zhengzhong Tu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SUMO is a zero-shot, training-free framework that integrates nonlinear dynamics (via a State Space Model and Unscented Filter) with vision-based segmentation to achieve state-of-the-art performance in both Visual Object Tracking and Moving Object Segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VOT and MOS methods rely predominantly on visual cues and fail in real-world scenarios where object motions are complex and nonlinear.

Method: Proposes a nonlinear State Space Model (SSM) inspired by robotics, a Selective Unscented Filter (SUF) with joint scoring and dynamic multi-source fusion for state estimation, and a memory selection mechanism to evaluate frame reliability.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on both VOT and MOS tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating nonlinear dynamics with vision-based segmentation in a zero-shot, training-free manner effectively handles complex motion patterns and yields superior tracking and segmentation accuracy.

Abstract: Visual Object Tracking (VOT) and Moving Object Segmentation (MOS) are two fundamental tasks in computer vision that involve both spatial and temporal object dynamics. Existing methods rely predominantly on visual cues and thus often falter in real-world scenarios where object motions are inherently complex and nonlinear. To address this limitation, we propose SUMO, a zero-shot, training-free, unified framework integrating nonlinear dynamics with vision-based segmentation for accurate and consistent VOT and MOS. Specifically, we develop a nonlinear State Space Model (SSM) inspired by robotics principles to capture the complex object dynamics. Building on this model, we propose a Selective Unscented Filter (SUF) for accurate state estimation, which features a joint scoring mechanism and dynamically fuses multi-source predictions to identify the most plausible object state over time. Furthermore, we apply a memory selection mechanism to evaluate the reliability of memory frames. Our extensive experimental results show that SUMO achieves state-of-the-art performance on both VOT and MOS tasks.

</details>


### [170] [LWDrive: Layer-Wise World-Model-Guided Vision-Language Model Planning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29879)
*Chen Yang,Yuhao Wei,Ze Xu,Ziheng Zou,Shuang Liang,Delin Ouyang,Lingfeng Qi,Jie Li,Guofa Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LWDrive is a VLM-based autonomous driving planning framework that refines coarse trajectory candidates using layer-wise world-model supervision, future-frame generation, and a Foresight Cascade Planner to achieve geometrically accurate, multi-view-grounded planning, scoring 92.0 on NAVSIM and 89.6 on NAVSIM-v2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs provide semantic understanding and commonsense reasoning for E2E-AD but produce only coarse driving intentions with insufficient geometric accuracy, future awareness, and multi-view grounding for trajectory planning.

Method: LWDrive treats VLM output as an intent-aware coarse plan, expands a diverse candidate space, and progressively refines candidates via a Foresight Cascade Planner (FCP). It introduces future-frame generation supervision to inject predictive dynamics into VLM hidden states, then FCP leverages multi-layer VLM features, historical temporal states, Action-Query representations, and multi-view BEV features for coarse-to-fine refinement. A score head selects the best trajectory.

Result: Achieves 92.0 on NAVSIM benchmark and 89.6 on NAVSIM-v2.

Conclusion: LWDrive effectively addresses VLM trajectory generation limitations by integrating world-model-guided coarse-to-fine refinement, demonstrating strong benchmark performance and enabling more precise, future-aware, and multi-view-grounded autonomous driving planning.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) provide powerful semantic understanding and commonsense reasoning for End-to-End Autonomous Driving (E2E-AD) planning. However, trajectories directly generated by VLMs often encode only coarse driving intentions and remain insufficient for geometrically accurate, future-aware, and multi-view-grounded planning. To address these limitations, we develop the Layer-Wise World-Model-Guided Driving framework (LWDrive). LWDrive is a VLM planning framework that refines coarse trajectories through layer-wise world-model guidance. Instead of treating the VLM output as the final trajectory, LWDrive uses it as an intent-aware coarse plan, expands a diverse candidate space around it, and progressively refines the candidates through a Foresight Cascade Planner (FCP). Specifically, we introduce future-frame generation supervision to encourage the VLM to learn forward-looking scene representations, thereby injecting planning-relevant predictive dynamics into its internal hidden states. Built upon these world-model-supervised representations, FCP exploits VLM features across multiple layers and integrates historical temporal states, Action-Query representations, and current-frame multi-view Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) features to refine candidate trajectories in a coarse-to-fine manner. This design enables progressive correction of spatial positions and motion trends while grounding trajectory refinement with multi-view scene cues and preserving the high-level driving intention produced by the large model. Finally, a score head evaluates the refined candidates and selects the best trajectory as the final planning output. Experiments show that LWDrive achieves a score of 92.0 on the NAVSIM benchmark and 89.6 on NAVSIM-v2. Code and models will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [171] [IREU: Identity-Related Encoder-Only Unlearning for Customized Portrait Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29880)
*Chaoyi Shi,Shanshan Zhang,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes IREU, a method for identity unlearning in Customized Portrait Generation that selectively perturbs identity-related features to suppress generation for target identities while preserving fidelity for other identities, achieving generalization across generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Customized Portrait Generation technologies pose significant privacy risks by enabling the generation of fake visual information of specific individuals. There is a need for methods that allow public generators to suppress their generation ability for a particular person when requested, but no previous methods exist for identity unlearning in CPG.

Method: The authors first propose a simple baseline that updates the image encoder by minimizing identity similarity between generated and input images for target identities to be unlearned while maximizing it for identities to be retained. They then propose IREU, which (1) locates identity-related features in an offline manner and (2) only performs feature perturbations on those localized features, avoiding global perturbation that harms fidelity for retained identities.

Result: IREU achieves better identity unlearning performance for target identities to be unlearned while maintaining high fidelity for other identities to be retained. The unlearned image encoder generalizes across different generators with the same encoder without requiring fine-tuning.

Conclusion: IREU effectively solves the identity unlearning problem for Customized Portrait Generation by precisely targeting identity-related features, balancing unlearning efficacy with generation fidelity, and offering practical deployment advantages through cross-generator generalization.

Abstract: Customized Portrait Generation (CPG) technologies have been widely used to generate high-fidelity person images given an input image indicating the identity and a text prompt indicating the required edits. Yet these methods pose significant privacy risks by spreading fake visual information. Against such risks, each public generator should be able to suppress its generation ability for a particular person when requested. Therefore, in this work we investigate the identity unlearning problem for CPG. Since there are no previous methods in this field, we propose a simple baseline that updates the image encoder by minimizing identity similarity between generated and input images for target identities to be unlearned, while maximizing it for identities to be retained. However, we find such a global perturbation in the feature space harms the fidelity of generated images for other identities to be retained. To solve this problem, we propose a novel method IREU, which first locates identity-related features in an offline manner and then only performs feature perturbations on them. The experimental results show that our proposed method IREU achieves better identity unlearning performance for target identities to be unlearned, and also keeps high fidelity for other identities to be retained. In addition, our unlearned image encoder is generalizable across different generators with the same encoder without fine-tuning, which is friendly for deployment in practice.

</details>


### [172] [Building artificial intelligence virtual tissue (AIVT) for tissue state representation, feature prediction, and dynamic simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29883)
*Qiqi Lu,Qianjin Feng,Shaoqun Zeng,Shenghua Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes AI Virtual Tissue (AIVT), an AI framework using spatial multimodal data to model tissue states and transitions, enabling unified representation, feature prediction, and spatiotemporal simulation of tissues in health and disease.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional computational modeling fails to capture the complexity of tissues as spatially organized, multiscale biological systems. The authors aim to leverage AI to overcome these limitations for understanding tissue homeostasis and pathological remodeling.

Method: AIVT is an AI framework grounded in spatial multimodal data, designed to learn unified, spatially resolved, and dynamically manipulatable representations of tissue state. It includes core capabilities for tissue state representation/analysis, molecular/morphological feature prediction, and spatiotemporal tissue dynamics simulation.

Result: The paper outlines the fundamental assumptions, core capabilities, architectural components, and data/algorithm foundations of the AIVT framework as a conceptual approach for AI-driven tissue modeling.

Conclusion: AIVT provides a new paradigm for modeling tissue complexity, enabling comprehensive characterization and simulation of tissue states and transitions across health and disease.

Abstract: Modeling tissue states and their transitions is essential for understanding tissue homeostasis in health and pathological remodeling in disease. However, conventional computational modeling approaches are inadequate to capture the complexity of tissues as spatially organized, multiscale biological systems. Artificial intelligence (AI) has shown a remarkable ability for representing intricate systems, creating new opportunities to characterize tissue states and their transitions. Here, we propose the concept of AI virtual tissue (AIVT), an AI framework grounded in spatial multimodal data for modeling tissues in health and disease. AIVT is designed to learn unified, spatially resolved, and dynamically manipulatable representations of tissue state, enabling tissue state representation and analysis, molecular and morphological feature prediction, and simulation of spatiotemporal tissue dynamics. We outline the fundamental assumptions, core capabilities, architectural components, as well as data and algorithm foundations of AIVT as a framework for AI-driven tissue modeling.

</details>


### [173] [LLM-based Multimodal Personality Recognition via Facial Action Unit-Text Semantic Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29900)
*Tianyi Zhang,Wei Shan,Yuan Zong,Tianhua Qi,Wenming Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes an LLM-based framework that fuses facial action units (AUs) converted into textual descriptions with textual responses for personality recognition in asynchronous video interviews, achieving superior performance on the AVI-6 benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unimodal (text-only LLM) methods ignore facial cues, while multimodal methods using full-face images or sparse frames miss fine-grained temporal dynamics needed for accurate personality assessment.

Method: Converts AU sequences into interpretable textual descriptions, fuses them with textual responses via an LLM, and adds a lightweight regression head to output continuous personality scores without disrupting the semantic space.

Result: Outperforms most baselines on AVI-6 benchmark with lower prediction errors and stronger correlations with human ratings. AU-derived semantics provide complementary non-verbal cues; decoupling semantic understanding from regression improves training stability and interpretability.

Conclusion: AU-text fusion offers a psychologically grounded and computationally efficient framework for personality recognition in AVIs.

Abstract: Personality recognition in asynchronous video interviews (AVIs) has become increasingly important due to their widespread adoption in modern recruitment. Existing approaches often rely on large language models (LLMs) to analyze textual responses of interviewees in AVI. However, unimodel methods often suffer from information loss (e.g., ignore facial cues). In contrast, multimodal methods that employ full-face images or sparsely sampled frames can discard fine-grained temporal dynamics critical for accurate personality assessment. To overcome these limitations, we propose an LLM-based framework that semantically fuse facial action units (AUs) with textual responses of AVI. AU sequences are first converted into interpretable textual descriptions, which are then fused with participants' textual responses through an LLM. A lightweight regression head transforms the resulting embeddings into continuous personality scores without disrupting the underlying semantic space. Experiments on the AVI-6 benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements over most baselines, with lower prediction errors and stronger correlations with human-rated scores across multiple traits. Further analysis reveals that AU-derived semantic representations offer complementary non-verbal cues to textual responses. Decoupling semantic understanding from regression prediction within the LLM also leads to greater training stability and clearer interpretability. Overall, these findings demonstrate that AU-text fusion provides a psychologically grounded and computationally efficient framework for personality recognition in AVIs.

</details>


### [174] [StrucTab: A Structured Optimization Framework for Table Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29905)
*Gengluo Li,Shangpin Peng,Chengquan Zhang,Binghong Wu,Hao Feng,Weinong Wang,Pengyuan Lyu,Huawen Shen,Xingyu Wan,Zhuotao Tian,Han Hu,Can Ma,Yu Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StrucTab is a table parsing model that improves vision-language models via intermediate structural supervision (decomposing parsing into subtasks like row-column counting and merged-cell analysis) and a unified RL framework (Uni-TabRL) with decomposed rewards (validity, structure, content), achieving SOTA results on public benchmarks and the new TableVerse-5K dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs for table parsing rely on direct end-to-end supervision without explicit intermediate reasoning, and RL-based optimization suffers from unstable/ambiguous reward designs, limiting performance gains.

Method: (1) Modeling: Decomposes table parsing into human-inspired subtasks (row-column counting, merged-cell analysis) unified via sequential reasoning. (2) Optimization: Uni-TabRL framework with decomposed rewards (validity, structure, content) for stable RL signals. (3) Evaluation: TableVerse-5K benchmark with diverse real-world table scenarios.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on all evaluated public benchmarks and significant improvements on the proposed TableVerse-5K benchmark.

Conclusion: Explicit structural modeling and decomposed reward optimization effectively improve table parsing performance, validated by extensive experiments.

Abstract: Table parsing aims to convert table images into structured, machine-readable representations, a task requiring the joint perception of complex spatial layouts and textual content. While recent vision-language models (VLMs) enable end-to-end parsing, they typically rely on direct supervision of the final output, thereby bypassing the explicit intermediate reasoning that is crucial for understanding complex table structures. Furthermore, attempts to optimize these models using reinforcement learning (RL) are often hindered by unstable or ambiguous reward designs, limiting potential performance gains. To address these limitations, we propose StrucTab, a table parsing model learned through intermediate structural supervision and reward decomposition. At the modeling level, by decomposing the parsing process into human-inspired subtasks, such as row-column counting and merged-cell analysis, StrucTab progressively unifies them through a sequential reasoning strategy. At the optimization level, we introduce Uni-TabRL, a unified RL framework that leverages decomposed rewards (validity, structure, and content) to provide stable and informative optimization signals. Finally, at the evaluation level, we present TableVerse-5K, a large-scale, challenging benchmark encompassing diverse, real-world table scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of StrucTab across all evaluated public benchmarks and significant improvements on TableVerse-5K, validating the effectiveness of explicit structural modeling and decomposed reward optimization. Code and benchmark are publicly available at https://github.com/VirtualLUOUCAS/StrucTab.

</details>


### [175] [Traffic-CBM: A Structurally Interpretable Multimodal Framework for Encrypted Traffic Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29909)
*Honglei Jin,Wenshuo Chen,Shaofeng Liang,Haozhe Jia,Menshuo Zhao,Shuxu Jin,Songning Lai,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Traffic-CBM is a structurally interpretable multimodal framework for encrypted traffic classification that organizes heterogeneous traffic signals into a unified hierarchical concept space instead of opaque latent features, achieving competitive performance while providing clearer structural explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing encrypted traffic classification methods achieve strong performance but are difficult to interpret because they fuse flow statistics, packet sequences, and byte-level representations into opaque latent features, making it unclear which type of evidence drives predictions.

Method: Traffic-CBM organizes heterogeneous traffic signals into a unified hierarchical concept space with predefined traffic evidence groups: grouped flow statistics map to statistical concepts, temporal encoders learn temporal concepts from disjoint feature subspaces, and byte-level evidence is organized into packet-level and cross-packet concepts, enabling explicit concept representation without manual semantic annotation.

Result: Traffic-CBM achieves competitive and balanced classification performance on multiple encrypted traffic benchmarks while providing a clearer structural interpretation interface than conventional end-to-end fusion models.

Conclusion: The learned concept space is actively used in the prediction process and provides a clearer structural explanation of multimodal traffic evidence, demonstrating that structural interpretability can be achieved without sacrificing classification performance.

Abstract: Encrypted traffic classification has achieved strong performance, but its decision process remains difficult to interpret. Existing methods usually combine flow statistics, packet sequences, and byte-level representations into opaque latent features, making it unclear which type of evidence actually drives the prediction. In this paper, we propose Traffic-CBM, a structurally interpretable multimodal framework for encrypted traffic classification. Instead of directly fusing heterogeneous traffic signals into a black-box representation, Traffic-CBM organizes them into a unified hierarchical concept space. These concepts are not manually annotated semantic attributes; rather, they are scalar evidence summaries constrained by predefined traffic evidence groups. More specifically, grouped flow statistics are mapped to statistical concepts, dedicated temporal encoders learn temporal concepts from disjoint feature subspaces, and byte-level evidence is further organized into packet-level and cross-packet concepts. This design turns heterogeneous traffic evidence into an explicit concept representation and makes different levels of traffic evidence easier to analyze. We evaluate Traffic-CBM on multiple encrypted traffic benchmarks. Results show that it achieves competitive and balanced classification performance while providing a clearer structural interpretation interface than conventional end-to-end fusion models. Further analyses suggest that the learned concept space is actively used in the prediction process and provides a clearer structural explanation of multimodal traffic evidence.

</details>


### [176] [H-GRPO: Permutation-Invariant Reinforcement Learning for Grounded Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29915)
*Eric Peh,Debaditya Roy,Basura Fernando*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes De-compositional Evidence Grounding for VLMs, which breaks queries into atomic sub-questions with localized evidence bounding boxes, enabling structured reasoning paths for more interpretable and less hallucinatory answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs achieve high benchmark performance but remain black boxes prone to hallucination and superficial shortcuts; the goal is to enhance both performance and interpretability.

Method: De-compositional Evidence Grounding: decomposes a global query into atomic sub-questions, each requiring an explicit sub-answer and a localized evidence bounding box, constructing a structured reasoning path that mirrors human-like deduction.

Result: The final answer emerges as a logical consequence of verified visual facts rather than a statistical guess, with intermediate steps (e.g., identifying a container, analyzing liquid properties) grounded in specific visual regions.

Conclusion: Grounding intermediate logical steps in localized visual evidence reduces hallucination and superficial shortcut reliance, producing more interpretable and logically sound VLM inference.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often achieve high performance on benchmarks while remaining "black boxes", yet they remain prone to hallucination or rely on superficial shortcuts. In this work, we propose a framework designed to enhance both performance and interpretability through De-compositional Evidence Grounding. Unlike monolithic inference approaches, our approach forces the model to decompose a global query into a sequence of atomic sub-questions, each requiring an explicit sub-answer and critically a localized evidence bounding box. By grounding intermediate logical steps (e.g. identifying a container, analyzing liquid properties, and assessing environmental context) in specific visual regions, we construct a structured reasoning path that mirrors human-like deduction. This allows the final answer to emerge as a logical consequence of verified visual facts rather than a statistical guess.

</details>


### [177] [DCGrasp: Distance-aware Controllable Grasp Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29924)
*Hiroyasu Akada,Jesús Pérez,Emre Aksan,Vasileios Choutas,Cristian Romero,Alberto Garcia-Garcia,Vladislav Golyanik,Christian Theobalt,Thabo Beeler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DCGrasp is a distance-aware controllable grasp generation system that uses a novel grasp energy term based on Distance Profile (signed distances from hand vertices to object surface) with distance-aware weighting. It generates grasps via a Diffusion Transformer then refines poses through optimization, achieving high-quality, physically plausible grasps with flexible control and strong generalization to diverse object/hand shapes and scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for 3D hand-object interaction generation rarely satisfy the requirements of flexible controllability and strong generalization to diverse object geometries, limiting their practical applicability in robotics, XR, and synthetic data generation.

Method: 1) A novel grasp energy term computing Distance Profile (signed distance from each hand vertex to nearest object point) with distance-aware weighting that captures semantically similar near-contact interactions invariant to object/hand identity. 2) A Diffusion Transformer generates the Distance Profile and a candidate hand pose. 3) An optimization stage refines the candidate pose to enforce consistency between the optimized hand pose and the generated Distance Profile in near-contact regions.

Result: DCGrasp produces high-quality, physically plausible grasps with flexible user control, generalizing to diverse object and hand shapes and scales.

Conclusion: The work establishes a robust and versatile pipeline for the synthesis of controllable 3D hand-object interactions.

Abstract: Generating 3D hand-object interactions is essential for applications in robotics, XR, and synthetic data generation, where flexible controllability and strong generalization to diverse object geometries are required. However, existing methods rarely satisfy these requirements, limiting their practical applicability. We present DCGrasp, a distance-aware controllable grasp generation system built on a novel grasp energy term. This term computes Distance Profile, a signed distance from each hand vertex to the nearest object point, coupled with distance-aware weighting, effectively capturing the semantically similar hand-object interaction in near-contact regions while remaining invariant to object and hand identity. Given various controllable signals, DCGrasp first generates a Distance Profile based on a Diffusion Transformer, together with a corresponding candidate hand pose. We then refine the candidate pose through optimization, enforcing consistency between the optimized hand pose and the generated Distance Profile in near-contact regions. Our experiments show that DCGrasp produces high-quality, physically plausible grasps with flexible user control, generalizing to diverse object and hand shapes and scales. Our work establishes a robust and versatile pipeline for the synthesis of controllable 3D hand-object interactions.

</details>


### [178] [Latent-CURE for Breast Cancer Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29928)
*Weiyi Zhao,Xiaoyu Tan,Lu Gan,Liang Liu,Xihe Qiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Latent-CURE is a breast ultrasound diagnostic framework using asymmetric weighted chain-of-thought reasoning in latent space to force sequential inference of BI-RADS descriptors before diagnosis, coupled with dual-asymmetric optimization to prevent rare malignant features from being overshadowed by common benign patterns, achieving robust performance on imbalanced clinical cohorts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal large models for breast ultrasound diagnosis use opaque end-to-end paradigms that prioritize global statistical correlations over structured clinical reasoning, making them susceptible to shortcut learning under extreme epidemiological class imbalance—where rare malignant indicators are bypassed for dominant benign patterns.

Method: Proposes Latent-CURE: (1) an asymmetric weighted chain-of-thought methodology operating in latent space that forces the model to sequentially infer standardized BI-RADS morphological descriptors before converging on a final diagnosis; (2) a dual-asymmetric optimization strategy with dynamically adjusted margins and weights to protect high-specificity malignant descriptors from being overshadowed by benign priors.

Result: The knowledge-injected approach provides transparent clinical evidence while achieving robust, accurate diagnostic performance on imbalanced medical cohorts.

Conclusion: Latent-CURE effectively addresses the disconnect between black-box deep learning and structured clinical reasoning in breast ultrasound diagnosis by injecting domain knowledge (BI-RADS) into the reasoning chain and using asymmetric optimization to handle extreme class imbalance.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Models have significantly advanced automated breast ultrasound diagnosis. However, most existing frameworks utilize opaque, end-to-end paradigms prioritizing global statistical correlations over structured clinical reasoning. Consequently, these models remain susceptible to shortcut learning amid extreme real-world epidemiological imbalances, often bypassing rare but decisive malignant indicators for dominant benign patterns. To address this disconnect, we propose Latent-CURE, a novel diagnostic framework driven by asymmetric weighted chain-of-thought methodology grounded in latent space reasoning. Unlike traditional approaches, our framework constructs an implicit reasoning trajectory forcing the model to sequentially infer standardized BI-RADS morphological descriptors before converging on a final diagnosis. Furthermore, to combat the extreme scarcity of critical malignant features, we couple this architecture with a dual-asymmetric optimization strategy. By dynamically adjusting margins and weights, this strategy safeguards high-specificity malignant descriptors from being overshadowed by common benign priors. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that our knowledge-injected approach provides transparent clinical evidence while achieving robust, accurate diagnostic performance in imbalanced medical cohorts.

</details>


### [179] [Scene-aware Prediction of Diverse Human Movement Goals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29942)
*Qiaoyue Yang,Amadeus Weber,Magnus Jung,Ayoub AI-Hamadi,Sven Wachsmuth*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multi-goal prediction method using Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) that leverages RGB scenes and human poses to predict diverse potential future goals of human movement, addressing the stochastic nature of human behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human behavior is stochastic due to varying goals, and predicting these goals is essential for autonomous systems in proactive planning. Existing methods either require semantic scene knowledge or only handle object interactions.

Method: A generative model based on Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) that takes current RGB scene and human pose as input, and generates multiple diverse movement goals by sampling in the latent space.

Result: The method generates multiple movement goals and demonstrates generalization capability across GTA-IM and PROX datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed CVAE-based approach effectively addresses the stochasticity of human movement by predicting diverse potential goals, without requiring semantic knowledge of the scene.

Abstract: Anticipation of human behaviours facilitates autonomous systems in proactive planning. Human behaviour could be stochastic due to varying goals. Human goals typically guide their own movement and could therefore help to predict the human trajectory and human motion in the long-term. To infer the human movement intentions, the environmental context plays a significant role, in addition to the social cues expressed by the individual. Previous works on human goals prediction either require semantic knowledge of the scene, or only tackle interactions with objects. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-goal prediction method using the generative model to address the stochasticity of human movement. It leverages the current RGB scene and the human pose to predict diverse potential future goals of human movement based on the Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). Our results demonstrate that our approach is capable of generating multiple movement goals in the scene via samplings in latent space of the CVAE and exhibits generalization capability across scenarios in GTA-IM dataset and PROX dataset. Code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/Q-Y-Yang/DiverseGoalsPrediction.git}{\texttt{https://github.com/Q-Y-Yang/DiverseGoalsPrediction}}.

</details>


### [180] [Explainability-Aware Frustum Attack: Exposing Structural Vulnerabilities in LiDAR-Based 3D Object Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29963)
*Chengzeng You,Binbin Xu,Soteris Demetriou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Saliency-LiDAR (SALL), an explainability-guided method that generates universal saliency maps for LiDAR-based 3D detectors, and introduces the Explainability-aware Frustum Attack (EFA) that selectively perturbs the most influential frustums. Experiments show EFA reduces detection recall by >15 percentage points while using 25-50% fewer perturbed frustums, exposing structural robustness vulnerabilities in modern 3D detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how LiDAR-based detectors rely on spatial evidence in complex driving scenes and whether these reliance patterns can be exploited more efficiently, addressing the gap between prior work on isolated 3D models and recent LiDAR spoofing attacks that focus primarily on physical realizability.

Method: The SALL method aggregates Integrated Gradient attributions across scenes to produce universal saliency maps for LiDAR-based 3D detectors. Guided by these maps, the EFA selectively perturbs only the most influential frustums rather than uniformly attacking entire object regions.

Result: On KITTI and nuScenes with PointPillars and SECOND detectors, EFA reduces detection recall by more than 15 percentage points while requiring 25-50% fewer perturbed frustums compared to the state-of-the-art non-saliency-aware baseline.

Conclusion: Modern 3D detectors concentrate discriminative evidence in a small subset of spatial regions, exposing a structural robustness vulnerability in current LiDAR perception systems.

Abstract: The structural vulnerabilities of point cloud-based 3D object detectors remain poorly understood. Prior work has studied adversarial robustness primarily on isolated 3D object models, while recent LiDAR spoofing attacks target richer and more realistic driving scenes but focus mainly on physical realizability rather than understanding detector behavior or attack efficiency. In this work, we investigate how LiDAR-based detectors rely on spatial evidence in complex scenes and whether these reliance patterns can be exploited to induce failures more efficiently. To this end, we propose an explainability-guided adversarial analysis methodology. We introduce the Saliency-LiDAR (SALL) method, which aggregates Integrated Gradient attributions across scenes to produce universal saliency maps for LiDAR-based 3D object detectors. Guided by these maps, we design the Explainability-aware Frustum Attack (EFA), which selectively perturbs only the most influential frustums rather than uniformly attacking entire object regions. Experiments on KITTI and nuScenes, across detectors such as PointPillars and SECOND, show that EFA reduces detection recall by more than 15 percentage points while requiring 25-50% fewer perturbed frustums than the state-of-the-art non-saliency-aware baseline. These findings reveal that modern 3D detectors concentrate discriminative evidence in a small subset of spatial regions, exposing a structural robustness vulnerability in current LiDAR perception systems. Our code is released at https://github.com/SecMindLab/Saliency_LiDAR.

</details>


### [181] [Variance Reduction on the Camera Axis: Multi-View Score Distillation for 3D](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29964)
*Marian Lupascu,Mihai Sorin Stupariu,Ionut Mironica*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MV-SDI aggregates gradients from K randomly sampled views per optimization step via gradient accumulation, using antithetic antipodal pairs for balanced angular coverage, reducing variance without retraining the 2D diffusion prior, achieving significant metric improvements at fixed UNet budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Score distillation generates 3D from a 2D diffusion model, but per-step gradients are high-variance and lack global shape consistency because they are estimated from a single randomly chosen view; prior work retrains on multi-view data but conflates sampling improvements with prior quality.

Method: Multi-View Aggregated Score Distillation (MV-SDI) aggregates gradients from K views per optimization step via gradient accumulation, keeping peak memory unchanged and the 2D prior frozen; views are drawn as antithetic antipodal pairs for balanced angular coverage without multi-view data or retraining.
