Result: At 10,000-UNet-call budget, K=2 improves CLIP R-Precision from 74.8% to 83.8%, CLIP score from 0.297 to 0.312, with gains on HPSv2 and ImageReward and 0.0% divergence rate; K=4 yields R-Precision 86.9% and CLIP 0.307; optimization steps halve in both cases.

Conclusion: MV-SDI demonstrates that simply aggregating gradients from multiple views per step reduces variance and improves 3D consistency without retraining the diffusion prior, and is compatible with existing score-distillation pipelines like SDI.

Abstract: Score distillation turns a pretrained 2D diffusion model into a 3D generator, but the per-step gradient is estimated from a single randomly chosen view: it is high-variance and blind to global shape consistency. Prior work addresses this by retraining the diffusion prior on multi-view data; this improves consistency but makes the sampling contribution inseparable from prior quality. We instead isolate the sampling axis. The per-step gradient is one noisy sample of an expectation over views; aggregating K samples per step at a fixed total UNet budget reduces variance without touching the prior. We introduce Multi-View Aggregated Score Distillation (MV-SDI), which aggregates gradients from K views per step via gradient accumulation, keeping peak memory unchanged and the 2D prior frozen, and draws views as antithetic antipodal pairs, a prior-independent geometric property, for balanced angular coverage. At a fixed 10,000-UNet-call budget, K=2 raises CLIP R-Precision from 74.8% to 83.8% and CLIP score from 0.297 to 0.312, with consistent gains on HPSv2 and ImageReward and a 0.0% divergence rate on the 43-prompt benchmark; optimization steps halve as a consequence. K=4 gives a fourfold step reduction at R-Precision 86.9% and CLIP 0.307, still well above the single-view baseline on every alignment metric. MV-SDI is compatible with gradient-based score-distillation pipelines, including Score Distillation via Inversion, and requires no retraining and no multi-view data.

</details>


### [182] [Learning Efficient 4D Gaussian Representations from Monocular Videos with Flow Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29976)
*Shengjun Zhang,Jinzhao Li,Xin Fei,Yueqi Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flow Splatting extends 3D Gaussian Splatting to 4D dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular videos by constructing a velocity field and rendering optical flow for supervision, achieving better quality with faster training and rendering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic 3D reconstruction methods suffer from long training time, low rendering speed, or high memory consumption for per-frame reconstruction of 4D volumes, without fully exploiting dense dynamic information from monocular videos.

Method: Flow Splatting extends 4D volumes with time-varying means and covariance to represent complex dynamics, constructs and approximates a velocity field from this representation, and extends volume rendering to splat the velocity field by accounting for camera motion influence to render optical flow for supervising dynamics learning.

Result: The method achieves better image quality with less time consumption and higher rendering speed compared to state-of-the-art methods on various benchmarks.

Conclusion: Flow Splatting effectively addresses the efficiency limitations of prior dynamic 4D reconstruction methods by leveraging velocity field splatting for optical flow supervision, enabling faster and higher-quality reconstruction from monocular videos.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos is challenging due to scene complexity and temporal dynamics. With the advancement of 3D Gaussian Splatting in novel view synthesis, existing methods extend 3D Gaussians to 4D domain with deformation fields, trajectories or spatiotemporal 4D volumes to model scene element deformation. However, these methods suffer from long training time, low rendering speed or high memory consumption for per-frame reconstruction of 4D volumes, without fully exploiting dense dynamic information. To address this issue, we propose Flow Splatting, which constructs the velocity field and enables the conventional splatting technique to render optical flow from the velocity field to supervise dynamics learning process from monocular videos. Specifically, we extend 4D volumes with time varying means and covariance to represent complex dynamics. Then, we construct and approximate the velocity field naturally based on this representations. While conventional volume rendering techniques support to render color fields, we extend the volume rendering strategy to splat the velocity field by considering the influence of camera motions. We conduct experiments on various benchmarks to demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of our method. Compared to the state-of-the-art methods, our model achieves better image quality with less time consumption and higher rendering speed.

</details>


### [183] [Rigel: Self-Distilled Score Adaptation for Image and Video Captioning Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29997)
*Shuitsu Koyama,Kazuki Matsuda,Yuiga Wada,Shinnosuke Hirano,Daichi Yashima,Komei Sugiura*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Rigel is an automatic evaluation metric for image/video captioning that uses self-distilled score adaptation with a specialized scoring head from a frozen LLM, achieving over 10-point improvements on ActivityNet-Fact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-as-a-Judge metrics for captioning evaluation suffer from misalignment between large-vocabulary language modeling and the small label set needed for evaluation, leading to limited correlation with human judgments.

Method: Rigel employs an evaluation-specific scoring head distilled from a frozen LLM to capture judgment signals in a task-aligned space, then refines the LLM backbone with human judgment data. It uses the newly constructed Vid-Lepus dataset (3,338 videos, 33,380 references, 5,637 candidates) for training.

Result: Rigel outperforms state-of-the-art metrics across multiple benchmarks, achieving over 10-point improvements on ActivityNet-Fact in the reference-free setting.

Conclusion: Self-distilled score adaptation effectively bridges the gap between LLM pre-training objectives and evaluation tasks, producing a metric with superior alignment to human judgments for both image and video captioning.

Abstract: Automatic evaluation of image and video captioning is essential for benchmarking multimodal systems, although standard evaluation metrics show limited alignment with human judgments. Recent approaches using large language models (LLMs), commonly referred to as LLM-as-a-Judge, have improved alignment with human judgments but still suffer from a mismatch between large-vocabulary language modeling and evaluation over a small label set. To address this, we propose Rigel, an automatic evaluation metric for image and video captioning, based on self-distilled score adaptation. The metric employs an evaluation-specific scoring head distilled from a frozen LLM, which captures judgment signals in a task-aligned space without relying on large-vocabulary token sets. We then refine the LLM backbone with human judgment data. To train Rigel, we constructed the Vid-Lepus dataset, which contains 3,338 video clips, 33,380 reference captions, and 5,637 candidate captions. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that Rigel outperforms state-of-the-art metrics, achieving over 10-point improvements on ActivityNet-Fact in the reference-free setting.

</details>


### [184] [GeoEdit: Geometry-Aware Object Editing via Dual-Branch Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30003)
*Yi He,Jiangming Wang,Xinyu Wang,Mark Fong,Songchun Zhang,Yuxuan Xue,Hai-Tao Zheng,Yue Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoEdit is a training-free, Lift-Manipulate-Render-Denoise pipeline for 3D-consistent object editing in single photographs. It lifts scene and object into 3D, manipulates via point correspondence, renders a structural depth proxy, and refines it with a Dual-Branch Denoising stage that injects 3D constraints only into the foreground at matching noise variance (variance-homogeneous injection), leaving the background to denoise freely. This avoids self-attention leakage, ghosting, and blur. A new benchmark, GeoEditBench, with pose-aware metrics is also introduced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based image editors fail to precisely manipulate objects (translation, rotation, scaling) while obeying 3D physical constraints. 2D methods lack spatial awareness and cause perspective violations; injecting structural proxies into latent space disrupts variance homogeneity, causing self-attention leakage, ghosting, and background blur. The core challenge is asymmetric: the object must follow rigid geometry but the uncovered background needs freedom to synthesize plausible content.

Method: GeoEdit uses a training-free Lift-Manipulate-Render-Denoise pipeline: (1) decouple scene and object in 3D; (2) align them via point correspondence; (3) render a geometry-aligned proxy with a structural depth map; (4) use a Dual-Branch Denoising stage—a video diffusion backbone preserves object identity, while 3D constraints are injected into the foreground within a narrow denoising window at matching noise variance (variance-homogeneous injection). The background denoises freely. The injected signal matches native latent statistics, keeping self-attention undisturbed.

Result: GeoEdit achieves consistent gains in geometric accuracy, identity fidelity, and background quality over existing methods, as evaluated on the introduced GeoEditBench benchmark with pose-aware metrics covering object translation, object rotation, and camera movement.

Conclusion: GeoEdit solves the asymmetric 3D object manipulation problem in single images by decoupling the rigid geometric constraints for the object from the free synthesis of the background. The variance-homogeneous injection strategy successfully avoids self-attention leakage, enabling high-quality editing without training.

Abstract: Precisely manipulating objects in a single photograph (translation, rotation, scaling) while obeying 3D physical constraints remains unsolved for diffusion-based editors. Current 2D methods lack spatial awareness and produce perspective violations. Forcing structural proxies into the latent space also disrupts variance homogeneity, and the resulting self-attention leakage leads to ghosting and background blur. The core difficulty is asymmetric: the relocated object must follow a rigid geometry, yet the uncovered background needs freedom to synthesize plausible content. We present GeoEdit, a training-free Lift-Manipulate-Render-Denoise pipeline that satisfies both constraints. We decouple scene and object in 3D, align them through point correspondence, and render a geometry-aligned proxy with a structural depth map. A Dual-Branch Denoising stage then refines this proxy: a video diffusion backbone preserves object identity, while 3D constraints are injected into the foreground within a narrow denoising window at matching noise variance (variance-homogeneous injection). The background denoises freely. Because the injected signal matches the native latent statistics, self-attention stays undisturbed. We also introduce GeoEditBench, a pose-aware benchmark covering object translation, object rotation, and camera movement with pose-aware evaluation metrics. Experiments confirm consistent gains in geometric accuracy, identity fidelity, and background quality. Our codes are available at https://github.com/Heey731/GeoEdit.

</details>


### [185] [SkelEM: Training-Signal Decoupling of Skeleton and Diffusion for Self-supervised Axial Super-Resolution in Volume Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30012)
*Bohao Chen,Yanchao Zhang,Yanan Lv,Chenxun Deng,Hua Han,Xi Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SkelEM is a self-supervised framework for axial super-resolution in volume microscopy that decouples low-frequency topology and high-frequency detail enhancement using a frozen topological network and diffusion refiner with cycle-consistent alignment, achieving high-fidelity restoration in ≤5 inference steps and state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks including a new Plasma-FIB BRAVE-ASR dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Volume microscopy suffers from severe anisotropic resolution due to physical axial sectioning, and existing self-supervised ASR methods face a trilemma between overly smooth regression textures, structural hallucinations from pure diffusion models, and prohibitive inference latency.

Method: SkelEM decouples ASR at the training-signal level using a frozen topological network for low-frequency topology and a diffusion refiner for high-frequency details, trained with disjoint objectives. A unified cycle-consistent mechanism on input sparse slices extracts a real-domain residual prior and bidirectionally aligns the diffusion refiner, enabling high-fidelity detail restoration in ≤5 steps by truncating reverse diffusion with this physical prior.

Result: SkelEM achieves the most favorable balance across the fidelity-perception trade-off among self-supervised methods, with state-of-the-art downstream membrane segmentation performance and robust zero-shot generalization across distinct modalities, validated on public benchmarks and the newly introduced BRAVE-ASR benchmark.

Conclusion: SkelEM effectively resolves the trilemma in self-supervised axial super-resolution by decoupling topology and detail learning, enabling fast, high-fidelity, and artifact-free super-resolution that generalizes across instruments and modalities.

Abstract: Volume microscopy, including electron and light microscopy, suffers from severe anisotropic resolution due to physical axial sectioning. Existing self-supervised axial super-resolution (ASR) methods face a trilemma bounded by overly smoothed regression textures, structural hallucinations of pure diffusion models, and prohibitive inference latency. In this paper, we propose Skeleton-refinE Microscopy (SkelEM), a self-supervised framework that decouples ASR at the training-signal level: a frozen topological network and a diffusion refiner are optimized by disjoint objectives, separating low-frequency topology formulation from high-frequency detail enhancement. Building on this deterministic skeleton, we exploit a unified cycle-consistent mechanism on input sparse slices to simultaneously extract a real-domain residual prior and bidirectionally align the diffusion refiner, washing away cross-plane artifacts without synthetic bias. By truncating the reverse diffusion process with this physical prior, SkelEM achieves high-fidelity detail restoration in merely $\le 5$ steps. To rigorously assess cross-instrument generalization, we further introduce BRAVE-ASR, a new benchmark of co-aligned anisotropic and isotropic volumes acquired on a Plasma-FIB instrument. Across public benchmarks, SkelEM achieves the most favorable balance across the fidelity-perception trade-off among self-supervised methods, with state-of-the-art downstream membrane segmentation performance and robust zero-shot generalization across distinct modalities.

</details>


### [186] [Shell-Supervised Gaussian Splatting for Urban Real-to-Sim Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30014)
*Yuan Yang,Peijun Lu,Fangzhou Lu,Sai Fan,Siqi Yan,Chenyuan Zhang,Haobo Liang,Yichen Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting uses an external facade structural shell as geometric supervision to improve surface geometry quality in close-range urban facade 3D reconstruction from video, while maintaining photorealistic rendering quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Close-range urban facades present challenges for video-to-3D reconstruction due to glass, reflections, repeated windows, and weak texture, which produce visually plausible but geometrically unstable surface reconstructions. Existing methods prioritize photorealistic novel-view synthesis over geometry useful for embodied AI tasks like collision reasoning and navigation.

Method: The method introduces shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting, a reconstruction-stage framework that leverages an external facade structural shell as lightweight geometric supervision. It aligns the exterior shell to the video reconstruction, renders per-view depth, camera-space normal, and valid-mask maps, and applies mask-gated losses during Gaussian optimization to regularize only visible shell-supported facade regions while preserving RGB-driven appearance.

Result: Experiments on close-range urban facade scenes show improved facade orientation and visible-surface point-cloud consistency compared to photo-only, monocular-cue, and surface-oriented Gaussian baselines, while maintaining comparable held-out rendering quality.

Conclusion: Shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting effectively bridges the gap between photorealistic rendering and physically accurate geometry for embodied AI applications in urban environments, demonstrating that lightweight geometric priors can regularize reconstruction without sacrificing visual quality.

Abstract: Real-to-sim reconstruction for embodied AI requires geometry that is useful for collision reasoning, navigation, and agent-environment interaction, not only photorealistic novel-view synthesis. However, close-range urban facades are difficult for video-to-3D reconstruction: glass, reflections, repeated windows, and weak texture can produce visually plausible renderings with unstable surface geometry. We introduce shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting, a reconstruction-stage framework that uses an external facade structural shell as lightweight geometric supervision for video-driven Gaussian reconstruction. The method aligns an exterior shell to the video reconstruction frame, renders per-view depth, camera-space normal, and valid-mask maps, and applies these cues through mask-gated losses during Gaussian optimization. This design preserves RGB-driven appearance while regularizing only visible shell-supported facade regions. Experiments on anonymized close-range urban facade scenes show improved facade orientation and visible-surface point-cloud consistency over photo-only, monocular-cue, and surface-oriented Gaussian baselines, while maintaining comparable held-out rendering quality.

</details>


### [187] [Monte Carlo Energy Aggregation for Mobile 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30017)
*Xiaobiao Du,YuAn Wang,Hao Li,Bosheng Wang,Xun Sun,Xin Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flux-GS is a real-time 3D Gaussian Splatting method for mobile platforms that reduces inference/storage overhead by aggregating specular energy into compact latent space, enhancing low-order SH with attribute-conditioned offsets, and using multi-view alpha-based densification/pruning, achieving competitive visual quality with significantly fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-order Spherical Harmonics (SH) in 3D Gaussian Splatting cause substantial inference and storage overhead, making them unsuitable for resource-constrained mobile platforms.

Method: (1) Monte Carlo Specular Energy Aggregator: samples third-order radiance residuals and aggregates specular energy into a compact latent space; (2) Attribute-Conditioned SH Enhancement module: predicts Gaussian-aware offsets to enhance first-order SH representation without extra inference cost; (3) Multi-view Alpha-based Densification and Pruning: leverages multi-view guidance for structure consistency and redundant primitive removal.

Result: Substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive visual quality, enabling robust real-time mobile rendering.

Conclusion: Flux-GS offers a robust and scalable solution for real-time Gaussian Splatting on mobile platforms by effectively compressing SH representations and ensuring multi-view consistency.

Abstract: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting have demonstrated unprecedented success in novel view synthesis. However, the substantial inference and storage overhead driven by high-order Spherical Harmonics (SH) are primary bottlenecks for mobile platforms. In this paper, we present Flux-GS, a real-time Gaussian Splatting method designed to achieve high-fidelity rendering with significantly reduced overhead for resource-constrained mobile platforms. We first propose a Monte Carlo Specular Energy Aggregator, sampling third-order radiance residuals and aggregating specular energy into a compact latent space. In this way, our method effectively preserves visually salient lighting features in lower-order bands without expensive distillation or pre-training. To mitigate the high-frequency details lost during compression, we introduce an Attribute-Conditioned SH Enhancement module. This module predicts Gaussian-aware offsets based on intrinsic Gaussian attributes, which enhance the first-order SH representation prior to inference, without extra inference costs. Furthermore, the original single-view gradient-based densification is prone to producing excessive Gaussians and overfitting to a certain view. We address these limitations by proposing a Multi-view Alpha-based Densification and Pruning strategy. By leveraging multi-view guidance, we ensure multi-view structure consistency and the precise removal of redundant primitives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Flux-GS achieves substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive visual quality, offering a robust and scalable solution for real-time mobile rendering. Code: \textcolor{magenta}{\href{https://xiaobiaodu.github.io/flux-gs-project/}{https://xiaobiaodu.github.io/flux-gs-project/}}.

</details>


### [188] [OmniDance: Multimodal Driven Dance Video Generation with Large-scale Internet Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30019)
*Kaixing Yang,Jiashu Zhu,Xulong Tang,Ziqiao Peng,Xiangyue Zhang,Chubin Chen,Puwei Wang,Jiahong Wu,Xiangxiang Chu,Hongyan Liu,Jun He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CIPE-Dance (300k clips, 400 hours) is the largest dance video dataset with choreography-informed text annotations. OmniDance integrates music into a text-conditioned video foundation model via depth-aware specialization, easy-to-hard curriculum learning, and modality-specialized CFG, achieving SOTA on TI2V, MI2V, and MTI2V tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods lack large-scale high-quality dance video datasets and principled frameworks for integrating music as a conditioning signal into Video Generation Foundation Models.

Method: CIPE-Dance built via progressive expert pipeline. OmniDance uses: (1) depth-aware specialization architecture, (2) anchored easy-to-hard curriculum learning, (3) modality-specialized time-dependent CFG strategy, treating text as low-frequency semantics and music as high-frequency temporal dynamics.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on text-to-image-to-video (TI2V), music-to-image-to-video (MI2V), and multimodal text-and-music-to-image-to-video (MTI2V) generation tasks.

Conclusion: OmniDance enables unified multimodal dance video generation with robust integration capability while preserving the foundation model's original controllability and visual fidelity.

Abstract: Music-driven dance video generation aims to synthesize expressive human motion that is temporally aligned with music while maintaining high visual fidelity. Despite recent progress, existing methods still face two key limitations: the lack of large-scale, high-quality dance video datasets, and the absence of principled frameworks for integrating music as a complementary conditioning signal into Video Generation Foundation Models. To address these limitations, we introduce CIPE-Dance, a large-scale Internet-sourced dance video dataset with choreography-informed text annotations, constructed via a progressive expert pipeline. To the best of our knowledge, CIPE-Dance is the largest dataset for dance video generation to date, comprising 300k high-quality clips over 400 hours and covering diverse dancers, environments, and dance genres. We further propose OmniDance, a framework-level recipe for integrating music into a TI2V foundation model without sacrificing its original controllability or visual fidelity. Motivated by the complementary roles of text as low-frequency semantics and music as high-frequency temporal dynamics, OmniDance co-designs a depth-aware specialization architecture, an anchored easy-to-hard curriculum learning strategy, and a modality-specialized time-dependent CFG strategy, enabling unified TI2V, MI2V, and MTI2V generation. Extensive experiments on CIPE-Dance demonstrate that OmniDance achieves state-of-the-art performance across all three tasks and exhibits robust multimodal integration capability. Project is available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/OmniDance.

</details>


### [189] [Uncertainty Estimation in Pathology Foundation Models via Deep Mutual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30020)
*Gbègninougbo Aurel Davy Tchokponhoue,Sevda Öğüt,Ali Idri,Dorina Thanou,Pascal Frossard*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DICE is a plug-and-play framework that ensembles multiple frozen pathology foundation models using deep mutual learning to align their representations, using disagreement as a proxy for uncertainty estimation, achieving reliable confidence estimates and patch-level abnormality localization without supervision across WSI benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pathology foundation models lack reliable confidence estimates and no single PFM is universally best across tasks, undermining trust in clinical adoption.

Method: Ensembles K frozen PFMs with deep mutual learning to align representations, using ensemble disagreement as a proxy for uncertainty estimation, with theoretical proof that the alignment objective upper-bounds model uncertainty.

Result: Provides reliable uncertainty estimates that flag failure-prone cases under in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, matches or outperforms SOTA baselines in classification, calibration, and localization across three WSI benchmarks.

Conclusion: DICE takes a crucial step toward translating PFMs into uncertainty-aware decision-support systems for clinical use.

Abstract: Pathology foundation models (PFMs) offer generalizable representations for whole-slide image (WSI) analysis, yet their clinical adoption remains limited. Specifically, their predictions lack reliable confidence estimates, and no single PFM is universally best across tasks, which severely undermines trust in medical settings. To overcome this, we propose $\mathtt{DICE}$, a plug-and-play framework that ensembles $K$ frozen PFMs and models their disagreement as a proxy for uncertainty estimation. To ensure this proxy yields meaningful estimates, we align the ensemble members via deep mutual learning, and theoretically show that this objective upper-bounds the model uncertainty. Additionally, we demonstrate that the ensemble's consensus localizes abnormalities at the patch level without any explicit supervision. We evaluate $\mathtt{DICE}$ on three challenging WSI benchmarks. Notably, our framework provides reliable uncertainty estimates that accurately flag failure-prone cases under in- and out-of-distribution settings, while matching or outperforming SOTA baselines in classification, calibration, and localization. Overall, $\mathtt{DICE}$ takes a crucial step toward translating PFMs into uncertainty-aware decision-support systems.

</details>


### [190] [IBRSteG: Learning a Generalizable Steganography Framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30024)
*Fanye Kong,Hongyu Xia,Yu Zheng,Boyang Gong,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IBRSteG is a generalizable feed-forward framework for 3DGS steganography that learns a scene-independent embedding function via GAS (Gaussian Attributes Steganographer) to conceal secret 3D scenes within cover scenes without per-scene finetuning, achieving high visual quality, capacity, and security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D steganographic approaches are rigidly coupled with specific scenes, lacking generalizability. Designing a generalizable steganographic method for 3D Gaussian Splatting that can embed meaningful 3D scene content across different scenes remains challenging.

Method: Formulates 3D steganography as a feed-forward 3D Gaussian embedding process. Introduces GAS (Gaussian Attributes Steganographer), a network that learns a scene-independent embedding function by injecting secret 3D Gaussian point attributes into a cover scene. Transforms 3D Gaussians into structured attributes compatible with 2D learning paradigms to enhance generalization.

Result: Effectively conceals different scenes with high visual quality, achieves superior capacity and security on established datasets.

Conclusion: IBRSteG enables undetectable concealment of secret 3D scenes within steganographic scenes without per-scene finetuning or optimization, generalizing across different 3DGS scenes.

Abstract: Recent advances in deep learning have notably improved steganographic message hiding. However, designing a generalizable steganographic approach for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that can embed meaningful 3D scene content remains challenging. In this paper, we propose IBRSteG, a generalizable framework for 3DGS steganography that enables undetectable concealment of secret scenes within a steganographic scene. Unlike existing approaches whose parameter generation is rigidly coupled with the specific scene, we formulate 3D steganography as a feed-forward 3D Gaussian embedding process that generalizes across different 3DGS scenes. To realize this, we introduce GAS (Gaussian Attributes Steganographer), a network that learns a scene-independent embedding function by injecting the attributes of secret 3D Gaussian points into a cover scene, thereby directly reconstructing the steganographic scenes without per-scene finetuning or optimization. By transforming 3D Gaussian into these structured attributes, these attributes are compatible with 2D learning paradigms and benefit from their structured nature, thereby enhancing generalization to unseen 3DGS scenes. Extensive experiments on established datasets demonstrate that IBRSteG can effectively conceal different scenes with high visual quality, and achieves superior capacity and security. Code is available at https://github.com/LingXiang2023/IBRSteG.

</details>


### [191] [MuseBench: Benchmarking Intent-Level Audiovisual Arts Understanding in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30026)
*Yuxuan Fan,Gyusik Seo,Jing Hao,Jaemin Cho,Mohit Bansal,Jaehong Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Musebench is a benchmark of 4,016 questions across cinematic, visual, stage, and game arts for evaluating MLLMs on artistic reasoning (not just recognition). The best model achieves 48.29% accuracy vs. 87.18% human expert performance, revealing a major gap in creative understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM benchmarks focus on perceptual recognition (what is depicted) but neglect reasoning about creative intent (why it is expressed through particular choices), leaving a critical gap in evaluating artistic understanding.

Method: Musebench is constructed by distilling 4,016 questions from over 10K video essays with professional commentary, covering four art domains. Questions are generated via a four-phase pipeline: shortcut filtering, adversarial distractors, and expert validation. Both single-select and variable-option multi-select formats are used.

Result: Zero-shot evaluation of 28 state-of-the-art MLLMs shows the best model achieves 48.29% accuracy, substantially below human expert performance at 87.18%.

Conclusion: Current MLLMs exhibit a significant gap in creative domain expertise, highlighting the need for benchmarks that measure reasoning about artistic intent rather than mere perceptual recognition.

Abstract: Audiovisual arts encompass diverse creative disciplines, including cinema, visual arts, stage performance, and game design, where artistic meaning arises from deliberate combinations of visual, auditory, and narrative elements (e.g., fear amplified through claustrophobic framing, or grief conveyed through silence and lingering close-ups). True artistic understanding extends beyond recognizing what is depicted to reasoning about why it is expressed through particular creative choices. Despite the strong progress of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), this critical aspect of artistic understanding remains underexplored, as existing benchmarks largely measure perceptual recognition while overlooking reasoning about creative intent. To address this gap, we introduce Musebench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate MLLMs on nuanced artistic understanding. It comprises 4,016 questions spanning cinematic arts, static visual arts, stage performing arts, and game arts, distilled from over 10K candidate video essays that pair professional commentary with visual demonstration. To capture the open-ended nature of artistic analysis at scale, the benchmark combines single-select and variable-option multi-select questions. All questions are generated and refined through a four-phase iterative pipeline combining shortcut filtering, adversarial distractors, and expert validation. Comprehensive zero-shot evaluation of 28 state-of-the-art MLLMs reveals that even the best-performing model achieves only 48.29% accuracy, substantially below human expert performance of 87.18%, exposing a significant gap in current models' creative domain expertise.

</details>


### [192] [Cross-Modal Iteration Distillation for Robust IHD Screening: The IDNet Framework and A New Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30027)
*Yongchang Gao,Junjie Pang,Shuaiyu Yang,Yusheng Yang,Xichao Jia,Shaojie Li,Hongfei Zhang,Jia Mu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IDNet is a multimodal framework for ischemic heart disease screening from color fundus photography, featuring a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) with learnable queries to integrate left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features. It also introduces a reproducible UK Biobank benchmark with 50,410 images from 25,205 subjects, outperforming image-only, clinical-only, and multimodal baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CFP offers low-cost IHD screening but suffers from scarce public benchmarks and ineffective fusion of retinal images with sparse clinical variables. The imbalance between high-dimensional visual features and low-dimensional tabular data is a key challenge.

Method: IDNet uses a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) with learnable queries to sequentially integrate left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features, addressing the modality imbalance. A reproducible UK Biobank benchmark with open-source curation and quality-control pipelines was constructed.

Result: IDNet outperforms image-only, clinical-only, and several multimodal baselines. CDA consistently improves multiple visual encoders as a plug-in fusion module.

Conclusion: IDNet and CDA effectively address multimodal fusion challenges in retinal image-based IHD screening, and the constructed benchmark provides a reproducible evaluation standard for future research.

Abstract: Color Fundus Photography (CFP) offers a low-cost and non-invasive route for ischemic heart disease (IHD) screening, but current studies are limited by scarce public benchmarks and ineffective fusion of retinal images with sparse clinical variables. We propose IDNet, a multimodal framework with a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) that uses learnable queries to sequentially integrate left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features, mitigating the imbalance between high-dimensional visual features and low-dimensional tabular inputs. We also construct a reproducible UK Biobank benchmark with open-source curation and quality-control pipelines, yielding 50,410 images from 25,205 subjects. On this benchmark, IDNet outperforms image-only, clinical-only, and several multimodal baselines, and CDA consistently improves multiple visual encoders as a plug-in fusion module.

</details>


### [193] [CogSENet: Blind Image Deblurring with Blur-Conditioned Semantic Routing and Explicit Frequency Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30030)
*Pan Wang,Yihao Hu,Xiujin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CogSENet is a blind image deblurring framework inspired by the eagle's visual system, featuring a Semantic-Driven State Space Module (SDSSM) for long-range dependencies, a BiFreqFusionBlock (BFFB) for frequency-decomposed texture recovery, and a Continuous Blur Field (CBF) estimation with CLIP semantic priors for spatially varying blur, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple restoration tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current blind deblurring methods struggle with real-world spatially varying degradations and lack semantic awareness to distinguish valid textures from artifacts, motivating the need for a more adaptive and semantically guided reconstruction framework.

Method: CogSENet uses three key components: (1) Semantic-Driven State Space Module (SDSSM) mimicking eagle's saccadic scanning for prompt-conditioned long-range dependency modeling via differentiable token routing; (2) BiFreqFusionBlock (BFFB) inspired by eagle's retina functional differentiation, using wavelet transforms to decompose features into high and low frequencies; (3) Continuous Blur Field (CBF) estimation fused with CLIP semantic priors to modulate deep latent features for spatially non-uniform blur adaptation.

Result: CogSENet outperforms state-of-the-art deblurring methods in visual quality and structural fidelity with fewer parameters, and also performs favorably on dehazing, deraining, and denoising tasks.

Conclusion: The bio-inspired design combining semantic-aware routing, frequency decomposition, and continuous blur field estimation enables effective blind deblurring under spatially varying degradations, with strong generalization to other low-level vision tasks.

Abstract: Blind image deblurring demands the recovery of high-fidelity details and coherent structures from complex, unknown degradations. Current blind image deblurring methods struggle with real-world, spatially varying degradations, and lack the semantic awareness necessary to reliably differentiate valid textures from artifacts. To bridge this gap, we propose CogSENet, a dynamic, semantic-aligned reconstruction framework inspired by the eagle's visual system. By mimicking the eagle's active saccadic scanning, we devise a Semantic-Driven State Space Module (SDSSM) with semantic-aware token regrouping via differentiable routing, enabling prompt-conditioned long-range dependency modeling. To ensure physically interpretable recovery of textures and structures, a BiFreqFusionBlock (BFFB) mirrors functional differentiation of the eagle's retina by decomposing features into high and low frequencies using wavelet transforms. Finally, we estimate a continuous Blur Field (CBF) from blur image and fuse it with CLIP semantic priors to modulate the deepest latent features, emulating focal adaptation and enabling adaptive restoration under spatially non-uniform blur. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CogSENetoutperforms state-of-the-art deblurring methods in both visual quality and structural fidelity with fewer parameters, while also performing favorably on dehazing, deraining, and denoising tasks.

</details>


### [194] [Consensus Clustering of Free-Viewing Gaze Data: New Insights into Human-Information Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30035)
*Beryl Gnanaraj,Jaya Sreevalsan-Nair,Saqib Alam Ansari,Maanasa Rajaraman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EnsembleGaze is an unsupervised ensemble learning system that uses consensus clustering of free-viewing gaze data to characterize user behavior and stimulus type, revealing robust ambient-vs-focal viewing distinctions across image stimuli while showing that user groupings are image-context-dependent, recoverable only by biclustering and conditional approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Free-viewing gaze data is rich but its human-information interaction (HII) patterns are understudied; conventional exploratory analysis focuses on fixations and areas of interest but lacks characterization of joint user-stimulus behavior.

Method: Proposes EnsembleGaze, an end-to-end unsupervised ensemble learning system: (1) feature engineering using statistical descriptors of fixation-based distributions; (2) consensus voting across multiple clustering methods to compute a co-association matrix; (3) two high-dimensional clustering strategies—consensus subspace clustering and spectral biclustering—for joint user-image characterization.

Result: Image stimuli groupings are highly consistent across methods, reflecting a robust ambient-vs-focal viewing mode distinction. User groupings are image-context-dependent, a structure recoverable only by biclustering and two-step conditional approaches. Dataset-specific patterns emerged across public datasets.

Conclusion: EnsembleGaze provides a replicable method for unsupervised analysis of fixation behavior in scene perception research, revealing that stimulus grouping is method-invariant while user grouping requires specialized clustering architectures to recover context-dependent structure.

Abstract: Free-viewing gaze data provides a rich, task-free window into human visual attention. Conventional exploratory data analysis of the data provides user attention patterns through fixations and areas of interest. However, despite the richness of this gaze data, its human-information interaction (HII) patterns are understudied. We address this gap using consensus clustering of gaze data with respect to users and stimulus characteristics. We present a novel end-to-end unsupervised ensemble learning system for consensus clustering of free-viewing gaze datasets, EnsembleGaze. With a goal of characterizing the user behavior and stimulus type, we propose a feature engineering step based on statistical descriptors of fixation-based distributions. EnsembleGaze involves consensus voting of selected clustering methods implemented on the feature vector to compute the co-association matrix. Using the separate consensus clustering of users and stimuli as a baseline, we further propose two high-dimensional clustering strategies for determining gaze clusters based on joint user and image characterization. They are consensus subspace clustering and spectral biclustering. Clustering performance is evaluated using selected standard metrics and is further interpreted through image-level properties. Our system provides a replicable method for the unsupervised analysis of fixation behavior in scene perception research. Our results show that image stimuli groupings are highly consistent across methods, reflecting a robust ambient-versus-focal viewing mode distinction, whereas user groupings are image-context-dependent, a structure that only biclustering and the two-step conditional approaches are architecturally capable of recovering. Testing on the publicly available datasets revealed dataset-specific patterns, with each offering complementary insights through distinct clustering strategies.

</details>


### [195] [Walking in the Implicit: Interactive World Exploration via Neural Scene Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30045)
*Zhiqi Li,Chengrui Dong,Zhenhua Du,Hangning Zhou,Cong Qiu,Hailong Qin,Mu Yang,Dongxu Wei,Peidong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeuWorld introduces a scene-centric paradigm for interactive video generation by replacing latent frame rollouts with a compact Neural Implicit Scene (NIS) that separates state transition from pose-conditioned rendering, achieving long-horizon consistency and efficient inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interactive video generation systems entangle state transition with high-frequency observation synthesis, leading to inefficiency and inconsistency over long horizons. The authors aim to factorize these processes for better scalability and coherence.

Method: The method factorizes interactive generation into: (1) stochastic transition of a compact Neural Implicit Scene (NIS) state, and (2) deterministic pose-conditioned rendering from the sampled state. NeuWorld uses a transformer VAE to learn locally anchored NIS from sparse posed frames, and a diffusion transformer to evolve NIS conditioned on future camera trajectories and geometry-aware retrieved history. The VAE encoder serves as a unified conditioner across camera, reference-image, and history cues.

Result: NeuWorld achieves strong long-horizon consistency with favorable inference efficiency, trained from scratch on public posed-view data without pretrained video backbones or auxiliary 3D reconstructors.

Conclusion: The Neural Implicit Scene paradigm effectively decouples scene dynamics from rendering, enabling more consistent and efficient interactive video generation for camera-controlled world exploration.

Abstract: Interactive video generation systems for camera-controlled world exploration roll out growing sequences of latent video frames, entangling state transition with high-frequency observation synthesis. We propose Walking in the Implicit, a scene-centric paradigm that changes the rollout variable from frame latents to a fixed-length, renderable implicit state, termed Neural Implicit Scene (NIS). This factorizes interactive generation into stochastic transition of a compact scene state and deterministic pose-conditioned rendering given the sampled state. We instantiate this paradigm as NeuWorld: a transformer VAE learns locally anchored NIS from sparse posed frames, and a diffusion transformer evolves NIS conditioned on future camera trajectories and geometry-aware retrieved history. By reusing the VAE encoder as a unified conditioner, NeuWorld maps camera, reference-image, and history cues into the same NIS modality, avoiding external heterogeneous encoders. Trained from scratch on public posed-view data without pretrained video backbones or auxiliary 3D reconstructors, NeuWorld achieves strong long-horizon consistency with favorable inference efficiency.

</details>


### [196] [Argus: Metric Panoramic 3D Reconstruction for Indoor Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30047)
*Xi Li,Linyuan Li,Yan Wu,Tong Rao,Kai Zhang,Xinchen Hui,Cihui Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Realsee3D is a hybrid dataset of 10K indoor scenes for metric panoramic 3D reconstruction, and Argus is a feed-forward network trained on it that achieves SOTA performance via a learned covisibility module and decomposed pixel-to-world mapping with geometric consistency constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Metric feed-forward 3D reconstruction for panoramic data is under-explored due to the lack of large-scale panoramic RGB-D training data.

Method: Argus uses a learned covisibility module to select the optimal reference view for anchoring the metric world frame, and decomposes bidirectional pixel-to-world mapping into interpretable sub-steps with per-step supervision and cross-coordinate joint constraints.

Result: On the Realsee3D benchmark, Argus achieves state-of-the-art metric performance in camera pose estimation, depth estimation, and point cloud reconstruction.

Conclusion: The hybrid dataset and proposed network effectively address metric panoramic 3D reconstruction, achieving SOTA results across multiple tasks.

Abstract: Metric feed-forward 3D reconstruction for panoramic data remains under-explored due to the lack of large-scale panoramic RGB-D training data.
  We present Realsee3D, a hybrid dataset of 10K indoor scenes (1K real, 9K synthetic) with 299K panoramic viewpoints and precise metric annotations,
  and Argus, a feed-forward network trained on it for metric panoramic 3D reconstruction.
  In the sparse unordered capture setting of Realsee3D,
  a poorly chosen coordinate anchor can cause global pose drift.
  Argus addresses this with a learned covisibility module that selects the geometrically optimal reference view to anchor the metric world frame.
  To further improve multi-task learning,
  we decompose the bidirectional pixel-to-world mapping into interpretable sub-steps with per-step supervision and cross-coordinate joint constraints,
  reinforcing geometric consistency across prediction branches.
  On the Realsee3D benchmark,
  Argus achieves state-of-the-art metric performance in camera pose estimation, depth estimation, and point cloud reconstruction.
  Project page: https://argus-paper.realsee.ai.

</details>


### [197] [Bridging the Gap Between Image Restoration and Navigational Safety in Hazy Conditions: A New Visibility Estimation Metric for Maritime Surveillance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30049)
*Wentao Feng,Guobei Peng,Wengang Mao,Ryan Wen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a visibility-oriented evaluation framework for maritime image dehazing that links restoration quality to practical visible-distance estimation, using a new simulated dataset (MSVD) and object-detection-based metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional image quality metrics (PSNR, SSIM, etc.) cannot establish a physically interpretable relationship between dehazing restoration quality and practical visibility thresholds critical for maritime navigational safety under hazy conditions.

Method: (1) Construction of Maritime Simulated Visibility Dataset (MSVD) using Unity3D with paired hazy/clear images and precise visibility annotations. (2) A dehazing visibility evaluation metric that uses object detection accuracy as an intermediate indicator, mapping visibility distance to detection performance.

Result: Six representative dehazing methods were evaluated; MSVD provides a reliable benchmark across graded visibility levels, and the proposed metric enables interpretable visible-distance estimation.

Conclusion: The proposed visibility-oriented framework supports assessment of navigational safety and operational efficiency by converting image restoration improvements into measurable visibility gains, overcoming the limitations of conventional quality metrics.

Abstract: Visibility distance is critical to maritime navigational safety because it determines the effective observation range of shipborne and shore-based monitoring systems. Under hazy conditions, degraded visual information shortens observable distance and increases navigational risks and economic losses. Although numerous image dehazing methods have been developed, conventional image quality assessment metrics, such as PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, FADE, and NIQE, cannot establish a physically interpretable relationship between restoration quality and practical visibility thresholds. To address this limitation, this work proposes a visibility-oriented evaluation framework that links dehazing performance with visible-distance estimation. First, a Maritime Simulated Visibility Dataset (MSVD) is constructed using Unity3D to simulate maritime traffic scenes under graded visibility conditions. The dataset provides paired hazy and clear images with precise visibility annotations, enabling quantitative analysis of visibility restoration. Second, a dehazing visibility evaluation metric is developed by using object detection accuracy as an intermediate indicator. By establishing a mapping between visibility distance and detection performance, the proposed metric converts image restoration improvements into measurable visibility gains. Six representative dehazing methods are evaluated using both conventional image quality metrics and the proposed visibility metric. Experimental results under different imaging conditions demonstrate that MSVD provides a reliable benchmark for evaluating dehazing performance across graded visibility levels, while the proposed metric enables interpretable and reliable visible-distance estimation, thereby supporting the assessment of navigational safety and operational efficiency.

</details>


### [198] [Illuminating Unified Multimodal Model for Free-form Interleaved Text-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30054)
*Chonghuinan Wang,Zhikai Chen,Chunwei Wang,Yecong Wan,Junwei Yang,Zhixin Wang,Wei Zhang,Jiaqi Xu,Renjing Pei,Xiaohe Wu,Fan Li,Wangmeng Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ILLUME-X is a unified multimodal model for free-form interleaved text-image generation, achieving state-of-the-art performance through improved data efficiency, progressive training, and a new evaluation metric (ILScore).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance multimodal intelligence by enabling models to autonomously generate free-form interleaved text-image sequences, addressing the limitations in data efficiency and training stability of existing unified models.

Method: ILLUME-X introduces three components: (i) an expanded training data pipeline optimized for interleaved text-image generation, (ii) a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for free-length multimodal token sequences, and (iii) ILScore, a comprehensive evaluation method for interleaved text-image sequences.

Result: ILLUME-X outperforms previous unified models across multiple interleaved text-image generation tasks including style transfer, image decomposition, and storytelling.

Conclusion: The proposed paradigm effectively advances free-form interleaved text-image generation, setting a new benchmark for unified multimodal generation models.

Abstract: The advancement of generative AI models capable of producing text and image marks a critical step forward in the realm of multimodal intelligence, particularly for tasks involving the interleaving of both modalities. To advance this intelligence to the next stage, it is crucial for models to autonomously generate free-form interleaved text-image sequences. In this paper, we introduce ILLUME-X, an advanced unified multimodal paradigm that enables high-quality, free-form interleaved text-image generation by improving multimodal data efficiency and stabilizing the multimodal training process. ILLUME-X comprises three key components: (i) an expanded training data pipeline optimized for interleaved text-image generation, (ii) a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for free-length multimodal token sequences, and (iii) an objective and comprehensive evaluation method ILScore for interleaved text-image sequences. Notably, our ILLUME-X outperforms previous unified models across multiple interleaved text-image generation tasks like style transfer, image decomposition and storytelling.

</details>


### [199] [Emergence of a Shared Canonical Object Frame from In-the-Wild Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30058)
*Tom Fischer,Martin Sundermeyer,Adam Kortylewski,Eddy Ilg*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised method that learns a shared canonical frame for objects from in-the-wild videos using noisy camera poses and a coarse canonical mesh, achieving competitive category-level pose estimation without manual annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional canonical frame establishment requires manual annotation, creating a scaling bottleneck that limits category and instance diversity.

Method: The method uses a shared coarse canonical mesh as a geometric bottleneck, learns dense correspondences from image pixels to this mesh, and estimates per-sequence alignments from noisy SfM geometry, training on 160,000 in-the-wild object videos in a self-supervised manner.

Result: Achieves competitive accuracy on category-level pose estimation benchmarks compared to methods relying on canonical pose supervision.

Conclusion: A shared canonical frame can emerge from self-supervised training on object-centric videos using only noisy camera poses, eliminating the need for manual canonical pose labeling.

Abstract: Comparing object orientations and positions across different instances requires their poses to be expressed in a shared canonical frame. Establishing such frames has traditionally required manual annotation, creating a scaling bottleneck that limits category and instance diversity. We show that a shared canonical frame can instead emerge from self-supervised training on object-centric videos captured in the wild, using only noisy camera poses from Structure-from-Motion. Our key idea is to route all training sequences through a shared geometric bottleneck: a coarse canonical mesh that carries no category-specific detail. By learning dense correspondences from image pixels to this mesh, and estimating per-sequence alignments from noisy SfM geometry, a common canonical frame emerges from multi-view consistency and the semantic priors of the feature extractor, without any canonical pose labels or category conditioning. Trained in a self-supervised manner on 160,000 in-the-wild object videos, our method achieves competitive accuracy on category-level pose estimation benchmarks compared to methods that rely on canonical pose supervision. The code and checkpoint is available on https://github.com/Fischer-Tom/Emergent-Canonical-Frame/.

</details>


### [200] [Clinical Risk-Aware Multi-Level Grading for Coronary Artery Stenosis through Curved Feature Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30082)
*Shishuang Zhao,Hongtai Li,Junjie Hou,Yuhang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multi-level grading model for coronary artery stenosis that fuses CCTA and 3D SCPR images using a Curved Feature Reconstruction (CFR) module and a Clinical Risk-Aware (CR) Loss, achieving superior performance on an in-house dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developing a multi-level grading model for coronary artery stenosis is clinically significant for diagnosing coronary artery disease, but existing approaches using CCTA or 3D SCPR images alone have inherent shortcomings: CCTA images are difficult due to tortuous vessel paths, and 3D SCPR images suffer from abnormal distortions. Additionally, incorporating clinical risk associations across different stenosis grades into the algorithm is non-trivial.

Method: The proposed method includes: (1) A Curved Feature Reconstruction (CFR) module that uses vessel curves as priors and a point-by-point correspondence strategy to align and fuse features from 3D SCPR and CCTA images; (2) A Clinical Risk-Aware (CR) Loss that introduces clinical risk relevance into network training.

Result: Experimental results on an in-house dataset show the proposed approach significantly outperforms other methods. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each proposed design component.

Conclusion: Fusing CCTA and 3D SCPR image features with curve-aware alignment and incorporating clinical risk information into the loss function effectively addresses the challenges of multi-level coronary artery stenosis grading.

Abstract: Developing a multi-level grading model for coronary artery stenosis holds great clinical significance for the diagnosis of coronary artery disease. However, designing an effective multi-level deep learning algorithm faces significant challenges. Specifically, utilizing CCTA or 3D SCPR images alone presents inherent shortcomings: CCTA images are difficult to analyze due to the tortuous paths of blood vessels, while 3D SCPR images are prone to abnormal distortions that hinder accurate grading. Furthermore, different stenosis grades are associated with varying clinical risks, and incorporating this association into the algorithm is non-trivial. To address the former problems, we propose the Curved Feature Reconstruction (CFR) module, which uses vessel curves as prior and employs a point-by-point correspondence strategy to precisely align and fuse features from both 3D SCPR and CCTA images. Meanwhile, a Clinical Risk-Aware (CR) Loss is employed to introduce clinical risk relevance into the network training so that the algorithm can better align with the clinical diagnosis. The experimental results on a in-house dataset reveal that our approach significantly outperforms other methods, and several ablation studies also demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed designs.

</details>


### [201] [One Forward Beats Two: InnerZoom for Accurate and Efficient GUI Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30084)
*Chen Liu,Ling Chen,Hanzhang Zhou,Liangyu Chen,Chenglin Cai,Xin Yu,Steven Hoi,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InnerZoom is a single-forward framework for GUI grounding that bridges cross-layer evidence to improve coordinate prediction accuracy without the extra cost of two-pass ZoomIn methods, achieving SOTA results across six benchmarks while reducing latency by 31.8% and TFLOPs by 29%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM-based GUI grounding methods generate coordinates autoregressively but suffer from a gap: target-region awareness emerges in intermediate decoder layers but is not retained or translated into final coordinate predictions. ZoomIn-style two-pass methods improve accuracy but add latency and computational cost.

Method: InnerZoom introduces a single-forward framework that transforms target-related cues from the original forward pass into a compact cross-layer evidence state, then preserves, refines, and reinjects this state throughout later decoding layers to guide coordinate prediction.

Result: InnerZoom-4B achieves SOTA on all six GUI grounding benchmarks: 64.7 on OSWorld-G, 40.2 on UI-Vision, 73.1 on OSWorld-GR, 87.6 on MMBench-GUI, surpassing previous best by 4.1, 3.2, 2.9, and 2.3 points respectively. It improves the same SFT+RL baseline by 5.3 points on average and outperforms two-pass ZoomIn by 1.3 points on average, while reducing latency by 31.8% and TFLOPs by 29%.

Conclusion: InnerZoom successfully retains the accuracy benefits of two-pass zooming without the extra computational cost, providing an efficient single-forward solution for GUI grounding.

Abstract: MLLM-based GUI grounding methods commonly formulate target localization as autoregressive coordinate generation, enabling models to leverage the strong instruction-following and semantic understanding capabilities of MLLMs. However, this formulation requires the model to retain region-level target evidence while decoding coordinate tokens with the spatial precision demanded by GUI clicking. Our diagnostic analysis reveals that target-region awareness emerges in intermediate decoder layers but is neither retained nor translated into the final coordinate prediction. Existing ZoomIn-style methods address this issue through an external crop-and-rerun pass, which improves localization but increases end-to-end latency and computational cost. To retain the accuracy benefits of two-pass zooming without this extra cost, we propose InnerZoom, a single-forward framework for cross-layer evidence bridging. InnerZoom transforms target-related cues from the original forward pass into a compact cross-layer evidence state, then preserves, refines, and reinjects this state throughout later decoding layers to guide coordinate prediction. Extensive experimental results suggest that InnerZoom-4B achieves state-of-the-art performance on all six GUI grounding benchmarks, obtaining 64.7 on OSWorld-G, 40.2 on UI-Vision, 73.1 on OSWorld-GR, and 87.6 on MMBench-GUI, surpassing the previous best results by 4.1, 3.2, 2.9, and 2.3 points, respectively. Under a controlled 4B setting, InnerZoom improves the same SFT+RL baseline by 5.3 points on average and outperforms two-pass ZoomIn by 1.3 points on average, while reducing end-to-end latency by up to 31.8% and TFLOPs by about 29%. Code and models will be publicly available.

</details>


### [202] [CylindTrack: Depth-Aware Cylindrical Motion Modeling for Panoramic Multi-Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30097)
*Buyin Deng,Kai Luo,Lingxin Huang,Xinqi Liu,Fei Cheng,Hang Zheng,Liming Yin,Kailun Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CylindTrack is a depth-aware cylindrical tracking-by-detection framework for panoramic Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) that addresses challenges unique to equirectangular video (periodic domain, seam artifacts, scale variation) via Depth-Temporal Trajectory Modeling (DTM), Spherical Spatio-Temporal Consistency Learning (SSTC), and a Topology-Aware Cylindrical Motion Model (TCMM), improving identity preservation and trajectory continuity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Panoramic cameras offer 360° FoV for embodied perception but MOT in equirectangular video is not a trivial extension of perspective MOT: the horizontal domain is periodic (breaking planar motion assumptions and making IoU unreliable near the 0°/360° seam), large-FoV scenes have more objects, stronger scale variation, and frequent interactions, and online association is sensitive to unstable frame-wise depth cues.

Method: CylindTrack is a tracking-by-detection framework with three key components: (1) Depth-Temporal Trajectory Modeling (DTM) — promotes depth from a frame-wise cue to a temporally filtered trajectory-level state; (2) Spherical Spatio-Temporal Consistency Learning (SSTC) — uses a Temporal Mixer and Spherical Geometry-aware Attention to enhance temporal coherence and panoramic geometric alignment; (3) Topology-Aware Cylindrical Motion Model (TCMM) — lifts horizontal motion into a continuous angular state space for seam-consistent motion prediction and association in the periodic panoramic domain.

Result: Improved identity preservation and trajectory continuity in challenging panoramic scenes by jointly modeling trajectory-level depth consistency and panoramic topology.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling trajectory-level depth consistency and panoramic topology is effective for addressing the unique challenges of panoramic MOT.

Abstract: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) is a core capability for embodied perception, and panoramic cameras are attractive for embodied systems because their 360° field of view reduces blind spots and keeps surrounding targets observable for longer durations. However, panoramic MOT is not a straightforward extension of perspective MOT. In equirectangular panoramic videos, the horizontal image domain is periodic rather than Euclidean, which breaks planar motion assumptions and makes IoU-based association unreliable near the 0°/360° seam. Meanwhile, large-FoV scenes often contain more objects, stronger scale variation, and more frequent interactions, making online association particularly sensitive to unstable frame-wise depth cues. To address these issues, we propose CylindTrack, a depth-aware cylindrical tracking-by-detection framework for panoramic MOT. CylindTrack first introduces Depth-Temporal Trajectory Modeling (DTM), which promotes instance depth from an isolated frame-wise cue to a temporally filtered trajectory-level state. To improve the reliability of depth observations, we further develop Spherical Spatio-Temporal Consistency Learning (SSTC), which combines a Temporal Mixer and Spherical Geometry-aware Attention to enhance temporal coherence and panoramic geometric alignment in depth-aware representations. Finally, we design a Topology-Aware Cylindrical Motion Model (TCMM) that lifts horizontal motion into a continuous angular state space and performs seam-consistent motion prediction and association in the periodic panoramic domain. By jointly modeling trajectory-level depth consistency and panoramic topology, CylindTrack improves identity preservation and trajectory continuity in challenging panoramic scenes. The source code will be released at https://github.com/warriordby/CylindTrack.

</details>


### [203] [LETT-NeXt: A Lightweight RECIST-Guided Model for 3D CT Lesion Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30108)
*Sebastian Aas,Elias Stenhede,Arian Ranjbar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LETT-NeXt is a lightweight RECIST-guided model that predicts 3D lesion masks from CT volumes by incorporating RECIST markers as prompt channels, achieving Dice scores of ~74-79% with fast CPU inference (~6.9s) and low memory usage (3.6 GB).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RECIST diameter measurements only provide limited 2D lesion description; there is a need for efficient 3D lesion segmentation guided by RECIST markers to improve tumor response assessment.

Method: The model extracts a RECIST-centered regional crop, encodes RECIST line and endpoints as two prompt channels, concatenates them with CT input, uses a compact MedNeXt-v2 encoder-decoder for mask prediction, followed by prompt-aware component selection and adaptive AutoZoom inference.

Result: On public validation: DSC 79.4±10.1, NSD 72.3±16.2. On hidden test: DSC 73.9, NSD 67.3, challenge score 70.6%. CPU inference: 6.9±3.0s per case, peak memory 3.6 GB.

Conclusion: LETT-NeXt demonstrates that lightweight, RECIST-guided 3D lesion segmentation is feasible with efficient inference, balancing accuracy and computational cost for practical clinical deployment.

Abstract: RECIST diameter measurements are widely used for tumor response assessment, but they provide only a limited 2D description of lesion extent. We present LETT-NeXt, a lightweight RECIST-guided model that predicts 3D lesion masks from CT volumes and RECIST markers for the CVPR 2026 Foundation Models for Pan-cancer Segmentation in CT Images competition. LETT-NeXt extracts a RECIST-centered regional crop, encodes the RECIST line and endpoints as two prompt channels, and concatenates them with the CT input. A compact MedNeXt-v2 encoder--decoder predicts the lesion mask, followed by prompt-aware component selection and adaptive AutoZoom inference. On the public validation set, LETT-NeXt achieved a Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) of 79.4 $\pm$ 10.1 and a Normalized Surface Dice (NSD) of 72.3 $\pm$ 16.2. On the hidden test set, it achieved a DSC of 73.9 and an NSD of 67.3, corresponding to a challenge score of 70.6\%. On the public validation mirror, LETT-NeXt completed CPU inference in 6.9 $\pm$ 3.0 s per case with a peak memory use of 3.6 GB. Code is available at github.com/Ahus-AIM/lett-next.

</details>


### [204] [SciIR: A Large-scale Training Dataset and Benchmark for Scientific Image Reasoning Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30124)
*Zhiyuan Ma,Zhengfeng Shi,Yuning An,Peize Li,Jiabao Wei,Ruijie Li,Junhao Xiao,Jianjun Li,Bowen Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SciIR, a framework and benchmark for scientific image generation, built on Peirce's Semiotic Triad. It includes SciIR-82k (80k+ scientific image-text pairs) and SciIR-Bench for evaluation. Fine-tuning Qwen-Image on this dataset improves SciIR-Bench score from 35% to 43%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current T2I models struggle with semantic alignment and logical reasoning needed for scientific imagery; there is a lack of training data and benchmarks specifically designed for scientific image generation.

Method: Formalizes scientific reasoning via Peirce's Semiotic Triad into Entity Structure (Icon), Scientific Process (Index), and Scientific Law (Symbol). Constructs SciIR-82k dataset from cutting-edge publications with hierarchical semiotic organization and Scientific Reasoning Chain-of-Thought (Sci-RCoT). Proposes SciIR-Bench with Atomic Checklist for fine-grained evaluation.

Result: Fine-tuning Qwen-Image on SciIR-82k yields the Qwen-Image-SciIR model, improving SciIR-Bench score from 35% to 43%, revealing significant deficiencies in current models' scientific reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: SciIR provides a solid foundation for advancing scientific image generation by bridging the gap between T2I models and rigorous scientific reasoning requirements.

Abstract: While Text-to-Image (T2I) models have shown remarkable success in generating photorealistic visual content, they still struggle with the rigorous semantic alignment and logical reasoning required for scientific imagery. Inspired by Peirce's Semiotic Triad, we introduce Scientific Image Reasoning (SciIR), a comprehensive resource for training and evaluation of scientific image generation. We formalize scientific reasoning into three core dimensions: Entity Structure (Icon), Scientific Process (Index), and Scientific Law (Symbol). Specifically, to overcome the scarcity of training data in scientific image generation, we elaborately create SciIR-82k, a large-scale dataset containing over 80,000 high-quality scientific image-text pairs from cutting-edge publications. The dataset is hierarchically organized according to the semiotic dimensions and incorporates a Scientific Reasoning Chain-of-Thought (Sci-RCoT) to explicitly model underlying visual logic. For evaluation, we propose SciIR-Bench, which aligns with these three semiotic levels and employs an Atomic Checklist to convert the outcome-oriented scientific accuracy into process-oriented, verifiable, fine-grained questions. Our extensive experiments reveal significant deficiencies in current models' scientific reasoning capabilities. Furthermore, by fine-tuning on the SciIR-82k dataset, we developed the Qwen-Image-SciIR model, which achieves a substantial improvement on the SciIR-Bench, increasing the final score from 35\% to 43\%, laying a solid foundation for future advances in scientific image generation.

</details>


### [205] [Hyper-Network Neural Functional Maps for Unsupervised Robust 3D Shape Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30131)
*Dongliang Cao,Florian Bernard*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hyper-network that predicts non-linear neural functional maps (NFM) to improve spectral basis alignment in challenging 3D shape matching scenarios like partiality, topological noise, and raw point clouds, achieving better accuracy when integrated into existing unsupervised deep functional map pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing functional map methods struggle with challenging scenarios (partiality, topological noise, raw point clouds) because significant intrinsic distortion prevents truncated spectral bases from being accurately aligned via linear transformations.

Method: The authors model NFM as an MLP with skip-connections to refine standard functional maps, and employ a hyper-network to predict its weights conditioned on the standard FM. Training uses a novel unsupervised spectral alignment loss.

Result: The approach can be seamlessly integrated into state-of-the-art unsupervised deep functional map pipelines, substantially improving matching accuracy in demanding scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed NFM with hyper-network effectively addresses intrinsic distortion issues in spectral alignment, enabling better performance on challenging 3D shape matching tasks while maintaining unsupervised learning.

Abstract: Functional maps are the cornerstone of recent non-rigid 3D shape matching methods due to their efficiency and performance. However, existing methods struggle with challenging scenarios, such as partiality, topological noise, and raw point clouds. A primary bottleneck is that significant intrinsic distortion prevents truncated spectral bases from being accurately aligned via linear transformations (i.e., functional maps). To address this, we introduce a hyper-network that predicts non-linear neural functional maps (NFM), learned in an unsupervised manner, to better align spectral bases. Specifically, we model the NFM as an MLP with skip-connection to refine standard FM and employ a hyper-network to predict its weights, conditioned on standard FM. Our framework is trained using a novel unsupervised spectral alignment loss. Experiments demonstrate that our approach can be seamlessly integrated into state-of-the-art unsupervised deep functional map pipelines, substantially improving matching accuracy in demanding scenarios.

</details>


### [206] [T2LDM++: A Self-Conditioned Representation Guided Diffusion Model for Realistic Text-to-LiDAR Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30147)
*Wentao Qu,Qi Zhang,Chenxu Wang,Guofeng Mei,Yongfei Liu,Xiaoshui Huang,Gim Hee Lee,Liang Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: T2LDM++ is a Text-to-LiDAR diffusion model that addresses over-smoothed scenes and limited controllability via Self-Conditioned Representation Guidance (SCRG), high-quality Text-LiDAR benchmarks, and support for multiple conditional generation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity of Text-LiDAR pairs leads to over-smoothed scenes and limited controllability in LiDAR generation. Existing methods suffer from insufficient training priors and lack of high-quality annotated benchmarks.

Method: Proposes T2LDM++ with Self-Conditioned Representation Guidance (SCRG) that uses a Guidance Network (GN) to provide reconstruction-based soft supervision to the Denoising Network (DN) for geometry-aware representations. Also introduces a directional position prior, high-quality Text-LiDAR benchmarks (>100K samples), and a control encoder for multi-condition support.

Result: SCRG is more effective and lightweight with decoupled inference (no computational overhead). The model generates realistic LiDAR scenes with rich geometric details in both unconditional and conditional settings, supporting tasks like (Semantic, Box, BEV, Camera)-to-LiDAR, Sparse-to-Dense, Dense-to-Sparse.

Conclusion: T2LDM++ effectively mitigates over-smoothing and street distortion while enabling controllable Text-LiDAR generation through efficient prior modeling and high-quality benchmarks.

Abstract: Recent progress in Text-to-Image generation benefits from large-scale Text-Image pairs. However, the scarcity of Text-LiDAR pairs often causes over-smoothed scenes and limited controllability. In this paper, we rethink the limitations of Text-LiDAR generation task, focusing on alleviating insufficient training priors and constructing controllable Text-LiDAR data. We propose a \textbf{T}ext-\textbf{to}-\textbf{L}iDAR \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{M}odel for LiDAR scene generation, T2LDM++, with a Self-Conditioned Representation Guidance (SCRG). Specifically, to alleviate object over-smoothing, SCRG employs a Guidance Network (GN) to provide reconstruction-based soft supervision to the Denoising Network (DN). This enables DN to learn geometry-aware representations through reconstruction guidance, leading to more accurate denoising in DDPMs. Meanwhile, through analysis and design, SCRG exhibits more effective and lightweight, while decoupled in inference, avoiding computational overhead. Furthermore, we construct two high-quality Text-LiDAR benchmarks ($>$100K samples) using a generalized strategy of geometric annotations, along with a controllability metric. Moreover, a directional position prior is designed to mitigate street distortion, further improving scene fidelity. Additionally, T2LDM++ supports multiple conditions, including (Semantic, Box, BEV, Camera)-to-LiDAR, Sparse-to-Dense, and Dense-to-Sparse generation, by learning a control encoder via frozen DN. With effective prior modeling and high-quality Text-LiDAR benchmarks, T2LDM++ can generate realistic LiDAR scenes with rich geometric details in unconditional and conditional settings.

</details>


### [207] [A Dual-domain Refinement Network with FBP-based Jacobian Learning for Sparse-view Dual-Energy CT Material Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30159)
