Consequently, DML models are vulnerable to shortcut learning driven by two structurally distinct confounders: background spurious correlations (which create backdoor paths via scene context) and foreground nuisance perturbations (which inject non-semantic variations like pose or illumination). Although existing methods have proposed targeted solutions for each pathway individually, none can simultaneously address both due to their fundamentally distinct causal roles. To bridge this gap, we propose the Counterfactual Causal Embedding (CouCE), a unified causal framework that explicitly models and neutralizes both confounders. Specifically, we introduce Orthogonal Dictionary-Based Backdoor Adjustment (ODBA), which isolates spurious background patterns into a variance-gated dictionary and stably disentangles them from the learned embeddings via soft orthogonal regularization. Simultaneously, we propose Multi-Scale Randomized Causal Intervention (MSRCI) to enforce causal invariance against foreground nuisances through multi-scale Fourier amplitude randomization and a symmetric KL invariance constraint. Notably, CouCE seamlessly integrates with any proxy-based loss, incurring modest training overhead without requiring architectural modifications during inference. Extensive experiments on CUB-200-2011, Cars-196, and Stanford Online Products demonstrate that CouCE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, providing a principled and robust solution for debiased DML.

</details>


### [233] [MUSE: Unlocking Timestep as Native Task Steering for One-Step Dense Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30370)
*Shuo Zhou,Zhaoxin Li,Xiujuan Chai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MUSE repurposes the fixed sinusoidal timestep embedding in one-step diffusion models as a parameter-free task steering signal, enabling efficient multi-task dense prediction (depth + normal estimation) without additional adapters or task tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-task diffusion methods introduce parameter-heavy adapters, experts, or learnable task tokens, leading to computational redundancy. The goal is to achieve efficient multi-task learning without added parameters.

Method: MUSE (Multi-task Unified eStimation via timestep Embedding) leverages the native sinusoidal timestep embedding in one-step diffusion models as an endogenous task steering signal. The mechanism is interpreted via Manifold Decoupling: discrete, fixed timestep values deterministically steer generation toward decoupled, task-specific latent manifolds.

Result: Highly competitive performance on monocular depth and normal estimation across 10 datasets, with efficacy generalizing across both U-Net and DiT architectures.

Conclusion: MUSE offers a concise, parameter-free path toward generalist vision models by unlocking latent potential in existing generation infrastructure.

Abstract: Monocular dense prediction has recently seen remarkable success by repurposing pre-trained diffusion models. This opens a promising yet challenging avenue for more efficient multi-task learning paradigm. However, existing multi-task diffusion methods often introduce parameter-heavy adapters, experts, or learnable task tokens, leading to computational redundancy. In this paper, we reveal an inherent mechanism within one-step diffusion models: the native, fixed sinusoidal timestep embedding can be repurposed as an endogenous task steering signal. Based on this discovery, we propose Multi-task Unified eStimation via timestep Embedding (MUSE), a parameter-free, single-model multi-tasking approach for dense prediction. We interpret this mechanism via Manifold Decoupling, where discrete, fixed timestep values deterministically steer the generation process towards decoupled, task-specific manifolds in the latent space. Extensive experiments across 10 datasets demonstrate that MUSE achieves highly competitive performance on both monocular depth and normal estimation, and its efficacy generalizes across U-Net and DiT architectures. Our work offers a concise and efficient path toward generalist vision models by simply unlocking the latent potential of existing generation infrastructure.

</details>


### [234] [Set-Inclusive Uncertainty Modeling for Robust Brain Tumor Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30374)
*Seunghun Baek,Jihwan Park,Jaeyoon Sim,Hoseok Lee,Seungjoo Lee,Won Hwa Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A probabilistic representation framework for brain tumor segmentation that models features as Gaussian distributions, using variance to quantify uncertainty from missing MRI modalities, with set-inclusive constraints ensuring consistent uncertainty relationships.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal MRI is essential for accurate brain tumor segmentation, but acquiring all modalities at inference is often challenging, causing intrinsic uncertainty from information loss. Existing methods produce deterministic representations that appear plausible but lack reliability under missing modalities.

Method: Models representations as Gaussian distributions (mean captures task info, variance measures uncertainty from missing evidence); regularizes mean from partial configurations toward full-modality counterpart; scales variance with discrepancy between aligned means; introduces set-inclusive strategy exploiting hierarchical modality subset structure with ordering constraints for consistent uncertainty relationships.

Result: Superior performance over baselines across diverse missing-modality scenarios on BraTS 2018 and BraTS 2020 datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed probabilistic representation framework with uncertainty modeling and set-inclusive constraints effectively handles missing MRI modalities, improving both reliability and accuracy over deterministic baselines.

Abstract: Multimodal MRI is essential for accurate brain tumor segmentation. However, acquiring all modalities at inference is often challenging in practice, which causes intrinsic uncertainty due to unavoidable information loss. Without modeling this uncertainty, existing methods encode incomplete evidence into deterministic representations that appear plausible but lack reliability. In this regime, we propose a probabilistic representation framework that models representations as Gaussian distributions, where their mean captures task information and their variance measures uncertainty from missing evidence. To make variance reflect information deficiency, we regularize the mean from each partial configuration toward its full-modality counterpart, while scaling the variance with the discrepancy between their aligned means. We further introduce a set-inclusive strategy that exploits the hierarchical structure of modality subsets and enforces an ordering constraint to maintain their consistent uncertainty relationships. Extensive experiments on BraTS 2018 and 2020 demonstrate that our approach offers superior performance over baselines across diverse missing-modality scenarios. Code and model checkpoint are available at https://github.com/atlas-sky/SIUM.

</details>


### [235] [OmniCoT: A Benchmark for Global and Multi-Step Panoramic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30378)
*Haocong He,Chenfei Liao,Zichen Wen,Zihao Dongfang,Xu Zheng,Bin Ren,Chang Su,Zixin Zhang,Harold Haodong Chen,Hongfei Zhang,Weijia Li,Kailun Yang,Conghui He,Xuming Hu,Nicu Sebe,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniCoT is a panoramic spatial reasoning suite with benchmarks (6.7K evaluation, 1K real-world), training data (14.3K with structured CoT), and a two-stage training method (SFT + GRPO) to enable MLLMs to perform global multi-step spatial reasoning across 360°×180° panoramic imagery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing panoramic benchmarks rely on simplistic, local-cue-based queries and single-/few-step reasoning, failing to exploit the fundamental advantage of panoramas' full 360°×180° field of view for complex global multi-step reasoning, which is crucial for applications like embodied intelligence.

Method: Construct OmniCoT-B (6.7K) for evaluation, OmniCoT-Real (1K) manually annotated real-world subset, and OmniCoT-T (14.3K) for training with structured stepwise Chain-of-Thought annotations linking intermediate reasoning to panoramic evidence. Propose OmniCoT-R1 with a two-stage training: SFT anchors reasoning to panoramic evidence (bearings, proximity), and GRPO penalizes geometrically incoherent paths to enforce global 360° spatial consistency.

Result: OmniCoT measures both answer accuracy and reasoning quality; provides a real-world subset to quantify Sim-to-Real gap; the two-stage training strategy is tailored to geometrically complex panoramic space.

Conclusion: OmniCoT recalibrates the difficulty of panoramic spatial reasoning to align with the intrinsic capabilities of panoramic imagery, fostering meaningful progress in MLLMs' spatial reasoning over panoramas.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated promising spatial reasoning capabilities, while these abilities remain underexplored in the emerging visual modality of panoramic imagery. The full 360°$\times$180° field of view of panoramas essentially supports complex global multi-step reasoning, which is also the fundamental advantage of panoramas in applications such as embodied intelligence. However, existing panoramic benchmarks largely focus on simplistic queries that rely on local cues or single-/few-step reasoning, thereby ignoring the fundamental advantage of panoramas and failing to fully exploit their potential. To address this gap, we introduce OmniCoT, a panoramic spatial reasoning suite designed to enable MLLMs to use global evidence and perform multi-step inference across viewpoints. It includes OmniCoT-B (6.7K data) for evaluation, which measures both answer accuracy and reasoning quality, OmniCoT-Real (1K data) as a manually annotated real-world subset to quantify the Sim-to-Real gap. For training, OmniCoT-T (14.3K data) is purpose-built with structured stepwise Chain-of-Thought annotations that explicitly link intermediate reasoning steps to panoramic evidence. Based on OmniCoT-T, we introduce OmniCoT-R1 and adopt a two-stage training strategy tailored to the geometrically complex panoramic space, where Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) anchors reasoning to panoramic evidence (e.g., bearings, proximity) and GRPO penalizes geometrically incoherent paths to consolidate global 360° spatial consistency. Through OmniCoT, we aim to recalibrate the difficulty of panoramic spatial reasoning to better align with the intrinsic capabilities of panoramic imagery, thereby fostering meaningful progress in this research area.

</details>


### [236] [SADL: What to Ignore? A Benchmark for Subject-Aware Distractor Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30393)
*Cao-Tri Nguyen,Nguyen-Khoa Luong,Vinh-Tiep Nguyen,Minh-Triet Tran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SADL is the first benchmark for subject-aware distractor localization in photographs, comprising 1,800 cases across 1,000 photos with 14,617 annotated candidates. Evaluation of seven VLMs reveals they identify distractors well but over-apply exclusion filtering, systematically suppressing true distractors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing saliency models and open-vocabulary detectors lack subject awareness and cannot adapt to shifting user intent; context-agnostic removal disrupts semantic coherence (e.g., removing a chair a person sits on). There was no benchmark for subject-conditioned distractor identification.

Method: Formalized subject-aware distractor localization; constructed SADL benchmark with 1,800 cases from 1,000 photographs (14,617 annotated candidates, 1,938 hard negatives); evaluated seven VLMs on a sequential pipeline of distractor classification followed by exclusion filtering structured around five inclusion factors and three contextual exclusion rules.

Result: VLMs are highly capable of identifying distractors but systematically over-apply exclusion, which suppresses true distractors at scale.

Conclusion: SADL exposes a critical bottleneck in multimodal reasoning (over-exclusion) and provides a foundational diagnostic tool to advance subject-conditioned reasoning in multimodal systems.

Abstract: Photographs frequently contain \emph{visual distractors} besides foregrounds and backgrounds of the intended subject, competing for attention and weakening composition. While modern editing tools streamline object removal, identifying which objects to remove remains a mostly manual process. Existing saliency models and open-vocabulary detectors operate without subject awareness, failing to adapt to shifting user intent. Furthermore, context-agnostic removal may disrupt the scene's semantic coherence (e.g., keep the person but remove the chair they are sitting on). To address these limitations, we formalize the task of subject-aware distractor localization, which identifies distractors while retaining compositionally essential objects. This paper introduces \textsc{SADL}, the first real-world benchmark for this task, comprising 1,800 subject-aware cases across 1,000 photographs to enable systematic evaluation and facilitate future research. In total, there are 14,617 annotated candidates, including a robust set of 1,938 hard negatives to stress-test exclusion calibration. We evaluate seven proprietary and open-weight Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on a sequential pipeline of distractor classification followed by exclusion filtering, structured around five inclusion factors and three contextual exclusion rules. Our analysis reveals that VLMs are highly capable of identifying distractors, but then over-apply exclusion, which systematically suppresses true distractors at scale. By exposing this critical bottleneck, \textsc{SADL} provides a foundational diagnostic tool to advance subject-conditioned reasoning in multimodal systems.

</details>


### [237] [SA-Homo: Scale Adaptive Homography Estimation for Scale Variation Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30408)
*Shangxuan Xie,Haifeng Wu,Yuhang Wang,Huarong Jia,Wen Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SA-Homo proposes a scale-adaptive homography estimation framework using a hierarchical alignment strategy (global-to-local) with a Scale-aware Discrepancy Bridging Module (SDBM) for initial alignment and a lightweight Iterative Homography Estimation Refinement Module (IHERM) for refinement, achieving robust performance even under 8× scale discrepancies, validated on a new HMSA satellite dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Homography estimation under scale variation scenarios remains challenging because existing deep learning methods rely on limited displacement assumptions and local feature consistency, which fail under large scale gaps, leading to significant performance degradation.

Method: SA-Homo adopts a hierarchical scale alignment strategy: (1) Scale-aware Discrepancy Bridging Module (SDBM) with Multi-scale Linear Attention Cascade (MLAC) for long-range dependencies and global Cross-scale Similarity Matrix Block (CSMB) for robust correlation; (2) lightweight Iterative Homography Estimation Refinement Module (IHERM) for progressive local refinement. A new HMSA dataset is also introduced.

Result: SA-Homo maintains high precision even under 8× scale discrepancies and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both conventional scale-similar and challenging scale variation scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed scale-adaptive framework effectively bridges large scale gaps via a global-to-local hierarchical strategy, and the HMSA dataset facilitates future research on scale-variant homography estimation.

Abstract: Homography estimation, as one of the fundamental problems in computer vision, remains challenged by scale variation scenarios where image pairs potentially exhibit significant scale discrepancies. Existing deep learning frameworks frequently suffer from a significant performance degradation in such cases, as they rely on limited displacement assumptions and local feature consistency that might not hold under large scale gaps. In this paper, we propose SA-Homo, a novel scale-adaptive homography estimation framework designed to achieve robust alignment across a wide range of scale discrepancy ratios. We adopt a hierarchical scale alignment strategy that transitions from the global perspective with a heavy module to a local perspective with a light module. Specifically, we introduce the Scale-aware Discrepancy Bridging Module (SDBM) for initial alignment, which utilizes a Multi-scale Linear Attention Cascade (MLAC) to capture long-range dependencies and mitigate feature inconsistencies, along with a global Cross-scale Similarity Matrix Block (CSMB) for scale robust correlation representation. Once the initial scale gap is bridged, a lightweight Iterative Homography Estimation Refinement Module (IHERM) progressively polishes the result using local correlations. To facilitate this research, we contribute the HMSA dataset, a high-resolution, multi-modal satellite benchmark specifically tailored for scale-variant challenges. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SA-Homo maintains high precision even under 8$\times$ scale discrepancies, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both conventional scale-similar scenarios and challenging scale variation scenarios. Code and collected datasets are available at https://github.com/shangxuanx330/SA_Homo

</details>


### [238] [Beyond Point Estimates for Glaucoma Visual Field Forecasting with Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30417)
*Marta Colmenar Herrera,Pablo Márquez Neila,Şerife Seda Kucur Ergünay,Martin S. Zinkernagel,Raphael Sznitman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes using conditioned denoising diffusion models for probabilistic visual field (VF) forecasting in glaucoma, producing well-calibrated distributions of future VFs that outperform deterministic point-estimate methods in both accuracy and uncertainty representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VF forecasting is inherently uncertain due to heterogeneous disease progression and measurement variability, yet existing methods produce single deterministic predictions that fail to represent this uncertainty.

Method: Formulate VF forecasting as a probabilistic prediction problem and use conditioned denoising diffusion models to generate distributions of plausible future VFs from longitudinal observations with irregular follow-up intervals.

Result: On two independent VF cohorts, diffusion-based predictions produce well-calibrated distributions for clinically relevant VF measures, and achieve state-of-the-art accuracy compared to clinical baselines and prior learning-based methods when reduced to point estimates.

Conclusion: Distributional modeling offers advantages for VF forecasting, supporting a shift from point-estimate prediction toward uncertainty-aware, clinically interpretable risk assessment in glaucoma.

Abstract: Forecasting visual fields (VFs) is critical for personalized monitoring and treatment planning in glaucoma. This is inherently uncertain due to heterogeneous disease progression and measurement variability, yet most existing methods produce single deterministic predictions that fail to represent this uncertainty. We formulate VF forecasting as a probabilistic prediction problem and the use of conditioned denoising diffusion models to generate distributions of plausible future VFs from longitudinal observations with irregular follow-up intervals. Experiments on two independent VF cohorts show that diffusion-based predictions produce well-calibrated distributions for clinically relevant VF measures. When reduced to a standard point-estimate, the proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy compared to clinical baselines and prior learning-based methods. Our results highlight the advantages of distributional modeling for VF forecasting and support a shift from point-estimate prediction toward uncertainty-aware, clinically interpretable risk assessment in glaucoma.

</details>


### [239] [OWMDrive: Causality-Aware End-to-End Autonomous Driving via 4D Occupancy World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30421)
*Junjie Cheng,Ruiqi Song,Ye Wu,Nanxing Zeng,Ximiao Li,Yunfeng Ai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OWMDrive is a generative end-to-end driving framework that uses an Occupancy World Model to forecast multi-step 3D occupancy, which serves as a conditional prior for diffusion-based planning. By explicitly modeling future scene evolution, it captures spatiotemporal causal dependencies, leading to more robust and foresighted trajectory generation, especially in challenging, partially observable scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-to-end driving methods rely on static scene representations without explicit future rollouts or modeling of temporal causal dynamics, resulting in unstable or overly conservative planning under high-uncertainty conditions like occlusions and unexpected events.

Method: OWMDrive builds an Occupancy World Model for multi-step 3D occupancy forecasting, uses predicted future states as a conditional prior, and employs diffusion-based planning to iteratively refine trajectory candidates for reinforced driving trajectory generation.

Result: Significant improvements in planning reliability and safety, particularly in challenging and partially observable driving scenarios.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling scene evolution over future horizons via occupancy world models enables more foresighted and robust trajectory generation compared to static-representation-based methods.

Abstract: Autonomous driving systems are steadily moving toward end-to-end paradigms to mitigate the limited adaptability of rule-based pipelines in complex traffic environments. However, most existing learning-based methods still make decisions from static representations of the current scene, without explicit future rollouts or modeling of the temporal causal dynamics in traffic interactions. This limitation often results in unstable or overly conservative planning under high-uncertainty conditions, such as occlusions and unexpected events. To overcome these challenges, we introduce OWMDrive, a generative end-to-end driving framework built upon an Occupancy World Model for multi-step 3D occupancy forecasting, which serves as a conditional prior to guide diffusion-based planning. Conditioned on both current observations and predicted future states, the planner iteratively refines trajectory candidates to generate a reinforced driving trajectory. By explicitly modeling scene evolution over future horizons, OWMDrive captures key spatiotemporal causal dependencies, which leads to more foresighted and robust trajectory generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OWMDrive significantly improves planning reliability and safety, especially in challenging and partially observable driving scenarios.

</details>


### [240] [Robust and Efficient Monocular 3D Gaussian SLAM for Kilometer-Scale Outdoor Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30436)
*Sicheng Yu,Dongxu Shen,Beizhen Zhao,Guanzhi Ding,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KiloGS-SLAM is a monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM system that addresses fragile pose tracking and excessive memory in large-scale outdoor environments via a motion-adaptive hybrid tracking module and a lifecycle-managed Gaussian mapping strategy, achieving SOTA performance on sequences over 10,000 frames.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling monocular 3DGS SLAM to kilometer-level outdoor environments faces two tightly coupled challenges: fragile long-term pose tracking and excessive memory overhead during large-scale mapping.

Method: (1) Motion-adaptive hybrid tracking module: a condition-triggered three-tier solving pipeline that dynamically switches between Essential matrix and PnP models, with an on-demand foundation model to rescue from catastrophic drift. (2) Lifecycle-managed Gaussian mapping strategy: integrates probabilistic initialization with chunk-based multi-view densification and pruning to reduce primitive redundancy while preserving high-frequency details.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art tracking accuracy and rendering quality across three challenging outdoor datasets, successfully scaling to sequences of over 10,000 frames on a single GPU.

Conclusion: Robust tracking guarantees the geometric foundation for accurate mapping, while memory-efficient lifecycle-managed mapping enables large-scale operation; the joint approach effectively scales monocular 3DGS SLAM to kilometer-level environments.

Abstract: Scaling monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) SLAM to kilometer-level outdoor environments poses two tightly coupled challenges: fragile long-term pose tracking and excessive memory overhead during large-scale mapping. In this paper, we propose KiloGS-SLAM, a highly efficient and robust monocular 3DGS-SLAM system that jointly addresses both bottlenecks. Since high-fidelity scene reconstruction fundamentally relies on drift-free camera poses, we first introduce a motion-adaptive hybrid tracking module. This module features a condition-triggered three-tier solving pipeline. It dynamically switches between Essential matrix and PnP models to handle geometric degeneracies. An on-demand foundation model can also be activated to rescue the trajectory from catastrophic drift. To ensure the system can sustain these long trajectories without memory exhaustion, we subsequently design a lifecycle-managed Gaussian mapping strategy. By integrating probabilistic initialization with chunk-based multi-view densification and pruning, this full-pipeline optimization effectively reduces primitive redundancy while preserving high-frequency details. Together, the robust tracking guarantees the geometric foundation required for accurate mapping, while the memory-efficient lifecycle-managed mapping enables large-scale operation. Extensive experiments across three challenging outdoor datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art tracking accuracy and rendering quality, successfully scaling to sequences of over 10,000 frames on a single GPU.

</details>


### [241] [$μ$Flow: Leveraging Average Images for Improving Generalisation of Deepfake Faces Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30528)
*Orazio Pontorno,Mattia Litrico,Luca Guarnera,Mario Valerio Giuffrida,Sebastiano Battiato*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: μFlow is a one-class deepfake detector trained only on real images that uses averaging of multiple images to amplify generative traces and normalizing flow to align feature spaces, achieving SOTA out-of-distribution generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deepfake detectors rely on supervised training with both real and fake images, limiting their generalization to unseen generators (especially across GANs and diffusion models). A one-class detector that trains only on real images is needed for better real-world applicability.

Method: μFlow trains a normalizing flow on features extracted from averaged real images to model their distribution, then aligns individual image features to this distribution, producing a likelihood-based criterion for separating real from fake samples.

Result: μFlow significantly outperforms state-of-the-art detectors on a fully out-of-distribution setting where both real and fake datasets are unseen during training.

Conclusion: One-class detection via averaging-based feature amplification and normalizing flow alignment enables strong generalization across unseen generator categories without requiring fake images or synthetic artifacts during training.

Abstract: Current generative models, including GANs and diffusion models, have reached an outstanding level of photorealism, posing significant risks to privacy and security. To ensure real-world applicability, deepfake detectors must generalise effectively to unseen generators. However, most existing approaches rely on supervised training with both real and fake images, which limits their generalisation especially across generators categories (e.g. GANs vs DMs). In this work, we introduce $μ$Flow, a one-class deepfake detector trained only on real images without relying on pseudo-deepfakes or synthetic artifacts. Our approach builds on the observation that averaging multiple images amplifies consistent generative traces, producing highly discriminative feature representations. We leverage this property by modelling the distribution of features extracted from averaged images and training a normalizing flow to align the feature space of individual images with this distribution. This alignment yields a likelihood-based criterion that separates real and fake samples while promoting strong generalisation. We evaluate $μ$Flow on a fully out-of-distribution setting, where both real and fake datasets are unseen during training. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms SOTA detectors. Project page: https://opontorno.github.io/MuFlow.

</details>


### [242] [Cross-Resolution Semantic Transfer for Robust Text-to-Image Retrieval in Low-Resolution Surveillance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30458)
*Wenjie Qian,Bin Yang,Xiao Wang,Wenke Huang,Ling Mei,Xin Xu,Mang Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CRST is a CLIP-style framework addressing cross-resolution text-to-image person re-identification by tackling Evidence Reliability Collapse (ERC) and Ranking Distribution Drift (RDD) through resolution-conditioned reasoning, text-guided refinement, and CR-RDA, achieving significant gains on low-resolution benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TIPR methods overlook resolution variance in real-world surveillance, where degraded visual tokens become unreliable (ERC) and mixed-resolution galleries distort similarity rankings (RDD).

Method: A CLIP-style framework with three modules: (1) resolution-conditioned reasoning for token reliability estimation, (2) text-guided refinement injecting semantic priors for discriminative cue recovery, and (3) CR-RDA transferring high-resolution neighborhood geometry to stabilize low-resolution ranking under mixed resolutions.

Result: Improves ultra-low-resolution Rank-1 and mAP by 5.7% and 5.3% on average across CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid, while stabilizing mixed-resolution retrieval without sacrificing high-resolution accuracy.

Conclusion: CRST effectively handles cross-resolution degradation in TIPR by decoupling and addressing both visual evidence unreliability and ranking instability, offering a robust solution for real-world surveillance scenarios.

Abstract: Text-to-image person re-identification (TIPR) retrieves target persons using natural language descriptions. However, existing methods largely overlook resolution variance in real-world surveillance. They characterize cross-resolution TIPR through two coupled failure modes: Evidence Reliability Collapse (ERC), where degraded visual tokens become unreliable for grounding fine-grained text, and Ranking Distribution Drift (RDD), where mixed-resolution galleries distort similarity neighborhoods and destabilize retrieval rankings. To address this challenge, we propose Cross-Resolution Semantic Transfer (CRST), a CLIP-style framework with three modules: resolution-conditioned reasoning, text-guided refinement and CR-RDA. Resolution-conditioned reasoning estimates token reliability to suppress corrupted evidence. Text-guided refinement injects semantic priors to recover discriminative cues. CR-RDA transfers HR neighborhood geometry to stabilize LR ranking under mixed resolutions. Experiments on CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid show that CRST improves ultra-low-resolution Rank-1 and mAP on average by 5.7% and 5.3%, while stabilizing mixed-resolution retrieval without sacrificing high-resolution accuracy.The code will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [243] [FR-DETR: Frequency and Recurrent Feature Refinement for Robust Object Detection under Adverse Weather](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30471)
*Tuan-Duc Nguyen,Duc-Trong Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FR-DETR is a detector-centric framework for object detection under adverse weather that refines features (not images) using a Frequency Refinement Module and a Recurrent Focus Refinement Module, achieving superior accuracy with much lower computational cost than enhancer-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing enhancer-based approaches cascade an enhancer with a detector, leading to redundant feature extraction, high computational cost, and limited accuracy gains when paired with SOTA detectors. The goal is to achieve efficient and accurate object detection under adverse weather.

Method: FR-DETR consists of two key components: (I) a Frequency Refinement Module that dynamically separates and reweights low- and high-frequency components to improve foreground-background discrimination, and (II) a Recurrent Focus Refinement Module (RFRM) that iteratively refines features using coarse predictions as guidance. The framework refines features rather than images, focusing enhancement on regions of interest and leveraging frequency-domain cues.

Result: FR-DETR achieves superior detection accuracy under adverse weather while being significantly more computationally efficient than enhancer-based methods.

Conclusion: The detector-centric feature refinement approach is more effective and efficient for adverse-weather object detection than the conventional enhancer-detector cascade paradigm.

Abstract: Object detection under adverse weather remains challenging due to severe visual degradations and domain shifts. Existing enhancer-based approaches attempt to improve detection by cascading an enhancer with a detector, but they introduce redundant feature extraction and incur high computational cost with limited accuracy gains when paired with SOTA detectors. We propose FR-DETR, a detector-centric framework that refines features rather than images, focusing enhancement on regions of interest and leveraging frequency-domain cues. Specifically, we design (I) a Frequency Refinement Module that dynamically separates and reweights low- and high-frequency components to improve foreground-background discrimination, and (II) a Recurrent Focus Refinement Module (RFRM) that iteratively refines features using coarse predictions as guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FR-DETR achieves superior detection accuracy under adverse weather while being significantly more computationally efficient than enhancer-based methods. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/ducnt1210/FR-DETR.

</details>


### [244] [PS-MOT: Cultivating Instance Awareness from Point Seeds for Multi-Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30476)
*Kai Luo,Fei Teng,Mengfei Duan,Wanjun Jia,Xu Wang,Hao Shi,Kunyu Peng,Zhiyong Li,Kailun Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PS-Track introduces a point-supervised multi-object tracking method using Temporal-Feedback Prompting, Point-Excited Wavelet Attention, and Uncertainty-Guided Gaussian Learning to address spatial ambiguity and identity drift, achieving SOTA on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce annotation cost, the authors propose Point-supervised Multi-Object Tracking (PS-MOT) as an alternative to bounding box supervision, shifting from spatial fitting to topological center-driven representation, while addressing challenges like spatial ambiguity and identity drift.

Method: A hierarchical pipeline (PS-Track) with three levels: (1) Data: Temporal-Feedback Prompting (TFP) evolves points into temporally consistent pseudo-labels using negative spatial cues and motion priors; (2) Model: Point-Excited Wavelet Attention (PEWA) uses semantic correlations to activate high-frequency components for hallucinating object boundaries; (3) Loss: Uncertainty-Guided Gaussian Learning (UGL) models pseudo-labels as probabilistic distributions to dynamically calibrate supervision intensity.

Result: PS-Track achieves state-of-the-art performance on DanceTrack, EmboTrack, SportsMOT, and JRDB for point-supervised multi-object tracking.

Conclusion: PS-Track provides a feasible and effective point-supervised alternative across diverse tracking scenarios, establishing a new SOTA for point-supervised tracking.

Abstract: We introduce Point-supervised Multi-Object Tracking (PS-MOT) as a cost-effective alternative to traditional bounding box supervision, shifting the focus from spatial fitting to topological center-driven representation. However, PS-MOT faces challenges, e.g., spatial ambiguity and identity drift due to the lack of explicit geometric structure and scale constraints. To address these, we propose PS-Track, a hierarchical pipeline transitioning from points to instances across data, model, and loss levels. At the data level, we introduce Temporal-Feedback Prompting (TFP) to evolve points into temporally consistent pseudo-labels using negative spatial cues and motion priors. At the model level, we design the Point-Excited Wavelet Attention (PEWA) module, which leverages semantic correlations to activate high-frequency components, ``hallucinating'' object boundaries. At the loss level, Uncertainty-Guided Gaussian Learning (UGL) models pseudo-labels as probabilistic distributions, dynamically calibrating supervision intensity. Experiments on DanceTrack, EmboTrack, SportsMOT, and JRDB demonstrate that PS-Track provides a feasible and effective point-supervised alternative across diverse tracking scenarios, establishing a new state-of-the-art for point-supervised tracking. The source code is available at https://github.com/xifen523/PS-MOT.

</details>


### [245] [PGE-SAM: Prompt-Guided Feature Enhancement for Interactive Segmentation under Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30477)
*Tuan-Duc Nguyen,Anh-Tuan Mai,Duc-Trong Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PGE-SAM enhances SAM's robustness against real-world imaging degradations (noise, blur, compression) by using user prompts and prior masks to guide feature restoration towards regions of interest, incorporating multi-scale feature interaction and foreground reconstruction loss. It also introduces DM-Seg, a benchmark for interactive segmentation on degraded medical images, achieving SOTA robustness with minimal parameter overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM's zero-shot segmentation performance degrades substantially under real-world imaging artifacts (noise, blur, compression). Existing restoration methods are suboptimal because they (1) restore features globally without focusing on segmentation-relevant regions, and (2) neglect SAM's iterative refinement mechanism in interactive settings.

Method: (1) Prompt Guidance Generator: leverages user prompts and prior mask predictions to spatially guide feature restoration toward regions of interest. (2) Multi-Scale Features Interaction: incorporates low-level encoder features to recover fine-grained details lost under degradation. (3) Foreground Reconstruction Loss: restricts feature-level supervision to the segmentation target. (4) DM-Seg benchmark: covers multiple medical imaging modalities with general and modality-specific degradations at varying severity levels.

Result: PGE-SAM achieves SOTA robustness on both medical and natural image domains across multiple degradation levels, maintains generalization to clean images, and adds less than one-fifth of the parameters of prior methods.

Conclusion: Prompt-guided, spatially-focused feature enhancement is an effective and parameter-efficient approach to making SAM robust against diverse real-world imaging degradations in interactive segmentation settings.

Abstract: Segment Anything Model (SAM) has revolutionized promptable image segmentation with strong zero-shot generalization. However, its performance degrades substantially under real-world imaging artifacts such as noise, blur, and compression. Existing methods restore features globally without focusing on segmentation-relevant regions and neglect SAM's iterative refinement mechanism, leading to suboptimal performance in interactive settings. We propose Prompt-Guided Feature Enhancement SAM (PGE-SAM), a framework that explicitly leverages user prompts and prior mask predictions to spatially guide the feature restoration process toward regions of interest through a Prompt Guidance Generator. To recover fine-grained details lost under degradation, we introduce Multi-Scale Features Interaction to incorporate low-level encoder features, along with a Foreground Reconstruction Loss that restricts feature-level supervision to the segmentation target. Furthermore, we present DM-Seg, a benchmark for interactive segmentation on degraded medical images, spanning multiple imaging modalities with both general and modality-specific degradations at varying severity levels. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PGE-SAM achieves SOTA robustness on both medical and natural image domains across multiple degradation levels, while maintaining generalization to clean images and adding less than one-fifth of the parameters of prior methods.

</details>


### [246] [RBE-Flow: Recurrent Bayesian Estimation on Feature Manifolds for Cross-Modal Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30492)
*Mengzhu Ding,Xin Song,Xiaoke Ding,Hongwei Ding,Xuecong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RBE-Flow proposes a closed-loop recurrent Bayesian estimation framework for dense cross-modal image registration, combining Recurrent Manifold Optimization with uncertainty-adaptive probabilistic updates to achieve state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal image registration suffers from severe non-linear radiometric discrepancies and geometric distortions, and existing deterministic methods lack uncertainty awareness, leading to error accumulation in ambiguous regions.

Method: RBE-Flow reformulates dense cross-modal flow estimation as a closed-loop recurrent Bayesian estimation problem. It uses a Recurrent Manifold Optimization (RMO) block for iterative flow observation generation with uncertainties, an Uncertainty-Adaptive Probabilistic Update (UAPU) via sigma-point projection for state assimilation, and a feedback mechanism where posterior covariance adaptively regularizes subsequent optimization. Training uses a hybrid supervision scheme with geometry-aware rectified NLL loss to prevent variance collapse.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on OSdataset, WHU-OPT-SAR, and RoadScene benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing methods especially under strict sub-pixel criteria.

Conclusion: The closed-loop recurrent Bayesian framework with uncertainty feedback provides robust self-correction for cross-modal registration, setting a new state-of-the-art.

Abstract: Cross-modal image registration is essential for multi-sensor perception but remains fundamentally challenging due to severe non-linear radiometric discrepancies and geometric distortions. Existing deterministic matching methods lack uncertainty awareness, struggling to navigate the resulting highly non-convex optimization landscape and frequently accumulating errors in ambiguous regions. In this paper, we propose RBE-Flow, a novel framework that reformulates dense cross-modal flow estimation as a closed-loop recurrent Bayesian estimation problem on learned feature manifolds. Diverging from standard feed-forward regression, RBE-Flow establishes a robust self-correcting mechanism by deeply coupling feature-metric non-linear optimization with probabilistic state updates. Specifically, a Recurrent Manifold Optimization (RMO) block iteratively generates flow observations and their associated uncertainties, which are then optimally assimilated into the prior state via an Uncertainty-Adaptive Probabilistic Update (UAPU) using deterministic sigma-point projection. Crucially, the resulting calibrated posterior covariance is fed back to adaptively regularize the damping of subsequent optimization steps, allowing the system to modulate its convergence based on predictive confidence. To ensure stable probabilistic training, we introduce a hybrid supervision scheme featuring a geometry-aware rectified NLL loss that structurally prevents variance collapse. Extensive experiments on challenging OSdataset, WHU-OPT-SAR, and RoadScene benchmarks demonstrate that RBE-Flow consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods by a significant margin, particularly under strict sub-pixel criteria. Project page: https://github.com/NEU-Liuxuecong/RBE-Flow

</details>


### [247] [On the Faithfulness of Post-Hoc Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30498)
*Laines Schmalwasser,Jan Blunk,Niklas Penzel,Julia Niebling,Joachim Denzler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies and formalizes two failure modes in Post-Hoc Concept Bottleneck Models—covariate shift from auxiliary data and systematic label noise from VLMs—that cause semantically unfaithful concept projections despite high accuracy, and introduces new metrics that decouple concept faithfulness from predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of post-hoc CBMs relies on target task accuracy, which can be misleading because even random concept projections achieve competitive accuracy. This obscures whether learned concepts are semantically meaningful or merely predictive artifacts, motivating the need to directly analyze concept faithfulness.

Method: The authors directly analyze learned concept projections and formalize two failure cases: (1) covariate shifts between auxiliary and target data, with a theoretical upper bound on the induced error; (2) systematic label noise from VLM-generated surrogate concept labels. They then propose novel metrics that separate concept faithfulness from predictive accuracy.

Result: Empirical results on both real-world and synthetic benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed metrics successfully identify unfaithful concept behaviors that standard accuracy-based evaluation fails to detect.

Conclusion: Accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating post-hoc CBMs; concept faithfulness must be assessed separately via dedicated metrics to ensure interpretability is meaningful rather than an artifact of predictive performance.

Abstract: Human decision-making interprets the world through high-level concepts, such as recognizing a bird by its belly color. To bridge the gap between opaque deep learning representations and human understanding, Post-Hoc Concept Bottleneck Models (post-hoc CBMs) project latent features onto interpretable concept spaces using auxiliary datasets or vision-language models. However, relying on target task accuracy as the primary measure of post-hoc CBM success obscures whether the learned concepts are semantically meaningful or merely predictive artifacts. For example, random concept projections can achieve competitive accuracy despite being semantically meaningless. In this work, we analyze the learned projections directly and identify two failure cases: First, for concept projections learned from auxiliary data, covariate shifts can lead to unfaithful concept representations for the target task. In particular, we provide an upper bound on the error introduced by this shift. Second, systematic label noise in surrogate concept labels generated by vision-language models leads to unfaithful projections. After formalizing these failure modes, we introduce novel metrics that decouple concept faithfulness from predictive accuracy. Our empirical results across real-world and synthetic benchmarks confirm that these metrics identify unfaithful behaviors that standard accuracy-based evaluation fails to detect.

</details>


### [248] [High-Resolution Flood Mapping With Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 via Misalignment-Robust Cross-Sensor Learning and Generative Despeckling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30511)
*David Ma,Jeremy Feinstein,Shreya Pandit,Arkaprabha Ganguli,Eugene Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces an integrated flood mapping framework that combines a curated Sentinel-2/Sentinel-1 dataset with shift-invariant loss functions and CVAE-based despeckling to improve high-resolution flood mapping, achieving strong multispectral performance (AUPRC up to 0.956) and significant gains in SAR-based mapping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing flood mapping from satellite imagery is constrained by sensor-specific artifacts: optical imagery suffers from clouds, shadows, and urban confounders, while SAR imagery faces speckle noise and co-registration uncertainty. Current benchmarks also lack challenging weather and urban scenarios.

Method: (1) Created a new Sentinel-2/Sentinel-1 dataset with pixel-accurate 10m water masks, with manual annotation for S2 and weakly labeled transfer to SAR via temporally coincident acquisitions. (2) Used a shift-invariant loss function to tolerate geolocation uncertainty between SAR and optical-derived labels. (3) Trained a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) on multitemporal SAR composites for despeckling. (4) Evaluated using UNet and UNet++ architectures.

Result: Achieved AUPRC up to 0.956 for multispectral flood mapping. Statistically significant improvements in SAR flood mapping when using shift-invariant loss and CVAE-based despeckling compared to classical filters.

Conclusion: High-fidelity datasets, misalignment-robust training, and generative despeckling are crucial for operational flood mapping, demonstrating the viability of these techniques for practical applications.

Abstract: Reliable high-resolution flood extent mapping from satellite imagery remains constrained by limited data fidelity and sensor-specific artifacts. Multispectral optical imagery is degraded by clouds, shadows, and urban confounders, while synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is affected by speckle noise and sensor co-registration uncertainty. This work presents an integrated flood mapping framework that jointly addresses these limitations through curated datasets and novel learning strategies. We introduce a new Sentinel-2 (S2) and Sentinel-1 (S1) dataset covering the contiguous United States, featuring pixel-accurate 10 m water masks with emphasis on challenging weather conditions and urban environments that are underrepresented in existing benchmarks. High-quality S2 annotations are manually produced using rigorous geospatial labeling protocols and transferred to SAR imagery through weakly labeled temporally coincident acquisitions. To address SAR-specific artifacts, a shift-invariant loss function is employed to tolerate residual geolocation uncertainty between SAR imagery and optical-derived labels, and a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) is trained on multitemporal SAR composites to suppress speckle while preserving flood-relevant spatial structure. Experiments using UNet and UNet++ architectures demonstrate strong multispectral performance (AUPRC up to 0.956) and statistically significant improvements in SAR flood mapping when using shift-invariant loss and CVAE-based despeckling compared to classical filters. These results underscore the importance of dataset fidelity, misalignment-robust training, and demonstrate the viability of generative despeckling for operational flood mapping.

</details>


### [249] [3D Scene-Adaptive Trajectory-Controllable Human Image Animation with Camera Movement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30514)
*Deyin Liu,Jicheng Xu,Lin Yuanbo Wu,Xiaowei Zhao,Xiatian Zhu,Zhe Jin,Anjan Dutta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A scene-adaptive human image animation framework that controls both human motion and camera trajectories within reconstructed 3D environments, using ground-adaptive 3D motion retargeting and viewpoint-adaptive latent fusion with point-cloud geometric priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing human animation works have upgraded from 2D to 3D guidance but still struggle with synthesizing trajectory-controllable human motion in natural scenes under changing camera views.

Method: (1) Ground-adaptive 3D motion retargeting for automatic adaptation to ground elevation and orientation changes; (2) Viewpoint-adaptive latent fusion mechanism that injects point-cloud geometric priors via scene-visibility masking into the generative process for precise viewpoint change guidance under camera control.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on two standard human image animation benchmarks across related video generation metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed scene-adaptive framework effectively enables controllable human motion and camera trajectories in 3D-aware video generation, achieving superior performance over existing approaches.

Abstract: Human image animation, which aims to generate a video of a reference subject following a provided action sequence, has received increasing research interest. With the development of diffusion-based/flow-based video foundation models, existing animation works have began to upgrade the guidance information from 2D skeleton/pose to 3D modeling conditions. Despite achieving reasonable results, these approaches face challenges in synthesizing trajectory-controllable human motion within natural scene under changed camera views. In this work, we present a scene-adaptive human image animation framework that controls both human motion and camera trajectories within a reconstructed 3D environment for video generation. To achieve this, we first develop a ground-adaptive 3D motion retargeting approach to enable user-friendly motion trajectory control adapting to the changes of elevations of ground and orientations automatically. Then we design a viewpoint-adaptive latent fusion mechanism to inject point-cloud geometric priors through scene-visibility masking into the generative process, providing precise guidance of viewpoint changes under camera control. Experiments on two standard human image animation benchmark datasets demonstrate remarkable improvements of our method over the state of the arts in related video generation metics. Project page: https://robinhood256100.github.io/web-disp

</details>


### [250] [HASTE: A Framework for Training-Free, Dynamic, and Steerable Compression of Pre-Trained Convolutional Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30516)
*Lukas Meiner,Jens Mehnert,Alexandru Paul Condurache*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HASTE is a plug-and-play convolution module that uses locality-sensitive hashing to dynamically merge redundant channels in pre-trained CNNs at inference time, achieving significant FLOPs reduction (e.g., 46.2% on ResNet34/CIFAR-10) with minimal accuracy loss (1.25%) and no retraining or data access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying large CNNs on resource-constrained devices is challenging due to high computational costs, and existing dynamic execution methods require specialized training or fine-tuning, limiting their applicability to pre-trained models.

Method: HASTE employs locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) at inference time to identify and merge redundant channels in latent feature maps on a patch-wise basis, simultaneously compressing the depth of both input features and their corresponding filters for cheaper convolutions.

Result: On CIFAR-10 with ResNet34, HASTE achieves 46.2% FLOPs reduction with only 1.25% accuracy drop, without any retraining. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet across various architectures validate the approach.

Conclusion: HASTE provides an effective, steerable solution for training-free dynamic compression of pre-trained CNNs at runtime, enabling efficient deep learning deployment and bridging connections to token merging in Vision Transformers.

Abstract: Deploying large convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on resource-constrained devices is challenging due to their high computational cost. While dynamic execution methods are promising, existing approaches for CNNs typically require specialized training or fine-tuning, limiting their effectiveness when applied to pre-trained models and requiring data access. To address this gap, we propose HASTE (Hashing for Tractable Efficiency), a plug-and-play convolution module that enables training-free, dynamic compression of large pre-trained CNNs. At inference time, HASTE uses locality-sensitive hashing to identify and merge redundant channels of latent feature maps on a patch-wise basis. This process simultaneously compresses the depth of both input features and their corresponding filters, resulting in computationally cheaper convolutions. We conduct extensive experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet across a range of architectures, demonstrating a 46.2% FLOPs reduction in a ResNet34 on CIFAR-10 with only a 1.25% drop in accuracy, without any retraining. We support our claims by comprehensive ablation studies to validate our core design choices, an analysis of the method's properties and limitations, and a discussion that connects our channel merging scheme to the conceptually related task of token merging in Vision Transformers. Our results demonstrate that HASTE provides an effective solution for steerable compression of pre-trained CNNs at runtime, opening new possibilities for the deployment of efficient deep learning methods.

</details>


### [251] [Orca: The World is in Your Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30534)
*Yihao Wang,Yuheng Ji,Mingyu Cao,Yanqing Shen,Runze Xiao,Huaihai Lyu,Senwei Xie,Euan Liu,Klara Tian,Tianfeng Long,Yichi Zhang,Zhengliang Cai,Ruike Chen,Jifan Zhao,Ruochuan Shi,Zihan Tang,Jing Lyu,Wenxing Tan,Ningbo Zhang,Yangtao Hu,Yuming Gao,Xiansheng Chen,Junkai Zhao,Congsheng Xu,Boan Zhu,Ziqi Wang,Yupu Feng,Qiongqiong Zhang,Yingli Zhao,Yulong Ao,Shaoxuan Xie,You Liu,Guocai Yao,Leiduo Zhang,Xiaodan Liu,Yunyan Zhang,Yance Jiao,Xinyan Yang,Jiaxing Wei,Xu Liu,Tengfei Pan,Shaokai Nie,Chunlei Men,Sen Cui,Xiaojie Jin,Hongyang Li,Jianlan Luo,Yao Mu,Yunchao Wei,Jun Yan,Hang Zhao,Xiaolong Zheng,Jiaming Li,Yonghua Lin,Tiejun Huang,Zhongyuan Wang,Pengwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Orca is a general world foundation model that learns a unified world latent space from multimodal signals via Next-State-Prediction, combining unconscious (dense video) and conscious (sparse event/VQA) learning, achieving strong downstream performance across text, image, and embodied action tasks with a frozen backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond isolated next-token, next-frame, or next-action prediction paradigms and instead build a unified world foundation model capable of understanding, predicting, and acting upon the world through a shared state-transition modeling approach.

Method: Orca uses Next-State-Prediction modeling with two complementary learning paradigms: (1) unconscious learning from dense natural state transitions in continuous videos, and (2) conscious learning from sparse meaningful state transitions via language-described events and VQA supervision. Pre-training uses 125K hours of video and 160M event annotations. The frozen backbone is evaluated via lightweight modality-specific decoders for text, image, and embodied action generation.

Result: The proposed paradigm shows scalability; stronger world latent enables stronger downstream readouts. Orca outperforms similar-sized specialized baselines across text generation, image prediction, and embodied action generation tasks.

Conclusion: Orca demonstrates a promising approach as a general world foundation model for unified understanding, prediction, and action, with identified limitations to guide future research.

Abstract: We introduce Orca, an initial instantiation of a general world foundation model. Orca learns a unified world latent space from multimodal world signals and exposes it through multimodal readout interfaces. Rather than optimizing isolated next-token, next-frame, or next-action prediction, we are centered on Next-State-Prediction modeling, offering a unified state-transition modeling route toward understanding, predicting, and acting upon the world. Orca learns through two complementary paradigms: unconscious learning captures dense natural state transitions from continuous videos, and conscious learning models sparse meaningful state transitions by language-described events and VQA supervision. For pre-training, we construct a large-scale world-learning inventory data, including 125K hours of video data and 160M event annotations. After pre-training, Orca learns a unified world latent space. To examine whether the learned latent supports downstream, we evaluate it by three representative downstream readouts: text generation, image prediction, and embodied action generation. Orca's backbone is frozen, and only the lightweight modality-specific decoders are trainable. Experiments show the scalability of the proposed paradigm and verify that stronger world latent enables stronger downstream readouts. Orca outperforms similar-sized specialized baselines. These results show that Orca, as a general world foundation model, presents a promising approach to understanding, predicting, and acting upon the world. Finally, we discuss the current limitations, aiming to provide useful insights and inspiration for the community.

</details>


### [252] [StereoGS: Sparse-View 3D Gaussian Splatting via Stereo Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30545)
*Wenhao Yuan,Yiyuan Ge,Deli Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StereoGS integrates stereo priors (binocular consistency, absolute scale) into 3D Gaussian Splatting for sparse-view novel view synthesis, using Stereo Depth Regularization, Gradient-Aware Opacity Decay, and Consistency-Aware Dense Initialization to achieve SOTA performance without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3DGS suffers from severe overfitting under sparse-view settings due to insufficient geometric constraints. Existing monocular depth priors struggle with scale ambiguity and cross-view inconsistency, leading to defective geometry.

Method: (1) Stereo Depth Regularization: constructs virtual stereo pairs during optimization and uses a foundation stereo model to enforce absolute scale and binocular-consistent structures. (2) Gradient-Aware Opacity Decay: dynamically penalizes Gaussians based on relative opacity gradient magnitudes to suppress overfitting and eliminate redundant primitives. (3) Consistency-Aware Dense Initialization: uses zero-shot multi-view depth estimation to anchor primitives to accurate scene surfaces.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on LLFF, DTU, Mip-NeRF360, and Blender datasets under sparse-view settings, without incurring any additional inference overhead.

Conclusion: Stereo priors effectively overcome the scale ambiguity and inconsistency issues of monocular depth priors, and the proposed regularization strategies robustly address overfitting in sparse-view 3DGS.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has achieved remarkable success in real-time novel view synthesis, yet it suffers from severe overfitting under sparse-view settings due to insufficient geometric constraints. While recent methods introduce monocular depth priors to mitigate this, they inherently struggle with scale ambiguity and cross-view inconsistency, leading to defective geometry. In this paper, we propose StereoGS, a novel sparse-view 3DGS framework that integrates stereo priors to establish reliable binocular consistency. Unlike scale-agnostic monocular constraints, StereoGS introduces a Stereo Depth Regularization by constructing virtual stereo pairs during optimization and leveraging a foundation stereo model to enforce absolute scale and binocular-consistent structures. To further suppress overfitting and eliminate redundant primitives, we design a Gradient-Aware Opacity Decay strategy that dynamically penalizes Gaussians based on their relative opacity gradient magnitudes. Combined with a Consistency-Aware Dense Initialization using zero-shot multi-view depth estimation, StereoGS effectively anchors primitives to accurate scene surfaces. Extensive experiments on LLFF, DTU, Mip-NeRF360, and Blender datasets demonstrate that StereoGS achieves state-of-the-art performance in sparse-view settings without incurring any additional inference overhead. Project Page: https://stringerywh00.github.io/StereoGS_project_page/

</details>


### [253] [EcoVideo: Entropy-Orchestrated Video Generation Paradigm in Cloud-Edge Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30557)
*Jiayu Chen,Hengyi Zhang,Maoliang Li,Minyu Li,Zihao Zheng,Xuanzhe Liu,Guojie Luo,Xiang Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EcoVideo is an entropy-orchestrated framework that dynamically decouples video generation across cloud and edge by using self-attention entropy to select keyframes for cloud denoising and reconstructing remaining frames via motion-aware interpolation with edge refinement, achieving up to 2.9x speedup under bandwidth/compute constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DiT video generation is latency-intensive due to iterative full-frame denoising, and existing cloud-edge methods rely on static inter-step decoupling that cannot leverage inter-frame similarity or adapt to system dynamics.

Method: EcoVideo uses early-stage self-attention entropy as a training-free estimate of frame-wise information density to select sparse high-entropy keyframes for cloud large model denoising; remaining frames are reconstructed via motion-aware interpolation with edge lightweight model refinement. The framework dynamically adapts keyframe budget and edge refinement depth to real-time bandwidth and compute availability.

Result: On representative DiT video generators, EcoVideo achieves improved quality-efficiency trade-offs and up to 2.9x end-to-end speedup in low-bandwidth, compute-limited edge settings.

Conclusion: EcoVideo demonstrates that dynamic, entropy-driven inter-frame decoupling across cloud and edge can substantially reduce latency while maintaining generation quality, especially under constrained system conditions.

Abstract: DiT video generation is latency-intensive due to iterative full-frame denoising, while prior cloud-edge methods largely rely on static inter-step decoupling and cannot leverage inter-frame similarity or adapt to system dynamics. We propose EcoVideo, an entropy-orchestrated framework for dynamic inter-frame decoupling: early-stage self-attention entropy provides a training-free estimate of frame-wise information density for frame selection; a cloud large model denoises sparse high-entropy keyframes; and an edge lightweight model reconstructs the remaining frames via motion-aware interpolation with refinement for temporal stability. EcoVideo further adapts the keyframe budget and edge refinement depth to real-time bandwidth and compute availability, optimizing end-to-end latency under constraints. Experiments on representative DiT video generators show improved quality--efficiency trade-offs and up to 2.9x end-to-end speedup in low-bandwidth, compute-limited edge settings. Code is available at https://github.com/IF-LAB-PKU/EcoVideo.

</details>


### [254] [Beyond 2D Matching: A Unified Single-Stage Framework for Geometry-Aware Cross-View Object Geo-Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30576)
*Liyao Wang,Ruipu Wu,Haojun Xu,Lei Shi,Linjiang Huang,Si Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a large-scale building dataset (220K+ pairs) for cross-view object geo-localization and proposes GAGeo, a single-stage geometry-aware framework based on a 3D foundation model that jointly predicts bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and camera poses, achieving state-of-the-art performance and zero-shot ground-to-drone localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CVOGL approaches rely heavily on 2D appearance matching and are constrained by limited datasets lacking geometric metadata, diverse prompts, and standard field-of-view imagery. The authors aim to address these intertwined challenges.

Method: The authors introduce a large-scale building dataset (220K+ ground-satellite and drone-satellite pairs) with multi-modal prompts (points, boxes, masks) and camera poses. They propose GAGeo, a single-stage framework built upon the permutation-equivariant 3D foundation model π³, which integrates visual features, referring prompts, and learnable task tokens to jointly predict bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and camera poses. A contrastive loss using satellite view as a universal anchor enables zero-shot ground-to-drone localization.

Result: The proposed approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods and exhibits exceptional generalization ability in unseen scenes and novel cross-view setups.

Conclusion: The combination of a large-scale dataset with geometric metadata and a geometry-aware single-stage framework leveraging 3D priors effectively addresses CVOGL challenges, enabling superior performance and zero-shot cross-view localization capabilities.

Abstract: Cross-view object geo-localization (CVOGL) aims to locate a target object from a query view (e.g., ground or drone) within a geo-tagged reference image (e.g., satellite). Existing approaches heavily rely on 2D appearance matching and are constrained by limited datasets lacking geometric metadata, diverse prompts, and standard field-of-view imagery. To address these intertwined challenges, we first introduce \dataset, a large-scale, high-fidelity building dataset comprising over 220,000 ground-satellite and drone-satellite pairs. It provides multi-modal prompts (points, boxes, masks) and camera poses to enable flexible target referring and explicit spatial modeling. Furthermore, we propose a novel single-stage Geometry-Aware Geo-localization framework (GAGeo), built upon the permutation-equivariant 3D foundation model $π^3$. By seamlessly integrating visual features, referring prompts, and learnable task tokens, our model adapts the inherited 3D prior to jointly predict bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and camera poses in a single forward pass. Additionally, we introduce a contrastive loss that utilizes the satellite view as a universal anchor, implicitly aligning ground and drone representations to enable zero-shot ground-to-drone localization without requiring triplet training data. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, exhibiting exceptional generalization ability in unseen scenes and novel cross-view setups.

</details>


### [255] [APRIL-MedSeg: A Modular Medical Image Segmentation Toolbox Embracing Modern Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30577)
*Juntao Jiang,Jinsheng Bai,Linxuan Fan,Yali Bi,Jiangning Zhang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: APRIL-MedSeg is a YAML-driven modular framework for 2D medical image segmentation that provides a unified ecosystem decomposing networks into reusable components, supports advanced paradigms (semi-supervised learning, domain adaptation, knowledge distillation, weakly supervised learning, text-guided segmentation, foundation models), uses a registry-based configuration system with inheritance for flexible experiment management, and offers unified interfaces for datasets, augmentation, deployment, and ensembling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of a unified, extensible, and reproducible ecosystem for medical image segmentation that bridges algorithmic innovation with practical deployment.

Method: A YAML-driven modular framework with a registry-based configuration system supporting inheritance, decomposing networks into reusable components, and integrating multiple advanced learning paradigms through a unified interface.

Result: A general-purpose research and development platform enabling seamless switching across models, datasets, and training strategies with support for semi-supervised learning, domain adaptation, knowledge distillation, weakly supervised learning, text-guided segmentation, and foundation models.

Conclusion: APRIL-MedSeg serves as a structured ecosystem for systematically organizing and reproducing advances in medical image segmentation, bridging algorithmic innovation and practical deployment.

Abstract: We present APRIL-MedSeg, a YAML-driven modular framework for 2D medical image segmentation. It provides a unified and extensible ecosystem that decomposes segmentation networks into reusable components. Also, the framework integrates a broad spectrum of advanced paradigms, including semi-supervised learning, domain adaptation, knowledge distillation, weakly supervised learning, and text-guided segmentation as well as foundation model support. A registry-based configuration system with inheritance enables flexible and reproducible experiment management, supporting seamless switching across models, datasets, and training strategies. In addition, the framework provides a unified interface for medical datasets, augmentation pipelines, deployment utilities and model ensembling. Overall, APRIL-MedSeg is designed as a general-purpose research and development platform that bridges algorithmic innovation and practical deployment, while also serving as a structured ecosystem for systematically organizing and reproducing advances in medical image segmentation. The code is available at https://github.com/juntaoJianggavin/APRIL-MedSeg under an Apache 2.0 license.

</details>


### [256] [Learning from Reliable Latent Prompts for Visual Recognition with Missing Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30597)
*Taixi Chen,Nancy Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes "Learning from Reliable Latent Prompts," a novel paradigm that uses input-agnostic learnable latent prompts (instead of instance-level dynamic prompts) as stable anchors to handle missing-modality inputs in large-scale multimodal models. This approach avoids reliability issues caused by corrupted instance-level features at high missing rates, achieving state-of-the-art performance even under extreme missing rates (e.g., 90%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LMMs degrade sharply under missing-modality inputs. Prior prompt-learning methods generate dynamic prompts from instance-level features, but these become unreliable as missing rates increase, leading to unstable prompt learning and limited performance.

Method: The authors propose learning from input-agnostic latent prompts that serve as stable latent anchors, decoupled from corrupted inputs. These latent prompts encapsulate modality-intrinsic priors and provide robust guidance and cross-modal knowledge compensation regardless of missing rate.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across three benchmark datasets under a wide range of missing-modality scenarios, including extreme missing rates (e.g., 90%).

Conclusion: The "learn-from-latent-prompts" paradigm offers a robust and effective solution to the missing-modality problem, outperforming prior input-conditioned dynamic prompt methods.

Abstract: Large-scale multimodal models (LMMs) have achieved superior performance in visual recognition by synergizing information across diverse, massive-scale paired modalities. In real-world scenarios, however, missing-modality inputs are ubiquitous, causing models optimized for modality-complete data to exhibit precipitous performance degradation. Existing research has introduced prompt learning to mitigate this issue, typically by generating dynamic prompts from instance-level features, regardless of whether the input modalities are complete or partially absent. However, such input-conditioned strategies are hindered by the escalating unreliability of instance-level features; as higher missing rates increase the proportion of incomplete modalities, the resulting instability in prompt learning limits the model's performance. To address this limitation, we hypothesize that learnable latent prompts themselves encapsulate stable, modality-intrinsic priors that are decoupled from corrupted inputs. Consequently, we propose a novel paradigm: Learning from Reliable Latent Prompts. Unlike prior methods, we model input-agnostic learnable prompts as stable latent anchors that enable robust guidance and effective cross-modal knowledge compensation, even under extreme missing rates (e.g., 90%). Empirical results across three benchmark datasets demonstrate that our "learn-from-latent-prompts" approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of missing-modality scenarios. Extensive experiments further confirm the effectiveness of this paradigm in providing a robust solution to the missing-modality problem.

</details>


### [257] [Towards in-the-wild Egocentric 3D Hand-Object Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30598)
*Siddhant Bansal,Zhifan Zhu,Shashank Tripathi,Jiahe Zhao,Michael J. Black,Dima Damen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces EPIC-Contact (an in-the-wild egocentric dataset with dense 3D hand-object contact annotations) and HOPformer (an end-to-end transformer that jointly predicts bi-manual hand and object pose). HOPformer achieves 82.4% success rate on ARCTIC (+6.2 pts over SOTA) and nearly doubles success rate on EPIC-Contact while reducing contact deviation by 75%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating accurate 3D hand-object pose from in-the-wild egocentric RGB remains challenging due to severe occlusions and ambiguous contact. Existing learning-based methods struggle to generalise to in-the-wild scenes and are limited by scarcity of supervision.

Method: Two contributions: (1) EPIC-Contact dataset — 2.3K clips (62.3K frames) with dense, bijective 3D hand-object contact correspondences and posed meshes. (2) HOPformer — an end-to-end transformer that jointly predicts bi-manual hand and object pose in a single forward pass, using a cross-attention decoder that conditions object features on hand priors.

Result: HOPformer reaches 82.4% success rate on ARCTIC (+6.2 pts over current SOTA). On EPIC-Contact, it nearly doubles the success rate while reducing contact deviation by 75%.

Conclusion: The EPIC-Contact dataset and HOPformer method significantly advance in-the-wild egocentric hand-object pose estimation, with strong quantitative gains over prior art. Code, checkpoints, and dataset are publicly released.

Abstract: Estimating accurate 3D hand-object pose from in-the-wild egocentric RGB remains challenging due to severe occlusions and ambiguous contact. Existing learning-based methods often struggle to generalise to in-the-wild scenes and are limited by the scarcity of supervision. We address these issues with two contributions. First, we introduce EPIC-Contact, an in-the-wild egocentric dataset of 2.3K clips (62.3K frames) with dense, bijective 3D hand-object contact correspondences and posed meshes. Second, we propose HOPformer, an end-to-end transformer that jointly predicts bi-manual hand and object pose in a single forward pass. A cross-attention decoder conditions object features on hand priors, producing robust pose estimation. We test HOPformer on the in-lab 3D dataset, ARCTIC, as well as our newly introduced EPIC-Contact dataset. HOPformer reaches 82.4% success rate on ARCTIC (+6.2 pts over current SOTA).
