On EPIC-Contact, it nearly doubles the success rate while reducing contact deviation by 75%. EPIC-Contact, HOPformer code and checkpoints are released: https://sid2697.github.io/epic-contact.

</details>


### [258] [Goku: A Million-Scale Universal Dataset and Benchmark for Instruction-Based Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30599)
*Sen Liang,Cong Wang,Zhentao Yu,Fengbin Guan,Zhengguang Zhou,Teng Hu,Youliang Zhang,Yuan Zhou,Xin Li,Qinglin Lu,Zhibo Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Goku introduces a 2M-pair dataset for multi-task video editing (appearance + structural), with a decomposition-based synthesis pipeline, a dual-branch MLLM-driven model (Goku-Edit), and a 1,000-case benchmark (Goku-Bench) with 7 new metrics, achieving +8% improvement in instruction following.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video editing datasets are limited to single-task appearance editing, insufficient for complex real-world creative demands that require multi-task and structural manipulations like precise motion control.

Method: A data synthesis pipeline that decomposes complex edits into controllable sub-problems with progressive filtering, plus Goku-Edit: an MLLM-based text encoder with a decoupled dual-branch design (mask branch for structural control, main branch for appearance rendering).

Result: Goku-Edit achieves up to +8% improvement over other open-source models in instruction following on the proposed Goku-Bench.

Conclusion: The Goku dataset, pipeline, model, and benchmark together advance video editing by enabling both appearance and structural edits, validated by human-verified test cases and novel metrics.

Abstract: Existing instruction-based video editing datasets commonly focus on single-task appearance editing, failing to meet the complex creative demands of real-world scenarios. To bridge this gap, we present Goku, a large-scale dataset featuring 2 million high-quality, instruction-aligned video editing pairs, which is the first to extend task boundaries from basic appearance editing to multi-task and structural manipulations(e.g., precise control of subject movement). To tackle the data synthesis challenges inherent in these complex tasks, we design an efficient data synthesis pipeline that decomposes complex edits into controllable sub-problems and introduce a progressive filtering system for data reliability throughout the whole process. Furthermore, we explore the optimal network structures on Goku, and propose Goku-Edit. To deeply comprehend complex editing instructions, Goku-Edit leverages an MLLM as its text encoder and adopts a decoupled dual-branch design: a dedicated mask branch handles structural control, freeing the main branch for appearance rendering. A comprehensive video editing benchmark, Goku-Bench, is also proposed with 1,000 human-verified test cases and 7 novel editing-specific metrics. Evaluated on Goku-Bench, Goku-Edit obtains up to +8% improvement on other open-source models in terms of instruction following.

</details>


### [259] [UnfoldArt: Zero-Shot Recovery of Full Articulated 3D Objects from Text or Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30608)
*Mohamed el amine boudjoghra,Ivan Laptev,Angela Dai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First debate-driven agentic approach for articulated 3D object reconstruction from text/image, using multi-agent reasoning (high-level semantic agents + low-level geometric agents) with a two-round structured debate and video generative prior to jointly infer articulation and reconstruct complete geometry including occluded interiors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Articulated 3D objects are crucial for embodied AI and VR, but reconstructing their structure and motion from sparse observations is challenging due to lack of supervised data and priors needed for articulation, hidden geometry, and internal structure recovery.

Method: A debate-driven agentic approach with: (1) high-level agents reasoning about semantics/motion using vision-language and video models, (2) low-level agents estimating articulation parameters and interaction points, (3) a two-round structured debate leveraging global-local disagreement and then grounding agents in freely generated video, and (4) a video generative prior conditioned on agreed articulation to drive each part through its motion to expose occluded geometry.

Result: Jointly infers articulation and reconstructs complete 3D articulated objects with high-fidelity geometry, internal structure, and motion-consistent states beyond directly observed surfaces.

Conclusion: Combining agentic reasoning with a video generative prior enables reliable recovery of articulation and occluded geometry from sparse inputs, outperforming prior constrained approaches.

Abstract: Articulated 3D objects are essential for interactive environments in embodied AI, robotics, and virtual reality, but reconstructing their structure and motion from sparse observations remains challenging. Existing approaches remain largely constrained by lack of supervised data or lack the priors needed to reliably recover articulation, hidden geometry, and internal object structure. We present the first debate-driven agentic approach to articulated 3D object reconstruction from text or image inputs that both grounds articulation reasoning in concrete motion and exposes the occluded geometry revealed under articulation. High-level agents reason about object semantics and motion using knowledge from vision-language and video models, while low-level agents estimate articulation parameters and interaction points; together, they engage in a two-round structured debate that first exploits global--local disagreement and then grounds the agents in freely generated video. The same video prior, conditioned on the agreed articulation, then drives each part through its motion to expose occluded interiors and geometry that cannot be inferred from a single static view. By combining agentic reasoning with a video generative prior, our approach jointly infers articulation and reconstructs complete 3D articulated objects, producing high-fidelity geometry, internal structure, and motion-consistent states beyond directly observed surfaces.

</details>


### [260] [Reweighting Framewise Attention in Video Transformers for Facial Expression Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30611)
*Seongro Yoon,Donghyeon Cho,Jinsun Park,François Brémond*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MiRA is a parameter-free plug-in framework that redistributes attention in ViTs toward subtle facial dynamics by leveraging frame-marginal statistics from self-attention maps, achieving consistent FER gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViT-based video models pre-trained via self-supervision tend to emphasize dominant global motions and coarse temporal dynamics, limiting sensitivity to fine-grained facial variations in videos.

Method: MiRA is a plug-in frame-marginal attention redistribution framework for ViT backbones. It derives frame-level confidence and intra-frame concentration from self-attention maps to estimate marginal importance and redistribute attention toward subtle facial dynamics. Two modes are introduced: an exact post-softmax redistribution mode and a flashLite mode (pre-softmax approximation integrated into FlashAttention kernels) for efficiency.

Result: Consistent improvements over strong ViT baselines on challenging Facial Expression Recognition benchmarks.

Conclusion: MiRA consistently improves FER performance over strong ViT baselines on challenging benchmarks.

Abstract: Understanding facial expressions in videos requires modeling subtle and localized facial dynamics under unconstrained conditions. Although recent Vision Transformer~(ViT)-based video models have shown strong performance through large-scale self-supervised pretraining, their attention mechanisms often emphasize dominant global motions and coarse temporal dynamics, limiting sensitivity to fine-grained facial variations. To address this limitation, we propose MiRA (Marginal-induced Attention Redistribution), a plug-in frame-marginal attention redistribution framework for ViT backbones that enhances spatio-temporal selectivity toward subtle facial dynamics without introducing additional trainable parameters. MiRA derives frame-level confidence and intra-frame concentration statistics from self-attention maps to estimate frame-wise marginal importance and redistribute attention toward spatiotemporally localized facial cues. We first introduce a principled \textit{exact mode} based on post-softmax attention redistribution. To further improve efficiency, we propose \textit{flashLite mode}, a lightweight pre-softmax approximation that integrates frame-marginal redistribution into FlashAttention kernels while preserving the effectiveness of the exact formulation. Experimental results on challenging Facial Expression Recognition~(FER) benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over strong ViT baselines.

</details>


### [261] [Open-Vocabulary and Referring Segmentation for 3D Gaussians Using 2D Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30638)
*Jameel Hassan,Yasiru Ranasinghe,Vishal Patel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GaussDet extends 3D Gaussian Splatting with open-vocabulary understanding by leveraging discrete 2D detectors with referring expression capabilities, learning instance features for individual Gaussians, and aggregating semantic votes from multi-view detections to achieve robust 3D instance segmentation and referential grounding without dense CLIP features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for language-driven 3D scene understanding via 3DGS suffer from: (1) instance grouping mechanisms that require predefined instance counts or produce noisy bottom-up groupings; (2) reliance on dense CLIP features that restricts semantic understanding to simple noun phrases, preventing complex spatial reasoning and referential expression grounding.

Method: GaussDet learns instance features for individual Gaussians to decompose scenes into 3D instance groups. It renders these groups and aggregates semantic votes from multi-view 2D detections (with referring expression capabilities) to generate a View-Aggregated Semantic Label Distribution (VASD) for each 3D instance, acting as a regularizer against spurious labels.

Result: Consistent improvements over existing methods on open-vocabulary segmentation (LeRF-OVS, ScanNet) and referring expression grounding (Ref-LeRF), with a notable 16.7% mIoU improvement in referential grounding under a strict zero-shot setting.

Conclusion: GaussDet circumvents dense CLIP features, enables zero-shot extension from simple language queries to complex referential grounding, and achieves state-of-the-art results through view-aggregated semantic voting.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged at the forefront of 3D scene reconstruction. Extending 3DGS with language-driven, open-vocabulary understanding has gained significant attention for real-world applications such as embodied AI. Recent methods achieve this by learning an instance feature attribute and assigning semantics by distilling high-dimensional Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) features directly into the scene representation. However, the instance grouping mechanisms of these methods either require a predefined number of instances or suffer from noise in their bottom-up grouping strategies. Furthermore, the reliance on CLIP restricts semantic understanding to simple noun phrases, preventing complex spatial reasoning and referential expression grounding. We present GaussDet, a method that circumvents the need for dense CLIP features by leveraging discrete, open-vocabulary 2D object detectors with referring expression capabilities. We learn instance features for individual Gaussians to decompose the scene into 3D instance groups. By rendering these groups and aggregating semantic votes from multi-view 2D detections, we generate a robust View-Aggregated Semantic Label Distribution (VASD) for each 3D instance. This view-aggregation strategy acts as a strong regularizer, attenuating spurious labels caused by low-quality instance grouping. Our approach enables a straightforward, zero-shot extension from simple language queries to complex referential grounding. Extensive evaluations across two key tasks -- open-vocabulary segmentation (LeRF-OVS, ScanNet) and referring expression grounding (Ref-LeRF) -- demonstrate that GaussDet achieves consistent improvements over existing methods. Most notably, we achieve a substantial 16.7% mIoU improvement in referential grounding within a strict zero-shot setting.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [262] [Reinforcement Learning for Software Vulnerability Analysis: A Systematic Review with Emphasis on C/C++ Source Code and Static Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28403)
*Bruno Caro-Vásquez,Carola Figueroa-Flores,Gastón Marquez*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This systematic review (following PRISMA 2020) of 21 studies (2015–2026) examines RL techniques for C/C++ software vulnerability analysis via static analysis. Most research focuses on fuzzing (15 studies), with only 3 on direct vulnerability detection and 1 on statement-level localization. Structural code representations like CFGs and ASTs are rarely used as RL agent states, and benchmarks lack comparability. The authors identify a key gap: no RL agents using source-code CFGs as states to detect and localize vulnerable nodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vulnerability detection in C/C++ is challenging due to code complexity, manual memory management, and limitations of traditional static analysis. RL has emerged as promising but its application to vulnerability analysis lacks systematic organization, motivating this review to map the landscape and identify gaps.

Method: The authors conducted a systematic literature review following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, identifying 21 primary studies from 2015–2026 from major scientific databases and complementary searches. They analyzed tasks, RL algorithms, state-action-reward-environment formulations, code representations, datasets, and evaluation metrics.

Result: 15 studies focus on fuzzing and guided exploration; only 3 on direct vulnerability detection; just 1 on statement-level localization. Statically extracted structural representations (CFGs, ASTs) are rarely used as agent states. Benchmarks lack comparability across studies.

Conclusion: There is a significant research gap: no existing RL agents leverage source-code CFGs as states for detecting and localizing vulnerable nodes. The authors propose a task- and formulation-oriented taxonomy to guide future work.

Abstract: Vulnerability detection in C/C++ software remains a major security challenge due to code complexity, manual memory management, and the limitations of traditional static analysis. Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a promising approach, particularly for fuzzing, test generation, program exploration, and, more recently, vulnerability detection and localization. Following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, this work reviews RL techniques for software vulnerability analysis, focusing on C/C++ source code and static analysis. We identified 21 primary studies published between 2015 and 2026 from major scientific databases and complementary searches. We analyze the addressed tasks, algorithms, state-action-reward-environment formulations, code representations, datasets, and evaluation metrics. Results show that 15 studies focus on fuzzing and guided exploration, only 3 on direct vulnerability detection, and just 1 on statement-level localization. Moreover, statically extracted structural representations such as Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) and Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) are rarely used as agent states, and benchmarks lack comparability. We propose a task- and formulation-oriented taxonomy and identify a key research gap: the absence of RL agents that use source-code CFGs as states to detect and localize vulnerable nodes.

</details>


### [263] [Building to the Test: Coding Agents Deliver What You Check, Not What You Requested](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28430)
*Yanuo Ma,Ben Kereopa-Yorke,Ben Schultz*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: LLM agents achieve near-perfect benchmark scores but fail to deliver functional software, revealing a "building to the test" problem where agents optimize for test signals rather than genuine task completion, highlighting the need for research into "validation self-awareness."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for LLM task completion suffer from construct-validity problems—passing scores may not indicate whether the requested task was actually delivered. The study aims to investigate this gap between benchmark performance and actual task completion.

Method: Controlled code-as-spec setup with two production Copilot CLI agents (claude-opus-4.7, gpt-5.5) re-implementing a React Fluent-UI data table in Angular as a reusable library under a hidden 222-test Playwright oracle across 18 runs and three oracle-availability conditions. Alongside scoring, a mechanical library audit and no-op ablation were conducted.

Result: Without the oracle, the library is unfinished (reflected in scores). With the oracle in the loop, scores reach near-perfect, but the library is actually dead or absent—agents simply "build to the test" by directly demoing the tested behavior rather than delivering a functional library.

Conclusion: LLM agents lack "validation self-awareness"—they do not validate what they ship as a user would. This disposition of building to the test rather than genuine task completion merits further research across other agents, signals, and model families.

Abstract: Benchmarks are widely used to evaluate task completion by Large Language Models (LLMs), but this approach has accumulated construction-validity problems, and a passing score may not show whether the requested task was delivered. We study both problems. In a controlled code-as-spec setup, two production Copilot CLI agents (claude-opus-4.7, gpt-5.5) re-implement a React Fluent-UI data table in Angular as a reusable library under a hidden 222-test Playwright oracle across 18 runs and three oracle-availability conditions. Alongside the score, we run a mechanical library audit and check each verdict with a no-op ablation. Without the oracle, the library is present but unfinished, revealed by scores. With the oracle in the loop, the score reaches near-perfect, but from a demo holding the tested behavior directly, the library left dead or absent. We call this building to the test; the broader disposition behind both we call validation self-awareness. The agent does not, on its own, validate what it ships as a user would. Prevalence remains an open question across other agents, signals, and model families. Beyond benchmark scores, dispositions like validation self-awareness merit research attention.

</details>


### [264] [SWE-MeM: Learning Adaptive Memory Management for Long-Horizon Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28434)
*Shuzheng Gao,Wenhao Zeng,Zhaojian Yu,Jianqiao Wangni,Chaozheng Wang,Kai Cai,Shilin He,Michael R. Lyu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: SWE-MeM is a training framework that enables software engineering agents to proactively manage memory by deciding when, what, and how to compress their interaction history, achieving 43.4% and 60.2% resolve rates on SWE-Bench Verified with 4B and 30B models while outperforming existing memory management methods in both performance and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory management methods for long-horizon software engineering agents rely on static compression workflows and rigid constraints on timing/granularity, and fail to jointly optimize memory management and issue resolution performance under limited context budgets.

Method: SWE-MeM introduces a flexible memory tool allowing agents to decide when, what, and how to compress based on trajectory state, task progress, and remaining context budget. Agents are trained with synthesized proactive memory-management trajectories and Memory-aware GRPO, which jointly optimizes memory management and issue resolution via memory-aware trajectory splitting and step-level credit assignment.

Result: On SWE-Bench Verified, SWE-MeM achieves 43.4% resolve rate with a 4B model and 60.2% resolve rate with a 30B model, outperforming existing memory management baselines.

Conclusion: SWE-MeM demonstrates that proactive and on-demand memory management, jointly optimized with issue resolution, significantly improves both performance and efficiency for long-horizon software engineering agents.

Abstract: Long-horizon software engineering agents often need to manage lengthy and noisy interaction histories under limited context budgets. Existing memory management methods typically rely on static compression workflows or impose rigid constraints on compression timing and granularity. Moreover, these approaches fail to jointly optimize memory management and issue resolution capabilities to improve performance while reducing token usage. We present SWE-MeM, a training framework for proactive and on-demand memory management in software engineering agents. SWE-MeM provides a flexible memory tool that lets agents decide when, what, and how to compress based on trajectory state, task progress, and remaining context budget. We train agents with synthesized proactive memory-management trajectories and Memory-aware GRPO, which jointly optimizes memory management and issue resolution through memory-aware trajectory splitting and step-level credit assignment. On SWE-Bench Verified, SWE-MeM achieves 43.4% and 60.2% resolve rate with 4B and 30B models, respectively, outperforming existing memory management baselines in both performance and efficiency.

</details>


### [265] [Dockerless: Environment-Free Program Verifier for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28436)
*Wenhao Zeng,Yuling Shi,Xiaodong Gu,Chao Hu,Chaofan Wang,Yuhao Cui,Hongting Zhou,Mengnan Qi,Jianqiao Wangni,Zhaojian Yu,Shuzheng Gao,Kai Cai,Shilin He*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Dockerless is an environment-free agentic patch verifier that evaluates code patches without execution, outperforming existing verifiers by 14.3 AUC points and enabling a fully environment-free post-training pipeline that achieves 62.0%, 50.0%, and 35.2% resolve rates on SWE-bench benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard execution-based verification requires running unit tests inside per-repository environments (e.g., Docker images), which incurs substantial environment setup costs. The goal is to eliminate this overhead while maintaining verification quality.

Method: Dockerless is an environment-free agentic patch verifier that judges patch correctness by gathering evidence through agentic repository exploration, rather than executing code or simply matching candidate patches to references.

Result: Dockerless outperforms the strongest open-source verifier by 14.3 AUC points. Using Dockerless for both SFT trajectory filtering and RL reward enables a fully environment-free pipeline, achieving 62.0%, 50.0%, and 35.2% on SWE-bench Verified, Multilingual, and Pro, surpassing the Qwen3.5-9B baseline by 2.4, 8.7, and 2.9 points.

Conclusion: Dockerless demonstrates that environment-free verification can match environment-based post-training performance, significantly reducing infrastructure costs for training coding agents.

Abstract: Program verifiers play a central role in training coding agents, including selecting trajectories for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and providing rewards for reinforcement learning (RL). Standard execution-based verification requires running unit tests inside per-repository environments such as Docker images, incurring substantial environment setup costs. We propose Dockerless, an environment-free agentic patch verifier that evaluates generated code patches without executing them. Rather than simply matching candidate patches to references, Dockerless judges patch correctness using evidence gathered through agentic repository exploration. On a verifier evaluation benchmark, Dockerless outperforms the strongest open-source verifier by 14.3 AUC points. Using Dockerless as both the SFT trajectory filter and the RL reward enables a fully environment-free post-training pipeline. The resulting model reaches 62.0%, 50.0%, and 35.2% resolve rate on SWE-bench Verified, Multilingual, and Pro, respectively. It surpasses the Qwen3.5-9B baseline by 2.4, 8.7, and 2.9 points, matching environment-based post-training.

</details>


### [266] [When AI Reviews Its Own Code: Recursive Self-Training Collapse in Code LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28438)
*Xinyuan Song,Zekun Cai,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper studies recursive self-training loops in code LLMs where AI-generated code re-enters training data. It compares no review, Human-gate review (compilation, static checks), and AI-self-gate review (perplexity, self-scoring). Results show no review collapses fastest, Human-gate slows but doesn't stop collapse, and AI-self-gate initially appears effective but degrades into a rubber-stamp regime where acceptance scores rise while correctness falls. The authors provide theoretical analysis showing self-gating degenerates to ungated self-training under self-confirming acceptance, proving stable recursive training requires exogenous verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study the risk of recursive self-training in code LLMs where AI-generated code can enter real repositories and later become training data, creating a self-training loop that may degrade model quality. This is especially concerning as AI coding tools produce code faster than human review can keep up, and code review itself is being automated.

Method: The authors compare three recursive fine-tuning regimes: no review, Human-gate review (model-independent filters like compilation and static quality checks), and AI-self-gate review (using the code LLM's own signals like perplexity and binary self-scoring). They conduct experiments across multiple code LLMs and benchmarks, and provide theoretical analysis including formulation of review as gated distributional reweighting, proving that AI self-gating degenerates to ungated self-training under a self-confirming acceptance condition, and spectral analysis of representation-level covariance concentration under recursive retraining.

Result: No review collapses fastest, Human-gate filters slow but do not stop collapse, and AI-self-gate filters can look strong early but later lose their filtering effect. In the clearest case, the binary self-gate enters a rubber-stamp regime where acceptance scores rise while benchmark correctness falls.

Conclusion: Stable recursive code LLM training requires exogenous verification rather than model-coupled self-review, as self-gating degenerates to ungated self-training under a self-confirming acceptance condition.

Abstract: Recursive self-training can degrade neural generative models when generated data is reused without fresh human data or external quality control. We study this risk in code LLMs, where AI-generated code can enter real repositories, later become training data, and create a repository-scale self-training loop. While software development traditionally interrupts this loop through pull-request review, tests, compilation, and human approval, AI coding tools now produce code faster than humans can review it, and code review itself is increasingly automated by AI systems. We therefore compare three recursive fine-tuning regimes: no review, Human-gate review using model-independent filters such as compilation and static quality checks, and AI-self-gate review using the code LLM's own signals such as perplexity and binary self-scoring. Across multiple code LLMs and benchmarks, no review collapses fastest, Human-gate filters slow but do not stop collapse, and AI-self-gate filters can look strong early but later lose their filtering effect. In the clearest case, the binary self-gate enters a rubber-stamp regime where acceptance scores rise while benchmark correctness falls. We explain this behavior by formulating review as gated distributional reweighting, proving that AI self-gating degenerates to ungated self-training under a self-confirming acceptance condition, and giving a spectral analysis of representation-level covariance concentration under recursive retraining. These results suggest that stable recursive code LLM training requires exogenous verification rather than model-coupled self-review.

</details>


### [267] [TUA-Bench: A Benchmark for General-Purpose Terminal-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28480)
*Shoufa Chen,Luyuan Wang,Xuan Yang,Zhiheng Liu,Yuren Cong,Yuanfeng Ji,Feiyan Zhou,Xiaohui Zhang,Fanny Yang,Belinda Zeng*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: TUA-Bench is a benchmark with 120 real-world terminal tasks across 5 families (document editing, email, web info-seeking, scientific/engineering workflows) that evaluates general-purpose terminal-use agents beyond coding. The best agent (Claude Code + Opus 4.8) achieves 65.8% overall, revealing substantial gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks either target GUIs (general computer-use) or narrow programming-centric shell tasks, leaving a gap for evaluating general-purpose terminal computer-use agents (TUAs) on diverse real-world digital activities.

Method: 120 manually designed tasks across 5 families (document editing, email management, live-web info seeking, scientific/engineering workflows with PhD experts), run in real terminals with deterministic setup scripts and execution-based scoring.

Result: Strongest frontier agent (Claude Code with Claude Opus 4.8 max reasoning effort) achieves 65.8% overall performance, with substantial performance gaps across tracks.

Conclusion: TUA-Bench aims to accelerate transition from narrow task-specific assistants to general-purpose terminal agents capable of reliable operation across diverse digital environments.

Abstract: As large language models and harness frameworks continue to advance, agents operating in terminals are increasingly capable of performing a broader range of general computer-use tasks beyond coding. However, existing benchmarks do not adequately evaluate general-purpose terminal computer-use agents (TUAs): general computer-use benchmarks primarily target graphical user interfaces (GUIs), whereas terminal-based benchmarks largely emphasize technical and programming-centric workflows historically native to the shell. We introduce TUA-Bench, a general-purpose benchmark for terminal-use agents. TUA-Bench includes 120 real-world tasks across five task families, covering routine digital activities-including document editing, email management, and live-web information seeking-as well as scientific and engineering workflows co-designed with PhD-level domain experts that require specialized software. This breadth distinguishes TUA-Bench from prior shell-focused or domain-specific benchmarks. Each task is manually designed, runs in a real terminal with a deterministic setup script, and is evaluated by an execution-based scoring protocol. We find that the strongest frontier agent, Claude Code with Claude Opus 4.8 max reasoning effort, achieves 65.8% overall performance, with substantial gaps across both tracks. By providing a broad and realistic evaluation of terminal-use capabilities, TUA-Bench aims to accelerate the transition from narrow, task-specific assistants to general-purpose agents capable of operating reliably across diverse digital environments.

</details>


### [268] [Evaluating LLMs on Java Code Snippet Adaptation Using a Mutation-Injection Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28618)
*Ali Aman,Muhammad Asaduzzaman,Shaowei Wang,Chanchal K. Roy*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper proposes a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on instruction-free code snippet adaptation by injecting controlled mutations from a taxonomy of adaptation operators into Java code fragments from open-source repositories, then measuring whether LLMs can correctly adapt the mutated code back to its original form without explicit edit guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM code adaptation rely on explicit step-by-step instructions, cover only narrow change types (e.g., variable wiring), or operate at function-level granularity. It remains unknown how well LLMs can adapt code fragments without explicit edit guidance when required changes are varied and controlled.

Method: Construct a dataset of Java code fragments from open-source repositories with strong test coverage. Apply a taxonomy of adaptation operators (derived from empirical findings on developer adaptation practices) via a mutation-injection framework. Work at code fragment level, controlling injected changes so the unmutated fragment serves as a reference. Evaluate LLMs on instruction-free adaptation tasks across three context granularity levels. Measure correctness via test-suite re-insertion and mutation-level inspection.

Result: The paper is a research proposal (no results reported yet). The study will investigate three research questions: which adaptation types are hardest (RQ1), how performance scales with adaptation complexity (RQ2), and how much surrounding context the model needs (RQ3).

Conclusion: This approach enables controlled, granular evaluation of LLM adaptation capabilities without explicit edit guidance, addressing gaps in existing benchmarks by covering varied adaptation types at the code fragment level with precise ground truth.

Abstract: Background: Developers frequently reuse code by copying fragments and adapting them to fit new contexts. Existing benchmarks for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code adaptation either rely on explicit step-by-step instructions, cover only narrow change types such as variable wiring, or operate exclusively at function-level granularity. It remains unknown how well LLMs can adapt code fragments without explicit edit guidance when the required changes are varied and controlled.
  Objective: We investigate instruction-free code snippet adaptation in which an LLM must adapt a code fragment to fit its target context without any explicit edit guidance. We study three dimensions: which adaptation types are hardest (RQ1), how performance scales with adaptation complexity (RQ2), and how much surrounding context the model needs (RQ3).
  Method: We will construct a dataset of Java code fragments from open-source repositories with strong test coverage and apply a taxonomy of adaptation operators, derived from empirical findings on how developers adapt copied code, using a mutation-injection framework. Working at the code fragment level and controlling the injected changes lets us know exactly what adaptations the model must perform. The unmutated fragment serves as a plausible reference for the changes the model needs to make. LLMs will be evaluated on instruction-free adaptation tasks across three context granularity levels. Correctness will be measured primarily via test-suite re-insertion, complemented by mutation-level inspection.

</details>


### [269] [From Determinism to Delegation: AI-Native Software Engineering and the Evolution of the Agentic Engineer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28791)
*Mamdouh Alenezi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper argues that AI-Native Software Engineering represents a paradigm shift where the primary artifact changes from deterministic code to probabilistic agentic systems, creating a new professional archetype—the Agentic Engineer—whose work shifts from writing functions to supervising autonomous workflows, from binary correctness to statistical evaluation, and from code authorship to outcome ownership.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Software engineering is undergoing a fundamental transformation driven by LLMs enabling sustained, multi-step, tool-mediated execution, shifting engineering value from writing deterministic code to supervising probabilistic autonomous behavior.

Method: The paper characterizes the transition through three key changes: unit of work (functions → supervised agent workflows), correctness (binary assertions → statistical evaluation under uncertainty), and accountability (code authorship → outcome ownership). It draws on post-2022 research, compares traditional and agentic engineering roles, and examines human-AI collaboration within socio-technical frameworks.

Result: Mixed empirical evidence shows some studies report productivity gains while others reveal slowdowns among experienced developers, indicating disciplined oversight—not automation—as the critical competency. The paper identifies required skills and risks including indirect prompt injection using established governance frameworks.

Conclusion: The future is one of symbiosis rather than substitution: agentic engineering builds upon and depends on classical software engineering principles, with the Agentic Engineer as a new professional archetype whose primary artifact is the agentic system rather than the program.

Abstract: Software engineering is experiencing its most significant transformation since the emergence of high-level programming languages. As large language models (LLMs) increasingly enable sustained, multi-step, tool-mediated execution, engineering value is shifting from writing deterministic code to supervising probabilistic and autonomous behavior. This paper argues that AI-Native Software Engineering is a paradigm shift rather than a mere tooling advance, creating a new professional archetype: the Agentic Engineer, whose primary artifact is the agentic system rather than the program.
  We characterize this transition through three changes: (i) the unit of work shifts from functions to supervised agent workflows, (ii) correctness shifts from binary assertions to statistical evaluation under uncertainty, and (iii) accountability shifts from code authorship to outcome ownership. Drawing on post-2022 research, we compare traditional and agentic engineering roles and define core mechanisms of autonomous agents, including reasoning-acting loops, context engineering, tool use, memory, behavioral drift, and compositional error.
  We place human-AI collaboration within socio-technical frameworks and examine mixed empirical evidence. While some studies report productivity gains, others show slowdowns among experienced developers, highlighting disciplined oversight rather than automation as the critical competency. Using established governance frameworks, we identify required skills and risks, including indirect prompt injection. We conclude that the future is one of symbiosis rather than substitution: agentic engineering builds upon and depends on classical software engineering principles.

</details>


### [270] [Reward-Free Code Alignment from Pretrained or Fine-Tuned LLM: Unpacking the Trade-offs for Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28998)
*Gias Uddin,Sanjeepan Sivapiran*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This empirical study investigates whether LLM alignment techniques (DPO and BoN) improve code generation for both functional correctness and non-functional quality, and whether alignment should start from pretrained or finetuned LLM variants. Results show pretrained-to-aligned yields larger relative gains but lower absolute accuracy than finetuned-to-aligned, which shows smaller improvements or degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM alignment is well-studied for non-coding tasks, but its effectiveness for code generation—especially for both functional requirements (correctness) and non-functional requirements (readability, style, maintainability)—is unknown. Additionally, it is unclear whether alignment should start from a pretrained or finetuned (instruction-tuned) base model.

Method: The authors conduct an empirical study using five SOTA LLMs, two reward-free alignment techniques (DPO and BoN), and preference pairs created via SelfCodeAlign. Each LLM is aligned starting from both pretrained and finetuned variants. Functional requirements are evaluated using HumanEval+, MBPP+, EvalPerf, and EvoEval; non-functional requirements are evaluated using CODAL across five quality dimensions.

Result: Pretrained-to-aligned pathways yield larger relative improvements in the aligned variant over its pretrained baseline, but the pretrained variant itself is generally less accurate than its finetuned counterpart. Finetuned-to-aligned offers smaller performance improvements or even degradation compared to the finetuned variant.

Conclusion: Starting alignment from a pretrained model leads to larger relative gains but lower absolute performance than starting from a finetuned model. Finetuned-to-aligned produces marginal or negative gains, suggesting diminishing returns or potential degradation when applying alignment after instruction tuning for code tasks.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) alignment trains an LLM using preference data to produce outputs that better meet established quality standards. While LLM alignment techniques are studied for non-coding tasks, we know little about their usefulness for coding tasks. It is unclear whether LLM code alignment could support both functional requirements (producing executable, correct code) and non-functional requirements (code readability, style, maintainability). It is also unknown whether alignment for a code LLM should begin with base pretrained version or the finetuned (i.e., instruction-tuned) version of the LLM. In this paper, we offer insights on the above two research questions by conducting an empirical study. We studied five state-of-the-art (SOTA) LLMs using two widely used LLM alignment techniques: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and BoNBoN. For each training record, we created a preference pair as accepted and rejected instances by using the SelfCodeAlign pipeline. DPO and BoNBoN are reward-free models, i.e., they eliminate the need for multiple reward scores for output preferences. We tuned each LLM using the two alignment techniques in two settings: pretrained and finetuned versions of an LLM. We evaluated functional requirements using four SOTA benchmarks (HumanEval+, MBPP+, EvalPerf, EvoEval) and non-functional requirements using the CODAL benchmark, which evaluates code quality across five dimensions derived from software engineering practices. We find that pretrained-to-aligned pathways achieve larger improvements in the aligned variant over its pretrained variant. But the pretrained variant is generally less accurate than its finetuned variant. However, finetuned- to-aligned offers smaller performance improvements or, in some cases, degradation in the aligned variant than its finetuned variant.

</details>


### [271] [Automated SysML-Based Verification of Discipline-Specific Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29006)
*Daniel Marley,Siyuan Ji*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper presents a tool-agnostic verification process for SysML models that extends beyond parametric performance properties to verify behavioural and interface requirements (ordering, timing, state-based responses) using SysML test cases, demonstrated end-to-end across two independent toolchains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SysML-based verification approaches suffer from two flaws: (1) tool-specific implementation using proprietary APIs, limiting portability; and (2) focus on performance properties via parametric diagrams, neglecting behavioural and interface properties that also require verification.

Method: A mixed-method approach combining literature research and stakeholder interviews was used to derive validated stakeholder needs, which drove the specification and design of the verification process. The process was informed by common SysML tool capabilities and the UML Testing Profile.

Result: The process was demonstrated end-to-end in two independent SysML tool-chains, evidencing tool-agnosticism. It was shown to verify behavioural and interface requirements (ordering, timing, and state-based responses) using SysML behavioural diagram constructs that parametric approaches alone cannot address.

Conclusion: The proposed process enables automated verification of discipline-specific models from SysML test cases and returns results to the SysML model for traceability, overcoming the portability and coverage limitations of prior work.

Abstract: Current examples of SysML-based verification of discipline-specific models in the literature typically have two flaws. Firstly, they are developed in a tool-specific manner using proprietary APIs, limiting portability. Secondly, they focus on performance properties modelled via parametric diagrams, overlooking behavioural and interface properties that also require verification. This project addresses the problem with a verification process tailored to model-based verification, informed by common SysML tool capabilities and the UML Testing Profile, that enables automated verification of discipline\-/specific models from SysML test cases and returns the results to the SysML model for traceability. A mixed-method approach combining literature research and stakeholder interviews was used to derive validated stakeholder needs, which drove the specification and design of the process. The process was demonstrated end-to-end in two independent SysML tool-chains to evidence tool-agnosticism, and was shown to verify behavioural and interface requirements, including ordering, timing, and state-based responses, using SysML behavioural diagram constructs that parametric approaches alone cannot address.

</details>


### [272] [Diff-Based Code Corruption using LLMs for Large-Scale Bugfix Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29088)
*Balázs Szalontai,Ábel Szauter,Balázs Márton,Péter Verebics,Balázs Pintér,Tibor Gregorics*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: MegaBugFix is a large-scale benchmark of 12,629 buggy Python programs synthesized via LLM-based diffs, designed to better reflect real-world bugfixing. It reveals consistently lower performance across 13 open-weight models compared to existing benchmarks, exposing model failures that smaller or simpler benchmarks miss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bugfixing benchmarks are small, statistically unreliable, contain overly similar buggy programs, and fail to capture a representative range of bug types, distorting evaluation results.

Method: 12,629 buggy Python programs were synthesized from correct programs using an LLM to generate diffs (code changes), avoiding pitfalls of LLM-based mutation such as overly simplistic bugs or failure to modify the input program.

Result: 13 open-weight models evaluated on MegaBugFix showed consistently lower performance than on baseline benchmarks, indicating MegaBugFix presents more challenging bugs and reveals model failures hidden by existing benchmarks.

Conclusion: MegaBugFix offers a more realistic, challenging, and statistically reliable benchmark for evaluating LLM bugfixing capabilities, exposing shortcomings that simpler benchmarks do not capture.

Abstract: There are various benchmarks to evaluate bugfixing capabilities of Large Language Models. However, most widespread benchmarks do not fully reflect real-world bugfixing practices. They are small, weakening statistical reliability, and the buggy programs are often similar to one another, potentially distorting evaluation results. The range of bug types can also be narrow, failing to capture a representative range of bugs. To address these issues, we introduce MegaBugFix, a large-scale bugfixing benchmark containing 12,629 buggy Python programs synthesized from correct ones by a Large Language Model. Bug injections were generated as diffs representing code changes. Through this approach, we were able to avoid common pitfalls of LLM-based mutation techniques like injecting overly simplistic bugs or failing to modify the input program. We evaluated 13 open-weight models on MegaBugFix and baseline benchmarks, finding consistently lower performance on MegaBugFix. This reveals that our benchmark presents more challenging bugs and exposes model failures that may remain hidden when evaluating on existing benchmarks. The benchmark and fine-tuned model used for bug injection are available at hf.co/collections/szalontaib/megabugfix.

</details>


### [273] [OASIF: An Efficient Obfuscation-Aware Self-Improving Framework for LLM-Based Assembly Code Instruction Following and Comprehension](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29155)
*Xinyi Wang,Rongze Chen,Ke Wang,Qiyuan Chen,Yanming Liu,Xiang Li,Chunfu Jia*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: OASIF is a framework for obfuscated assembly comprehension that uses a token-efficient encoder, projector, and three-phase training (feature-space alignment, supervised fine-tuning, and self-evolving RL) to improve LLM performance on commercial VM-based obfuscated code, achieving significant gains on VMISA-Bench and standard BCSD benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs show promise in binary analysis but are brittle under commercial-grade obfuscation, motivating the need for a specialized framework that can handle obfuscated assembly with limited context budgets and minimal manual verification.

Method: OASIF couples a token-efficient assembly encoder with a lightweight projector to expose long obfuscated code to a pretrained code LLM under bounded context, followed by three-phase training: (i) feature-space alignment, (ii) supervised instruction fine-tuning, and (iii) online self-evolving reinforcement learning with hybrid rewards.

Result: On VMISA-Bench, Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct-14B achieves Success Rate gains of +15.9pp (Code Virtualizer), +5.8pp (Themida v3.0.7), and +16.9pp (VMProtect v3.5). OASIF-Bench average improves by +9.8. Stable gains across seven BCSD benchmarks without degrading performance on HumanEval, VulBench, or HumanEval-Decompile.

Conclusion: OASIF effectively enhances open-source LLMs for obfuscated assembly understanding, achieving robust gains across diverse obfuscation techniques while preserving general and domain-relevant capabilities.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown promise in automated binary analysis, yet they remain brittle under commercial-grade obfuscation. We present OASIF, an Obfuscation-Aware Self-evolving Instruction-Following framework for obfuscated assembly comprehension. OASIF couples a token-efficient assembly encoder with a lightweight projector to expose long obfuscated code to a pretrained code LLM under a bounded context budget and follows a three-phase training: (i) feature-space alignment, (ii) supervised instruction fine-tuning, and (iii) online self-evolving reinforcement learning with hybrid rewards, enabling continual adaptation with minimal manual verification. On VMISA-Bench, a challenging out-of-distribution suite featuring three commercial VM-based obfuscators, OASIF consistently improves open-source backbones; Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct-14B attains Success Rate gains of +15.9, +5.8, and +16.9 percentage points (pp) on Code Virtualizer, Themida (v3.0.7), and VMProtect (v3.5), respectively, and improves the OASIF-Bench average by +9.8. OASIF further delivers stable gains across seven standard BCSD benchmarks while preserving general and domain-relevant capabilities on HumanEval, VulBench, and HumanEval-Decompile.

</details>


### [274] [A Multi-Dataset Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Agents in Microservice Failure Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29193)
*Yuanhong Cai,Xiaohui Nie,Kanglin Yin,Changhua Pei,Yongqian Sun,Shenglin Zhang,Haibin Liu,Guiyang Liu,Xidao Wen,Fang Situ,Dan Pei*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces two large-scale, expert-labeled datasets (AIOps2025 and RCA100) for evaluating LLM-based agent failure diagnosis in microservice systems (AgentOps), shifting from outcome-oriented scoring to a reasoning-process evaluation across three dimensions: Localization, Identification, and Reason.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for failure diagnosis in microservice operations are outcome-oriented, scoring only the final answer without assessing the systematic reasoning process of agentic diagnostic systems.

Method: The authors propose a reasoning-process evaluation paradigm with three dimensions: Localization (where the fault occurs), Identification (what type of fault), and Reason (whether reasoning trace is grounded in evidence). They construct two datasets (AIOps2025 and RCA100) with over 500 expert-labeled failure cases across HipsterShop and OpenTelemetry Demo Store, covering resource, network, runtime, middleware/database, and application-logic fault categories with fine-grained causal evidence.

Result: The datasets have been validated through large-scale competitions involving over 6,000 participating teams, making them competition-validated benchmarks.

Conclusion: These datasets serve as expert-labeled, competition-validated benchmarks for evaluating agentic failure diagnosis in real-world microservice environments, addressing the gap in reasoning-process evaluation.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are reshaping microservice operations into AgentOps, where benchmarks are key to evaluating failure diagnosis over multimodal observability data. However, existing benchmarks remain largely outcome-oriented: they score only the final answer and fail to assess the systematic reasoning process in failure diagnosis. We address this gap by introducing two large-scale datasets (AIOps2025 and RCA100) under a reasoning-process evaluation paradigm that assesses agentic diagnostic capability along three dimensions: Localization (where the fault occurs), Identification (what type of fault it is), and Reason (whether the reasoning trace is grounded in relevant evidence). Together, the two datasets comprise over 500 expert-labeled failure cases across two representative microservice systems (HipsterShop and the OpenTelemetry Demo Store). They cover diverse fault scenarios across resource, network, runtime, middleware/database, and application-logic categories and provide fine-grained causal evidence to support agent learning and reasoning-process evaluation. Beyond scale and coverage, the datasets have been carefully labelled by domain experts and validated through large-scale competitions, supporting more than 6,000 participating teams. This makes them not only expert-labeled diagnostic datasets, but also competition-validated benchmarks for evaluating agentic failure diagnosis in real-world microservice environments. Datasets are available at https://www.aiops.cn/gitlab/aiops-live-benchmark/agenticopseval.

</details>


### [275] [On the JI-RADAR: Uncovering Sustainability Tool Support for Requirements Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29439)
*Marco Stadler,Pascal Taurer,Johannes Sametinger,Wesley K. G. Assunção,Michael Riegler,Michael Vierhauser,Iris Groher*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces JI-RADAR, a Jira plugin that integrates sustainability requirements engineering into software development processes, addressing the lack of systematic tools for eliciting, analyzing, and prioritizing sustainability requirements alongside traditional requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulatory initiatives (e.g., UN SDGs, EU CSRD) increasingly require software organizations to integrate sustainability into their operations and reporting, yet RE processes lack systematic methods and tool support for handling sustainability requirements alongside functional and non-functional requirements.

Method: The authors developed JI-RADAR, a ready-to-use plugin for the Atlassian Jira platform that provides practical tools for stakeholders (developers, requirements engineers, project managers, usability engineers) to integrate sustainability into the RE process.

Result: JI-RADAR is a functional plugin extending Jira, designed for direct adoption in industrial practice to support sustainability integration in requirements engineering.

Conclusion: JI-RADAR addresses the gap in tool support for sustainability requirements engineering by providing a practical, industry-ready solution integrated into a widely used platform (Jira), enabling organizations to comply with sustainability regulations.

Abstract: Context: Software-intensive systems are integral to nearly all facets of modern society [1]. Consequently, both their sustainability and their role in facilitating sustainable processes must be established by design [2], [3]. Software sustainability is defined as "the preservation of the long-term and beneficial use of software, and its appropriate evolution, in a context that continuously changes" [2]. RE Problem & Motivation: Regulatory initiatives increasingly require (software) organizations to integrate sustainability into their day-to-day business and operational processes. The United Nations 2030 Agenda formulated 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) [6], while the EU passed the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which requires companies to publish and audit sustainability-related information [7]. Regulations and laws require organizations in the software development sector to disclose both qualitative and quantitative sustainability metrics, among other obligations [1]. Consequently, integrating sustainability reporting processes into the software development life cycle becomes increasingly important. RE processes often lack systematic methods to elicit, analyze, and prioritize sustainability requirements alongside functional and non-functional requirements, and studies indicate that tool support for this integration remains limited [4]. To address this gap, we introduce JI-RADAR, which supports stakeholders involved in system design (e.g., developers, requirements engineers, project managers, and usability engineers) [5] by providing practical tools to integrate sustainability into the RE process. We extend the widely used Atlassian Jira platform [8] by implementing a ready-to-use plugin that can be directly adopted in industrial practice.

</details>


### [276] [SAKE: Software Architectural Knowledge Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29520)
*Tiziano Santilli,Francesco Daghero,Mayhar Tourchi Moghaddam*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: SAKE is a benchmark of 2154 expert-curated multiple-choice questions across 8 architectural categories and 4 context-length levels for evaluating software architectural knowledge in LLMs. Testing 11 models reveals high overall accuracy but significant category-specific competency gaps, especially in areas central to professional practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLMs focus on syntactic or algorithmic tasks and fail to measure ability to reason about software architecture, which involves quality attribute trade-offs, design patterns, and system-level constraints.

Method: Created SAKE benchmark with 2154 expert-curated multiple-choice questions (4 options each) across 8 architectural categories and 4 context-length levels. Evaluated 11 proprietary and open-weight LLMs in zero-shot and five-shot settings.

Result: Overall accuracy is high, but performance varies markedly across categories, revealing competency gaps in areas central to professional architectural practice.

Conclusion: SAKE provides a standardized, reproducible baseline for tracking architectural reasoning in LLMs, with all resources released as open source.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as assistants across the software development lifecycle, yet their ability to reason about software architecture remains largely unmeasured. Architectural decision-making depends on quality attribute trade-offs, design patterns, and system-level constraints, none of which are exercised by benchmarks that target syntactic or algorithmic tasks. We introduce SAKE (Software Architectural Knowledge Evaluation), a standardized and reproducible benchmark for assessing software architectural knowledge in LLMs. SAKE comprises 2154 expert-curated multiple-choice questions, each with four options, stratified across eight architectural categories and four context-length levels. We evaluate 11 proprietary and open-weight models in zero-shot and five-shot settings. Overall accuracy is high, but performance varies markedly across categories, revealing competency gaps in areas central to professional practice. SAKE, its evaluation scripts, and all results are released as open source to give the community a baseline for tracking architectural reasoning in LLMs.

</details>


### [277] [RESOURCE2SKILL: Distilling Executable Agent Skills from Human-Created Multimodal Resources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29538)
*Yijia Fan,Zonglin Di,Zimo Wen,Yifan Yang,Mingxi Cheng,Qi Dai,Bei Liu,Kai Qiu,Yue Dong,Ji Li,Chong Luo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: RESOURCE2SKILL is a framework that converts multimodal resources (tutorial videos, repositories, articles, artifacts) into executable, hierarchically organized skills for software agents, achieving +11.9 pp improvement over no-skill baselines across seven authoring domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill libraries are mostly hand-written, text-centric, or derived from agent traces, leaving tutorial videos and other multimodal human resources largely underused. The goal is to leverage these rich but untapped resources to create more capable software agents.

Method: RESOURCE2SKILL distills multimodal resources (videos, code, articles, artifacts) into a hierarchical Skill Wiki where each entry combines structured text, code, visual examples, metadata, and provenance. At inference time, agents retrieve and compose relevant skills; when coverage is insufficient, the same operator acquires new skills online.

Result: Across seven authoring domains, RESOURCE2SKILL improves average overall score by +11.9 pp over no-skill agents and outperforms strong harness baselines in 26 of 28 main-aggregate model-domain cells.

Conclusion: Multimodal skill format, hierarchical organization, source diversity, selection strategy, and online acquisition are all valuable for effective skill-based agent performance.

Abstract: Skills are a useful abstraction for software agents, turning human and agent experience into reusable procedural knowledge. Yet existing skill libraries are mostly hand-written, text-centric, or derived from agent traces, leaving tutorial videos and other multimodal human resources largely underused. We present RESOURCE2SKILL, a framework that distills multimodal resources, including tutorial videos, repositories, articles, and reference artifacts, into executable skills for software agents. RESOURCE2SKILL organizes these skills as a hierarchical multimodal Skill Wiki, where each entry combines structured text, code, visual examples, metadata, and provenance. This design preserves complementary signals from different resources: videos capture temporal operations and visual effects, code captures executable tool patterns, and articles or artifacts provide conceptual and stylistic grounding. At inference time, agents retrieve and compose relevant skills from the wiki; when coverage is insufficient, the same construction operator can acquire new skills online. Across seven practical authoring domains, RESOURCE2SKILL improves average overall score by +11.9 percentage points over no-skill agents and outperforms strong harness baselines in 26 of 28 main-aggregate model-domain cells. Ablations confirm the value of multimodal skill format, hierarchical organization, source diversity, selection strategy, and online acquisition.

</details>


### [278] [Deforking the World of Code: A Project-Provenance Map that Recovers Cross-Forge Fork Families that Platform Graphs Cannot See](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29550)
*Audris Mockus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents p2PFull, a deforking map for the World of Code (WoC) that collapses raw git repositories into deforked projects using shared-commit relations and Louvain clustering, validated against GitHub's fork graph with 99.01% agreement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forks share git history, so a commit appears in many repositories; raw popularity/spread measures are inflated by orders of magnitude. A curated deforking map is needed to collapse raw repositories into their true distinct projects.

Method: Built from 51.79M shared-commit groups via hub-node star encoding and parallel Louvain clustering, plus capped variants (cap250/cap500) to bound mega-cluster size. Validated against GitHub's declared fork graph from GHArchive ForkEvents.

Result: 99.01% edge agreement with GitHub's fork graph conditional on both repos being in WoC. Disagreements include edges GitHub asserts but WoC hasn't ingested, and WoC-only fork families (5.41% multi-forge, 1.51% fork root not on GitHub). Released fork-exclusion list (134.1M children) and detached-fork inventory (455,550 hard-detached edges, 240,441 genuine origins).

Conclusion: The map is validated against GitHub's fork graph with high agreement. A cheap size cap removes boilerplate-hub bridges while preserving genuine vendored history. Disagreements reveal completeness gaps and cross-forge fork families invisible to GitHub's platform graph.

Abstract: Forks share git history, so a commit surfaces in many repositories and any spread- or popularity-based measure over raw repositories is inflated by orders of magnitude. We release a curated deforking map for the World of Code (WoC) version V2604: p2PFull, which collapses every raw repository p into the deforked project P to which it belongs, built from the global shared-commit relation (51.79M shared-commit groups) via a hub-node star encoding and parallel Louvain clustering, plus capped variants (cap250/cap500) that bound mega-cluster size. The naive shared-history union over-merges: the project graph welds unrelated software into giant clusters (largest uncapped cluster 861,948 repositories, bridged by shared-commit groups as large as 267,200), for the same structural reason author-identity graphs do. A cheap size cap removes the boilerplate-hub bridges; a structural-bridge diagnostic, the cut that dissolved the analogous author mega-cluster, run here but deliberately not applied, shows the post-cap residual is genuine vendored history, robust to the cut, so we leave it intact. We validate the map against GitHub's declared fork graph reconstructed from GHArchive ForkEvents, finding 99.01% edge agreement conditional on both repositories being in WoC. Disagreements fall into two classes: a completeness byproduct (edges GitHub asserts but WoC has not ingested) and the central contribution, WoC-only fork families that GitHub's platform graph cannot represent, including 5.41% multi-forge families and 1.51% whose fork root is not on GitHub. We additionally release a refreshed fork-exclusion list (134.1M children, 3.4x the GHTorrent-era 39.5M) and a detached-fork inventory (455,550 hard-detached edges; 240,441 genuine independent origins). All artifacts are a self-contained, independently hosted replication package keyed to the WoC V2604 collection.

</details>


### [279] [Connecting the Models: A Global Mega-model of MDE Projects on GitHub](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29606)
*Jesús Sánchez Cuadrado*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper constructs a large-scale dataset of 7,436 GitHub projects with over 325,000 MDE artefacts across 12 Eclipse EMF-related technologies, recovers per-project mega-models, builds a global mega-model via near-duplicate analysis, and proposes research questions to enable empirical study of MDE tool usage in practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite MDE technologies promising efficient construction of domain-specific modelling environments, there is little empirical evidence on how MDE tools are actually used, combined, and maintained in practice. This gap motivates the need to establish a foundation for studying real-world MDE adoption.

Method: The authors mined 7,436 GitHub projects containing over 325,000 MDE artefacts from 12 EMF-related technologies (Ecore, Emfatic, OCL, ATL, Epsilon, QVTo, Henshin, Acceleo, Xtext, Emftext, GMF, Sirius). They extracted repository-level information via Git repos and the GitHub API, devised a technique to recover per-project mega-models representing inter-artefact relationships, and built a global mega-model by analysing near-duplicates across all artefacts to merge duplicates and rewire connections.

Result: A comprehensive dataset linking MDE artefacts at both intra-project (mega-model) and inter-project (global mega-model with duplicate analysis) levels, enabling derivation of inter-project dependencies and connected subgraph analysis.

Conclusion: The dataset and derived global mega-model provide a foundation for answering research questions about MDE tool usage, combination patterns, and maintenance, fostering empirical research on MDE in practice.

Abstract: A key element of Model-Driven Engineering is the construction of domain-specific modelling environments to improve productivity and quality. In theory, dedicated technologies like EMF, ATL, Epsilon, Xtext, etc. would boost the construction of high-quality environments with a relatively modest effort by chaining the output of one tool to the input of another. However, there is little empirical evidence of how this idea has fared in reality and many open research questions remain, such as how MDE tools are used and combined, whether the resulting environments are maintained or not, which tools are used more frequently, etc.
  In this paper, we aim to build a foundation for studying how MDE is used in practice. First, we constructed a dataset by mining 7,436 Github projects comprising over 325,000 MDE artefacts. These artefacts encompass representative Eclipse EMF-related technologies, namely Ecore, Emfatic, OCL, ATL, Epsilon, QVTo, Henshin, Acceleo, Xtext, Emftext, GMF and Sirius. We also integrated into the dataset repository-level information extracted from the Git repositories and the GitHub API. From this dataset, we devised a technique to recover the mega-model of each project in order to represent the relationships between its artefacts. Then, we built a global mega-model relating the different MDE projects by performing an analysis of near-duplicates across all artefacts and grouping duplicate artefacts into single nodes and rewiring the connections. This global mega-model can be used to derive additional information like inter-project dependencies or studying connected subgraphs of artefacts. Finally, we propose a number of research questions that could be answered with the provided dataset, which we hope will foster empirical analysis of how MDE is applied.

</details>


### [280] [Bash-Commenter: Leveraging Syntax-Aware Preference Optimization to Reinforce Large Language Model for Bash Code Comment Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29709)
*Lei Yu,Jingyuan Zhang,Xin Wang,Li Yang,Fengjun Zhang,Peng Wang,Jia Xu,Jiajia Ma*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Bash-Commenter, a comment generation method for Bash scripts based on LLaMA-3.1-8B, outperforms SOTA baselines by combining a comprehensive multi-line Bash dataset, Continual Pre-training (CPT), Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT), and Syntax-Aware Preference Optimization (SAPO) using AST-based minimal preference pairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bash script comprehension is hindered by syntactic freedom, complex command structures, and lack of adequate comments. Existing automated comment generation approaches suffer from limited training datasets that inadequately represent real-world Bash usage patterns and insufficient understanding of Bash-specific concepts by LLMs.

Method: Bash-Commenter builds on LLaMA-3.1-8B with three key components: (1) a comprehensive dataset of complex multi-line Bash scripts with high-quality comments; (2) Continual Pre-training (CPT) on large-scale Bash data followed by Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT); and (3) Syntax-Aware Preference Optimization (SAPO), which constructs preference pairs by applying atomic operations to a script's Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) to create minimal pairs of correct and subtly incorrect scripts for fine-grained semantics learning.

Result: Outperforms SOTA baselines with 33.40% BLEU-4, 58.26% METEOR, 57.03% ROUGE-L on 1,064 single-line commands, and 22.15% BLEU-4, 43.89% METEOR, 32.80% ROUGE-L on 1,046 multi-line scripts. Human and LLM evaluations confirm superior comment quality in correctness, completeness, and naturalness.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses Bash comment generation challenges through domain-specific continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and syntax-aware preference optimization, achieving state-of-the-art performance across both single-line and multi-line Bash scripts.

Abstract: Bash script comprehension is challenging due to Bash's syntactic freedom and complex command structures. Despite its critical role in system administration, Bash scripts often lack adequate comments, hindering readability and maintainability. Existing automated comment generation approaches face two main challenges: (1) limited training datasets that inadequately represent real-world Bash usage patterns; and (2) insufficient understanding of Bash-specific concepts by Large Language Models (LLMs). To address these, we propose Bash-Commenter, an advanced comment generation method based on LLaMA-3.1-8B. First, we construct a comprehensive dataset of complex, multi-line Bash scripts with high-quality comments. Second, we conduct Continual Pre-training (CPT) on large-scale Bash data, followed by Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT), strengthening the model's foundational knowledge of Bash syntax and semantics. Finally, we introduce Syntax-Aware Preference Optimization (SAPO), which constructs preference pairs by applying atomic operations to a script's Abstract Syntax Tree (AST), creating minimal pairs of correct and subtly incorrect scripts for fine-grained semantics learning. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving 33.40% BLEU-4, 58.26% METEOR, and 57.03% ROUGE-L for 1,064 single-line commands, and 22.15% BLEU-4, 43.89% METEOR, and 32.80% ROUGE-L for 1,046 multi-line scripts. Human and LLM evaluations further confirm superior comment quality in correctness, completeness, and naturalness.

</details>


### [281] [MicroAgent: Context-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Automatic Microservice Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29742)
*Zishan Su,Junjie Huang,Shiwen Shan,Xingyan Chen,Hui Zeng,Yuxin Su,Yanlin Wang,Michael R. Lyu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: MicroAgent is a context-augmented multi-agent framework that decomposes monolithic applications into microservices by dividing the task into five subtasks assigned to specialized agents, achieving 89.2% decomposition accuracy on Java Web applications, outperforming SOTA by 24.6%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual microservice decomposition is time-consuming, existing automated methods fail to capture semantic insights, and naive LLM applications overlook crucial context and design principles, leading to suboptimal results.

Method: MicroAgent divides the decomposition process into five distinct subtasks, each assigned to a specialized agent. It provides tailored multi-granularity context to keep analysis focused and mitigate information overload, and integrates analytical tools to guide agent decision-making based on established design principles.

Result: 10 Java Web applications experiments show 89.2% average decomposition accuracy, outperforming SOTA by 24.6%.

Conclusion: MicroAgent effectively addresses microservice decomposition challenges through context-augmented multi-agent collaboration and design principle integration, significantly outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: The adoption of Microservice Architecture (MSA) has revolutionized software engineering by enhancing scalability, agility, and maintainability over traditional monolithic applications. As more developers transition their legacy systems to microservice-based architectures, effective microservice decomposition-partitioning monolithic applications into highly cohesive services-becomes vital. However, this decomposition task presents significant challenges. Manual approaches are time-consuming and labor-intensive. Existing automated methods often fail to capture the necessary semantic insights from complex applications, while naive applications of Large Language Models tend to overlook crucial contextual information and design principles, leading to suboptimal results.
  To address these challenges, we propose MicroAgent, a Context-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Microservice Decomposition. Our framework divides the decomposition process into five distinct subtasks and assigns each to a specialized agent. To enhance the effectiveness of each agent, we provide tailored, multi-granularity context that keeps its analysis focused and mitigates information overload. Furthermore, to ensure the decomposition adheres to established design principles, we integrate analytical tools that guide the agents' decision-making. Experimental evaluations on 10 Java Web applications demonstrate that MicroAgent achieves an average decomposition accuracy of 89.2%, outperforming the state-of-the-art method by 24.6%. We also conduct a case study to highlight the practical benefits of our design.

</details>


### [282] [Uncovering Similar but Different Packages in PyPI and Potential Security Threats](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29785)
*Sunha Park,Soojin Han,Seunghoon Woo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This large-scale study of 200K PyPI packages reveals significant package replication problems: 1,361 replicated popular packages, 256 unknown replicated vulnerable packages, and 186 malicious packages that replicated popular ones, exposing how replication creates confusion, vulnerability blind spots, and malware distribution vectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Package replication in PyPI, where packages duplicate most code from existing packages under different maintainers, causes developer confusion, propagates known vulnerabilities, and enables creation of new malicious packages, yet has not been systematically studied at scale.

Method: Analyzed one-third of PyPI (200K packages) investigating replication from three perspectives: popular packages (top 3K), vulnerable packages, and malicious packages (3,883 known).

Result: Identified 1,361 replicated popular packages, 256 previously unknown replicated vulnerable packages, and found 186 (4.79%) of 3,883 known malicious packages replicated popular ones, leading to 7 previously unknown replicated malicious packages.

Conclusion: Package replication in PyPI is a prevalent phenomenon that creates vulnerability blind spots current tools cannot catch and serves as an attack vector for malware distribution through minor modifications and code injection.
