TL;DR: This paper identifies a shared measurement problem in LLM evaluation and AI safety, introduces the EvalSafetyGap hypothesis with Instability Decomposition and Alignment Trilemma constructs, performs a ten-model audit showing capability-safety correlations are statistically indeterminate, and provides a vocabulary and evidence map for improving evaluation and safety measurement practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark scores, reward-model signals, and safety metrics can improve while the latent properties they represent remain difficult to verify, creating a fundamental measurement problem that undermines both LLM evaluation and AI safety assurance.

Method: Hybrid survey (systematic search with narrative synthesis and grey evidence tracking) spanning eight evidence streams (2018-2026), combined with a conceptual framework (EvalSafetyGap, Instability Decomposition, Alignment Trilemma) and a structured ten-model audit measuring capability, behavioral safety, and governance separately.

Result: Capability-sustained adversarial robustness correlation (Pearson r = +0.232, p = 0.520) is statistically indeterminate; open-closed safety gap is modest and driven by governance/disclosure rather than behavioral robustness; results are protocol-dependent; attempt-budget findings are protocol sensitive.

Conclusion: The field needs a shared vocabulary and evidence map to support dynamic evaluation, transparent source reporting, multi-attempt safety measurement, and auditable alignment practice.

Abstract: LLM evaluation and AI safety face a shared measurement problem: benchmark scores, reward-model signals, and reported safety metrics can improve while the latent properties they are meant to represent remain difficult to verify. This paper combines a hybrid survey - a systematic search paired with narrative synthesis and separately tracked grey evidence - with a conceptual framework and a structured ten-model audit. The synthesis spans eight evidence streams: benchmark validity, dynamic evaluation, LLM-as-judge reliability, safety evaluation, jailbreak/refusal robustness, reward hacking, mechanistic interpretability, and governance/auditability, covering 2018-2026 evaluation-safety measurement work. We introduce EvalSafetyGap as an organizing hypothesis for comparing evaluation-side and alignment-side proxy failures under optimization pressure, using Goodhart's Law together with two constructs we develop here - an Instability Decomposition and an Alignment Trilemma - as tools for generating testable comparisons. The audit shows how conclusions shift when capability, behavioral safety, and governance are measured separately. In this sample (n = 10), the association between capability and sustained adversarial robustness is statistically indeterminate using the displayed Table 3 inputs (Pearson r = +0.232, p = 0.520), and the apparent open-closed safety gap is modest, driven mainly by governance and disclosure rather than behavioral robustness, and sensitive to how a single borderline model is classified; attempt-budget results are protocol dependent. Because the public evidence uses heterogeneous protocols, the audit is diagnostic rather than rank-generating. The contribution is a shared vocabulary and evidence map to support dynamic evaluation, transparent source reporting, multi-attempt safety measurement, and auditable alignment practice.

</details>


### [335] [Characterizing Large Language Model Agentic Workflows: A Study on N8n Ecosystem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29116)
*Yutian Tang,Yuming Zhou,Huaming Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study of LLM agentic workflows in low-code automation platforms, analyzing 6,000+ n8n workflows. It finds LLMs are embedded within broader automation structures (control logic, external tools, human review) but reliability mechanisms like fallback paths and human approval gates remain uncommon, revealing a gap between LLM agent deployment and engineering support for reliability and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are rapidly being adopted in low-code/no-code automation platforms where non-expert users design workflows combining NL understanding with external services. However, there is no large-scale empirical understanding of how LLM agents are actually designed and deployed in these practical automation ecosystems, particularly regarding reliability, safety, and governance.

Method: The authors conducted a large-scale empirical study analyzing over 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows, examining four aspects: task distribution, structural and tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels.

Result: LLM workflows are not merely prompt response pipelines but are commonly embedded within broader automation structures involving control logic, external tools, communication services, storage systems, and human review points. While lightweight post-processing or routing logic is common, explicit reliability mechanisms (structured fallback paths, repair loops, failure-specific alerts, human approval gates) remain relatively uncommon. The study provides ten empirical findings and five research takeaways.

Conclusion: There is a significant gap between the increasing deployment of LLM agents in practical automation ecosystems and the limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. This calls for researchers, platform developers, and practitioners to improve real-world LLM agentic workflows.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted in low-code and no-code automation platforms, where non-expert users design workflows that combine natural language understanding with external services and APIs. LLM agents are LLM systems that use LLMs as a core "brain" to reason, plan, and autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks. In this paper, we present the first large-scale empirical study of LLM agentic workflows in low-code automation platforms. We analyze more than 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows and examine four aspects of their design: task distribution, structural and tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels. Our analysis shows that LLM workflows are not merely prompt response pipelines. Instead, LLMs are commonly embedded within broader automation structures involving control logic, external tools, communication services, storage systems, and human review points. We further find that while many workflows include lightweight post-processing or routing logic after LLM execution, explicit reliability mechanisms such as structured fallback paths, repair loops, failure-specific alerts, and human approval gates remain relatively uncommon. These results reveal a gap between the increasing deployment of LLM agents in practical automation ecosystems and the limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. Overall, our study provides ten empirical findings and five research takeaways for researchers, platform developers, and practitioners seeking to understand and improve real-world LLM agentic workflows.

</details>


### [336] [Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30294)
*Rahul Khedar,Mayank Malhotra,Avinash Karn,Mouli V,Prakhar Mehrotra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that automates live software demonstrations by combining UI exploration, source-code analysis, scripted narration, and real-time voice Q&A, with a rehearsal loop and runtime synchronization to ensure robust, interactive demos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Live product demonstrations are high-cost, labor-intensive activities requiring human presenters to select features, operate applications, narrate, and answer questions. Existing solutions (browser agents, demo videos) only handle fragments and lack interactivity or robustness to UI drift.

Method: The system uses: (1) cross-modal feature representation merging UI exploration with source-code analysis via discrete focus tiers; (2) a grounded scripter constrained to observed UI elements with multi-strategy semantic locators; (3) a pre-presentation rehearsal loop with convergence and graceful degradation; (4) a runtime synchronization invariant linking browser actions to audio-end events of narration segments.

Result: Across six sessions on four applications (including Excalidraw), the rehearser's locator-firing rate (sigma-bar) ranged 0.31-1.00 over 147 actions. On a 53-action workload with full tier differentiation, sigma-bar ≈ 0.92. On the public-domain reference, locator-repair drove convergence to sigma-bar = 1.00 at iteration 2.

Conclusion: Rhetor demonstrates feasibility of automated, interactive live demos. The authors propose a ten-metric benchmark across six application categories for future evaluation of design choices.

Abstract: Live product demonstrations are a recurring, high-cost activity in software organizations: a human presenter must select features, dispatch the corresponding interactions on a running application, narrate them coherently, and answer questions in real time. Existing automation addresses only fragments -- generalist browser agents target instruction-conditioned task completion, and demo-video tools produce fixed MP4 artifacts that cannot be questioned and silently break under interface drift. We propose Rhetor, a multi-agent system that takes a running web application and its source-code repository as input and produces a rehearsed live demonstration with segment-synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. The architectural contributions are a cross-modal feature representation that merges UI exploration with source-code analysis into features tagged with discrete focus tiers, a grounded scripter constrained to UI elements observed during exploration and dispatched through multi-strategy semantic locators, a pre-presentation rehearsal loop with explicit convergence and graceful degradation to narration-only segments, and a runtime synchronization invariant that ties each browser action to the audio-end event of its narration segment. Across six pipeline sessions on four deployed applications -- including the public-domain whiteboard application Excalidraw -- the rehearser's internal locator-firing rate (sigma-bar) spans 0.31-1.00 over 147 scripted actions; on the substantial workload (53 actions, full tier differentiation), sigma-bar is approximately 0.92, and on the public-domain reference point the locator-repair step drives convergence to sigma-bar = 1.00 at iteration 2. We additionally define a benchmark protocol of ten metrics across six application categories that would establish, beyond the case study, whether each design choice contributes positively.

</details>


### [337] [HiComm: Hierarchical Communication for Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29126)
*Runze Zhao,Dongruo Zhou,Sumit Kumar Jha,Nathaniel D. Bastian,Ankit Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HiComm is a plug-in communication module for cooperative MARL that replaces flat dense message vectors with structured, hierarchical information retrieval, where receivers query and selectively extract entity-level features from senders' hierarchical observations, achieving comparable or better performance while reducing communication volume by up to 23×.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MARL communication protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors detached from the structure of observations, missing the inductive bias inherent in many cooperative environments where observations naturally follow a hierarchy (groups and entities).

Method: HiComm proposes a receiver-driven, three-stage hierarchical decoding process: (1) select a group, (2) select a sender within that group, (3) select an entity within that sender, returning the corresponding feature slice as the message. Uses Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and a lightweight shared projection design.

Result: Matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines across cooperative MARL tasks while reducing communication volume by up to 23× per receiver per episode.

Conclusion: Structured, hierarchical communication grounded in observation structure is more efficient and effective than flat dense vector communication in cooperative MARL, converting unstructured transmission into structured information retrieval.

Abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) often relies on communication to mitigate partial observability, yet most existing protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors detached from the structure of the observations they summarize. This design overlooks an important source of inductive bias in many cooperative environments, where observations naturally follow a hierarchy such as groups and entities. We propose \textsc{HiComm}, a plug-in communication module that grounds messages in the sender's hierarchical observation. \textsc{HiComm} is receiver-driven: the receiver issues a query, and the hierarchy is resolved through a three-stage decoding process that first selects a group, then a sender, and then an entity within that group, returning the corresponding feature slice as the message. This converts communication from unstructured vector transmission into structured information retrieval over the sender's observation hierarchy. We instantiate this mechanism with Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and a lightweight shared projection design that attaches to standard MARL pipelines. Experiments across cooperative MARL tasks with different observation structures and coordination demands show that \textsc{HiComm} matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines while reducing communication volume by up to $23\times$ per receiver per episode.

</details>


### [338] [Flow Reasoning Models: Scaling Reasoning Through Iterative Self-Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29150)
*Alec Helbling,Andrey Bryutkin,Mauro Martino,Nima Dehmamy,Hendrik Strobelt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs) introduce a training and test-time-scaling framework for structured reasoning using discrete flow models. Despite poor initial solve rates (~36%), flow models can act as self-verifiers via dynamic stability (correct answers are stable fixed points under re-noising/re-solving). Test-time scaling via searching for stable solutions achieves high accuracy (≈100% Sudoku, 95.9% Zebra) and generalizes out-of-distribution. Training improvements (self-conditioning + DPO) boost efficiency, reaching 99.2% on Sudoku in 7 forward passes (8× fewer than baselines).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete flow models show promise for few-step text generation but fail on structured reasoning tasks (e.g., Sudoku ~36% solve rate). The authors aim to bridge this gap by leveraging flow models' self-verification capability and improving their efficiency for structured reasoning.

Method: 1) Test-time scaling: propose candidate solutions, select those that are dynamically stable (return to themselves under re-noising and re-solving). 2) Training: add self-conditioning channel (closed at inference) for iterative refinement; apply direct preference optimization (DPO) to avoid past failed generations.

Result: Test-time scaling alone achieves ~100% on Sudoku-Shah, 95.9% on Zebra, and generalizes to Sudoku-Extreme (96.1%) without training on that distribution. Training improvements yield 99.2% on Sudoku in 7 forward passes (8× fewer than masked-diffusion baseline). Combined approach solves hard OOD puzzles far more efficiently.

Conclusion: Flow models can be made effective for structured reasoning by leveraging self-verification via dynamic stability at test time, and training refinements (self-conditioning + DPO) dramatically improve efficiency, making them competitive with or superior to existing diffusion-based reasoning methods.

Abstract: Discrete flow models have recently shown promising performance on few-step text generation; however, when naively applied to structured reasoning tasks such as Sudoku and Zebra puzzles, they converge confidently to incorrect answers (solving only $\sim$36% of Sudoku puzzles). We introduce Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs), a training and test-time-scaling framework for structured reasoning with flow models. We make the observation that, despite their poor solve rate, flow models can act as their own verifiers. A correct answer is a stable fixed point of the denoising dynamics, returning to itself when re-noised and re-solved. This enables a test-time-scaling paradigm: propose many candidate solutions and keep those that are dynamically stable, which alone reaches high solve rates on Sudoku-Shah (~$100\%$) and Zebra ($95.9\%$). This even generalizes to harder out-of-distribution puzzles like Sudoku-Extreme ($96.1\%$), without ever training on that distribution. This pure search, however, wastes a great deal of computation generating incorrect candidate solutions. We therefore design a training recipe to improve the base model's efficiency. First, we train flow models with a self-conditioning channel and close it at inference, letting them refine their own past predictions. Second, we train models to avoid their own failed generations using direct preference optimization. These changes substantially improve the base model's efficiency, letting it reach $99.2\%$ on Sudoku in just $7$ forward passes, over $8\times$ fewer than the strongest matched masked-diffusion baseline we compare needs for the same accuracy. When combined with test-time scaling, this lets flow models solve hard out-of-distribution puzzles (e.g. Sudoku-Extreme) far more efficiently.

</details>


### [339] [Pooled Leaderboards Hide System-Specific Winners: A Reporting-Protocol Audit of Offline Root-Cause Analysis Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29159)
*Lining Hu,Ting Liu,Yuzhuo Fu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper audits offline RCA benchmarks and finds that pooled top-1 accuracy rankings are unreliable across subsystems. Using three benchmark families (OpenRCA, RCAEval, PetShop) with 11 subsystems and 778 scoring units, the analysis shows subsystem-level effects of both signs, prediction intervals crossing zero, and non-exchangeability in most method pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection incurs regret up to 24.8 pp.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Engineers commonly interpret pooled winner rankings from RCA benchmarks as a universal recommendation for their own subsystem, but this reading may be invalid if performance varies across subsystems. The paper aims to audit whether such pooling conceals significant subsystem-level heterogeneity.

Method: The authors audit three public RCA benchmark families (OpenRCA, RCAEval, PetShop) covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. They retain four methods/comparators (BARO, CD-1min adapter, max-|Z|, per-service alert-count) with complete coverage. They perform six pairwise comparisons using random-effects models (95% prediction intervals), case-level interaction tests for exchangeability, and leave-one-system-out selection to measure regret.

Result: All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs; every random-effects 95% prediction interval crosses zero; case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 held-out subsystems, with regret reaching 24.8 pp on RCAEval / Sock-Shop.

Conclusion: Pooled rankings in RCA benchmarks are not stable across subsystems, so engineers should not blindly trust pooled winners as universal recommendations. The authors release an audit module to recompute per-subsystem stability checks alongside pooled scores.

Abstract: Offline root-cause-analysis (RCA) benchmarks commonly rank methods by a single pooled top-1 accuracy across multiple subsystems, and engineers often read the pooled winner as a recommendation for their own subsystem. We audit that reading on three public RCA benchmark families -- OpenRCA, RCAEval, and PetShop -- covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. To keep pairwise comparisons on identical cases, the main analysis retains four methods or comparators with complete coverage: BARO, a CD-1min adapter, max-$|Z|$, and per-service alert-count. All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs, every random-effects 95\% prediction interval crosses zero, and case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 held-out subsystems, with regret reaching 24.8 pp on RCAEval / Sock-Shop. We release a 320-line audit module; given a matched RCA benchmark score table, it recomputes the same per-subsystem stability checks alongside pooled scores.

</details>


### [340] [Direct Causation in International Humanitarian Law and the Challenge of AI-Mediated Civilian Cyber Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29175)
*Alice Saito,Harold Godsoe,Phan Xuan Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the ICRC's direct causation test for direct participation in hostilities because autonomous multi-agent systems produce harm through post-disengagement system decisions, causing the "one causal step" and "integral-part" standards to fail, resulting in default classification as indirect participation. It introduces goal-specification granularity as a key property and proposes a five-level classification spectrum.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a gap in international humanitarian law where AI-mediated civilian cyber operations do not fit existing doctrinal frameworks for determining direct participation in hostilities, particularly regarding the causation element of the ICRC's three-criterion test.

Method: Doctrinal legal analysis applying the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance framework to AI-mediated civilian cyber operations, supplemented by identification of goal-specification granularity as a technical-legal property, classification along a five-level spectrum, and evaluation of existing AI governance instruments.

Result: The "one causal step" standard fails because autonomous multi-agent systems generate harm-producing decisions after human disengagement. The integral-part requirement fails because it presupposes downstream human contributors with independently classifiable conduct. Existing AI governance instruments do not log or report goal-specification granularity, the property on which the concreteness component implicitly turns.

Conclusion: The current ICRC framework defaults to classifying AI-mediated civilian cyber deployments as indirect participation, which contradicts its purpose of capturing individuals who personally take part in hostilities, revealing a structural gap that requires doctrinal or governance reform.

Abstract: International humanitarian law protects civilians from direct attack unless and for such time as they take direct part in hostilities, with the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance operationalising this rule through a three-criterion cumulative test. This paper argues that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the direct causation element of this test in a structurally specific way: when a civilian deploys an autonomous multi-agent cyber system of the kind recently demonstrated in offensive AI research, the "one causal step" standard fails because harm is produced by system-generated decisions made after human disengagement, and the integral-part requirement does not extend because it presupposes downstream human contributors whose conduct can be independently classified. The framework therefore defaults to treating such deployments as indirect participation, in tension with its purpose of capturing civilians who personally take part in hostilities. Beyond the doctrinal analysis, this paper identifies goal-specification granularity as the property on which the integral-part test's concreteness component implicitly turns, classifies AI-mediated operations along a five-level spectrum, and argues that existing technical AI governance instruments do not log or report this property.

</details>


### [341] [Selective Memory Retention for Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29178)
*Pranath Reddy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceRetain is a bounded external memory framework for frozen LLM agents that scores entries by interpretable features and evicts low-scoring entries. On clean ALFWorld, memory improves over no memory but retention policies show no significant differences. Under noisy-write stress (75% distractors), unbounded and FIFO-K50 degrade while TraceRetain-CEM remains stable, preserving 97/100 task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study when retention matters for memory-augmented LLM agents and whether bounded external memory with interpretable eviction policies can robustly handle memory pollution from noisy write streams.

Method: TraceRetain framework: bounded external memory for frozen LLM agents that scores entries by seven interpretable features (success, age, access frequency, redundancy, specificity, similarity, downstream utility) and evicts lowest-scoring entries at capacity. Tested on clean and noisy ALFWorld with gpt-5-mini.

Result: Clean ALFWorld: memory improves over no memory but bounded retention policies show no significant differences (within Wilson 95% CIs). Noisy-write stress: unbounded memory Precision@5 drops from 20.2% to 12.4%, FIFO-K50 from 15.8% to 3.8%, while TraceRetain-CEM remains stable (16.9% to 16.6%) and preserves 97/100 task success. In-distribution: 47-49/50 tasks solved vs. 39/50 for no memory.

Conclusion: Bounded retention buys memory and step efficiency on saturated clean benchmarks at no task-success cost, and only differentiates from cache heuristics when streams contain noise. TraceRetain-CEM is robust to noisy write stress where unbounded memory and FIFO fail due to distractor similarity in embedding space.

Abstract: When does retention matter for memory-augmented LLM agents? We study this with TraceRetain, a lightweight framework for bounded external memory in frozen LLM agents that scores entries by interpretable features (success, age, access frequency, redundancy, specificity, similarity, downstream utility) and evicts the lowest-scoring ones at capacity. On clean ALFWorld with gpt-5-mini, external memory robustly improves over no memory across two seeds, but differences among bounded retention policies fall within Wilson 95% CIs: clean ALFWorld at T=100 to T=200 does not naturally exhibit the memory pollution retention is designed to address. Under a controlled noisy-write stress (75% synthetic distractors), unbounded memory and FIFO-K50 degrade on Precision@5 (20.2% to 12.4% and 15.8% to 3.8%) while TraceRetain-CEM is essentially unchanged (16.9% to 16.6%) and preserves 97/100 task success. The mechanism: unbounded memory has the highest mean similarity (0.87) but lowest precision, indicating failed distractors close to the query in embedding space. Held-out in-distribution evaluation shows memory-augmented policies solving 47 to 49 of 50 tasks vs. 39/50 for no memory. Bounded retention buys memory and step efficiency on saturated clean benchmarks at no task-success cost, and only differentiates from cache heuristics when streams contain noise.

</details>


### [342] [Measuring Graph-to-Graph Semantic Similarity in Knowledge Graphs: An Empirical Evaluation of Knowledge Graph Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29180)
*Seungryeol Baek,Wooseok Sim,Hogun Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies graph-to-graph semantic similarity in Knowledge Graphs (KGs) by constructing a semantic matching dataset from modified text documents and introducing two KG embedding-based scoring functions (EmbPairSim and AvgEmbSim). EmbPairSim achieves up to 5.3 pp higher MRR than Sentence-BERT with fewer parameters, demonstrating that KGE representations effectively capture graph-level semantic similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KG embedding methods focus on entities, relations, and triples, neglecting graph-level semantics. Conventional graph-level methods relying on structural similarity cannot guarantee semantic similarity between KGs, motivating the need for evaluating graph-to-graph semantic similarity.

Method: Constructed a semantic matching dataset by modifying text documents, extracting KGs from original and modified documents, and transferring known correspondences to KG pairs. Compared text-based, structure-based, and KG embedding-based approaches. Proposed EmbPairSim (maximal pairwise entity similarity) and AvgEmbSim (frequency-weighted centroid) scoring functions.

Result: EmbPairSim achieves up to 5.3 pp higher MRR than Sentence-BERT while using substantially fewer parameters on WikiText-2 and CC-News datasets.

Conclusion: KGE representations serve as compact and effective signals for graph-to-graph semantic similarity in KGs.

Abstract: A Knowledge Graph (KG) represents facts as structured triples and is widely used to organize relational knowledge across diverse domains. Just as textual information ranges from words and sentences to complete documents, KG information can be interpreted at multiple levels, from entities, relations, and triples to subgraphs and entire KGs. However, existing KG embedding methods mainly focus on entities, relations, and triples, leaving graph-level semantics largely unaddressed. Conventional graph-level methods, which typically compare graphs based on structural patterns, are also insufficient because structural similarity alone cannot guarantee semantic similarity between KGs. To evaluate how well different methods capture such graph-level semantic information, we study graph-to-graph semantic similarity, which determines whether a pair of KGs represents semantically corresponding underlying information. To obtain reliable ground-truth correspondences, we construct a semantic matching dataset by modifying text documents, extracting KGs from both original and modified documents, and transferring their known correspondences to KG pairs. We compare text-based, structure-based, and KG embedding-based approaches on each dataset. For the KG embedding-based approach, we introduce two scoring functions: \textit{EmbPairSim}, which uses maximal pairwise entity similarity, and \textit{AvgEmbSim}, which uses a frequency-weighted centroid. Experiments on WikiText-2 and CC-News show that \textit{EmbPairSim} achieves up to 5.3 pp higher MRR than Sentence-BERT while using substantially fewer parameters. These results suggest that KGE representations can serve as compact and effective signals for graph-to-graph semantic similarity in KGs. Our code is available at https://github.com/SeungRyeolBaek/KG-to-KG-Semantic-Similarity.

</details>


### [343] [Evidence-Informed LLM Beliefs for Continual Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29182)
*Dhruv Agarwal,Reece Adamson,Andrew McCallum,Peter Clark,Ashish Sabharwal,Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies that current LLM-based scientific discovery methods (like AutoDiscovery) use static Bayesian surprise as a reward signal, which fails to account for evolving beliefs. The authors propose using evidence-informed LLM beliefs with non-stationary surprisal, finding that embedding-based RAG best anticipates posteriors and identifies 37.5% of static surprisals as spurious. Their method, incorporating belief-update filtering and diversity maximization, increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average across five domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-driven scientific discovery treats Bayesian surprise as a static quantity, while human reasoning involves non-stationary surprisal relative to evolving beliefs. This mismatch limits continual scientific discovery, motivating the need for evidence-informed belief updates.

Method: The authors compare in-context belief-updating mechanisms, identify embedding-based retrieval-augmented generation as best for anticipating posteriors, and modify the search procedure with two complementary changes: belief-update filtering (to remove spurious rewards) and diversity maximization (to prioritize hypotheses that remain surprising under non-stationary beliefs).

Result: The method identifies 37.5% of static surprisals as spurious and increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average across five discovery domains compared to the original AutoDiscovery procedure.

Conclusion: Continual scientific discovery with LLMs requires not only better belief measurement (non-stationary surprisal via evidence-informed beliefs) but also search procedures that avoid redundancy and encourage diversity.

Abstract: Open-ended scientific discovery with large language models (LLMs) increasingly operates as a long-horizon loop of hypothesis search and verification, where a reward signal guides which hypotheses to test next. A notable recent example is AutoDiscovery, which uses "Bayesian surprise" - the belief shift an LLM undergoes after observing evidence for a hypothesis - as both a discovery metric and a reward for search. We first observe that AutoDiscovery treats surprisal as a static quantity, while surprisal in human reasoning is non-stationary - it is defined relative to beliefs that evolve with experience, a prerequisite for continual scientific discovery. We address this mismatch with evidence-informed LLM beliefs: priors updated with evidence from previous hypotheses to compute non-stationary surprisal for new hypotheses. We compare in-context belief-updating mechanisms and find that embedding-based retrieval-augmented generation over prior discoveries best anticipates eventual posteriors, identifying 37.5% of static surprisals as spurious. We then modify search to avoid these spurious rewards and prioritize hypotheses that remain surprising under non-stationary beliefs. Concretely, we introduce two complementary changes to the original search procedure: belief-update filtering and diversity maximization. Across five discovery domains, our method increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average compared to the original search procedure, demonstrating that continual scientific discovery with LLMs requires not only better belief measurement but also search procedures that avoid redundancy and encourage diversity.

</details>


### [344] [AI Trading's Alpha Singularity: Emergent Market Reasoning through Agent-to-Agent Self-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29194)
*Yuqi Li,Siyuan Liu,Bingjun Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agora is a framework (Sealed Joint Search) that treats the scoring function as a search artifact alongside alpha factors, preventing overfitting and self-confirmation. Using multi-agent LLM interaction with provenance-sealed evaluation, it achieves holdout Sharpe +1.87 on CSI 1000, significantly outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated alpha mining suffers from a generalization gap because search converges against a fixed scoring function, overfitting whatever the scorer cannot penalize. Treating the scoring function as a search artifact—rather than a fixed external oracle—could prevent this collapse, but doing so risks self-confirmation where the evaluator merely confirms what the search wants to hear.

Method: Sealed Joint Search (SJS) imposes structural conditions: role decomposition, typed inter-role communication, provenance-sealed reads, versioned stores, and substrate-local promotion. Agora instantiates SJS with five LLM agent classes communicating via three channels, evolving eight skill libraries and alpha libraries built on AlphaGen operators. Three evaluators write reports aggregated into one brief, preserving disagreement instead of voting.

Result: On CSI 1000 with 100 rounds and a 91-day 2026 holdout sealed from all LLM inputs, Agora achieves holdout Sharpe +1.87. Best baseline achieves +1.334 at favorable seed but -0.755 cross-seed mean. Pre-loading metrics into frozen-library ablation recovers only +0.40 of +2.25 Sharpe gap. Adding PPO without library evolution worsens the gap.

Conclusion: The two metrics that drive performance emerge rather than being designed. Caveats: single-seed run, short-side concentrated signal, intended for long-short. The framework demonstrates that joint search over both alpha factors and scoring functions, when properly sealed, can substantially outperform fixed-scorer approaches.

Abstract: Automated alpha mining holds the scoring function fixed and varies the search algorithm over it. A search that converges against a fixed scorer overfits whatever the scorer cannot penalize, a primary cause of the out-of-sample generalization gap. We treat the scoring function as a search artifact alongside the alpha factors and study what conditions make this joint search admissible. Sealed Joint Search (SJS) is a framework: a set of structural conditions on information flow in an autonomous-discovery system that prevent joint search from collapsing into self-confirmation while keeping the evaluator sealed. Conditions cover role decomposition, typed inter-role communication, provenance-sealed reads, versioned stores, and substrate-local promotion. Agora tests SJS empirically: five LLM agent classes communicate via three channels, evolving eight skill libraries, with alpha libraries built on AlphaGen operators. Three evaluators write reports aggregated into one brief, carrying forward disagreement instead of voting. We run Agora for 100 rounds on CSI 1000 and evaluate on a 91-day 2026 holdout sealed from all LLM inputs. Agora achieves holdout Sharpe +1.87; best baseline +1.334 at favorable seed and -0.755 cross-seed mean. Pre-loading Agora's two metrics into a frozen-library ablation recovers only +0.40 of the +2.25 Sharpe gap, and adding PPO without library evolution worsens the gap. The two metrics emerge rather than being designed. Caveats: single-seed run, short-side concentrated signal, intended for long-short.

</details>


### [345] [A Cognition-Emotion-Personality Framework for Modeling Human-Like Awareness and Behavior in Emergency Evacuations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29212)
*Zoi Lygizou,Michalis Zervas,Helena G. Theodoropoulou,Vasilis Zafeiropoulos,Dimitris Kalles,Chairi Kiourt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents an extended evacuation framework integrating cognitive, emotional, social, and personality mechanisms (dynamic event awareness, memory-based exit knowledge, continuous fear model, OCEAN personality) to model human behavior under uncertainty, demonstrating that these factors reduce evacuation efficiency and produce realistic crowd phenomena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent-based evacuation models rely on unrealistic assumptions (perfect event awareness, complete exit knowledge, fully rational decision-making). This work aims to address these limitations by incorporating cognitive, emotional, social, and personality-driven mechanisms into a unified framework.

Method: The framework includes: (1) dynamic event-awareness via a continuous Event Certainty Level; (2) memory-based exit knowledge with acquisition, forgetting, and recall; (3) a continuous fear model where panic emerges at high intensity; (4) OCEAN-based personality representation with neuroticism integrated into emotional dynamics; (5) individualized decision thresholds for behavioral heterogeneity. Simulation experiments examine spatial familiarity, memory robustness, decision sensitivity, emotional dynamics, and personality variation.

Result: Cognitive, emotional, and personality-driven processes substantially influence evacuation dynamics, reducing evacuation efficiency and generating realistic crowd phenomena including delays, confusion, injuries, and socially influenced behaviors.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a more realistic representation of human behavior in emergency evacuations and supports systematic investigation of interactions between cognition, emotion, personality, and crowd dynamics.

Abstract: Agent-based evacuation simulations are widely used to study crowd behavior during emergencies, but many models rely on assumptions such as perfect event awareness, complete exit knowledge, and fully rational decision-making. This paper presents an extended evacuation framework that integrates cognitive, emotional, social, and personality-related mechanisms into a unified model of human behavior under uncertainty. The framework incorporates a dynamic event-awareness mechanism based on a continuous Event Certainty Level, a memory-based representation of exit knowledge subject to acquisition, forgetting, and recall, a continuous fear model in which panic emerges as a high-intensity state, and an OCEAN-based personality representation. Neuroticism is explicitly integrated into the emotional model, influencing fear generation, escalation, social contagion, and recovery. Behavioral heterogeneity is further captured through individualized decision thresholds that affect responses to perceived risk. The framework is evaluated through simulation experiments examining the effects of spatial familiarity, memory robustness, decision sensitivity, emotional dynamics, and personality variation. Results show that cognitive, emotional, and personality-driven processes substantially influence evacuation dynamics, reducing evacuation efficiency and generating realistic crowd phenomena such as delays, confusion, injuries, and socially influenced behaviors. The proposed framework provides a more realistic representation of human behavior in emergency evacuations and supports systematic investigation of the interactions between cognition, emotion, personality, and crowd dynamics.

</details>


### [346] [PolicyGuard: A Dialogue-Grounded Sub-Agent Verifier for Policy Adherence in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29225)
*Seongjae Kang,Taehyung Yu,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POLICYGUARD is a sub-agent verifier that improves LLM agent policy adherence by leveraging full conversation context, self-reasoning over policies, and providing actionable feedback, outperforming prior safeguard methods on the tau^2-BENCH airline benchmark across multiple vendors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work treats policy adherence as a safeguarding problem limited to external checks on individual argument values, but real agent workflows require multi-turn compliance, user confirmations, prerequisite reads, and dialogue-level reasoning — capabilities underestimated by existing approaches.

Method: Introduces POLICYGUARD, a sub-agent verifier that shares the agent's dialogue view, reasons over company policy in context, and generates actionable remediation for the agent's next turn, enabling conversation-specific policy adherence without blocking actions unnecessarily.

Result: On tau^2-BENCH across GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Gemini 2.5 Pro (4 trials each), POLICYGUARD improves PASS4 by +12.0/+6.0/+12.0 pp, achieving higher policy-violation recall while blocking roughly half as often as argument-level guards.

Conclusion: Policy adherence for LLM agents requires full conversation context and self-reasoning, and POLICYGUARD demonstrates that a context-aware sub-agent verifier with actionable feedback is more effective and less intrusive than traditional argument-level safeguarding approaches.

Abstract: LLM agents handle user requests on behalf of organizations through tool calls and must follow the company policies stated in their system prompts. Prior work approaches this as a safeguarding problem -- external checks that block non-compliant agent actions. We argue that policy adherence is a broader problem: real workflows unfold across many turns, require explicit user confirmation and prerequisite reads, and hinge on the content of the dialogue rather than on any single argument value. Meeting this bar requires (i) full conversation context, (ii) self-reasoning over the policy and the current dialogue, and (iii) conversation-specific remediation that guides the agent's next turn -- three capabilities that prior safeguard work has often underestimated. We introduce POLICYGUARD, a sub-agent verifier that shares the agent's view of the dialogue, reasons over the policy in context, and provides actionable feedback for the agent's next turn. On tau^2-BENCH airline across three vendors (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro) with four trials per setting, POLICYGUARD improves PASS4 by +12.0 / +6.0 / +12.0 pp. Per-call analyses show POLICYGUARD achieves higher policy-violation recall while blocking roughly half as often as argument-level guards.

</details>


### [347] [SurgVLA-Bench: Towards Evaluating Vision-Language-Action Models for Laparoscopic Surgical Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29247)
*Jiashuo Sun,Yue He,Wenxuan Liu,Tao Mao,Jiazheng Wang,Xiang Chen,Min Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SurgVLA-Bench is the first benchmark for evaluating Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in laparoscopic surgical robotics, using SurRoL simulation to assess autoregressive and flow matching models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the prevalence of VLA benchmarks for general robotics, standardized evaluation platforms specifically designed for surgical contexts remain absent.

Method: Leveraging the SurRoL simulation platform, the authors construct a hierarchical task taxonomy ranging from atomic actions to complete surgical procedures, complemented by a multi-dimensional evaluation framework assessing action accuracy and semantic consistency. They evaluate autoregressive models (OpenVLA) and flow matching models (π₀, π₀.₅, SmolVLA).

Result: Autoregressive models excel in semantic understanding, while flow matching models achieve higher task precision but face generalization trade-offs. Even the best models remain unsatisfactory due to constrained endoscopic field of view, restricted viewing angles, and frequent occlusions.

Conclusion: SurgVLA-Bench fills a critical gap in surgical VLA evaluation; current VLA models are still far from satisfactory in surgical settings, with physical bottlenecks (limited FOV, occlusions) persisting as fundamental challenges.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models represent a promising direction for embodied intelligence in surgical robotics. Despite the prevalence of VLA benchmarks for general robotics, standardized evaluation platforms specifically designed for surgical contexts remain absent. To address this limitation, we present SurgVLA-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating VLA models in laparoscopic surgical robotics. Leveraging the SurRoL simulation platform, we construct a hierarchical task taxonomy ranging from atomic actions to complete surgical procedures, complemented by a multi-dimensional evaluation framework assessing action accuracy and semantic consistency. We then systematically evaluate two representative paradigms, including autoregressive models such as OpenVLA, and flow matching models such as $π_{0}$, $π_{0.5}$, and SmolVLA. Our experiments show that autoregressive models tend to excel in semantic understanding, while flow matching models often achieve higher task precision but may face generalization trade-offs. However, even the best-performing models remain far from satisfactory, as the constrained endoscopic field of view, restricted viewing angles, and frequent occlusions persist as fundamental physical bottlenecks. The code and data are available at https://github.com/VCL-HNU/SurgVLA

</details>


### [348] [When Summaries Distort Decisions: Information Fidelity in LLM-Compressed Financial Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29251)
*Hoyoung Lee,Suhwan Park,Seunghan Lee,Jun Seo,Jaehoon Lee,Sungdong Yoo,Minjae Kim,CheolWon Na,Zhangyang Wang,Zach Golkhou,Minkyu Kim,Sotirios Sabanis,Alejandro Lopez-Lira,Dhagash Mehta,Soonyoung Lee,Chanyeol Choi,Wonbin Ahn,Yongjae Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based compression of financial documents can produce fluent outputs that nonetheless alter investment decisions, a problem framed as information fidelity loss, which is mitigated by Agentic Context Compression that audits multiple compression candidates for decision-altering disagreements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial decision-makers face information overload and need context compression, but LLM-based compression can alter the investment judgments supported by original sources, introducing fidelity loss that may amplify across decision processes.

Method: The paper identifies two diagnostic patterns of fidelity loss: decontextualization (retaining evidence while stripping caveats) and model dependency (different compressors yielding different views). It proposes Agentic Context Compression, which generates multiple candidate compressions and audits their disagreements against the original source.

Result: LLM-based compression produces fluent, factually plausible compressed contexts that nevertheless alter downstream decisions across financial filings and earnings-call transcripts.

Conclusion: Financial compression should be evaluated not only by efficiency or factuality, but also by its ability to preserve decision-relevant context.

Abstract: Financial decision-makers face more information than they can directly inspect, making context compression necessary. Yet when large language models (LLMs) compress financial source material, they can alter the investment judgment supported by the original source. We frame this problem as information fidelity: compression loses fidelity when it changes the decision induced by the source. In agentic systems, such losses may recur across intermediate steps and amplify throughout the decision process. Across financial filings and earnings-call transcripts, we find that LLM-based compression can produce fluent and factually plausible compressed contexts that nevertheless alter downstream decisions. We analyze two diagnostic patterns associated with fidelity loss: decontextualization, where salient evidence is retained but separated from the caveats and contextual qualifiers needed for correct interpretation, and model dependency, where different compressors expose different views of the same source. We then propose Agentic Context Compression, which generates multiple candidate compressions and audits their disagreements against the original source. Our results suggest that financial compression should be evaluated not only by efficiency or factuality, but also by its ability to preserve decision-relevant context.

</details>


### [349] [The Complexity Ceiling Benchmark: A Multi-Domain Evaluation of Sequential Reasoning Under Depth Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29278)
*Shubh Chapra,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Yash Sinha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CCB is a benchmark that measures how LLM reasoning degrades with increasing sequential steps across three task regimes, revealing geometric per-step decay with varying domain ceilings, flawed reasoning in 14.5% of correct answers, and that first-divergence step k* predicts accuracy better than model size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks conflate semantic difficulty with sequential depth when evaluating LLM reasoning. The authors aim to isolate the effect of reasoning depth N (number of sequential steps) while keeping semantic content fixed, to rigorously measure how reasoning decays as step count increases.

Method: CCB varies only depth N∈{5,...,50} across three structurally distinct regimes: grounded spatial state-tracking, abstract symbolic pointer manipulation, and transitive relational inference. 6,000 trials over five frontier and open-weight LLMs. A trace-level metric (TFBC) identifies flawed-but-correct reasoning. Forced verbose state-tracking tested via McNemar test. Geometric decay model fitted per task family.

Result: Geometric per-step decay with widely separated domain ceilings: strong models retain pd>0.92 across N=50 on first two regimes, but collapse by N=5 on transitive relational inference (best H0.5~4.7 steps despite pd=0.863). 14.5% of correct answers use incorrect intermediate reasoning. Forced verbosity does not shift ceiling (McNemar p=1.000). First divergence step k* predicts accuracy better than parameter count.

Conclusion: CCB and the geometric decay model reduce a model's long-horizon reasoning profile to one interpretable number per task family. The sharp collapse on transitive relational inference reveals a fundamental bottleneck not explained by model scale or verbosity, and the prevalence of flawed-but-correct traces cautions against relying solely on final-answer metrics.

Abstract: We introduce the Complexity Ceiling Benchmark (CCB), a controlled evaluation of how language-model reasoning decays as the number of required sequential steps grows. CCB fixes the semantic content of a task and varies only its depth N in {5,...,50} across three structurally distinct regimes: grounded spatial state-tracking, abstract symbolic pointer manipulation, and transitive relational inference. Across 6,000 trials over five frontier and open-weight LLMs we find a consistent pattern of geometric per-step decay with widely separated domain ceilings: on the first two regimes the strongest models retain pd>0.92 across N=50; on the third every model collapses by N=5, with the best model's 50%-success horizon at H0.5~4.7 steps despite pd=0.863. A trace-level metric (TFBC) shows that 14.5% of correct answers across the benchmark are reached via incorrect intermediate reasoning. Forced verbose state-tracking does not move the ceiling (McNemar p=1.000), and the mean step at which reasoning first diverges, k*, predicts within-domain accuracy better than parameter count. CCB and the geometric decay model together reduce a model's long-horizon reasoning profile to one interpretable number per task family.

</details>


### [350] [Process Advantage Signal Shaping: A Paradigm-Agnostic Middleware for Process-Supervised RL in LLM Reasoners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29296)
*Chao Wang,Hongtao Tian,Tao Yang,Yunsheng Shi,Ting Yao,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PASS (Process Advantage Signal Shaping) is a middleware that fixes three structural pathologies in GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) when using dense process supervision for LLM reasoning. It delivers consistent gains across multiple domains and signal paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO with process supervision suffers from three pathologies: channel contamination (mixing process, outcome, and format streams at group standardization), resolution mismatch (granularity mismatch between process signals and logical decisions), and a cumulative trap (length inflation or truncated exploration from return-to-go computation).

Method: PASS introduces three components: (1) Advantage Fusion — standardizes process, outcome, and format streams independently within each group; (2) Chunk-by-Value — derives value-homogeneous chunks from the process signal and broadcasts credit within each chunk; (3) Divide-Length — converts cumulative objective into an average-value-density score.

Result: PASS delivers consistent pass@1 improvement over GRPO baselines across two domains (mathematical reasoning, multi-hop QA), two process-signal paradigms (learned PRM, on-policy-distillation KL signal), and two group-standardization operators.

Conclusion: PASS effectively addresses the structural pathologies in GRPO with process supervision, providing a compact and generalizable middleware solution that consistently improves performance across different settings.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is a default recipe for process-supervised reinforcement learning of LLM reasoners, and dense process supervision -- via learned process reward models (PRMs) or on-policy-distillation KL signals -- is a common way to densify its otherwise weak outcome reward. Layering such a step-level signal on top of GRPO's group-standardized advantage, however, exposes three structural pathologies: \emph{channel contamination} between the pooled process, outcome, and format streams at group standardization; \emph{resolution mismatch} between the granularity of the process signal and the granularity of the logical decisions being credited; and a \emph{cumulative trap} by which GRPO's return-to-go sum surfaces either length inflation or truncated exploration depending on the sign regime of the signal. We propose \textbf{PASS} (\emph{Process Advantage Signal Shaping}), a compact middleware that sits between any scalar step-level process signal and GRPO's clipped surrogate and addresses the three pathologies in turn: \emph{Advantage Fusion} standardizes the three streams independently within each group, \emph{Chunk-by-Value} derives value-homogeneous chunks from the signal itself and broadcasts credit within each chunk, and \emph{Divide-Length} converts the cumulative objective into an average-value-density score. We validate PASS across two domains and two process-signal paradigms -- a learned PRM on mathematical reasoning and an on-policy-distillation KL signal (with a generalized variant) on multi-hop question answering -- and under two group-standardization operators. In every regime PASS delivers a consistent pass@1 gain over the corresponding GRPO baseline.

</details>


### [351] [Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29315)
*Abhranil Chandra,Sankaran Vaidyanathan,Utsav Dhanuka,Varun Gandhi,Scott Niekum*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HExA is a training-free framework that enables LLM agents to learn through active experimentation in novel domains, achieving 77% success on the Interphyre benchmark compared to 2% for baseline Claude Sonnet 4.6.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle in novel domains and with sophisticated queries that cannot be answered from prior parametric knowledge, retrieval, or search alone. Knowledge of physics laws does not enable LLMs to handle complex physical systems without active experimentation.

Method: HExA (Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents) is an in-context self-improvement framework that iteratively designs query-relevant experiments, learns a reusable library of composable skills from experience, and integrates experimental evidence to answer queries or take actions. It is training-free, black-box compatible, and requires no external supervision.

Result: Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves only 2% success on Interphyre, while HExA improves the same model to up to 77% success. HExA also improves open-weight models and outperforms ReAct and Reflexion baselines. Transferred skills from easier levels achieve 44% success without active experimentation.

Conclusion: Learning through active experimentation helps agents discover useful knowledge, acquire reusable skills, and make efficient progress on novel long-horizon tasks in complex physical systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to take actions in the real world and support human decision-making, yet most agents rely on parametric knowledge, fixed post-training data, retrieval, or search. This paradigm breaks down in novel domains and for sophisticated queries that cannot be answered from prior knowledge alone. Knowing the laws of physics, for instance, does not by itself enable LLMs to answer queries or complete long-horizon tasks in a complex physical system. To address this, we introduce Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents (HExA), an in-context self-improvement framework to learn from active experimentation. HExA iteratively designs and refines query-relevant experiments, learns a reusable library of composable skills from experience, and integrates experimental evidence to answer queries or take actions. HExA is training-free, compatible with any black-box model, and does not require external supervision, oracles, or offline data. To evaluate active experimentation, we introduce Interphyre, a tool-calling benchmark built on the PHYRE 2D procedural physics environment, where agents propose interventions and test hypotheses through simulation APIs. Experiments show that current LLM agents struggle in these settings, especially on the hardest levels of Interphyre. Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves only 2% success, while HExA improves the same model to up to 77% success. HExA also improves open-weight models and outperforms agentic baselines such as ReAct and Reflexion. Moreover, using only skills learned from easier levels and transferred without active experimentation, HExA achieves 44% success, demonstrating the reusability and generalization of its learned skills. Overall, HExA shows that learning through active experimentation can help agents discover useful knowledge, acquire reusable skills, and make efficient progress on novel long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [352] [PHF: Privileged Hidden Flow for On-Policy Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29340)
*Yuhan Li,Mingxu Zhang,Dazhong Shen,Ying Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Privileged Hidden Flow (PHF) extends on-policy self-distillation for reasoning models by distilling hidden-state trajectory geometry (transition directions and local structures) from a privileged teacher, improving performance over OPSD baselines by ~1.5-2.2 points across Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, and 8B models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OPSD methods only supervise the output token distribution, lacking direct supervision on the internal computation (hidden states) that produces that distribution, leaving privileged context underutilized.

Method: PHF aligns token-to-token transition directions and trajectory geometry between student and privileged teacher hidden states over selected generated positions, using an all-layer recipe with adjacent-layer relations, without pointwise hidden-state imitation.

Result: PHF improves Average@12 by +2.2, +1.5, and +1.7 points on Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, and 8B respectively under the same 100-step training schedule.

Conclusion: PHF provides a compact hidden-flow extension to OPSD that effectively leverages privileged hidden-state information, with invariance properties to shared trajectory offsets and orthogonal transformations, outperforming pointwise matching and other ablation variants.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) trains a reasoning model on rollouts sampled from its own policy by matching a privileged teacher that also sees verified reference solutions. Existing OPSD objectives supervise only the output distribution, so privileged context affects training through a token-level divergence without directly supervising the internal computation that produced that distribution. We propose Privileged Hidden Flow (PHF), which additionally distills how a privileged teacher's hidden states move along the same rollout. Rather than forcing each student hidden vector to match the teacher vector at the same token position, PHF aligns token-to-token transition directions and trajectory geometry over selected generated positions. The all-layer recipe also includes an adjacent-layer relation computed from these same transitions, without pointwise hidden-state imitation. Under the same 100-step training schedule, PHF improves the Average@12 aggregate over our reproduced OPSD baseline on Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, and 8B, with observed gains of about +2.2, +1.5, and +1.7 points. The transport objective is exactly invariant to shared trajectory offsets; its local geometry term is also invariant to orthogonal transformations of transition directions. Ablations distinguish the fixed PHF recipe from pointwise hidden-state matching, single-channel transition losses, and layer-subset choices, supporting PHF as a compact hidden-flow extension to OPSD.

</details>


### [353] [When LLMs Develop Languages: Symbolic Communication for Efficient Multi-Agent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29354)
*Zhengqi Pei,Qingming Huang,Shuhui Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CLSR is a test-time framework where multiple LLM agents invent and evolve compact symbolic languages (LSFs) to replace verbose Chain-of-Thought reasoning, with a router adaptively selecting or composing these languages per query, achieving 3-6x token reduction while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-Thought reasoning, while effective, produces long natural-language rationales that are inefficient for machine reasoning. The authors aim to replace verbose CoT with compact, reusable symbolic protocols that reduce token cost without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Multiple LLM agents autonomously invent, evolve, and share compact Language Symbolism Frameworks (LSFs)—each a symbolic protocol with compact symbols, usage rules, and a message-passing contract. An evolutionary loop driven by correctness and token cost refines LSFs. At inference, a latent-free router adaptively selects a single LSF, ensembles multiple LSFs, or executes multi-round LSF composition per query based on difficulty.

Result: CLSR reduces latency-oriented generated token completion by 3-6x compared to standard CoT across challenging benchmarks while maintaining accuracy. An information-theoretic lower bound on token cost under arbitrary symbolism is derived.

Conclusion: Compact symbolic languages can effectively replace verbose natural-language reasoning chains, offering a principled accuracy-token trade-off. Multi-round LSF protocols conditionally subsume program-execution pipelines under an interpreter-realizability premise.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) improves large language models (LLMs) on difficult reasoning tasks, but it often incurs long natural-language rationales that are poorly aligned with efficient machine reasoning. We propose Communicative Language Symbolism Routing (CLSR), a test-time framework in which multiple LLM agents autonomously invent, evolve, and share compact Language Symbolism Frameworks (LSFs), while a latent-free router adaptively selects and composes these languages per query to optimize the accuracy-token trade-off. Unlike prompt optimization that refines surface instructions, CLSR treats each LSF as a reusable symbolic protocol with compact symbols, usage rules, and a message-passing contract, and improves it through an evolutionary loop driven by correctness and token cost. At inference time, the router may invoke a single low-cost LSF call, ensemble multiple LSFs, or execute a multi-round LSF composition protocol on harder queries. Across challenging benchmarks, CLSR reduces latency-oriented generated token completion by $3\sim 6\times$ compared to standard CoT while maintaining accuracy. We further derive an information-theoretic lower bound on token cost under arbitrary symbolism and show that, under an interpreter-realizability premise, multi-round LSF protocols conditionally subsume program-execution pipelines. Code is publicly available (https://github.com/pzqpzq/LSF_MDia).

</details>


### [354] [Diagnosing and Repairing Factual Errors in RAG under Budget Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29377)
*Soroush Hashemifar,Havva Alizadeh Noughabi,Fattane Zarrinkalam,Ali Dehghantanha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: D2R-RAG is a model-agnostic, resource-aware framework that diagnoses failures in RAG systems from observable signals (query, evidence, response) and applies lightweight corrective actions under latency/VRAM constraints, improving reliability and accuracy-efficiency trade-offs on FEVER and HotpotQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG systems are fragile in real-world deployments due to missing/weak evidence and unfaithful generation. Current approaches rely on fine-tuning, internal model signals, or resource-insensitive strategies, limiting their practicality in black-box and budget-constrained settings.

Method: D2R-RAG combines lightweight failure diagnosis (deriving interpretable failure signatures from observable signals in query, retrieved evidence, and generated response) with adaptive repair (selecting corrective actions under explicit latency and VRAM constraints), without requiring fine-tuning or internal model access.

Result: On FEVER and HotpotQA benchmarks, D2R-RAG improves reliability over recent baselines and achieves better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across multiple compute budgets.

Conclusion: D2R-RAG demonstrates that effective RAG failure mitigation can be achieved without fine-tuning or privileged model access, using lightweight diagnosis and resource-aware repair strategies that adapt to computational constraints.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves the factuality of large language models by grounding responses in external evidence, yet real-world deployments remain fragile. Failures often stem from missing or weakly relevant evidence, as well as from generation that does not faithfully reflect the retrieved context. Many existing approaches rely on fine-tuning, privileged access to internal model signals, or resource-insensitive escalation strategies, which limits their practicality in black-box and budget-constrained settings. We propose D2R-RAG (Diagnose-to-Repair RAG), a model-agnostic and resource-aware framework that combines lightweight failure diagnosis with adaptive repair. D2R-RAG derives interpretable failure signatures from observable signals in the query, retrieved evidence, and generated response, and then selects from a small set of corrective actions under explicit latency and VRAM constraints. Experiments on FEVER and HotpotQA show that D2R-RAG improves reliability over recent baselines and achieves better accuracy--efficiency trade-offs across multiple compute budgets. The code is available at https://github.com/CyberScienceLab/D2R-RAG/.

</details>


### [355] [LLM-Guided Planning for Multi-hop Reasoning over Multimodal Nuclear Regulatory Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29399)
*Mingyu Jeon,Bokyeong Kim,Suwan Cho,Jae Young Suh,Yonggyun Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An LLM agent with dynamic KG and planning achieves 81.5% accuracy on nuclear regulatory QA (200-question FSAR benchmark), outperforming non-planning baselines by +38pp, matching RAPTOR without offline indexing, with 0.04% edge violations as auditable traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nuclear regulatory document review requires multi-hop reasoning across tens of thousands of pages, where judgments depend on evidence assembled across multiple chapters. Existing methods lack structured planning and state tracking for this complex evidence-gathering task.

Method: An LLM-based agent operates over a vectorless document tree using browse, read, and search tools, with a dynamic knowledge graph (KG) as state. The agent plans which document fragment to inspect next based on evidence collected so far, stopping when sufficient evidence is gathered.

Result: 81.5% accuracy (RAGAS Faithfulness 0.93) on 200-question NuScale FSAR benchmark. +38.0pp over PageIndex (43.5%, p<0.001). Outperforms LightRAG (73.0%, p<0.05), HippoRAG (70.5%, p<0.01), GraphRAG (49.5%, p<0.001). Matches RAPTOR (75.5%, p=0.11) without offline indexing. Edge inference adds 2.8x cost with no accuracy gain; 0.04% edges flagged as Violates.

Conclusion: Planning is the dominant performance factor for nuclear regulatory document QA. The dynamic KG-based agent with state-conditioned action selection significantly outperforms non-planning baselines. Edge inference is retained as a traceability module despite cost overhead, as violations (0.04%) provide auditable annotations for human review.

Abstract: Reviewing nuclear regulatory documents requires multi-hop reasoning across tens of thousands of pages, where judgments depend on evidence assembled across multiple chapters. We frame this task as planning: an LLM-based agent observes the evidence collected so far, picks the next document fragment to inspect, and stops when the evidence is sufficient. The agent operates over a vectorless document tree using browse, read, and search tools, and maintains a dynamic knowledge graph (KG) as state. On a 200-question benchmark over NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents, the system reaches 81.5% accuracy with a RAGAS Faithfulness of 0.93. The dominant performance factor is planning: against PageIndex, which uses the same document tree without state-conditioned action selection, the gap is +38.0pp (43.5% to 81.5%, p<0.001). The system also outperforms LightRAG (73.0%, p<0.05), HippoRAG (70.5%, p<0.01), and GraphRAG (49.5%, p<0.001), and matches RAPTOR (75.5%, p=0.11) without offline indexing. Edge inference adds 2.8x cost without raising accuracy; we retain it as a traceability module. Of 7,391 inferred edges, 3 Violates edges (0.04%) flag scope boundaries (Q058) and partial conformance (Q176) as typed annotations that a human reviewer can audit.

</details>


### [356] [FADE: Mitigating Hallucinations by Reducing Language-Prior Dominance in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29431)
*Yichen Guo,Kai Tang,Fenglai Lin,Yiding Sun,Dongshuo Zhang,Wenya Wang,Lin William Cong,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FADE is a training-free decoding method that attenuates FFN outputs in critical layers to reduce language-prior dominance and mitigate hallucination in LVLMs, validated across multiple models and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs hallucinate due to language priors overriding visual evidence; the mechanistic origin of this dominance was unexplored.

Method: Analyzes information flow per transformer layer, finding attention modules aggregate visual evidence while FFN modules at critical layers inject language priors. Proposes FADE, which attenuates FFN outputs during decoding without additional training.

Result: FADE effectively reduces hallucinations on POPE, CHAIR, and MME benchmarks across LLaVA-1.5, mPLUG-Owl2, and InstructBLIP, while preserving inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Targeting FFN modules at critical layers is a viable training-free approach to mitigate language-prior-driven hallucinations in LVLMs.

Abstract: Despite the impressive capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), they remain susceptible to hallucination, generating content inconsistent with the input image. Recent studies attribute this to the dominance of language priors over visual inputs and employ contrastive decoding methods to mitigate this dominance, but the mechanistic origin remains unexplored. We investigate the information flow through each transformer layer and find that attention modules consistently aggregate visual evidence, while FFN modules at critical layers act as the source of language priors. These priors can override visual evidence, causing correct predictions in intermediate layers to drift toward incorrect outputs. Based on this insight, we propose FADE (FFN Attenuation for DEcoding), a training-free method that attenuates FFN outputs to reduce language-prior dominance. Evaluations on POPE, CHAIR, and MME benchmarks across LLaVA-1.5, mPLUG-Owl2, and InstructBLIP show that FADE effectively mitigates hallucinations while preserving inference efficiency.

</details>


### [357] [How Much Due Diligence Before You Bid? Learning in Intractable Takeover Auctions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29457)
*Zain Naboulsi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper uses a self-play computational model to study how much due diligence is optimal in competitive bidding contests, finding that modest and finite diligence is optimal, and that simple self-play AI methods rival specialized algorithms when the game is too complex to solve exactly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how much costly due diligence is worth acquiring when two bidders compete for the same target under uncertainty, and to test whether simple self-play AI methods can replace specialized algorithms for analyzing such complex strategic interactions.

Method: The authors build a simple computer model of a bidding contest with due diligence, using self-play reinforcement learning (like game engines learning chess) to train bidding strategies. They vary the number of private information pieces to control problem complexity, and compare simple self-play methods against specialized exact algorithms across different problem sizes.

Result: Optimal due diligence is modest and finite; it decreases as diligence gets more expensive and when both sides invest in it (competition erodes information value). Simple self-play methods outperform other self-learning approaches and are competitive with exact methods when the game is too large to solve outright, which is the regime of real-world deals.

Conclusion: The paper provides a cheap, reproducible framework for studying deal-making under uncertainty, a concrete model-based answer about optimal due diligence, and evidence that lightweight general-purpose AI suffices for complex bidding problems where exact solutions are infeasible.

Abstract: When two companies bid to buy the same target, no one knows exactly what the target is worth. Each bidder pays for due diligence: costly, imperfect homework that sharpens its own private estimate before it bids. How much of that homework is worth buying? We build a simple computer model of the bidding contest and let it teach itself to bid well by playing against itself, the way a game engine learns chess. The economic question, how much diligence pays for itself, and the computational question, when the contest becomes too complex to solve exactly, are both controlled by a single thing: how many pieces of private information a bidder carries. Our main finding is that the right amount of diligence is modest and finite. It falls as diligence gets more expensive, and it falls further when both sides are doing their homework, because competition erodes the value of knowing more. We also test a recent claim from AI research: that simple, general self-play methods can rival the specialized, expensive algorithms usually built for games like these. Running on an ordinary laptop with no costly frontier AI, we find the simple methods are the best of the self-learning approaches, though purpose-built exact methods still win whenever the game is small enough to solve outright. The simple methods earn their keep only once the game grows too large to solve exactly, which is the regime real deals live in, and there we show they still find strong bidding strategies. The contribution is threefold: a cheap, reproducible way to study deal-making under uncertainty; a concrete, model-based answer to how much due diligence is worth buying; and evidence about when lightweight, general-purpose AI is good enough to replace specialized methods. We release all the games, code, and experiments.

</details>


### [358] [Agent-Computer Observation Interfaces Enable Dynamic Computer Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29472)
*Bojie Li,Noah Shi*

Main category: cs.AI
