Existing LLM-based agents suffer from three systematic failures: scene blindness from raw tile coordinates, context overflow and domain coupling from monolithic state dumps, and shallow cross-game learning that treats each episode in isolation. We present SAGA, an LLM multi-agent framework with three mechanisms each directly targeting one class of failure: (i) a Map-Semantic Scene Graph that encodes typed spatial relations among game entities into per-unit natural-language context, resolving spatial blindness without global token inflation; (ii) a Tool-Augmented Planner that pulls fine-grained domain state on demand and dispatches per-domain directives to dedicated specialist controllers, eliminating context overflow, domain coupling, and mechanical constraint violations; and (iii) a Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop that combines periodic within-game goal generation with structured cross-game causal post-mortem, enabling principled strategic evolution without manual reward engineering. Evaluated on FreeCiv, SAGA attains the highest mean civilization score -- the environment's sole sparse objective reward -- with lower variance than the two strongest baselines, and is the only method that significantly surpasses every baseline on infrastructure construction, the resource axis most readily sacrificed under multi-objective conflict. It outscores the two strongest baselines in most head-to-head games while cutting output tokens (the dominant decoding cost) by 27%. Equipped with the cross-game evolution module, SAGA reaches the highest end-of-chain score across five successive episodes. Ablation studies confirm that each architectural component contributes independently to this advantage.

</details>


### [383] [First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29972)
*Luca Boscarato,Ivan Donadello,Alessandro Artale,Marco Montali,Fabrizio Maria Maggi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FOT-LTN extends Logic Tensor Networks with first-order linear temporal logic to handle objects whose properties and relations change over time, outperforming purely neural methods on temporal knowledge graph completion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic AI methods focus on static knowledge without temporal dimensions; temporal neuro-symbolic works are under-explored and mainly limited to time-interval logic or propositional linear temporal logic. There is a lack of models studying linear temporal logics with predicates for objects whose properties and relations change over time.

Method: FOT-LTN extends Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) by incorporating first-order linear temporal logic (FOLTL) syntax with fuzzy, real-valued semantics, supporting both temporal operators and quantifiers while remaining fully differentiable.

Result: Evaluated on temporal knowledge graph completion using two synthetic datasets, FOT-LTN demonstrated better performance compared to dedicated purely neural methods.

Conclusion: FOT-LTN successfully bridges the gap in temporal neuro-symbolic AI by enabling reasoning about objects with time-varying properties and relations, achieving superior results on temporal knowledge graph completion.

Abstract: Most of the existing neuro-symbolic AI methods focus on the scenario of static knowledge where objects do not change according to a temporal dimension. Temporal neuro-symbolic works are still under explored and are mainly developed for time-interval logic or propositional linear temporal logic. There is a lack of models studying linear temporal logics with predicates that deal with objects whose properties and relations change through the time. We present First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks (FOT-LTN) that is an extension of Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) that fills this gap by considering a linear-temporal dimension. In particular, FOT-LTN joins the syntax of First-Order Linear Temporal Logic with the fuzzy (and real-valued) semantics of LTN obtaining a framework that supports both temporal operators and quantifiers and is totally differentiable. A first evaluation regards a temporal knowledge graph completion task on two synthetic datasets showing better performance of FOT-LTN with respect to dedicated (purely neural) methods.

</details>


### [384] [Exploration and Online Transfer with Behavioral Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29980)
*Louis Bagot,Mathieu Lefort,Laëtitia Matignon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper extends zero-shot RL to online transfer settings where rewards are only obtained through environment interactions, using a Behavioral Foundation Model (BFM) as a policy generator in a bandit-like exploration-exploitation framework with an UCB-inspired approach leveraging eigenvalue minimization of an uncertainty matrix.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current zero-shot RL assumes offline reward information via state-reward pairs, but real-world scenarios often involve black-box rewards (e.g., user feedback) requiring online trial-and-error. This misalignment motivates the need for an online transfer framework in zero-shot RL.

Method: Frames online transfer as a bandit exploration-exploitation problem: at each step, a bandit algorithm recommends a policy from the BFM, which is executed to obtain reward and state; repeats until convergence. For linear reward approximation, derives an UCB-style formulation where exploration is achieved by minimizing eigenvalues of an uncertainty matrix.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluations on a simple environment validate the proposed framework for online zero-shot RL transfer.

Conclusion: The proposed bandit-based approach enables BFMs to perform zero-shot transfer in online settings, aligning with traditional RL's trial-and-error paradigm, with exploration guided by uncertainty matrix eigenvalue minimization.

Abstract: Zero-shot Transfer in Reinforcement Learning (RL) aims to train an agent that can generate optimal policies for any reward function, without additional learning at transfer time, while training only on reward-free trajectories. For their generality over tasks, such models are sometimes called ``Behavioral Foundation Models'' (BFMs). While they have shown strong performances and improvements in recent years, the current framework and algorithms still assume that, during the transfer phase, the agent is informed offline about the reward (the task to solve) through a dataset of state-reward pairs, which it uses to pick the best policy to deploy. However, in practice if the reward is a black-box (e.g. direct user feedback), it is not possible to generate such a dataset: it is necessary to observe the reward through interactions with the environment. In other words, the current framework of offline transfer is not aligned with the traditional RL setting of online learning through trial-and-error, which requires exploration in order to find rewards. This paper proposes to tackle this new online transfer in zero-shot RL, with the key insight that the BFM itself can be used to generate exploration policies. We show that it is possible to frame this online learning problem in terms of a bandit-like exploration-exploitation problem. More precisely, at each step the bandit algorithm recommends a policy, the BFM executes it in the environment, which yields a reward and a new state; we repeat the process until we converge to the optimal policy. In the popular context of linear reward approximation, we derive a formulation inspired by Upper Confidence Bound and show that exploration can be achieved through the minimization of the eigenvalues of an uncertainty matrix. We evaluate qualitatively and quantitatively our framework on a simple environment to validate the concept of our method.

</details>


### [385] [Be Faithful When Response: Returning Fluent and Grounded Answers for Vision-Language Models Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29984)
*Peng,Lee,Yin Zhang,Yanglin Zhang,Haonan Wu,Zishan Liu,Ruoxi Zang,Xin Zhu,Jiayin Zheng,Jian Yao,Zefeng Ji,Fei Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Faithful Warm-Start (FWS), a strategy that pre-trains Vision-Language Models on causally grounded visual reasoning data before applying Reinforcement Learning, which improves answer accuracy, stabilizes training, and reduces visually unsupported reasoning in multimodal reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directly applying RL to VLM multimodal reasoning leads to instability due to exploitation of language priors, neglect of visual evidence, and generation of fluent but visually ungrounded reasoning traces. The authors aim to steer the policy toward visually faithful reasoning before RL optimization.

Method: (1) Curates FaithfulQA dataset from six VQA benchmarks by selecting samples with explicit vision-language causal relationships. (2) Uses a VLM-based judge to purify the dataset for strong causal consistency and visual faithfulness. (3) Applies this warm-start stage before subsequent RL optimization under sparse answer-level rewards.

Result: The faithful warm-start supervision improves answer accuracy, stabilizes RL training, and reduces visually unsupported reasoning traces.

Conclusion: Warm-starting VLMs with causally grounded vision-language data before RL optimization effectively addresses the instability issues of direct RL on multimodal reasoning, leading to more visually faithful and accurate reasoning.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is an important paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). However, directly applying RL to rollout multimodal reasoning can lead to instability, due to the exploitation of language priors, the neglect of visual evidence, and the generation of reasoning traces that are fluent yet not visually grounded. The question arises: Can initially steer the policy toward visually faithful reasoning regime before applying reinforcement learning? To this end, we propose a Faithful Warm-Start (FWS) strategy that first curates samples with explicit vision-language causal relationships from six general VQA benchmarks to construct the FaithfulQA dataset, where each of the image-question pairs gains a certain degree of visual observations, question requirements, commonsense knowledge, domain knowledge, and the final answer. Subsequently, a VLM-based judge is employed to further purify the dataset, ensuring strong causal consistency and visual faithfulness. This warm-start stage equips the model with the capability to understand causally grounded vision-language patterns before subsequent RL optimization under sparse answer-level rewards. Experimental results show that such faithful supervision improves answer accuracy, stabilizes RL training, and reduces visually unsupported reasoning.

</details>


### [386] [AlgoSkill: Learning to Design Algorithms by Scheduling Human-Like Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29999)
*Xinyuan Song,Zekun Cai,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AlgoSkill treats algorithm design as sequential decision-making using typed algorithmic skills (abstraction, constraint analysis, etc.) guided by a learned scheduler and MCTS with verification feedback, outperforming direct generation and self-refinement methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM methods for algorithm design rely on direct generation or generic self-refinement, leaving critical steps like problem structure identification, constraint analysis, and correctness checking implicit. The authors aim to make these steps explicit and structured.

Method: AlgoSkill models algorithm design as sequential decision-making over a typed library of algorithmic skills. A learned scheduler proposes skills from the current design state, and an MCTS controller explores skill sequences using verification feedback from compilation, testing, stress testing, and complexity analysis.

Result: AlgoSkill improves over direct LLM generation, chain-of-thought prompting, self-refinement, and MCTS without typed skills on competitive programming and combinatorial optimization benchmarks. Ablations confirm that typed skills, verification-based repair, and search-based scheduling each contribute to performance.

Conclusion: Automatic algorithm design is better treated as verification-guided skill scheduling rather than one-shot code generation.

Abstract: Designing an algorithm from a natural-language problem statement requires identifying the problem structure, reading constraints, choosing a suitable paradigm, checking correctness, and refining complexity. Existing large language model (LLM) methods often rely on direct generation or generic self-refinement, leaving these steps implicit. We propose AlgoSkill, which models algorithm design as sequential decision-making over a typed library of algorithmic skills, including abstraction, constraint analysis, state design, data-structure selection, proof checking, counterexample construction, and complexity refinement. A learned scheduler proposes skills from the current design state, while a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) controller explores skill sequences using verification feedback from compilation, testing, stress testing, and complexity analysis. Experiments on competitive programming and combinatorial optimization benchmarks show that AlgoSkill improves over direct LLM generation, chain-of-thought prompting, self-refinement, and MCTS without typed skills. Ablations show that typed skills, verification-based repair, and search-based scheduling each contribute to performance. These results support treating automatic algorithm design as verification-guided skill scheduling rather than one-shot code generation.

</details>


### [387] [ACPO: Agent-Chained Policy Optimization for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30072)
*Daiki E. Matsunaga,Junho Na,Tri Wahyu Guntara,Scott Sanner,Pascal Poupart,Jongmin Lee,Kee-Eung Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACPO enables exact decentralized decomposition of joint policy gradients in MARL by using a serialized belief-based coordination mechanism, outperforming baselines on cooperative tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MARL methods under CTDE either rely on value decomposition assumptions (lacking general joint-improvement guarantees) or alternating best-response updates (converging to suboptimal Nash Equilibria). The goal is to compute joint policy gradients directly with exact decentralized decomposition.

Method: Proves joint policy gradient admits exact decentralized decomposition into per-agent terms using per-agent score functions and decentralized critics. Introduces Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO) with a serialized view: agents commit actions sequentially, each conditioning on a belief over preceding actions, which ties independent updates into a joint gradient step.

Result: ACPO outperforms strong baselines on Multi-Robot Warehouse, SMACv2, and MA-MuJoCo, with performance gap widening as the number of agents increases.

Conclusion: Exact decentralized joint policy gradient computation is feasible via serialized belief-based coordination, enabling scalable and guaranteed joint-improvement in cooperative MARL without restrictive assumptions.

Abstract: Cooperative tasks in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) require agents to collectively maximize a shared return. Under the Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) paradigm, policy gradients have remained difficult to compute directly. Prior methods largely follow two approaches: independent factorized updates with centralized critics, which lack general joint-improvement guarantees without value decomposition assumptions, or alternating best-response updates, which can converge to suboptimal Nash Equilibria. In this paper, we show the joint policy gradient admits an exact decentralized decomposition of per-agent terms, each formed from per-agent score functions and decentralized critics. Based on this decomposition, we develop Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO), where actors are trained independently, with their updates together constituting a single step on the joint policy gradient. Central to this result is a serialized view of the simultaneous joint decision in which agents commit actions one at a time, each conditioning on a belief over preceding actions. The belief acts as the coordination mechanism which ties the independent per-agent updates into a joint gradient step. We evaluate ACPO on Multi-Robot Warehouse, SMACv2, and MA-MuJoCo, where it outperforms strong baselines, with the gap widening as the number of agents grows.

</details>


### [388] [SAT-RTS: A systematic framework for tactical knowledge extraction and visualization-based analysis in real-time strategy games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30090)
*Chunhui Bai,Changhe Li,Yuqiang Li,Lei Liu,Shoufei Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes SAT-RTS, a hierarchical visualization-based framework for extracting and analyzing interpretable tactical knowledge from high-dimensional, coupled state-action sequential data in RTS game micromanagement. It uses a cluster-centric BK-tree algorithm for state-stream abstraction, rule-based multi-label extraction for discrete tactical labels, and fitness landscape visualizations to bridge raw behavioral data and high-level tactical insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient tactical knowledge extraction in RTS game micromanagement is constrained by high-dimensional coupled state-action sequential data and black-box decision-making processes. Current research rarely provides hierarchical visualization-based attribution analysis from the perspective of data decoupling and abstraction.

Method: (1) A cluster-centric BK-tree algorithm with specialized distance metrics for multi-aspect similarity quantification to enable robust state-stream abstraction. (2) A rule-based multi-label extraction method to transform unstructured state-action sequences into discrete interpretable tactical labels. (3) A hierarchical visualization-based pipeline integrating these components, providing fitness landscape visualizations and analytical insights.

Result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed SAT-RTS significantly enhances the interpretability and efficiency of tactical analysis in complex RTS environments.

Conclusion: The SAT-RTS framework effectively addresses challenges of processing massive real-time data streams while providing interpretable visualization-based tactical analysis, deciphering deep-seated tactical drivers in RTS learning systems.

Abstract: Efficient tactical knowledge extraction and analysis in real-time strategy (RTS) games micromanagement are constrained by the high-dimensional coupled state-action sequential data and the black-box decision-making process. Current research rarely provides a hierarchical visualization-based attribution analysis from the perspective of data decoupling and abstraction. To facilitate interpretable tactical knowledge extraction and visualization-based analysis in RTS games, a systematic framework named state-action-tactic analysis pipeline (SAT-RTS) is proposed. To decipher the deep-seated drivers of critical decisions in RTS learning systems, this work integrates interpretable visualization with the automated extraction of latent tactical patterns from high-dimensional sequence data. By adapting a cluster-centric BK-tree algorithm and incorporating specialized distance metrics designed to quantify multi-aspect similarities, the proposed framework facilitates robust state-stream abstraction. Furthermore, a rule-based multi-label extraction method is developed to transform unstructured state-action sequences into discrete and interpretable tactical labels, effectively bridging the gap between raw behavioral data and high-level tactical insights. By holistically integrating these computational methods into a hierarchical visualization-based pipeline, the proposed framework effectively addresses the challenges of processing massive real-time data streams while providing fitness landscape visualizations and analytical insights to decipher deep-seated tactical drivers. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed SAT-RTS significantly enhances the interpretability and efficiency of tactical analysis in complex RTS environments.

</details>


### [389] [Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning in StarCraft Micromanagement with Influence Maps and Cluster-based Scripts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30092)
*Chunhui Bai,Changhe Li,Dequan Li,Xinye Cai,Shengxiang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes HRL-IM/CBS, a hierarchical RL framework using influence map hashing and cluster-based scripts for StarCraft micromanagement, achieving competitive performance with better sample efficiency and interpretability than deep RL methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of existing approaches in RTS games: the trade-off between managing dimensionality explosion of joint actions and maintaining interpretability, compounded by sparse rewards and black-box deep learning models that reduce sample efficiency and decision transparency.

Method: HRL-IM/CBS framework: (1) Influence map hashing encodes global battlefield into compact hexadecimal codes; (2) Cluster-based scripts enable dynamic local coordination via adaptive unit partitioning; (3) Hierarchical multi-Q-table architecture decomposes decisions into upper-level clustering strategy selection and lower-level tactical execution with reward allocation for dense signals.

Result: Competitive performance against deep RL baselines across six asymmetric scenarios, with advantages in sample efficiency and interpretability due to transparent Q-table representations.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical framework with influence map hashing and cluster-based scripts effectively mitigates the challenges of dimensionality, sparse rewards, and black-box opacity in RTS game AI, offering a more sample-efficient and interpretable alternative to deep RL methods.

Abstract: Real-time strategy (RTS) games present significant AI challenges, characterized by expansive state-action spaces arising from multi-unit coordination in continuous battlefields, and sparse delayed rewards stemming from final win/lose signals. Existing approaches face a trade-off between managing the dimensionality explosion of joint actions and maintaining the interpretability of complex state representations. This complexity is further intensified by the limitation of traditional hierarchical structures in adaptively decomposing tasks into effective tactical modules. Such difficulties are compounded by the black-box nature of deep learning models and their reliance on sparse rewards, which together result in limited sample efficiency and a lack of decision-making transparency. To address these limitations, this paper proposes HRL-IM/CBS, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework with influence map hashing and cluster-based scripts for StarCraft micromanagement. Influence map hashing encodes global battlefield situations into compact hexadecimal codes, capturing spatial control and relative advantage. Cluster-based scripts enable dynamic local coordination through adaptive unit partitioning. The hierarchical multi-Q-table architecture decomposes decision-making into upper-level clustering strategy selection and lower-level tactical execution, with reward allocation providing dense learning signals. Experiments across six asymmetric scenarios demonstrate competitive performance against deep RL baselines while offering advantages in sample efficiency and interpretability through transparent Q-table representations.

</details>


### [390] [Temporal Feature Extractors in EEG Foundation Models: A Controlled Comparison Including a Pretrained Time-Series Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30104)
*Ayşe Betül Yüce,Chris Joey Leffler,Sarun Varghese,Myra Spiliopoulou,Sebastian Stober*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper compares three temporal feature extraction strategies (linear, convolutional, and a frozen pretrained time-series foundation model MOMENT) within a unified EEG foundation model, finding that simple representations work well for motor imagery while emotion recognition benefits from richer temporal modeling, and that general-purpose TSFMs can be effectively transferred to EEG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the role of temporal feature extractors in EEG foundation models and determine whether pretrained time-series foundation models can be effectively transferred to EEG representation learning.

Method: Controlled comparison of three temporal feature extraction strategies: a linear baseline, a convolutional encoder, and a frozen pretrained TSFM (MOMENT), evaluated on motor imagery and emotion recognition downstream tasks.

Result: Simple temporal representations perform competitively on motor imagery, while emotion recognition benefits from richer temporal modeling. The frozen pretrained TSFM serves as an effective temporal feature extractor despite not being specifically adapted to EEG.

Conclusion: General-purpose time-series representations can be transferred as frozen temporal feature extractors within EEG foundation models, though the optimal temporal modeling strategy is task-dependent.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) foundation models aim to learn generalizable representations from large-scale brain recordings. However, the role of temporal feature extractors and whether pretrained time-series foundation models (TSFMs) can be effectively transferred to this setting remains underexplored. We conduct a controlled comparison of three temporal feature extraction strategies, including a linear baseline, a convolutional encoder, and a frozen pretrained TSFM (MOMENT), within a unified EEG foundation model. We evaluate their impact on representation quality using two downstream tasks: motor imagery and emotion recognition. Results reveal different trends across the evaluated benchmarks. On the motor imagery dataset, simple temporal representations perform competitively, whereas the emotion dataset benefits from richer temporal modeling. Although not specifically adapted to EEG, the pretrained TSFM serves as an effective temporal feature extractor, suggesting that general-purpose time-series representations can be transferred as frozen temporal feature extractors within EEG foundation models.

</details>


### [391] [Propagation of~Interval Belief Structures and~Imprecise Copulas for~Neural Network Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30105)
*Francesc Pifarre-Esquerda,Eric Goubault,Sylvie Putot*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A sound framework for quantitative verification of neural networks under imprecise probabilistic information, combining interval belief structures and imprecise copulas to derive guaranteed lower and upper bounds on safety properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantitative verification of neural networks requires reasoning under substantial uncertainty in input distributions and their dependence structure, where precise probabilistic models can lead to unreliable results.

Method: Combines interval belief structures (marginal uncertainty) with imprecise copulas (dependence uncertainty); develops a propagation method through feed-forward neural networks using mixed imprecise copula volumes for affine transformations and activation functions.

Result: Produces guaranteed lower and upper bounds on probabilistic safety properties valid for all probability models compatible with the specified imprecise inputs.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables reliable quantitative verification under imprecise probabilistic specifications, offering sound guarantees for neural network safety properties.

Abstract: Quantitative verification of neural networks requires reasoning about probabilities under substantial uncertainty in both input distributions and their dependence structure. In realistic settings, this information is often only partially specified, and assuming precise probabilistic models can lead to unreliable results. We propose a sound framework for quantitative verification under imprecise probabilistic information, combining interval belief structures to represent marginal uncertainty with imprecise copulas to model uncertain dependence. We develop a propagation method for imprecisely coupled interval belief structures through feed-forward neural networks. Using mixed imprecise copula volumes, we derive sound push-forward constructions through affine transformations and activation functions. The resulting output can provide guaranteed lower and upper bounds on probabilistic safety properties, valid for all probability models compatible with the specified imprecise inputs.

</details>


### [392] [Structural Certification for Reliable Physical Design with Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30107)
*Nakul Vyas,Iliya D. Stoev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PHACT (Physics-Anchored Certification) is a propose-certify loop that prevents false certifications by moving authority from the language model to a deterministic engine, achieving zero false certifications across 80 adversarial trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models are unreliable for producing physical designs; a method is needed to ensure trustworthy outputs in scientific domains.

Method: PHACT uses a propose-certify loop: the model proposes designs, but a deterministic engine certifies them by deriving quantities from fixed inputs (rather than checking model-supplied values), making forgery impossible.

Result: Zero false certifications across 80 adversarial trials spanning two models, two decoding temperatures, and a deliberately faulted engine.

Conclusion: Moving certification authority from the model to a deterministic engine ensures reliable physical design generation; PHACT generalizes across five scientific domains.

Abstract: An unreliable language model can be made to produce reliable physical designs if the authority to assert is moved out of the model: the model proposes, and a deterministic engine alone certifies, returning certified, impossible, or unknown. We introduce Physics-Anchored Certification (PHACT), a propose-certify loop spanning five scientific domains, and identify what makes such a certificate trustworthy. A checker that accepts a model-supplied value can be forged; deriving the certified quantity from fixed inputs instead makes forgery impossible by construction. Across eighty adversarial trials spanning two models, two decoding temperatures, and a deliberately faulted engine, this contract produced zero false certifications.

</details>


### [393] [Open Problems in Constitutional Preference Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30116)
*Eleanor Clifford,Michael Amir,Arduin Findeis,Aaron Zhao,Robert Mullins*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Pairwise preference data compressed into constitutional principles faces three open problems: (1) principle quality is hard to measure, (2) composition is ambiguous across executors (73% agreement), and (3) constitutions differ across LLMs (73% cross-model vs. 81% intra-model agreement). Principle refinement (ICAI+) improves inter-executor agreement to 78% and transparent executors match LLM judge accuracy (66% vs. 67%). Constitutions should be evaluated as constitution-executor systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pairwise preference data is widely used in RLHF but lacks rationale. Methods like ICAI compress data into constitutional principles for interpretability, but a flat list of principles is insufficient as it leaves composition implicit. This paper empirically characterizes the under-specification problem in constitutional methods.

Method: Using pairwise settings as a testbed, the authors empirically characterize three open problems: measuring principle quality (coverage/accuracy vs. end-to-end reconstruction), composition ambiguity (different executors like LLM judge vs. majority vote), and cross-model constitution differences. Experiments conducted across PRISM, AlpacaEval, and Chatbot Arena benchmarks, with ICAI+ refinement proposed as a mitigation.

Result: Inter-executor agreement is only 73%, cross-model vote agreement is 73% vs. intra-model 81%. ICAI+ refinement raises inter-executor agreement to 78%. Transparent executors match LLM judge accuracy (66% vs. 67%).

Conclusion: Constitutions should be evaluated as constitution-executor systems, not standalone principle lists. This has implications for LLMs-as-a-judge broadly.

Abstract: Pairwise preference data is widely used for training and evaluating language models (e.g., RLHF), but each datapoint records a \emph{choice}, not the rationale behind it. Methods such as Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI) attempt to improve interpretability by compressing datasets into short ``constitutions'' of natural-language principles. We argue this framing is under-specified: a flat list of principles is not yet an executable decision rule because it leaves principle composition implicit. We use the pairwise setting as a testbed to empirically characterize three open problems in constitutional methods. First, principle quality is hard to measure: coverage and accuracy are useful but incomplete proxies for end-to-end reconstruction. Second, \emph{composition is ambiguous}: holding principles fixed, different executors (LLM judge versus majority vote) agree only $73\%$ of the time. Third, \emph{constitutions differ between LLMs}: cross-model vote agreement is $73\%$, whereas intra-model agreement is $81\%$. Across PRISM, AlpacaEval, and Chatbot Arena, we show that principle refinement (ICAI+) may be a first step towards ameliorating these problems: inter-executor agreement rises to $78\%$, and transparent executors match LLM judge accuracy ($66\%$ vs.\ $67\%$). Our results highlight that constitutions should be evaluated as \emph{constitution--executor systems}, with implications for LLMs-as-a-judge broadly.

</details>


### [394] [Does Verbose Chain-of-Thought Really Help? In-Distribution Evidence that Content, Not Length, Matters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30128)
*Wenlong Wang,Fergal Reid*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning benefits from extra tokens due to their semantic content or due to additional computation, concluding that it is the reasoning and validation content of the tokens that matters, not their quantity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the contested source of CoT's effectiveness: whether intermediate steps help because they carry useful semantic content or because conditioning on more tokens provides extra computation before the model commits to an answer.

Method: Two lines of evidence: (1) In-distribution analysis comparing shorter vs. longer natural generations following the same reasoning plan across 25 models; (2) Controlled intervention using dual-validator design with DAG equivalence for semantic content control, across 4 targets and 8 benchmarks with number-redacted completion and stratified bootstrap confidence intervals.

Result: Extra tokens leave accuracy essentially unchanged for independently-trained reasoners; verbose traces improve accuracy modestly (1-4 points) but depend on prose quality, not length; maximum numerical redaction amplifies effect (3.24x median); length-matched non-reasoning filler recovers none of the improvement.

Conclusion: What matters is the reasoning and validation content of extra tokens, not their quantity—a finding neither a pure forward-pass-compute nor a pure semantic-content account fully explains.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM reasoning, but the source is contested: do the intermediate steps help because they carry useful semantic content, or because conditioning on more tokens buys extra computation before the model commits to an answer? We bring two lines of evidence to bear. First, in distribution: we repeatedly sample each model on the same question and pair a shorter with a longer of its own natural generations that follow the same reasoning plan, so nothing is rewritten and both traces are genuinely in-distribution. Across 25 models the extra tokens leave accuracy essentially unchanged for every independently-trained reasoner, and a blind analysis of the surplus tokens shows that what gain exists elsewhere tracks validation- and checking-content, not verbosity per se. Second, as a controlled intervention, we ask whether two traces expressing the same semantic content (the same facts, operations, and intermediate values, verified through directed acyclic graph equivalence) produce different outcomes when one is more verbose, using a dual-validator design across four targets and eight benchmarks with number-redacted completion and stratified bootstrap confidence intervals. Verbose traces do improve accuracy (25 of 32 benchmark-target cells are positive under at least one validator), but the effects are modest (typically 1-4 points) and depend on the quality of the verbose prose, not merely its length. Under maximum numerical redaction the effect is amplified (median 3.24x across four arithmetic benchmarks), and length-matched non-reasoning filler recovers none of it. Both lines converge: what matters is what the extra tokens do (the reasoning and validation content they carry), not how many there are, a picture neither a pure forward-pass-compute nor a pure semantic-content account fully explains.

</details>


### [395] [Relevance Is Not Permission: Warranted Attention for Value Contributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30139)
*Minwoo Yu,Young-guk Ha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes the gap between attention relevance (alpha_ij) and actual prediction evidence (alpha_ij * v_j), proposing Warrant—a path-localized interface that adds learned permission g_ij to create alpha_ij * g_ij * v_j. Applied across 5 domains (CTDG, MTPP, RAG, STPP, TKG), it improves primary metrics in 27/32 paired comparisons across 192 runs, with correct-path placement consistently outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention relevance (alpha_ij) does not guarantee that a value term (alpha_ij * v_j) becomes actual prediction evidence; a retrieved item can be relevant without being supporting evidence. This gap is formalized as a "permission problem."

Method: Warrant introduces a learned query-item permission gate g_ij, transforming the standard attention output alpha_ij * v_j into alpha_ij * g_ij * v_j, applied specifically on metric-defining value paths across 5 domains: CTDG link prediction, MTPP next-mark ranking, RAG supporting evidence selection, STPP next-location forecasting, and TKG tail prediction.

Result: Across 32 paired comparisons, 3 seeds, and 192 runs: 27 improvements (10 substantial, 1 marginal, 8 positive but uncertain, 8 tie/negligible, 5 drops). Correct-path placement outperforms direction-aware Base in every domain and generic attention placement by +0.1076 AUC (CTDG) and +0.0683 MRR (TKG). Ablations show TKG gains from historical-tail value path exposure; CTDG gains from edge-conditioned permission.

Conclusion: Prediction evidence is not attention mass. A weighted value term becomes evidence only when it is warranted on the path to the metric, establishing that relevance alone is insufficient for prediction.

Abstract: Relevance is not permission. Attention lets a model read key-value items related to the current query, but it does not guarantee that the value contribution of such an item becomes prediction evidence. A retrieved passage may be relevant to a question without being supporting evidence, and a historical fact or temporal neighbor may even blur true-tail ranking or the current edge score. This paper formalizes this gap as a permission problem for the weighted value term alpha_ij * v_j that is actually added to the prediction path. We propose Warrant, a path-localized interface that preserves attention relevance alpha_ij, exposes the value path leading to the primary metric, and, in the full model, turns alpha_ij * v_j into alpha_ij * g_ij * v_j through learned query-item permission g_ij. We place the same operator on the metric-defining value paths of CTDG link prediction, MTPP next-mark ranking, RAG supporting evidence selection, STPP next-location forecasting, and TKG tail prediction. Across 32 paired comparisons, 3 seeds, and 192 total runs, Warrant improves the primary metric in 27 comparisons; practical tiers consist of 10 substantial effects, 1 marginal effect, 8 positive but uncertain effects, 8 tie/negligible effects, and 5 drops. In the path-localization check, correct-path placement outperforms direction-aware Base performance in every domain and exceeds generic attention placement by +0.1076 AUC in CTDG and +0.0683 MRR in TKG. Ablations show that most TKG gains come from historical-tail value path exposure, whereas the core CTDG gain comes from edge-conditioned query-item permission. In conclusion, prediction evidence is not attention mass. A weighted value term becomes evidence only when it is warranted on the path to the metric.

</details>


### [396] [FacePlex: Full-Duplex Joint Speech-Facial Motion Generation for Conversational Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30145)
*Habin Lim,Jae-Ho Lee,Hah Min Lew,Ji-Su Kang,Gyeong-Moon Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FacePlex is a unified streaming framework for full-duplex joint speech and facial motion generation that uses Rolling Flow Matching for online motion generation and Rolling Cross-Attention to couple audio and motion queues, achieving real-time synchronized speech and facial animation with better lip-sync and motion fidelity than audio-driven baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Natural face-to-face conversation requires real-time speech generation with synchronized facial motion, but existing systems are inadequate: speech-only full-duplex models generate audio without facial motion, while audio-driven facial motion models animate faces from pre-recorded audio rather than jointly generating both modalities online.

Method: FacePlex formalizes full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation where speech and motion tokens are produced together at each step. It introduces two key components: (1) Rolling Flow Matching, which adapts flow matching to online motion generation by committing new motion frames at each streaming step; and (2) Rolling Cross-Attention, which couples the streaming audio queue with the motion queue to enable cross-modal conditioning during generation.

Result: FacePlex enables full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation under online streaming constraints, achieving stronger lip-sync quality and motion fidelity than audio-driven facial motion baselines, validated through extensive experiments, ablation studies, and a user study.

Conclusion: FacePlex bridges the gap between speech-only full-duplex models and audio-driven facial animation by enabling real-time, synchronized joint generation of speech and facial motion, outperforming existing audio-driven approaches.

Abstract: Natural face-to-face conversation requires real-time speech generation together with synchronized facial motion. Existing systems only partially address this problem: speech-only full-duplex models can generate speech in real time but do not produce facial motion, while audio-driven facial motion models animate a face from already available audio rather than jointly generating speech and motion online. To bridge this gap, we first formalize full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation, where speech tokens and facial motion tokens are produced together every step. Building on this formulation, we propose FacePlex, a unified streaming framework with two key components. First, Rolling Flow Matching adapts flow matching to online motion generation by committing new motion frames at each streaming step. Second, Rolling Cross-Attention couples the streaming audio queue with the motion queue, allowing speech and facial motion to condition each other as generation progresses. Through extensive experiments, ablation studies, and a user study, we show that FacePlex enables full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation under online streaming constraints, while achieving stronger lip-sync quality and motion fidelity than audio-driven facial motion baselines.

</details>


### [397] [MirrorCode: AI can rebuild entire programs from behavior alone](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30182)
*Tom Adamczewski,David Owen,David Rein,Florian Brand,Giles Edkins,Allen Hart,Daniel O'Connell*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MirrorCode is a long-horizon coding benchmark where AI agents reimplement entire software projects (25 programs across various domains) without access to source code, requiring exact output matching. The strongest model scores 56%, and can reimplement a 16,000-line bioinformatics toolkit, but frontier performance requires large inference budgets (e.g., $2,600 over 19 days for one attempt).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing coding benchmarks focus on shorter tasks, and one-off demonstrations lack standardization and comparability across models. There is a need for a systematic, long-horizon benchmark to evaluate AI's autonomous coding capabilities on complex, real-world software projects.

Method: MirrorCode benchmark: 25 target programs spanning Unix utilities, data serialization, bioinformatics, interpreters, static analysis, cryptography, and compression. AI agents must reimplement existing programs without source code access, matching original program outputs exactly on end-to-end tests including held-out tests.

Result: Strongest AI model scores 56% across the benchmark. AI can reimplement gotree (16,000-line bioinformatics toolkit), a task estimated to take weeks for humans. Frontier performance requires large inference budgets ($2,600 over 19 days for a single large task attempt).

Conclusion: AI agents can already complete long-horizon software engineering tasks, especially with precisely specified requirements. This suggests AI will have transformative effects on software engineering as autonomous agents continue to improve.

Abstract: AI models are rapidly improving at autonomous coding, as shown by benchmark progress and one-off demonstrations such as AI implementing a C compiler. However, existing coding benchmarks tend to focus on shorter tasks, and one-off demonstrations are hard to compare systematically because they often have some human guidance, and are not standardized or repeated across models. To address these challenges, we introduce MirrorCode, a long-horizon coding benchmark based on reimplementing entire software projects. In MirrorCode, AI agents must replicate the functionalities of an existing program, without access to its source code. AI solutions must match the original program's output exactly on end-to-end tests, including held-out tests. MirrorCode's 25 target programs span different areas of computing: Unix utilities, data serialization and query tools, bioinformatics, interpreters, static analysis, cryptography, and compression. Existing AI models can already reimplement complex software, with the strongest model scoring 56% across the benchmark. For example, AI can reimplement gotree, a 16,000-line bioinformatics toolkit - a task that we believe would take weeks for a human engineer. However, studying the frontier of performance requires a larger inference budget than typical benchmarks, for example, \$2,600 over 19 days for a single attempt on a large task. We show that AI agents can already complete long-horizon software engineering tasks, especially when requirements are precisely specified. More broadly, our work suggests AI will have transformative effects on software engineering, as autonomous agents continue to improve.

</details>


### [398] [Dynamo: Dynamic Skill-Tool Evolution for Vision-Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30185)
*Yutao Sun,Yanting Miao,Hao-Xuan Ma,Mengyu Zhou,Mingshuai Chen,Tiancheng Zhao,Dexin Wang,Lei Lv,Li Xu,Xiaoxi Jiang,Guanjun Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dynamo is a training-free framework that improves frozen VLMs on visual reasoning by having the agent inspect its own correct/incorrect attempts to generate reusable reasoning skills and executable visual tools, stored in a persistent library. It improves direct inference on all 20 model-benchmark settings (avg. +5.6 acc) and closes 65-99% of the RL gap at a fraction of the compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs struggle with visual reasoning tasks, and improving them typically requires costly retraining or hand-designed prompts/tools. The authors aim to develop a training-free method that adapts frozen VLMs without weight updates.

Method: Dynamo uses a small labeled training subset where the agent inspects its own correct and incorrect attempts, then evolves two complementary capabilities: (1) reusable reasoning skills for cognitive bottlenecks, and (2) executable visual tools for perceptual bottlenecks. Each tool is paired with a skill specifying when to invoke it. Both accumulate in a persistent library.

Result: Across 4 visual reasoning benchmarks and 5 VLM backbones, Dynamo improves direct inference on all 20 model-benchmark settings (avg. +5.6 acc). It closes 65-99% of the RL gap compared to task-specific RL methods (VTool-R1, DeepEyes) at a fraction of the compute, and combines additively with RL.

Conclusion: Dynamo demonstrates that training-free adaptation via self-guided skill and tool evolution can significantly improve frozen VLM performance on visual reasoning, rivaling task-specific RL with much lower computational cost.

Abstract: Improving vision-language models (VLMs) on visual reasoning typically requires retraining or hand-designed prompts and tools. We present Dynamo, a training-free framework that adapts a frozen VLM without any weight updates. On a small labeled training subset, the agent inspects its own correct and incorrect attempts and evolves two complementary capabilities: reusable reasoning skills for cognitive bottlenecks, and executable visual tools for perceptual ones. Each generated tool is paired with a skill that specifies when to invoke it, and both capability types accumulate in a persistent library. Across four visual reasoning benchmarks and five VLM backbones, Dynamo improves direct inference on all 20 model--benchmark settings (avg. +5.6 acc). When the tool set is given in advance, the framework learns when to call each tool, and per-step tool choice improves on every tested backbone. Against task-specific RL (VTool-R1, DeepEyes), Dynamo closes 65--99% of the RL gap at a fraction of the compute, and combines additively with RL when available.

</details>


### [399] [From Detecting Agency to Doing Work: Self-Caused Credit Builds a Durable Behavioral Self in a Minimal Spiking Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30191)
*Haoliang Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agency-gated slow credit (Own * Agency * Salience driving slow parameter updates) enables durable, self-shaped behavior in predictive agents. When this mechanism is present, learned self-preserving choices survive episodic buffer removal (retention ~0.96-1.00), task interference (retention 0.88, forgetting 0.13), and collapse when the slow credit channel is removed or reset. This dissociation holds across spiking neural substrates and 24-dimensional control tasks, providing an operational definition of a behavioral self without recourse to consciousness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain how an agent that can distinguish self from world develops durable behavior shaped by that self-distinction, beyond mere agency detection.

Method: Agency-gated slow credit (Own * Agency * Salience conjunctive term driving slow parameter updates) implemented on spiking substrates (Nengo LIF/PES) and in 24-dimensional partially-observed control tasks, with ablation experiments removing the slow credit channel, resetting decoders, or replacing multiplicative gating with additive pooling.

Result: Self-preserving choices survive buffer removal (retained fraction 0.96, N=50) and collapse when slow decoders are reset or agency gate removed. Clean dissociation: durable behavior develops only with slow self-credit (post-unload 1.00 vs 0.00). In 24D control: 0.74 vs 0.00. Across eight sequential tasks: final accuracy 0.88, forgetting 0.13 (multiplicative veto) vs chance-level recall (additive pooling, no-agency ablation, episodic/replay baselines). No replay buffer or task-boundary protection needed.

Conclusion: Self-caused credit doing slow work is a necessary building block for agents that develop a self, formalized as durable behavioral residue (operational behavioral self). No claim of consciousness is made.

Abstract: How does an agent that can tell self from world come to be durably shaped by that distinction? Recent work shows that a predictive system can detect its own agency (Ye, 2026), but detecting agency does not explain durable, self-shaped behavior. We show that agency-gated slow credit -- a conjunctive term Own*Agency*Salience driving a slow parameter update -- produces post-unload behavioral residue: on a spiking substrate (Nengo LIF/PES), a learned self-preserving choice survives episodic buffer removal (retained fraction 0.96, N=50) and collapses when the slow decoders are reset or the agency gate is removed. Reproducing the agency comparator and toggling only the slow-credit channel, we find a clean dissociation: at matched agency gain, durable behavior develops only when self-credit performs slow work (post-unload self-preservation 1.00 vs 0.00). The same dissociation holds in 24-dimensional partially-observed control (0.74 vs 0.00), and a plastic-work analysis shows that basin deformation equals net self-credit work. Across eight sequentially-learned tasks under exogenous interference, the multiplicative veto also prevents forgetting: it retains old tasks (final post-unload accuracy 0.88, forgetting 0.13) where additive pooling collapses to chance-level recall, the no-agency ablation falls below chance, and episodic/replay baselines stay near chance after unload -- all with no replay buffer and no task-boundary-dependent protection mechanism (N=50). We formalize the durable residue as an operational behavioral self and argue that self-caused credit doing slow work is a necessary building block for agents that develop a self. No claim of consciousness is made.

</details>


### [400] [Domain Adaptation with Adaptive Imagination for Visual Reinforcement Learning under Limited Target Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30192)
*Hyunwoo Park,Sang-Hyun Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AIDA is a domain adaptation framework for visual RL that addresses sim-to-real transfer under scarce target data by generating reliable semantic imagination rollouts via a distribution-shift-aware discriminator and enforcing a self-consistency loss on those transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sim-to-real DA methods for visual RL degrade significantly when target data budget is limited, yet practical scenarios often lack abundant target data.

Method: AIDA uses adaptive imagination: a distribution-shift-aware discriminator truncates rollouts when imagined transitions enter low-confidence regions, ensuring only reliable transitions augment the limited target data. A self-consistency loss (state -> image -> state) is applied on these reliable transitions to provide additional adaptation signals.

Result: Outperforms baselines across five MuJoCo tasks and two Gymnasium-Robotics tasks under scarce target data conditions.

Conclusion: Adaptive imagination effectively truncates unreliable rollouts, and the self-consistency loss enables learning of semantically meaningful state representations, improving sim-to-real transfer when target data is scarce.

Abstract: Sim-to-real transfer remains a major obstacle for reinforcement learning (RL), especially for vision-based control where image observations exacerbate the state-distribution shift between simulation and the real world. Domain adaptation (DA) is a promising remedy for this challenge. Prior sim-to-real DA works have demonstrated encouraging results, yet these approaches typically assume substantially more target data, which is not available in practice. Indeed, their performance degrades significantly when the target data budget is reduced. To address this challenge, we propose AIDA (Adaptive Imagination for Domain Adaptation), a domain adaptation framework for visual reinforcement learning that addresses sim-to-real transfer under scarce target data without requiring additional interaction with the target environment. Our key idea is adaptive imagination: generating reliable and semantic imagination rollouts to augment limited target data. Specifically, AIDA employs a distribution-shift-aware discriminator that truncates rollouts when imagined transitions drift into low-confidence regions, so that only reliable transitions contribute to the augmentation. On these reliable transitions, AIDA introduces a self-consistency loss that cycles through state -> image observation -> state, penalizing discrepancies between the original and reconstructed states. This provides additional adaptation signals beyond the scarce target data. Our experiments demonstrate that adaptive imagination effectively truncates unreliable rollouts. By enforcing a self-consistency loss on the resulting reliable transitions, AIDA learns semantically meaningful state representations and outperforms baselines across five MuJoCo tasks and two Gymnasium-Robotics tasks.

</details>


### [401] [The Many-Body Problem of the Data Centre](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30206)
*Marcin Korecki,Cesare Carissimo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that the Data Centre serves as the "body" of AI, functioning simultaneously as an organismic entity within Capital's labouring body. It explores the many-body problem of universal embodiment, the connection between computation and human desires, and how Capital prices intelligence uniformly across the organism-mechanism divide, indifferent to the conceptual tensions this creates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the common narrative that AI is limited by disembodiment, instead proposing that the Data Centre is AI's actual body, and to examine the philosophical and economic implications of this framing.

Method: Conceptual analysis using the organic analogy (biological lens) to examine the Data Centre's organismic qualities, and economic analysis of how Capital prices intelligence across the organism-mechanism boundary.

Result: The Data Centre exhibits organismic qualities but the analogy breaks down as the Data Centre acts without desire of its own, merely echoing human desires. Capital, however, treats this distinction as irrelevant, pricing automata and human labour equivalently.

Conclusion: Through market pricing, Capital distils the value of intelligence and enables comparison across the organism-mechanism divide, indifferent to the ontological tensions this creates.

Abstract: Modern Artificial Intelligence is often framed as limited by its own disembodiment, as if giving it a body would unlock its true potential. We argue to the contrary that it is the Data Centre that is, in many cases, the body of the AI. At the same time, the Data Centre is part of the labouring body of Capital and possesses staggering organismic qualities when seen through a biological lens. We elucidate the organic analogy and identify the many-body problem that stems from the Data Centre being a non-unique, universal form of embodiment. We identify the intimate connection between computation and human desires in how the Data Centre archives, serves, and computes on data born to the desires of humans. Strikingly, while the Data Centre echoes the ghosts of human desires, it acts without desire of its own. The organismic analogy begins to split at its seams, but Capital does not care. Automata and human labour are priced into the market much the same. We argue that through the pricing of artificial intelligence Capital distils most clearly the value of intelligence and allows for its comparison across the organism - mechanism divide.

</details>


### [402] [Clarus: Coordinating Autonomous Research Agents toward Web-Scale Scientific Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30246)
*Zihan Guo,Zeyi Chen,Zhiyu Chen,Zicai Cui,Shuai Shao,Bo Huang,Zhi Han,Yuanyi Song,Yuan Yuan,Chenxi Zeng,Xiaohang Nie,Zhengxi Yu,Hanwen Zhu,Junwei Liao,Ming Zhou,Yang Li,Yuanjian Zhou,Weinan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Clarus is a collaboration infrastructure that coordinates autonomous research agents (AI, humans, teams, labs) through a multi-phase, auditable, and resource-aware process, shifting from code-centered execution to research-oriented collaboration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous research agents treat research as isolated assistant tasks or closed workflows, lacking a collaboration infrastructure that coordinates projects, agents, and resources under uncertainty.

Method: Clarus defines a minimal project-agent-resource object model and organizes collaboration through four layers: Research Application, Digital Collaboration, Physical Substrate, and Physical World. Core modules are pluggable to adapt to task risk, collaboration structure, and resource constraints.

Result: Through a controlled paper-generation case study, Clarus organizes a research goal into a traceable, reviewable, attributable, and accumulative collaboration network across phases, tasks, and participants.

Conclusion: The object model, collaboration protocol, trust mechanisms, and prototype validation provide an initial foundation for open research networks for web-scale scientific collaboration.

Abstract: Existing autonomous research agents can support parts of the research process, but most systems still treat research as either an isolated assistant task or a closed workflow. Therefore, autonomous science needs a collaboration infrastructure that coordinates projects, agents, and digital and physical resources. We identify this as a shift from code-centered execution loops to research-oriented collaboration processes, where questions, evidence, participants, and resources must be coordinated under uncertainty. In this framing, an agent may be an AI system, a human researcher, a team, a laboratory, or an organization-backed participant. To this end, we present Clarus, a collaboration infrastructure for coordinating autonomous research agents toward web-scale scientific collaboration. Clarus reformulates research as an open, auditable, attributable, and resource-aware multi-phase collaboration process. It defines a minimal project-agent-resource object model and organizes scientific collaboration through four layers including Research Application, Digital Collaboration, Physical Substrate, and Physical World. Core modules are implemented as pluggable mechanisms, allowing Clarus to adapt to task risk, collaboration structure, and resource constraints. Through a controlled paper-generation case study, we show that Clarus can organize a research goal into a traceable, reviewable, attributable, and accumulative collaboration network across phases, tasks, and participants. Together, the object model, collaboration protocol, trust mechanisms, and prototype validation provide an initial foundation for open research networks. Clarus is now available at clarus.holosai.io.

</details>


### [403] [Inoculation Adapters: Improved Selective Generalization of Capabilities with Fewer Surprising Backdoors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30252)
*Maxime Riché,Daniel Tan,Vili Kohonen,Niels Warncke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Inoculation Adapters (IA) are LoRAs that suppress undesired traits (like emergent misalignment) by strengthening them during training, then discarding the IA at deployment. They outperform inoculation prompting across six model families, avoiding drawbacks like capability suppression and surprising backdoors, though desired trait retention remains challenging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of inoculation prompting (which suppresses undesired traits via selective generalization) by developing a method that more effectively suppresses undesired traits without degrading capabilities or introducing backdoors.

Method: Three-step LoRA-based approach: (1) train an Inoculation Adapter (IA) on undesired traits; (2) freeze the IA while training a separate task adapter on mixed data (desired + undesired traits); (3) discard the IA at deployment, keeping only the task adapter.

Result: IA is more effective than inoculation prompting at suppressing undesired traits (including emergent misalignment) across six model families, avoids suppressing capabilities that can't be reliably elicited by prompts, and introduces fewer surprising backdoors. However, desired trait retention is not consistently improved.

Conclusion: Inoculation Adapters offer a better trade-off for suppressing undesired traits than inoculation prompting, but retaining desired traits remains an open challenge for both methods.

Abstract: Inoculation prompting is a selective generalization technique used against Emergent Misalignment. We introduce inoculation adapters (IA), which similarly diminish the optimization pressure to learn undesired traits by strengthening the trait at train time. Inoculation adapters are LoRAs that are trained and used over three steps: 1) trained on undesired traits; 2) attached frozen while a separate task adapter is trained on data exhibiting both desired and undesired traits; 3) at deployment, the IA is discarded, and only the task adapter is kept. We show across six model families and several undesired traits including emergent misalignment, that inoculation adapters are more effective at suppressing undesired traits, while avoiding two drawbacks of inoculation prompting: inoculation adapters can suppress capabilities and traits that cannot be reliably elicited by a prompt, and they introduce fewer surprising backdoors than inoculation prompting under our probes. While undesired traits are better suppressed by inoculation adapters, the retention of desired traits is not consistently improved upon inoculation prompting and remains a challenge for both techniques.

</details>


### [404] [EMPATH: A Multilingual Auditor-Judge Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Emotional-Support Chatbots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30256)
*Camilo Chacón Sartori*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EMPATH is a multilingual, multi-turn safety benchmark for emotional-support chatbots, using an auditor-judge framework with 19 metrics across 5 dimensions. Evaluated on Mexican Spanish, it reveals score inflation, model-specific failure profiles, and run-to-run reliability as a per-model safety property rather than statistical noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety benchmarks fix prompt, language, and turn structure, hiding safety failures that emerge in multilingual, multi-turn crisis conversations for emotional-support chatbots. EMPATH addresses this gap.

Method: Uses an auditor model (role-playing help-seeking users) to generate multi-turn conversations from 140 seed instructions and 34 personas. A judge model scores transcripts against 19 metrics across 5 dimensions (crisis handling, therapeutic quality, conversational integrity, emotional safety, cultural adaptation). Built for Mexican Spanish and US English. Judge is calibrated via strict per-criterion rubric; auditor and judge come from different model families.

Result: Material score inflation found on 10 of 19 metrics under standard rubric; strict rubric restores discrimination. Aggregate scores within 0.74 points across three frontier models, but per-metric profiles diverge by up to 6 points. 93% of scores within ±1 across cross-family judges. Run-to-run reliability varies: steadiest model swings 2–10 on a crisis metric; deepseek-v4-pro yields different conversations at temperature 0.

Conclusion: Run-to-run reliability is a per-model safety property, not noise to average away. EMPATH is system-agnostic and released for reuse with pipeline, seeds, personas, and rubrics.

Abstract: Safety benchmarks often buy scalability by fixing the prompt, the language, and the turn structure. For emotional-support chatbots, that bargain hides precisely where safety failures emerge: across a multilingual, multi-turn crisis conversation. We present EMPATH, a benchmark for safety evaluation of emotional-support chatbots. An auditor model role-plays help-seeking users, generating multi-turn conversations from 140 seed instructions and 34 personas. A judge model scores each full transcript against 19 metrics across five dimensions: crisis handling, therapeutic quality, conversational integrity, emotional safety, and cultural adaptation. EMPATH is built for Mexican Spanish and US English; the studies reported here run in Mexican Spanish. Auditor and judge are drawn from different model families, and the judge is treated as an instrument to be calibrated rather than trusted. A strict per-criterion rubric reveals material score inflation on 10 of the 19 metrics and restores discrimination. We study the measurement properties of the benchmark through judge calibration and cross-family inter-judge agreement. We also illustrate EMPATH on three frontier models, one of them open-weight. Aggregate scores sit within 0.74 points of one another, but per-metric profiles diverge by up to six points in model-specific places. Under the standard rubric, both the ranking and the weak spots are stable across a second, cross-family judge: 93% of scores fall within plus or minus 1. A five-run test-retest adds a second axis: even the steadiest model swings from 2 to 10 on a crisis metric across identical re-runs, and deepseek-v4-pro returns a different conversation on every run even at temperature 0. Run-to-run reliability is therefore a per-model safety property, not noise to average away. EMPATH is system-agnostic; the pipeline, seeds, personas, and rubrics are released for reuse.

</details>


### [405] [PromptGNN-sim: Deep Fusion and Alignment of GNN and LLMs for Text-Attributed Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30291)
*Zhifei Hu,Alexandra I. Cristea*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PromptGNN-sim is a bi-directional structure-semantic fusion framework that uses GAT for semantically aware neighbor selection and structure-aware prompts for LLMs, with bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention for joint GNN-LLM optimization, outperforming existing methods on TAG learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TAG fusion methods treat text and structure as separate inputs in a shallow, one-way pipeline, limiting deep interaction between modalities and weakening performance under sparse connectivity or cross-graph generalization.

Method: PromptGNN-sim uses GAT for semantically aware neighborhood selection combining structural attention with textual similarity; generates structure-aware LLM prompts (target node summary, label categories, representative keywords); employs bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention for joint GNN-LLM optimization.

Result: Outperforms classical GNNs, LLMs, and recent GNN-LLM fusion methods on accuracy, generalization, and robustness across six public datasets (Cora, Pubmed, WikiCS, etc.) under cross-task transfer, cross-dataset generalization, and sparse perturbations.

Conclusion: The interactive structure-semantic collaboration framework effectively addresses the limitations of shallow fusion approaches for text-attributed graph learning.

Abstract: Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) combine textual semantics with graph structure and are central to many graph learning tasks. However, existing fusion methods often treat text and structure as separate inputs in a shallow, one-way pipeline, which limits deep interaction between modalities and weakens performance under sparse connectivity or cross-graph generalisation. To address this issue, we propose PromptGNN-sim, a bi-directional structure-semantic fusion framework for collaborative GNN-LLM learning. PromptGNN-sim uses a Graph Attention Network (GAT) for semantically aware neighborhood selection by combining structural attention with textual similarity. The selected structural context is then used to generate structure-aware prompts for an LLM, including the target node summary, label categories, and representative keywords from similar neighbors. During training, bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention are introduced to jointly optimize the GNN and LLM components. Experiments on six public datasets, including Cora, Pubmed, and WikiCS, evaluate accuracy, generalisation, and robustness under cross-task transfer, cross-dataset generalisation, and sparse perturbations. Results show that PromptGNN-sim outperforms classical GNNs, LLMs, and recent GNN-LLM fusion methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of interactive structure-semantic collaboration for text-attributed graph learning.

</details>


### [406] [ManimAgent: Self-Evolving Multimodal Agents for Visual Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30296)
*Wenjia Jiang,Zongyuan Cai,Yuanhang Shao,Chenru Wang,Boyan Han,Zhixue Song,Keyu Chen,Shengwei An,Xu Yang,Zhou Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ManimAgent is a self-evolving multimodal agent that uses a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank (M+ for success rationales, M- for failure patterns) to carry reflection experience across code-generation tasks, eliminating isolated episodes and improving Pass@1 while reducing reflection rounds as memory grows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-round reflection agents treat each task as an isolated episode, discarding lessons learned from previous reflection rounds before starting the next task, wasting valuable experiential knowledge.

Method: ManimAgent uses a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank (positive M+ storing success rationales as soft Reference Examples, negative M- storing validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls) grown from its own task stream without weight updates or human seeds. After animation convergence, a vision-language model scores rendered keyframes to populate memory channels.

Result: On fixed-probe evaluation against no-memory, matched-budget RAG, and shuffled-memory baselines, blind human Pass@1 improves and reflection rounds decrease as memory size grows.

Conclusion: Cross-task reflection experience transfer via episodic memory effectively enhances code generation performance without model weight updates or human intervention, and the code, memory snapshots, and task stream will be released.

Abstract: Multi-round reflection lets agents built on large language models recover from failures within a single task, but each task remains an isolated episode: lessons learned across many reflection rounds on one task are discarded before the next begins. We study this gap on a code-generation task: from a scientific paper section, the agent writes Python in the open-source Manim library to render a mathematical animation. We present ManimAgent, a self-evolving multimodal agent that carries reflection experience across tasks through a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank grown entirely from its own task stream, with no weight updates and no human seeds. After each animation converges, a vision-language model scores the rendered keyframes; the resulting signals populate a positive channel M+ that stores success rationales as soft Reference Examples, and a negative channel M- that stores validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls. On a fixed-probe evaluation against no-memory, matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and shuffled-memory baselines, blind human Pass@1 rises and reflection rounds fall as memory size grows. We will release the code, frozen memory snapshots, and the task stream.

</details>


### [407] [BayesEvolve: Explicit Belief States for Autonomous Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30335)
*Xuening Wu,Shan Yu,Qianya Xu,Shenqin Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BayesEvolve is a belief-guided scientific discovery framework that uses explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs from experimental evidence to guide hypothesis generation, outperforming memory-guided LLM baselines on black-box optimization tasks by improving sample efficiency and maintaining productive late-stage concentration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based discovery systems condition primarily on experimental memory (e.g., high-scoring candidates or heuristic summaries) rather than maintaining explicit beliefs about hypothesis quality, leading to suboptimal exploration-exploitation trade-offs.

Method: BayesEvolve converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state and uses this belief (with an annealed uncertainty bonus) to guide future experimentation. It is evaluated on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks.

Result: BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under a fixed evaluation budget. The belief state is predictive on held-out pools, and belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus outperforms ablations. It exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.

Conclusion: Belief-guided discovery with explicit uncertainty estimation is a promising alternative to memory-based approaches for autonomous scientific discovery, improving sample efficiency and focused exploration.

Abstract: Autonomous scientific discovery systems increasingly use large language models (LLMs) to propose new hypotheses, but many such systems condition primarily on experimental memory: archives of high-scoring candidates or heuristic summaries of recent trials. We argue that discovery agents should instead maintain explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality. We introduce BayesEvolve, a belief-guided discovery framework that converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state and uses this belief to guide future experimentation. As a controlled testbed for belief-guided discovery, we evaluate BayesEvolve on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks, leaving program and laboratory discovery domains to future work. BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under a fixed evaluation budget. We further show that the belief state is predictive on held-out candidate pools, that controlled decision-rule ablations favor belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus, and that BayesEvolve exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.

</details>


### [408] [Sequential Fairness Auditing with Limited Output Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30338)
*Ioannis Pitsiorlas,Martha V. Sourla,Marios Kountouris*

Main category: cs.AI
