### [433] [Improving Coherence in Hierarchical Time Series Forecasting using Structured Temporal Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28553)
*Ruchi Pakhle*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HTF extends Temporal Fusion Transformer with hierarchical embeddings and coherence-aware loss to enforce forecast consistency across hierarchy levels, outperforming classical reconciliation methods on M5 and energy datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional hierarchical forecasting methods (Bottom-Up, MinT) enforce coherence via post-processing but cannot capture complex nonlinear temporal dependencies and covariate interactions.

Method: HTF integrates structured hierarchical embeddings with a coherence-aware loss function that penalizes differences between aggregated child forecasts and parent forecasts during training, embedding coherence directly into the optimization objective.

Result: On M5 Walmart and hierarchical energy consumption datasets, HTF reduces forecast incoherence and improves accuracy versus classical reconciliation methods and deep learning baselines.

Conclusion: Embedding coherence into the training objective via HTF jointly learns temporal dynamics and structural consistency, with attention visualization and embedding analysis revealing how temporal and structural information contribute to performance.

Abstract: In many real-world applications, such as retail sales, energy usage, and supply chain planning, forecasting is performed across hierarchical structures. These structures often represent aggregations (e.g., products to categories to regions), where forecasts must not only be accurate but also coherent, meaning that lower-level predictions sum correctly to higher-level forecasts. Traditional statistical methods, such as Bottom-Up and MinT, enforce coherence through post-processing but fail to model complex nonlinear temporal dependencies and covariate interactions.
  We propose Hierarchical Temporal Fusion (HTF), a novel extension of the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) that integrates structured hierarchical embeddings with a coherence-aware loss function to ensure consistent forecasts across all levels of a hierarchy. Rather than applying reconciliation after forecasting, HTF embeds coherence directly into the training objective. The coherence loss penalizes the difference between aggregated child forecasts and their corresponding parent forecasts during training, enabling the model to learn both temporal dynamics and structural consistency simultaneously.
  We evaluate HTF on two publicly available benchmark datasets: the M5 Walmart forecasting dataset and a publicly available hierarchical energy consumption dataset. Results demonstrate that HTF substantially reduces forecast incoherence while improving forecasting accuracy compared with classical reconciliation methods and deep learning baselines. In addition, attention visualization and embedding analysis provide insight into how temporal and structural information contribute to hierarchical forecasting performance.

</details>


### [434] [Geometric Measurements of the Axiom of Choice in Neural Proof Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28572)
*Rodrigo Mendoza-Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper empirically demonstrates that the axiom of choice leaves a measurable geometric signature in proof space (via tactic sequences) that decays with dependency distance, and this signature has operational consequences for neural and automated theorem provers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the philosophical distinction between classical and constructive proofs with a quantitative, empirical measurement, and to investigate whether this distinction has practical implications for automated theorem proving.

Method: Partitioned 471,260 Mathlib declarations by transitive dependence on the axiom of choice; represented 42,355 traced theorems via tactic sequences; trained a self-supervised proof encoder; measured anomaly score, reconstruction loss, and density-superlevel containment against distance from axiom in the dependency graph; conducted robustness controls (length, file, author, topic); evaluated performance of aesop and ReProver on classical vs. constructive theorems.

Result: Classical and constructive proofs are sharply separable near the axiom (AUC 0.847 at distance 2) and become indistinguishable at distance 9+; the signature is robust to controls; aesop solves constructive theorems at 13x the rate of classical ones; neural-guided ReProver compresses the gap to 5x; anomaly score predicts aesop failure beyond proof length.

Conclusion: The axiom of choice has a measurable geometric correlate in proof space that decays with dependency distance, and this signature carries operational significance for prover performance.

Abstract: The axiom of choice has divided the foundations of mathematics for over a century, but the distinction between classical and constructive proofs has remained a philosophical and methodological one. We use Lean 4's kernel-level tracking of axiom dependence to show that the axiom of choice has a measurable geometric correlate in proof space that obeys a one-parameter mixture law and has operational consequences for neural theorem provers. To do this, we partition $471{,}260$ declarations of Mathlib by transitive dependence on the axiom of choice and represent a filtered population of $42{,}355$ traced theorems by their sequences of tactic invocations. We use the constructive proofs in this dataset to train a self-supervised proof encoder and show that when using it to measure classical proofs, three complementary measurements (anomaly score, reconstruction loss, and density-superlevel containment) exhibit a common decline with the proof's distance from the axiom in the dependency graph, from sharp separation at the shallow boundary (AUC $0.847$ at distance $2$) to indistinguishability at distance~$9{+}$. Robustness controls show that the signature survives length, file, author, and topic controls, and replicates under full-source encoders trained on normalised proof source. Operationally, we show that on an evaluation sample of $251$ Mathlib theorems, Lean's \texttt{aesop} tactic solves constructive theorems at $13\times$ the rate of classical ones, and a neural-guided hybrid using the ReProver tactic generator compresses the gap to $5\times$. The geometric anomaly score predicts \texttt{aesop} failure beyond proof length, providing an operational link between the geometric signature and prover performance.

</details>


### [435] [Replica Symmetry Breaking and Algorithmic Thresholds in Empirical Risk Minimization under Multi-Index Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28573)
*Andrea Montanari,Kangjie Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the empirical risk landscape for high-dimensional non-convex optimization in supervised learning, proposing an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm that achieves near-optimal performance among polynomial-time algorithms when features are Gaussian and responses depend on a low-dimensional projection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why gradient-based optimization converges to near-global optima in high-dimensional non-convex empirical risk minimization, despite the presence of many local optima.

Method: The authors propose an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm and analyze it in the high-dimensional asymptotic regime (n,d→∞ with n/d→α), using i.i.d. Gaussian features and responses depending on a k-dimensional subspace projection, with a model depending on an m-dimensional projection.

Result: The paper precisely characterizes the training error achieved by IAMP and the relationship between test and training error in the high-dimensional limit.

Conclusion: The performance of the IAMP algorithm is expected to be optimal among polynomial-time algorithms for this setting, providing a precise picture of which parts of the empirical risk landscape are accessible by polynomial-time methods.

Abstract: Modern machine learning models are trained by optimizing high-dimensional non-convex empirical risk functions. Such cost functions can have a multitude of local optima and yet, gradient-based optimization appears to converge to near-global optima.
  Within a simple supervised learning setting, we develop a precise picture of which parts of the empirical risk landscape are accessible by polynomial-time algorithms. We are given i.i.d. pairs $\{(\boldsymbol{x}_i,y_i):\; 1 \le i\le n\}$ with $\boldsymbol{x}_i\in \mathbb{R}^d$ standard Gaussian feature vectors, and $y_i\in\mathbb{R}$ response variables that depend on $\boldsymbol{x}_i$ through their projections on an unknown $k$-dimensional subspace. We use empirical risk minimization to learn a model that depends on an $m$-dimensional projection of the data (e.g., an $m$-neurons neural network).
  We propose an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm and precisely characterize the training error it achieves, as well as the relation between test and training error, in the high dimensional asymptotics $n,d\to\infty$, with $n/d\toα\in (0, +\infty)$. Based on earlier work in related models, we expect that the performance achieved by our algorithm is optimal among polynomial-time algorithms.

</details>


### [436] [What LLMs explain is not what they believe: Evaluating explanation sufficiency under models' own input beliefs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28615)
*Nhi Nguyen,Shauli Ravfogel,Rajesh Ranganath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends the concept of explanation sufficiency to LLM free-text explanations, proposes a metric (SCSuff) to evaluate self-consistent sufficiency using the LLM itself, and empirically shows that LLM explanations are generally insufficient, with sufficiency varying by input distribution and predictable from internal representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are used in high-stakes domains with free-text explanations (e.g., chain-of-thought), but it is unclear whether these explanations are sufficient—i.e., whether they contain enough information to explain the model's output-generating process. Existing sufficiency notions focus on feature attributions and do not generalize to arbitrary free-text explanations.

Method: The authors generalize classical sufficiency from feature attributions to arbitrary explanations, prove sufficiency depends on the input distribution, and propose using the LLM itself to generate alternative inputs conditioned on an explanation. They formalize self-consistent sufficiency and introduce SCSuff, an information-theoretic metric that evaluates free-text explanations without predefined biases or shortcuts.

Result: SCSuff agrees with targeted perturbation tests; explanation sufficiency varies with input distribution; LLM explanations are generally insufficient and weakly correlated with model size, accuracy, or output entropy; top and bottom SCSuff scores are predictable from final-token hidden states.

Conclusion: The proposed SCSuff metric can guide detection and improvement of sufficient LLM explanations, addressing a critical need for reliable explanation in high-stakes LLM deployments.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, where free-text explanations such as chain-of-thought and post-hoc rationales are used to justify model outputs. Yet it remains unclear whether these explanations are sufficient, i.e., if they contain enough information to explain the model's output-generating process. We generalize classical sufficiency from feature attributions to arbitrary explanations and prove that explanation sufficiency can change depending on the input distribution, which must be explicitly defined for LLM explanations. We propose using the LLM itself to generate alternative inputs conditioned on an explanation, capturing its beliefs about possible inputs. We formalize self-consistent sufficiency as a goal for free-text explanations and introduce an information-theoretic metric, SCSuff, that enables evaluation of free-text explanations without relying on predefined biases or shortcuts. Our experiments show that SCSuff agrees with targeted perturbation tests where applicable and demonstrate that explanation sufficiency can vary with the input distribution. We find LLM explanations are generally insufficient and weakly correlated with model size, accuracy, or output entropy. Analysis of final-token hidden states shows that top and bottom SCSuff scores can be predicted from internal representations, suggesting that SCSuff can guide detection and improvement of sufficient LLM explanations. The code for this paper is available at https://github.com/rajesh-lab/self-consistent-sufficiency .

</details>


### [437] [Randomized Exploration for Linear Bandits via Absolute Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28616)
*Toshinori Kitamura,Shuai Liu,Csaba Szepesvári*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Absolute Thompson Sampling (ATS) and its ensemble variant (EATS) for stochastic linear bandits, which combine the computational efficiency of Thompson Sampling with the analytical simplicity of UCB-style regret analysis by ensuring optimism in expectation through absolute-valued exploration noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thompson Sampling is computationally attractive but hard to analyze due to non-optimistic nature requiring anti-concentration arguments; UCB is analyzable but computationally demanding. The authors aim to bridge randomized exploration and deterministic optimism both theoretically and practically.

Method: ATS modifies Thompson Sampling by replacing signed exploration noise with its absolute value to ensure optimism in expectation. EATS takes the maximum over multiple absolute perturbations normalized by ensemble size, converging to the UCB objective as ensemble size grows.

Result: ATS achieves $\tilde{O}(d^{3/2}\sqrt{K})$ regret, matching existing TS bounds for linear bandits. EATS with moderate ensemble sizes yields strong empirical performance and recovers UCB behavior in the limit.

Conclusion: ATS and EATS provide a bridge between randomized exploration (TS) and deterministic optimism (UCB), offering computational efficiency with simple UCB-style regret analysis.

Abstract: In stochastic linear bandits, the canonical Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithm admits a simple frequentist regret analysis but can be computationally demanding, while Thompson Sampling (TS) is computationally attractive yet typically harder to analyze due to its non-optimistic nature. We propose Absolute Thompson Sampling (ATS), a simple modification of TS that ensures optimism in expectation by replacing the signed exploration noise with its absolute value. This preserves the computational efficiency of TS while avoiding the technically involved anti-concentration arguments common in TS analyses, enabling a simple UCB-style regret analysis. We show that ATS achieves $\tilde{O}(d^{3/2}\sqrt{K})$ regret, matching existing bounds for TS in linear bandits. We further introduce Ensemble Absolute Thompson Sampling (EATS), which takes the maximum over multiple absolute perturbations with normalization by the ensemble size. As the ensemble size grows, EATS converges to the UCB objective, recovering UCB behavior in the limit. Experiments show that moderate ensemble sizes already yield strong performance. Our results point to a bridge between randomized exploration and deterministic optimism both in theory and practice.

</details>


### [438] [Improving Patient Subtyping on Longitudinal Data using Representations from Mamba-based Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28623)
*Md Mozaharul Mottalib,Rahmatollah Beheshti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-supervised Mamba-based model for learning EHR representations that enables effective patient subtyping (clustering/grouping), demonstrating superior performance over baselines on public and private real-world EHR datasets for both classification and clustering tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Patient subtyping using EHR data is crucial for precision medicine, but temporal EHR data is challenging due to its complexity, irregularity, and noise. Existing methods struggle to learn effective representations from such data.

Method: A self-supervised Mamba-based model is proposed to learn EHR representations. The model is evaluated on public and private real-world EHR datasets for classification (using available labels) and patient subtyping (via clustering on learned representations). Multiple clustering techniques are assessed.

Result: The proposed model design choices outperform competitive baselines for prediction tasks. The learned representations also provide valuable insights for subtyping patients based on temporal EHR records.

Conclusion: The self-supervised Mamba-based approach is effective for learning temporal EHR representations and enabling enhanced patient subtyping, contributing to precision medicine efforts.

Abstract: Effective sub-typing (also known as grouping or clustering) of patients using their electronic health record (EHR) data can greatly inform precision medicine efforts. However, subtyping temporal EHR datasets is known to be challenging due to inherent EHR issues, including complexity and irregularity. In this study, we propose a self-supervised Mamba-based model that learns effective EHR representations and enables enhanced patient subtyping. We evaluate the proposed model on public and private real-world EHR datasets to classify the data based on the available labels and subtype patients based on the representations learned from the model. Through an extensive set of experiments, we demonstrate that our model's design choices lead to better performance compared to competitive baseline models for prediction. Moreover, we evaluate several clustering techniques to demonstrate that our findings offer valuable insights into subtyping patients based on temporal records from EHR models\footnote{Our implementations are available at https://github.com/healthylaife/triplet_mamba.

</details>


### [439] [When More Sampling Hurts: The Modal Ceiling and Correlation Ceiling of Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28661)
*Yong Yi Bay,Kathleen A. Yearick*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that while test-time scaling (sampling many times) improves coverage (finding a correct answer somewhere), it doesn't improve selection (returning one correct answer). The "identifiability gap" means extra sampling beyond a limit yields no benefit, adds cost, and can worsen results. The authors propose a metric—effective number of samples—to determine this cutoff.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem that language models over-sample during test-time scaling, wasting compute without improving the final selected answer, and to identify the point where additional sampling becomes counterproductive.

Method: The authors characterize the "identifiability gap" between climbing coverage and stalled selection, define "modal ceiling" and "correlation ceiling" as stopping points, and propose the "effective number of samples" as a single-number metric to determine the optimal cutoff from any sampling run.

Result: For selecting an answer, voting stabilizes within a few dozen samples (modal ceiling); for scoring a benchmark, convergence is even faster (correlation ceiling). Beyond these ceilings, extra samples add cost without benefit and can degrade performance.

Conclusion: The bottleneck in test-time scaling is recognizing a correct answer, not generating one. Sampling beyond the identifiability ceiling is wasteful and harmful; the effective number of samples provides a practical stopping rule.

Abstract: People overthink; language models over-sample, and the extra effort can talk both into a worse answer. Reasoning systems answer a hard question by sampling it many times (test-time scaling), and the more they draw, the more often a correct answer turns up somewhere, so coverage, the fraction of problems with at least one correct try, climbs and appears to be progress. But a deployed system must return one answer, and choosing it, not knowing which try is right, is selection; selection is capped, and past a point extra samples only make the model surer of a confident mistake, even as every draw adds cost. The gap between climbing coverage and stalled selection, the identifiability gap, is the answer a model can produce but not pick. So the real question is not whether to sample but how far, and the answer is: not far. For picking an answer, the vote has already settled within a few dozen draws, the modal ceiling; for scoring a benchmark, sooner still, the correlation ceiling. Beyond that, extra draws cost compute and add nothing, and can even make the answer worse. This paper turns the cutoff into a single number, the effective number of samples, that any sampling run already reveals. The bottleneck is recognizing a right answer, not generating one.

</details>


### [440] [Closed-Form Steepest Descent Direction toward Flat Minima: Reducing Upper Bounds on the Loss Hessian Eigenspectrum in Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28662)
*Yuto Omae,Kazuki Sakai,Yohei Kakimoto,Makoto Sasaki,Yusuke Sakai,Hirotaka Takahashi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper derives the analytical gradient of the Wolkowicz-Styan (WS) upper bound on the maximum eigenvalue of the cross-entropy loss Hessian in three-layer NNs, and proposes Hessian Spectral Range (HSR) Regularization to guide optimization toward flat minima by updating parameters along the steepest descent direction of this bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing eigenvalue-reducing algorithms lack clarity on how data distributions and NN parameters structurally determine directions toward flat minima; analytical characterization of these directions is generally intractable.

Method: Analytically derives the gradient of the differentiable Wolkowicz-Styan (WS) upper bound on the maximum Hessian eigenvalue for cross-entropy loss in three-layer NNs, and uses this closed-form gradient to formulate Hessian Spectral Range (HSR) Regularization.

Result: HSR Regularization narrows the Hessian eigenvalue spectrum, avoids sharp minima and saddle points, and promotes convergence to flat minima.

Conclusion: This is the first study to report a closed-form gradient that promotes convergence to flat minima without numerical approximations, though currently limited to cross-entropy loss and three-layer architectures.

Abstract: The flatness hypothesis suggests that flatness of the loss landscape, as measured by the eigenvalues of the loss Hessian, correlates with better neural network generalization. While various algorithms reduce these eigenvalues, most focus on procedural design, leaving it unclear how data distributions and NN parameters structurally determine directions toward flat minima. Characterizing these directions analytically is generally intractable. To overcome this mathematical difficulty, recent studies derived the Wolkowicz-Styan (WS) upper bound on the maximum eigenvalue of the cross-entropy loss Hessian in three-layer NNs. Although this upper bound is differentiable, its gradient was not derived. Therefore, we analytically derive the gradient of the WS upper bound to characterize directions leading to flat minima. Based on this, we propose Hessian Spectral Range (HSR) Regularization, which updates parameters along the steepest descent direction of the WS bound. Experiments demonstrate that HSR Regularization narrows the Hessian eigenvalue spectrum, avoids sharp minima and saddle points, and promotes convergence to flat minima. Although the applicability of this method is currently limited to cross-entropy loss and three-layer architectures, to the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study to report a closed-form gradient that promotes convergence to flat minima without numerical approximations. Therefore, the theoretical analysis of this gradient is expected to contribute to the further development of NNs.

</details>


### [441] [Entropy Regularized Reinforcement Learning for Zero-Sum Stochastic Differential Games in a Regime-Switching Jump-Diffusion Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28669)
*Congde Hu,Zhuo Jin,Danping Li,Lin Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a distributional control approach for zero-sum stochastic differential games under regime-switching jump-diffusion processes, addressing parameter misspecification and structural changes. It derives HJBI equations, provides semi-analytical solutions for LQ problems, and develops an Actor-Critic algorithm for general settings, with applications to an investment game.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of conventional SDG frameworks that assume perfectly known parameters and stable environments, which fail under parameter misspecification and sudden structural/environmental changes.

Method: Introduces a distributional control approach where optimal strategies are probability distributions over actions conditioned on continuous state, discrete regime state, and parameters. Uses DPP to derive coupled HJBI equations. For LQ problems, obtains semi-analytical solutions via coupled ODEs. For general settings, develops an Actor-Critic policy improvement algorithm.

Result: Semi-analytical solutions for linear-quadratic problems via coupled ODEs; an Actor-Critic algorithm for general problems; numerical examples from an investment game demonstrating the effect of temperature parameter and regime transitions on optimal policies and values.

Conclusion: The distributional control framework effectively handles parameter misspecification and structural breaks in SDGs, with the temperature parameter and regime transitions significantly influencing equilibrium strategies and value functions.

Abstract: To address parameter misspecification and sudden structural environmental changes in conventional stochastic differential game (SDG) frameworks, this paper introduces a distributional control approach that characterizes optimal strategies as probability distributions over actions, conditioned on the continuous state, the discrete regime state, and parameters. This forms a reinforcement learning framework for entropy-regularized zero-sum stochastic differential games (ERRL-ZSSDGs) in a regime-switching jump-diffusion process. Using the dynamic programming principle (DPP), we derive the associated coupled systems of Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs (HJBI) equations, from which equilibrium strategies are expressed via gradients of the value function. For linear-quadratic problems, semi-analytical solutions for both value function and equilibrium strategies are obtained by solving a system of coupled ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In more general settings, an Actor-Critic policy improvement algorithm is developed to approximate the value functions and equilibrium policies across different regimes. The method is applied to an investment game, and numerical examples illustrate the effect of the temperature parameter and regime transitions on optimal policies and values.

</details>


### [442] [Entropy-Regularized Reinforcement Learning for Linear-Quadratic Stackelberg Differential Games in Regime-Switching Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28671)
*Congde Hu,Danping Li,Lin Xu,Wenying Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an entropy-regularized RL approach for linear-quadratic Stackelberg differential games under Markovian regime switching, using neural networks to approximate value functions and solve high-dimensional PDEs, effectively escaping suboptimal equilibria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional SDG methods rely on complex dynamic programming and HJBI equations, which are computationally challenging in high-dimensional systems and often converge to suboptimal equilibria.

Method: Derives exploratory weakly-coupled HJBI equations with entropy regularization, integrates neural networks for regime-dependent value function approximation and high-dimensional PDE solving, and employs a novel sampling technique for computational tractability.

Result: The framework effectively escapes suboptimal equilibrium traps via exploratory policies, outperforming conventional SDG approaches in numerical experiments.

Conclusion: Entropy regularization and neural network approximations are critical for achieving robust solutions in hierarchical decision-making under abrupt environmental shifts.

Abstract: Stackelberg differential games (SDGs) provide a powerful framework for hierarchical decision-making in stochastic and continuous-time environments, yet their solution remains computationally challenging due to the complexity of traditional dynamic programming and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs (HJBI) methods, especially in high-dimensional systems. This paper proposes an entropy-regularized reinforcement learning (ERRL) approach for linear-quadratic SDGs (LQ-SDGs) within a continuous-time diffusion framework governed by Markovian regime switching. The key innovation lies in deriving exploratory weakly-coupled HJBI equations with entropy regularization, which promotes stochastic policies that actively avoid suboptimal equilibria -- a limitation of classical SDG methods. Neural networks are integrated to approximate regime-dependent value functions and solve high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) efficiently, while a novel sampling technique enhances computational tractability. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework compared to conventional approaches, particularly in escaping suboptimal traps through exploratory policies. The study highlights the critical role of entropy regularization and neural network approximations in achieving robust solutions for hierarchical decision-making problems under abrupt environmental shifts.

</details>


### [443] [Constrained Tabular Diffusion for Finance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28674)
*Michael Cardei,Jose M Munoz,Oscar Barrera,Shreyas K Chandrahas,Partha Saha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CTDF integrates training-free feasibility operators into tabular diffusion models to enforce hard constraints for financial data generation, achieving zero violations and improved scarce data utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard tabular diffusion models cannot satisfy both realism and strict regulatory/economic constraints required in finance.

Method: Incorporates a training-free feasibility operator into the reverse-diffusion sampling loop of mixed-type tabular diffusion to enforce hard constraints.

Result: Zero constraint violations and improved utility on scarce financial data across large-scale datasets.

Conclusion: CTDF provides a robust method for generating trustworthy and compliant synthetic financial data, enabling rigorous generative modeling under constraints.

Abstract: Generative models in finance face the dual challenge of producing realistic data while satisfying strict regulatory and economic objectives, a requirement that standard tabular diffusion models cannot provide. To address this difficulty, we introduce Constrained Tabular Diffusion for Finance (CTDF), a novel integration of sampling-time feasibility operations with mixed-type tabular diffusion in financial applications. By incorporating a training-free feasibility operator into the reverse-diffusion sampling loop, CTDF enforces hard constraints for applications such as simulation, legal compliance, and extrapolation. Extensive experiments on large-scale financial datasets demonstrate zero constraint violations and improvement in scarce data utility. CTDF establishes a robust method for generating trustworthy and compliant synthetic data, opening new avenues for rigorous generative modeling and analysis in the financial domain.

</details>


### [444] [A Path-Space Formulation of Prediction in World Models: From a Single Action to Prediction, Planning, and Irreversibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28751)
*Gunn Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A path-space formulation for prediction in AI world models is proposed, where world models define probability measures over future trajectories. Using Onsager-Machlup form, prediction, planning, and uncertainty emerge from a single action functional. Experiments show attention asymmetry encodes irreversibility, and symmetrizing it suppresses entropy production and degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI world models typically use sequences of one-step conditional distributions for prediction, lacking a unified probabilistic framework for trajectory-level reasoning. This paper argues that world models implicitly define probability measures over future paths, and a proper formulation should treat the full distribution over trajectories rather than just states.

Method: The authors propose a path-space formulation where the world model's latent dynamics define a path measure of Onsager-Machlup form when effective Markovian description exists. They decompose dynamics into reversible and irreversible components, introducing operational measures of entropy production from model rollouts. They test on controlled small-scale attention-based models.

Result: Attention asymmetry is acquired during training in proportion to data irreversibility. Symmetrizing learned attention suppresses entropy production and selectively degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics, while preserving relaxational prediction.

Conclusion: Irreversibility may serve as a computational resource for predictive world models. The fundamental predictive object is a distribution over future paths rather than states.

Abstract: We propose a path-space formulation of prediction in AI world models. Rather than sequences of one-step conditional distributions, we argue that a world model implicitly defines a probability measure over future trajectories. In the local regime where latent dynamics admit an effective Markovian description, this path measure takes the Onsager-Machlup form. Within this framework, prediction (most probable trajectory), planning (constrained optimization), and uncertainty (fluctuations) emerge as operations on a single action functional. We decompose the latent dynamics into reversible and irreversible components and introduce operational measures of entropy production from model rollouts. In controlled small-scale attention-based models, we find that attention asymmetry is acquired during training in proportion to the irreversibility of the data. Symmetrizing the learned attention suppresses entropy production and selectively degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics while preserving relaxational prediction. These results suggest that irreversibility may serve as a computational resource for predictive world models. More generally, the fundamental predictive object is a distribution over future paths rather than states.

</details>


### [445] [Hierarchical Decision Making with Structured Policies: A Principled Design via Inverse Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28764)
*Yuexuan Wang,Jingyuan Zhou,Kaidi Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a principled hierarchical RL-OC framework using inverse optimization to align lower-level objectives with long-term goals, outperforming baselines on resource allocation and collision avoidance tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hierarchical policies suffer from two key limitations: RL-based methods cannot guarantee constraint satisfaction, while OC-based approaches rely on myopic and computationally expensive formulations. The lower-level optimization in hierarchical RL-OC frameworks remains underexplored and often heuristic.

Method: The framework integrates upper-level goal abstraction with structured lower-level decision making via an inverse optimization approach, which learns the lower-level problem structure from expert demonstrations to ensure alignment with long-term task goals.

Result: Evaluated on network-based resource allocation and continuous collision avoidance tasks, the method consistently outperforms end-to-end RL, learning-augmented optimal control, and existing hierarchical RL approaches in both efficiency and decision quality.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical RL-OC framework with inverse optimization-based lower-level objective learning effectively resolves the trade-off between constraint satisfaction and computational tractability, achieving superior performance on complex decision-making tasks.

Abstract: Hierarchical decision-making frameworks are pivotal for addressing complex control tasks, enabling agents to decompose intricate problems into manageable subgoals. Despite their promise, existing hierarchical policies face critical limitations: (i) reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods struggle to guarantee strict constraint satisfaction, and (ii) optimal control (OC)-based approaches often rely on myopic and computationally prohibitive formulations. To reconcile these trade-offs, hierarchical RL-OC architectures have emerged as a promising paradigm. However, the formulation of the lower-level optimization within these frameworks remains underexplored, often relying on heuristic or myopic objectives. In this work, we propose a principled framework that systematically integrates upper-level goal abstraction with structured lower-level decision making. We adopt an inverse optimization approach to inform the structure of the lower-level problem from expert demonstrations, ensuring that the objective of the lower-level policy remains aligned with the overall long-term task goal. To validate the approach, our framework is evaluated on distinct decision making tasks: network-based resource allocation and continuous collision avoidance. Empirical results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines based on end-to-end RL, learning-augmented optimal control, and existing hierarchical RL approaches in both efficiency and decision quality.

</details>


### [446] [Generative Learning as a Tool to Improve Perception of Emotional Body Motion Expressions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28769)
*Huakun Liu,Miao Cheng,Xin Wei,Felix Dollack,Victor Schneider,Hideaki Uchiyama,Chia-huei Tseng,Yoshifumi Kitamura,Monica Perusquia-Hernandez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether a Transformer-based generative model can implicitly learn emotional body motions from motion-capture data of 49 Japanese actors across 13 discrete emotions, without explicit emotion-motion guidance. Evaluations show ~23-25% recognition accuracy from machine and human observers, and demonstrate utility for emotion recognition augmentation, pattern extraction, and emotion intensity transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emotional body motion is crucial for non-verbal communication in VR avatars and social robotics, but generating accurate emotional expressions is challenging due to subtle emotional cues, individual variability, and cultural differences. The authors aim to explore whether generative models can implicitly learn these expressions without explicit guidance.

Method: A Transformer-based generative model was trained on a dataset of emotional performances by 49 Japanese actors with 13 discrete emotion labels. The model learns to generate expressive motions conditioned on emotion labels. Evaluation includes an LSTM-based classifier (machine recognition) and a human perception study with Japanese raters, plus three practical application tasks.

Result: Machine recognition accuracy: 22.80% (LSTM classifier). Human recognition accuracy: 24.91% (Japanese raters). The model also proved useful for augmenting emotion recognition models, extracting representative emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing smooth transitions between emotion intensities.

Conclusion: Implicit, data-driven generative modeling shows promise for enhancing affective computing applications and deepening understanding of emotion expressions, despite modest recognition accuracies.

Abstract: Emotional body motion expressions are an essential element of non-verbal communication. Effectively conveying these expressions through technology is of utmost importance, for example, with virtual reality avatars and in social robotics. Recent advances in generative models have opened new opportunities for advancing research on emotional body motion learning. However, generating accurate emotional expression representations is challenging, given the subtlety of emotional cues, individual variability, and cultural differences. We investigate whether a generative model can implicitly learn emotional body motions directly from culturally grounded motion-capture data, without explicit emotion-motion guidance. Using a dataset of emotional performances by 49 Japanese actors, we trained a Transformer-based generative model to generate expressive motions conditioned on 13 discrete emotion labels. We evaluate the generated motions from two perspectives: (1) an LSTM-based classifier to assess recognizability by machine observers, achieving a recognition accuracy of 22.80%, and (2) a human perception study with Japanese raters to assess alignment with human affective interpretations, yielding a recognition accuracy of 24.91%. Beyond these, we evaluate the utility of generative modeling for three practical tasks: augmenting emotion recognition models, extracting representative emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing smooth transitions between emotion intensities. Our findings highlight the potential of implicit, data-driven generative modeling to enhance affective computing applications and our understanding of emotion expressions.

</details>


### [447] [On design-unbiased algorithmic Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28795)
*Li-Chun Zhang,Siu-Ming Tam,Luis Sanguiao-Sande,Wesley Yung,Anders Holmberg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies conditions under which machine learning algorithms (e.g., kNN, random forest) can achieve unbiased prediction/classification for finite populations without assuming true data models, using design-based inference from known probability sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical ML focuses on predictive performance (MSE, F-score) but does not guarantee unbiasedness, which is critical in domains like official statistics. The authors aim to bridge this gap by identifying conditions for unbiasedness without relying on true data models.

Method: The authors employ design-based inference relying on known probability designs of samples and training sets, rather than distributional or model assumptions. They examine how training data sampling, algorithm tuning, and out-of-sample performance assessment can be structured to achieve unbiasedness.

Result: The study identifies specific conditions under which algorithmic ML can yield unbiased predictions/classifications for a finite population, including sampling design considerations and tuning procedures that avoid dependence on model assumptions.

Conclusion: Unbiased prediction/classification from ML algorithms is achievable for finite populations under design-based inference, without requiring true data models, by carefully controlling how training data are sampled and how algorithms are tuned and evaluated.

Abstract: Machine Learning (ML) algorithms, such as k-Nearest Neighbours (kNN) or random forest, eschew the ideal of true data models in favour of predictive performance. However, minimising the MSE or F-score cannot lead to unbiasedness directly, which is important in many situations such as official statistics. We study the conditions of algorithmic ML, other than the existence and knowledge of true data models, which lead to unbiased prediction or classification for a given finite population, including how the training data may be sampled from the population, how a trained prediction algorithm can be tuned to achieve unbiased prediction or classification for that population, and how the performance of out-of-sample prediction or classification can be assessed unbiasedly. The inference is based on the known probability design of samples and training sets, rather than any assumed distributions or models.

</details>


### [448] [HARD-KV: Head-Adaptive Regularization for Decoding-time KV Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28831)
*Yuxuan Yang,Feiyang Ren,Bowen Zeng,Dalin Zhang,Jinpeng Chen,Gang Chen,Huan Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HARD-KV resolves the conflict between dynamic head-adaptive KV cache compression and static inference engine constraints via a Cascade Cache hierarchy, Logits Calibration for unified Top-p budgeting, and system-level index rewriting, achieving 2× throughput improvement on long-context math reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context LLM inference suffers from a "Static-Dynamic" mismatch: head-adaptive compression algorithms (e.g., Top-p sampling) require dynamic memory budgets for accuracy, but modern inference engines (e.g., vLLM) demand rigid static memory patterns to leverage CUDA Graphs and PagedAttention.

Method: (1) Cascade Cache hierarchy managing token lifecycle across dense, sparse, and condensed tiers; (2) Logits Calibration mechanism normalizing diverse importance metrics into a unified probability space for consistent Top-p budgeting across heterogeneous heads; (3) system-level solution rewriting fragmented dynamic indices into contiguous physical layouts compatible with high-performance inference engines.

Result: Up to 2× throughput improvement over static baselines while maintaining high-fidelity generation in 10k+ token scenarios on math-reasoning benchmarks (AIME, U-Math).

Conclusion: HARD-KV effectively bridges dynamic selection with rigid system constraints, offering a practical solution for efficient long-context LLM inference without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Long-context LLM inference faces a fundamental conflict: head-adaptive compression algorithms (e.g., Top-$p$ nucleus sampling) offer superior accuracy by dynamically fluctuating memory budgets, yet modern inference engines (e.g., vLLM) demand rigid, static memory patterns to leverage CUDA Graphs and PagedAttention. We resolve this ``Static-Dynamic'' mismatch with HARD-KV, a unified framework that that bridges dynamic selection with rigid system constraints. HARD-KV introduces a Cascade Cache hierarchy, managing the token lifecycle across dense, sparse, and condensed tiers. Crucially, we propose a Logits Calibration mechanism that normalizes diverse importance metrics into a unified probability space, enabling consistent Top-$p$ budgeting across heterogeneous heads. To bridge the efficiency gap, we offer a system-level solution, which rewrites fragmented, dynamic indices into contiguous physical layouts compatible with high-performance inference engine. Extensive experiments on math-reasoning benchmarks (AIME, U-Math) verify that HARD-KV achieves up to 2$\times$ throughput improvement over static baselines while maintaining high-fidelity generation in 10k+ token scenarios. Code is available at https://github.com/SuDIS-ZJU/HARDInfer.

</details>


### [449] [Active Quantum Kernel Acquisition for Gaussian Process Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28833)
*Jian Xu,Delu Zeng,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a shot allocation method for quantum kernel estimation in Gaussian process regression, using three closed-form sensitivity measures to guide non-uniform shot distribution, achieving 10–21% test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation on benchmarks and real quantum kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum kernel estimation on near-term hardware is shot-budgeted (finite circuit executions per kernel entry). Prior work addressed this for classification, but GP regression has tighter coupling between kernel error and downstream quantities (posterior variance, log-determinant, marginal likelihood), motivating a tailored sensitivity-aware allocation strategy.

Method: Three closed-form pair-level sensitivity measures are derived: (1) |α_i α_j| product, (2) leave-one-out residual, (3) marginal-likelihood gradient. These are combined with a Neyman-style minimum-variance allocation rule. A high uniform coverage floor (Frobenius lower bound) prevents over-concentration from noisy warm-up estimates.

Result: 10–21% test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation on four UCI benchmarks and two synthetic controlled studies (RBF + Bernoulli). Gains transfer to genuine ZZ and Pauli-Z quantum kernels (−13–15% at low budget, p<0.05) and four downstream tasks. Gains disappear on UCI features embedded into ZZ kernel due to exponential-concentration regime.

Conclusion: Sensitivity-driven non-uniform shot allocation significantly improves quantum GP regression under shot budgets, but the benefit vanishes when the kernel enters an exponential-concentration regime where shot allocation has nothing to exploit.

Abstract: Quantum kernel estimation on near-term hardware is shot-budgeted: every entry of the kernel Gram matrix is a Bernoulli expectation that must be sampled with a finite number of circuit executions. Recent work on quantum kernel classification has shown that allocating shots non-uniformly across kernel entries, weighted by their downstream task sensitivity, can reduce the shot budget required to reach a target accuracy. We extend this idea to Gaussian process (GP) regression, a setting whose downstream quantities (full-spectrum posterior variance, log-determinant, marginal likelihood) couple to kernel error more tightly than the sign-only outputs of classification. We derive three closed-form pair-level sensitivities predictive coupling $|α_iα_j|$, leave-one-out residual, and marginal-likelihood gradient and plug them into a Neyman-style minimum-variance allocation rule. To prevent catastrophic over-concentration when the warm-up sensitivity estimate is itself noisy, we add a high uniform coverage floor justified by a Frobenius lower bound on the missing-entry perturbation. On four UCI benchmarks and two synthetic RBF + Bernoulli controlled studies, the resulting allocator delivers $10$--$21\%$ test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation across the moderate-budget regime. The gain transfers (i) to genuine ZZ and Pauli-Z quantum kernels on quantum-natural data ($-13$--$15\%$ at low budget, $p<0.05$ paired) and (ii) to four downstream tasks (Bayesian quadrature, heteroscedastic regression, hyperparameter learning, multi-output Cokriging). On UCI features embedded into a ZZ kernel the gain disappears, consistent with the exponential-concentration regime where shot allocation has nothing to exploit.

</details>


### [450] [Fisher-Routed Mixture of Experts for Federated Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28835)
*Wenhao Yuan,Chenchen Lin,Jian Chen,Jinfeng Xu,Zewei Liu,Edith Cheuk Han Ngai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedFMX is a novel framework for Federated Class-Incremental Learning that uses Fisher-Routed Mixture of Experts with adaptive expert selection and routing-aware regularization to address capacity conflict, catastrophic forgetting, Non-IID heterogeneity, and class misalignment, achieving O(T^{-1}) convergence and superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending Federated Learning to class incremental learning introduces three key challenges: capacity conflict and catastrophic forgetting from shared model overloading, heterogeneity from Non-IID data, and synchronized class misalignment across clients.

Method: FedFMX consists of three modules: (1) Fisher-Routed Expert Scoring (FRES) estimating expert importance via Fisher-based stability cost and gradient-based plasticity gain; (2) Adaptive Expert Selection (AES) quantifying marginal contributions for adaptive expert subset determination; (3) Routing-Aware Regularization (RAR) for load balance and efficient FL training. Theoretical convergence rate of O(T^{-1}) is proven.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks comparing with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the superiority of FedFMX.

Conclusion: FedFMX effectively addresses unique FL class-incremental learning challenges via adaptive expert specialization, achieving both knowledge acquisition and retention with provable convergence guarantees.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) emerged as a promising distributed machine learning paradigm. However, extending FL to the class incremental learning scenarios introduces unique challenges: 1) Capacity conflict and catastrophic forgetting from the shared model overloading, 2) Heterogeneity from Non-Independent and Identically Distributed (Non-IID) data, and 3) Synchronized class misalignment. In this paper, we propose \textbf{F}isher-Routed \textbf{M}i\textbf{X}ture of Experts for \textbf{Fed}erated Class-Incremental Learning (\textsc{FedFMX}), a novel framework to address these challenges via adaptive expert specialization across clients. The crucial insight is to route each sample to an expert subset that jointly optimizes knowledge acquisition and retention. Specifically, we introduce a Fisher-Routed Expert Scoring (FRES) module to estimate expert importance via Fisher-based stability cost and gradient-based plasticity gain. Then, we design an Adaptive Expert Selection (AES) module by quantifying marginal contributions for adaptive expert subset determination. Finally, by the routing-aware regularization (RAR), we achieve load balance and efficient FL training. We theoretically prove the $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1})$ convergence rate. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks compared with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the superiority of \textsc{FedFMX}.

</details>


### [451] [The Contagion Tensor: A Framework for Measuring Output-Distribution Coupling in Multi-Agent LLM Systems -- and Auditing the Claims It Enables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28839)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Contagion Tensor and Coupling Amplification Factor (CAF) to measure how LLM outputs couple across modalities, agents, and time. Experiments show image-condition super-linear effects (CAF=1.40) collapse to sub-linear (CAF=0.87) when image module is ablated. Real-API tests on DeepSeek-Chat and GPT-4o-mini validate the simulation, with GPT-4o-mini vision reaching CAF=1.72. The contribution is a falsifiable measurement instrument and a transferable ablation protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks lack a quantitative, falsifiable way to measure how LLM output distributions couple across different modalities (text, vision), multiple agents, and sequential time steps. The authors aim to fill this gap with a tensor-based framework and ratio metric.

Method: The authors propose the Contagion Tensor to capture coupling across modalities, agents, and time steps, and derive CAF = E[T_condition] / E[T_baseline]. They instantiate four CAF variants, evaluate the strongest with a 2x2x2 block-orthogonal simulation with modality-specific ablation, and supplement with real-API experiments on DeepSeek-Chat and GPT-4o-mini.

Result: Simulation: image-condition CAF=1.40 collapses to CAF=0.87 after image module ablation (Δ=-0.53). Real APIs: text-only CAF≈1.0 in both models; diverse personas drive convergence (CAF=0.88). GPT-4o-mini vision CAF=1.72 vs text CAF=1.02 (Δ=+0.70). 5/11 conditions tested on real APIs.

Conclusion: The Contagion Tensor and CAF provide a falsifiable measurement framework for LLM output coupling across modalities and agents. The ablation protocol enables distinguishing genuine coupling from design artifacts in modular multi-agent simulators.

Abstract: We introduce the Contagion Tensor, a measurement framework for quantifying how large language model (LLM) output distributions couple across modalities, agents, and time steps. From the tensor we derive the Coupling Amplification Factor (CAF), a family of ratio-based metrics sharing the form CAF = E[T_condition] / E[T_baseline], providing unitless, baseline-referenced measurement with bootstrap confidence intervals. We instantiate CAF in four variants and evaluate the strongest in a complete 2x2x2 block-orthogonal simulation design with modality-specific ablation. The ablation reveals that an apparent image-condition super-linear effect (CAF = 1.40) collapses to sub-linear (CAF = 0.87) when the image perturbation module is disabled, a shift of -0.53 with zero effect on text conditions. We supplement with real-API experiments across two model families: DeepSeek-Chat (R=30) and GPT-4o-mini (R=15, real vision). Under uniform personas, text-only communication produces CAF approx 1.0 in both models. Diverse personas drive convergence (CAF = 0.88). A within-model comparison on GPT-4o-mini reveals: C3 (text) CAF = 1.02 vs. C5 (real vision, R=30) CAF = 1.72 [1.700, 1.733], delta = +0.70, validating the simulation's super-linear image-condition prediction. Of 11 conditions, 5 have been tested on real APIs and 6 remain unverified. Our contribution is two-layered: (1) a measurement instrument that makes output-distribution coupling quantitatively falsifiable; and (2) a transferable ablation protocol that any modular multi-agent simulator can adopt to distinguish genuine coupling from design artifacts.

</details>


### [452] [Analysis of Adam Algorithms for Stochastic Dynamic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28879)
*Xin Zheng,Yifei Jin,Lei Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes a general theory of Adam for time-varying and nonstationary stochastic systems, moving beyond conventional i.i.d. assumptions. It introduces new techniques for analyzing nonstationary random matrix products and constructs a stochastic Lyapunov function to derive tracking and prediction error bounds, validated by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theoretical analyses of Adam rely on time-invariant parameters and i.i.d. data assumptions, which are violated in time-varying and nonstationary systems. This gap calls for a theoretical investigation beyond the idealized i.i.d. setting.

Method: The paper introduces novel techniques for analyzing products of nonstationary and dependent random matrices induced by Adam's coupled first- and second-moment recursions, and constructs a new stochastic Lyapunov function that blends these two moment dynamics.

Result: Derives both parameter tracking and output prediction error bounds explicitly under a stochastic excitation condition, quantifying the effects of stepsize, momentum parameters, gradient noise, and parameter drift.

Conclusion: The bounds provide guarantees for Adam performance and offer guidelines for hyperparameter selection. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data validate the theory and design guidelines.

Abstract: The adaptive moment estimation algorithm, known as Adam, is widely used in modern machine learning, owing to its low per-iteration complexity and strong empirical performance. Despite its prevalent use, the theoretical foundation of Adam remains largely unexplored for time-varying and nonstationary systems. In fact, the existing theoretical analyses of Adam-type algorithms are primarily concerned with time-invariant model parameters and explicitly or implicitly rely on independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data assumptions, under which the learning taskcan be formulated as minimizing a fixed expected objective with a static minimizer. However, such assumptions are often violated in time-varying and nonstationary systems, thereby calling for a theoretical investigation beyond the conventional yet idealized i.i.d. setting. The main objective of this paper is to solve this challenging problem by establishing a general theory of Adam for time-varying and nonstationary stochastic systems. We will introduce some new techniques for analyzing the products of nonstationary and dependent random matrices induced by Adam's coupled first- and second-moment recursions, and will construct a new stochastic Lyapunov function that blends these two moment dynamics. Under a stochastic excitation condition that allows nonstationary and dependent data, we will derive both parameter tracking and output prediction error bounds explicitly, quantifying the effects of stepsize, first- and second-momentum parameters, gradient noise and parameter drift. These bounds not only provide guarantees for Adam performance, but also provide guidelines for hyperparameter selection. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world data validate our theory and design guidelines.

</details>


### [453] [An Integrated Machine Learning and Hierarchical Variance Decomposition Pipeline for Student Performance Prediction and Metacognitive Calibration on Multi-Signal Telemetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28881)
*Gurdeep Singh Virdee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UBP-CAP integrates performance prediction, metacognitive calibration assessment, and variance decomposition into a single pipeline. Applied to 1,195 records, Logistic Regression outperforms LightGBM (AUC=0.903 vs 0.878), students show significant miscalibration (ECE 0.109 vs 0.068), and calibration is situational (ICC=0.123). The proposed PEDI index (0.081, p=0.327) shows alignment between predictive and explanatory feature profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work treats performance prediction, calibration error calculation, and variance decomposition as separate pipelines, preventing a unified interpretation of student behavior and metacognitive alignment.

Method: UBP-CAP integrates three modules: (1) LightGBM with SHAP for correctness prediction, (2) formal calibration metrics (ECE, MCE, Brier score decomposition), and (3) crossed GLMM for decomposing calibration deviations. A novel PEDI metric quantifies divergence between predictive and explanatory feature profiles.

Result: Logistic Regression achieves AUC-ROC=0.903 (LightGBM: 0.878). Student naive ECE (0.109) exceeds model ECE (0.068). Crossed GLMM yields ICCStudent=0.123. PEDIcos=0.081 (p=0.327).

Conclusion: The unified pipeline enables joint analysis of prediction and calibration. Student miscalibration is systematic, and calibration is more situational than dispositional. Predictive and explanatory feature profiles are structurally aligned.

Abstract: Predicting student performance and characterizing metacognitive calibration are essential for personalization in intelligent tutoring systems. Prior research treats performance prediction, calibration error calculation, and variance decomposition as separate pipelines, preventing unified interpretation. I propose the Unified Behavioral Prediction and Calibration Analysis Pipeline (UBP-CAP), an integrated framework processing student pre-execution behavioral telemetry through three linked modules: (1) a LightGBM classifier with SHAP for binary correctness prediction, (2) formal calibration metrics (ECE, MCE, and Brier score decomposition) to evaluate metacognitive alignment, and (3) a crossed Generalized Linear Mixed-Effects Model (GLMM) for decomposing calibration deviations. I introduce the Predictive-Explanatory Divergence Index (PEDI), which quantifies structural divergence between predictive and explanatory feature profiles. Evaluated on 1,195 interaction records (27 students, 45 tasks), Logistic Regression achieves AUC-ROC = 0.903, outperforming LightGBM (0.878). Student naive ECE (0.109) significantly exceeds model ECE (0.068), confirming systematic miscalibration. The crossed GLMM yields ICCStudent = 0.123, showing calibration is situational rather than dispositional. PEDIcos = 0.081 (p = 0.327) indicates structural alignment between prediction and explanation on shared behavioral features.

</details>


### [454] [MALOQ: Massively Accelerated Learning of Operators for Quantum Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28911)
*Manasa Kaniselvan,Alexander Maeder,Denghui Lu,Alexandros Nikolaos Ziogas,Mathieu Luisier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MALOQ is a scalable application using SO(2)-equivariant neural networks to predict DFT Hamiltonian/density matrices for systems up to 100k atoms, achieving 30% faster training via edge-wise distribution and running on up to 256 GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable electronic-structure calculations at unprecedented scales (up to 100k atoms) by accelerating DFT Hamiltonian/density matrix prediction with ML models, overcoming the computational bottlenecks of traditional DFT.

Method: Uses an SO(2)-equivariant backbone architecture with (i) custom data-processing kernels for high-rank Hamiltonian matrices and (ii) scalable edge-wise distribution of atomic graphs, trained on the largest molecular Hamiltonian datasets.

Result: Reduces time-per-epoch by over 30% vs. molecule-wise distribution; enables training on 3,000-12,000 atoms using up to 192 GPUs and inference on up to 256 GPUs on the Alps supercomputer.

Conclusion: MALOQ demonstrates that scalable, edge-wise distributed, equivariant ML models can efficiently predict electronic-structure matrices for large-scale systems, opening the door to previously unfeasible quantum transport simulations.

Abstract: Machine-learned (ML) operator models can be trained to predict density functional theory (DFT) Hamiltonian/density matrices at significantly reduced computational cost, thus extending electronic-structure calculations to previously unfeasible scales. Here, we introduce MALOQ (Massively Accelerated Learning of Operators for Quantum Transport), an application built to train on and predict electronic-structure matrices for systems made of few to 100k atoms, described by large basis sets, and covering a wide range of atomic elements. Based on a state-of-the-art, SO(2)-equivariant backbone architecture, MALOQ provides (i) custom data-processing kernels to handle high-rank Hamiltonian matrix data and (ii) a scalable edge-wise distribution of atomic graph(s). Trained on the largest molecular Hamiltonian datasets available today, it reduces time-per-epoch by over 30% compared to a molecule-wise-distributed framework, and enables inference on material graphs of arbitrary size. We demonstrate scalable training and inference for 3,000-12,000 atoms on the Alps supercomputer, up to 192 GPUs and 256 GPUs, respectively.

</details>


### [455] [ML-Powered LDAP Reconnaissance Detection using Weak Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28917)
*Shaefer Drew,Edward Raff,Michael Brautbar,Yaron Zinar,Benjamin Malmberg,Dor Agron,Sagi Sheinfeld,Avraham Kama,Asaf Romano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents two ML frameworks to detect LDAP reconnaissance in Active Directory: a weakly supervised classifier for malicious query detection and a statistical hypothesis-testing method for mining malicious query signatures, achieving practical detection performance without costly manual labeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LDAP reconnaissance is a common initial step for threat actors after compromising an identity in Active Directory environments, as default read access to AD data makes LDAP a primary tool for information gathering. Early detection during this reconnaissance phase is critical but under-addressed.

Method: Two ML frameworks: (1) a weakly supervised classifier that correlates LDAP queries with endpoint detections to label and classify queries as malicious/benign; (2) a statistical hypothesis-testing method on top of the first approach to mine novel malicious LDAP query signatures.

Result: The classifier achieved up to 65% True Positive Rate (TPR) on the holdout set while limiting false positives; the signature mining method achieved 81.48% field precision validated by CrowdStrike's Managed Detection and Response team.

Conclusion: Weak supervision is a practical and scalable alternative to manual labeling for LDAP reconnaissance detection, enabling large-scale automated corpus construction with reduced cost and time. Both approaches met performance benchmarks suitable for deployment.

Abstract: Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) is a protocol that allows users to query and modify Active Directory (AD) data. By default, all users have read access to all AD data through LDAP, making it a common initial tool for reconnaissance when a threat actor first compromises an identity. To capture threat actors early in the reconnaissance phase, we developed two machine learning frameworks to detect LDAP reconnaissance: an ML classifier to predict malicious LDAP queries and an ML-based data-mining method to extract malicious query signatures. By correlating LDAP queries with endpoint detections, the first framework uses weak supervision to label a massive dataset and classify LDAP queries as malicious or benign. For immediate deployment, a second technique was developed on top of this approach to employ a rigorous statistical hypothesis-testing framework for mining novel, malicious LDAP signatures. While this weakly supervised approach is limited compared with manual human labeling, it is more practical for this use case because it leverages large-scale automated corpus construction, reducing costs and time. Ultimately, both the LDAP classifier and the ML-based LDAP signature mining method achieved performance benchmarks, with the classifier achieving up to a 65\% True Positive Rate (TPR) on the holdout set while limiting false positives, and mined signatures demonstrating 81.48\% field precision with CrowdStrike's Managed Detection and Response team.

</details>


### [456] [Towards Improved Anomaly Detection for Cloud Cybersecurity via Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28923)
*Manu Nandan,TJ Jaymes,Michael Brautbar,Edward Raff*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a self-supervised graph neural network approach applied to AWS CloudTrail logs for detecting suspicious cloud security events, reducing alert volumes from thousands to ~1 per hour across five organizations, though limitations include lack of false negative estimation and constrained evaluation on flagged events only.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increased reliance on cloud infrastructure necessitates automated threat detection, but existing heuristic or anomaly-based methods suffer from high false positive rates due to static nature, motivating a dynamic self-supervised learning approach using graph neural networks.

Method: Self-supervised learning with graph neural networks applied to AWS CloudTrail logs; produces an anomaly score per event and adapts dynamically to organizational changes without periodic retraining; evaluated across five organizations against a domain expert rule-based baseline.

Result: The model reduced alert volumes to approximately 1 per hour from thousands generated by traditional methods across five organizations, substantially outperforming the rule-based baseline in alert reduction.

Conclusion: The approach is effective as a practical deployment study offering real-world insights, but evaluation is limited to flagged events with no false negative estimates; future work includes extending to other cloud environments.

Abstract: Detecting security threats in an organization's cloud computing environment has become necessary due to the increased reliance on cloud infrastructure. Logging of all cloud computing events enables investigation into any incidents after they are detected. Automated detection of threats using the logs based on heuristics or anomaly detection could result in a high false positive rate due to its relatively static nature. In this article, we present an industrial case study of a self-supervised learning method using graph neural networks applied to AWS CloudTrail logs to surface suspicious events for analyst review. The model produces an anomaly score for each event and dynamically adapts to changes in the organization without requiring periodic retraining. Based on our experiments across five organizations, the proposed model produced substantially fewer alerts than a domain expert rule-based baseline in almost all cases, reducing alert volumes to approximately 1 per hour from thousands generated by traditional methods. We note that this evaluation covers only flagged events, and false negatives cannot be estimated from the current data; findings should therefore be interpreted as a practical deployment study offering insights into real-world constraints rather than a fully validated detection system. We discuss these limitations and the requirements for extending the approach to other cloud environments as future work.

</details>


### [457] [Multi-Agent Routing as Set-Valued Prediction: A WildChat Benchmark and Cost-Aware Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28925)
*Ananto Nayan Bala,Faisal Muhammad Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A benchmark derived from WildChat with 3,000 prompts over a 12-agent catalog for multi-agent routing evaluation, showing supervised routers (especially fine-tuned encoders and linear multilabel models) outperform nearest-neighbor and zero-shot LLM routing, with weighted routing improving cost-accuracy trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool and agent routing from natural-language prompts is a set-valued prediction problem where a single query may require multiple agents and over-selection increases cost, yet no standardized benchmark existed for evaluating multi-agent routing accuracy-cost trade-offs.

Method: Created 3,000 prompts from WildChat with AI-assisted heuristic labels over a fixed 12-agent catalog; evaluated methods including nearest-neighbor, linear multilabel classification, dependency-aware baselines, fine-tuned encoder, deterministic weighted post-scoring via Weighted Agent Routing (WAR), and zero-shot LLM baseline using set-level metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, Jaccard, Exact Match), latency, capability-coverage simulation, and constrained weighted-routing.

Result: Supervised routers substantially outperform nearest-neighbor and zero-shot LLM routing. Fine-tuned encoder achieves strongest unconstrained set accuracy; linear multilabel model provides strongest practical baseline. In constrained settings, weighted routing layer (Encoder+WAR) improves utility on top of strong supervised scorers.

Conclusion: The benchmark and evaluation protocol support reproducible study of accuracy-cost trade-offs in fixed-catalog multi-agent routing.

Abstract: Tool and agent routing from natural-language prompts is naturally a set-valued prediction problem: a single query may require multiple agents, while over-selection increases execution cost. The benchmark introduced here is derived from WildChat and contains 3,000 prompts over a fixed 12-agent catalog, with AI-assisted heuristic labels under a fixed schema and controlled rebalancing for multi-label evaluation. The evaluation protocol combines set-level metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, Jaccard, and Exact Match), latency, an execution-oriented capability-coverage simulation, and a constrained weighted-routing setting based on ordinal agent-cost tiers. Compared methods include nearest-neighbor matching, linear multilabel classification, dependency-aware baselines, a fine-tuned encoder, deterministic weighted post-scoring via Weighted Agent Routing (WAR), and a zero-shot LLM baseline. Results show that supervised routers substantially outperform nearest-neighbor and zero-shot LLM routing. The fine-tuned encoder achieves the strongest unconstrained set accuracy, while the linear multilabel model provides the strongest practical baseline. In the constrained setting, the weighted routing layer improves utility when applied on top of strong supervised scorers, with the largest gain observed for Encoder+WAR. Overall, the benchmark and evaluation protocol support reproducible study of accuracy-cost trade-offs in fixed-catalog multi-agent routing.

</details>


### [458] [DLR: Zero-Inference-Cost Latent Residuals for Low-Rank Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28932)
*Dong Wang,Wenwu Tang,Yun Cheng,Olga Saukh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DLR is a parameter-free plug-in that improves low-rank pre-training by adding a fixed structured residual to the up-projection, boosting quality without increasing deployment cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-rank pre-training reduces cost but typically underperforms full-rank training; DLR aims to close this quality gap without adding parameters or inference overhead.

Method: DLR augments the low-rank output Bz with a fixed structured residual α/√K · Expand_K(z) that replicates each latent coordinate K = ceil(d_out/r) times. After training, it is absorbed into B via B* = B + α/√K R^T, adding zero learnable parameters and zero deployment overhead.

Result: Across LLaMA models (60M–7B), DLR improves low-rank pre-training on C4 validation perplexity, with clearest gains at 130M+ scale. Folded checkpoints transfer cleanly to supervised fine-tuning.

Conclusion: DLR effectively strengthens low-rank pre-training at no extra deployment cost, making it a practical drop-in enhancement for scalable model training.

Abstract: Large language models have driven recent progress in language and multimodal AI, yet pre-training them at scale is prohibitively expensive. Low-rank pre-training, which factorizes each weight matrix into a rank-r product to reduce both parameters and FLOPs, is a promising response but typically lags full-rank training in quality. We propose Duplicated Latent Residual (DLR), a training-only, parameter-free, foldable plug-in for low-rank pre-training. DLR augments the standard low-rank output Bz with a fixed structured residual alpha/sqrt(K) * Expand_K(z) that replicates each latent coordinate K = ceil(d_out/r) times across the output. With alpha fixed, DLR adds zero learnable parameters per layer; after training, it is absorbed into the up-projection in closed form, B* = B + alpha/sqrt(K) R^T, so deployment parameter count, FLOPs and memory match the underlying low-rank backbone exactly. Across LLaMA models from 60M to 7B parameters, DLR strengthens low-rank pre-training on C4 validation perplexity in most settings, with the clearest gains at 130M and above; folded checkpoints transfer cleanly to supervised fine-tuning on standard benchmarks.

</details>


### [459] [ReGuide: From Test-Time Guidance to Self-Improving Diffusion Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28939)
*Tzu-Hsiang Lin,Srinivas Shakkottai,Dileep Kalathil,P. R. Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReGuide is a self-improving framework for behavior-cloned diffusion policies that generates corrective rollouts via Phase-Conditioned Guidance (PCG) and then absorbs them back into the policy through fine-tuning or retraining, achieving 1.3–7.7× success improvement on Robomimic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
