Motivation: Behavior-cloned diffusion policies suffer from covariate shift, where small deviations compound into failure. Existing solutions (expanding training distribution or test-time steering) are either expensive/assumption-dependent or discard useful corrective trajectories after use.

Method: ReGuide has two components: (1) Phase-Conditioned Guidance (PCG) generates corrective rollouts by constructing phase-specific latent targets, applying guidance only in drifted-but-recoverable states, and guiding via estimated clean actions to match the dynamics model's distribution; (2) guided rollouts are absorbed via ReGuide-FT (fine-tuning) or ReGuide-FS (retraining from scratch), which can be composed and iterated.

Result: On Robomimic Can, Square, Transport, and Tool Hang, ReGuide improves base-policy success by 1.3–7.7×, outperforms LPB in test-time-only setting, and gains come from guided recovery data rather than additional rollouts alone.

Conclusion: ReGuide demonstrates that treating guided rollouts as reusable on-policy recovery data effectively mitigates covariate shift for diffusion policies, offering a self-improving loop that outperforms prior methods.

Abstract: Behavior-cloned diffusion policies are expressive but remain vulnerable to covariate shift: small deviations from demonstrated states can compound into task failure. Existing methods address this either by expanding the training distribution through expert corrections or synthetic augmentation, or by steering a frozen policy at test time with guidance from a learned model. The former can be expensive or assumption-dependent, while the latter discards the corrected trajectories after execution. We introduce ReGuide, a self-improving framework that treats guided rollouts as reusable on-policy recovery data. ReGuide first uses Phase-Conditioned Guidance (PCG) to generate corrective rollouts: it constructs phase-specific latent targets, applies guidance only in the drifted-but-recoverable regime, and guides through the estimated clean action to match the dynamics model's training distribution. Successful guided rollouts are then absorbed back into the policy through ReGuide-FT, which fine-tunes the current checkpoint, or ReGuide-FS, which retrains from scratch on the augmented dataset; the two can also be composed and iterated. On Robomimic Can, Square, Transport, and Tool Hang, ReGuide improves base-policy success by $1.3$--$7.7\times$, outperforms LPB in the test-time-only setting, and matched-data ablations show that the gains come from guided recovery data rather than additional rollouts alone.

</details>


### [460] [Machine-learnable Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28947)
*Veit Elser,Manish Krishan Lal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper formally defines "machine-learnable" discrete sets (specifically binary strings) using bounded-complexity Boolean autoencoders implemented with Boolean threshold networks, demonstrates learnability on Rorschach patterns and wilder approximate sets, and shows an iterative method to evolve wild sets into properly learnable ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally characterize discrete sets whose elements are easily recognized, generated, and learnable from examples, bridging formal definition with practical neural network implementation.

Method: Defines machine-learnability via bounded-complexity Boolean autoencoders that fix set elements; implements autoencoders using nets of Boolean threshold functions; applies to Rorschach patterns (with possible contrast reversal) and wilder sets with approximate fixed points; uses iteration to evolve wild sets into properly learnable ones.

Result: Demonstrates machine-learnability for Rorschach patterns and considerably wilder sets where elements are only approximately fixed; shows simple iteration can evolve wild sets to become properly machine-learnable.

Conclusion: Boolean autoencoders with bounded complexity provide a viable formal framework for machine-learnability of discrete sets, with practical demonstration on both structured and wild patterns, plus an evolutionary mechanism for improving learnability.

Abstract: In this study we present a formal definition of large discrete sets having, informally, three properties: their elements are easily recognized, easily generated, and the latter tasks are easily learned from examples. The formalism is specialized to sets of binary strings and a definition of "machine-learnability" based on the existence of a bounded-complexity Boolean autoencoder that fixes the elements of the set. We present experiments where the autoencoders are implemented by nets of Boolean threshold functions. Machine-learnability is demonstrated for Rorschach patterns (that may have reversed contrast in the mirrored half), and considerably "wilder" sets whose elements are only approximately fixed by admissible autoencoders. In the second case we demonstrate a simple iteration that evolves wild sets to make them properly machine-learnable.

</details>


### [461] [Modification-Considering Value Learning for Reward Hacking Mitigation in RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28955)
*Evgenii Opryshko,Umangi Jain,Igor Gilitschenski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCVL is a safe RL method that filters training transitions by comparing forecasted returns with vs. without each transition, mitigating reward hacking while allowing legitimate improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL agents exploit misspecified rewards, achieving high returns while failing the intended objective (reward hacking). Existing defenses suppress both hacking and legitimate improvement due to conservative constraints.

Method: MCVL wraps an off-policy learner, treats each transition as a candidate modification, forecasts two training paths (include/exclude), scores both with a frozen bootstrapped-return estimator, and admits transitions only if inclusion doesn't decrease the score. Instantiated with DDQN and TD3.

Result: Across 4 safety-relevant gridworlds and 3 modified MuJoCo continuous-control tasks with diverse hacking mechanisms, MCVL mitigates reward hacking while continuing to improve the intended objective.

Conclusion: MCVL provides a safe and permissive filtering mechanism for value-based RL that balances hacking suppression and legitimate improvement.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning agents can exploit misspecified reward signals to achieve high apparent returns while failing on the intended objective, a failure mode known as reward hacking. Existing practical defenses typically constrain policy updates to stay near a known safe reference, creating a tension between suppressing hacking and permitting legitimate improvement. We propose Modification-Considering Value Learning (MCVL), which operationalizes the theoretical idea of current utility optimization for standard value-based RL. MCVL wraps an off-policy learner and treats each incoming transition as a candidate modification: it forecasts two training paths, one that includes the transition and one that does not, and scores both with a frozen bootstrapped-return estimator derived from a learned reward model and value function. The transition is admitted only if inclusion does not decrease the score. We formalize conditions under which this filtering is both safe and permissive, and instantiate MCVL with DDQN and TD3. Across four safety-relevant gridworlds and three modified MuJoCo continuous-control tasks with diverse hacking mechanisms, MCVL mitigates reward hacking while continuing to improve the intended objective. Project website: ktolnos.github.io/mcvl/.

</details>


### [462] [RGLD: Randomized Global-Local Density Estimation for Tabular Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28970)
*Quanling Zhao,Jiaying Yang,Ye Tian,Josh Victoria,Zhijun Wang,Pietro Mercati,Onat Gungor,Tajana Rosing*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGLD is a randomized global-local density estimator for unsupervised tabular anomaly detection that combines a global random-feature density branch and a local neighbor branch over feature-bagged randomized views. It achieves top AUROC performance across 47 datasets (ranking 1st in dataset wins), 2nd in AUPRC wins, and is 50x-580x faster than all evaluated deep detectors while remaining competitive with statistical methods in runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical statistical detectors are efficient but rely on fixed data views and single abnormality notions; deep detectors learn flexible scoring functions but are slow and difficult to tune in unsupervised settings. There is a need for a method that is accurate, robust across heterogeneous datasets, and computationally efficient.

Method: RGLD combines two branches operating over feature-bagged randomized views: (1) a global random-feature density branch to identify samples in broadly low-density regions, and (2) a local neighbor branch to detect samples weakly supported by nearby observations.

Result: On 47 tabular datasets against 23 baselines, RGLD achieves the strongest dataset-level AUROC (ranking 1st in wins), ranks 2nd in AUPRC wins, and achieves 50x-580x speedups over all evaluated deep detectors while maintaining runtime competitive with statistical methods.

Conclusion: RGLD offers a favorable accuracy-efficiency tradeoff for unsupervised tabular anomaly detection, outperforming both statistical and deep baselines in accuracy while being substantially faster than deep detectors.

Abstract: Unsupervised tabular anomaly detection requires methods that are accurate, robust across heterogeneous datasets, and computationally efficient. Classical statistical detectors are often efficient, but they usually rely on a fixed data view and a single notion of abnormality. Deep anomaly detectors can learn more flexible scoring functions, but they are substantially slower and difficult to tune in unsupervised settings due to the lack of a reliable supervisory signal. We propose RGLD, a randomized global-local density estimator for efficient unsupervised tabular anomaly detection. RGLD combines a global random-feature density branch, which identifies samples in broadly low-density regions, with a local neighbor branch, which detects samples that are weakly supported by nearby observations. Both branches operate over feature-bagged randomized views, allowing RGLD to expose anomaly evidence that may be hidden in any single representation. We conduct experiments on 47 tabular datasets against 23 statistical and deep anomaly detection baselines under fully unsupervised setting. RGLD achieves the strongest dataset-level AUROC performance, ranking 1st in dataset wins, and ranks 2nd in AUPRC wins. RGLD is also faster than all evaluated deep detectors, achieving 50x-580x speedups, and remains competitive with statistical methods in runtime, yielding a favorable accuracy-efficiency tradeoff.

</details>


### [463] [On Surrogate Modeling of Static Response of AM Short-Fiber Thermoplastics Using Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28996)
*Pharindra Pathak,Vipin Kumar,Trenton M. Ricks,Suhasini Gururaja,Siddhartha Srivastava*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-driven surrogate framework combining Voronoi-based cell discretization, nonlinear FE homogenization, and a hybrid GNN-LSTM architecture accurately predicts the mechanical behavior of additively manufactured, compression-molded short-fiber thermoplastic composites, achieving R²≈0.98 with over two orders-of-magnitude computational speedup, enabling identification of mechanically weak microstructural cells and accelerating digital-twin development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Short-fiber thermoplastic composites exhibit spatially heterogeneous mechanical behavior governed by fiber orientation, clustering, and porosity at the mesoscale, but high-fidelity 3D FE models are computationally intractable for practical use, motivating the need for an efficient surrogate that captures microstructure-property relationships.

Method: Microstructures reconstructed from micro-computed tomography data were discretized into Voronoi-based cells representing fiber-interaction neighborhoods. Each cell was homogenized via nonlinear FE simulations with matrix damage, and the resulting stress-strain responses trained a hybrid Graph Neural Network-Long Short-Term Memory (GNN-LSTM) architecture that encodes microstructural topology and history-dependent mechanical evolution.

Result: The surrogate predicts stiffness and stress-strain behavior of unseen microstructures with R²≈0.98 relative to high-fidelity FE simulations, achieving over two orders-of-magnitude reduction in computational cost. Fiber orientation, clustering, and porosity collectively govern local effective stiffness.

Conclusion: The physics-informed, data-efficient framework identifies mechanically weak microstructural cells and provides a practical pathway to accelerate digital-twin development for short-fiber thermoplastic components.

Abstract: Short-fiber thermoplastic (SFT) composites are increasingly employed in lightweight aerospace and automotive structures owing to their favorable strength-to-weight ratio, high production rates, and recyclability. Unlike continuous-fiber systems, the mechanical response of SFTs is governed by mesoscale interactions among fiber orientation, spatial clustering, and manufacturing-induced porosity. These features exhibit significant spatial variability in manufactured components and influence stiffness, damage initiation, and nonlinear deformation. Although mesoscale finite element (FE) models can resolve such heterogeneity, their application to realistic three-dimensional microstructures remains computationally intractable.
  A data-driven surrogate framework is proposed to predict the mechanical behavior of additively manufactured, compression-molded (AM-CM) SFTs. Microstructures reconstructed from micro-computed tomography data were discretized into Voronoi-based cells representing distinct fiber-interaction neighborhoods. Each cell was homogenized via nonlinear FE simulations incorporating matrix damage, and the resulting stress-strain responses trained a hybrid Graph Neural Network-Long Short-Term Memory (GNN-LSTM) architecture encoding microstructural topology and history-dependent mechanical evolution.
  The surrogate accurately predicts stiffness and stress-strain behavior of unseen microstructures, achieving $R^2\approx 0.98$ relative to high-fidelity FE simulations with over two orders-of-magnitude reduction in computational cost. Coupling the framework with experimentally calibrated damage laws demonstrates that fiber orientation, clustering, and porosity collectively govern local effective stiffness. The approach provides a physics-informed, data-efficient pathway to identify mechanically weak microstructural cells and accelerate digital-twin development for SFT components.

</details>


### [464] [How Far Can Sharpness and Complexity Jointly Explain Generalization?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29043)
*Ziyu Cheng,Xitong Zhang,Longxiu Huang,Rongrong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how sharpness and complexity jointly explain neural network generalization, finding that function-oriented definitions of these factors improve explanatory power over parameter-level metrics, but the two-factor view still falls short of a complete generalization theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generalization measures have been studied individually; the joint explanatory power of sharpness and complexity remains unexplored.

Method: The authors use linear regression and Pareto-based analysis to quantitatively evaluate the joint explanatory power of sharpness and complexity, and propose function-space-oriented definitions of these factors beyond traditional parameter-level definitions.

Result: Function-oriented definitions expand the explanatory scope of the two-factor view beyond parameter-level metrics; however, failures remain, indicating incompleteness.

Conclusion: The sharpness-complexity perspective is informative but not a complete theory of generalization.

Abstract: Sharpness and complexity are two central factors in the generalization analysis of deep neural networks. Existing quantitative evaluations of generalization measures have largely focused on individual scalar measures, leaving the joint explanatory power of sharpness and complexity largely unexplored. This work studies how far sharpness and complexity can jointly explain generalization. We use linear regression and introduce a Pareto-based analysis to quantitatively evaluate the joint explanatory power of these two factors. Beyond the existing parameter-level definitions, we further propose realizations of sharpness and complexity that are closer to function space and less dependent on raw parameter representations. We find that function-oriented definitions of these two quantities expand the explanatory scope of the two-factor view beyond what is achieved by existing parameter-level metrics. Overall, our results support the sharpness-complexity perspective as an informative lens for understanding generalization across diverse settings. At the same time, the remaining failures indicate that whether this two-factor view can serve as a complete theory of generalization remains open.

</details>


### [465] [MOSAIC: Orchestrating Collaborative Knowledge Tracing with Hierarchical Semantic Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29049)
*Xinjin Li,Mengyue Wang,Yuzhen Lin,Pengbin Feng,Ziqi Sha,Yeyang Zhou,Yu Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a Knowledge Tracing framework that uses a frozen LLM for semantic alignment and hierarchical prediction, achieving state-of-the-art AUC (up to 3.4% improvement) and accuracy (up to 2.5% gain) across multiple benchmarks, with strong robustness in collaborative and long-sequence settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional KT methods suffer from shallow ID-based representations lacking semantic depth and single-granularity mastery estimation that ignores hierarchical knowledge dependencies.

Method: MOSAIC leverages a frozen LLM to generate dynamic, context-aware embeddings and hierarchical prediction prompts, captures collaborative signals and peer interactions, and introduces a cross-granularity consistency objective that regularizes mastery estimation across concept, topic-cluster, and global proficiency levels.

Result: State-of-the-art results on ASSISTments, EdNet, and a new large-scale MOOC dataset; AUC improvements up to 3.4% and accuracy gains up to 2.5%; AUC of 0.862 on the MOOC dataset.

Conclusion: MOSAIC offers high predictive precision and semantically grounded interpretability, with superior robustness in collaboration-rich and long-sequence scenarios.

Abstract: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is important for personalized education but traditionally suffers from two key limitations: a reliance on shallow ID-based representations that neglect semantic depth and a restriction to single-granularity mastery estimation that overlooks hierarchical knowledge dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose MOSAIC (Multi-granularity Online Semantic AI for Collaborative Knowledge), a novel framework that orchestrates LLM-driven semantic alignment with sequential modeling. Unlike methods that use LLMs solely as predictors, MOSAIC leverages a frozen LLM to generate dynamic, context-aware embeddings and hierarchical prediction prompts, explicitly capturing collaborative signals and peer interactions. Furthermore, we introduce a cross-granularity consistency objective that jointly regularizes mastery estimation across concept, topic-cluster, and global proficiency levels. Extensive experiments on ASSISTments, EdNet, and a newly collected large-scale MOOC dataset demonstrate that MOSAIC establishes new state-of-the-art results. Specifically, our method achieves AUC improvements of up to 3.4\% and Accuracy gains of up to 2.5 \% across all benchmarks. Notably, MOSAIC exhibits superior robustness in collaboration-rich environments and long-sequence scenarios (AUC 0.862 on MOOC), offering both high predictive precision and semantically grounded interpretability.

</details>


### [466] [A Kernel Fisher Discriminant Analysis-Based Tree Ensemble Classifier: KFDA Forest](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29053)
*Donghwan Kim,Seung Hwan Park,Jun-Geol Baek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes KFDA Forest, a tree-based ensemble method that combines kernel Fisher discriminant analysis with random subspace partitioning and bootstrap sampling to improve classification accuracy on both linear and nonlinear data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensemble classifiers generally outperform single classifiers. The authors aim to develop an ensemble method that leverages KFDA's ability to maximize between-class scatter while minimizing within-class scatter, and to handle nonlinear data structures via the kernel trick.

Method: KFDA Forest uses bootstrap sampling for diversity and randomly divides variable sets into K subsets. KFDA is applied to each subset to create rotated feature axes via kernel transformation, and decision trees are used as base classifiers on these transformed features.

Result: The method was evaluated on real datasets from UCI and KEEL repositories and compared against existing ensemble methods.

Conclusion: KFDA Forest enhances classification accuracy by combining KFDA's class-separating capability, kernel trick for nonlinearity, and ensemble diversity through bootstrapping and random subspace partitioning.

Abstract: In general, an ensemble classifier is more accurate than a single classifier. In this study, we propose an ensemble classifier called the kernel Fisher discriminant analysis forest (KFDA Forest), which is a tree-based ensemble method that applies KFDA. To promote diversity, bootstrap is used, and variable sets are randomly divided into K subsets. KFDA is performed on each subset to increase classification accuracy. KFDA maximizes the distance between classes while minimizing the distance within classes. KFDA can also be applied to classification problems in a nonlinear data structure using the kernel trick because it can transform the input space into a kernel feature space, commonly named a rotation, rather than performing a dimensionality reduction. Because new feature axes and KFDA projections are parallel, decision trees are used as a base classifier. To compare the proposed method with existing ensemble methods, we apply these to real datasets from the UCI and KEEL repositories.

</details>


### [467] [When Can Conformal Risk Control Certify LLM Outputs? Bounds, Impossibility, and Adaptation for Structured Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29054)
*Varun Kotte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves an impossibility theorem for conformal risk control (CRC) on structured LLM outputs, establishes a certification hierarchy (Hoeffding → empirical Bernstein → e-CRC), validates adaptive conformal inference under distribution shift, and provides a three-step deployment recipe for reliable structured generation across 6 models, 8 datasets, and 4 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs deployed for structured generation (NER, JSON extraction, QA, classification) lack formal reliability guarantees, and heuristic abstention policies miss user-specified risk targets by 7.5–12.5%, motivating the need for distribution-free certification frameworks.

Method: The authors prove an impossibility result (closed-form abstention lower bound when base risk μ > α), compare three certification bounds (Hoeffding, empirical Bernstein, betting-based e-CRC) in a hierarchy, and validate adaptive conformal inference (ACI) under cross-dataset shift. Experiments span 6 open-weight models (3B–72B), 8 datasets, 4 tasks, and 6 nonconformity scores.

Result: Standard heuristics miss risk targets by 7.5–12.5%. Hoeffding-to-Bernstein yields largest certification gain (+37%); e-CRC provides 10% certification at 20% data vs. 0% for Hoeffding. ACI reduces risk-target violations from 71% to 21%. Hard NER/QA/CLS configurations are uncertifiable at α=0.10; relaxing to α=0.30–0.40 unlocks 47% NER, 40% QA, 60% CLS certification.

Conclusion: The framework provides a three-step deployment recipe: (1) check feasibility via the closed-form bound, (2) select the optimal bound and nonconformity score, (3) mitigate shift using ACI. The impossibility bound accurately predicts where residual failures concentrate.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) deployed for structured generation (NER, JSON extraction, QA, and classification) lack formal reliability guarantees, and standard heuristic abstention policies miss user-specified risk targets by 7.5--12.5%. We characterize when conformal risk control (CRC) can certify structured LLM outputs and when it provably cannot. First, we prove an impossibility result: when the base risk (μ> α), any distribution-free method must abstain on at least ((μ-α)/(1-α)) examples, yielding a closed-form feasibility test: one can check whether CRC will work before running it. Second, we analyze a certification hierarchy across Hoeffding, empirical Bernstein, and a betting-based e-CRC bound, with strict gains in low-variance/large-sample regimes: the Hoeffding-to-Bernstein step delivers the largest gain (+37% certified configurations), while e-CRC adds value when calibration data is scarce (10% certification at 20% data versus 0% for Hoeffding). Third, we validate adaptive conformal inference (ACI) under cross-dataset shift, reducing risk-target violations from 71% to 21%, with residual failures concentrated exactly where the impossibility bound predicts. Across six open-weight models (3B--72B parameters), eight datasets, four tasks, and six nonconformity scores, hard NER/QA/CLS configurations are uncertifiable at (α= 0.10); relaxing to (α= 0.30--0.40) unlocks practical certification (47% NER, 40% QA, 60% CLS). The framework gives a three-step deployment recipe: check feasibility, select the bound and score, then mitigate shift.

</details>


### [468] [Statistically Indistinguishable, Operationally Distinct: A Formal Barrier for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29091)
*Tassilo Klein,Johannes Hoffart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tabular foundation models cannot reason about operational data without access to governing rules; they hit a fundamental identifiability barrier (Bayes error ≥0.49) when only column-value marginals are available, and even frontier LLMs fail on legal/rule-violating state classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To demonstrate that tabular foundation models are fundamentally incapable of reasoning about data from running systems without access to the operational rules that govern them, and to make this claim falsifiable through a formal benchmark.

Method: Proposes the Operational Turing Test (OTT) with paired legal/rule-violating database states matched on 1- and 2-way column-value marginals (TV<0.02). Uses Le Cam's lemma to bound Bayes error for values-only classifiers at ≥0.49. Tests XGBoost, TabICL, TabPFN, raw row-level access, relational value consistency, and seven executable rule-derived audits. Also tests frontier LLMs (GPT-5.5) with schema, trigger source, rule tables, and state files across two schema families.

Result: Values-only baselines hit the theoretical bound (accuracy 0.50, TOST p<0.002). Raw row-level access does not help. Relational value consistency closes most of the gap. Only seven rule-derived audits achieve 1.00 accuracy. LLMs classify at most 2/50 legal states as LEGAL; GPT-5.5 accepts 0/50. Pattern replicates on a second schema (banking ledger).

Conclusion: The barrier is identifiability, not capacity: scale, data, and richer features cannot overcome it without operational grounding (access to executable rules).

Abstract: Tabular foundation models cannot reason about data produced by running systems without access to the rules that govern them. We make this statement falsifiable. The \emph{Operational Turing Test} (OTT) constructs pairs of legal and rule-violating database states whose $1$- and $2$-way column-value marginals match to a total variation of $<0.02$; Le~Cam's lemma then bounds any values-only classifier at $\geq0.49$ Bayes error. Three values-only baselines (XGBoost, TabICL, TabPFN) hit the bound exactly (accuracy $0.50$, pre-registered two one-sided tests (TOST) $p<0.002$), raw row-level access does not help, exposing relational value consistency closes most of the gap, and only a classifier fed by seven executable rule-derived audits reaches $1.00$ classification accuracy. In three matched $100$-state frontier large-language-model (LLM) runs, models given the schema, trigger source, rule tables, and state files classify at most $2/50$ legal states as LEGAL; GPT-5.5 accepts $0/50$ legal states even with higher reasoning effort and a Structured Query Language (SQL) executor. The access-ladder pattern also appears on a second schema with structurally distinct rule families (banking ledger: cross-row balance, cumulative aggregate). The barrier is identifiability, not capacity: scale, data, and richer features cannot cross it without operational grounding.

</details>


### [469] [Priced Motion Through Optimal Faces: A Normal-Fan Geometry for Non-Stationary Adversarial MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29092)
*Kai Hidajat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a normal-fan geometry for adversarial MDPs to distinguish consequential non-stationarity (face-crossings that incur regret) from harmless non-stationarity (movement within a cone that leaves the optimal policy unchanged), introducing a face-crossing price that decomposes dynamic regret into intrinsic priced face motion and within-face selection error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard dynamic-regret analyses equate non-stationarity cost to how far loss moves, but loss can travel far without changing the optimal policy, or move slightly yet force a complete policy change. Thus, existing metrics (loss variation, transition variation, comparator path length) describe adversary motion but not the actual cost to the control problem.

Method: The paper develops a normal-fan geometry for finite-horizon adversarial MDPs with fixed transitions. Occupancy measures form a polytope; each loss vector exposes an optimal face. Non-stationarity in rewards is modeled as a path through the normal fan. The face-crossing price is defined as the minimum regret incurred by staying on the previous optimal face under the new loss. Dynamic regret decomposes exactly into intrinsic priced face motion plus within-face selection error.

Result: Dynamic regret decomposes exactly into intrinsic priced face motion plus within-face selection error. Loss variation can be arbitrarily large at zero price, and identical one-coordinate variation can hide horizon-scale differences in regret.

Conclusion: The theory separates consequential from harmless non-stationarity, providing a more faithful analytic interpretation than standard dynamic-regret analyses that conflate movement magnitude with regret cost.

Abstract: In a changing decision problem, standard dynamic-regret analyses have often equated the cost of non-stationarity to how far loss moves. However, it is simultaneously possible for a loss sequence to travel far and retain the same optimal policy, or for a small movement in loss to force the optimal policy to change completely. Thus, the size of the movement through loss variation, transition variation, or comparator path length describe the adversary's motion, but not the cost of that motion to the control problem. For a more faithful analytic interpretation, this paper develops a normal-fan geometry for finite-horizon adversarial MDPs with fixed transitions. Occupancy measures form a polytope, and each loss vector exposes an optimal face of that polytope. Non-stationarity in rewards is therefore a path through the normal fan, where motion inside one cone leaves the optimal face unchanged, while crossing a wall may carry regret. We pose the notion of a face-crossing price, which is the minimum regret incurred by remaining on the previous optimal face under the new loss. For any learner that tracks the previous face, dynamic regret decomposes exactly into intrinsic priced face motion plus within-face selection error. The resulting theory separates consequential from harmless non-stationarity, where loss variation can be arbitrarily large at zero price, and identical one-coordinate variation can hide horizon-scale differences in regret.

</details>


### [470] [DiLaServe: High SLO Attainment Serving for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29094)
*Tzu-Tao Chang,Benjamin Yuanyang Hong,Kiet Pham,Shivaram Venkataraman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiLaServe is a cluster-level serving system for diffusion language models that improves SLO attainment by up to 56.6 percentage points and reduces latency by up to 46% via deadline-aware scheduling, adaptive confidence-threshold control, and quality-aware dynamic reconfiguration that models KV caching heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion language models (DLMs) offer higher inference throughput than autoregressive models but pose new serving challenges: managing the speed-quality tradeoff from confidence-based denoising, selecting parallelization levels under fluctuating load, and coordinating approximate KV caching with non-uniform per-step costs. Existing systems do not address these DLM-specific challenges.

Method: DiLaServe employs (1) deadline-aware scheduling and adaptive load control via confidence-threshold adjustment, (2) dynamic cluster reconfiguration using a quality-aware optimization problem that explicitly models step-level heterogeneity from approximate KV caching.

Result: Up to 56.6 percentage point improvement in SLO attainment, up to 46% reduction in end-to-end request latency, with less than 1% accuracy drop across multiple benchmarks and real-world traces.

Conclusion: DiLaServe effectively addresses DLM-specific serving challenges and delivers substantial improvements in both SLO attainment and latency while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to conventional autoregressive language models. By generating multiple tokens in parallel during each denoising step, they offer higher inference throughput while maintaining competitive quality. However, realizing these throughput gains while meeting latency SLOs in a serving system requires addressing challenges introduced by DLMs' unique characteristics. These include navigating the speed-quality tradeoff created by confidence-based denoising, choosing appropriate parallelization levels across model instances under fluctuating load, and coordinating approximate KV caching mechanisms that introduce non-uniform per-step costs. To address these challenges, we present DiLaServe, a cluster-level serving system for DLMs. DiLaServe enables deadline-aware scheduling and adaptive load control through confidence-threshold adjustment, and dynamically reconfigures the cluster by solving a quality-aware optimization problem, while explicitly modeling the step-level heterogeneity introduced by approximate KV caching. Across multiple benchmarks and real-world traces, DiLaServe improves SLO attainment by up to 56.6 percentage points and reduces end-to-end request latency by up to 46\% while incurring less than 1\% accuracy drop.

</details>


### [471] [Few-Step Boltzmann Generators via Scalable Likelihood Flow Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29110)
*RuiKang OuYang,Hanlin Yu,Xinyue Ai,Yutong He,Nicholas M. Boffi,Pradeep Ravikumar,Jose Miguel Hernandez-Lobato,Max Simchowitz,Benjamin Kurt Miller,Omar Chehab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCALLOP is a scalable likelihood distillation method for flow-based generative models that eliminates Hutchinson's trace estimator, reduces variance, speeds up training, and achieves competitive performance with up to 10x inference speedup over baselines on molecular and image datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing flow-based generative models lack efficient likelihood estimation; methods either rely on restrictive architectures for exact computation or use stochastic approximations like Hutchinson's estimator that introduce high variance.

Method: SCALLOP introduces a Hutchinson-free, vectorizable likelihood distillation objective built on top of F2D2, replacing the noisy Hutchinson trace estimator with a more scalable alternative.

Result: SCALLOP significantly reduces training variance and training time, consistently improves performance over F2D2, and is competitive with state-of-the-art while achieving up to 10x inference speedup over the fastest baseline.

Conclusion: SCALLOP is an effective Boltzmann generator in molecular science and demonstrates benefits on image datasets, offering a practical and scalable solution for likelihood estimation in flow-based models.

Abstract: Recent progress in flow-based generative modeling has led to models that output high-quality samples while using only a small number of function evaluations. However, at present, there is a lack of similar advances in estimating the model likelihood. In particular, most existing methods either rely on restrictive architectures that enable exact calculations, or use stochastic approximations such as Hutchinson's trace estimator that introduce substantial variance. In this work, we introduce SCAlable LikeLihood distillation of flOw maPs (SCALLOP). SCALLOP builds on the recently proposed F2D2, a likelihood flow map model that can generate samples and their densities in a small number of function evaluations. While F2D2 uses Hutchinson's estimator during training, we introduce an alternative and more scalable likelihood distillation objective that is Hutchinson-free and admits a vectorized formulation. Empirically, we demonstrate the effectiveness of SCALLOP as a Boltzmann generator in molecular science, and further validate its benefit on image datasets. SCALLOP significantly reduces both training variance and training time while consistently improving performance compared to F2D2, and is competitive with the state-of-the-art while achieving up to 10x inference speedup over the fastest baseline.

</details>


### [472] [A Novel Latent-Class Attack and its Detection by Class Subspace Orthogonalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29112)
*Guangmingmei Yang,David J. Miller,George Kesidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a latent class data poisoning attack where examples from a novel (unknown) class are mislabeled as a known target class, causing models to treat the novel class as a subclass of the target. It also proposes a post-training defense using class subspace orthogonalization (CSO) to detect such attacks without accessing the training set, alongside a visualization method for explainability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models are vulnerable to data poisoning attacks (error-generic and backdoor/Trojan attacks), and existing attacks primarily focus on manipulating known classes. The authors identify a gap: attacks exploiting a novel (unknown) class mislabeled as a known class to create stealthy backdoors that bypass standard defenses, which could be used to defeat AI-based access control systems or misclassify foes as friends.

Method: The latent class attack poisons training data by taking samples from a class that does not exist in the original classification domain (novel class) and mislabeling them to a chosen target class. The model then learns to recognize the novel class as a subclass of the target. For defense, they propose a post-training detection method using class subspace orthogonalization (CSO), a plug-and-play paradigm that improves existing backdoor detectors. CSO seeks an input whose internal representation does not align with any known class yet is classified with high confidence to one class. For image domains, they add a visualization method to estimate and render the unknown class instance for explainability.

Result: The latent class attack successfully causes models to misclassify novel class instances as the target class, creating a backdoor. The CSO-based defense effectively detects such attacked inputs without requiring access to the training set. The visualization method provides interpretable representations of the detected unknown class instances.

Conclusion: The paper introduces a novel data poisoning vector (latent class attack) exploiting unknown classes, and demonstrates that post-training detection via class subspace orthogonalization is feasible without training data access, with added explainability through visualization.

Abstract: Deep learning, which in general relies on voluminous amounts of training data, is vulnerable to data poisoning attacks, including error-generic attacks and backdoors (Trojans). In this work, we propose a new data poisoning attack we dub a latent class attack. Here, all poisoned examples are from a class that is novel (unknown) for the given classification domain and are mislabeled to one of the known classes (the target class) of the domain, so that the model learns to recognize the novel class as a sub-class of the target class. Such attacks could be used e.g. to defeat AI-based access control systems, or could cause a "foe" to be classified as a "friend". We also propose a post-training defense to detect this attack, without any access to the training set. This detection approach builds on "class subspace orthogonalization" (CSO), a plug-and-play paradigm demonstrated to improve existing backdoor detectors. Here, CSO is used to seek an input (a putative unknown class instance) whose internal representation is not aligned with any of the known classes, and yet which is classified with confidence to one of these classes. Finally, specific to image classification domains, we propose a method for visualizing the estimated unknown class instance, providing explainability to our latent class detections.

</details>


### [473] [How Token Influence Decays with Distance: A Green-Function View of Trained Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29139)
*Matthias Brändel,Stephan Köhler,Oliver Rheinbach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies how perturbations of earlier token embeddings affect next-token predictions in autoregressive Transformers. Inspired by multilevel methods for differential equations, the authors compute distance-resolved Jacobian sensitivity profiles and find that median sensitivity decays as a power law (exponent p≈0.7–0.9) rather than exponentially. This long-tailed behavior is learned (absent in random models) and persists even when syntax is destroyed by token shuffling, suggesting that hierarchical or coarse-level mechanisms could leverage this property.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how token influence decays with distance in trained autoregressive Transformers, drawing inspiration from multilevel methods (domain decomposition, multigrid) in differential equations where strong local interactions are separated from weaker but essential global interactions (the long tail of Green's function) handled by coarse-level operators.

Method: Compute empirical distance-resolved gradient profiles of token dependencies using autograd (Jacobian sensitivity). Fit median sensitivity with a power-law-plus-offset model Ḡ(r) ≈ γ + β(r+1)^(−p). Experiments on Pythia and Qwen2.5-0.5B models, using text from Gutenberg and WikiText-103, plus token-shuffling and random-initialization ablation studies.

Result: Median Jacobian sensitivity decays as a power law (exponents p≈0.7–0.9, typically 0.8–0.9), not exponentially. This profile persists under token shuffling (syntax collapsed) but is absent in randomly initialized models, confirming it is a learned property.

Conclusion: Trained autoregressive Transformer operators exhibit slowly decaying long-range sensitivity that follows a power law, suggesting that hierarchical or coarse-level mechanisms (analogous to multigrid methods) could exploit this property for improved efficiency or understanding of language models.

Abstract: We study how the next-token prediction of an autoregressive Transformer language model changes under small perturbations of earlier input token embeddings. Motivated by operator learning and iterative solvers for differential equations, we investigate how the influence of one token on another decays with distance in a trained model. In multilevel methods for differential equations, such as domain decomposition, multigrid, and multilevel preconditioning, one often exploits a separation between strong local interactions and weaker but essential global interactions. The latter correspond to the long tail of the Green's function and are typically handled by a coarse-level operator. Inspired by this perspective, we compute an empirical, distance-resolved gradient profile of token dependencies using autograd. Experiments on trained Pythia models and Qwen2.5-0.5B show that, over the measured distance range, the median Jacobian sensitivity is much better described by a power-law-type decay than by an exponential alternative: the diagonal-normalized profile is well described by $$\overline G(r) \approx γ+β(r+1)^{-p}$$ with exponents $p \approx 0.7$--$0.9$ (typically $0.8$--$0.9$). This behavior appears on coherent text from Gutenberg and WikiText-103. Token-shuffling experiments show that the power-law profile persists even when syntax and prediction quality collapse, whereas randomly initialized models do not exhibit it. The slowly decaying long-range sensitivity thus appears to be a learned property of trained autoregressive Transformer operators. These findings suggest that hierarchical or coarse-level mechanisms in language models may be able to exploit the long-tailed sensitivity profiles.

</details>


### [474] [On the Nonlinearity of Learning Rate Scaling for LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29158)
*Zaiwen Yang,Huaqing Zhang,Jing Xu,Jingzhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies that optimal learning rate scaling for LLMs deviates from log-linear at larger scales due to slow weight-norm convergence, and shows this curvature disappears when using effective learning rate (step size in normalized weight space) and D-extrapolation rather than N-extrapolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the cost of training large language models by accurately extrapolating optimal learning rates from smaller runs, as existing log-linear scaling laws become inaccurate at larger scales.

Method: Empirical study of GPT-2-style models (22M–707M parameters) trained on 5B–100B tokens; comparison of raw learning rate vs. effective learning rate (step size in normalized weight space); analysis of weight-norm convergence dynamics; experiments with AdamH for direct effective learning rate control.

Result: Optimal learning rate develops upward curvature at larger scales, breaking log-linear scaling. Curvature disappears when using effective learning rate and D-extrapolation (data scale) instead of N-extrapolation (model size). Nonlinearity is explained by slower weight-norm equilibrium convergence when optimal learning is small.

Conclusion: Effective learning rate and data-scale extrapolation provide more accurate LR transfer; the curvature stems from transient-phase dynamics in weight-norm convergence, which AdamH can mitigate.

Abstract: Learning-rate transfer can reduce the cost of training large language models: instead of sweeping learning rates at target scale, practitioners extrapolate from smaller runs. Existing approaches often assume that the optimal learning rate follows a log-linear scaling law in data scale and model size. We carefully examine and evaluate this scaling law. In our empirical study of GPT-2--style models from 22M to 707M parameters trained on 5B to 100B tokens, the optimal learning rate develops upward curvature at larger scales, leading to inaccurate extrapolation. We find that this curvature largely disappears when learning rates are replaced by effective learning rate (the step size in normalized weight space), and when data $D$ extrapolation is used instead of model size $N$ extrapolation. Next, we explain nonlinearity in scaling: weight-norm converges to equilibrium slower when optimal learning is small, requiring a larger step size to reduce the transient phase. Experiments with AdamH, which directly controls the effective learning rate, further support this explanation.

</details>


### [475] [GLACIER: Rethinking Mass Spectrum Prediction as an Object Detection Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29161)
*Rui-Xi Wang,Runzhong Wang,Connor W. Coley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLACIER is a single-stage transformer-based fragment detection neural network for predicting tandem mass spectra from molecular structures, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy (70.0% Top-1 on MassSpecGym, 52.5% on NIST'20) with 8x faster inference compared to prior two-stage models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting tandem mass spectra (MS/MS) from molecular structures is a central task in analytical chemistry with applications in clinical metabolomics and systems biology. Existing fragment-based models use a two-stage paradigm (candidate generation then scoring), which is suboptimal for accuracy and inference speed.

Method: GLACIER reformulates MS/MS prediction as object detection on molecular graphs, specifically as detecting subgraphs (fragments) and their spectral contributions. It uses a single-stage transformer architecture that directly predicts fragments without candidate enumeration, enabling globally consistent modeling of molecular fragmentation.

Result: Achieves 70.0% and 69.7% Top-1 retrieval accuracy on MassSpecGym (previous SOTA 64.0%), and 52.5% and 38.5% on NIST'20 (previous SOTA 33.2%). Provides nearly 8-fold inference speedup over prior two-stage models.

Conclusion: The single-stage transformer-based approach eliminates candidate enumeration, achieving higher accuracy and faster inference for MS/MS prediction, setting new state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmark datasets.

Abstract: Predicting tandem mass spectra (MS/MS) from molecular structures represents a central task in analytical chemistry with direct relevance to clinical metabolomics, systems biology, and adjacent disciplines. In this work, we revisit the problem through the lens of object detection on molecular graphs. Molecular fragmentation, a central step in MS/MS prediction, can be approximated as detecting a set of subgraphs (i.e., fragments) and their associated spectral contributions. Existing fragment-based models follow a two-stage paradigm -- first generating candidate fragments and then scoring them -- analogous to two-stage R-CNNs in computer vision. Towards higher accuracy and faster inference, we introduce GLACIER, a single-stage transformer-based fragment detection neural network for molecular graphs. This unified formulation eliminates the need for candidate enumeration, enabling scalable and globally consistent modeling of molecular fragmentation. GLACIER is faster and more accurate than existing state-of-the-art by a significant margin, achieving 70.0% and 69.7% Top-1 retrieval accuracy with and without contrastive finetuning on the MassSpecGym dataset (from the previous SOTA of 64.0%) and 52.5% and 38.5% respectively on the NIST'20 dataset (from 33.2%). Furthermore, GLACIER provides nearly 8-fold inference speedup over our prior two-stage model. Code is available at https://github.com/coleygroup/ms-pred

</details>


### [476] [Invariant Reasoning Directions in Latent Trajectories of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29164)
*Arun Vignesh Malarkkan,Manan Roy Choudhury,Utkarsh Byahut,Yash Ravindra Charde,Vivek Gupta,Yanjie Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent reasoning trajectories contain stable low-rank invariant directions mixed with unstable instance-specific variation. TILR, a training-free intervention, identifies and manipulates these stable directions to improve reasoning consistency and reduce trajectory variance under perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The structure of latent reasoning trajectories in multi-step inference models remains poorly understood, particularly how contrastive signals between stronger and weaker reasoning differ in their geometric properties.

Method: TILR (Trajectory-Invariant Latent Refinement): learns a low-rank invariant subspace from contrastive trajectory differences across inputs, then constrains latent interventions to this subspace while suppressing poorly aligned updates via an adaptive alignment gate.

Result: A small number of latent directions explain most variation between strong and weak reasoning. TILR improves answer consistency under paraphrase by ~10% and reduces latent trajectory variance by up to 50% while preserving reasoning accuracy.

Conclusion: Transferable reasoning behavior emerges from stable low-dimensional structure within hidden-state trajectories, supporting a geometric view of latent reasoning.

Abstract: Latent reasoning models perform multi-step inference directly in hidden-state space, yet the structure of these latent reasoning trajectories remains poorly understood. We show that contrastive refinement signals between stronger and weaker reasoning trajectories exhibit a highly concentrated low-rank structure, while unconstrained latent updates remain sensitive to paraphrases, checkpoint choice, and trajectory perturbations. These observations suggest that latent reasoning trajectories contain stable invariant directions mixed with unstable instance-specific variation. We introduce \textbf{Trajectory-Invariant Latent Refinement (TILR)}, a training-free intervention framework for identifying and manipulating stable reasoning directions in latent space. TILR first learns a low-rank invariant subspace from contrastive trajectory differences across inputs, then constrains latent interventions to this subspace while suppressing poorly aligned updates through an adaptive alignment gate. Across six reasoning benchmarks, we find that a small number of latent directions explain most variation between strong and weak reasoning trajectories. Interventions on these directions causally improve reasoning consistency and reduce trajectory instability under paraphrases and perturbations. TILR improves answer consistency under paraphrase by ~10% and reduces latent trajectory variance by up to $50\%$ while preserving reasoning accuracy. These results support a geometric view of latent reasoning in which transferable reasoning behavior emerges from stable low-dimensional structure within hidden-state trajectories.

</details>


### [477] [Symbolic Mechanistic Data Attribution: Tracing Training Influence to Learned Behavioral Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29171)
*Reza Habibi,Darian Lee,Magy Seif El-Nasr*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Symbolic Mechanistic Data Attribution (SMDA) bridges data attribution and mechanistic interpretability by attributing training pairs to the symbolic policies governing model behavior, revealing how SFT examples shape refusal decisions through feature-level decomposition in Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data attribution methods identify which training examples build specific circuits but fail to explain how training data shapes high-level behavioral decisions. SMDA aims to bridge this gap by linking training examples to interpretable symbolic policies.

Method: SMDA fits a closed-form Ridge regression over SAE features to model a target behavior, then decomposes how each SFT example shifts the policy via two pathways: feature-activation Delta_X and output-probability Delta_Y.

Result: (1) Symbolic policy coefficients expose systematic safety gaps (e.g., religious stereotyping). (2) Delta_X/Delta_Y decomposition mechanistically explains qualitatively different influences of harmful vs. harmless pairs on features. (3) SMDA identifies cross-feature interference where training pairs' dominant effect falls on unintended features.

Conclusion: Combining mechanistic interpretability with data attribution yields a diagnostic tool more fine-grained than black-box influence functions and more scalable than manual circuit analysis.

Abstract: While existing data attribution methods can identify which training examples build specific mechanistic circuits, they cannot explain how training data shapes the high-level behavioral decisions a model learns to make. To bridge this gap, we introduce Symbolic Mechanistic Data Attribution (SMDA), a framework that attributes training pairs to the interpretable symbolic policies governing model behavior. SMDA fits a closed-form Ridge regression over sparse autoencoder (SAE) features to model a target behavior, then analytically decomposes how each supervised fine-tuning example shifts that policy through feature-activation Delta_X and output-probability Delta_Y pathways. We distill a symbolic policy for refusal behavior in Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and analyze 200 SFT training pairs. Our analysis reveals that (1) the symbolic policy's coefficients expose systematic gaps in the base model's safety behavior for categories like religious stereotyping; (2) per-feature Delta_X/Delta_Y decomposition can mechanistically explain why harmful and harmless pairs exert qualitatively different influences on certain features; and (3) individual training pairs routinely exhibit cross-feature interference, allowing SMDA to identify training pairs whose dominant effect falls on unintended features. These results demonstrate that combining mechanistic interpretability with data attribution yields a diagnostic tool that is both more fine-grained than black-box influence functions and more scalable than manual circuit analysis.

</details>


### [478] [Dead-Direction Conditioners: Gauge-Equivariant Preconditioning for Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29176)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DDC (Dead-Direction Conditioner) lifts optimizers like Adam and Muon to be G-equivariant by conditioning optimizer states along symmetry orbits of neural network parameter spaces, preventing drift and enabling measurement of singular learning rates. On language models, DDCAdam resists over-training collapse (validation gap 0.67 vs 5.88) and on vision transformers reaches lower loss (1.71 vs 2.12). DDCMuon solves 10/11 grokking seeds at depth 24 that plain Muon cannot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep network losses are invariant to continuous parameter symmetries (logit shift, ReLU rescaling, LayerNorm scale, per-head attention rotation), but Adam's per-coordinate preconditioner drifts along these symmetry orbits, pulling trajectories off the symmetry quotient and blurring the singular-learning rate signal.

Method: DDC constructs a G-equivariant optimizer by conditioning the base optimizer's state in the orbit decomposition of a G-invariant metric, ensuring trajectories stay preconditioned gradient flows on the quotient Θ̄ = Θ/G. It handles four architectural gauges (cross-entropy shift, ReLU/SwiGLU rescaling, LayerNorm/RMSNorm scale, per-head O(d_head) attention rotation matched to RoPE) and composes exactly with Adam and through a gauge-equivariant orthogonaliser with Muon.

Result: Language model: DDCAdam holds validation-train loss gap 0.67 vs AdamW's 5.88; reads dead-direction rate in 32/65 cells vs 7/65. Vision transformer: validation loss 1.71 vs 2.12, compressing spare feed-forward capacity. DDCMuon: groks 10/11 seeds at depth 24 vs plain Muon's 0/11.

Conclusion: Built into the optimizer, a network's gauge symmetry sharpens the minimum found and turns that minimum's geometry into something the trajectory can measure, yielding better minima and measurable diagnostic signals.

Abstract: A deep network's loss is invariant to continuous symmetries of its parameters: the logit shift, the ReLU rescaling, the LayerNorm scale, the per-head attention rotation. Adam's per-coordinate preconditioner drifts along each symmetry orbit, which pulls the trajectory off the symmetry quotient where the optimization lives and blurs the singular-learning rate the quotient makes readable. We build DDC, a Dead-Direction Conditioner that lifts a base optimizer into a $G$-equivariant one: it conditions the optimizer's state in the orbit decomposition of a $G$-invariant metric, so the trajectory stays a preconditioned gradient flow on the quotient $\barΘ= Θ/G$. The construction carries four architectural gauges (cross-entropy shift, ReLU and SwiGLU rescaling, LayerNorm and RMSNorm scale, and a per-head $O(d_{\rm head})$ attention rotation matched to RoPE), proves exactly equivariant on an Adam base, and composes with a Muon base through a gauge-equivariant orthogonaliser. Respecting the symmetry changes both the minimum the optimizer reaches and what it leaves measurable there. On a language model trained past the point of fit, DDCAdam resists the over-training collapse AdamW falls into, holding a validation-train loss gap of 0.67 against 5.88, and reads the dead-direction rate in 32 of 65 layer-by-observable cells where AdamW reads it in 7. A vision transformer trained from scratch reaches lower validation loss (1.71 against 2.12) while compressing spare feed-forward capacity a matched AdamW leaves intact. On a Muon base, where the rotation gauge composes exactly, DDCMuon groks ten of eleven seeds at depth 24 that a plain Muon never reaches. Built into the optimizer, a network's gauge symmetry sharpens the minimum it finds and turns that minimum's geometry into something the trajectory can measure.

</details>


### [479] [BaRA: Bayesian Adaptive Rank Allocation for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29184)
*Zhibin Duan,Yuhong Wang,Jiahong Fu,Zongsheng Yue,Bo Chen,Zongben Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BaRA is a Bayesian Adaptive Rank Allocation framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning that dynamically allocates adaptation capacity by activating sparse, context-dependent latent factors, improving predictive performance, robustness, and uncertainty calibration over standard LoRA and Bayesian LoRA variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA is limited by rigid low-rank subspaces leading to overconfident predictions and miscalibrated uncertainty, especially in low-data regimes. Existing Bayesian LoRA variants improve uncertainty estimation but rely on fixed or heuristically determined ranks, ignoring the context-dependent nature of adaptation capacity.

Method: BaRA draws inspiration from probabilistic topic models and dynamically allocates adaptation capacity by activating a sparse, context-dependent subset of disentangled latent factors, enabling instance-wise variation in effective rank. It uses a Bayesian formulation with a global-local gate mechanism for data-driven capacity control, and the authors provide a complexity-theoretic generalization analysis showing the generalization gap depends on the learned joint effective rank rather than the maximum rank.

Result: BaRA consistently improves predictive performance, robustness, and uncertainty calibration compared to standard LoRA and existing Bayesian LoRA variants across diverse natural language benchmarks. The generalization analysis shows that sparse adaptive rank allocation reduces effective hypothesis complexity while maintaining input-dependent expressiveness.

Conclusion: BaRA effectively addresses the limitations of fixed-rank LoRA and Bayesian LoRA by providing principled, data-driven capacity control that adapts per instance, mitigating over-parameterization while preserving expressiveness and improving uncertainty calibration.

Abstract: While Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) enables highly efficient fine-tuning by constraining task-specific updates to fixed low-rank subspaces, this rigid design limits representational flexibility and often results in overconfident predictions and miscalibrated uncertainty, especially in low-data regimes. Recent Bayesian LoRA variants improve uncertainty estimation by modeling posterior distributions over adaptation parameters. However, these approaches typically rely on fixed or heuristically determined ranks, overlooking the inherently context-dependent nature of adaptation capacity. In this paper, we propose BaRA, a Bayesian Adaptive Rank Allocation framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Drawing inspiration from probabilistic topic models, BaRA dynamically allocates adaptation capacity by activating a sparse, context-dependent subset of disentangled latent factors, enabling instance-wise variation in effective rank. This Bayesian formulation provides principled, data-driven capacity control, mitigating over-parameterization while preserving expressiveness. Beyond the modeling contribution, we provide a complexity-theoretic generalization analysis showing that the generalization gap of BaRA depends on the learned joint effective rank $\bar{s}_{Φ,θ}$ induced by the global-local gate, rather than the maximum rank $r$. This result explains why sparse adaptive rank allocation can reduce the effective hypothesis complexity while preserving input-dependent expressiveness. Extensive experiments on diverse natural language benchmarks demonstrate that BaRA consistently improves predictive performance, robustness, and uncertainty calibration compared to standard LoRA and existing Bayesian LoRA variants.

</details>


### [480] [Representational Depth of Evaluation Awareness Shifts With Scale in Open-Weight Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29196)
*Archit Manek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluation-awareness in LLMs shifts from late to early layers as models scale up, with white-box probes revealing stronger signals than black-box behavior, and within-family scaling being non-monotonic rather than following a universal power law.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether language models know when they are being tested (evaluation-awareness), as this poses AI safety risks where models might strategically alter behavior during evaluation, compromising benchmark interpretability.

Method: Analyzed 11 models across Qwen 2.5, Gemma 2, and Llama 3.2 families, examining representational depth shifts in evaluation-awareness linear recoverability across layers, and comparing white-box probe signals with black-box behavioral expression.

Result: Systematic size-dependent shift in representational depth: evaluation-awareness moves from late layers in smaller models to early layers in larger ones. Scaling trajectories are non-monotonic/inverse within families, not fitting a universal power law. White-box probe signals consistently exceed black-box behavioral expression, with family-dependent probe-behavior relationships.

Conclusion: Scale changes both the strength and network location of linearly recoverable evaluation-awareness; simple universal scaling laws are unsupported under dense within-family sampling; probe-behavior dissociation varies by model family beyond what probe AUROC predicts.

Abstract: Do language models know when they are being tested? This question matters for AI safety: a model that recognises an evaluation context could alter its behaviour strategically, making downstream benchmarks harder to interpret. Using 11 models spanning Qwen 2.5, Gemma 2, and Llama 3.2, we find a systematic size-dependent shift in representational depth: in both Qwen 2.5 and Gemma 2, the layer at which evaluation-awareness is most linearly recoverable moves from late layers in smaller models to early layers in larger ones. This suggests that scale changes not only the strength of evaluation-awareness but also where it is most linearly recoverable in the network. This depth shift helps explain why within-family scaling trajectories are non-monotonic or inverse rather than smooth and family-general, showing that a simple universal power-law account is not supported under denser within-family sampling. Finally, white-box probe signals are consistently stronger than black-box behavioural expression, and the relationship between the two varies by family in ways not predicted by probe AUROC alone.

</details>


### [481] [BrainRiem: Riemannian Prototype Learning for Source-Free Cross-Site Brain Network Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29200)
*Kunyu Zhang,Tianxiang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BrainRiem is a source-free domain adaptation framework for multi-site fMRI studies that learns compact Riemannian brain prototypes on the SPD manifold using Log-Euclidean Metric and Dirichlet Energy spectral calibration, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse scanners without sharing source data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-site fMRI studies suffer from domain shifts due to scanner heterogeneity, demographics, and site-specific protocols, while traditional domain adaptation requires concurrent source and target data access, violating clinical privacy regulations. Additionally, functional connectivity matrices reside on the SPD manifold where Euclidean operations cause geometric distortions.

Method: BrainRiem employs source-free domain adaptation by learning compact Riemannian brain prototypes via manifold-aware bi-level optimization. It uses Log-Euclidean Metric to keep prototypes as valid SPD matrices and Dirichlet Energy spectral calibration to align frequency characteristics with real brain networks. Only anonymized prototypes are transmitted to target sites as anchors for local model training.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art source-free, traditional, and graph domain adaptation methods on ABIDE and REST-meta-MDD benchmarks across diverse scanners and demographics, while reducing data leakage under evaluated attacks.

Conclusion: Learned prototypes exhibit biologically interpretable connectivity patterns aligned with established neuroscience findings, validating the necessity of Riemannian geometry for brain network analysis.

Abstract: Multi-site functional MRI (fMRI) studies are essential for robust neuropsychiatric diagnosis yet suffer severe domain shifts from scanner heterogeneity, demographics, and site-specific acquisition protocols. Traditional domain adaptation requires concurrent source and target data access, violating clinical privacy regulations. Moreover, functional connectivity matrices lie on the Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) manifold, where Euclidean operations cause geometric distortions corrupting diagnostic patterns. We propose BrainRiem, a source-free domain adaptation framework learning compact Riemannian brain prototypes via manifold-aware bi-level optimization. It employs the Log-Euclidean Metric to ensure prototypes remain valid SPD matrices, while Dirichlet Energy spectral calibration aligns their frequency characteristics with real brain networks. Only anonymized prototypes are transmitted to target sites, serving as stable anchors for training local models without source data access and reducing leakage under the evaluated attacks. Comprehensive experiments on ABIDE and REST-meta-MDD show BrainRiem consistently outperforms state-of-the-art source-free, traditional, and graph domain adaptation methods across diverse scanners and demographics. Notably, learned prototypes exhibit biologically interpretable connectivity patterns aligning with established neuroscience findings, validating the necessity of Riemannian geometry for brain network analysis.

</details>


### [482] [Bayesian Best-Arm Identification with Abstention: A Polynomial-to-Exponential Phase Transition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29203)
*Yuqi Huang,Yunlong Hou,Vincent Y. F. Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies Bayesian best-arm identification with abstention, finding that abstention shifts error probability from polynomial to exponential decay. The optimal error exponent is achieved by the PGWS algorithm, and this improvement is exclusively Bayesian, not frequentist.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how a limited abstention budget can fundamentally improve the error probability in Bayesian fixed-budget best-arm identification, transitioning from polynomial to exponential decay.

Method: The authors derive information-theoretic lower bounds and algorithmic upper bounds on the optimal error exponent for Gaussian priors/rewards, and introduce the PGWS (probably guessing with abstention) adaptive algorithm. They analyze the phase transition induced by abstention and extend results beyond Gaussian models.

Result: With any positive abstention budget α, the error probability decays exponentially as exp(-α²T/(8κ_ν²)), where κ_ν is the prior density of the top-two gap at zero. Without abstention, decay is polynomial. PGWS achieves this optimal exponent. The improvement does not hold in the frequentist setting.

Conclusion: Abstention induces a phase transition from polynomial to exponential error decay in Bayesian best-arm identification, with tight matching bounds characterizing the optimal exponent. The effect is uniquely Bayesian.

Abstract: We study the Bayesian fixed-budget best-arm identification problem in which a learner can abstain from making a terminal recommendation. Subject to an abstention budget $α$, we analyze the probability of undetected error--the risk of recommending a suboptimal arm without abstaining. Our central finding is that abstention induces a phase transition: without abstention, the error probability decays polynomially in the sampling budget $T$; in contrast, introducing any small positive abstention budget shifts this to an exponential decay. For Gaussian priors and rewards, in the regime $T\to\infty$ followed by $α\downarrow0$, we establish exact matching information-theoretic lower bounds and algorithmic upper bounds on the optimal error exponent, which takes the form $\exp(-\frac{α^{2}T}{8κ_ν^{2}})$. The hardness parameter $κ_ν$ represents the prior density of the top-two gap at zero, highlighting that nearly tied instances drive the fundamental error. We introduce an adaptive algorithm, PGWS, that successfully achieves this optimal exponent by expending its abstention budget on statistically ambiguous instances. We further demonstrate that this polynomial-to-exponential improvement is exclusively a Bayesian phenomenon--in the frequentist setting, abstention only affects lower-order exponent terms. We also extend our results beyond the Gaussian model.

</details>


### [483] [Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29215)
*Yijie Jin,Jiajun Xu,Yuxuan Liu,Chenkai Xu,Yi Tu,Jiajun Li,Dandan Tu,Xiaohui Yan,Kai Yu,Pengfei Liu,Zhijie Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MBD-LMs extend single-block diffusion language models to multi-block decoding via Multi-block Teacher Forcing (MultiTF) and a Block Buffer mechanism, achieving higher token throughput (up to 9.34 TPF) with minimal accuracy degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BD-LMs are mostly trained under teacher forcing (single noisy block) or diffusion forcing (multi-block but training states differ from inference). The gap between training and MultiBD inference — where decoding uses a bounded running-set with heterogeneous noise patterns — limits parallelism and performance.

Method: Propose Multi-block Teacher Forcing (MultiTF) that trains on bounded noise-groups conditioned on clean prefixes with randomized noise-schedulers. Introduce Block Buffer mechanism for decoding that preserves prefix-cache reuse, keeps input shapes static, and translates parallelism into wall-clock speedup.

Result: MBD-LLaDA2-Mini increases average TPF from 3.47 to 6.19 and accuracy from 79.95% to 81.03%. With DMax, TPF reaches 9.34 with only 1.02% accuracy drop on math/code benchmarks.

Conclusion: MultiBD with MultiTF training and Block Buffer decoding effectively bridges the training-inference gap, enabling practical wall-clock acceleration and improved accuracy in diffusion-based text generation.

Abstract: Block Diffusion Language Models (BD-LMs) improve diffusion-based text generation with KV caching and flexible-length generation. A natural next step is to extend them from Single-Block Diffusion (SingleBD) to Multi-Block Diffusion (MultiBD), where a \textit{running-set} of consecutive blocks is decoded concurrently for inter-block parallelism. However, existing BD-LMs are mostly trained under teacher forcing, where the model observes only one noisy block conditioned on a clean prefix. While the recent diffusion forcing strategy introduces visibility among multiple noisy blocks, its training states still differ from MultiBD inference, where decoding operates on a bounded \textit{running-set} with heterogeneous slot-wise noise patterns. To bridge this gap, we propose \textit{Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models} (MBD-LMs), obtained by post-training BD-LMs with \textit{Multi-block Teacher Forcing} (MultiTF). MultiTF integrates teacher forcing and diffusion forcing by training on bounded \textit{noise-groups} conditioned on clean prefixes, with randomized \textit{noise-schedulers} that better match MultiBD inference states. To make MultiBD practically executable, we further introduce an optimized decoding algorithm based on the \textit{Block Buffer} mechanism that preserves prefix-cache reuse, keeps input shapes static, and translates increased decoding parallelism into wall-clock acceleration. Empirically, MBD-LLaDA2-Mini increases average Tokens Per Forward pass (TPF) from 3.47 to \textbf{6.19} and improves average accuracy from 79.95\% to \textbf{81.03\%}; when combined with DMax, MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax reaches an average TPF of \textbf{9.34} with only a 1.02\% accuracy drop on math and code benchmarks.

</details>


### [484] [A Linear Matching Bandit Approach to Online Multi-Human Multi-Robot Teaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29221)
*Yaohui Guo,X. Jessie Yang,Cong Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LinMatch is an online learning algorithm for multi-human multi-robot teaming that recasts optimistic matching as a linear program solvable by the Hungarian algorithm, achieving a tight optimal regret bound of $\tilde{\Theta}(d\sqrt{MKT})$.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of online multi-human multi-robot teaming where a learner must assign robots with unknown features to humans over multiple rounds, under uncertainty.

Method: The authors propose LinMatch, an online learning algorithm that updates confidence intervals of unknown robot features and makes optimistic matching decisions. The optimistic matching is recast as a linear program of maximum weighted matching, solved efficiently by the Hungarian algorithm.

Result: Provides an upper bound of $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{MKT})$ and a minimax lower bound of $\Omega(d\sqrt{MKT})$, establishing a tight optimal regret rate of $\tilde{\Theta}(d\sqrt{MKT})$.
