Conclusion: LinMatch achieves strictly optimal achievable regret with respect to $T$, $d$, $M$, and $K$, and the algorithm extends to other matching problems like housing allocation and recommendation systems.

Abstract: We address the problem of online multi-human multi-robot teaming through the lens of a linear matching bandit framework, where a learner assigns robots with unknown features from a fixed pool to distinct sets of human agents over multiple rounds. To solve this problem, we propose LinMatch, an online learning algorithm that updates the confidence intervals of the unknown features and makes the optimistic matching under uncertainty. The contributions and novelty of this work are twofold. First, we recast the optimistic matching problem in each round as a linear program of maximum weighted matching, efficiently solvable by the celebrated Hungarian algorithm. Second, we provide novel bounds for matching with linear feature problems, showing an upper bound of $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{MKT})$ and a minimax lower bound of $Ω(d\sqrt{MKT})$, establishing a tight optimal regret rate of $\tildeΘ(d\sqrt{MKT})$. This demonstrates that LinMatch achieves strictly optimal achievable regret with respect to the total number of rounds $T$, the feature dimension $d$, and the matching parameters $M$ and $K$. The proposed algorithm and bounds apply to a wide range of matching problems with applications beyond human-robot matching, such as housing allocation, recommendation systems, and more.

</details>


### [485] [Depth Exploration for LLM Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29223)
*Weisi Yang,Zipeng Sun,Stephen Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DEX is a lossless decoding algorithm that explores multiple candidate depths in parallel during LLM decoding, replacing single-depth selection, and validates them against the final-depth model to reduce computation while preserving equivalence to standard autoregressive decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lossless depth-adaptive methods use single-depth selection, which wastes computation when exiting too late or triggers fallback/discards drafts when exiting too early, leaving substantial headroom for improvement.

Method: DEX uses an expand-commit-collapse procedure: at each commit position, it explores multiple candidate depths in parallel, validates candidates against the final-depth reference, commits the final-depth token, and collapses the exploration lattice to retain only reusable branch states.

Result: DEX outperforms representative depth-selection baselines across early-exit-trained and standard LLMs, achieves competitive end-to-end throughput against speculative and distributed decoding methods, and improves with finer depth exploration granularity.

Conclusion: Parallel depth exploration provides a scalable way to exploit the underused depth axis of LLM decoding, reducing computation cost per token while maintaining lossless equivalence to standard autoregressive decoding.

Abstract: Autoregressive LLM decoding evaluates every generated token through the full layer stack, even though many tokens become predictable at intermediate depths. Existing lossless depth-adaptive methods exploit this redundancy by choosing a single non-final exit depth and verifying its prediction with the final-depth model. However, our measurements show that this selection-based strategy leaves substantial headroom: choosing an exit too late wastes computation, while choosing one too early triggers fallback and discards dependent drafts. We propose Depth Exploration Decoding (DEX), a lossless decoding algorithm that replaces single-depth selection with parallel exploration over multiple candidate depths. At each commit position, DEX validates candidates against the final-depth reference, commits exactly the final-depth token, and collapses the exploration lattice to retain only reusable branch states. This expand--commit--collapse procedure preserves equivalence to standard autoregressive decoding while reducing the cost of committing each token. Across early-exit-trained and standard LLMs, DEX outperforms representative depth-selection baselines and achieves competitive end-to-end throughput against speculative and distributed decoding methods. Moreover, DEX improves as the explored depths become finer, showing that parallel depth exploration provides a scalable way to exploit the underused depth axis of LLM decoding.

</details>


### [486] [On the Policy Gradient Foundations of Group Relative Policy Optimization: Credit Assignment, Gradient Sparsity, and Rank Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29238)
*Amritansh Mishra,Supriyo Chakraborty,Berkcan Kapusuzoglu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO's use of group-mean reward as baseline causes token-level credit assignment collapse—every token gets the same advantage—leading to gradient sparsity and an intrinsic rank-2 gradient structure, limiting its effectiveness for multi-step reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously derive GRPO from policy gradient first principles and identify its fundamental credit assignment limitations, particularly whether eliminating the critic (as in PPO) is theoretically justified.

Method: Theoretical derivation from policy gradient theorem; empirical SVD analysis of GRPO gradients on Nemotron-4B/GSM8K with varying group sizes R ∈ {2,4,8}.

Result: GRPO assigns identical advantage to every token under output-only reward (collapse to scalar); gradient matrix exhibits effective rank ≈ 2 regardless of group size; formalized as intrinsic rank-2 structure from zero-sum advantage constraint.

Conclusion: GRPO's simplicity is theoretically justified under certain conditions, but its credit assignment bottleneck—stemming from collapse of token-level differentiation—is the key limitation for multi-step reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) eliminates the learned critic in PPO by using the mean reward of grouped rollouts as a baseline. We provide a rigorous derivation of GRPO from first principles of the policy gradient theorem, revealing a fundamental credit assignment failure: under output-only reward, every token in a rollout receives identical advantage, collapsing token-level credit to a single scalar. We prove this induces gradient sparsity that intensifies over training, and demonstrate empirically via SVD analysis of GRPO gradients on Nemotron-4B/GSM8K that the gradient matrix has effective rank $\approx$ 2 regardless of group size $R \in \{2, 4, 8\}$. We formalize this as an intrinsic rank-2 structure arising from the zero-sum constraint on advantages and derive conditions under which GRPO's baseline is optimal. Our results characterize when GRPO's simplicity is theoretically justified and identify the credit assignment bottleneck as the key limitation for multi-step reasoning.

</details>


### [487] [Blackknife: Hard-Label Query-Limited Black-Box Attacks on Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29240)
*Honglin Gao,Junhao Ren,Lan Zhao,Yue Yang,Jindong Chang,Gaoxi Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Blackknife is a hard-label, query-limited, structure-limited black-box evasion attack framework for heterogeneous graph neural networks that operates with only locally observable one-hop structures and limited hard-label queries, achieving strong attack success rates on ACM, DBLP, and IMDB datasets while remaining effective under topology-based defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attacks on HGNNs typically require access to model gradients, soft prediction scores, or complete graph structures—assumptions that are unrealistic when HGNN-based services are deployed as closed systems. The robustness of HGNNs under realistic black-box adversarial settings remains insufficiently explored.

Method: Blackknife first constructs a local relation-aware surrogate model from observable one-hop heterogeneous neighborhoods. It then relaxes discrete edge addition/deletion operations into continuous soft weights and optimizes them via projected gradient descent. Finally, optimized perturbations are discretized into relation-preserving structural rewiring operations and verified using limited hard-label feedback from the victim model.

Result: Extensive experiments on ACM, DBLP, and IMDB datasets demonstrate that Blackknife consistently achieves strong attack success rates against representative HGNN models and remains effective under topology-based defense strategies, revealing the vulnerability of HGNNs to local structure-limited black-box attacks.

Conclusion: HGNNs are vulnerable to realistic black-box evasion attacks that rely only on locally observable one-hop structures and limited hard-label queries, highlighting the need for more robust HGNN defenses under strict information constraints.

Abstract: Heterogeneous graph neural networks (HGNNs) have achieved strong performance in modeling complex graph-structured data with multiple node and relation types. However, their robustness under realistic black-box adversarial settings remains insufficiently explored. Existing attacks on HGNNs usually assume access to model gradients, soft prediction scores, or the complete graph structure, which is often unavailable when HGNN-based services are deployed as closed systems. In this paper, we propose Blackknife, a hard-label, query-limited, and structure-limited black-box evasion attack framework for heterogeneous graph neural networks. Blackknife assumes no access to the victim model architecture, parameters, gradients, logits, confidence scores, or the full graph structure. Instead, it only relies on locally observable one-hop heterogeneous structures and a small number of hard-label queries. To generate effective perturbations under these strict constraints, Blackknife first constructs a local relation-aware surrogate model from observable heterogeneous neighborhoods. It then relaxes discrete edge addition and deletion operations into continuous soft weights and optimizes them through projected gradient descent. Finally, the optimized perturbations are discretized into relation-preserving structural rewiring operations and verified using limited hard-label feedback from the victim model. Extensive experiments on three benchmark heterogeneous graph datasets, including ACM, DBLP, and IMDB, demonstrate that Blackknife consistently achieves strong attack success rates against representative HGNN models. The results further show that Blackknife remains effective under topology-based defense strategies, revealing the vulnerability of HGNNs to local structure-limited black-box attacks.

</details>


### [488] [Towards Evaluating Data Priors for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29241)
*Zeynep Türkmen,Kürşat Kaya,Alexander Pfefferle,Frank Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified framework to compare data-generating priors from tabular foundation models independently of architectures and training protocols, finding that different priors favor different downstream behaviors and that data-level similarity only partially explains performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-generating priors are central to tabular foundation models but are rarely evaluated as independent components, making it unclear how much they affect downstream model behavior. The authors aim to enable fair, architecture-agnostic comparison of priors.

Method: The authors implement a unified interface for publicly available priors from recent tabular foundation models and priors from real datasets. They generate training tasks from each prior, train the same model architecture under a fixed training protocol, and evaluate on shared downstream classification tasks. Priors are compared via generated-task statistics and downstream predictive performance.

Result: Different priors favor different downstream behaviors: some achieve stronger absolute performance while others exhibit more consistent relative rankings across datasets. Data-level similarity only partially explains downstream behavior.

Conclusion: Priors significantly influence downstream model behavior independently of architecture and training protocol, and their effects cannot be fully explained by data-level similarity. This highlights the need for careful prior selection and evaluation in tabular foundation model development.

Abstract: Data-generating priors are a central component of tabular foundation models because they define the task distribution used during pretraining. However, priors are rarely evaluated as independent components, making it difficult to understand how much they affect downstream model behavior. This raises a methodological question: how can priors from different tabular foundation models be compared independently of the architectures and training protocols they were introduced with? To study this question, we implement a unified interface for publicly available priors from recent tabular foundation models and priors constructed from real datasets. We generate training tasks from each prior, train the same model architecture under a fixed training protocol, and evaluate the resulting models on shared downstream classification tasks. We compare priors through both generated-task statistics and downstream predictive performance. Our results show that different priors favor different downstream behaviors, with some achieving stronger absolute performance and others exhibiting more consistent relative rankings across datasets. We further find that data-level similarity only partially explains downstream behavior. Our code is available at https://github.com/automl/TFM-Playground/tree/prior-dev.

</details>


### [489] [KrishokChat: A Citation-Grounded Dataset and Benchmark for Bengali Agricultural Advisory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29243)
*Khan Raiyan Ibne Reza,Omar Ibne Shahid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KrishokChat is the first citation-grounded Bengali agricultural QA dataset with 145,500 instances built from 290 hierarchical knowledge nodes extracted from 129 domain-filtered manuals. While fine-tuning improves structured formatting, models still struggle with chemical dosage generalization, validating the dataset's primary value for RAG over parametric memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of citation-grounded, low-resource agricultural instruction datasets for Bengali-speaking farmers, enabling trustworthy crop advisory in low-resource settings.

Method: (1) Extracted 290 hierarchical Knowledge Nodes (disease symptoms, management, dosages, citations) from 129 Bengali agricultural manuals. (2) Used a Partitioned Seed Generation Matrix to expand into 139,200 SFT pairs. (3) Augmented with 5,300 chemical safety + 1,000 adversarial safety instances, totaling 145,500 QA pairs across 18 crop categories. (4) Evaluated using Farmer Benchmark (1,001 real farmer queries) on Gemma-4-E2B.

Result: Fine-tuning on KrishokChat greatly improves structured formatting, but standalone models struggle with exact chemical dosage generalization. The dataset is more valuable as a verified knowledge base for RAG than for parametric memorization.

Conclusion: KrishokChat serves as a verified, citation-grounded resource best suited for retrieval-augmented generation in agricultural advisory, with all data/code/benchmarks released under CC-BY-4.0.

Abstract: We present KrishokChat, the first citation-grounded Bengali agricultural instruction-tuning dataset for crop advisory in low-resource settings. We establish a foundation of 290 hierarchical Knowledge Nodes, extracting disease symptoms, management practices, chemical dosages, and verbatim citations from 129 domain-filtered agricultural manuals. Every training instance inherits a verified citation header, guaranteeing 100% citation provenance. Using a Partitioned Seed Generation Matrix, these nodes are expanded into 139,200 supervised fine-tuning pairs, and augmented with 5,300 chemical safety and 1,000 adversarial safety instances, yielding 145,500 QA pairs across 18 crop categories. To evaluate real-world performance, we introduce the Farmer Benchmark, comprising 1,001 authentic farmer queries curated from field surveys and digital portals. Empirical evaluation on Gemma-4-E2B reveals that while fine-tuning on KrishokChat vastly improves structured formatting, standalone models still struggle with exact chemical dosage generalization. This highlights the dataset's true value as a verified knowledge base for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) rather than mere parametric memorization. All data, code, and benchmarks are released under CC-BY-4.0.

</details>


### [490] [When Prices Double in a Week: Forecasting of Agricultural Volatility in Import-Isolated Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29248)
*Ranuga Weerasekara,Heshan Nethmina,Manuja Ranathunga,Vinma Wettasinghe,Dinithi Navodya,Subavarshana Arumugam,Nirasha Munasinghe,Nisansa de Silva,Sandareka Wickramanayake*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A machine learning framework (XGBoost + LightGBM) forecasts vegetable price volatility in Sri Lanka using supply-chain-aware features and season-specific modeling (Maha/Yala). Season-specific models improve fit (Yala R²=0.9420), while the unified model achieves best overall accuracy (90.84%) and maintains 85.96% accuracy on an unseen 2024 hyperinflation period without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vegetable prices in Sri Lanka are highly volatile due to import isolation, where supply disruptions quickly drive price surges. Existing studies are limited to ARIMA/GARCH on single markets without supply-chain features, seasonal segmentation, or cross-regime validation, motivating a more comprehensive ML-based approach.

Method: An integrated dataset combining retail and farmer-gate prices with origin-aligned weather, diesel costs, and exchange rates across 12 vegetable varieties, 14 market centers, and 7 years (2013-2019). A gradient-boosted ensemble (XGBoost + LightGBM) was optimized via Optuna, comparing unified vs. season-specific (Maha/Yala) configurations.

Result: Yala-specific model achieved highest R² of 0.9420 (95% CI [0.690, 1.000]); unified model achieved best overall predictive accuracy of 90.84% (95% CI [88.34%, 91.52%]) and R² of 0.9281 (95% CI [0.760, 1.000]), with 85.96% accuracy on a 2024 hyperinflationary unseen period.

Conclusion: Agricultural price movements in import-constrained markets are meaningfully predictable when models capture supply-chain dynamics and seasonal patterns, offering practical value for early warning systems and decision making by farmers, traders, and policymakers.

Abstract: Vegetable prices in Sri Lanka are highly volatile because the market is largely import-isolated, so supply disruptions quickly drive prices up. This study develops a machine learning framework to forecast such volatility by incorporating supply-chain-aware features and explicitly modelling the country's two cultivation seasons, Maha (October-April) and Yala (May-September). An integrated dataset was constructed by combining retail and farmer-gate prices with origin-aligned weather variables, diesel costs, and exchange rates across 12 vegetable varieties and 14 market centres from 2013 to 2019. A gradient-boosted ensemble model (XGBoost and LightGBM) was trained and optimised using Optuna, and unified and season-specific configurations were compared. Results show that season-specific models improve within-season fit, with the Yala-specific model achieving the highest R2 of 0.9420 (95% CI [0.690, 1.000]), while the unified model delivers the best overall predictive accuracy of 90.84% (95% CI [88.34%, 91.52%]) and an R2 of 0.9281 (95% CI [0.760, 1.000]). Notably, the unified model maintains 85.96% accuracy on a completely unseen 2024 hyperinflationary period without retraining, successfully tracking major price surges. These findings suggest that agricultural price movements in import-constrained markets are meaningfully predictable when models capture supply-chain dynamics, offering practical value for early warning and decision making by farmers, traders, and policymakers. Existing studies on Sri Lankan vegetable prices are confined to Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) and Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (GARCH) applied to single markets, with no supply-chain features, seasonal segmentation, or cross-regime validation.

</details>


### [491] [Learning to Bid in Discriminatory Auctions with Budget Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29252)
*Negin Golrezaei,Sourav Sahoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies repeated multi-unit discriminatory auctions for a single bidder with cost-of-capital parameter α, proposing polynomial-time learning algorithms based on shortest paths in DAGs that achieve sublinear regret under full-information and bandit feedback, with budget-constrained extensions using a primal-dual approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Repeated bidding in multi-unit discriminatory auctions is challenging due to exponential action space in maximum demand M and time-varying valuation vectors. Even without budgets, existing methods struggle with scalability; with budget constraints, the problem becomes more complex.

Method: The authors exploit a utility decomposition across units to develop DAG-based shortest path algorithms. For bandit feedback, they leverage complete cross-learning (utility of chosen action under realized context reveals utility for same action under all counterfactual contexts). For budget constraints with ρ<1, they design a coupled primal-dual algorithm: DAG-based procedure with dual-adjusted edge weights for primal updates, and online gradient descent for dual variable updates.

Result: Sublinear regret under both full-information and bandit feedback; regret independent of number of contexts in bandit setting; ρ-approximate sublinear regret under budget constraints; per-round time and space complexity independent of number of contexts, scaling to large/infinite context spaces.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithms achieve polynomial-time learning with strong regret guarantees in challenging auction settings, demonstrating scalability to large context spaces through context-independent implementations.

Abstract: We study repeated bidding in multi-unit discriminatory (pay-as-bid) auctions for a single bidder with per-round utility equal to value minus $α$ times payment, where $α\in[0,1]$ is a cost-of-capital parameter. The bidder aims to maximize cumulative utility over $T$ rounds subject to a total budget $B$. The problem is challenging even without budgets: the action space is exponential in $M$, the maximum demand of the bidder and the valuation vector (context) varies over time. Exploiting a decomposition of utility across units, we develop polynomial-time learning algorithms based on shortest paths in a directed acyclic graph, obtaining sublinear regret under both full-information and bandit feedback. In the bandit setting, the regret is independent of the number of contexts due to complete cross-learning: observing the utility of the chosen action under the realized context reveals the utility for the same action under all counterfactual contexts. With budget constraints, when the average normalized per-round budget $ρ=\frac{B}{MT}<1$, we design a coupled primal-dual algorithm in which the DAG-based procedure uses dual-adjusted edge weights for primal updates, while online gradient descent updates the dual variable, yielding $ρ$-approximate sublinear regret. Finally, we give implementations whose per-round time and space are independent of the number of contexts, enabling scalability to large or even infinite context spaces.

</details>


### [492] [Nonlinear mixture model motivated subspace clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29261)
*Ivica Kopriva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper derives the linear union-of-subspaces model for subspace clustering from a nonlinear mixture model used in blind source separation, showing that the smoothness order K corresponds to subspace dimension d, KC equals the number of anchors, and representation sparsity equals K. These relationships enable estimation of subspace dimension bounds, validated on six datasets with five SC algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a theoretical connection between the linear union-of-subspaces (UoS) model for subspace clustering (SC) and the nonlinear mixture model (NMM) from blind source separation (BSS), thereby providing a principled way to estimate subspace dimension bounds and improve post-processing of self-representation matrices.

Method: The authors derive the linear UoS model from the NMM by assuming the nonlinear mapping is differentiable up to an unknown order K and applying a K-th order Taylor expansion. They then formally establish relationships between K, subspace dimension d, the number of anchors (KC), and representation vector sparsity (K). Validation is performed on six benchmark datasets using five established SC algorithms.

Result: Three key relationships are established: (i) smoothness order K equals subspace dimension d; (ii) KC equals the number of anchors; (iii) representation vector sparsity equals K (i.e., d). These enable estimation of bounds on subspace dimension, validated across multiple datasets and algorithms.

Conclusion: The derived theoretical results are important for post-processing of self-representation matrices estimated by SC algorithms, providing a principled way to estimate subspace dimension bounds.

Abstract: We derive the linear union-of-subspaces (UoS) model for subspace clustering (SC) from the nonlinear mixture model (NMM) used in blind source separation (BSS) to represent a D-dimensional observation vector as an unknown multivariate nonlinear mapping of C latent variables. Assuming the mapping is differentiable up to an unknown order K, we approximate NMM by a K-th order Taylor expansion, yielding a model equivalent to the linear UoS framework underlying SC. This establishes that: (i) the smoothness order K corresponds to the unknown subspace dimension d; (ii) KC equals the number of anchors; and (iii) the sparsity of the representation vector equals K (i.e., d). These relationships enable estimation of bounds on subspace dimension, and that is validated on six benchmark datasets using five established SC algorithms. Established theoretical results are important for post-processing of self-representation matrices estimated by SC algorithms.

</details>


### [493] [PCGD: Physics-Guided Conditional Graph Diffusion for TCAD Device Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29272)
*Yihan Zhang,Zhiteng Zhang,Kun Chen,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCGD is a Physics-Guided Conditional Graph Diffusion framework for TCAD semiconductor simulation that predicts coupled electrostatic and carrier density fields on unstructured meshes. It uses a Condition-Aware MeshGraphNet denoiser with global cross-attention and a physics-guided hybrid objective (exponent-free quasi-Fermi gradient matching + noise-aware PDE residuals). It achieves 0.835% mean relative field error on mixed PN/MOS benchmarks, reduces PDE residuals by ~3 orders of magnitude vs. pure diffusion, and transfers to unseen SOI topologies via LoRA with 14.34× fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TCAD semiconductor device simulation is computationally expensive due to iteratively solving coupled drift-diffusion equations. Existing ML surrogates either reduce physics to scalar regressions or use single-step mappings lacking iterative refinement for stiff, coupled fields.

Method: PCGD employs a Condition-Aware MeshGraphNet denoiser with global cross-attention to inject boundary conditions and device structure context. It uses a physics-guided hybrid objective combining exponent-free quasi-Fermi gradient matching with noise-aware PDE residuals to enforce physical constraints during the diffusion trajectory.

Result: PCGD achieves 0.835% mean relative field error (vs. 1.207% for regression and 1.585% for local diffusion baselines), reduces maximum PDE residual errors by nearly three orders of magnitude, and transfers to unseen SOI topologies with 0.815% error using LoRA (5.30× less data, 14.34× fewer parameters than full fine-tuning).

Conclusion: PCGD bridges generative surrogate efficiency with rigorous TCAD physical fidelity, enabling scalable, field-level analysis for robust device engineering.

Abstract: Technology computer-aided design (TCAD) semiconductor device simulation is fundamentally constrained by the high computational cost of iteratively solving coupled drift-diffusion equations. Existing ML surrogates either reduce internal physics to macroscopic scalar regressions, or rely on single-step mappings that lack the iterative refinement required to resolve stiff, coupled fields. To address this, we introduce PCGD, a Physics-Guided Conditional Graph Diffusion framework operating natively on unstructured TCAD meshes to predict coupled electrostatic and carrier density fields. PCGD employs a Condition-Aware MeshGraphNet denoiser that explicitly injects boundary conditions and device structure context via global cross-attention. By augmenting data-driven denoising with a physics-guided hybrid objective that integrates exponent-free quasi-Fermi gradient matching with noise-aware PDE residuals, PCGD progressively enforce physical constraints in the iterative diffusion trajectory. This strategy successfully bypasses the numerical instabilities typical of stiff drift-diffusion equations. Evaluated on a challenging mixed PN/MOS benchmark, PCGD significantly outperforms deterministic one-step regression (1.207% error) and local diffusion (1.585% error) baselines by achieving a sub-percent mean relative field error of 0.835%, while concurrently reducing maximum PDE residual errors by nearly three orders of magnitude compared to pure diffusion. It also transfers robustly to unseen SOI topologies (0.815% error) via LoRA adaptation, using 5.30$\times$ less data and 14.34$\times$ fewer parameters than full fine-tuning. Ultimately, PCGD bridges the computational efficiency of generative surrogates with the rigorous physical fidelity of traditional TCAD, unlocking highly scalable, field-level analysis for robust device engineering.

</details>


### [494] [Adaptive Block Diffusion: Resolving Training-Inference Mismatch in Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29275)
*Gagan Jain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Block Diffusion (ABD) trains a single diffusion language model over a distribution of prefix-window configurations to resolve the mismatch between fixed training contexts and arbitrary inference configurations, preventing off-grid performance degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Language Models suffer from a mismatch between training (fixed context structures with predetermined token subsets) and inference (arbitrary configurations), causing performance degradation off the training grid.

Method: ABD treats the prefix-window configuration as a stochastic variable and optimizes denoising risk over a distribution of configurations, training a single model over the full configuration space without architectural changes.

Result: ABD exhibits structural invariance across decoding scales, avoids off-grid collapse, recovers a monotonic relationship between block size and perplexity, and matches or outperforms fixed-block specialists at their target scales.

Conclusion: Generalization across decoding strategies is governed by the training distribution's support, and ABD guarantees denoising optimality for any inference policy whose configurations are covered during training.

Abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) are typically trained under fixed context structures, restricting denoising to predetermined token subsets. This creates a mismatch between training and inference, where models must operate over arbitrary configurations, leading to degradation off the training grid. We propose Adaptive Block Diffusion (ABD), which resolves this mismatch by optimizing denoising risk over a distribution of prefix-window configurations. By treating the configuration as a stochastic variable, ABD trains a single model over the full configuration space without architectural changes. We show that generalization across decoding strategies is governed by the support of the training distribution, and that ABD guarantees denoising optimality for any inference policy whose configurations are covered during training. Empirically, ABD exhibits structural invariance across decoding scales, avoiding off-grid collapse and recovering a monotonic relationship between block size and perplexity, while matching or outperforming fixed-block specialists at their target scales.

</details>


### [495] [Deterministic Decisions for High-Stakes AI. A Zero-Egress Pipeline with the Deployability of RAG and the Accuracy of Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29280)
*Craig Atkinson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Zero-shot LLM educational advisors exhibit intervention bias (recommending action when inaction is optimal), causing 43% false-positive rate. Supervised policies (Decision Transformer, XGBoost) eliminate this bias. LLM-as-judge evaluation fails to detect this bias, revealing an Evaluation Gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and quantify "intervention bias"—a previously unmeasured failure mode where zero-shot LLM agents over-prescribe actions even when oracle policy dictates inaction—in educational advisory contexts, and to demonstrate that supervised policy learning resolves it.

Method: Six-arm ablation on OULAD (N=800, four temporal cutoffs). Compared zero-shot GPT-4o, commercial RAG, SQL-augmented retrieval vs. supervised policies: an ONNX Decision Transformer (trajectory-conditioned) and XGBoost classifier (snapshot, prefix-only features), both trained on oracle-labelled trajectories. Evaluated calibration error, macro-F1, action flip rate, latency.

Result: At day 56 (70.1% need no intervention), GPT-4o recommends action for 73% (43 pp false-positive rate). Supervised DT achieves macro-F1 0.79, macro-recall 0.85, 0% action flip rate, <5ms latency. DT and XGBoost have near-zero calibration error. LLM-as-judge (G-Eval) rewards fluent over-prescription, blind to intervention bias.

Conclusion: Zero-shot LLMs suffer from intervention bias leading to massive false positives (~4,300 unnecessary contacts per 10K students). Supervised policy learning eliminates this bias. The most robust finding is the bias contrast, not absolute accuracy. Current LLM evaluation metrics fail to capture this decision-quality gap.

Abstract: We identify intervention bias as a previously unquantified failure mode of zero-shot large-language-model (LLM) educational advisory agents: without task-specific training, they recommend action when a hindsight-optimal oracle policy mandates inaction. In a six-arm ablation on the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (N=800 students, four temporal cutoffs), at day 56 -- when the oracle designates 70.1% of students as needing no intervention -- zero-shot GPT-4o recommends action for 73%, a 43 percentage-point false-positive rate. Commercial RAG and SQL-augmented retrieval are comparably miscalibrated; at 10,000 students this implies about 4,300 unnecessary advisor contacts per cycle.
  Supervised policy learning eliminates this bias: a trajectory-conditioned ONNX Decision Transformer (DT) and a snapshot XGBoost classifier, trained on the same oracle-labelled trajectories under strict prefix-only features, both achieve near-zero calibration error. The DT reaches macro-F1 0.79 (macro-recall 0.85) across all five action classes, predicting even the rare load-reduction action without collapsing, at a 0% action flip rate and sub-5 ms CPU decision latency. The two supervised arms are on par; the DT's edge over XGBoost at the final cutoff is indicative only (unpaired across cohorts).
  Scope: we validate Stage-2 decision-making (EAV state vector to supervised policy) under controlled oracle input from structured OULAD data; high fidelity reflects feature-oracle alignment, not general high-stakes-AI capability. The most robust finding is the intervention-bias contrast, not the absolute accuracies. We also show an Evaluation Gap: LLM-as-judge scoring (DeepEval G-Eval) is blind to intervention bias, rewarding fluent over-prescription rather than decision quality.

</details>


### [496] [Beyond Trajectory Matching: Reflow with Marginal Distribution Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29287)
*Chen Wang,Peiran Yun,Pan Xie,Ke Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reflow-based distillation for accelerating generative models suffers from under-determined endpoint distributions despite matching ODE trajectories. The paper proposes a marginal-alignment regularizer that penalizes discrepancy between student and teacher marginal distributions at distillation interval endpoints, computed via log-density tracking and score evaluation from the frozen teacher model without auxiliary networks. This improves few-step generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate ODE discretization in diffusion/continuous-flow generative models requires many steps. Reflow-based distillation simplifies teacher ODE trajectories for few-step generation, but trajectory matching can under-determine the student-induced distribution—two student models with identical trajectory-matching loss can yield different endpoint marginals and generation quality.

Method: Introduces a marginal-alignment regularizer that penalizes discrepancy between student-induced marginal and teacher marginal at each distillation interval endpoint. The regularizer computes log-density changes along the student ODE and evaluates scores from the frozen teacher model, requiring no auxiliary trainable networks or adversarial optimization. Applies uniformly to vanilla reflow and piecewise reflow. A telescoping total-variation bound is proved showing local marginal alignment controls final-time distribution discrepancy.

Result: Experiments on benchmark backbones demonstrate effectiveness for few-step generation. The method improves generation quality compared to standard reflow-based distillation approaches.

Conclusion: Marginal-alignment regularization addresses the theoretical limitation of under-determined endpoint distributions in trajectory-matching distillation. The proposed framework is uniform across reflow variants and provably controls final-time distribution discrepancy via local alignment, enabling better few-step generative performance.

Abstract: Diffusion and continuous-flow generative models achieve high-quality generation, and their deterministic sampling can be formulated as solving learned ODE dynamics. However, accurate ODE discretization often requires many steps, making efficient few-step generation a key challenge. Among acceleration strategies, reflow-based distillation simplifies teacher ODE trajectories so that a student model can approximate the teacher transport with fewer steps. We identify a theoretical limitation of this paradigm, namely that trajectory matching can under-determine the distribution induced by the student model. In particular, two student models can attain the same trajectory-matching loss while inducing different endpoint marginal distributions, which may lead to different generation quality. To address this limitation, we introduce a marginal-alignment regularizer that penalizes the discrepancy between the student-induced marginal and the corresponding teacher marginal at the endpoint of each distillation interval. The regularizer is computed by tracking log-density changes along the ODE induced by the student model and evaluating scores from the frozen teacher model, without requiring auxiliary trainable networks or adversarial optimization. The resulting framework applies uniformly to the reflow family, including vanilla reflow and piecewise reflow. We further prove a telescoping total-variation bound showing that local marginal alignment controls the final-time discrepancy between the student-induced and teacher-induced distributions. Experiments on benchmark backbones demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method for few-step generation.

</details>


### [497] [SP-CACW: Convergence-Aware Client Weighting for Selfish Personalized Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29322)
*Yaron Kiselman,Kfir Y. Levy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes SP-CACW, a convergence-aware client-weighting framework for selfish personalization in federated learning that selects aggregation weights by minimizing an upper bound on the target client's convergence error, trading off peer bias against stochastic variance and enabling zero-weight assignment to harmful peers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard federated learning optimizes a global average objective that can underperform for clients with data distributions differing from the population. Collaborative learning is sustainable only when each participant benefits, so there is a need for methods that allow a target client to minimize its own risk while avoiding negative transfer from harmful peers.

Method: SP-CACW (Selfish Personalization with Convergence-Aware Client Weighting) selects aggregation weights by minimizing an upper bound on the target client's convergence error. The weighting rule explicitly trades off peer bias against stochastic variance and can assign zero weight to harmful peers. The method provides convergence guarantees under smoothness and bounded-variance assumptions.

Result: Evaluated on MNIST, CIFAR-100, and LEAF Shakespeare datasets. SP-CACW is competitive with or improves over strong personalized and clustering baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed selfish personalization framework enables a target client to benefit from collaborative learning while avoiding negative transfer, making participation sustainable even for clients with non-i.i.d. data distributions.

Abstract: Collaborative learning is sustainable only when it benefits each participant. Standard federated learning optimizes a global average objective, which can under perform for clients whose data distributions differ substantially from the population. We study selfish personalization: how a designated target client can use peer gradients to minimize its own risk while avoiding negative transfer. We propose SP-CACW, a convergence-aware client-weighting framework that selects aggregation weights by minimizing an upper bound on the target client's convergence error. The resulting rule explicitly trades off peer bias against stochastic variance and can assign zero weight to harmful peers. We provide convergence guarantees under smoothness and bounded-variance assumptions and evaluate the method on MNIST, CIFAR-100, and LEAF Shakespeare, where it is competitive with or improves over strong personalized and clustering baselines.

</details>


### [498] [Deciphering Region-Level Signatures from Latency Measurements in LEO Satellite Internet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29324)
*Xiang Shi,Yifei Zhang,Peng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hierarchical analytical framework to characterize region-level latency in LEO satellite Internet (Starlink) using RTT measurements, achieving 83% accuracy on short-term data but showing limited temporal generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Region-level latency performance principles in LEO satellite Internet are insufficiently understood, limiting the ability to identify region-specific latency signatures under dynamic network conditions.

Method: The authors formulate region-level latency characterization using Starlink RTT measurements from the LENS dataset, and propose a hierarchical analytical framework that transforms raw RTT sequences into multi-scale statistical features for cross-region comparison. Mutual information analysis and XGBoost-based feature importance are used to identify discriminative features.

Result: Latency differences are strongly associated with infrastructure availability and Starlink dish-to-Point-of-Presence distance. Minimum RTT is identified as the most discriminative feature. The model achieves 83% accuracy on short-term data but performance degrades over longer periods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework shows effectiveness for short-term region-level latency characterization but has limited temporal generalization, motivating the need for adaptive models and feature representations for long-term performance.

Abstract: Low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite Internet has become an indispensable infrastructure that provide growing coverage for global users. Despite extensive measurement efforts, the principles underlying region-level performance characteristics remain insufficiently understood, limiting the ability to identify region-specific latency signatures under dynamic network conditions. In this paper, we formulate the problem of region-level latency characterization using Starlink round-trip time (RTT) measurements from the public LENS dataset. We then propose a hierarchical analytical framework that transforms raw RTT sequences into multi-scale statistical features for cross-region comparison. Using data from five geographically representative regions, we demonstrate that latency differences are strongly associated with deployment factors, particularly infrastructure availability and Starlink dish-to-Point-of-Presence distance. Mutual information analysis identifies minimum RTT as the most discriminative feature, which is further supported by XGBoost-based feature importance. The proposed model well achieves 83% accuracy on short-term data. However, its performance degrades over longer periods, indicating limited temporal generalization and motivating the need for adaptive models and feature representations for long-term performance in the future.

</details>


### [499] [Sample Complexity of Scientific Discovery: PAC Learnability of Compositional Function Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29331)
*Şuayp Talha Kocabay,Talha Rüzgar Akkuş,Kerem Yalçın*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that symbolic regression via compositional function trees has generalization bounds controlled by depth and Lipschitz constants (O(L^d/√n)), not exponential in symbolic structure count, and validates this empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic regression is often viewed as statistically intractable due to combinatorial growth of the hypothesis space. The authors aim to show that generalization is actually controlled under PAC learning theory.

Method: Uses PAC learning with Rademacher complexity analysis: finite-union bound over vocabulary size K, Maurer-type vector contraction, yielding complexity bound ℜ_n(ℋ_comp^d) ≤ (Kb√2L)^{d-1}ℜ_n(ℋ_comp^1). Validated with a modular codebase training differentiable operator trees on synthetic physics-like targets.

Result: Risk bounds scale as O(L^d/√n) when K,b=O(1) and ℜ_n(ℋ_comp^1)=O(n^{-1/2}). Empirical generalization gap correlates positively with predicted complexity term L̂^d/√n.

Conclusion: Symbolic regression via compositional trees is statistically tractable; generalization is controlled by depth and operator Lipschitz constants, not by combinatorial explosion of symbolic structures.

Abstract: Scientific discovery via symbolic regression is often viewed as statistically and computationally intractable because the hypothesis space of expressions grows combinatorially with depth. This paper revisits the statistical side through the lens of PAC learning, focusing on compositional function trees built from a finite vocabulary of smooth operators (e.g., $\{+,\times,\sin,\exp\}$ and affine maps). We prove that the relevant generalization quantity, Rademacher complexity, hence the excess risk, does not necessarily blow up exponentially with the number of distinct symbolic structures, but is controlled by (i) the depth $d$ and (ii) the Lipschitz constants of the base operators along the composed computation graph. Concretely, under mild Lipschitz conditions on operators and bounded affine leaves, a finite-union bound over a vocabulary of size $K=|\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{base}}|$ together with Maurer-type vector contraction yields $\mathfrak{R}_n(\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{comp}}^{d}) \leq (Kb\sqrt{2}L)^{d-1}\mathfrak{R}_n(\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{comp}}^{1})$ with arity bound $b$; corresponding high-probability risk bounds scale as $\mathcal{O}(L^{d}/\sqrt{n})$ when $K,b=O(1)$ and $\mathfrak{R}_n(\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{comp}}^{1})=O(n^{-1/2})$. We complement the theory with a modular codebase that trains differentiable operator trees (not MLPs) on synthetic "physics-like" targets of controlled depth and shows that the empirical generalization gap correlates positively with the predicted complexity term $(\widehat{L}^{d})/\sqrt{n}$.

</details>


### [500] [Reliability, Faithfulness, and the Limits of Post-hoc Explanations of Opaque Scientific Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29346)
*Nick Oh,Helen Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc explanation methods for scientific ML models cannot directly reveal how a phenomenon is structurally structured, even if the model is reliable and the explanation is faithful; external corroboration is needed for such claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges the common assumption that reliable ML models with faithful post-hoc explanations can directly provide insights into the structure of the underlying scientific phenomenon.

Method: Conceptual/logical argumentation distinguishing between reliability (model matches outcomes), faithfulness (explanation matches model), and structural correspondence (model works as the phenomenon works).

Result: Even when a model is reliable and its explanations are faithful, these conditions alone do not guarantee that the explanation reveals the true structural organization of the phenomenon.

Conclusion: Post-hoc explanations can generate candidate hypotheses about structure, but they require external corroboration to support claims about how a phenomenon is actually structured.

Abstract: Post-hoc explanation methods are routinely used to interpret scientific machine learning models, with the deliverable understood to be insight into the phenomenon the model has been trained on. The transition may be taken to be secured once the model is reliable enough and the explanation faithful enough. We argue it is not. Reliability checks that the model's predictions match the phenomenon's outcomes, and faithfulness checks that the explanation matches the model, but neither checks whether the model works as the phenomenon works, which is what a claim about structure requires. The chain can support candidate hypotheses under external corroboration, but it cannot, on its own, support claims about how the phenomenon is in fact structured.

</details>


### [501] [Adaptive Financial Transformer with Regime-Gated Attention for Stock Return Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29347)
*Dishan Sarkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Adaptive Financial Transformer (AFT) for stock return prediction under non-stationary markets, using semantic feature grouping (95 features into 11 categories), adaptive self-attention via Market Regime Encoder and Adaptive Gate Network, and a financially-aware composite objective. Achieves competitive performance with 15.2% fewer parameters and improved interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional Transformers treat all financial features uniformly and lack adaptivity to non-stationary market regimes, leading to suboptimal predictions; additionally, sequence alignment and backtesting issues can inflate reported trading performance.

Method: Groups 95 engineered financial features into 11 semantic categories; uses Market Regime Encoder, Adaptive Gate Network, and Adaptive Financial Context module to dynamically bias self-attention; optimizes a composite objective combining prediction error, directional accuracy, and non-overlapping Sharpe ratio.

Result: Competitive predictive performance with 15.2% reduction in model complexity and improved parameter efficiency via feature selection, validated through chronological evaluation, ablation studies, hyperparameter optimization, and multi-stock validation.

Conclusion: AFT provides an interpretable, parameter-efficient Transformer architecture for financial time-series forecasting that adapts to changing market conditions and addresses common backtesting pitfalls.

Abstract: Adaptive Financial Transformer (AFT) is proposed for stock return prediction under non-stationary financial markets. The model incorporates a Market Regime Encoder, an Adaptive Gate Network, and an Adaptive Financial Context module to dynamically bias self-attention based on semantic relationships between financial indicators. Unlike conventional Transformer architectures that treat all input features uniformly, the proposed approach groups 95 engineered financial features into 11 semantic categories and adapts attention according to latent market regimes. The study also identifies and corrects sequence alignment and backtesting issues that can inflate reported trading performance, and introduces a financially-aware composite objective that jointly optimizes prediction error, directional accuracy, and non-overlapping Sharpe ratio. Extensive experiments compare the proposed architecture against classical machine learning models, recurrent neural networks, and Transformer baselines using chronological evaluation, five random seeds, ablation studies, hyperparameter optimization, explainability analysis, and multi-stock validation. Results demonstrate competitive predictive performance while reducing model complexity by 15.2% and improving parameter efficiency through feature selection, providing an interpretable Transformer architecture for financial time-series forecasting.

</details>


### [502] [Interventional Flow Matching: Prospective Dose-Response Forecasting with Velocity-Field Jacobian Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29386)
*Amirreza Dolatpour Fathkouhi,Justin Lee,Heman Shakeri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IFM is a continuous-time generative framework that uses solver-free Jacobian regularization to enforce physiologically correct prospective forecasts for glucose management, outperforming baselines on interventional response metrics while maintaining good observational accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard time-series forecasting cannot guarantee correct responses to planned interventions because future drivers (insulin, carbohydrates) are policy-dependent—coupled to patient state, behavior, and clinical decision rules. A prospective interventional forecasting framework is needed.

Method: Interventional Flow Matching (IFM) conditions a flow-matching velocity field on patient history and planned future drivers in a bounded latent glucose space. It uses a solver-free Jacobian regularization that penalizes the Jacobian of the velocity field w.r.t. smoothed treatment drivers to impose signed, dose-bounded local sensitivities (insulin lowers glucose, carbohydrates raise it) directly in the learned dynamics.

Result: On UVA/Padova type 1 diabetes simulations, IFM achieves the strongest balance between observed-driver RMSE and interventional response metrics, consistently producing physiologically correct responses to both insulin and carbohydrate drivers with high directional and ranking consistency.

Conclusion: IFM demonstrates that imposing physiological constraints via Jacobian regularization of the velocity field is a viable alternative to mechanistic ODEs or rollout-based simulations for prospective interventional forecasting in glucose management.

Abstract: Predicting a patient's physiological trajectory under a planned treatment sequence is a prospective interventional problem, not standard time-series extrapolation. We study this problem in glucose management, where insulin and carbohydrate records are policy-dependent: future drivers are coupled to patient state, behavior, and clinical decision rules, so observational forecasting accuracy alone does not guarantee correct responses to planned interventions.
  We introduce Interventional Flow Matching (IFM), a continuous-time generative framework for physiologically constrained prospective forecasting. IFM conditions a flow-matching velocity field on patient history and planned future drivers in a bounded latent glucose space. Rather than embedding strict mechanistic glucose--insulin ODE equations or enforcing causality through rollout-based simulations, IFM uses a solver-free regularization: it penalizes the Jacobian of the instantaneous velocity field with respect to smoothed treatment drivers. This imposes signed, dose-bounded local sensitivities directly on the learned dynamics: insulin lowers glucose, carbohydrates raise it, and both responses remain within plausible ranges.
  On a simulated UVA/Padova type 1 diabetes cohort, IFM achieves the strongest balance between observed-driver RMSE and interventional response metrics. Across experiments, it consistently produces physiologically correct responses to both insulin and carbohydrate drivers while maintaining high directional, and ranking consistency.

</details>


### [503] [Temporal Posed and Spontaneous Gesture Recognition from Electromyography in the Rock-Paper-Scissors Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29423)
*Xin Wei,Huakun Liu,Felix Dollack,Monica Perusquia-Hernandez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study explores EMG-based gesture recognition in a Rock-Paper-Scissors game, finding that muscle activation precedes visible gesture onset by ~800 ms (onset) and ~342 ms (peak), with posed gesture recognition accuracy of 63.4% and spontaneous accuracy of 53.6%. Opponent EMG also carries predictive information (65% accuracy at ~2 seconds post-visual onset), highlighting the potential of EMG for rapid intent recognition in interactive contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time gesture recognition is needed in many domains, particularly for multiuser contexts. EMG signals may offer temporal advantages over visual-based recognition, but their predictive timing and accuracy in interactive, spontaneous settings remain underexplored.

Method: 24 participants played RPS in dyads; two-channel forearm EMG was recorded. Temporal characteristics of EMG (onset and peak relative to visible gesture onset) were analyzed. Self-gesture recognition accuracy was evaluated for posed and spontaneous gestures, and cross-participant opponent EMG recognition was tested.

Result: EMG onsets detected ≥800 ms before visible onset; EMG peaks ~342 ms before visible onset. Posed gesture accuracy: 63.4%; spontaneous accuracy: 53.6% (high inter-individual variability). Opponent EMG recognition peaked at 65% accuracy, 2082 ms after visual onset.

Conclusion: EMG provides a temporal predictive advantage for gesture recognition, useful for applications requiring rapid intent recognition (e.g., HCI, assistive tech). Future work should refine onset detection and address spontaneous movement variability to improve real-world performance.

Abstract: The importance of gesture recognition has been acknowledged in many domains requiring real-time recognition systems. Two requirements for these are fast recognition in multiuser contexts. Therefore, we explored the temporal characteristics of electromyography (EMG) and its accuracy in recognizing gestures in a Rock-Paper-Scissors (RPS) game. Twenty-four participants played RPS in dyads, while a two-channel EMG was recorded from the forearm. We found out that EMG onsets could be detected at least 800 ms before the gesture's visible onset, and that the EMG peaks around 342 ms before the visible onset of the gesture. Furthermore, we evaluated self-gesture recognition in both posed and spontaneous gesture conditions. The mean accuracy for posed gestures reached 63.4%. The model trained on posed gestures achieved 53.6% for spontaneous gestures, with considerable variation across individuals. We also checked whether detecting a player's gesture from the opponent's EMG was possible. The peak mean accuracy was 65%, peaking at 2082 ms after the visual onset of the gesture. This suggests that the opponent's reaction to an observed gesture contains information about the observed gesture due to the dynamics of the interactions while playing. The temporal predictive advantage of EMG signals, where muscle activation precedes observable movement, offers potential benefits for applications requiring rapid intent recognition, such as human-computer interaction and assistive technologies. Future work should focus on refining onset detection and reducing the impact of spontaneous movement variability across conditions to improve recognition performance in dynamic and real-world environments.

</details>


### [504] [Randomized neural operator for parametric PDEs with fast training and conformal uncertainty quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29440)
*Zirui Deng,Jingbo Sun,Deyu Meng,Fei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCA--RaNN combines PCA with fixed random features and closed-form least-squares readout to solve parametric PDEs, reducing training time by 1–3 orders of magnitude while maintaining accuracy, and supporting uncertainty quantification via ensembles and online adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional neural operators require expensive non-convex training, limiting efficiency for many-query tasks like uncertainty quantification, design optimization, and inverse problems.

Method: PCA--RaNN uses PCA-based dimensionality reduction, fixed random features, and a closed-form least-squares readout to reformulate latent operator learning as fixed-feature linear regression. It introduces energy-matched scaling, two-parameter BFGS refinement, and ensemble averaging for variance reduction.

Result: Training time reduced by 1–3 orders of magnitude on Burgers, Darcy, Navier-Stokes, and backward heat equation benchmarks with competitive accuracy against operator-learning baselines. Ensemble supports split-conformal prediction intervals.

Conclusion: PCA--RaNN provides an efficient, uncertainty-aware surrogate for many-query scientific workflows, with rapid online adaptation via recursive least squares without retraining hidden features.

Abstract: Repeatedly solving parametric PDEs is essential for uncertainty quantification, design optimization and inverse problems, but conventional neural operators require expensive non-convex training. We introduce PCA--RaNN, a randomized latent neural operator that combines PCA-based dimensionality reduction with fixed random features and a closed-form least-squares readout. It recasts latent operator learning as fixed-feature linear regression, reducing training time by one to three orders of magnitude across benchmarks while maintaining competitive accuracy. We introduce an energy-matched scaling rule and a lightweight two-parameter BFGS refinement to correct suboptimal feature scales. Ensemble averaging reduces predictive variance. On Burgers, Darcy, Navier--Stokes and backward heat equation benchmarks, PCA--RaNN provides a favorable speed--accuracy trade-off against operator-learning baselines. The ensemble supports split-conformal prediction intervals, and the linear readout enables rapid online adaptation via recursive least squares without retraining hidden features. This provides an efficient, uncertainty-aware surrogate for many-query scientific workflows.

</details>


### [505] [Interpretable Inverse Design of Metal-Organic Frameworks with Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29459)
*Kyungmin Nam,Seunghee Han,Jihan Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM4MOF is a closed-loop framework using language-model agents to perform interpretable inverse design of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) through iterative hypothesis generation, constraint-based candidate selection, and simulation-grounded testing, achieving strong performance across multiple tasks without task-specific model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse design of MOFs faces a combinatorially vast search space where property labels are expensive to obtain and most ML models lack interpretability about why a structure succeeds.

Method: A closed-loop framework with two LLM agents: one proposes interpretable design hypotheses over metal nodes, linkers, pore geometry, and functional chemistry; the second translates them into constraints to select candidate MOFs. Each hypothesis is tested via four diagnostic beams applying different constraint subsets. The loop runs autonomously for ten iterations.

Result: LLM4MOF concentrates search on top-performing structures across six adsorption, separation, and electronic-structure tasks within 400 property evaluations; generates novel MOFs de novo validated in live simulation; outperforms random search and genetic algorithms at ~$1 per campaign.

Conclusion: Language-model agents can run interpretable, simulation-grounded inverse design without training a model per objective, offering an efficient and cost-effective alternative for MOF discovery.

Abstract: Inverse design of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) requires searching a combinatorially vast space where property labels are expensive and most machine-learning models reveal little about why a structure succeeds. We introduce LLM4MOF, a closed-loop framework in which language-model agents reason about chemistry, build candidate MOFs, and test them in simulation, refining hypotheses over ten autonomous iterations. One agent proposes interpretable design hypotheses over metal nodes, linkers, pore geometry, and functional chemistry, and a second translates them into constraints that select candidate MOFs, each made of a metal node, organic linker, and matching topology. Each hypothesis is tested through four diagnostic beams that apply different subsets of its constraints, so comparing them shows whether geometry, chemistry, or metal choice drives performance. Even when blind to the global property landscape of databases, LLM4MOF concentrates its search on top-performing structures across six adsorption, separation, and electronic-structure tasks within 400 property evaluations. The same loop also generates new MOFs de novo and validates them in live simulation, where it adapts the geometry to each requested condition, outperforming random search and a genetic algorithm at roughly $1 per campaign. LLM4MOF shows that language-model agents can run interpretable, simulation-grounded inverse design without training a model per objective.

</details>


### [506] [Prototype Latent World Model Replay for Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29465)
*Weizhi Nie,Hui Wang,Weijie Wang,Yuting Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Prototype Latent World Model Replay, a memory-free class-incremental learning framework that replays old class prototypes (distributions over latent states) from a frozen pretrained encoder, using a contrastive adapter to separate old and new classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class-incremental learning struggles with catastrophic forgetting when raw old samples cannot be stored; the goal is to preserve decision regions for old classes without exemplar memory.

Method: Uses a frozen ImageNet-pretrained encoder to map images into a latent state space. Each old class is summarized as prototype-centered distributions with class-specific variances. During new task learning, latent states are sampled from this prototype world model, and a lightweight adapter + classifier is trained on both sampled old states and real new features, with a supervised contrastive loss for intra-class compactness and old-new separation.

Result: On Split CIFAR-100 under Inc5/Inc10/Inc20, LastAcc improves from 4.55%→31.64%, 9.06%→37.06%, 16.96%→43.10%; AvgAcc reaches 45.86%, 52.19%, 56.18% respectively.

Conclusion: Prototype latent memory effectively preserves reusable class-state distributions rather than merely fitting the current classifier, enabling memory-free continual learning with strong forgetting mitigation.

Abstract: Class-incremental learning requires a model to learn new classes while preserving decision regions for old ones. This is difficult when raw old samples are no longer available. We propose Prototype Latent World Model Replay, a memory-free framework that stores old classes as distributions over stable hidden states rather than as images. A frozen ImageNet-pretrained encoder maps each image into a latent state space. In this space, each class is summarized by several prototype-centered distributions with class-specific variances. When new classes arrive, the model samples old latent states from this prototype world model. It then trains a lightweight adapter and classifier using both sampled old states and real new-class features. We also add a supervised contrastive term in the adapter space to promote intra-class compactness and old-new class separation. On Split CIFAR-100, our method improves over fine-tuning under Inc5, Inc10, and Inc20 without storing raw exemplars. The full Ours-LWM+Con model raises LastAcc from 4.55% to 31.64%, from 9.06% to 37.06%, and from 16.96% to 43.10% in Inc5, Inc10, and Inc20, respectively. It also achieves AvgAcc of 45.86%, 52.19%, and 56.18%. Ablation and retention analyses show that stable latent-state replay is the main source of the gain. Contrastive separation further refines the old-new geometry. These results suggest that prototype latent memory preserves reusable class-state distributions, rather than only fitting the current classifier.

</details>


### [507] [Self-Supervised Calibration of Scientific Instruments Using Physical Consistency Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29466)
*M. Rejmund,A. Lemasson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed self-supervised framework is introduced that jointly learns latent calibration parameters and task predictions directly from raw measurements, demonstrated for ionic charge-state determination in the VAMOS++ magnetic spectrometer, enabling self-calibrating and self-monitoring scientific instruments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration remains a major obstacle for deploying machine learning in scientific instrumentation because it typically requires expert intervention, dedicated procedures, and manually labeled data.

Method: A physics-informed self-supervised framework that exploits known physical constraints to generate pseudo-labels iteratively, transforming calibration into a self-supervised optimization problem. It uses iterative fractional pseudo-labeling driven by the discrete nature of atomic masses, starting from a weak prior on the mean ionic charge state.

Result: Accurate ionic charge-state reconstruction; inferred calibration coefficients provide compact detector state representation enabling automated monitoring of gain drifts, pressure variations, and detector aging; labels can be transferred to specialized models for quantifying detector imperfections.

Conclusion: Establishes a general paradigm for self-calibrating and self-monitoring scientific instruments, representing a step toward intelligent experimental systems capable of autonomous calibration, analysis, and performance optimization.

Abstract: Calibration remains one of the principal obstacles to the deployment of machine learning in scientific instrumentation because it typically relies on expert intervention, dedicated procedures, and manually labelled data. We introduce a physics-informed self-supervised framework that jointly learns latent detector calibration parameters and task-specific predictions directly from raw measurements without requiring pre-calibrated signals or external labels. The method exploits known physical constraints to generate pseudo-labels iteratively, transforming calibration into a self-supervised optimization problem. The approach is demonstrated for ionic charge-state determination in the VAMOS++ magnetic spectrometer, where the calibration of a segmented ionization chamber and the inference of ionic charge states are learned simultaneously. Starting from a weak prior on the mean ionic charge state, the model progressively refines its predictions through iterative fractional pseudo-labelling driven by the discrete nature of atomic masses. Beyond accurate ionic charge-state reconstruction, the inferred calibration coefficients provide a compact representation of the detector state that enables automated monitoring of gain drifts, pressure variations, and detector aging. The resulting labels can subsequently be transferred to specialized models that quantify detector imperfections and track their spatial and temporal evolution. These results establish a general paradigm for self-calibrating and self-monitoring scientific instruments and represent a step toward intelligent experimental systems capable of autonomous calibration, analysis, and performance optimization.

</details>


### [508] [Structured Proper Loss Geometries for Multiclass Classification: Theory and Controlled Empirical Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29471)
*Soumyadip Sarkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes three multiclass scoring-rule objectives (CAPM, HPG, APMS) for classification, deriving theoretical bounds on regret, curvature, and penalty properties. Controlled experiments on varied datasets show these methods are close to cross-entropy on clean data with some gains under label noise, but overall do not demonstrate general superiority over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the curvature of strictly proper scoring rules affects optimization and finite-sample behavior in multiclass classification, and to evaluate whether specially designed objectives (quadratic Bregman, log-cosh ridge, probability-margin penalty) offer practical advantages over cross-entropy.

Method: Three multiclass objectives are studied: CAPM (class-aware quadratic Bregman score), HPG (strongly convex generator with constrained log-cosh ridges), and APMS (HPG with annealed probability-margin penalty). Theoretical analysis derives conditional-regret, curvature, range, and logit-gradient bounds for CAPM and HPG, and penalty-range and conditional-target displacement bounds for APMS. Five-seed controlled experiments on Digits, Wisconsin breast cancer, and synthetic datasets under various corruptions and imbalances.

Result: Candidates are close to cross-entropy on clean data with descriptive gains in some noisy-label scenarios. Selected noisy-label baselines outperform on Digits with 40% symmetric noise; prior-adjustment methods excel on the 30:1 synthetic long-tail problem. Ablations show no consistent benefit from candidate-specific components. Overall, no general superiority is supported.

Conclusion: The mathematical analysis establishes the stated theoretical properties, but the combined theoretical and empirical evidence does not support a claim of general superiority for the proposed objectives over standard cross-entropy or existing baselines.

Abstract: Strictly proper scoring rules identify the true conditional class distribution at population level, but their curvature can alter optimization and finite-sample behavior. We study three multiclass objectives: a class-aware quadratic Bregman score (CAPM), a strongly convex generator with constrained log-cosh ridges (HPG), and an HPG objective with an annealed probability-margin penalty (APMS). CAPM is treated as a structured instance of established quadratic scoring-rule theory. We derive conditional-regret, curvature, range, and logit-gradient bounds for CAPM and HPG, and prove exact penalty-range and conditional-target displacement bounds for APMS. Controlled five-seed experiments use Digits, Wisconsin breast cancer, and synthetic confusion and long-tail problems under clean labels, symmetric and pair-flip corruption, class imbalance, calibration evaluation, input corruption, and first-order adversarial perturbations. The candidates are close to cross-entropy on clean data and show descriptive gains in some noisy-label cells, but the five-seed comparisons are interpreted descriptively rather than as significance evidence. The selected noisy-label baselines perform better on Digits with 40% symmetric label noise, and explicit prior-adjustment methods perform better in the 30:1 synthetic long-tail experiment. Ablations do not show a consistent benefit from the candidate-specific graph, ridge, or margin components. The mathematical analysis establishes the stated properties, and the experiments delimit the empirical evidence; together they do not support a claim of general superiority.

</details>


### [509] [CRAFT: Counterfactual Credit Assignment from Free Sibling Rollouts for Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29476)
*Zibin Meng,Kani Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFT introduces a three-pillar credit-assignment scheme for self-distilled agentic RL that addresses two key limitations of prior methods: retrospective scoring and sign-blindness. It provides signed per-token counterfactual credits, adaptive gate control, and token-level polarized KL penalties, with bit-exact comparability to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-distilled agentic RL methods gate token-level distillation by a scalar teacher-student log-probability gap, which is doubly limited: (1) retrospective—it only scores the realized rollout, not counterfactual ones; (2) sign-blind—it never signals when a teacher-preferred action would have harmed the trajectory.

Method: CRAFT comprises three pillars: (1) Counterfactual Token Importance—reuses GRPO's G-1 sibling rollouts, importance-weighting them by the log-probability gap to form a self-normalized estimate of the group-level counterfactual change in advantage, yielding signed per-token credit; (2) an asymmetric controller that adjusts distillation vs. reference-KL weights based on an EMA of gate activity; (3) token-level polarized KL penalty that switches between mode-seeking and mode-covering updates according to the credit sign. Each pillar can be independently disabled for bit-exact comparability.

Result: Evaluated across three agentic environments, four model scales, five end-to-end methods, and two tabulated prior-work baselines. Includes Adaptive-CRINGE as a comparator sharing Pillar 2 with CRAFT to isolate the counterfactual contribution.

Conclusion: CRAFT provides consistency proofs and variance bounds for its estimator, structural and bit-exact reproducibility guarantees, and outperforms baselines by addressing both the retrospective and sign-blind limitations of prior distillation gating.

Abstract: Self-distilled agentic reinforcement learning augments trajectory-level reward with a token-level distillation loss, using as its teacher the same policy conditioned on privileged context.
