Coupling coefficients range from 0.00 to 1.18 across per-condition means (CV approx 0.9, n=8 conditions). Four conditions show strong coupling (N=36; GPT-4o May, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3.7-plus, DashScope 30r); four collapse to near-zero (N=76; GPT-4o June, qwen-plus N=30, symmetric LR, DeepSeek self-eval). The May-to-June GPT-4o drift -- an N=8 re-replication inverting the study's conclusion -- is the most informative measurement: a diagnostic instrument detecting its own instability demonstrates the fragility it was designed to measure. Self-evaluation (97% zero, JSD=0.003) consistently collapses, though floor effects are possible. Output-format confound analysis finds per-strategy aggregate rho=0.89 but per-instance rho=0.219 (p=0.093); PCI reported as preference-convergence metric. We release EPC with all data. The finding is not any single coupling magnitude but the pattern of version-conditional instability that makes single-snapshot evaluator studies unreliable.

</details>


### [534] [The Hidden Cost of Resampling: How Imbalance Correction Degrades Probability Calibration in Tree Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29720)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the calibration costs of resampling methods (SMOTE, undersampling) for class-imbalanced classification, finding that SMOTE has small calibration damage but undersampling severely inflates error, and post-hoc recalibration effectively repairs both.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work established that undersampling distorts probability calibration by altering the training prior, but the calibration impact of synthetic oversampling (SMOTE) was not well understood. The authors aim to provide a practical, evidence-based guide on when calibration damage from resampling matters and how to fix it.

Method: Empirical evaluation across five public datasets (imbalance ratio 1.9-70) with two ensemble models (random forest, gradient boosting), ten seeds, and paired statistics. Compare SMOTE, random undersampling, and random oversampling on calibration (ECE) and discrimination (AUC). Test post-hoc recalibration (Platt scaling, isotonic regression) and analytic prior-shift correction.

Result: (1) SMOTE's calibration cost is small (ECE +0.009, Cliff's delta = +0.27) and its discrimination gains outweigh the penalty; (2) random undersampling is more dangerous, inflating ECE from 0.008 to 0.395 at ratio 70 due to small training sets; (3) post-hoc recalibration reduces ECE by up to 66% with negligible AUC cost (-0.002); (4) analytic prior-shift correction works for undersampling but not SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts class-conditional densities rather than just the prior.

Conclusion: Imbalanced-learning studies should report calibration alongside discrimination, and practitioners should recalibrate after resampling whenever predicted probabilities drive decisions.

Abstract: Resampling methods such as SMOTE and random under/over-sampling are standard tools for class-imbalanced classification, almost always evaluated by minority-class accuracy or F1. Prior work has established that undersampling degrades probability calibration by distorting the training prior [1]. We extend this lens to synthetic oversampling (SMOTE) and provide a practical, evidence-based guide to when calibration damage matters and how to fix it. Across five public datasets (imbalance ratio 1.9-70) and two ensemble models (random forest, gradient boosting), with ten seeds and paired statistics, we find: (1) SMOTE's calibration cost is real but small (ECE +0.009; Cliff's delta = +0.27, small-to-moderate) across the studied imbalance range (IR 1.9-70) and its discrimination gains typically outweigh the calibration penalty; (2) random undersampling is the genuine danger -- its damage grows sharply with imbalance, inflating ECE from 0.008 to 0.395 on a dataset with ratio 70, largely because the resulting training sets are too small to estimate probabilities reliably; (3) a single post-hoc recalibration step (Platt or isotonic) eliminates the damage, reducing ECE by up to 66% at a negligible ranking-power cost (AUC -0.002, Cliff's delta = -0.07); and (4) the analytic prior-shift correction that repairs undersampling does not transfer to SMOTE, because SMOTE distorts the class-conditional density rather than only the prior -- so data-driven recalibration remains necessary. We recommend that imbalanced-learning studies report calibration alongside discrimination, and that practitioners recalibrate after resampling whenever predicted probabilities drive decisions.

</details>


### [535] [Redefining Maritime Anomaly Detection via Equation-Grounded Synthetic Anomalies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29721)
*Youngseok Hwang,Sungho Bae,Dohun Lee,Jaeeun Seo,Jeehong Kim,Wonhee Lee,Hyunwoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel equation-grounded anomaly taxonomy for maritime anomaly detection using AIS data, defining three anomaly types (unexpected AIS activity, route deviation, and close approach). It introduces a score-synthesize-label pipeline with LLM-guided plausibility scores, benchmark evaluation settings, and evaluates multiple time-series and anomaly detection models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing maritime anomaly detection methods rely on distribution-based rarity or rule/expert-assisted labeling, both of which are limited: statistical rarity often misses practically critical events, expert labeling is costly and subjective, and both overlook interaction-driven hazards like near-miss vessel approaches.

Method: Proposes an equation-grounded anomaly taxonomy (A1: unexpected AIS activity, A2: route deviation, A3: close approach) under a limited AIS observation schema, and a unified score-synthesize-label pipeline that uses LLM-guided plausibility scores to synthesize anomalies with timestamp-level labels, plus benchmark evaluation settings with varying temporal-window length and anomaly-type composition.

Result: A systematic evaluation of a broad range of time-series models and anomaly detection models across different anomaly types, demonstrating the effectiveness and extensibility of the proposed taxonomy and pipeline.

Conclusion: The proposed taxonomy and pipeline provide a systematic, scalable, and objective foundation for evaluating maritime anomaly detection methods, addressing key limitations of prior labeling paradigms.

Abstract: Maritime anomaly detection is essential for ensuring maritime safety, security, and efficient traffic management at sea, with Automatic Identification System (AIS) data serving as a primary data source. Despite its importance, most publicly available AIS datasets lack predefined anomaly labels, forcing prior studies to rely on either distribution-based rarity or domain rule/expert-assisted labeling. These approaches, however, face fundamental limitations: statistical rarity often fails to reflect practically critical events, while expert-based labeling is costly, subjective, and difficult to scale. Moreover, both paradigms tend to overlook interaction-driven hazards such as near-miss approaches between vessels. To address these challenges, we propose an equation-grounded anomaly taxonomy that is implementable under a limited AIS observation schema and extensible to other AIS datasets. Specifically, the taxonomy defines three anomaly types: unexpected AIS activity (A1), route deviation (A2), and close approach (A3), covering both single-vessel and inter-vessel anomalies. Building on this taxonomy, we introduce a unified score-synthesize-label pipeline that produces LLM-guided plausibility scores, uses them to synthesize anomalies, and assigns timestamp-level labels. To rigorously assess detection performance, we further design benchmark evaluation settings that account for variations in temporal-window length and anomaly-type composition, and evaluate a broad range of time-series models and anomaly detection models. Together, these contributions provide a systematic basis for evaluating maritime anomaly detection methods across different anomaly types. Our code is available at https://github.com/snudial/open-maritime-anomaly-detection.

</details>


### [536] [ScaleAware-JEPA: Latent Representation for Discovery in Multiscale Physical Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29723)
*Guang-Xing Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ScaleAware-JEPA introduces a self-supervised framework that learns dense latent coordinates for continuous scalar fields using Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD) to separate fields into scale components, enabling a JEPA objective where masking geometry is tied to diffusion scale rather than arbitrary patch size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous physical fields exhibit multiscale structures, but useful coordinates are unknown in advance. Standard self-supervised methods define context and targets in fixed image coordinates, misaligned with fields organized across a continuous scale hierarchy.

Method: CDD separates each field into pixel-registered scale components, providing scale coordinates that define masking geometry. The JEPA objective predicts hidden structure with context footprint tied to diffusion scale of each component rather than arbitrary patch size.

Result: Across MHD turbulence, interstellar molecular gas, and urban nighttime-light data, the learned geometry maps back to coherent morphology, forming dense structural atlases without labels or predefined segmentation rules.

Conclusion: By tying latent prediction to the scale hierarchy of a field, ScaleAware-JEPA constructs latent coordinates for inspecting complex physical patterns before their relevant structures have been prescribed.

Abstract: Continuous physical fields represent a large fraction of data under scientific investigation. Their multiscale structures are central to discovery, yet useful coordinates are not known in advance. Standard self-supervised methods define context and targets in fixed image coordinates, posing a predictive task misaligned with fields organized across a continuous scale hierarchy. We introduce ScaleAware-JEPA, a framework that constructs dense, label-free latent coordinates for continuous scalar fields. Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD) separates each field into pixel-registered scale components and provides the scale coordinates that define the masking geometry. The resulting JEPA objective predicts hidden structure with a context footprint tied to the diffusion scale of each component rather than to an arbitrary patch size. Across MHD turbulence, interstellar molecular gas and urban nighttime-light structure, the learned geometry maps back to coherent morphology, forming dense structural atlases without labels or predefined segmentation rules. By tying latent prediction to the scale hierarchy of a field, ScaleAware-JEPA constructs latent coordinates through which complex physical patterns can be inspected before their relevant structures have been prescribed. Code is available at https://github.com/gxli/SA-JEPA.

</details>


### [537] [Simplifying Flow Matching Transformations with Low-Rank Mixture Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29724)
*Liam A. Kruse,Houjun Liu,Alexandros E. Tzikas,Mansur M. Arief,Mykel J. Kochenderfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using mixtures of probabilistic principal component analyzers (MPPCA) as the latent distribution in normalizing flows, replacing the standard normal density to reduce topological mismatch and improve training efficiency and generative performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard normal latent density in normalizing flows causes topological mismatch with complex data distributions, forcing unnecessarily complex flow transformations that slow training and degrade performance.

Method: Replace the typical standard normal latent prior with an MPPCA distribution, which can be efficiently fit via expectation-maximization and more closely aligns with the data distribution in terms of KL divergence.

Result: Consistent improvements in training efficiency and generation quality on both tabular and image datasets compared to normalizing flows with standard normal latent priors.

Conclusion: MPPCA-based latent distributions are a practical and effective alternative to standard normal priors for normalizing flows, enabling faster convergence and better generative performance across diverse data modalities.

Abstract: Normalizing flows are powerful generative models that learn an invertible mapping between complex data distributions and simple latent distributions, typically a standard normal density. However, this choice of latent density can impose unnecessary complexity on the learned flow transformation due to the topological mismatch between the latent and data densities, leading to slower training and suboptimal performance. In this work, we propose using mixtures of probabilistic principal component analyzers (MPPCA) as the latent density for normalizing flows. We simplify the learned flow transformation by learning a latent distribution that more closely aligns with the data distribution in terms of KL divergence, thus enabling faster convergence and improved generative performance. Critically, MPPCA models can be fit quickly and cheaply using the expectation-maximization algorithm, making them a practical choice for initializing latent distributions even in high-dimensional generative tasks. We validate our method on both tabular and image datasets, demonstrating consistent gains in training efficiency and generation quality compared to baselines.

</details>


### [538] [Optimizing Nursing Care Taxi Dispatch Leveraging Integer Linear Programming Solvers and Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29725)
*Riku Nakao,Akihito Hiromori,Hamada Rizk,Hirozumi Yamaguchi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a supervised Transformer-based approach for the Nursing Care Taxi Dispatch problem, a complex variant of VRP with multiple constraints (wheelchair use, compatibility, time windows, vehicle limits). The method uses ILP-generated training data and post-processing to ensure feasibility, achieving up to 8% reduction in operating time while maintaining low violation rates compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural VRP methods handle only simple constraints, but real-world dispatch problems like nursing care taxi involve multiple complex constraints (wheelchair use, user compatibility, time windows, vehicle limits) with fewer feasible destinations, making it harder to find valid solutions with limited vehicles. There is a need for a method that balances violation rate, computational efficiency, and solution quality.

Method: A supervised learning approach using Transformer architecture. High-quality solutions are first obtained via an integer linear programming (ILP) solver on given inputs, then used to train the Transformer model. Post-processing is applied to the generated paths to ensure all constraints are satisfied.

Result: The proposed method achieved balanced performance across operating time, execution time, and constraint violation rate. It reduced operating time for all problem sizes and regions while keeping violations minimal, with up to 8% reduction for instances with fewer than 30 users compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The supervised Transformer approach with ILP-based training data and constraint-enforcing post-processing successfully balances solution quality, computational efficiency, and feasibility for the complex Nursing Care Taxi Dispatch problem, outperforming both ILP and existing ML-based methods.

Abstract: In this paper, we formulate a new vehicle dispatch optimization problem, called Nursing Care Taxi Dispatch, as a variant of the Vehicle Routing Problem, considering constraints related to wheelchair use, user compatibility, pick-up and drop-off times, and vehicle limitations. Previous neural-based methods for Vehicle Routing Problems have typically addressed a few simple constraints, while our new problem involves multiple complex constraints, resulting in having fewer destinations to select. This complexity makes it more difficult to obtain solutions that allow all nodes to be visited with a limited number of vehicles. To balance low violation rate, computational efficiency, and solution quality, we propose a supervised machine learning approach based on the Transformer architecture. We first obtain a set of high-quality solutions using an integer linear programming solver for given inputs and then train our learning model through supervised learning. Additionally, we introduce the post-processing of the paths generated by the learning model, ensuring that all constraints are satisfied. We compared each instance's objective function value (operating time), execution time, and constraint violation rate across different methods: our proposed method and some existing methods including integer linear programming and machine learning-based methods, using real-world facility data. Our method successfully produced balanced solutions regarding operating time, execution time, and constraint violation rate. Notably, we observed a decrease in the operating time for all problem sizes and regions, while keeping constraint violations to a minimum compared to existing methods. Especially, the decrease reached up to 8% for problem sizes with fewer than 30 users.

</details>


### [539] [PS-PPO: Prefix-Sampling PPO for Critic-Free RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29758)
*Doo Hwan Hwang,Kee-Eung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PS-PPO reduces training compute and memory in critic-free RLHF by sampling a cutoff timestep and backpropagating only through prefixes, using importance-weighting to maintain unbiased gradients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing critic-free RLHF methods propagate trajectory-level signals uniformly across all tokens, causing high optimization costs for long reasoning traces, even when intermediate prefixes are largely informative of the final outcome.

Method: PS-PPO introduces a prompt-conditioned cutoff distribution, samples a cutoff timestep per trajectory, backpropagates only through the sampled prefix, and applies importance-weighting correction for unbiased gradient estimation.

Result: PS-PPO achieves large reductions in training compute and peak GPU memory while maintaining accuracy comparable to strong critic-free baselines on mathematical reasoning and RLHF benchmarks.

Conclusion: Exploiting temporal redundancy via prefix sampling is an effective and efficient strategy for critic-free RLHF, addressing the computational bottleneck of full-trajectory updates.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) for Large Language Models increasingly relies on critic-free methods as a practical alternative to actor--critic training. Despite their simplicity, existing critic-free approaches propagate a trajectory-level learning signal uniformly across all tokens in a trajectory. This requires full-trajectory policy updates for every rollout, leading to substantial optimization cost for long reasoning traces, even though intermediate prefixes often contain enough information to largely determine the final outcome. We propose Prefix-Sampling Proximal Policy Optimization (PS-PPO), a compute-efficient critic-free method for RLHF that exploits this temporal redundancy. PS-PPO introduces a prompt-conditioned cutoff distribution and samples a cutoff timestep for each trajectory. During the update pass, PS-PPO backpropagates only through the sampled prefix of each trajectory and applies an importance-weighting correction so that the resulting truncated gradient estimator remains unbiased with respect to the full-trajectory objective. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and RLHF benchmarks show that PS-PPO achieves large reductions in training compute and peak GPU memory, while maintaining accuracy comparable to strong critic-free baselines.

</details>


### [540] [GLIP: Graph and LLM Joint Pretraining for Graph-Level Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29773)
*Haoxin Sun,Yiqing Lin,Yajun Huang,Chenhui Dong,Mingjun Li,Zhongzhi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLIP is a Graph-LLM joint pretraining framework for graph-level tasks that combines graph augmentation, multi-token selection, a diffusion-based projector, and a joint objective integrating LLM semantic judgments with contrastive alignment loss, outperforming SOTA methods on graph-level classification and reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing works focus on node-level and edge-level tasks, leaving graph-level tasks underexplored. Additionally, graph pretraining methods are designed solely for GNNs without involving LLMs, limiting their ability to capture complex structural and feature information at the graph level.

Method: GLIP performs (1) graph augmentation to construct positive/negative pairs, (2) multi-token selection to identify informative patches, (3) a diffusion-based projector to enrich patches with contextual information, and (4) a joint objective combining LLM semantic judgments with contrastive alignment loss for consistent supervision at semantic and structural levels.

Result: Extensive experiments show GLIP outperforms state-of-the-art methods on graph-level classification and reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: GLIP effectively addresses the gap in graph-level pretraining by jointly leveraging GNNs and LLMs, achieving superior performance with limited labeled data.

Abstract: Graphs are widely used to model relational systems, with applications in domains such as social networks, finance, and biomedicine. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have become a mainstream approach for learning graph representations. With the rise of large language models (LLMs), recent studies have attempted to combine GNNs with LLMs. However, most existing works concentrate on node-level and edge-level tasks, while graph-level tasks, which require capturing more complex structural and feature information, remain relatively underexplored. Moreover, graph pretraining is a widely adopted strategy to alleviate the challenge of label scarcity. Most existing approaches are designed solely for GNNs such as GraphCL, leaving LLMs uninvolved in the process. To address these limitations, we propose GLIP, a Graph-LLM JoInt Pretraining framework for graph-level tasks. GLIP first performs graph augmentation to construct positive and negative pairs and introduces a multi-token selection strategy to identify patches informative in both structure and features. It further leverages a diffusion-based projector to enrich them with contextual information, enabling GLIP to capture signals from both global and local perspectives. Finally, GLIP employs a joint objective that integrates the LLM's semantic judgments with a contrastive alignment loss, ensuring consistent supervision at both the semantic and structural levels. After pretraining, GLIP is fine-tuned with limited labeled data for downstream tasks, and extensive experiments show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods on graph-level classification and reasoning tasks. Our source code is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GLIP.

</details>


### [541] [Towards Generalizable and Evidential Nuclear Magnetic Resonance-Based Molecular Structure Elucidation via Large Language Model Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29776)
*Zheng Fang,Chen Yang,Yusen Tan,Yunpeng Zhao,Fanjie Xu,Hongxin Xiang,Hanyu Sun,Hanyu Gao,Xiaojian Wang,Wenjie Du,Yuqiang Li,Jun Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NMRAgent is an LLM-powered evidential reasoning agent that interprets NMR spectra for molecular structure elucidation, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by combining spectral analysis tools with chemical knowledge graphs to provide transparent, atom-level interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current NMR-based molecular structure elucidation faces a trade-off: database retrieval cannot identify novel scaffolds, and de novo AI models operate as black boxes lacking atom-level interpretability required for rigorous scientific validation.

Method: NMRAgent uses large language models to integrate spectral analysis tools with chemical knowledge graphs, mimicking expert deductive reasoning: it takes experimental NMR spectra and molecular formula as input, plans elucidation, proposes candidate structures, verifies peak-atom consistency, and refines misaligned substructures through formula-aware fragment optimization.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods with 46.5% improvement in top-1 accuracy and 0.502 improvement in Tanimoto similarity on a scaffold-split benchmark; successfully elucidated two previously unknown natural products and corrected structural misassignments in established literature.

Conclusion: NMRAgent establishes a new paradigm for interpretable AI in analytical chemistry by combining high-accuracy prediction with transparent, evidence-based reasoning.

Abstract: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy is the gold standard for molecular structure elucidation, yet interpreting complex spectra for unknown molecules remains a bottleneck reliant on human expertise. While artificial intelligence has advanced this field, current methods face a critical trade-off: database retrieval cannot identify novel scaffolds, while de novo molecular structure elucidation models operate as black boxes, lacking the atom-level interpretability required for rigorous scientific validation. Here, we present NMRAgent, an evidential reasoning agent powered by large language models (LLMs) that bridges this gap by integrating specialized spectral analysis tools with chemical knowledge graphs. Unlike previous approaches, NMRAgent mimics the deductive reasoning of human experts: it takes experimental NMR spectra and molecular formula as input, plans the elucidation process, proposes candidate structures, verifies peak-atom consistency, and refines misaligned substructure through formula-aware fragment optimization. Enabled by its evidential reasoning, NMRAgent outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving top-1 accuracy by 46.5% and Tanimoto similarity by 0.502 on a scaffold-split benchmark with novel scaffolds in the test set. Besides, we demonstrate the agent's practical utility by elucidating the structures of two previously unknown natural products isolated from Hydrangea davidii and Vitex trifolia, and by correcting structural misassignments in established literature. By combining high-accuracy prediction with transparent and evidence-based reasoning, NMRAgent establishes a new paradigm for interpretable AI in analytical chemistry.

</details>


### [542] [MemLeak: Diagnosing Information Leaks in Multimodal Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29788)
*Kuan Wang,Chao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that deleting text facts from multimodal AI memory systems fails because Visual Language Models can recover the facts from retained images via implicit visual cues. The authors introduce Information Provenance Graph (IPG) taxonomy and MemLeak benchmark, showing direct probing yields <1% recovery but retained images enable 12.0% recovery (47% not text-recoverable). Content-aware semantic deletion reduces residual to 2.0%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory systems for multimodal AI agents delete text entries and claim success, but the authors find that facts remain recoverable from retained user images, including images tagged to different facts, due to VLMs using implicit visual cues at inference time. This reveals a critical gap in evaluating deletion completeness in multimodal systems.

Method: The authors introduce the Information Provenance Graph (IPG), a taxonomy classifying memory representations by deletion affordance. They build the MemLeak benchmark to measure information leakage across a deletion cascade, including direct probing, retained correlated text, and retained images. They also propose content-aware semantic deletion to reduce residual leakage.

Result: Direct probing of deletion-capable systems yields <1% recovery. Retained correlated text enables 18.3% recovery, and retained images enable 12.0% recovery (0.0% blind baseline, 0.3% FPR). 47% of image leaks are not text-recoverable. Content-aware semantic deletion reduces image residual to 2.0%. The residual appears across multiple VLMs, a production memory system, and real photographs. Human validation shows high judge reliability (kappa = 0.88).

Conclusion: Multimodal memory deletion is fundamentally incomplete due to implicit visual cues in retained images. Content-aware semantic deletion mitigates but does not eliminate the problem. The residual leakage is consistent across models and systems, highlighting a systematic vulnerability in multimodal AI memory management.

Abstract: When a multimodal AI agent is asked to forget a fact, current memory systems usually delete the text entry and report success. We find that the fact can remain recoverable from retained user images, including images tagged to entirely different facts, because VLMs use implicit visual cues at inference time. We introduce the Information Provenance Graph (IPG), a taxonomy that classifies memory representations by deletion affordance. The IPG reveals that deletion fails through multiple channels. Our benchmark, MemLeak, measures this across a deletion cascade: direct probing of deletion-capable systems yields <1%, but retained correlated text enables 18.3% recovery, and retained images enable 12.0% recovery (0.0% blind baseline, 0.3% FPR) -- with 47% of image leaks not text-recoverable. Content-aware semantic deletion reduces the image residual to 2.0%. The residual appears across multiple VLMs, a production memory system, and real Unsplash-licensed photographs. Dual-annotator human validation (kappa = 0.88) confirms judge reliability.

</details>


### [543] [What Drives the Inlier-Memorization Effect? A Theory of Outlier Detection via Early Training Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29791)
*Kunwoong Kim,Dongha Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a theoretical analysis of the inlier-memorization (IM) effect in unsupervised outlier detection, showing that autoencoders memorize inliers before outliers under mild assumptions, and derives practical guidelines (data preprocessing and parameter initialization) that achieve state-of-the-art results on ADBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite empirical success of the inlier-memorization (IM) effect for outlier detection, its theoretical understanding remains limited. The authors aim to bridge this gap by formally characterizing when, why, and how the IM effect emerges.

Method: The authors conduct a theoretical study of the IM effect using a simple autoencoder. They derive conditions under which inliers are memorized before outliers, characterize the effect's strength and persistence as functions of data distribution and parameter initialization, and propose practical guidelines (data preprocessing and initialization schemes) based on these insights.

Result: The theoretical analysis shows that under mild assumptions, the autoencoder successfully memorizes inliers while failing to memorize outliers during early training. The proposed practical guidelines achieve state-of-the-art performance on the ADBench datasets.

Conclusion: This work provides a theoretical foundation for the IM effect in unsupervised outlier detection and offers actionable directions (data preprocessing and parameter initialization) to improve IM-based methods.

Abstract: Outlier detection (OD) aims to identify anomalous instances by learning the underlying structure of normal data (inliers), and is particularly challenging in fully unsupervised settings where no information about anomalies is available during training. Recent advances have leveraged the inlier-memorization (IM) effect, a phenomenon in which deep models memorize inlier patterns earlier than those of outliers, as a powerful signal for distinguishing outliers. However, despite its empirical success, the theoretical understanding of the IM effect remains limited. In this work, we present a theoretical study of the IM effect. Focusing on a simple autoencoder, we show that, under mild assumptions, the model can successfully memorize inliers while failing to memorize outliers during certain stages of early training. In particular, we characterize not only the emergence of the IM effect, but also its strength and persistence, and analyze how these properties depend on the data distribution and parameter initialization. In addition, building on these insights, we derive simple yet practical guidelines for enhancing the IM effect, including data preprocessing and parameter initialization schemes, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the ADBench datasets. Our findings provide a theoretical foundation for the IM effect and offer actionable directions for improving IM-based outlier detection methods.

</details>


### [544] [Accelerating Q-learning through Efficient Value-Sharing across Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29806)
*Prabhat Nagarajan,Brett Daley,Martha White,Marlos C. Machado*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a mean-expansion layer that accelerates action-value learning in RL by sharing value structure across actions within a state and transforming the learning problem into a lower-norm representation, improving performance on Atari games while reducing value overestimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Action-value learning in RL is slow because values must be updated from near-zero initialization to potentially far-from-zero true values, and each state-action pair is updated independently without sharing value structure across actions within a state.

Method: The mean-expansion layer is a parameter-free addition to Q-network architectures that shares values across actions within a state and transforms action-value learning into learning a lower-norm representation of them.

Result: Applied to deep Q-networks and implicit quantile networks, the method improves aggregate performance across 57 Atari games, increases action gaps, and dramatically reduces value overestimation.

Conclusion: The mean-expansion layer effectively accelerates action-value learning by addressing initialization bias and enabling value sharing across actions, leading to better performance and reduced overestimation in deep RL.

Abstract: Action-values are foundational to many control algorithms such as Q-learning. Therefore learning action-values efficiently is central to reinforcement learning (RL). However, learning them can be slow, requiring many updates to move values from their initialization, typically near zero, to their true values, which may be far from zero. Moreover, action-value learning algorithms typically update each state-action pair independently, without learning shared value structure across actions within a state. In this paper, we address these inefficiencies by introducing the mean-expansion layer, which accelerates action-value learning by sharing values across actions within a state and by changing the problem from directly learning potentially large action-values to learning a lower-norm representation of them. In deep RL, this layer can be applied as a parameter-free addition to Q-network architectures without altering the underlying algorithm. Applied to deep Q-networks and implicit quantile networks, it improves aggregate performance across 57 Atari games while increasing action gaps and dramatically reducing value overestimation.

</details>


### [545] [Dual-Flow Reinforcement Learning with State-Aware Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29820)
*Qijun Li,Zheng Fu,Qi Song,Yifei He,Weitao Zhou,Kun Jiang,Diange Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dual-Flow RL uses conditional flow matching to jointly model return distributions and multimodal policies, with an Entropy-Covariance Exploration Regulator, achieving SOTA on continuous-control RL tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unimodal Gaussian value estimates are biased and lack expressiveness; generative policies collapse to few modes and underexplore high-value regions. A unified framework for reliable value estimation and multimodal exploration is needed.

Method: Proposes Dual-Flow RL, an actor-critic framework using conditional flow matching (CFM) to jointly model continuous return distributions and multimodal policy distributions. Includes an Entropy-Covariance Exploration Regulator (ECER) for state-aware exploration regulation via policy entropy and action-uncertainty covariance.

Result: Outperforms prior diffusion-based and flow-based methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance on most tasks in DeepMind Control Suite and Humanoid-Bench.

Conclusion: Dual-Flow RL effectively addresses biased value estimation and multimodal exploration collapse, providing a unified solution for complex continuous-control RL.

Abstract: In complex continuous-control reinforcement learning tasks, multimodal optimal actions often coincide with uncertain, multimodal return distributions, making reliable value estimation and multimodal exploration challenging. Existing value estimation methods using unimodal Gaussians restrict expressiveness and yield biased estimates. Recent generative policies can represent multimodal actions but often collapse to a few modes and under-explore high-value areas of the action space. Motivated by these challenges, we propose Dual-Flow RL, a unified actor-critic framework that jointly models a continuous return distribution and a multimodal policy distribution using conditional flow matching (CFM). This design supports reliable value estimation and sustained multimodal exploration. To further enhance exploration, we introduce an Entropy-Covariance Exploration Regulator (ECER) that enables state-aware exploration regulation leveraging policy entropy and action-uncertainty covariance. Experiments on DeepMind Control Suite and Humanoid-Bench show that Dual-Flow RL achieves state-of-the-art performance on most tasks, significantly outperforming prior diffusion-based and flow-based methods.

</details>


### [546] [The Forgetting-Retention Dilemma: Certified Unlearning Theory in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29832)
*Yiting Hu,Lingjie Duan,Qian Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes the first theoretical foundation connecting continual learning and machine unlearning, analyzing the trade-off between preserving historical knowledge and targeted forgetting, and proposing a hybrid unlearning strategy that balances effectiveness with storage efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current certified unlearning algorithms fail to account for the complex, cumulative model evolution inherent to continual learning frameworks, creating a gap in privacy preservation for sequentially updated models.

Method: The authors formulate the unlearning objective as minimization of post-unlearning excess risk, decomposing it into CL excess risk and unlearning loss. They establish an upper bound for CL excess risk in non-convex models, then adapt gradient-based and Hessian-based certified unlearning approaches to CL, and propose a hybrid strategy.

Result: The gradient-based approach is less effective than Hessian-based in minimizing unlearning loss but offers nearly zero storage overhead. Experiments validate the theoretical findings, confirming the trade-off between storage cost and unlearning effectiveness.

Conclusion: A hybrid strategy can reduce storage costs while maintaining post-unlearning performance, offering a practical solution for privacy-preserving continual learning.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to eliminate the influence of specific data from trained models to safeguard privacy. However, this presents a significant challenge in the context of continual learning (CL), where models update sequentially on dynamic datasets. A major limitation is that current certified unlearning algorithms fail to account for the complex, cumulative model evolution inherent to CL framework. In this work, we establish the first theoretical foundation bridging CL and machine unlearning. We formulate the CL's unlearning objective as the minimization of post-unlearning excess risk, which decomposes into CL excess risk and unlearning loss, characterizing the fundamental trade-off between preserving historical knowledge and targeted forgetting. Under mild assumptions, we first establish an upper bound for the CL excess risk in non-convex models. We then adapt two certified unlearning approaches, gradient-based and Hessian-based, to the CL framework. Our analysis reveals that while the gradient-based approach is less effective than the Hessian-based method in minimizing unlearning loss, it offers the distinct advantage of nearly zero storage overhead for enabling unlearning. This insight motivates a hybrid strategy that reduces storage costs while maintaining post-unlearning performance. Experimental results further validate our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [547] [Theory of Continual Learning Against Data Poisoning Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29841)
*Yiting Hu,Lingjie Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a theoretical framework for analyzing attacks and defenses in regularization-based continual learning, framed as an online zero-sum game. It establishes fundamental limits (no defense succeeds against linear-proportion poisoning with unbounded noise), identifies defensible scenarios (infrequent attacks or bounded noise), and proposes corresponding defenses (task-to-task verification for infrequent attacks, sensitivity minimization for bounded noise).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning is increasingly deployed in critical domains (LLMs, image recognition) but remains highly vulnerable to data poisoning that causes learning divergence or excess risk, yet a principled theoretical foundation for understanding attack and defense in CL is lacking.

Method: The authors frame the adversary-defender interaction as an online zero-sum game. They analyze fundamental limits by considering an adversary that poisons a linear proportion of tasks with unbounded noise/pattern shifts. They then study two defensible regimes: (1) infrequent attacks, where a task-to-task verification mechanism detects poisoning and reduces cumulative bias; (2) bounded noise per attack, where a robust defense minimizes model sensitivity to poisoned features to accelerate convergence.

Result: (1) A fundamental performance limit: no defense can succeed if an adversary poisons a linear proportion of tasks with unbounded noise/pattern shifts. (2) For infrequent attacks, the proposed task-to-task verification mechanism detects poisoning and reduces cumulative bias. (3) For bounded noise per attack, the robust defense provably accelerates convergence rate. (4) Extensive experiments on realistic tasks validate the theoretical results.

Conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for understanding strategic attacks and defenses in regularization-based continual learning, characterizing both fundamental impossibility results and achievable defenses under specific threat models.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL), where a model is trained on a sequence of data tasks, is increasingly being adopted across key fields such as large language models and image recognition, yet it remains highly vulnerable to data poisoning that triggers learning divergence or severe excess risk. Despite these threats, a principled theoretical foundation in CL for understanding attack and defense remains lacking. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework to analyze strategic attacks and defenses in regularization-based CL, a cornerstone of recent CL theory. By framing the adversary-defender interaction as an online zero-sum game, we first establish a fundamental performance limit: no defense succeeds when an adversary poisons a linear proportion of tasks by injecting unbounded noise or pattern shifts in regularization-based CL. We then analyze two possibly defensible scenarios: infrequent attacks and bounded noise per attack. For the former regime, we propose a task-to-task verification mechanism to detect data poisoning and reduce cumulative bias for learning convergence. For the latter regime, we derive a robust defense that minimizes the model's sensitivity to poisoned features, provably accelerating the convergence rate. Extensive experiments on realistic tasks further validate our theoretical results.

</details>


### [548] [Comparing Chatbot Performance Enhanced with Persistent Homology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29857)
*Nithisha Raghavaraju,Barbara Giunti,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes enhancing chatbot training datasets with persistent homology vectorizations to improve model performance without expanding datasets, particularly for privacy-sensitive domains like mental health where local training is required.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chatbots trained on large datasets are effective but face challenges in privacy-sensitive domains (e.g., mental health) where patient data cannot be shared on servers and datasets are limited. The authors aim to improve chatbot performance locally without increasing dataset size.

Method: The authors enhance input datasets using persistent homology (PH) vectorizations computed from raw datasets. They then compare multiple chatbot models across several metrics, with and without PH enhancement.

Result: Experiments show that while PH enhancement is sometimes not particularly beneficial, it can bring remarkable advantages at virtually no additional cost in certain cases.

Conclusion: Persistent homology vectorization is a low-cost method that can significantly improve chatbot performance in some scenarios without requiring larger datasets or compromising patient confidentiality.

Abstract: Chatbots have become increasingly prevalent across various domains, offering automated assistance in many areas, especially mental health support. The training is done using extremely large datasets, which are sometimes not available in very specific domains. Moreover, it would sometimes be ideal to train the chatbot with personal information about the patients, which, of course, cannot be done on shared servers since it would violate patient confidentiality. Hence, being able to improve the performance of a chatbot, possibly trained locally and on a restricted dataset, without having to increase the dataset itself, would be extremely beneficial. In this work, we will enhance the input datasets using persistent homology (PH) vectorizations computed from the raw datasets themselves. Then we will compare, across several metrics, the performance of multiple chatbot models with or without the PH enhancement. Our experiments suggest that, while at times the PH enhancement is not particularly beneficial, it sometimes brings remarkable advantages for virtually no cost.

</details>


### [549] [RoAd-RL: A Unified Library and Benchmark for Robust Adversarial Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29867)
*Adithya Mohan,Daniel Kriegl,Torsten Schön*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RoAd-RL is an open-source benchmarking framework for adversarial reinforcement learning that provides unified abstractions, reproducible evaluation pipelines, and integration with Stable-Baselines3 and Gymnasium. Evaluation of DQN, PPO, and SAC agents under 192 attack-defense configurations shows substantial robustness variations and reveals that some defenses are more harmful than attacks, while temporal smoothing is consistently effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial RL research suffers from fragmented implementations, inconsistent evaluation protocols, and poor reproducibility, necessitating a standardized benchmarking framework.

Method: RoAd-RL provides unified abstractions for policies, attacks, defenses, and robustness metrics with reproducible evaluation pipelines and seamless integration with Stable-Baselines3 and Gymnasium. DQN, PPO, and SAC agents were evaluated in LunarLander and Highway-v0 under 192 attack-defense configurations.

Result: Substantial variations in robustness were observed across environments; some commonly used defenses are more detrimental than the attacks they aim to mitigate; temporal smoothing consistently achieves strong performance.

Conclusion: RoAd-RL establishes a standardized benchmark for adversarial RL research, promoting reproducibility and fair comparisons, and is publicly available.

Abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has achieved significant success in robotics and autonomous systems, yet remains vulnerable to adversarial perturbations that can severely degrade performance. Research in adversarial reinforcement learning is often limited by fragmented implementations, inconsistent evaluation protocols, and poor reproducibility. To address these challenges, we present \textbf{RoAd-RL}, an open-source benchmarking framework that provides unified abstractions for policies, attacks, defenses, and robustness metrics, together with reproducible evaluation pipelines and seamless integration with Stable-Baselines3 and Gymnasium.
  We evaluate DQN, PPO, and SAC agents in LunarLander and Highway-v0 under 192 attack-defense configurations. Results reveal substantial variations in robustness across environments and show that some commonly used defenses can be more detrimental than the attacks they aim to mitigate, while temporal smoothing consistently achieves strong performance. RoAd-RL establishes a standardized benchmark for adversarial reinforcement learning research and is publicly available at https://pypi.org/project/road-rl.

</details>


### [550] [Decision-Value Attribution in Predict-then-Optimize Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29878)
*Konstantinos Ziliaskopoulos,Alexander Vinel,Alice E. Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DVA is a Shapley-based framework that attributes decision value in predict-then-optimize pipelines, offering three variants (InfoDVA, DesignDVA, DVI) and two evaluation modes (pre-DVA, post-DVA) to diagnose alignment between model predictions and realized operational performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard explanation methods explain forecasts rather than the decisions they induce, which is problematic because large forecast changes may leave the optimizer's action unchanged while small changes can alter decisions and realized value.

Method: Proposes Decision Value Attribution (DVA), a Shapley-based framework defining cooperative games whose payoff is downstream decision value. Includes three variants: InfoDVA (feature attribution), DesignDVA (operational configuration attribution), and Decision-Value Interactions (joint information-design attribution). Also distinguishes post-DVA (realized outcomes) from pre-DVA (model's full prediction).

Result: Case studies in electricity storage arbitrage and emergency medical service coverage show predictive explanations are poor proxies for operational value, DVA guides targeted interventions, and optimization configurations determine when predictive information is decision-relevant.

Conclusion: Attributions are expressed in operational objective units, decompose gain/loss relative to baseline, and serve as decision-level diagnostics for belief alignment between model predictions and realized performance.

Abstract: Predictive models are increasingly embedded in operational decision-making, yet standard explanation methods typically explain forecasts rather than the decisions those forecasts induce. This distinction is important in predict-then-optimize systems: large forecast changes may leave the optimizer's action unchanged, while small changes can alter the selected decision and its realized value. We propose Decision Value Attribution (DVA), a Shapley-based framework for attributing the value of a fixed prediction--optimization pipeline. The framework defines cooperative games whose payoff is the downstream decision value, allowing the players to be information sources, optimization or design parameters, or both. We present three variants: InfoDVA attributes value to features, DesignDVA attributes value to operational configurations, and Decision-Value Interactions (DVI) quantifies how information and design jointly create value. We further distinguish post-DVA, which evaluates decisions using realized outcomes, from pre-DVA, which evaluates decisions under the model's full prediction. This separation turns attribution into a decision-level diagnostic of whether the model's operational beliefs align with realized performance. The resulting attributions are expressed in the units of the operational objective and decompose the gain or loss relative to a baseline. Case studies in electricity storage arbitrage and emergency medical service coverage show that predictive explanations can be poor proxies for operational value, that DVA can guide targeted information-control interventions, and that optimization configurations determine when predictive information is decision-relevant.

</details>


### [551] [Same Concept, Different Directions: Cross-Modal Feature Heterogeneity in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29888)
*Chungpa Lee,Jihoon Kwon,Kyle Min,Jy-yong Sohn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies cross-modal feature heterogeneity in vision-language joint embeddings—where the same concept activates different directions in image vs. text modalities—and proposes modality-specific sparse autoencoders with post-hoc alignment to address this issue, improving reconstruction fidelity, cross-modal retrieval, and concept steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models entangle multiple semantic features in joint embeddings, limiting interpretability and controllability. Sparse autoencoders have been used to decompose these embeddings into monosemantic features but rely on an untested assumption that semantically corresponding features share the same directions across modalities. The authors challenge this assumption.

Method: (1) Identify cross-modal feature heterogeneity: discrepancies in feature directions for the same concept across image and text modalities. (2) Demonstrate that this heterogeneity drives the modality split, where a shared concept activates different latents depending on modality. (3) Propose training modality-specific sparse autoencoders to preserve each modality's feature geometry, then align corresponding features post hoc.

Result: The proposed approach improves reconstruction fidelity and enhances performance in cross-modal retrieval and concept steering tasks.

Conclusion: Cross-modal feature heterogeneity is a key driver of modality splits in joint embeddings. Aligning latent activations alone is insufficient; preserving modality-specific feature geometry and post-hoc alignment is necessary. Modality-specific sparse autoencoders with post-hoc alignment outperform shared approaches.

Abstract: Vision-language models map images and text into a joint embedding space. However, these embeddings often entangle multiple semantic features, which limits their interpretability and controllability. While sparse autoencoders have emerged as a useful tool for decomposing these embeddings into monosemantic features, their application to joint embedding spaces has largely relied on an implicit, untested assumption that semantically corresponding features share the same directions across modalities. In this paper, we challenge this assumption by identifying discrepancies in feature directions for the same concept across image and text modalities, a phenomenon we term cross-modal feature heterogeneity. We demonstrate that this heterogeneity is a key driver of the modality split, where a shared concept activates different latents depending on the modality. This finding further reveals why aligning latent activations alone is insufficient to resolve the underlying feature mismatch. Motivated by this observation, we propose an approach that trains modality-specific sparse autoencoders to preserve each modality's feature geometry, and then aligns corresponding features post hoc. Our method improves reconstruction fidelity and enhances performance in cross-modal retrieval and concept steering.

</details>


### [552] [Golden Hour Divide: Trauma Care Accessibility and Resource Vulnerability in Sri Lanka](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29889)
*Sonath Kirindage,Vihanga Nimsara,Sakindu Rajapaksa,Kavyanga Hathurusinghe,Lahiru Dilshan,Subavarshana Arumugam,Nathali Athukorala,Sandareka Wickramanayake,Nisansa de Silva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study evaluates emergency healthcare resilience across Sri Lanka's 25 districts using H3 hexagonal modeling and K-Means clustering, identifying four district archetypes and revealing that specialist scarcity (not bed capacity) drives systemic pressure, with Northern and Eastern provinces facing severe deficits where the Golden Hour is unachievable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emergency infrastructure is unevenly distributed in Sri Lanka, and the transition from pre-hospital care to definitive care remains a critical bottleneck; the study aims to quantify the gap between clinical demand and specialized resource availability.

Method: Used national epidemiological data and terrain-aware H3 hexagonal modeling to analyze accessibility for seven critical conditions across spatial gaps, clinical need-gaps, lethality, coverage, and resource availability; applied unsupervised K-Means clustering to categorize districts into four policy-actionable archetypes.

Result: Severe service deficits in Northern and Eastern provinces with spatial gaps exceeding 70%, rendering the Golden Hour unachievable; specialist scarcity drives systemic pressure more than bed capacity; underserved regions function as institutional mirages; improving accessibility by 25% in high-priority clusters would reduce the national need-gap by 9.65%.

Conclusion: Strategic redistribution of specialists is needed for healthcare equity; the archetype framework provides a roadmap for policy action to address structural exclusion, institutional mirages, and operational capacity strain.

Abstract: Timely intensive care dictates survival, yet emergency infrastructure remains unevenly distributed across Sri Lanka. While pre-hospital services have expanded, the transition to definitive care remains a critical bottleneck. This study evaluates national emergency resilience by quantifying the gap between clinical demand and the availability of specialized resources across all 25 districts. Using the latest national epidemiological data and terrain-aware H3 hexagonal modeling, we analyzed accessibility for seven critical conditions based on spatial gaps, clinical need-gaps, lethality, coverage, and resource availability. Based on these metrics, unsupervised K-Means clustering was applied to categorize districts into four policy-actionable archetypes: Critical Structural Exclusion, Institutional Mirages, Operational Capacity Strain, and High-Resilience Benchmarks. Our study suggests that severe service deficits exist in the Northern and Eastern provinces, where spatial gaps exceed 70%, rendering the Golden Hour operationally impossible. Notably, specialist scarcity drives systemic pressure more than bed capacity; underserved regions effectively function as institutional mirages. This study suggests that improving accessibility by 25% in high-priority clusters would reduce the national need-gap by 9.65%, providing a roadmap for the strategic redistribution of specialists to ensure healthcare equity.

</details>


### [553] [CW-B: Class Weighted Boosting Framework for Imbalance Resilient Multi Class Cardiac Phenotyping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29907)
*Sijia Li,Xiaoyu Tan,Chen Zhan,Yuanji Ma,Haoyu Wang,Xihe Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CW-B is a class-weighted XGBoost pipeline for five-class cardiac discharge phenotyping that handles real-world class imbalance and missingness through fold-specific balanced weighting, missingness-indicator augmentation, and classwise error auditing, achieving superior performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world cardiac discharge records are often incomplete and class-imbalanced, leading to increased risk of missed high-risk phenotypes that need proper post-discharge treatment and follow-up.

Method: CW-B pipeline uses XGBoost with fold-specific class-balanced instance weighting, missingness-indicator augmentation, and classwise error auditing for five-class cardiac discharge phenotyping.

Result: In five-fold stratified cross-validation, CW-B achieves the best Accuracy, Macro-F1, Balanced Accuracy, and Prioritized F1 among tree-based, ensemble, and neural baselines.

Conclusion: CW-B provides a practical, deployment-oriented approach for more reliable cardiac discharge phenotyping in real-world clinical settings.

Abstract: Cardiac discharge phenotyping informs post-discharge treatment and follow-up, but real-world records are often incomplete and class-imbalanced, increasing the risk of missed high-risk phenotypes. We propose CW-B, a clinical risk-aligned class-weighted XGBoost pipeline for five-class cardiac discharge phenotyping under real-world class imbalance and missingness. CW-B combines fold-specific class-balanced instance weighting, missingness-indicator augmentation, and classwise error auditing to improve recognition of clinically prioritized phenotypes while preserving interpretable and auditable decision logic. In five-fold stratified cross-validation, CW-B achieves the best Accuracy, Macro-F1, Balanced Accuracy, and Prioritized F1 among tree-based, ensemble, and neural baselines. Overall, CW-B provides a practical and deployment-oriented approach for more reliable cardiac discharge phenotyping in real-world clinical settings.

</details>


### [554] [EVAF: A Test-Retest Protocol for Selective Parametric Consolidation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29916)
*Haoliang Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVAF introduces a gated LoRA consolidation mechanism (Echo-Valence Attractor Field) that selectively internalizes high-valence, high-surprise experiences into model parameters while keeping factual memory retrieval-accessible, demonstrating that parametric consolidation and retrieval are distinct operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-running language agents lack mechanisms for selectively consolidating experiences into model behavior after working context is lost; pure retrieval systems can reinsert text but fail to show that an experience has been internalized into the model's own parametric behavior.

Method: EVAF (Echo-Valence Attractor Field) uses gated LoRA consolidation for selective parametric updates, complemented by a routed memory path for retrieval-accessible factual memory. A test-retest protocol measures selective parametric consolidation under controlled interference across GPT-2 and TinyLlama.

Result: EVAF preferentially consolidates high-valence, high-surprise experiences, shows stronger post-interference behavioral persistence than frozen, retrieval-only, and ungated continual-update baselines, while keeping parameter drift and cross-persona contamination low.

Conclusion: Retrieving a fact and internalizing an experience are distinct computational operations; memory access and memory depth should be separated in long-running language agents.

Abstract: Long-running language agents need mechanisms for deciding which experiences should persist after the working context is gone. Retrieval systems can reinsert past text, but they do not by themselves show that an experience has been selectively consolidated into the model's own behavior. We introduce EVAF, an Echo-Valence Attractor Field mechanism for gated LoRA consolidation, and a test-retest protocol for measuring selective parametric consolidation under controlled interference. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, EVAF preferentially consolidates high-valence, high-surprise experiences while preserving retrieval-accessible factual memory through a complementary routed memory path. Test-retest measurements show stronger post-interference behavioral persistence than frozen, retrieval-only, and ungated continual-update baselines, while keeping parameter drift and cross-persona contamination low. The results support a separation between memory access and memory depth: retrieving a fact and internalizing an experience are distinct computational operations.

</details>


### [555] [Bandwidth Selection in Kernel Density Estimation for Model Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29925)
*Han Zhou,Teodora Popordanoska,Matthew Blaschko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Risk Alignment (RA), a framework that selects optimal KDE bandwidth by aligning KDE-reconstructed risk with empirical risk, improving calibration uncertainty estimates over standard methods like MLE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models in high-stakes applications need well-calibrated uncertainty estimates. KDE offers smooth calibration estimation but its reliability depends heavily on bandwidth choice, and standard selection methods like MLE fail to produce optimal bandwidths for this task.

Method: Introduces Risk Alignment (RA), an optimization framework that determines optimal bandwidth by aligning KDE-reconstructed risk with empirical risk. Theoretically proven to minimize calibration estimation bias across the data distribution, applicable to various metrics including canonical calibration error.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple architectures and datasets show RA consistently outperforms standard bandwidth selection methods, yielding more reliable calibration assessments.

Conclusion: Risk Alignment provides a principled, theoretically grounded bandwidth selection criterion for KDE-based calibration estimation that reliably outperforms existing approaches.

Abstract: As deep learning models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications, providing well-calibrated uncertainty estimates has become as critical as achieving high predictive accuracy. While Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) has emerged as a smooth and continuous alternative to traditional binning for quantifying miscalibration, its reliability is heavily dependent on the choice of the kernel bandwidth. Standard selection techniques, such as Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), often fail to produce optimal bandwidths for calibration tasks. In this work, we introduce Risk Alignment (RA), a novel optimization framework that determines the optimal bandwidth by aligning KDE-reconstructed risk with empirical risk. We theoretically demonstrate that this alignment minimizes calibration estimation bias across the data distribution, establishing a principled bandwidth selection criterion applicable to various metrics, including the challenging case of canonical calibration error. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures and datasets show that RA consistently outperforms standard bandwidth selection methods, yielding more reliable calibration assessments.

</details>


### [556] [Improved Predictive Performance and Interpretability for Mesomorphic Neural Networks Using Local Fidelity Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29951)
*Hugo L. Hammer,Vajira Thambawita,Kristoffer Herland Hellton,Pål Halvorsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IMNs can produce unreliable interpretations due to degenerate weight collapse, which L1 penalties exacerbate; Local Fidelity Regularization (LFR) fixes this by aligning output weights with local data variations, improving both interpretability and predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IMNs lack safeguards to ensure learned interpretations are reliable; the network can concentrate explanatory variance into a single weight, producing meaningless explanations, and the L1 penalty paradoxically worsens this degenerate configuration.

Method: Local Fidelity Regularization (LFR), a penalty term that aligns linear output weights with local data variations to prevent degenerate weight collapse and guarantee faithful explanations.

Result: LFR achieves improved AUROC over unregularized IMN across the OpenML benchmark suite, and yields results highly competitive with state-of-the-art black-box models.

Conclusion: LFR provides reliable interpretability without sacrificing accuracy, offering dual benefits of faithful explanations and superior predictive performance.

Abstract: Interpretable Mesomorphic Neural Networks (IMNs) offer a promising framework that combines the predictive power of deep neural networks with the interpretability of linear models. However, the original formulation lacks safeguards to ensure that the learned interpretations are in fact reliable. In particular, the network is free to concentrate all explanatory variance into a single weight of the linear output layer, achieving strong predictive performance while producing interpretations that are largely meaningless. Paradoxically, the L1 penalty proposed to encourage sparse solutions exacerbates this problem by further incentivizing such degenerate configurations.
  To address this vulnerability, we introduce Local Fidelity Regularization (LFR), a novel penalty term that prevents degenerate weight collapse by aligning the linear output weights with local data variations. This structural constraint guarantees faithful explanations and substantially improves the reliability of model interpretations. Furthermore, empirical evaluations across the OpenML benchmark suite demonstrate that LFR does not compromise accuracy for explainability; rather, it achieved improved AUROC over the unregularized IMN. By yielding results highly competitive with state-of-the-art black-box models, LFR provides the dual benefit of reliable interpretability and superior predictive performance. Source code and usage instructions are available at https://github.com/hugohammer/LFR-IMN.git.

</details>


### [557] [Exploiting Local Flatness for Efficient Out-of-Distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29952)
*Seonghwan Park,Hyunji Jung,Dongyeop Lee,Namhoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates curvature discrepancies between ID and OOD data, finding OOD inputs have larger Hessian curvature. It proposes Fold, a lightweight flatness-modulated OOD detector using feature Hessian and partial feature normalization, plus AutoFold for self-supervised tuning. Fold improves AUROC by 1.63% and reduces FPR95 by 2.30% with computational efficiency comparable to a standard forward pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing post-hoc OOD detection methods that exploit loss-landscape curvature are computationally expensive and rely on implicit assumptions about how flatness differs between ID and OOD data. The paper aims to systematically investigate this curvature discrepancy and develop a more efficient, principled approach.

Method: Fold uses feature Hessian and partial feature normalization to improve ID-OOD separability, avoiding costly parameter-space curvature approximations. AutoFold is a self-supervised tuning scheme that synthesizes pseudo-OOD samples via ID logit masking for automatic calibration without external data.

Result: Fold improves average AUROC by 1.63% and reduces FPR95 by 2.30% compared to prior methods, while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to a standard forward pass.

Conclusion: Fold provides a principled and practical solution for robust real-world OOD detection, supported by theoretical analysis and extensive ablations.

Abstract: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) data is crucial for reliable machine learning deployment. Among detection strategies, post-hoc methods are particularly attractive due to their efficiency, as they operate directly on pre-trained networks without requiring retraining. Within this paradigm, one promising direction exploits loss-landscape curvature to estimate model uncertainty; however, such methods incur substantial computational cost and rely on implicit assumptions about how landscape flatness differs between in-distribution (ID) and OOD data. In this work, we provide the first systematic investigation of this curvature discrepancy and show that OOD inputs exhibit larger Hessian curvature than ID data, with the gap widening under stronger distributional shifts. Motivated by these observations, we propose Fold, a lightweight flatness-modulated OOD detector that leverages the feature Hessian and partial feature normalization to improve ID-OOD separability while avoiding costly parameter-space curvature approximations. To optimally adapt this normalization across diverse datasets, we further introduce AutoFold, a self-supervised tuning scheme that synthesizes pseudo-OOD samples via ID logit masking for automatic calibration without requiring external data. Experiments on OOD benchmarks show that Fold outperforms prior methods, improving the average AUROC by 1.63% and reducing FPR95 by 2.30%, while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to a standard forward pass. Supported by theoretical analysis and extensive ablations, Fold provides a principled and practical solution for robust real-world deployment.

</details>


### [558] [DuoMem: Towards Capable On-Device Memory Agents via Dual-Space Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29961)
*Peyman Hosseini,Ondrej Bohdal,Ahmed Alajrami,Andrea Maracani,Ignacio Castro,Matthew Purver,Mete Ozay,Savas Ozkan,Taha Ceritli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DuoMem is a dual-space distillation framework that transfers procedural problem-solving from large LLMs to compact models, achieving 77.9% task success on ALFWorld with a 4B model (vs 87.1% for 72B teacher) using fewer than 10M trainable parameters, while running 3x faster for edge deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents can solve complex procedural tasks but typically require large models, long contexts, and repeated inference calls, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices. The goal is to enable compact student models to retain advanced procedural reasoning without the computational overhead.

Method: DuoMem uses dual-space distillation: (1) context-space distillation replaces student-generated memories with higher-quality teacher-generated procedural memories prepended to the student's input, and (2) parameter-space distillation fine-tunes lightweight LoRA adapters on successful teacher trajectories.

Result: On ALFWorld, DuoMem improved a 4B model from 4.3% to 77.9% task success rate (teacher: 87.1%), adding fewer than 10M trainable parameters. The enhanced 4B model ran over 3x faster than the 72B teacher in wall-clock time. Ablations across eight models (2B-72B) confirmed both distillation axes contribute complementarily.

Conclusion: DuoMem effectively transfers procedural problem-solving ability to compact models with minimal overhead, enabling real-time edge deployment while closing most of the performance gap with much larger teacher models.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents can solve complex procedural tasks by interacting with environments over multiple turns, but this ability typically depends on large models, long contexts, and repeated inference calls. This makes advanced memory-augmented agents difficult to deploy on resource-constrained devices. We introduce DuoMem, a dual-space distillation framework that transfers procedural problem-solving ability from a large teacher model to compact student models. DuoMem distils in two complementary spaces: (1)context-space distillation, which replaces student-generated memories with higher-quality teacher-generated procedural memories prepended to the student's input, and (2)parameter-space distillation, which fine-tunes lightweight LoRA adapters on successful teacher trajectories. Evaluated on ALFWorld, a challenging embodied decision-making benchmark, DuoMem boosts a 4B-parameter model from 4.3% to 77.9% task success rate, closing most of the gap to a 72B teacher model (87.1%), while adding fewer than 10M trainable parameters and only a few megabytes of pre-computed teacher memories. Moreover, the DuoMem-enhanced 4B model completes tasks over 3x faster than the 72B teacher in wall-clock time, making it viable for real-time edge deployment, which would be challenging for the teacher.Extensive ablations across eight models spanning 2B-72B parameters reveal that both distillation axes contribute complementary

</details>


### [559] [NeuReasoner: Theory-grounded Mapping of Reasoning Elicitation Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29971)
*Aydin Javadov,Shyngys Aitkazinov,Tobias Hoesli,Florian von Wangenheim,Bjoern Schuller,Joseph Ollier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeuReasoner is a theory-grounded elicitation instrument that pairs a Neuro Lens with a Cognitive Lens to recover reasoning capabilities from base LLMs. It matches or exceeds post-trained thinking modes on arithmetic, code, Bayesian reasoning, and reward learning, but reveals clear boundaries—risk-taking and decision-making under uncertainty remain hard to recover, and model scale has bidirectional effects on elicitation success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work suggests LLM reasoning is latent in base models and post-training amplifies it, but evidence is limited to math and coding benchmarks. The authors aim to explore the boundary conditions of this claim—which cognitive tasks can be recovered via elicitation and where recovery fails—across a broader behavioral spectrum.

Method: NeuReasoner: an orchestrator pairs a Neuro Lens (inspired by functional specificity) with a Cognitive Lens (from Erotetic Theory of Reasoning), integrates their outputs through internal modularization of a single model (no external tools), and evaluates on CogBench (cognitive psychology tasks) plus standard math/coding benchmarks.

Result: At sufficient scale, NeuReasoner matches or exceeds thinking-mode baselines on arithmetic reasoning, code generation, Bayesian reasoning, and reward learning. Gains persist against self-consistency and iterative-refinement baselines matched to its call budget. However, risk-taking and decision-making under uncertainty are not recoverable through elicitation alone; model scale widens advantage on some cognitive signatures but erases it on others.

Conclusion: Using NeuReasoner as a modular, interpretable, theory-grounded elicitation instrument, the paper empirically maps where reasoning elicitation succeeds and fails beyond math and coding benchmarks, revealing both the recoverable and non-recoverable boundaries of latent reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: A growing body of work suggests that the reasoning capabilities of large language models are largely latent in their base form, with post-training primarily amplifying rather than introducing them.
