However, this evidence comes mainly from mathematical and coding benchmarks, leaving the boundary conditions of that claim largely unexplored, namely which cognitive tasks can be recovered through elicitation and where that recovery fails. To investigate this, we introduce NeuReasoner, a theory-grounded elicitation instrument. At each step, an orchestrator pairs a Neuro Lens, inspired by functional specificity, with a Cognitive Lens, drawn from the Erotetic Theory of Reasoning, and integrates their outputs through internal modularization of a single model, without external tools. We evaluate NeuReasoner on CogBench, a suite of behavioral tasks from cognitive psychology, alongside standard mathematical and coding benchmarks, measuring both its improvement over vanilla inference and its ability to match a model's post-trained thinking mode. At sufficient scale, NeuReasoner matches or exceeds thinking-mode baselines on arithmetic reasoning, code generation, Bayesian reasoning, and reward learning; these gains persist against self-consistency and iterative-refinement baselines matched to NeuReasoner's per-decision call budget. Using NeuReasoner allows us to find clear boundaries: risk-taking and decision making under uncertainty remains hard to recover through elicitation alone, and model scale interacts with elicitation in both directions: widening its advantage on some cognitive signatures while erasing it on others. Overall, through NeuReasoner as a modular, interpretable, theory-grounded elicitation instrument, we empirically map where reasoning elicitation succeeds and fails, beyond the mathematical and coding benchmarks where prior claims have rested.

</details>


### [560] [Atompack: A Storage and Distribution Layer for Read-Heavy Atomistic ML Training Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29975)
*Ali Ramlaoui,Daniel T. Speckhard,Sagar Pal,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros,Alexandre Duval,Victor Schmidt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Atompack is an append-oriented storage format for atomistic machine learning datasets that outperforms HDF5, LMDB, and ASE baselines by 96x on shuffled reads with 79% smaller artifacts, optimized for training pipeline workloads consuming complete molecular records.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing storage formats (HDF5, LMDB, ASE) are designed for interactive scientific curation with mutable records and ad hoc inspection, but atomistic ML training workloads require repeated reading of immutable snapshots, random shuffling across epochs, and efficient distribution across clusters.

Method: Atompack uses an append-oriented design: appends records efficiently during dataset construction, commits an immutable index, then serves records through a memory-mapped read path optimized for training pipelines that consume complete molecular records in randomized order.

Result: On a 64-atom workload, Atompack is 96x faster than ASE LMDB on shuffled training-style reads while producing artifacts about 79% smaller, outperforming HDF5, LMDB, and ASE baselines across sequential reads, shuffled reads, shared-filesystem behavior, write throughput, and artifact size.

Conclusion: Serving complete molecule records rather than field chunks or reconstructed objects improves shuffled training throughput while keeping artifacts compact enough for public distribution, demonstrating that storage format design should be tailored to ML training workloads rather than general scientific curation.

Abstract: Atomistic machine learning datasets are increasingly used for training: large immutable snapshots are read repeatedly, shuffled across epochs, staged across clusters' storage systems, and republished as reusable scientific artifacts. This workload differs from interactive scientific curation, where mutable records and ad hoc inspection are often more important than random indexed throughput. We present Atompack, an append-oriented storage format and distribution layer designed around a simple workload: training pipelines usually consume complete molecular records, while the order of records is randomized by the learning algorithm. Atompack appends records efficiently during dataset construction, then commits an immutable index and serves records through a memory-mapped read path optimized for training. We compare Atompack with HDF5, LMDB, and ASE baselines representing array stores, key-value records, serialized records, and object-oriented databases. The benchmarks measure sequential reads, shuffled reads, shared-filesystem behavior, write throughput, and artifact size. On a representative 64-atom workload, Atompack is 96x faster than ASE LMDB on shuffled training-style reads while producing artifacts about 79\% smaller. The results indicate that serving complete molecule records, rather than field chunks or reconstructed objects, improves shuffled training throughput while keeping artifacts compact enough for public distribution.

</details>


### [561] [Stabilizing Extrapolation in Looped Transformers via Learned Stochastic Stopping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29983)
*Hsun-Yu Kuo,El Mahdi Chayti,Patrik Reizinger,Wieland Brendel,Martin Jaggi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Looped Transformers with variable loop counts during training reduce out-of-distribution variance and improve stability on algorithmic tasks; RL-Halting as a learned stochastic schedule often improves the accuracy-stability trade-off compared to heuristic randomization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Looped Transformers exhibit brittle length generalization with high OOD variance due to spurious correlation between sequence length and number of loops; the paper aims to understand and mitigate this variance.

Method: Introduces stochasticity in loop count during training; compares heuristic randomization with learned stochastic halting (RL-Halting) across binary addition, Dyck-1, Unique Set, and Copy tasks.

Result: Stochastic loop counts sharply reduce OOD variance and stabilize predictions; RL-Halting generally improves accuracy-stability trade-off but can also stabilize suboptimal computations.

Conclusion: "When to stop" should be treated as a training-time design choice, not merely an inference-time computation-allocation rule.

Abstract: Looped Transformers, which repeatedly apply a shared transformer block, are an architecturally natural fit for variable-length algorithmic tasks. Although they can exhibit strong length generalization beyond the length of training sequences, this behavior is brittle, yielding high out-of-distribution (OOD) variance, even across well-performing in-distribution solutions. We trace this variance to the spurious correlation in simple algorithmic tasks between sequence length and number of loops. Introducing stochasticity into the number of loops during training sharply reduces OOD variance and stabilizes predictions across inference-time loop counts. To improve upon heuristic randomization schemes, we further analyze RL-Halting as a learned stochastic schedule and find that it generally improves the accuracy-stability trade-off. Across binary addition, Dyck-1, Unique Set, and Copy, learned stochastic stopping often improves this trade-off but can also stabilize a suboptimal computation. Our work suggests that "when to stop" should be treated as a training-time design choice, not merely an inference-time computation-allocation rule.

</details>


### [562] [T3R: Deeper Test-Time Adaptation for Graph Neural Networks via Gradient Rotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30011)
*Huy Truong,Alexander Lazovik,Victoria Degeler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: T3R proposes a Test-Time Training method for GNNs that uses multiple Rotograd matrices and rotation techniques to enable deeper adaptation across the entire architecture without labeled data, achieving significant improvements in regression and classification tasks under distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs deployed in real-world systems suffer from degraded performance under distribution shifts because they have fixed weights. Conventional fine-tuning is often infeasible due to the expense of collecting labeled data.

Method: T3R leverages multiple Rotograd matrices to improve task affinity between target and auxiliary tasks, and introduces a rotation technique that reorients self-supervised signals to create surrogate gradients for the target task, enabling deeper adaptation across nearly the entire architecture.

Result: T3R reduces MAE by 0.172 points over standard inference in regression datasets and achieves at least 9.37% relative improvement on cross-domain OGB classification benchmarks compared to models without adaptation.

Conclusion: T3R highlights the potential to develop an adaptation pipeline for graph-based systems, particularly in settings where conventional fine-tuning or retraining is infeasible.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) deployed in real-world systems typically have fixed weights, often leading to degraded performance under distribution shifts. This issue can be mitigated by conventional fine-tuning, but in many real-world cases, collecting labeled data is expensive or infeasible. A potential approach is Test-Time Training (TTT), which adapts models' weights using unlabeled test data, yet it is typically limited to shallow updates that affect only a subset of model parameters. We propose T3R, leveraging multiple Rotograd matrices to improve task affinity between the target and auxiliary tasks, essential for effective test-time training. T3R further introduces a rotation technique that reorients self-supervised signals using these matrices to create surrogate gradients for the target task, allowing deeper adaptation across nearly the entire architecture. Empirically, T3R reduces MAE by 0.172 points over standard inference in regression datasets and achieves at least 9.37% relative improvement on cross-domain OGB classification benchmarks compared to models without adaptation. These results highlight the potential to develop an adaptation pipeline for graph-based systems, particularly in settings where conventional fine-tuning or retraining is infeasible.

</details>


### [563] [Heads, Not Backbones: Output Heads Dominate Architectures on Fat-Tailed Returns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30037)
*Sichao He,Yansong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: For short-horizon fat-tailed financial return forecasting, the output head (especially Gaussian mixture) matters more than the backbone architecture; the mixture head captures tail risk during high-volatility regimes where it most counts, though the backbone re-asserts dominance at longer horizons (h ≥ 6).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine which component of a deep forecasting pipeline—backbone architecture or output head—has greater impact on performance for fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons, a question with practical implications for model design in risk management.

Method: Compared four backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) under three output heads (point, single-Gaussian density, Gaussian mixture with K=4) on S&P 500 monthly log-returns (1871-2023) using anchored walk-forward validation; evaluated via CRPS, squared error, pinball, coverage, and Model Confidence Set.

Result: The heads form a strict gradient: Gaussian improves CRPS ~1.3% over point, mixture adds ~2.4% over Gaussian. Backbone changes yield <1.5% CRPS variation under point head; density-head backbone spread reaches 5.1% but head gradient (3.7pp) still dominates. Model Confidence Set fails to exclude any variant on squared error. Mixture head gains peak during 1970s stagflation (13.9%). At h≥6, backbone re-takes lead.

Conclusion: On fat-tailed returns at short horizons, the output head dominates the backbone; mixture density heads provide genuine value during crisis periods for risk management, but the backbone becomes more important at longer horizons.

Abstract: In a deep forecasting pipeline for fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons, which matters more - the backbone architecture or the output head? We compare four modern backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) under three output heads: a point head, a single-Gaussian density head, and a Gaussian mixture density head with K=4 components. On S and P 500 monthly log-returns (1871-2023) under anchored walk-forward validation, the three heads form a strict gradient: switching from point to Gaussian improves CRPS by about 1.3 percent; switching from Gaussian to mixture adds a further about 2.4 percent. Switching between backbones, in contrast, changes CRPS by less than 1.5 percent on the point-head row and on the backbone-mean axis; density-head backbone spread is larger (up to 5.1 percent on the h=1 Gaussian row, driven by N-BEATS) but the head gradient (3.7 percentage points) still dominates. The Model Confidence Set on squared errors does not exclude any of the 12 variants at the 5 percent level: the head separates them only on distributional metrics (CRPS, pinball, coverage), not on squared error. The mixture head incremental value over a single Gaussian is largest in the highest-volatility regimes (13.9 percent in 1970s stagflation at h=12), confirming the mixture captures tail risk beyond what a unimodal Gaussian can express. The picture is horizon-dependent: the head dominates at short horizons, but at long horizons (h >= 6) the backbone re-takes the lead - an h-split we document against classical baselines (section 5.1). We conclude that on fat-tailed returns at short horizons, the head dominates the backbone, and the mixture distribution adds genuine value over a single Gaussian during crisis periods when risk-management decisions actually matter.

</details>


### [564] [Building Multi-Task Agentic LLMs via Two-Phase Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30044)
*Huaijie Wang,Shusheng Xu,Yi Wu,Kaifeng Lyu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a two-phase distillation approach (off-policy then on-policy) to consolidate multiple task-specific RL experts into a single multi-task model, showing it outperforms either distillation method alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training a single model capable of performing multiple tasks is a key step toward AGI. The authors explore distilling separate RL experts into one model as an alternative to direct multi-task training, identifying limitations of existing distillation approaches.

Method: The authors analyze forward KL (off-policy) distillation's mode-covering failure in multi-task settings due to capacity overloading, and on-policy distillation's mode-seeking nature requiring strong initialization. They propose a two-phase approach: first off-policy distillation (to get a reasonable initialization), then on-policy refinement (to shift from mode-covering to mode-seeking behavior).

Result: The two-phase approach matches single-task RL expert performance across conversational agents and text-based games. Off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails to match this performance.

Conclusion: Combining off-policy and on-policy distillation sequentially effectively consolidates multi-task RL experts into a single model, overcoming the individual limitations of each distillation paradigm.

Abstract: A key step toward artificial general intelligence is to train models that can perform multiple tasks. In this paper, we study how to build such models by first training separate RL experts for individual tasks and then consolidating them via distillation, as an alternative to directly training a single model on mixed tasks. We show that off-policy distillation degrades in multi-task settings due to the mode-covering nature of forward KL: aggregating data from multiple tasks introduces a large number of behavioral modes that can exceed the student's capacity, forcing it to average across behaviors and leading to degraded performance. In contrast, on-policy distillation is mode-seeking but requires strong initialization. Inspired by these observations, we propose a two-phase approach: off-policy distillation followed by on-policy refinement. Evaluation across conversational agents and text-based games confirms that this two-phase approach matches single-task RL expert performance for each individual task, whereas off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails to match this performance.

</details>


### [565] [From Failure Taxonomy to Intervention: A Diagnostic Methodology for Industry-Scale AVLM in Video and Live-Streaming Platform Moderation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30059)
*Shuchang Ye,Jinqiang Yu,Zhujun Xiao,Yajing Kong,Yist Y. Lin,Yang Ma,Jiaxi Liu,Xiaolei Xu,Zheng Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Model (AVLM) development that categorizes failure signatures into a taxonomy and links each failure class to targeted interventions, addressing the gap between generic public model guidance and the specific needs of video/live-streaming moderation at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industry-scale video and live-streaming moderation requires platform-specific adaptation (data distributions, policy objectives, safety constraints) that generic pretrained models or external APIs cannot satisfy, yet existing research lacks systematic guidance on localizing failures and translating them into targeted interventions for model development.

Method: The methodology maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures, links each failure class to an intervention space, and instantiates this approach across the development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model for a large-scale video/live-streaming platform.

Result: The resulting system supports over 100 regions and is designed for noisy, ambiguous, and highly diverse content from global platform traffic.

Conclusion: Targeted interventions based on failure taxonomy are essential for moving beyond heuristic trial-and-error in model improvement, enabling traceable attribution of failures to underlying causes in industry-scale AVLM development.

Abstract: Industry-scale video and live-streaming moderation imposes requirements that are difficult to satisfy with generic pretrained public models or external APIs, including adaptation to platform-specific data distributions, policy-specific objectives, and product-level safety constraints. As a result, platforms must undertake internal model development, naturally turning to shared public research for guidance. However, existing multimodal foundation-model studies primarily report architectures, training recipes, data scaling strategies, and benchmark results, but provide less systematic guidance on how failures should be localized and translated into targeted model-development interventions. Interventions are essential because deployment failures are rarely self-explanatory. Similar failures can originate from different causes. Without targeted interventions, improvement reduces to heuristic trial-and-error, where benchmark improvements are weakly attributable, and failures are difficult to trace to their underlying causes. To address this gap, we present a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Models AVLM development. The methodology maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures and links each class of failure to an intervention space. We instantiate this methodology across the development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model for a large-scale video and live-streaming platform. The resulting system supports over 100 regions and is designed for noisy, ambiguous, and highly diverse content drawn from global platform traffic.

</details>


### [566] [Data-Driven Energy-Based Learning via Gibbs Measures on Hierarchical Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30064)
*L. U. Abdullaev,F. Herrera,U. A. Rozikov,M. V. Velasco*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a probabilistic framework using Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures to model learning systems, where data defines a distribution over possible inference states rather than a single optimal solution. The authors derive fixed-point equations for admissible learning states, analyze existence/uniqueness conditions, and demonstrate phase transitions with multiple coexisting learning states on tree structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond standard empirical risk minimization (which yields a single optimal parameter) and instead model learning as a probabilistic process where data defines an entire landscape of possible inference states via Gibbs distributions on hierarchical structures.

Method: The authors (1) transform empirical loss functions into interaction potentials for energy-based Gibbs models on trees; (2) formulate consistency conditions for finite-volume distributions; (3) derive nonlinear integral fixed-point equations for admissible learning states; (4) analyze translation-invariant solutions via positive compact operators; (5) study phase transitions on Cayley trees with critical inverse temperature thresholds.

Result: Established existence and uniqueness conditions for one-dimensional settings; demonstrated phase-transition phenomena where multiple Gibbs measures emerge beyond a critical inverse temperature on Cayley trees; numerical experiments with non-separable kernels confirmed multiple solution branches and coexisting learning states.

Conclusion: Data in energy-based learning should be viewed as defining a probabilistic landscape of possible inference states rather than determining a single optimal model through minimization, with hierarchical structure enabling phase transitions and multiple equilibrium prediction regimes.

Abstract: We introduce a data-driven probabilistic framework for learning systems based on Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures. Unlike standard empirical risk minimization, where a dataset is used to identify a single optimal parameter, our approach transforms the empirical loss function into an interaction potential defining an energy-based model. The resulting Gibbs distribution describes a family of equilibrium learning states generated by the data.
  We formulate the consistency conditions of the associated finite-volume distributions and derive nonlinear integral fixed-point equations whose solutions characterize the admissible learning states. These equations provide a rigorous connection between empirical loss landscapes and probabilistic inference on trees. For translation-invariant solutions, the problem reduces to the analysis of positive compact operators induced by data-dependent kernels, allowing us to establish existence and uniqueness conditions in the one-dimensional setting.
  Furthermore, we show that hierarchical learning systems may exhibit phase-transition phenomena: for certain empirical kernels on Cayley trees, multiple Gibbs measures emerge beyond a critical inverse temperature, corresponding to distinct equilibrium prediction regimes. Numerical experiments with non-separable kernels illustrate the appearance of multiple solution branches and demonstrate the coexistence of several data-induced learning states.
  Our results provide a new perspective on energy-based learning, where data do not merely determine an optimal model through minimization but define an entire probabilistic landscape of possible inference states.

</details>


### [567] [Neural Subspace Reallocation: Continual Learning as Retrieval-Based Subspace Memory Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30067)
*Byeong Hoon Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural Subspace Reallocation (NSR) reframes continual learning as memory management over parameter subspaces, using SVD compression, similarity-based retrieval, and reallocation of LoRA modules to achieve 10x faster cyclic recovery and 9x less forgetting than memoryless heuristics, with a compressed memory footprint of only 0.29 MB per task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continual learning approaches treat LoRA modules as disposable per-task adapters, ignoring the potential to reuse knowledge across tasks. This work aims to reframe continual learning as a memory management problem over parameter subspaces, enabling efficient storage, retrieval, and reallocation of learned knowledge.

Method: NSR operates via a recurring four-step cycle: (1) compress learned LoRAs via SVD, (2) reserve them in a TaskKnowledgeBank, (3) recall related past LoRAs by embedding similarity to warm-start new or returning tasks, and (4) reallocate the active subspace accordingly, with distillation protecting prior tasks. A theoretical regret bound is provided for cyclic environments.

Result: On Split-CIFAR-100, the Bank reduces cyclic recovery time by 10x. On 5-Datasets benchmark, NSR achieves the highest accuracy and least forgetting, about 9x closer to zero backward transfer than memoryless heuristics. A controlled study shows similarity-based retrieval matches or beats a learned RL controller (0 vs 1.8 steps recovery). Memory budget is 0.29 MB per task.

Conclusion: The memory mechanism—compression and similarity retrieval—rather than a learned allocation policy, drives continual-learning performance under fixed capacity. The compressed Bank remains small, enabling practical deployment with bounded memory footprint.

Abstract: We introduce Neural Subspace Reallocation (NSR), which reframes continual learning as memory management over parameter subspaces. Instead of treating Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules as disposable per-task adapters, NSR manages them as compressible, retrievable memory units on a frozen backbone through a recurring cycle: (1) compress learned LoRAs via SVD, (2) reserve them in a TaskKnowledgeBank, (3) recall related past LoRAs by embedding similarity to warm-start new or returning tasks, and (4) reallocate the active subspace accordingly, with distillation protecting prior tasks. We prove that in cyclic environments any memoryless allocation policy incurs cumulative regret Omega(T(M-1)Delta_switch) relative to a history-aware policy backed by the Bank (Theorem 1). Empirically, on Split-CIFAR-100 the Bank reduces cyclic recovery time by 10x, exactly as predicted, and on the heterogeneous 5-Datasets benchmark NSR achieves the highest accuracy and the least forgetting, about 9x closer to zero backward transfer than the memoryless heuristics. Crucially, we run a controlled study that isolates which component matters: holding the Bank fixed and varying only the allocation rule, we find that a simple similarity-based retrieval rule matches or beats a learned reinforcement-learning controller (recovering recurring tasks in 0 vs 1.8 steps and reaching equal accuracy). Our central, honest finding is therefore that the memory mechanism -- compression and similarity retrieval -- rather than a learned allocation policy, drives continual-learning performance under fixed capacity. A memory-budget analysis confirms the compressed Bank stays small -- 0.29 MB of parameter memory per task -- so a top-K retention cap bounds the total footprint while preserving fast recovery for retained tasks.

</details>


### [568] [Predictive Objectives Discard Exogenous Control-Relevant Features: A Controlled Mechanistic Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30068)
*Ayan Pendharkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JEPA-style objectives discard exogenous but control-relevant features because they optimize temporal predictability rather than control-relevance; reward-grounded variants (with as little as 2% reward labels) recover these features, while reward-free predictive objectives fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and isolate a specific failure mode of JEPA-style predictive objectives: discarding features that are exogenous (uncontrollable) yet control-relevant, despite being trivially encodable.

Method: Controlled 2x2 experimental design varying feature controllability and relevance independently, with a predictability knob decoupling temporal predictability from control-relevance. Compared six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA. Tested across two environments and latent dimensions 16-1024.

Result: All reward-free predictive objectives leave exogenous control-relevant features near chance accuracy; reward-grounded JEPA retains the feature selectively with as little as 2% reward-labeled transitions. JEPA latent achieves only a small fraction of class separation compared to supervised bisimulation reference.

Conclusion: Reward-free predictive objectives inherently discard control-relevant exogenous features; the remedy is label-efficient reward grounding.

Abstract: Joint-embedding predictive (JEPA-style) objectives learn representations by predicting future latents. In doing so they can discard features that are exogenous (uncontrollable by the agent) yet control-relevant, even when those features are trivially encodable. This occurs because the objective optimizes temporal predictability rather than control-relevance. We isolate this failure mode in a controlled 2x2 experimental design that varies feature controllability and relevance independently, using a predictability knob that decouples a feature's temporal predictability from its control-relevance. Comparing six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under a random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA, we observe that all evaluated reward-free predictive objectives leave the exogenous control-relevant feature near chance accuracy, while a reward-grounded variant retains it selectively. The remedy is label-efficient and robust: as little as 2% of reward-labeled transitions recovers the feature, the effect holds across two environments with different surface forms, and it persists across latent dimensions from 16 to 1024. Comparing the learned latent geometry against bisimulation theory's prediction, the JEPA latent realizes only a small fraction of the class separation a supervised reference attains.

</details>


### [569] [Online Data Selection for Instruction Tuning via Gaussian Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30077)
*Jun Wang,Quoc Phong Nguyen,Julien Monteil,Vu Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GAIA proposes a Gaussian Process regression-based framework for global adaptive data valuation in instruction tuning, overcoming limitations of batch-constrained online selection methods, and achieves superior performance with dynamic regret guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing online data selection methods for LLM training are "batch-constrained," limiting optimization to local utility within random batches, which fails to capture global data quality dynamics.

Method: Uses Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across the semantic space, with an adaptive strategy fusion mechanism to dynamically prioritize high-utility samples. The strategy-posterior update is cast as a fixed-share Hedge framework for tracking the best expert, providing dynamic-regret guarantees.

Result: Empirical evaluations on three datasets show GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like GREAT.

Conclusion: GAIA provides a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning.

Abstract: With Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training and fine-tuning shifting its focus from data volume to data quality, quality data selection has emerged as a critical research topic. Existing online data selection methods for LLM training are typically "batch-constrained", limiting optimization to local utility within random batches. To overcome this, we propose GAIA (Global Adaptive Instruction tuning via GAussian processes), a framework that formulates data valuation as a global estimation process. GAIA employs Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across the semantic space, utilizing an adaptive strategy fusion mechanism to dynamically prioritize high-utility samples. By casting the strategy-posterior update as an instance of the classical fixed-share Hedge framework for tracking the best expert, we inherit a dynamic-regret guarantee that characterizes GAIA's robustness under non-stationary quality scores during training. Empirical evaluations on three datasets demonstrate that GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like \greats, establishing our method as a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning.

</details>


### [570] [Query-Aware Spreading Activation for Multi-Hop Retrieval over Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30133)
*Illia Makarov,Mykola Glybovets*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a spreading-activation method for query-aware graph traversal in Graph RAG, using a single semantic gate (cosine similarity) per step instead of iterative flow-diffusion solvers. The entire retrieval process is expressed as a single Cypher query executed in Neo4j, eliminating the need to load the graph into Python memory. The method matches or approaches state-of-the-art performance on MuSiQue and 2WikiMultiHopQA while significantly reducing latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Graph RAG systems either use "query-blind" traversal (only graph structure) or require complex query-aware methods like QAFD-RAG that involve loading the full graph into Python memory and running iterative solvers with variable iterations, complicating database integration.

Method: Proposes a spreading-activation method with a fixed number of iterations and a per-step semantic gate—the cosine similarity between the candidate entity's description and the question. The entire pipeline (seed mapping, propagation, top-K selection, context assembly) is expressed as a single Cypher query executed in one round-trip to Neo4j.

Result: On MuSiQue: matches QAFD-RAG (32.80 vs 33.50 EM), outperforms HippoRAG by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1. On 2WikiMultiHopQA: HippoRAG and QAFD-RAG retain advantage due to phrase-node architectures. Ablation: gate contributes 3.6–7.4 F1 gain and reduces latency by 1.5×–4.9×.

Conclusion: The proposed spreading-activation method achieves query-aware graph traversal with a simple, fixed-iteration mechanism that stays entirely within the graph database, matching state-of-the-art performance while significantly reducing latency and computational overhead.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation built on knowledge graphs (Graph RAG) outperforms flat passage retrieval on multi-hop question answering by leveraging graph structure. In most existing systems, however, the question only sets the seed nodes; the subsequent traversal becomes "query-blind", depending solely on the graph structure. The exception is QAFD-RAG, which implements query-aware traversal via a flow-diffusion solver with combined edge re-weighting. This architecture requires loading the full graph into Python memory and an iterative solver with a variable number of iterations complicating integration with the graph database. We propose a spreading-activation method that achieves the same query-aware traversal with a single per-step semantic gate: the step weight is the cosine similarity between the candidate entity's description and the question, and the number of iterations is fixed. The whole retrieval procedure - seed mapping, propagation, top-K selection and context assembly - is expressed as a single Cypher query executed in one round-trip to Neo4j; the graph never leaves the database. On MuSiQue our method matches QAFD-RAG by exact match (32.80 vs 33.50) and outperforms the strongest purely-structural baseline in our comparison, HippoRAG, by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1; on 2WikiMultiHopQA HippoRAG and QAFD-RAG retain an advantage due to their phrase-node architectures. An ablation with the gate disabled confirms that the gate is the source of a simultaneous F1 gain of 3.6 to 7.4 points and a retrieval-latency reduction by a factor of 1.5 to 4.9.

</details>


### [571] [Robust Strategic Classification under Decision-Dependent Cost Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30136)
*Sura Alhanouti,Güzin Bayraksan,Parinaz Naghizadeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses strategic classification where manipulation costs depend on past algorithmic decisions, proposing a two-stage robust optimization framework with decision-dependent uncertainty sets to reduce gaming behavior over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing strategic classification literature assumes manipulation costs are fixed and independent of the classifier's decisions, but in practice, today's decisions influence tomorrow's costs. This gap limits the effectiveness of existing robust algorithms in real-world settings.

Method: The paper proposes and analyzes a two-stage robust optimization framework with a decision-dependent uncertainty set to model evolving manipulation costs that depend on past algorithmic decisions.

Result: Awareness of policy-dependent costs reduces uncertainty and better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time compared to approaches that treat costs as fixed.

Conclusion: Incorporating decision-dependent cost structures into robust optimization improves the algorithm's ability to manage strategic behavior in dynamic settings where manipulation costs evolve based on past decisions.

Abstract: Humans facing algorithmic decision systems have been found to ``game'' them by altering their input data (at a cost to them) in order to favorably change the algorithmic outcomes they receive (at a cost to the algorithm). The growing literature on strategic classification seeks to develop robust machine learning algorithms that account for, and reduce, unwanted strategic behavior. A limitation of these existing works is that they assume the cost of strategic behavior to be fixed and independent of the classifier's decision. In practice, however, manipulation costs evolve and depend on past algorithmic decisions: today's decisions influence tomorrow's costs. This paper proposes and analyzes a two-stage robust optimization framework with a decision-dependent uncertainty set to capture such dependencies. We highlight that awareness of policy-dependent costs not only reduces uncertainty, but also better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time.

</details>


### [572] [Federated Learning with Energy-Based Structured Probabilistic Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30161)
*Dario Fenoglio,Daniil Kirilenko,Martin Gjoreski,Marc Langheinrich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A CRF-based framework for learning optimal client aggregation weights in federated learning under heterogeneous data conditions, outperforming fixed/heuristic weighting baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing federated learning methods use fixed or heuristic client aggregation weights, which are suboptimal when clients have heterogeneous data distributions and varying contributions to the global model.

Method: The framework applies Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to model client aggregation: unary potentials capture individual client reliability, and pairwise potentials model interactions between client pairs. CRF inference yields optimized aggregation weights.

Result: Under non-IID heterogeneity, the proposed approach consistently improves performance over well-established federated learning baselines.

Conclusion: CRF-based aggregation weight refinement enables better convergence of the global training objective compared to fixed or heuristic weighting schemes.

Abstract: Federated learning typically aggregates client updates using fixed or heuristic weighting rules, which can be suboptimal when clients have heterogeneous data and varying contributions to the global model. We propose a framework that refines client aggregation weights using Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). Our method defines unary potentials for individual clients and pairwise potentials for all client pairs, allowing the server to model both client-specific reliability and interactions between clients. The resulting CRF inference produces aggregation weights that enable better convergence of the global training objective. Experiments show that, under non-IID heterogeneity, our approach consistently improves performance over well-established federated learning baselines.

</details>


### [573] [Beyond Drug Discovery: The Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30170)
*Matthias Blaschke,Daniel Kienzle,Zsuzsanna Koczor-Benda,Julian Lorenz,Rainer Lienhart,Fabian Pauly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark, replacing proxy drug-like property oracles with quantum simulations to create physically rigorous tasks for generative molecular design. Advanced ML methods underperform simpler baselines on NMO tasks, and the authors develop a new method with novel representations and domain-agnostic pretraining that surpasses state-of-the-art physical properties and discovers new structural motifs for nanotechnology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative molecular design relies on proxy benchmarks and pharmaceutical pretraining, which limits transferability to domains structurally distinct from drug discovery, such as nanotechnology. The authors aim to bridge ML and quantum materials science to drive genuine scientific discovery beyond leaderboard-oriented overfitting.

Method: The authors introduce the NMO Benchmark, which replaces proxy oracles with quantum simulations and strict protocols prioritizing scientific utility. They develop a new baseline method including a novel representation for modeling structural constraints and a domain-agnostic pretraining strategy to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias.

Result: Advanced molecular optimization methods underperform much simpler approaches on NMO tasks. The proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art physical properties and reveals previously unknown structural motifs for nanotechnology.

Conclusion: The NMO Benchmark serves as a rigorous testbed for ML and a discovery engine for nanotechnology. ML can drive genuine scientific discovery when paired with physics-based tasks, and domain-agnostic strategies can outperform pharmaceutical-biased pretraining in structurally distinct domains.

Abstract: Generative molecular design is shaped by simple proxy benchmarks for drug-like properties and models pretrained on large pharmaceutical datasets. This combination yields strong benchmark metrics but limits transferability to domains structurally distinct from drug discovery. To overcome this limitation and drive discovery toward real, scientifically grounded targets, we introduce the Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark, which bridges machine learning (ML) and quantum materials science. NMO acts simultaneously as a rigorous testbed for the ML community and a discovery engine for nanotechnology research. The suite replaces proxy oracles with quantum simulations and introduces strict protocols that prioritize scientific utility over leaderboard-oriented overfitting. The physics-based NMO tasks impose hard structural constraints and rugged fitness landscapes, posing fundamentally new requirements on generative models. Notably, advanced molecular optimization methods underperform much simpler approaches on the NMO tasks. We develop a new baseline method identifying the critical components to solve the NMO tasks, including a novel representation for modeling structural constraints and a domain-agnostic pretraining strategy to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias. Our results surpass state-of-the-art physical properties and reveal previously unknown structural motifs, offering new insights for the nanotechnology community and demonstrating that ML can drive genuine scientific discovery.

</details>


### [574] [Characterizing Optimizer-Dependent Training Dynamics Through Hessian Eigenvector Displacement and Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30226)
*Marcelina Marjankowska,Valerio Modugno,Paolo Barucca*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies how leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during neural network training, revealing that SGD leads to progressively stable curvature directions while Adam causes stronger eigenvector reorganization and localization of curvature in a small subset of parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hessian eigenvalues are a standard tool for analyzing training, but eigenvector dynamics during training remain poorly understood. This work aims to characterize how leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve and how they affect learning trajectories.

Method: The authors track training dynamics of multilayer perceptrons on a classification problem, measuring eigenvector dynamics via (i) displacement over time (inspired by glassy systems analysis) and (ii) localization via inverse participation ratio. Results are compared against a random null model of the architecture-induced Hessian.

Result: SGD leads to progressively more stable leading curvature directions. Adam exhibits substantially stronger reorganization of eigenvectors throughout training and a localization phenomenon where a small subset of parameters dominates leading curvature directions.

Conclusion: Hessian eigenvector dynamics capture key differences in optimizer behavior and resulting training trajectories, with distinct patterns for SGD (stabilizing) vs Adam (reorganizing and localizing).

Abstract: Hessian spectral properties are a standard tool in analysing neural-network training, with eigenvalues linked to sharpness, generalization, and optimization dynamics. Eigenvalues quantify curvature magnitude, while eigenvectors identify which parameters generate that curvature. In this work, we study how the leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during training and how they affect the learning trajectories. We track the training dynamics of multilayer perceptrons on a classification problem and measure eigenvector dynamics through two complementary statistics: (i) displacement over time, inspired by analyses of glassy systems, and (ii) localization via the inverse participation ratio. The metrics are compared against a random null model of the Hessian induced by the architecture. Our results reveal clear optimizer-dependent behaviour. SGD leads to progressively more stable leading curvature directions, while Adam exhibits substantially stronger reorganization of eigenvectors throughout training. We also observe a localization phenomenon under Adam, where a small subset of parameters contributes disproportionately to the leading curvature directions. These results suggest that Hessian eigenvector dynamics capture key differences in optimizer behaviour and the resulting training trajectories.

</details>


### [575] [B3O: Scalable Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30228)
*Maximilian Bloor,Liyuan Xu,Hrvoje Stojic,Victor Picheny*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: B3O reframes batch Bayesian Optimization as a pure sampling problem using Boltzmann distributions, achieving scalable large-batch performance with theoretical regret guarantees and strong empirical results across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing batch BO methods struggle with large-batch scenarios due to high computational cost or approximations that reduce batch diversity, motivating the need for a scalable, diversity-preserving approach.

Method: B3O generates batch queries by directly sampling from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function, avoiding costly optimization or greedy selection strategies.

Result: Outperforms existing batch BO methods on standard synthetic benchmarks; robustly adapts to complex applied tasks including multi-objective electrode design and mixed-variable race car configuration.

Conclusion: Theoretical analysis proves negligible additional regret from Boltzmann-sampled queries, and empirical validation confirms B3O's effectiveness and robustness across diverse settings.

Abstract: Modern engineering workflows increasingly rely on massive parallel simulation, driving the need for scalable, large-batch Bayesian Optimization (BO). Existing batch BO methods, however, incur large computational cost or rely on approximations that erode batch diversity. We propose B3O (Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization), a framework that reframes batch generation as a pure sampling problem: drawing samples directly from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function avoids the bottlenecks of existing large-batch methods. Theoretically, we prove that queries sampled from this distribution incur only negligible additional regret. Empirically, B3O outperforms existing batch BO methods on standard synthetic benchmarks and adapts robustly across complex applied tasks, including multi-objective electrode design and mixed-variable race car configuration.

</details>


### [576] [Curvature-Guided Sheaf Diffusion for Unsupervised Community Detection on Heterophilic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30249)
*Feifan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CGSD is a fully unsupervised community-detection algorithm for heterophilic graphs that uses discrete Forman–Ricci curvature as the core signal, combining a curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder with a curvature-aware spectral clusterer, achieving strong results on five heterophilic benchmarks with interpretable curvature-based mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting communities in heterophilic graphs (where connected nodes often belong to different classes) is difficult for unsupervised methods because classical approaches (modularity, spectral) are feature-agnostic and deep graph-clustering methods rely on opaque contrastive or generative machinery.

Method: CGSD uses three components: (i) a curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder that gates edge messages by σ(κ_e) and is trained with three label-free losses (modularity, anti-collapse, curvature-weighted reconstruction); (ii) a curvature-aware spectral clusterer (CSpec) that re-weights the k-NN affinity of the embedding by σ(ακ_{e*}) before Ng–Jordan–Weiss; (iii) a unified label-free evaluation against nine unsupervised baselines.

Result: On five heterophilic benchmarks (Cora, Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin, Chameleon), CGSD wins outright on Wisconsin and Chameleon and is competitive on the remaining three. CSpec improves mean NMI from 0.091 (K-Means) to 0.107 (+15%, paired t-test p=0.008) on the same embedding. Intra-community and inter-community curvature distributions are visibly separated.

Conclusion: CGSD demonstrates that discrete Forman–Ricci curvature is a powerful topological signal for unsupervised community detection in heterophilic graphs, with interpretable curvature distributions and consistent improvements driven by the curvature-aware clusterer.

Abstract: Detecting communities in heterophilic graphs -- where connected nodes often belong to different classes -- is hard for unsupervised methods: classical modularity and spectral methods are feature agnostic, while deep graph-clustering methods rely on contrastive or generative machinery that is opaque. We propose Curvature-Guided Sheaf Diffusion (CGSD), a fully unsupervised community-detection algorithm that uses the discrete Forman--Ricci curvature of each edge as its single topological signal, propagated through every stage of an end-to-end pipeline. CGSD makes three concrete contributions: (i)~a curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder that gates edge messages by $σ(κ_e)$ and is trained from three label-free structural losses (modularity, anti-collapse, curvature-weighted reconstruction); (ii)~a curvature-aware spectral clusterer (CSpec) that re-weights the $k$-NN affinity of the embedding by $σ(ακ_{e^*})$ before Ng--Jordan--Weiss; and (iii)~a unified label-free evaluation against nine truly-unsupervised baselines. On five heterophilic benchmarks (Cora, Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin, Chameleon), CGSD wins outright on Wisconsin and Chameleon and is competitive on the remaining three against nine unsupervised baselines. The gain over the strongest baseline is driven by the clusterer, not the encoder: on the same embedding, CSpec improves mean NMI from $0.091$ with $K$-Means to $0.107$ ($+15\%$, paired $t$-test $p=0.008$). The mechanism is interpretable: intra-community and inter-community curvature distributions are visibly separated. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/woodywff/cgsd.

</details>


### [577] [KnowsTFM: Knowledge-Informed Fine-Tuning of Small Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30258)
*Boshko Koloski,Xiangjian Jiang,Senja Pollak,Blaž Škrlj,Mateja Jamnik,Nikola Simidjievski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Knowledge-informed fine-tuning (KnowTab) of small tabular foundation models, using structural attention priors from knowledge graphs and low-rank updates, improves performance in niche domains but offers marginal gains on general tasks; continual fine-tuning of frontier models may cause knowledge collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular foundation models fail in niche domains (scarce, high-dimensional, shifted data) where domain-specific methods excel, and it is unclear how to leverage curated relational knowledge (knowledge graphs/banks) to improve small specialist tabular models.

Method: Proposes KnowTab: fine-tuning small tabular foundation models (TabPFN- and TabICL-style variants) using two mechanisms—(1) structural attention priors from knowledge graphs and (2) parameter-efficient low-rank updates—under controlled synthetic prior families.

Result: In specialist settings, injecting domain-specific structural knowledge yields meaningful gains over vanilla variants; gains on general-domain tasks are marginal. Continual fine-tuning of frontier models can cause collapse of pretrained knowledge.

Conclusion: Knowledge-informed fine-tuning effectively steers small tabular foundation models in niche domains, but care is needed to avoid catastrophic forgetting during continual fine-tuning of larger models.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models have advanced deep learning for tabular data by delivering strong default performance across many small and medium tasks. Yet in niche domains, where data is scarce, high-dimensional, and shifted from the pretraining distribution, they may still fail to outperform carefully designed domain-specific methods. Many such domains also provide curated relational knowledge in the form of knowledge graphs and knowledge banks, but how to use this knowledge to improve and steer \textit{small} specialist tabular foundation models remains unclear. We address this problem through \textbf{Know}ledge-informed fine-tuning of \textbf{s}mall \textbf{T}abular \textbf{F}oundation \textbf{M}odels (\modelname). Specifically, we study nanoscale TabPFN- and TabICL-style variants, pretrained under controlled synthetic prior families and adapted using two complementary mechanisms: structural attention priors derived from knowledge graphs and parameter-efficient low-rank updates. We show that injecting domain-specific structural knowledge during fine-tuning yields meaningful gains over vanilla variants in specialist settings, whereas gains on general-domain tasks are marginal. We further observe that continual fine-tuning of frontier models can trigger collapse of pretrained knowledge and mechanisms.

</details>


### [578] [When Is a Draft Accepted? A Theory of Acceptance in Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30265)
*Aaryam Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a theoretical framework for speculative decoding in practical regimes (greedy decoding, relaxed acceptance rules, tree-based candidates), characterizing rejection regions as lower level sets of the target distribution and deriving KL divergence certificates, margin-based bounds, and exact certifications for various acceptance criteria, validated on Qwen3 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theory for speculative decoding largely focuses on stochastic, distribution-preserving settings (exact sampling), but practical systems use greedy decoding, relaxed acceptance rules, and tree-based candidates where success depends on local ranking/threshold events rather than distributional equality, lacking a unified theoretical understanding.

Method: The authors identify that common acceptance criteria have rejection regions characterized as lower level sets of the target distribution. They derive exact KL divergence certificates and sharp margin-based bounds for strict greedy decoding, additive/multiplicative relaxed acceptance, top-(m) relaxed criteria, and entropy-thresholded acceptance. They extend to greedy tree decoding with exact and margin-only certificates for target greedy token coverage by drafter's top-(m) candidates. Certificates are evaluated on Qwen3 models.

Result: Relaxed and tree-based criteria substantially enlarge the region of certified acceptance compared to strict greedy decoding, especially on decoding steps with low target model distribution margin. The derived certificates are validated empirically on Qwen3 models.

Conclusion: This work complements existing distribution-preserving analyses of speculative decoding by characterizing deterministic local acceptance events common in practical inference systems, providing a theoretical foundation for relaxed and tree-based acceptance criteria.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates language model inference by using a fast drafter to propose candidate tokens that are then verified by a larger target model. Existing theory largely studies the stochastic, distribution-preserving setting, where the goal is to exactly sample from the target distribution. In contrast, many practical systems use greedy decoding, relaxed acceptance rules, or tree-based candidate sets, where success is governed by local ranking and threshold events rather than exact distributional equality. We develop a theory for these regimes. We identify that many common acceptance criteria have rejection regions that can be characterized as lower level sets of the target distribution. For these, we characterize the exact KL divergence required for rejection yielding exact certificates and sharp margin-based bounds for strict greedy decoding, additive and multiplicative relaxed acceptance, top-(m) relaxed criteria, and entropy-thresholded acceptance. We then extend the framework to greedy tree decoding, deriving exact and margin-only certificates for when the target greedy token remains covered by the drafter's top-(m) candidates. Finally, we evaluate the resulting certificates on Qwen3 models, showing that relaxed and tree-based criteria substantially enlarge the region of certified acceptance, especially on decoding steps with low target model distribution margin. These results complement existing distribution-preserving analyses of speculative decoding by characterizing the deterministic local acceptance events common in practical inference systems.

</details>


### [579] [Towards Continual Motion-Language Agents: LoRA Variants for Incremental Motion Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30266)
*Bertram Taetz,Hugo Albuquerque Cosme da Silva,Gabriele Bleser-Taetz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses continual learning in bidirectional motion-language agents (M2T and T2M) by proposing LoRA-based mixture-of-experts architectures with an autoencoder-based router that selects task-specific experts at inference time without requiring task labels. The method achieves near-zero forgetting on a five-task benchmark derived from HumanML3D, with hard expert selection outperforming soft blending.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion-language foundational models perform well in static settings, but autonomous agents operating in dynamic environments must continuously learn new motion concepts (e.g., novel athletic styles, specialized gestures) without catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired skills. The stability-plasticity trade-off in bidirectional motion-language learning under sequential task exposure has not been systematically investigated.

Method: The approach builds on a frozen large language model backbone with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) variants to mitigate inter-task interference. A mixture-of-experts architecture is proposed, featuring an autoencoder-based router that selects task-specific experts at inference time without requiring task labels. A five-task benchmark is created from HumanML3D via semantic clustering of motion descriptions.

Result: Near-zero forgetting is achieved across both M2T (motion-to-text) and T2M (text-to-motion) directions while maintaining high generation and captioning quality. Hard expert selection via routing significantly outperforms soft expert blending in quality metrics, demonstrating that preserving expert isolation is critical. A divergence between token-level accuracy and downstream generation quality is observed.

Conclusion: The proposed continual learning approach effectively addresses the stability-plasticity trade-off in bidirectional motion-language learning. Hard expert selection is superior to soft blending. The observed divergence between token-level metrics and generation quality highlights the need for more comprehensive evaluation protocols in future lifelong motion-language agent research.

Abstract: Motion-language agents must possess the bidirectional capability to both understand human movement (motion-to-text, M2T) and generate it from natural language (text-to-motion, T2M). While foundational models have achieved strong performance in static settings, autonomous agents operating in dynamic environments must continuously incorporate new motion concepts -- such as novel athletic styles or specialized gestures -- without catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired skills. We investigate the stability-plasticity trade-off in bidirectional motion-language learning under sequential task exposure. Building on a frozen large language model backbone, we introduce low-rank adaptation (LoRA) variants designed to mitigate inter-task interference. We specifically propose mixture-of-experts architectures that utilize an autoencoder-based router to select task-specific experts at inference time, so that no task-label is needed. To evaluate these methods, we establish a reproducible five-task benchmark derived from HumanML3D through semantic clustering of motion descriptions. Our experimental results demonstrate near-zero forgetting across both M2T and T2M directions while maintaining high generation and captioning quality. Furthermore, we show that hard expert selection via routing significantly outperforms soft expert blending in quality metrics, indicating that preserving expert isolation is critical for maintaining performance in our continual learning setting. Finally, we observe that a divergence between token-level accuracy and downstream generation quality may occur, highlighting the need for more comprehensive evaluation protocols in future research on lifelong motion-language agents.

</details>


### [580] [DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview: A Low-Compute Real-Time Controllable World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30292)
*Daniyel Ayupov,Artur Markov-Tsoy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview is a lightweight, real-time interactive world model achieving 14-15 FPS at 480p on a single RTX 4090, built by adapting open video backbones with a residual action pathway for consumer-GPU deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a low-compute, consumer-GPU-compatible alternative to frontier-scale world simulators, enabling real-time interactive world simulation without requiring massive computational resources.

Method: Adapts the LongLive autoregressive video stack (derived from Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B) with a residual action pathway inspired by Matrix-Game family, using open video backbones and targeted adaptation runs for cost-efficient training.

Result: Achieves 14-15 FPS at native 480p resolution on a single RTX 4090 with low memory footprint, supporting live keyboard/mouse control, multimodal initialization, mid-stream reprompting, dual-view operation, and minute-scale interactive rollouts.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical low-compute route toward real-time controllable world-model previews on consumer GPUs, though not yet a memory-complete or frontier-quality world simulator.

Abstract: We present DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview, a preview foundational world model for real-time interactive world simulation. The system adapts the LongLive 1 autoregressive video stack, itself derived from Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B, with a residual action pathway inspired by the Matrix-Game family. DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview focuses on a complementary axis to frontier-scale world simulators: low-compute adaptation, consumer-GPU runtime, and broad interactive capability coverage. It supports live keyboard and mouse control, multimodal initialization, mid-stream reprompting, dual-view operation, and minute-scale interactive rollouts at native 480p resolution, reaching up to 14 to 15 FPS FPS on a single RTX 4090 with a low memory footprint. By leveraging open video backbones and applying targeted adaptation runs, we build the preview system with high cost-efficiency. DF-World 0.1 Preview is not yet a memory-complete or frontier-quality world simulator, but demonstrates a practical low-compute route toward real-time controllable world-model previews on consumer GPUs.

</details>


### [581] [Toward an Energy-Optimized Operation of Data Centers Located in Wind Farms Using Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30316)
*Jan Stenner,Alexander Kilian,Sebastian Peitz,Hermann de Meer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates Reinforcement Learning as an online controller for curtailment-aware workload shifting in wind-turbine-integrated HPC data centers. Using a reproducible fixed-day simulation framework, the authors find that pure RL suffers from credit assignment and underuses free wind energy early in the day. They test Imitation Learning and Reward Shaping as countermeasures; PPO and a SAC variant with on-policy updates perform best, though a gap to the offline optimizer remains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an online controller for workload shifting in wind-turbine-integrated HPC data centers that can handle curtailment awareness, addressing the limitations of pure RL (credit assignment, underuse of free wind energy) in this setting.

Method: A reproducible fixed-day simulation framework with synthetic wind and price signals and delayed completion feedback. The minimal case (one turbine, one co-located data center) is studied using PPO, SAC with on-policy updates, Imitation Learning, and Reward Shaping.

Result: PPO and the SAC variant with on-policy updates show strong empirical performance. Both Imitation Learning and Reward Shaping improve relevant configurations. A performance gap to the offline optimizer persists due to the fundamental difference in information access (online vs. full-day foresight).

Conclusion: The benchmark and ablation results provide a transparent basis for extending the approach toward richer multi-site and continuous-time scenarios.

Abstract: This paper studies Reinforcement Learning as an online controller for curtailment-aware workload shifting in wind-turbine-integrated high-performance computing (HPC) data centers. We introduce a reproducible fixed-day simulation framework with synthetic wind and price signals and delayed completion feedback, designed to be extensible toward more complex scenarios. As a controlled benchmarking basis, we then focus on the minimal case with one wind turbine and one co-located data center. In this setting, pure Reinforcement Learning exhibits a pronounced credit-assignment problem and tends to underuse free wind energy early in the day. We therefore evaluate two complementary countermeasures: optimization-based Imitation Learning and potential-based Reward Shaping. Across multi-seed training and a 200-day test set, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) variant with an additional on-policy update routine achieve strong empirical performance among learned policies, and both Imitation Learning and Reward Shaping provide improvements in relevant configurations. A performance gap to the optimizer remains, which is expected: the optimizer plans offline with full-day foresight, whereas Reinforcement Learning must decide online from current observations without future realizations. The benchmark and ablation results provide a transparent basis for extending the approach toward richer multi-site and continuous-time scenarios.

</details>


### [582] [Hybrid Active-Online Learning Framework for Label-Efficient Concept Drift Adaptation in Optical Network Failure Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30322)
*Yousuf Moiz Ali,Jaroslaw E. Prilepsky,João Pedro,Sasipim Srivallapanondh,Antonio Napoli,Sergei K. Turitsyn,Pedro Freire*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid active-online learning framework using margin-based selective labeling achieves near-ceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples for optical network failure detection under concept drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address label efficiency challenges in adapting to concept drift for optical network failure detection, where labeling all streaming samples is costly or impractical.

Method: A hybrid active-online learning framework with margin-based selective labeling that queries only informative streaming samples.

Result: Achieves near-ceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples, with negligible latency overhead compared to static inference.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid active-online learning framework is highly label-efficient for concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection, achieving strong performance with minimal labeling cost and latency.

Abstract: We propose a hybrid active-online learning framework for label-efficient concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection. Using margin-based selective labeling, our method achieves nearceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples, with negligible latency overhead compared to static inference.

</details>


### [583] [FlexTab: A Flexible Encoder-Decoder Architecture for In-Context Learning Across Diverse Tabular Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30336)
*Marek Polewczyk,Maximilian Schambach,Marco Spinaci,Sam Thelin,Johannes Höhne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlexTab is a flexible encoder-decoder architecture for tabular in-context learning that uses a task-agnostic encoder with task-specific decoders, achieving SOTA on classification, regression, anomaly detection, and entity matching while being competitive on entity classification in relational databases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tabular in-context learners entangle feature representations with specific prediction targets, lacking flexibility. The authors aim to produce target-agnostic row embeddings usable across diverse downstream tasks within a table-native in-context learning setup.

Method: FlexTab pairs a single, task-agnostic encoder with a suite of task-specific decoders. Both encoder and decoders are trained on a large corpus of real-world, unlabeled tables. The design produces target-agnostic row embeddings.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on classification, regression, anomaly detection, and entity matching. Remains competitive with specialized models on entity classification in a relational setting.

Conclusion: A single shared encoder paired with task-specific decoders can serve as an effective general-purpose backbone for diverse tabular prediction problems.

Abstract: We introduce FlexTab, a flexible encoder-decoder architecture for in-context learning on tabular data that pairs a single, task-agnostic encoder with a suite of task-specific decoders. Unlike existing tabular in-context learners, which entangle feature representations with a specific prediction target, our design produces \textit{target-agnostic} row embeddings that can be leveraged across a wide range of downstream tasks within a table-native in-context learning setup. We demonstrate this flexibility on six distinct problems: classification, regression, anomaly detection, clustering, entity matching, and entity classification in relational databases. Both the encoder and the task-specific decoders are trained on a large corpus of real-world, unlabeled tables. FlexTab achieves state-of-the-art performance on classification, regression, anomaly detection and entity matching, while remaining competitive with specialized models on entity classification in a relational setting. These results demonstrate that a single shared encoder, paired with task-specific decoders, can serve as an effective general-purpose backbone for diverse tabular prediction problems. The inference code and checkpoints will be made publicly available at https://github.com/SAP-samples/flextab.

</details>


### [584] [DRIFT: Difficulty Routing Self-DIstillation with Rhythm-Gated Exploration and Success BuFfer Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30345)
*Haisen Luo,Yiwei Liu,Haoning Wang,Dan Liu,Junxi Yin,Haotian Wang,Lei Zhang,Xiaoyu Tian,Shuaiting Chen,Yuansheng Song,Baoyan Guo,Xiongfei Yan,Bolan Yang,Chengwei Liu,Ming Cui,Jiong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRIFT is a self-evolution framework for LLMs that uses Difficulty Routing and Rhythm Gating to dynamically allocate optimization signals at both problem and token levels, achieving state-of-the-art results across five benchmarks (79.5% average accuracy, outperforming GRPO by 9.5% and SDPO by 7.5%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-distillation and RL methods for LLM self-improvement lack explicit mechanisms to track problem-level learning progress and adapt optimization strategies, leading to over-optimization on easy problems, weak supervision on hard problems, and insufficient exploration of borderline cases.

Method: DRIFT proposes Difficulty Routing (identifies problem-level learning state and dynamically allocates self-distillation vs. RL signals) and Rhythm Gating (refines policy updates at token level, focusing exploration on critical reasoning positions), augmented by a success buffer and two-stage curriculum learning.

Result: Across five benchmarks and three model scales, DRIFT surpasses GRPO and SDPO on all metrics, achieving 79.5% average accuracy (vs. GRPO 70.0% and SDPO 72.0%), and 79.2% on ToolUse (vs. GRPO 65.7% and SDPO 68.5%).

Conclusion: DRIFT establishes a new state-of-the-art for self-improving LLMs without external supervision, demonstrating that joint problem-level routing and token-level gating effectively stabilizes and improves self-evolution.

Abstract: Enabling large language models to achieve stable self-improvement without external expert supervision remains a central challenge in complex reasoning tasks. Existing self-distillation and reinforcement learning methods lack explicit mechanisms for tracking problem-level learning progress and adapting optimization strategies accordingly. Consequently, training may over-optimize easy problems, receive weak supervision from hard problems, and fail to sufficiently explore borderline cases. To resolve these issues, we propose DRIFT, an online self-evolution policy optimization framework for large language models. DRIFT regulates the model's self-improvement process through the joint use of Difficulty Routing and Rhythm Gating. The former identifies the model's learning state at the problem level and dynamically allocates self-distillation and reinforcement learning signals, while the latter refines policy updates at the token level, concentrating exploration on critical reasoning positions. By further incorporating a success buffer and a two-stage curriculum learning strategy, DRIFT preserves high-quality historical experience while progressively guiding the model from reliable behavior acquisition toward stable policy evolution. Evaluated across five benchmarks and three model scales, DRIFT surpasses the peak performance of both GRPO and SDPO across all evaluated metrics. On the average score over the five benchmarks, DRIFT achieves 79.5$\%$, outperforming GRPO by 9.5$\%$ and SDPO by 7.5$\%$, establishing a new state-of-the-art result. Notably, on ToolUse, DRIFT reaches an accuracy of 79.2$\%$, improving over GRPO by 13.5$\%$ and SDPO by 10.7$\%$, setting a new state-of-the-art and substantially outperforming all concurrent methods.

</details>


### [585] [On the Vulnerability of Parameter-Level Defenses to Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30360)
*Kuangpu Guo,Qingyan Zheng,Jian Liang,Yongcan Yu,Zilei Wang,Ran He,Tieniu Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies a vulnerability in parameter-level defenses for model merging (protected task vectors are small in magnitude, dominated by pretrained weights), proposes Anchor-Guided Attack (AGA) to recover transformation matrices by using the pretrained model as an anchor, and introduces Anchor-Repulsive Fine-tuning (ARF) as a counter-defense.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training-free model merging via parameter-level defenses (linear parameter transformations) to prevent unauthorized (free-rider) model merging has security risks; the authors aim to systematically analyze these defenses and reveal their inherent weaknesses.

Method: AGA aligns the protected model with the pretrained model (static reference anchor) to analytically recover the transformation matrix, bypassing both individual and composite defenses. ARF mitigates anchor dominance to counter AGA.

Result: AGA consistently bypasses both individual and composite defenses under realistic defense-agnostic scenarios; ARF effectively defeats AGA.

Conclusion: Parameter-level defenses are vulnerable due to small protected task vectors dominated by the pretrained model; both an effective attack (AGA) and a counter-defense (ARF) are demonstrated.

Abstract: The training-free integration of expert models via model merging has exposed significant security risks, enabling free-riders to combine specialized models without authorization. Recent works propose parameter-level defenses that employ linear parameter transformations to neutralize this threat. In this paper, we systematically analyze such defenses and reveal that their protected task vectors are inherently small in magnitude. Consequently, the protected weights remain overwhelmingly dominated by the pretrained model. Based on this observation, we designate the pretrained model as a static reference anchor and propose the Anchor-Guided Attack (AGA) to circumvent existing safeguards. Specifically, AGA aligns the protected model with this anchor to recover the transformation matrix analytically.
